author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Bhel Puri for a Party  Recipe
commentBody: Here's a massive cheat, if you're going to the Indian store anyways. Buy the Haldiram brand bhel puri mix which has the puffed rice already in there, buy the tamarind chutney, buy the green chutney. Go home and add your own potato, onions, mango, cilantro, all the fresh stuff. And then assemble! Voila!

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Bhel Puri for a Party  Recipe
commentBody:
It is available in most Indian Grocery stores. Pro tip: Buy a bag with the air removed, it should be a vacuum packed one. If the puffed rice/Murmura is loose in the bag, air has got into it, and it may be a bit stale or "soggy." Not something you want, it should be crispy. If you go to an Indian grocery store and no one speaks English, as for "Murmura" = "Moor Mura" (phonetically.

If you live in a humid state, keep it in a tight plastic box.

author: Priya Krishna

commentBody: BTW, as "chapeauchin" wrote, you can also find Bhel mix and skip all the involved prep routine...

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Bhel Puri for a Party  Recipe
commentBody: This is addictive and delicious. Sure, you could buy all the stuff premade and assemble, but I doubt it will be nearly as good. So I’d recommend making the chutneys and following this as written. I was confused about buying bhel mix to make bhel mix, but trust the process! The only change was that I used roti instead of flour tortillas.

author: Priya Krishna

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Pescado a la Talla (Contramar’s Red and Green Grilled Snapper) Recipe
commentBody: i did this with just the red sauce (i was lazy and the read sauce was my favorite when i ate the real deal at contramar). it came out wonderfully. the challenge is grilling the fish. it would be fine in a pan but the grill, and grilling over real charcoal, is what makes the original dish so amazing. it was hard to keep the butterflied fish together during the flip but that's just a matter of practice. search around to find contramar's green salsa with lettuce to have on the side. perfect

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Pescado a la Talla (Contramar’s Red and Green Grilled Snapper) Recipe
commentBody: I couldn't find cascabel chiles so I used more guajillo and the sauce came out so delicious with layers of smokiness and spice. Since I had so much leftover red sauce, I made chilaquiles with it the next day. Butterflying the fish was the most difficult part of making this dish, but serving it that way definitely makes it a dinner showpiece.

commentBody:
This is heavenly. The flavors are beautiful.
And by the way, ruby chocolate isn't white chocolate with pink food coloring. It's a type of chocolate with a natural pink hue from ruby cocoa beans.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: You’re close, but incorrect. The main difference between ruby chocolate and other types of chocolate is the production process. Ruby chocolate uses unfermented cocoa beans, while other types of chocolate use fermented cocoa beans. The citric acid is combined with the cocoa beans to create the pink color and fruity flavor.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I was afraid this recipe was going to be fiddly, based on other reviews, but I was pleasantly surprised. I followed the recipe pretty much to the letter, but did stir the almond flour mixture for a couple of minutes longer than indicated when I added it to the saucepan. The greased spatula made spreading it easier, and it wasn't sticky. The bark cut beautifully, and the mixture of scents, colors and flavors was dreamy. This was an actually an easy bake, once I had all the ingredients!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Fun to make and turned out well just by following the recipe closely. I wanted to expand the recipe to use all 340 grams of ruby chocolate I ordered from Amazon. -- and it worked perfectly by adding a sized up bake to a 9X9 pan, along with the 8X8 pan.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I just made this and really liked the flavor.  I followed the recipe exactly.  Even used a thermometer to check sugar mixture temperature. I only cooked the sugar and almond mixture for a few minutes... should I cook it longer? Or is the bottom layer supposed to be super sticky? But flavor wise its very unique, but very sweet!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
I weighed my ingredients, thought I followed instructions and ended up with tasty sand. When I added the almond to the sugar it was a solid. Heated it anyways, crumbled more evenly and got browner but not candy-like. 
Not going to waste the chocolate… I’ll try to incorporate the sweet almond sand into a dessert… it’s tasty.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I followed the directions, exactly.  Opted for using 235 deg vs. the one-string method.  I think 235 may be too hot, or my thermometer is off - when combined with the almond mixture I ended up with a crumble.  Made more syrup which solved the texture problem, waiting for the completed project to cool so I can try cutting it.  Fingers crossed it doesn't fall apart.  The components are delicious.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: @Tracy The exact same thing happened to me! I used my candy thermometer and once it hit 235, I added the almond mix. It’s tasty sand. I think the temperature is off.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
I wonder if cooking the syrup only until it coats the back of a spoon would work.  I am going to try that approach next time I make this. 
It takes so long to reach 235 deg that much of the water boils off, which is probably why the almond mixture is so dry and crumbly - not enough liquid to bind it properly, and what there is, is too viscous.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Does it need to cook longer ? Used the right ingredients and measurements … do you freeze the almond mixture before adding the chocolate ?

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: If you’re not accustomed to making Badam Burfi I recommend looking at another recipe online in terms of the consistency of the almond/sugar syrup mix you’re going for before pulling it off the heat. The directions here (bubbled up and pulling away from the side) resulted in a sticky gooey mess of a final product. In looking at other recipes online it looks much more like the consistency of a cookie dough when pulled from the heat and then is kneaded into shape. The chocolate / flowers and dehydrated fruit it gorgeous and delicious. I’ll just cook the almond flour an additional 10min or so next time!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
I had the same issue as a few readers, where I ended up with a sort of extremely tasty sand after adding the burfi mixture to the syrup. I tried to save it with adding more syrup, as suggested by another reader (another 1/3 of the recipe), but it didn't solve the texture issue. 

I went on with the chocolate and, after making a sort of biscuit pastry base with the 'sand', poured the choc on top. I ended up with a sort of choc bark wafer that was tasty but not the recipe. Won't make again.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: The word peda should be removed from this recipe. Making peda is completely different, and uses ingredients that are not even listed here. This looks like a good recipe, but should be called Melted Icecream with Milk Powder. Please don't Americanize Indian recipes to the point where they are unrecognizable.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I just made a version of this ! Something in the melted ice cream made incorporating the powdered milk easy. I had a saffron, pistachio ice cream flavour. I added mango pulp and cooked it. It is fabulous!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
Indians would never melt icecream to make peda ! Peda are made with mawa (semisolid product of evaporating  full fat milk on heat). Ice creams are enjoyed as sweet and cold dessert in warm weather. 
Such a backward bend of evaporating icecream with milk powder. Quite loss of energy resources!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: So no need to worry about the "overrun," the amount of air in the ice cream? 4 cups of ice cream would yield between about 2 and 2-2/3 cup melted ice cream depending on how fluffy vs dense it is, which seems big enough to matter.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I went to the Indian market and made this with pistachio ice cream and mawa, the Indian milk powder. The woman running the store recommended that to me rather than plain milk powder. Sounds like many Indians use this method, myself included now.

commentBody: I make these sandwiches using fresh chopped mint instead of dill and they’re perfect.  Really, you can use any cucumber variety as long as they are fresh. I even use the crisp pickling cukes from my small garden. Serve them on a hot day with gin & tonics!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: When I was a private chef in Manhattan I used Pepperidge Farm Thin Sliced Sandwich Bread for these and other tea sandwiches. Belgian Cress was a must add to a platter for tea service. Butterfield Market always stocked it. Cucumber, Belgian cress and French ham sufficed for a nice variety.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: The Middle East (Lebanon and other Levantine countries) have a version of this that uses labneh (strained yogurt) instead of cream cheese. Healthier (probiotics) and includes a delicious tang!

author: Priya Krishna

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: @Steph B. Please no jalapeños. If you must have “heat,” mince a very small guajilo chili (seeds and ribs removed) so as not to offend the delicacy of this tea sandwich.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
@Steph B

Yes, chives work well.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: @Steph B Jalapeños are a great idea. Zaynab Issa’s new book has a Cucumber and Green Chutney sandwich recipe, and the chutney has jalapeños in it. Good intuition for something different!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
I had an elderly friend who made a variation of this. Sourdough bread smeared with mayo, thinly sliced cucumbers sprinkled with dried dill and a little salt. No crusts, small tea sandwiches. She served it with chips, tomato bisque soup with a dash of nutmeg and dollop of sour cream- hazelnut coffee.
Simple and delicious- she died in a car wreck in 1997. I miss her and our lunches -She was one of my first clients when I started vet house calls.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Sweet memory

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
Dill? No, simply no. Dill will completely overwhelm the delicate flavor of cucumber.

Use the thinnest sliced bread you can get - Pepperidge Farm do a specialty bread. Use soft salted butter. A twist of pepper. Cut off the crusts. Cut to squares or 'fingers'. Never triangles - the late Queen did not eat triangular sandwiches!  You could use cream cheese, but nothing should get in the way of the cucumber.  
These sandwiches should be so insubstantial that you hardly know you have eaten them.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: It’s called Extra Thin and it comes in brown or white. Can’t always find it though.  I freeze before cutting stack of slices first. It’s easier to work with.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I thought the whipped cream cheese deflated a little too much when I stirred the dill in; next time I might just put the dill on top of the cream cheese instead of mixing it.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Pro Tips: Make sure the cream cheese is soft enough to spread without tearing the bread, ideally room temperature; trim the crusts off when the sandwiches are assembled– preferably after a rest in the refrigerator– and if possible, just before serving. Bread-wise, you're looking for a Pullman loaf from an artisanal bakery.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Trim the crusts before assembling the sandwiches. Otherwise the crust will likely have cream cheese on them, which will mean that they can't be made into bread crumbs. Pro tip: whipped cream cheese, as called for in the recipe, is already spreadable.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I first had cucumber sandwiches in Brasilia at a monthly needlework group for diplomatic wives almost 50 years ago. We usually served them on a very dark, very thinly sliced German or Swedish pumpernickel. Radishes work well, too. Outstanding.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I'll never forget my first cucumber sandwiches.  I was in my first residency at the Edward MacDowell Colony in New Hampshire and they were in my lunch basket.  I'd only heard about them previously in Oscar Wilde.  I was instantly hooked.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Oh you made me smile.  I grew up in Peterborough and was enthralled whenever an artist would visit my high school art class.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Did this for lunch as a normal sandwich, for one (made all the necessary adjustments) and it was super delish. The salting of the cukes really makes a difference. Only change I made was to use Spice Islands Beau Monde seasoning salt in the cream cheese instead of just salt (a favorite from my childhood & amazingly still available). But any seasoning salt, or garlic/ onion salt would add that extra oomph. Can't wait to make the actual tea sandwiches.

author: Priya Krishna

author: Priya Krishna

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Mayonnaise has eggs.  Some don't want.

author: Priya Krishna

author: Priya Krishna

author: Priya Krishna

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Women's meeting this coming afternoon, and these cucumber sandwiches will be my contribution.  And thank you.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
A question for Priya: What's the best method to neatly cut the sandwiches?
Far too often the cucumber slices slide around while attempting to cut into rectangles or triangles. Or the cream cheese oozes and smears the cut-side of the bread. Is there a way to avoid those unsightly problems?

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Cut the bread before assembling the sandwiches.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I use a round cookie cutter for the bread, perfect fit for the cucumber round, no slippage and easy handling for guests, making bread crumbs with the edges - perfect.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I've made Ina Garten's version (which uses thyme instead of dill and half goat cheese) and they are wonderful.  The real key is to bake or buy some Japanese milk bread (shokupan) for the sandwiches.  The texture of the bread makes these sandwiches wonderful.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Another variation of this sandwich uses salted butter instead of the cream cheese/dill spread and a thin layer of mint chutney. The cucumber and mint go together very well. The mint chutney should be fresh or at least a frozen variety. No need to add the pepper if the chutney has a bit of green chili for the kick.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: They are not going to be Bridgerton-grade with dreadful American soggy white bread. You need bread that is pert, yeasty, and without much sugar to off-set the cucumbers.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: "Pert" is an all-time hall-of-foodie-fame food descriptor.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Did no one else's grandmother always make her cucumber sandwiches open-faced on cocktail rye bread?! I can't imagine them any other way. She also sprinkled seasoned salt on top of the cucumber.

author: Priya Krishna

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I wasn't even necessarily planning to make this recipe, but after reading some of these comments I feel I have to make it now. There are some STRONG feelings!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I must be doing something wrong, as the cucumbers kept sliding out of the sandwich as I ate it.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Perhaps salting, draining, and then drying the cucumber slices will remedy this situation.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
Some ingredient issues, I believe.  
Should not be 28 slices of bread.  Should be around 8.
8 oz of whipped cream cheese is not sufficient to be generous with the cream cheese slathered on each piece of bread.  I would say double that.

I did roll my bread with a rolling pin, as one writer suggested.  I also added a bit of kalamata tapenade to the cream cheese mixture.  In the future, I will spread the tapenade on the slices because I was not fond of the color it made the cream cheese and dill.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
@Carole Nicholson
Yes...your old friend did it the way our family did---mayo, not cream cheese. Very tasty. We saved the butter for our radish sandwiches!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: To get a cleaner edge and beautiful side view: leave the crusts on, assemble the sandwich, cut it in half diagonally, and then finally cut the crusts off. I like to then stack them on the diagonal edge in a row on the serving plate.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I mix zesty Italian dry mix in with the cream cheese for mine. I’ll try this recipe as well to compare.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: please let us know the results of your comparison.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
As a cook in Main Line Philadelphia, many years ago, I worked in a kitchen that produced thousands of tea sandwiches every year, for Tuesday afternoon clubs, Thursday afternoon clubs, and any other club that met on some day of the week.  
We used cookie cutters to make the sandwiches into little flower shapes or circles (as alternative to rigid squares or triangles) and filled them with finely chopped tuna or chicken salad. 
Another, with watercress and cream cheese was rolled.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: For afternoon tea last summer I used the original Boursin cheese with thin English cucumber slices...they didn't last long.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: My mother and I used to help make many trays of these tea sandwiches for the Ladies Auxiliary of the Royal Victoria Hospital in Montreal, back in the 70’s. The secret to perfectly sliced sandwiches was to use an electric carving knife to remove the crusts.  Never failed, no sawing or tearing of the bread (Pepperidge Farm thin bread was preferred).   I still have my decades old electric knife ready any time tea sandwiches are required!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Pepperidge Farm makes a “very thin” sliced white bread—great for tea sandwiches.  I’ve added minced herbs (dill, chives, etc) w a little S&P to crème fraiche to spread thinly on the bread—then top w thin cucumber slices and a little watercress. Also fun:  a finely chopped curried egg salad or the vintage classic: ham salad (which I put on wheat).  And yes:  if making many ahead of time, cover your tray/plate of sandwiches with a barely damp tea towel and then cover with a plastic bag or wrap

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: When I was a teenager and helped make this tea sandwiches for various family events my mother ordered crustless bread from the grocery bakery. White, rye or pumpernickel.  It was the size of cookie sheets.  This was amazing really.  I inquired about this recently and the bakery staff looked at me like I was an alien.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Back in the 1960’s, my mother would make canapés for cocktail parties and showers.  She would go to the bakery and request a loaf of bread sliced lengthwise.  After trimming the crusts, my mom would place the filling on the long slices of bread, then roll it and slice it, creating spirals.  Ham salad and cream cheese with olives were the most popular fillings.  I don’t know of any bakeries that can still slice bread lengthwise.

author: Priya Krishna

I found a bakery in North Vancouver,  BC, Canada that still has their Pullman pans and they know exactly what I want!

author: Priya Krishna

Cucumber and Cream Cheese sandwiches are good. You could also switch out the butter for Cream Cheese for the salmon. Mix Cream Cheese with the chives and dill etc.
Or make them club sandwiches... use the white bread with cream chese, then cucumber, then a slice of wholemeal bread (light brown), a little butter for adhesion only, then the salmon, then top off with dark rye/pumpernickel with the herby cream cheese. 
Another nice combo, egg mayo salad one side with thin sliced ham on other.

author: Priya Krishna

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
Here's my kids lunch version: 
1. Wake up, wash rice, use short grain if possible, if not, no worries, cook on quick setting in the rice cooker.
2. Find the onigiri mold you bought on Amazon.
3. Cut nori into strips, or whatever fits your mold. Follow directions here about wetting/salting your hands.
4. Find something in the fridge to put in the middle. Smoked salmon and Kewpie? Avocado and cukes? Leftover tuna salad?
5. Put rice, then filling, then rice in mold, wrap in nori. Happy kids!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
Mixing furikake with the cooked rice makes for umami-rich onigiri!  Especially love furikake made with dried shiso. 

My husband's favorite Japanese convenience store onigiri is tuna with mayo :).

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Per a Japanese recipe, I always put my onigiri together using plastic wrap. What you do is this: Lay a piece of plastic wrap flat. Scoop rice onto it and spread it out sort of flat. (I often salt the rice a bit at this stage.) Place your filling on top in the center. Using the plastic wrap, fold the rice over the filling so it forms a ball, then shape into a triangle. Unwrap the plastic so you can wrap the nori around the rice, then re-wrap the plastic and you're ready to go! Simple and no mess.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Babies in blankets? Diapers? Thankfully I've managed to make these – well before encountering this recipe – without any of this imagery. I do use seasoned sushi vinegar, contrary to one commentator's preferences, as I would if making maki rolls, and I got an inexpensive onigiri press on Amazon that shapes the rice perfectly. My favorite filling is Ikura with a little hot sauce.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I’ve not made this, but aside from the rice cooker/onogiri mold comment, I can’t see how this recipe as described has 5 minutes prep time. The 3-4 rinses of the uncooked rice would take 2-3 minutes and I’ve not even begun to diaper the salmon.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: My family always used the salted water technique Priya describes for food safety.  Pickled plums (umeboshi) were often placed inside for the same reason.  Then we would eat them with chicken karaage that had been sitting out for hours, so go figure.  I don’t know how I survived my childhood.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Can someone provide more guidance on how to wrap the seaweed around the rice and salmon? I have two kids but really don’t see how the diaper reference helps.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I made my rice more sushi like by using rice wine vinegar (5tbsp), salt (1tsp), and sugar (1tbsp) (whisk together in a small bowl then fold in the rice after it was off heat for 10min), and had no issues getting the nori to stick.  This recipe doesn’t include flavoring for the rice and this step makes it *very* sticky.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Simple, good instructions. Proportions of rice to salmon seem a bit off though. I doubled the recipe but it only made 11 onigiri and I had about half the salmon left. This wasn't a big issue because I ate the leftover salmon on its own, but would recommend trial and error if you are planning on making these to share as you may not get the same yield as the recipe author.

author: Priya Krishna

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I would recommend high quality short grain rice such Tamanishiki (grown in Sacramento valley) that is less than a year old after milling. Cook the rice in a Zojirushi rice cooker with the sushi rice setting. Also the salmon can be perfectly cooked in a plastic bag in a sous vide cooker at 131F for 45 minutes.

author: Priya Krishna

author: Priya Krishna

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: The rice should be seasoned -- rice vinegar and sugar. And shouldn't the salmon be seasoned as well? Soy and mirin might be nice. Anyway, anything can be stuffed into onigiri, not sure why this very generic set of instructions merits a spot on the Nytimes recipe page.

author: Priya Krishna

It turned out amazing, and was super filling.  Will definitely be making this again.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Much as I love Priya Krishna, it is impossible to cook Dal of any type so quickly. One the source of heat, is it electric, gas, induction etc? Two the size & type of pot. Three the dal to water ratio. Four with or without a lid. Seriously it is no joke. And it is vital to add salt AFTER cooking as it will affect the texture. Ginger & Garlic are essential ingredients. Washing the dal ten times is a must. With an Instant Pot I make it in 17 minutes flat. Pure joy with the Instant Pot.Enjoy.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
Option 1: Grandma's Approach! Soak the dal overnight and then cook it in the morning.

Alternatively, widely used in India as it is something we cook if we are in hurry or don't have meal planned or weekday menu! 
Option 2: For a quicker method, use an instant pot. Don't worry about the water to dal ratio - at a minimum, you should add 1 part dal to 3 parts water to avoid burning. Cook for about 10 minutes in the instant pot on the sealing mode and let the pressure release naturally. You can't overcook dal, so 10-13 minutes should be okay. 
If you don't have an instant pot or pressure cooker, it will take much longer to cook.

Regardless of the method you choose, use a hand blender if available. If not, a fork or whisk will also work.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Cannot beat the Bob’s Red Mills dal recipe found on their bag of red lentils.   My Indian neighbor asked me for the recipe!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I make this dish regularly. It's so easy, quick, and delicious. Depending on my mood, I'll temper the dal with the ghee and spices at the end, like this recipe does. Other times, I'll bloom all the spices (turmeric, cumin, and chili peppers) first in ghee and then add grated garlic and ginger before adding in the red lentils and water. I usually use 4 cups of water because we like it on the soupy side. I always serve it with a side of basmati rice. So good!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: @Erica H. Thank you! This is so helpful!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: @Erica H. Turmeric should not be bloomed. It should be boiled with the lentils.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Always rinse red lentils.  Many many times.  One comment here suggests ten times.  That sounds fine to me.  Same with any dry beans, chick peas, green or brown lentils and rice.   Especially rice.  You can either take it from me and start doing that today or go to the trouble of  visiting a rice factory in Thailand, India or Pakistan and start rinsing rice and pulses after getting home from that trip.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Yes it is essential to wash ALL lentils a minimum of TEN times to remove all dust etc whether you cook in a pot or pressure cooker or an Instant Pot.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: In my part of India (Bengal), we temper Massor Dal with nigela seeds (kalonji) rather than cumin. No asafoetida. This is serves with a bit of lime juice. Alternatively, we add fried onions (and skip the lime juice).

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I was taught never to cook masoor dal without rinsing it out thoroughly in several changes of water. It make it less gassy and is so much easier on the stomach.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
Some regional Indian variations, both vegan:
One Coarsely chopped onion 1 TBS each garlic and ginger into food processor.  Add small amount of water until it forms a slurry.  Temper the spices as described using canola, and then brown the slurry in the oil.  Mix this with the dal.  Or... Make the dal a bit thicker to start and with all spices in the pot, and then add coconut milk.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Not sure if it was because of my Indian boyfriend’s mom, or just the Indian restaurants I frequented when I was young, but most of the dals I ate weren’t complete unless they had some chopped cilantro (I actually learned the term coriander leaves first) on top - *chef’s kiss*.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Instant Pot: 10 minutes high pressure.  Always remember to sort and pick over any beans or lentils before cooking.  Also, when cooking lentils on the stove top, a foamy scum will form when it comes to a boil.  Skim this off and discard.  You can reduce this affect by washing the lentils in three rinses of cold water before cooking, and you should do this if using the Instant Pot.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
@Tony if FL.
I love my pressure cooker and nearly always use it for beans. However- I had one explode (and I’d been using them since helping my grandmother on her farm as a child) when making lentils. Lentils are small enough to block the air pressure escape valve. It’s why the old fashioned ones had a pressure gauge and “rocker”. I had a trusty Presto with all parts cleaned, pre- checked and intact including the seal. It was the worst scenario. We were aboard our sailboat in the boatyard held above (we were doing our work power sanding and repainting the bottom). We gained access via a tall ladder. It was rudimentary camping. Lentils were literally everywhere.  No one was injured. My SO merely looked at me. Closest food was a dinghy ride away. I nodded- setting to the initial clean - up. Months of finding bean spatter in unlikely places. I’d picked through. I will never cook lentils under pressure again. My SO was buried at sea this August. We had many adventures together. I still have the Calphalon cooking set he gave me. Scrubbed with saltwater and still quite serviceable. I’m on a 2nd pressure cooker following the Italian version I purchased in San Juan. When the cantilevered lid lock stopped working, I replaced it with my current All Clad.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
@Saffron oh my! Glad no one was hurt. And very sorry for your more recent loss. 
I am using an Instant Pot, do you still prefer the stovetop kind? The IP seems a bit safer.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: @Saffron I am also so sorry for the loss of your partner. I am from Seattle and helped my parents clean the bottom of their 28’ sailboat in Shilshole Bay.  I went with them each summer for a week around the Gulf Islands.  My mother cooked many meals in its tiny galley. I know how tight sailboats are inside. I can’t imagine a pot of lentils exploding all over!  My best to you.

commentBody: Asefitida will help the flavor of this dish.  But I keep it double out triple wrapped as it is very stinky.  Original container inside a sealed jar.  And I use little enough at each use that I buy smallest container.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
@Veggie
I bought solid asafetida resin from an Indian store a couple of decades ago. I break off small lumps, which melt into the food. I never noticed any odor, even when I first purchased it.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I have cooked a variety of dals, and this one is my absolute favorite. Thank you for this yummy, quick recipe. Asafated is key, good quality asfateda. It adds a lovely deliciousness. I purchase it online from Pure Indian Foods

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Thanks for that recommendation!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I've made Dal at home since I was a child and I just tried this recipe.  I did everything exactly as she recommended. Sorry nay-sayers - this is simple, delicious and it works ion 15 minutes. I ate with a side of rice, dollop of plain yogurt, and a flat bread. Perfect and delicious.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I don't know why she suggests not cleaning the dal. I find mine pretty dirty and in need of many rinses. Also they need to cook for 35 to 40 minutes before adding the ghee. One other point- I use whole red chilies instead of ground.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I have actually found tiny stones a couple times while sorting and washing lentils... You bite on one of those tiny stones, and break a tooth, and you will always sort & wash lentils, beans. Takes just a minute to do.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Growing up in Sri Lanka eating dhal everyday, I was advised never to add salt until the lentils boil as salt slows the boiling process and makes the lentils hard.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I grew up eating daal - my mum makes it nearly everyday. The fun thing is, daal varies from region to region in India. For example, my mother never added ginger and garlic to her basic masoor dal. I believe hers was closer to what Priya's got here (I loved this for a quick weeknight daal) except that my mum added onion and a bit of tomato paste to hers. Some folks add garam masala, or a creamy element (like coconut milk), or a tadka over top. Key is to have fun and find what you like!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: What no onions, garlic, ginger nor coconut milk???

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: "There are countless ways to make dal" from Priya's intro to this recipe.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: @Tizzylish None of those ingredients are essential when making dal, especially the coconut milk . . Indian dishes are not homogenous in their ingredients.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I love this recipe. It’s so simple and delicious. My modifications: add a handful of minced garlic and a knob of minced ginger to a couple tablespoons of ghee and cook for 2 min. Then follow the rest of the recipe as is. At the end, add a chopped Serrano pepper to taste. So good!!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
I doubled the asafoetida (as I love the flavor) but otherwise made this recipe exactly as written. It was beyond incredibly delicious… it was decadent (thanks, butter!). I say this as someone who LOVES dal and makes it all the time. 

This is a perfect weeknight recipe that is quick and easy to prepare (and the eight minute timing is perfect).  My husband raved about it and wants to serve it to guests. Even without the tadka (ghee), this is wonderful.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Most manufacturers don’t label it, but commercially available asofetida includes gluten. This was the bane of my life until a friend informed me about this! I now use Deep brand gluten free asofetida that’s manufactured in New Jersey and my cooking is back to its original aroma and flavor while being safe for me to eat.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Actually, it's much easier and just as good to throw everything in the pot and cook it...no need to bloom the spices separately. I put several whole peeled garlic cloves in, they melt into the dal.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: This has become our go to recipe when we are out of everything. Super easy. We use olive oil instead of ghee to make it vegan and because we always have it.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I’ve been eyeing dal recipes for years but was always scared off by something sounding scary, silly as that may sound. I was tempted to listen to some of the comments about adding this or that. But thought I had been so slow to finally do this. I should follow the recipe just as stated. I told myself I could always add next time. Outside of rinsing lentils and I did not have asafetida, I followed it as stated. Delicious. I’m so proud of myself to have this very basic recipe now to work with.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I grew up in India, and I have always found dal and rice to be my ultimate comfort food. Thank you for sharing this delicious recipe! During the cooking process, I added an extra cup of water and a red Thai chili. For the temper, I thinly sliced two cloves of garlic and added them.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: This is now our preferred breakfast after tiring of oatmeal with maple syrup. I'm trying to make dosas to accompany the dal. I usually serve it with basmati rice. The dal is very friendly toward leftover veggies - roasted cauliflower, sichuan broccoli, braised eggplant.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: We loved it! Kids enjoyed it very much and I really appreciate that I can make it fast! We used fennel seeds and 1 tsp ground cumin in coconut oil (as we have 2 dairy-free folks).

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: If, like me, you’ve never made dal before, this is a great first-time recipe—and it’s affordable, with readily available ingredients even for someone like me, who lives in a small Yukon town, where we don’t always get the best of everything, which I really appreciate. I make this every week now, with  variations, as a quick weeknight dinner that supplies left overs that only get better for lunch over several days.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
After reading the comments, I was up for an adventure. The end result was very tasty, though it looked not so good. I doubled the recipe and tempered my spices in Ghee (cumin seed, garam masala, cardamom, Chile pepper). I thoroughly washed the lentils and decided on the soupy side. I replaced part of rhe 4 C water w 1 can coconut milk and used 3 T ginger paste.
At the end if cooking I added t he ghee and blossomed spices. The result was delicious tasting and very unappealing visually. Grayish.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: That's great, you made a good decision to add the Coconut Milk & Garam Masala. Did you add Turmeric during boiling up the Masoor Dal, which would definitely turn it yellow. The Garam Masala will darken the dal, so perhaps that is why you were not pleased with the colour. When I cook Dal I do add my own roasted & ground Garam Masala, Turmeric, Roasted Coriander Powder, Red Kashmir Chili Powder with ginger & garlic; it is a bright yellow.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: You should always sort and wash dried lentils and beans before cooking. Unless you grow and dry your own, they're produced through a commercial process that introduces dust and occasionally even small sticks and stones. Sort through the lentils to find and remove those, rinse multiple times to get rid of the dust.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I like this simple and flavorful recipe even w/o onions, ginger etc. I do finely chop a small green chilli and throw it in the finished dal, though! I agree with some of the others who have said it's necessary to wash (or even briefly soak) the red lentils to avoid that frothy scum. (Of course you can keep removing it...). And I agree also that 10 mins on the stove seems short, 20 at least, even 30 (max) at a *slow* simmer once it's come to the boil. It'll be creamier and yummier.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Thank goodness, a dal recipe with no onions or garlic... I am violently allergic to both, but a tiny pinch of asafetida goes a long way.  This recipe is great using authentic New Mexico red chile powder.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I've made this recipe from an I dian cookbook that uses dried red chilis (the pods) instead of ground. Wanting to increase the volume, nutrition and fiber, I usually add 1 to 2 cups of chopped celery to the lentils and water. I love that additional flavor. This stuff is so delicious and easy to eat!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: If you don't live in NYC or otherwise have access to asafetida 🙄 and cumin seeds and ghee, this dish is great made with garlic powder, ground cumin, and butter. Comes together super fast and is extraordinarily flavorful.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
@Scott Windham
Or they can be ordered from Amazon.  ;-)

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: This tastes just like the dal I ate for years while living in India.  Wonderful!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
Note to Shoba: It is certainly not impossible to cook masoor dal this quickly (or at least within 5 mins. or so of this).  I make this dish frequently and it's great/

To be precise, I make the dish from Madhur Jaffrey's recipe, which is only slightly different, and have never understood why she thought it took so long for the dal to cook.  It just doesn't.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Hi. Just seen your reply. If you have made Masoor Dal in less than 10 minutes you must be fortunate in having the right combination of heat, pot, water to dal ratio. Cleaning dal is important as sometimes you will find stones. If you enjoy Dal, do experiment with other types of dal & recipes. All the best, Shobha

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
I can't wrap my head around the author's recommendation to not wash the red lentils in "Everyday Dal" recipe. You just have to wash all lentils especially red lentils. Their color literally changes from bright red to light faded red once yo wash them.

The pods are threshed to get the lentils from the plants so there is a lot of say dust and pesticides etc. and washing the lentils help get rid of all that contamination.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Ignoring the pleas and warnings, I dared myself to make these lentils without washing them. It felt like the culinary version of BASE jumping and was truly exhilarating. Will likely wash them in the future, but this a great recipe to explore variations on the lentil theme. Lovely, easy and comforting.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Not washing lentils???!!! Have you ever seen the factories where pulses, beans, etc. are produced? Yes, pick thru and wash wash wash, rinse, rinse, rinse!!!!!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: This came together well. I added cubed zucchini and topped it with the ghee mixture , chopped cilantro and a squeeze of lime. Served with various chutneys. Personal fave was the KFI Coriander Chutney Sauce.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: This is a scary recipe because as you cook it you can't help but fear that with so few ingredients it won't be good. It is good, just as written (except that I cooked it longer). It's great even without such seemingly essential ingredients as garlic, onion, ginger. Be bold and daring! Resist the temptation to add additional ingredients that aren't in the recipe.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Excellent. I like thick dal, so I used only two and a half cups of water. I was skeptical about the teaspoon of salt, but went with it. It was just right. I didn't add any additional salt at the end. I cooked it over very low heat for a longer time as the lentils I was using (half each of red and yellow) were trying to stick to the pot in the beginning. Asafetida is essential.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I have made this excellent dish countless times. Whoever says 'its impossible to cook so quickly' and wash the split lentils a bunch, that just is not true. It takes 8 minutes flat every time, on the stovetop. I use the instant pot to cook rice in the same time (1C water/1cup rice, set for pressure cook 5 minutes). So whenever the urge hits to order a delivery pizza or whatever I remind myself that in way less time I can have a delicious meal that is actually super healthy!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Dal cooking time in 10 minutes? I live in Dubai and have been cooking dal for my family for over 20 years. I wash and soak my dal for at least 30 minutes before cooking in medium heat for at least 30 minutes to break it down so it it would have the "porridge" consistency. Additionally, super finely chopped (or finely grated) onion and garlic are essential, even if you add asafetida at the end.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Not as interesting as I hoped, particularly for buying Asafetida just for this dish. I added onions and garlic to the ghee, but even with those, the flavor didn’t pop, and the color wasn’t as pretty as the photo. Still, it was fast, and everybody ate it, even my 11 year old. I served with brown rice and homemade naan.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Using the split red lentils makes this dal SO fast. I loved it. It was flavorful as written. No changes from me, and it is on my regular rotation.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
so delicious and adaptable!! 

to make it one pot and more filling, i saute some onion, garlic, ginger, and a green bird chili in some olive oil. then add ~ 2tbs tomato paste and cook until it darkens, then add cumin and turmeric. add the lentils, 5 cups water/ broth, s&p. as it's coming to boil, chop 2 carrots then throw those in. finish with some peas or spinach, dill or cilantro, and some ghee or butter.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Nice and easy, though I did rinse the lentils a few times. Next time? Less salt, less water: even three cups made it soupy. But a lovely soup.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Asafetida is an amazing gift to my cooking, as is this recipe.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Well, that was one of most delicious easy weeknight recipes ever. I made it exactly as written, except that I didn’t have asafoetida, so I used 1/8 tsp each of garlic powder and onion powder. (I have ordered some online for next time!) I found that 3 cups of liquid was a bit too much, as I prefer my dal thick—next time maybe 2.5 cups. Served with warm pita bread.  Yum.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Delicious and fast. Made according to recipe except subbed one of the tsp of cumin seed with nigella seeds. The lentils needed more than 10 min on my gas stove -at 17 min they were perfect. I will likely use 2 3/4 cup water next time for a less soupy dal. Served with flatbread. Yum.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: This sounds delicious, but I do not keep cumin seeds in my extensive selection of spices and herbs (3 shelves worth). Can one "bloom" the ground cumin in ghee before adding the dal to achieve a similar flavor.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
*very* briefly and at a lower temp so it won't burn.
That said, the flavor of the whole seeds is so much better, at least to me.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Totally appreciate the intention here - to have a basic palette on which to elaborate, according to one's custom or preferences.  A palette that is a worthy add in the most basic form described here, that in the spirit of 'comfort food' I will make again and again.  I added (this time) some baby potatoes (halved) and some leftover brown basmati rice, topping it with chopped cilantro and pumpkin seeds (because I had them handy) and a lime squirt.  Leftovers for lunch will be delicious!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Great meal and very easy and quick following Priya's guide.  I lightly cooked a minced clove of garlic and a crushed red pepper, then bloomed the turmeric before adding the lentils and liquids.  Added a half block of frozen spinach, cut into cubes after the liquids and before covering.  To the ghee I added about 3/4 pound of peeled shrimp and cooked them briefly before adding to the nearly finished lentils.  Delicious!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
1. add a split green chilly, and finely chopped ginger (1 teaspoon for two servings) into the dal when it boils. 
2. on the ghee prep - ensure you put the mixture into the dal while it is hot. in fact you could pour a bit of the dal into this and swirl it round and then add it to the pot of cooked dal. One could add a split dry red chilly to the ghee, it adds a ton of taste without it becoming too spicy - just don't let it cook too much in the hot ghee. first the chilly, then the cumin seed...

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: It takes me an hour to cook masur dal. Are you using a pressure cooker?

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: @NaomiH you’re thinking of whole brown lentils (masoor dal) which take much longer to cook, this recipe calls for split or hulled lentils (split masoor dal) which are red lentils.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: So easy and so so delicious!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Perfect recipe. But not washing your lentils is crazy.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Delicious as written. Amazing how quickly it is ready to be served.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Finely chop onions and garlic.  Caramelize the onions and roast the garlic (without browning) and add to the Dal for additional flavor.  Best served over steaming hot Basmati rice !

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: What’s the best non-dairy substitute for ghee in this dish? Thank you :)

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I'd say olive oil.  You could do coconut, but it's saturated.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: added washed lentils, ghee, cumin, chili, garlic powder, salt, ginger & garlic to the pot, med-high heat. added 3 cups water, brought to boil, simmer w/ cover for abt 5 mins. served w rice + labne sauce w/ salt, pepp, oil, cilantro + lemon squeeze all ovr everything

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I have made a version of this recipe for 40 years, only adding fried slivers of garlic in their oil to the salted purée. I look forward to trying the seasoning suggested here. If given the choice of one last meal before I die, pink lentil dal would be it.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Love red lentil dal. Curry leaves in the oil are essential for me - I buy a bunch and freeze them. Also add hing, mustard seed, split red chiles and ginger. So dang good.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I learned from Camellia Panjabi that cumin and asafoetida are both helpful to our bowels to prevent excessive flatulence.  In addition to providing wonderful flavour, they do help out our guts.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
This dhal recipe lacks flavoring.
So many ingredients are missing , to bring this dhal into a flavor profile:
Add: to the washed and boiled pink lentils: 
Into the pan the hot 1/4 cup ghee tempering add :1/2 teaspoon each of : black mustard seeds, whole cumin, ground coriander , salt , red chili flakes, sugar : add 1 tablespoon of crushed peanuts , 1 teaspoon of fresh grated ginger, 1 teaspoon of crushed garlic: allow to cook for 3 minutes: pour this over the lentils.Cook for 5 minutes.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Recommend using thin slice of raw ginger after the dal has cooked, and to the tadka or vaggarney (possibly unacceptable in comparison to upmarket chhonk) love the hing and the use of a few curry leaves and why for not the cilantro garnish?

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I washed my hands and rinsed my dal—easy. The recipe is terrific. We love garlic, and I saw that a lot of people added garlic, but I found that the asafetida more than did the trick (thanks, Amazon). I didn't have any ghee, so I clarified the butter myself. For the cumin, I toasted it in a hot pan and then ground it up in a mortar. I didn't like the idea of having whole cumin seeds in the temper.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
I always pick through all beans and lentils for stones.  Then I rinse the mansoor dal, but just once and quickly, otherwise it begins to dissolve in the water.

I also cook it with sauteed chopped onions and grated ginger, then add the bloomed spices.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Is red chili powder what we call cayenne in the States?

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Yes, it's close enough for most. You want spicy, not the chili powder we'd use in a pot of chili. But if you're able to find it, Kashmiri Chili powder is very nice. It has more flavor and a kind of floral sweetness that plain old cayenne doesn't. Any southeast Asian store will have it or get it online. I use it in place of cayenne for everything.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I make dal fairly often, so I was surprised how absolutely fabulous this recipe tasted. My whole family loved it. Perhaps we were hungry, or perhaps it’s because I added the asafetida, but it really was special. I paired it with Nigella Lawson’s Bright Rice recipe.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: This was a great, easy pantry meal. Subbed some of the water for a can of coconut milk, added a package of frozen spinach, plus some ginger and garlic. The cumin seeds (not powder) were a surprising delight.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I don't understand the first commenter's complaint. We use all sorts of Dal, more often Toor Dal and Moong Dal as an ingredient for Tamil Sambar. I pressure-cook it, granted using an old style, gas, induction ready, non-electric one which is slow, very slow to cook. As they say "back home" and innumerable South Indian cooking blogs "three whistles." Wait for the pressure gauge to normalize, and boom, Dal is ready. Mash it and add the other stuff, right inside that cooker. Piece of cake.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Loved it!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I've made plenty of dals, this is just average, I should have added the extra cup of water, it turned hard quickly.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
First of all you need to wash the lentils. I follow the Doyenne of Indian cooking Madhur Jaffrey's recipe:
 
Wash lentils
Bring to a boil, skim foam
Add sliced ginger and turmeric
Simmer for 1-1.5 hours
Add salt

For the tadka:
Ghee/Oil
Curry leaves (my addition)
Cumin seeds
Ground Coriander 
Cayenne

Enjoy!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: This was perfect to make on a weeknight and tasted great. Used vegan butter instead of ghee to make it plant-based.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Good, simple. Could take a bit more seasoning.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I made the recipe just as written. It was delicious and just as easy as promised. I used Bob's Red Mill lentils, but not his recipe. First time I mixed in frozen spinach. Second time I made 2.5 times the recipe for an International pot luck at work and it was a hit.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I've made this recipe twice now and found it as easy and delicious as promised. I used Bob's Red Mill lentils, but not his recipe.  I didn't rinse the lentils and had no problems. I cook on an electric glass stop stove, 13+ years old, so nothing fancy. The first time I mixed in frozen spinach, the second time I made 2.5 times the recipe for an International pot luck at work and it was a hit!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Like others have said, I did rinse the lentils several times, and I also added several cloves of diced garlic and a thinly sliced onion to the ghee mixture, because there isn't much that doesn't taste better with garlic and onion.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: add losts of salt!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Added one onion, 3 garlic cloves, a teaspoon of fresh ginger and used chicken stock instead of water. Exceptionally good!!!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: This was absolutely delicious.  Didn't have ghee so used butter.  I almost never use butter but this paid off in spades.  I wish I had the Asafetida but I goosed up the tumeric, added a 1/2 tsp of ginger and garlic and also a splash of chili oil.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: This is excellent, easy and very delicious. However… do wash your red lentils and double the amount of water and take your time cooking it. IMHO, the original recipe creates a Dahl that is too dry and allowing the Dahl to simmer slowly over 30-45 minutes creates a smoother, creamier texture. We served this Dahl with basmati rice, steamed Brocolini and pan-cooked roti. It’s a winner!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Try fresh curry leaves in the tadka.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
To Shoba,
Actually, it depends on which dal you're referencing, as it is a generic term: dals are actually any split pulses (legumes), including lentils, beans and peas, the cooking times of which will vary considerably.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: You can add fresh curry leaves before the chili & asafetida.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Used coconut oil instead of ghee which added a nice flavor. Added ginger as recommended by many others and subbed garlic powder per Priya’s suggestion. Very quick, ready in 15-20 min, served with paratha and yogurt/cilantro. Would add some frozen spinach next time for veg. Great quick weeknight meal.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I love this recipe. Even though I have an electric pressure cooker I still cook it on my stove top. Yes you could add ginger and garlic and I think I'll try that sometime, but for simplicity, ease and great taste you can't beat the original. Even more non-purist I add a green vegetable after it's cooked! My faves are cooked, chopped spinach or swiss chard. I do make it a little soupy sometimes which doesn't alter the flavor. I've made this loads of times and never get tired of it. Thanks!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Excellent and easy recipe for dal - I added chopped frozen spinach.  I did not have asafetida, but I did everything else as the recipe is written.  This will be on repeat!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: As easy and foolproof as everyone says. I didn't have cumin seeds or asafoetida, but tempered cardamom, mustard seeds, garlic powder, chile powder, and cumin powder. I will definitely seek out asafoetida in the future though.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I pick and rinse.  Always.  Agree it takes more than 5-8 minutes to get to porridge texture.  That said, DELICIOUS.  We added a plop of plain yogurt.  I'll be adding greens next time.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: This is a wonderful recipe.  Note that i did rinse the lentils.  Also added minced garlic and ginger when the cumin seeds were toasted.  I happen to have asafetida and used that as well.  My husband lived in India for a few years and pronounced the dal genuine.  We ate it with some yogurt and roti.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I too use Bob's Redmill red lentils - since we don't have a variety of grocery items I eventually ordered the hing online - used it as directed and honestly did not notice a difference. I guess some things are better than others (laugh). I have thoroughly e joyed this simple recipe for myself mostly - my husband doesn't know what he's missing steering away from Indian cuisine  - but; more for me. I did not find this a difficult recipe in the least; so I'm a little perplexed that some couldn't just figure it out - choose a pot to cook in - use the stove they have in front of them and enjoy a nice hot soup. it was snowing here (yes! still) when I finally decided to cook this - no rice - just delicious warming soup on (another) wintery day.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I found this to be incredibly dull. followed recipe.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I made this recipe- had all of the ingredients (including the asafetida) except ghee- I used olive oil.  It definitely would have been better with ghee.  If you have true, split red lentils- the time to cook is perfect.  I guess I didn't realize how soupy dal is- I used the lesser amount of water and it still seemed somewhat soupy to me.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: @Very Good made this again with ghee- so good.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: The dal must be rinsed at least ten times or until the water is clear. Soak them in the clear water for at least 30 minutes. When cooking the dal, I add cumin seeds, celery seeds, fenugreek seeds, bay leaf, cinammon stick, salt, turmeric, garam masala and black pepper. The dal should be cooked between low to medium heat. Add lemon juice to dal afterward. Serve with white basmati rice on the side or cook the rice with the dal for a delicious kichidi (50/50 mixture). Make sure the rice is rinsed at least ten times, too, before cooking separately or with the dal to make kichidi.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I live alone and I’m always on the lookout for a quick healthy weeknight meal. This REALLY hit the spot. It was even better the next day foe lunch. My colleagues were impressed. Need to find Hing for my next attempt.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: This was so good as is- I used garlic powder as suggested. Could not believe the flavor with such simple ingredients.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I will definitely make this dish! One question: instead of asafetida or garlic powder, why (even in general in NYT Cooking) nobody ever mentions BLACK GARLIC? Tastes fantastic. As I am not a big fan of cumin, I'll try cardamome (in form of a star). Thanks for the recipe!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I loooove the depth of flavor that asafetida adds… however it can get pretty pungent in the pantry… I learned to keep it in a sealed mason jar.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I don’t know where I went wrong, but this was the blandest thing I’ve ever eaten. Even with the asafetida.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
Maybe our red lentils were old - but they took far longer to get to a porridge consistency than just 10 minutes! Just be aware and don’t assume it will be as quick as the recipe says. 
Also, 3 cups of water to 1 cup of lentils produced an extremely watery porridge - not even thick enough to be considered a soup, let alone a daal! The flavor was good, but next time we make this I’m going to try starting with just 2 cups of water and add more as needed.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Pick over lentils.  Look for rocks or debris and remove.  Rinse!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Do you have any turmeric alternatives as turmeric conflicts with a medication that I take?

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: It is perfectly acceptable no to add Turmeric, as it will not make much difference to the flavour.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I used split yellow moong dal in place of the red lentils. Increased cooking time to about 40 minutes. Delicious!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Is this cook time of 10 minutes at all realistic? Most recipes for masoor dal have you boiling it for 20-30 minutes.

author: Priya Krishna

author: Priya Krishna

commentBody:
Is it ok to use  regular butter or oil
In lieu of ghee.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I would use oil to temper spices because it needs to be med high heat and butter will burn. Ghee has a higher smoke point.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I'd like to try this using ingredients I have on hand, since I always have red lentils in the pantry. I can't be bothered with ghee. What would be a good substitute? Butter? Oil? How much powdered cumin would replace the 2 teaspoons cumin seeds?

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Use oil, butter will burn. Start with 1 teaspoon ground cumin. Be careful, the ground spice will burn faster. Use a lower temp and work quickly.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: What type of red Chile powder should I use?

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Kashmir Chili Powder is the mildest. Perhaps use half the amount required whilst cooking, and taste when its ready, if you feel you want a little more heat, add a few pinches. Good luck.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: For vegans, thoughts on coconut oil? Or plant based butter?

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Can you use yellow dal or small brown? Any cooking adjustments required?

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Hi yes use either Yellow Moong Dal or Toor Dal. No soaking required but do clean. All the best.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I love this dish but I include onions and garlic and use an immersion blender to smooth half of it out. Love the asafetida tip!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I cannot stand the taste of red lentils. Can I use other lentils?

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: You can use chana dal or yellow split peas (yellow lentils).

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Try Moong Dal it is a mild dal but if you want more flavourful Dal then use Chana Dal. It needs to be soaked & thoroughly cleaned before cooking. Good luck.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I use moong dal with this recipe and works nicely. Always rinse.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I tried to follow the instructions closely, but the fault was probably mine. Nevertheless, pizza was ordered.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Really don’t rinse that is a cardinal sin!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: How would you make this vegan, anybody know?

author: Priya Krishna

Also, are you omitting onions, garlic, and curry leaves from the recipe? There are many better recipes available online compared to this one.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Fantastic recipe. So fast and easy and with just a handful of ingredients. Will definitely be adding to my rotation.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
Made for one, with cooking time "just until red lentil will be soft" (pretty quick for one person)
asafetida is available (imported from India)here in Japan, though slightly pricey, so I omitted, also didn't add substitution to suit for the breakfast.
I didn't wash red lentils, I always don't. It tastes great, doesn't shoot up blood sugar. 

I will try asafetida when I am going to make for dinner!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I got sick from not soaking or rinsing the lentils and due to the brief cooking time. Following directions! Delicious but I paid the price.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Oh dear, to cook good Dal, not only must you wash it & cook it well but add the Salt after cooking. The texture of Dal will not be good if Salt is added during the boiling stage, it makes it difficult to digest. Adding ginger during boiling stage, be it either minced or sliced, will make it easier to digest. Do please read my earlier note.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Very easy and good.  Not my favorite however to add the cumin seed and I love cumin.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I followed this recipe exactly as printed ......my husband hated it, and with 3T of ghee, that's pretty much what the flavor of this dal was, pretty horrible, had to throw it away. I'm returning to my usual dal recipe next time, the one with all the vegetables, curry, ginger, tomato paste, coconut milk, etc. in it, and cooking time is much longer.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Asafoetida may contain lead, does anyone have a recommendation on where to source lead free Asafoetida?

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I’ll pass on making this again. Very bland except for the overwhelming cumin, which we found unpleasant between our teeth also. In fact, I used only a bit more than half of the cumin. I think I’ll try the Bob’s Red Mill version next time as recommended by a couple of people here.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Note: most prepackaged Asafoetida usually contains gluten. Those with Celiacs disease or gluten intolerance take note.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:

Garlic
Coconut milk powder

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Unfortunately I couldn’t get asofoetida (the Asian grocer here didn’t have it in stock so subbed it for garlic flakes). I doubled quantities and added another cup of water and let the lentils slow cook over one hour so it was really smooth.  It was delicious when paired with brown rice and steamed Broccolini. author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: This could not have been simpler and was very tasty. Great weeknight dinner.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I used asafetida for the first time in the recipe. Delicious! It's wonderful that such a simple recipe can be so good. It took 38 minutes from start to finish.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: This was quick and delicious! I was able to find the asafetida powder at Wegmans. Did not add red chili as my hubby does not like too much heat. To me it tastes restaurant worthy!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Where do you find asafetida?

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: @Ray Schnee just Google it, as it really depends on where you live! A lot of Indian spices are available online through mail order or Amazon if you’re in a more remote area and can’t find a suitable shop.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Easy, quick, delicious!! Loved it.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Having made many dals that are more labor intensive and take longer, the short prep and cook time here is appealing and works. A good dish ready in minutes is a win. Rinsed lentils and cooked 10mins in Instant Pot.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Has anyone made this with regular lentils to know whether they're a good substitute for red lentils?

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: @Roberta if by “regular” lentils you mean brown lentils, no, they are different entities and this recipe as it’s written wouldn’t work. Red lentils have been hulled so are softer and smaller and cook very fast, brown lentils being whole lentils with a tougher outer skin will not be edible in 5-8 minutes. There are plenty of great recipes out there for brown lentil dals however and they’re still delicious so worth playing around with.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Is it worth my buying prepared ghee in a jar for this, or could I just substitute melted butter, or some other butter preparation that I can make at home?

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I doubled the recipe, eliminated the asafetida (couldn't find it) and added about 1/2 a teaspoon of minced garlic and 1/2 a teaspoon of minced ginger to the ghee mixture. Was also generous with salt after mixing everything together and sprinkled about 1/2 a teaspoon of garlic powder over the top at the end.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I did rinse the lentils out of habit. I then cooked them in a 1970s 3-quart All-Clad sauce pan without a lid in the allotted time. They were quite soft. I broke the cumin (a little) in a mortar so there was fresh flavor and some texture but not whole seeds. Fast, easy, and very tasty.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: This is delicious.  In my rotation!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Exactly as Priya described: easy and so comforting. I made Sohla’s butter rice alongside.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I love this recipe! I make it pretty much exactly as written (although I tend to eyeball amounts for most of the spices), and it turns out perfectly. Great with some flatbread or just eaten like soup. Going to try adding some garlic to the tempered spices this time!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: OMG! This was so simple to make and so very delicious and nourishing!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: This dal is amazing and dead simple. If you can get your hands on some cultured ghee it is heavenly.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: This is great.  Made it as described, delicious.  Now experimenting with adding mustard seed and curry leaves.  Results pending!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I really disagree That lentils should not be washed. I bought a good organic brand and like a good cook I rinsed them and there was plenty of dust that rose to the surface.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Instead of the ground red pepper, saute 1-2 seeded dried hot red peppers along with the cumin seeds.  Pick them out before serving (or leave them in and eat them if you are bolder).

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I am vegan, and avoid added oil; has anyone made this without ghee or oil? Simply dry sauteeing, or adding a bit of water or broth?

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: You really can't unless you want something flavorless. The flavoring comes from the tempering at the end and the flavor comes from blooming the whole spices in oil. Use coconut oil

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
Good basic recipe, though I found it too salty. Next time I will start with half as much salt and add up at the end. I suspect my sweet spot will be somewhere around 3/4 t salt.

Other than that, I appreciate how quick and easy this dal is to make. It will be adaptable if I want to make it more interesting. So while the results were nothing special, as an "everyday" recipe it's fine.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: this is the very basic , no drama , very good dal. or, with more water or stock, lentil soup. but i did rinse my lentils. just a simple rinse in a fine colandar. in my experience, Bob’s Red , any (!) Bob’s product is superior. i pick around to be safe. this is NOT an ad. i do know that some sources do not provide the quality on any product Americans are used to, including rice. i make various lentil-based soup, usually following Ina , she uses soaked green lentils. but this is the basic recipe.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
Well, would use just 1/4 teaspoon tumeric for 1.5 cups lentils!
Cook lotsa dals.  Can't lose with Julie Sahni's
