author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I grew up with a similar version, this one is faster with tons of flavor! Extremely satisfying!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I was skeptical of this recipe at first because it has no onion/garlic/ginger and seems impossible simple. We tried it anyway and I am so glad we did. It tastes very different than the dals we typically make/eat but is delicious in its own way. Oh... and it comes together in no time. Perfect for a busy night.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: We love this recipe, especially for our 10 month old baby. He loves it as a soup and it’s made so quickly! Only a tiny bit of salt, no chile & asafetida, but yes to the rest of the ingredients.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I cooked it and it was delicious. Very surprising to me. Can you make dal with green lentis?

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: This is so delicious I make it at least once a week. Same tarka. Same everything. I see comments but am confused - I use dal (red lentils, split) they usually cook in 5 mn. It’s magic and delicious. The asafetida is really the next level part of dish. Might be my most consistent 5 star on NYT cooking!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Can I sub out the ghee for something vegetarian?

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: You can use coconut oil

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
Made this just now following all the instructions with a minor substitution, using butter instead of ghee. It took longer, at least 15 minutes, for the dal to breakdown to the consistency I expect of daal.

It is really yummy for how simple this recipe is. The Asafoetida really makes the dish. This recipe as follows was enough 4 as only a side dish. Served it up with leftover last nights homemade naan. May need to double it the recipe next time.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: The ratio of taste to effort is extremely high. it's unbelievable how easy this is to make, yet how tasty. It has become a regular weeknight meal in our house.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: We cooked this exactly as written and it was delicious! My husband is Pakistani and he gave it two thumbs up, as did I. I had it on rice and he had it with roti. The chhonk makes all the difference and adds a whole new layer of flavor. We had it as an entree, so between the two of us, the recipe of 4 servings was perfect. I’ll make twice as much next time so I can take it to work for lunch.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
I made these as an add-on for a larger Indian dinner. I grew up in India and came to love very ordinary every day doll.
I used locally procured small red lentils, rinse them a few times, and they did indeed cook up in 8 to 15 minutes. The texture is excellent.
I also took the suggestion of using Kalonji because I had a lot of of it. I also added just a little bit of cumin and I did include the asafoetida. 
Overall result is delicious

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Love this simple Dahl. I’ve been making it all winter. It’s a great base to add whatever else u might want. I love the flavor busts of the toasted cumin seeds.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: This has been on regular rotation in our house for years. If you can get black cumin, try it with that and the hinge. Sometimes I add chopped garlic and ginger to the finishing oil, which is always a hit. The instant pot is so easy here- I find they take way too long on the stove (I live at 7000 ft). 5 minutes in the instant pot, wait at least 10 minutes to release and the lentils are done.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I had to make some tweaks to make it edible for my family. It didn't work out for us.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
I used half the salt called for in the recipe, and it was fine. I’ve cooked this recipe several times and have even passed it on to my son and daughter-in-law, who have loved it. 
I just used ghee instead of canola oil and found that it definitely adds a nice buttery, nutty taste, although my wife found it a bit rich. I’ll probably cut back to 2  Tbsp on the ghee next time. 3 Tbsp is a lot and is probably not needed to brown the cumin seeds. It seems like a lot of oil going in at the end. And there’s also the cholesterol factor to consider.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I cooked this according to the recipe and it was absolutely delicious.  And so easy.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
I cooked it by the book last night for twice the time the recipe suggested and my chana dal was still not cooked through.

So I tried it again tonight in the instant pot (just throwing all the ingredients in after using saute mode for the cumin seeds) and it’s the same thing — 25 minutes on high pressure and the chana dal is still not cooked.

Am I doing something wrong or do I just have an unusually dry batch of chana dal? Should i be soaking my chana dal?

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I spent more time trying to improve the recipe than making the recipe. Not special.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: In the past Goan Catholics did not eat dal as regularly as their Hindu brethren. We are big fish eaters and we also eat meat thanks to 500 years of Portuguese colonization. On Good Friday we would thus enhance our dal while meeting our Catholic requirements by poaching an egg in the dal :) I do it even now although my food habits have changed and i eat dal a lot.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: What is meant by "ground red chile"? Is this the same as chili powder? Or is it something else?

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Crispy Green Bean and Potato Sabzi Recipe
commentBody: I usually hit spuds for 3-4 minutes in the microwave before frying. It doesn’t really soften them, but gets them hot so they have a head start before going into the skillet.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Crispy Green Bean and Potato Sabzi Recipe
commentBody: Same here - always.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Crispy Green Bean and Potato Sabzi Recipe
commentBody: I always parboil potatoes before pan-cooking them so they’re not underdone. Just make sure you don’t boil the cut potatoes too long, they’ll absorb the water and then less absorb the lovely flavors in your sabzi. I cut them to the size I plan to serve them in & boil the water before adding them. For smaller cubes 5-7 minutes should be more than enough.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Crispy Green Bean and Potato Sabzi Recipe
commentBody: 1 - 2 minutes in the instant pot, and you don't have to worry about the taters absorbing water.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Crispy Green Bean and Potato Sabzi Recipe
commentBody: @AmyQ Agreed on par-cooking potatoes. Easier yet, I use Kenji’s version - microwave cut potatoes on a dinner plate covered in a couple layers of damp paper towel or clean dish cloth for 3-5 minutes, depending on the size of the chop.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Crispy Green Bean and Potato Sabzi Recipe
commentBody: Lovely dish, even though I slightly undercooked the potatoes. Next time, I’d experiment with a different variety of potato. The nut butter brings it all together. Worked well with roti.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Crispy Green Bean and Potato Sabzi Recipe
commentBody: I realize that the chaat masala is optional, and as I really don't have room for any more spices, is there some acceptable substitute? How would Garam Masala be?

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Crispy Green Bean and Potato Sabzi Recipe
commentBody: Chaat masala is quite different than garam masala -- it adds a funky sour and salty note due to the green mango powder (amchur) and black salt. Someone else suggested tajin, which has similar notes. Otherwise, maybe pomegranate seeds, sumac, or just a little more lemon juice.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Crispy Green Bean and Potato Sabzi Recipe
commentBody: Sunflower seed butter is a good substitute for almond butter for those who can’t eat nuts.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Crispy Green Bean and Potato Sabzi Recipe
commentBody: In response to the person wanting to substitute something for the chaat masala—I would not sub graham masala alone. Instead a squeeze of lime. Or so tajin (chili-lime seasoning) Chaat masala has a mix of bright and sweet and savory. It really is delightful and worth trying someday. My favorite part is the dried mango powder—which should give you an idea of the flavor profile.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Crispy Green Bean and Potato Sabzi Recipe
commentBody: Love the sound of this recipe and the comments, but all my non-stick cookware says NOT to use high heat.  How about a seasoned cast iron pan?

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Crispy Green Bean and Potato Sabzi Recipe
commentBody: This recipe was so tasty! I didn’t have almond butter so I subbed in tahini. It worked perfectly!

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Crispy Green Bean and Potato Sabzi Recipe
commentBody: Hmmm.  I loved the flavor but the almond butter did not make a "tempura-like" coating on the beans.  I suspect the timing of the steps of this dish is very critical -- and I probably got it wrong.  My beans and potatoes both were overcooked and mushy.  In the future I might cook the potatoes and beans separately, then finish them together with the almond butter.  It seems to me that trying to get the potatoes and beans cooked correctly when cooked together is almost impossible.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Crispy Green Bean and Potato Sabzi Recipe
commentBody: Although I worried at every turn—was my pan nonstick enough? was I going to get the potatoes browned? was the heat too high? was my spice mixture too strong?—the dish turned out beautifully (even though I substituted peanut butter!). The potatoes were perfectly cooked, as were the green beans, and the spices were heavenly. (Don’t forget the lime.)

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Crispy Green Bean and Potato Sabzi Recipe
commentBody: I'm gonna try w/garbanzos instead of potatoes...

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Crispy Green Bean and Potato Sabzi Recipe
commentBody: Wow...I'll never go back to how I made green beans before I tried this recipe! What an amazing flavor! And super easy too!

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Crispy Green Bean and Potato Sabzi Recipe
commentBody:
I used two potatoes. Served with brown  basmati rice. Excellent! 
I couldn’t find chaat masala, so I used garam masala plus lime juice. (I read that garam masala is a base for chaat masala plus a sour or citrus.)

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Crispy Green Bean and Potato Sabzi Recipe
commentBody: I did not want potatoes, so doubled the green beans and otherwise made as written (minus the water phase). Fast, delicious.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Crispy Green Bean and Potato Sabzi Recipe
commentBody: For people who don't know, chaat masala has dried mango powder in it. I am super allergic (life-threatening) to mangos and have found many others are allergic as well. Make sure your eaters are OK with mango.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Crispy Green Bean and Potato Sabzi Recipe
commentBody: Made with just a few tweaks (due to family taste preferences).  Left out coriander and cut cumin in half.  Absolutely delicious and very easy to make.  A keeper for sure!

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Crispy Green Bean and Potato Sabzi Recipe
commentBody: Instead of adding water with the beans, mix it with the almond butter before you add it to the pan, that way it will distribute better, otherwise it tends to clump.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Crispy Green Bean and Potato Sabzi Recipe
commentBody: This was very delicious. I sauteed some onion first, removed it then started with potatoes which I microwaved first for 3 minutes. Followed recipe, taking longer to steam green beans to al dente. Added veggie broth instead of water and needed more to get to right texture. Used spices and tijan. Added sauteed onions when added mixed nut butter. Wonderful flavor when finished.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Crispy Green Bean and Potato Sabzi Recipe
commentBody: This is a great side dish for everyone.  One suggestion I would make is to take the potatoes out once they are crisp.  I found leaving them in while steaming the green beans immediately makes them a bit soggy.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Crispy Green Bean and Potato Sabzi Recipe
commentBody: add paneer for protein

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Crispy Green Bean and Potato Sabzi Recipe
commentBody:
I made this last night, using Japanese sweet potatoes in lieu of the russet, because that's what I had. I also parboiled them first. In hindsight, I don't think that was really necessary, because of the textural differences between the two types of potato.

No chaat masala--won't buy an expensive ingredient for a possible one-time optional use. But I do have tajin, so I used that, after reading about the flavor profile for chaat masala. I think it worked just fine. Big hit. Will make again.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Crispy Green Bean and Potato Sabzi Recipe
commentBody:
@Susan
You could pick up a box of Shan  Chaat Masala for about $2.99/3.5 oz. at any Indian Market( even in NYC at Kalustyan’s)

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Crispy Green Bean and Potato Sabzi Recipe
commentBody: Delicious. Used what I had, which was yukon gold potatoes, peanut butter, tajin. Came out great

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Crispy Green Bean and Potato Sabzi Recipe
commentBody: A cast iron skillet worked really well. The potatoes browned beautifully, the almond butter does form a nice crust, and you can get aggressive with a metal spatula.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Crispy Green Bean and Potato Sabzi Recipe
commentBody: Very nice! I found this while trying to find a way to use up potatoes and green beans. I made recipe exactly as described. I cut the potatoes pretty tiny (probably more like 1/4- inch pieces) so did not have an issue with cook time compared to the beans. Served alongside bhindi masala, roasted squash, roti and raita for an Indian-themed night. Delicious.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Crispy Green Bean and Potato Sabzi Recipe
commentBody: Made with sweet potatoes—delicious and easy to cook.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Crispy Green Bean and Potato Sabzi Recipe
commentBody: Delicious, easy, and filling.  Followed the recipe.  Used farmer’s market produce.  I was pretty generous with the chaat masala.  I thought the chaat masala really made the dish.  Served alongside brown rice, which I was too full to finish.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Crispy Green Bean and Potato Sabzi Recipe
commentBody: This recipe is ridiculous. Firstly, the potatoes need to be fully cooked after steaming them or else they’ll taste underdone by the end. Secondly, you throw in all your spices and the raw ginger and garlic about a minute or two from the end without any water or oil to bloom them in. To no one’s surprise, the spices tastes raw and untempered in every aspect alongside your rock hard potatoes. Priya is a perplexing recipe writer because half her stuff is great and the other half is awful.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Crispy Green Bean and Potato Sabzi Recipe
commentBody: I love this recipe so much! I usually have it for weeknight dinners but I made it camping recently and it was amazing after being in the woods all day. The chunky chat masala is perfect on top. Thanks Priya! :)

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Crispy Green Bean and Potato Sabzi Recipe
commentBody: I cook Indian cuisine often and this recipe looked like a keeper. I had all ingredients available with the exception of chat masala. I used lime juice as suggested in the comments. We enjoyed it very much and I will make again.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Crispy Green Bean and Potato Sabzi Recipe
commentBody: Very good. I subbed sunflower butter.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Crispy Green Bean and Potato Sabzi Recipe
commentBody:
Just made this dish, and it was absolutely delicious!
I didn’t have chaat, used tajin (as suggested by others that reviewed this recipe). Looking forward to making it in the near future! Thank you Priya for sharing your mother’s recipe!

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Crispy Green Bean and Potato Sabzi Recipe
commentBody: I wonder if adding paneer to this recipe would make this recipe a bit more filling.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Crispy Green Bean and Potato Sabzi Recipe
commentBody: What can I substitute for the almond butter ? ( family members have nut allergies)

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Crispy Green Bean and Potato Sabzi Recipe
commentBody: Yeah, is the potato supposed to be undercooked? It takes forever to pan fry potatoes. At '1/2 inch pieces' sized,  it's definitely more than ~10-15 mins.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Crispy Green Bean and Potato Sabzi Recipe
commentBody: Who has almond butter in their pantry? What is a substitute?

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Crispy Green Bean and Potato Sabzi Recipe
commentBody: I do and I'm sure I'm not alone.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Crispy Green Bean and Potato Sabzi Recipe
commentBody: To be fair, I far prefer almond butter and always have it in my cabinet! But I'm sure peanut butter would work just fine.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Crispy Green Bean and Potato Sabzi Recipe
commentBody: Could you use peanut butter? I imagine you could.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Crispy Green Bean and Potato Sabzi Recipe
commentBody: Why not?

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Crispy Green Bean and Potato Sabzi Recipe
commentBody: Can almond butter be substituted with peanut butter?

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Crispy Green Bean and Potato Sabzi Recipe
commentBody: No potatoes in the pantry and snow falling outside but I must try now. What to do? A bag of frozen 'country style potatoes' worked just great and reduced the prep time. I made sure to cook them as I would for hash browns, getting them nice and crispy before adding green beans. Delish.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Crispy Green Bean and Potato Sabzi Recipe
commentBody: Did not use a non-stick pan...potatoes stuck a bit, but added a little more water to soften and scrape up everything on the bottom of the pan.  Did not have chaat masala, but thoroughly enjoyed this.  Oh, and I used yukon gold bc that was all I had. Will make this (and maybe a variation) again!

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Crispy Green Bean and Potato Sabzi Recipe
commentBody: Just for fun, has anyone tried tahini instead of almond butter?

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Crispy Green Bean and Potato Sabzi Recipe
commentBody:
this was DELICIOUS. I used yukon gold instead of russet potatoes and it was still wonderful.
Chat Masala really makes the dish--if not using would add more salt (it's salty and tangy).

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Crispy Green Bean and Potato Sabzi Recipe
commentBody: I normally like a lot of spice, but this was way too much for me and the spice profile didn't come together somehow - perhaps it was too much coriander. The green beans were way past the tender-crisp stage at 3 minutes. The almond butter did not coat the vegetables but stuck to the bottom of the skillet.  (I did use seasoned cast iron since I've given up non-stick cookware for health reasons). I ate it unenthusiastically. All in all, not a rousing success.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Crispy Green Bean and Potato Sabzi Recipe
commentBody: We loved this.  This recipe is quick enough and hardy enough for a stand-alone meal without Roti or rice.  Although, I will admit, I had just baked, and pulled out of the oven, some fresh homemade sourdough baguettes....

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Crispy Green Bean and Potato Sabzi Recipe
commentBody: I substituted Costco peanut butter for the almond butter.  A wonderful flavor.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Crispy Green Bean and Potato Sabzi Recipe
commentBody: Delicious!   Made with leftover kale slaw instead of beans.   Everything else the same.    Will be a go-to for me from now on.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Crispy Green Bean and Potato Sabzi Recipe
commentBody: This is a delicious pantry-staples dish, easy to scale up or cut in half, and so simple to make. I’ve now tried it twice, once with sweet potato and once with a russet. Equally good. I’ve also had it with rice and with roti, and find that I prefer rice.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Crispy Green Bean and Potato Sabzi Recipe
commentBody: I had to substitute peanut butter- it was all I had. And I didn't have any chaat masala. (Nor do I know what it is.) But we found this to be an outstanding recipe all the same. I don't make many things twice, because, well, so many recipes, so little time. But this is one to make regularly. Delicious and interesting.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Crispy Green Bean and Potato Sabzi Recipe
commentBody: Made exactly as written (except without the chaat masala) in my good old cast iron and it was amazingly good. I doubled it and it was a terrific vegetarian entree.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Crispy Green Bean and Potato Sabzi Recipe
commentBody: I made this recipe for the first time.  The challenge, IMO, is adding the green beans.  If the beans are tough then cooking until tender will likely have the potatoes over cooked.  Green beans that are tender will cook quickly potentially resulting in under cooked potatoes.  My potatoes ended up somewhat over cooked.  Overall, the dish came out pretty good.  But, next time I will likely cook the potatoes to nearly done and steam the green beans to to nearly done then combine.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Crispy Green Bean and Potato Sabzi Recipe
commentBody:
This might just be my absolute favorite recipe from the NYT cooking website.  Yum, simply excellent, easy and fast.
I made it as written and had some Chat Masala in the cupboard to use at the end.  Served it over “forbidden rice”, perfect.
As a mostly vegetarian, I will likely make this again and again.  Always delighted to find a recipe with ingredients that are generally on hand.  I’ll look forward to trying it with other vegetables as well.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Crispy Green Bean and Potato Sabzi Recipe
commentBody: Delicious! I had some tired green beans that I wanted to use up. This really dressed them up! I didn't have russets so I used Yukon gold. They worked out OK, but I think the texture of russets is probably better.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Crispy Green Bean and Potato Sabzi Recipe
commentBody: So much better than slimy green beans swimming in canned soup.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Crispy Green Bean and Potato Sabzi Recipe
commentBody: Very tasty.  I just tested potatoes for semi-doneness before adding quartered green beans.  I used less garlic, lemon instead of lime, and the spices, etc above, including a "curry powder" mix.  No almond butter; I halved some salted macarena almonds, added with the green beans, and added some (unsalted) butter near the end.   No water, just fresh lemon juice, cooked uncovered.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Crispy Green Bean and Potato Sabzi Recipe
commentBody: I made this dish today.  Being Indian, I regularly make a version of this dish but without the almost butter.  So I was intrigued and pleasantly surprised. The beans weren’t “tempura like” but the dish had a richness that made it very appealing. Also, inspired by this recipe, last week I had roasted green beans in the air flyer and once they were crisp tender, sautéed them with some peanut butter.  Again, the results were very good.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Crispy Green Bean and Potato Sabzi Recipe
commentBody: Substitute tofu for potatoes

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Crispy Green Bean and Potato Sabzi Recipe
commentBody: Absolutely delicious. Parboiled the potatoes first, and so didn’t add the water with the beans. Otherwise followed the recipe exactly and it was fabulous! Didn’t need the rice or any extra carb. Perfect one dish meal!

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Crispy Green Bean and Potato Sabzi Recipe
commentBody: Used the sweet potatoes I had and threw in some spinach that was almost gone for. Just cut the pieces of potato smaller and fried for longer. Apparently we love potatoes and almond butter!

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Crispy Green Bean and Potato Sabzi Recipe
commentBody: Tried this with wing beans and squash,still tasted delicious too. This is a keeper.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Crispy Green Bean and Potato Sabzi Recipe
commentBody: Great dish and recipe, loved the addition of almond butter. Potatoes took a little longer than expected but imagine that's down to the variety we used. Next time, I'll steam the potatoes on their own before the addition of the green beans.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Crispy Green Bean and Potato Sabzi Recipe
commentBody: It's a shame that the crispiness of the potatoes disappears when the water gets poured into the pan. I think next time I try this, I might parboil the beans, as well as the potatoes. Works nicely cashew butter, by the way.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Crispy Green Bean and Potato Sabzi Recipe
commentBody: Great recipe! Quick, easy and very tasty. My gf (meat eater) was impressed with the flavor and ate a lot. I followed another commenter and used garam masala and tajin as a replacement for chaat masala. Worked very well!

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Crispy Green Bean and Potato Sabzi Recipe
commentBody: Had low expectations but I loved it! I followed the suggestion to microwave the potatoes before pan cooking, used sun butter instead of almond butter. Wow, it was good. Not too complicated but very good.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Crispy Green Bean and Potato Sabzi Recipe
commentBody: Delicious recipe that is going on my "don't order delivery, just cook" list. Great video, leftover breakfast taco pro-tip is A+. Thank you Priya!

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Crispy Green Bean and Potato Sabzi Recipe
commentBody: I made this last night.   Incredibly tasty, but textually a little bland.  Maybe the addition of sliced almonds will help next time.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Crispy Green Bean and Potato Sabzi Recipe
commentBody: After reading you-all's notes, I cubed the potato and put all the cubes, covered in water, in the microwave for 3 min. That got them soft on the surface and then I drained them fairly dry. Followed the rest of the recipe and it was quite good. I also looked online for Chaat Masala recipes and made some minus a few spices I didn't have. Still tasty though.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Crispy Green Bean and Potato Sabzi Recipe
commentBody: I keep coming back to this recipe and find it unexpectedly delicious and satisfying - and easy to make.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Crispy Green Bean and Potato Sabzi Recipe

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Crispy Green Bean and Potato Sabzi Recipe
commentBody: Easy and delicious. Will make again. Did potatoes in airfryer and added at the end to coat in seasoning and they were cooked through and stayed crunchy.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Crispy Green Bean and Potato Sabzi Recipe
commentBody: What about sunflower seed butter? No nuts. Tastes yummy, too.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Crispy Green Bean and Potato Sabzi Recipe
commentBody: I would not parboil russets. If you must, boil them whole with their skins intact. But I wouldn't

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Crispy Green Bean and Potato Sabzi Recipe
commentBody: You can steam potatoes in advance. I steam 3 Yukon potatoes for consumption over 3 days. Use cast iron - you don't need to put it over high heat - it get there after 5 minutes or so. Make sure that whatever pan you use, preheat ot first, add oil second, etc. The flavorful crust on the vegetables is delicious!

recipeTitle: Crispy Green Bean and Potato Sabzi Recipe
commentBody: I tried sweet potatoes and they turned into a mush and my green beans didn’t coat. But it tasted good! Might try it with chicken.

author: Priya Krishna

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Crispy Green Bean and Potato Sabzi Recipe
commentBody: Did anyone successfully use sweet potato? One person said it was a mush, but can I assume that was a cooking issue not an ingredients issue?

recipeTitle: Crispy Green Bean and Potato Sabzi Recipe
commentBody: We cooked it in a cast iron. The potatoes stuck a little and broke apart a bit as a result. However this was a delicious dish. We also subbed peanut butter instead of almond butter bc it is was we had in the house.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Crispy Green Bean and Potato Sabzi Recipe
commentBody:
I made this the other night, had read the notes and thinly sliced my fingerlings. This is one of the better recipes I’ve ever made for green beans, it’s absolutely delicious!
Healthy fats and spices as well- I loved the lime flavor as well!

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Crispy Green Bean and Potato Sabzi Recipe
commentBody: Yum, I substituted some tempeh steamed with a little bit of tamari for the potatoes, but followed the recipe for the rest. Very tasty. Nice blend of spices!

author: Priya Krishna

recipeTitle: Crispy Green Bean and Potato Sabzi Recipe
commentBody: This was great. I made it exactly as directed.

recipeTitle: Crispy Green Bean and Potato Sabzi Recipe
commentBody: I don't have a non-stick pan.  Is using one essential?

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Crispy Green Bean and Potato Sabzi Recipe
commentBody: The combination of the nut butter and chaat masala are addictive!

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Crispy Green Bean and Potato Sabzi Recipe
commentBody: The flavor of this dish is good but "tempura-like coating" is false advertising. The texture came out more pasty than crispy. I usually love chaat masala, but on a dish this dry it gives an unpleasant dusty texture. Green beans with pasty sawdust. Meh.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Crispy Green Bean and Potato Sabzi Recipe
commentBody: Donna: nonstick probably isn’t necessary. My seasoned cast iron was just fine.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Crispy Green Bean and Potato Sabzi Recipe
commentBody: I made this this past Sunday to go with a simple salmon dish. The timing and temperatures were perfect. I didn't have either almond butter or masala seasoning, so perhaps that's why the flavors were very mild, almost not there. I will definitely make this again, maybe double the seasoning and find some of the missing seasonings.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Crispy Green Bean and Potato Sabzi Recipe
commentBody: Took the advice to microwave the potato for 3 minutes. That was really helpful. The recipe was easy to make but, man, is the result dry. We puzzled over what we could add to lighten things up. Settled on Greek yogurt or avocado. Tried the avocado and that made the dish better. I’d make it again, serves 4, but will try the yogurt next time with a ripe avocado on hand just in case.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Crispy Green Bean and Potato Sabzi Recipe
commentBody: Added garbanzo. Prefer Yukons. Used more almond butter and a dash of cayenne. Garam Masale plus lime works. A bit dry

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Crispy Green Bean and Potato Sabzi Recipe
commentBody:
I cooked the beans for a short time on high heat (inspired by Kenji’s wok technique) to retain texture. Potatoes and beans needed to be cooked separately to keep them crunchy. 
I was skeptical about the almond butter originally but used crunchy salted almond butter from Trader Joe’s, doubled the spices, doubled the ginger, left out garlic and used a healthy pinch of chat masala. Served with cooked quinoa. Surprisingly pleasant meal.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Crispy Green Bean and Potato Sabzi Recipe
commentBody: Can anyone recommend a substitute for almond butter? I'm allergic :(

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Crispy Green Bean and Potato Sabzi Recipe
commentBody: May I use tahini or peanut butter instead of almond butter?

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Crispy Green Bean and Potato Sabzi Recipe
commentBody: I'm at a loss to define "sabzi."  My dictionary doesn't recognize it and I live in the hinterlands without access to Zabar's or other international food resources.  Help!

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Crispy Green Bean and Potato Sabzi Recipe
commentBody: @Glenn it’s the Hindi/Punjabi word for vegetable. But it’s also often used to refer to some sort of cooked vegetable dish (ie: gajar ki sabzi is a cooked carrot dish). The preparation can vary, it’s a bit of a catch-all word with respect to recipes.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Crispy Green Bean and Potato Sabzi Recipe
commentBody: I substituted a sweet potato (after reading that others had done so successfully), as I already had one that needed to be used. Overall it was tasty but not at all crispy. Couldn't achieve the "crust" from the almond butter, which I also found challenging to distribute evenly among the vegetbles.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Crispy Green Bean and Potato Sabzi Recipe
commentBody: This is a great recipe! We cooked it with different butters and we find the best to be cashew butter.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Crispy Green Bean and Potato Sabzi Recipe
commentBody: This was an easy and delicious recipe. I followed it exactly and the result was terrific. My wife loved it too.  Looking forward to the leftovers, which might be even better.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Crispy Green Bean and Potato Sabzi Recipe
commentBody: Liked this *much* better before the almond butter!

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Crispy Green Bean and Potato Sabzi Recipe
commentBody: Surprisingly good! I used cashew butter and that worked really well. I think I added too much water in Step 2 because my potatoes started sticking to the pan. All in all - enjoyed it - ate it with the chicken kofta recipe on here.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Crispy Green Bean and Potato Sabzi Recipe
commentBody: This is so good. But it comes together fast. So plan ahead if cooking two or three dishes

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Crispy Green Bean and Potato Sabzi Recipe
commentBody: Really excellent and very easy to make. I ended up using a half oregano/half cumin mixture as a sub for the coriander. Maybe the dish would've been a tad more flavorful if I'd had coriander on-hand, but it was still delicious even with that substitution! Potatoes turned out perfectly when cooked as directed. I wouldn't recommend serving this as an entree as it's a small dish, but it works perfectly as a side/starter or as part of a tapas spread. Would absolutely make again!

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Crispy Green Bean and Potato Sabzi Recipe
commentBody: Can this be made a day in advance and reheated? Anyone try that?

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Crispy Green Bean and Potato Sabzi Recipe
commentBody: @C Yes, and it’s even better the next day.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Crispy Green Bean and Potato Sabzi Recipe
commentBody: I substituted a sweet potato, and slightly pre-cooked both vegetables, but followed directions otherwise.  The taste was just not to my liking.  My husband thought it would be better with a regular potato, as called for.  I would not cook it again.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Crispy Green Bean and Potato Sabzi Recipe
commentBody:
Fantastic!   I listened to Priya this time and ground my own spices.  I do think it made a big difference (I did not do this on her Coconut Saag recipe - but I can now!). More spice heat than I was expecting which made it even better!
I made this with sweet potatoes and green beans - but I will definitely try this with many other veggies.  I ramped the heat up for the almond butter part and it went well - Also carbon steel for hight heat Yo!

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Crispy Green Bean and Potato Sabzi Recipe
commentBody: Wonderful recipe - increase amounts of spices. Potatoes took a lot longer to cook despite microwaving them initially. Love the adding of the nut butter - it really changes the flavor.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Crispy Green Bean and Potato Sabzi Recipe
commentBody: I could eat this every day. It smells good, it feels healthy, my house smells good after I cook it. My family eat it all

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Crispy Green Bean and Potato Sabzi Recipe
commentBody:
I followed the recipe and thought it was so easy and delicious. 
I added some cappers in the end and thought it added a very nice touch!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: This is the first nyt recipe I've ever commented on, I'm that taken aback by how good this turned out. After reading other's comments, I dry toasted the coriander seeds and cardamom seeds and then ground them in a spice grinder, before cooking them with the onions in the oil. I also added a hint of chili powder and turmeric to the onions. If using store bought paneer, I'd recommend soaking the cut pieces in warm water while cooking the rest of the recipe - makes it much softer!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I plan to make this with mature spinach, maybe even frozen, as I find cooked baby spinach has less flavor - it is great raw in salads etc. but not as lush as the older versions when cooked. Age is sometimes a good thing. ;0)

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
I make this all the time as a riff on Hawaiian squid / chicken lu’au. 

You’re better off using a few boxes of frozen spinach and squeezing all the water out with a cloth or paper towel and giving it a coarse chop. 

If you use fresh spinach you’ll need a boatload and will be waiting forever for all that liquid to reduce.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I used a pound of mustard greens, and the bitterness overwhelmed my palette. And my kids definitely wouldn’t touch it. I plan to make again with spinach, which I think will taste much better!  Another tip: Spring for high-quality Asian coconut milk, Chaokoh brand if you can find it. This recipe deserves better than Trader Joe’s brand.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: @Vivian Word. First time I used it I used a brand imported from Thailand and it was phenomenal. Second time the only brand available was Taste of Thailand and it was just as underwhelming as possible.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: @Vivian my Indian mother in-law taught me to always to use at least two different types greens, instead of only one type of green.  It makes a more richer saag.  We often used broccoli (when no more green leaves were on hand), chard, mustard.  It was always delicious whatever combo we used.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: @Viviansame here! Looking forward to trying with spinach.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
My recommended alterations to the beginning of Step 1:
1) Shell the cardamom pods. Discard pods, just keep the seeds.
2) Dry-roast the coriander and cardamom (hot pan, no oil, heat spices until fragrant). Then grind to a fine powder.
3) Add oil to pan, then add ground spices and onions. Proceed with rest of Step 1 as written.

Otherwise, unless you have a Super Blender you will get gritty, half-ground coriander in Step 3 as the other reviewers bemoan.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: @Anjali save the cardamom pods to use in tea!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I made this with 400g kale and 100g arugula, and a 500g block of firm tofu with great results. I used 50/50 regular coconut milk and light coconut milk. I added about a tsp of garam masala at the end. One thing I would adjust next time is to use ground cumin in the coconut oil in step 4 instead of whole seeds because I found their texture to be a little off-putting in the final dish.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: @Eilsel “Light” coconut milk just has water added to it.  It’s a ripoff.  Just buy regular & add water if you want to lighten it

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: If using green cardamon pods, shouldn't the seeds inside be removed and ground first?

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: The whole family enjoyed this dish. I made it almost exactly per the recipe, but skimped on the fresh pepper, taking out the membrane and seeds of a homegrown red habañero. We ended up adding more red pepper spice at the end, however, to make up for it, so I would suggest following the recipe exactly. A little extra salt as we ate helped bring out the flavor, too. I will definitely make this again. Thank you, Priya, for this lovely dish - one of my first forays into northern Indian cuisine.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Delicious! I crushed the coriander seeds before toasting and used swiss chard and sorrel. The lemony taste in the sorrel was a delicious complement. Because I crushed the seeds an immersion blender was just fine. Also I was out of whole cumin seeds and used ground cumin for the finishing oil and it was still delicious (plus I hate chewing on whole cumin seeds). Will make again and again!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: absolutely delicious made as is. for those who are asking about the coriander and cardamom, you are going to pulverize them when you blend the greens, onions etc. so no need to pre-grind. I lightly crushed with a heavy chef's knife both the seeds and the whole cardamom pods before toasting.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Loved this. Used Kenji's vegan saag tofu prep and a mix of dandelion and kale for the greens, otherwise followed this to a t. Kenji's tofu thing is so worth doing: cut the tofu into squares; mix 1 tbsp miso, juice of half a lemon plus zest, 1 tbsp oil, salt and pepper; toss the tofu in the dressing; roast or air fry until browned. Really made this wonderful.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Tasty variation! I doubled it and used frozen spinach instead of mustard greens.(Two 32 oz baga since each bag was about 12 cups). Might grind coriander seeds next time and use more salt. For cheese I used Mexican panela, which is a decent substitute for paneer. Though it melts a bit more easily it's still pretty dense. And usually easy to find in most grocery stores, plus relatively inexpensive.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Delicious! I made this as written, with the exception of only .75 lbs of mustard greens (why Whole Foods doesn't bundle them in standard weights despite charging per bunch... I digress). I added the coconut milk to the Vitamix when I blended, and that helped to capture and blend everything. Like Priya's Saag Feta, I found this could support a bit more tofu/feta, so next time I may up to 24oz of tofu to stretch the recipe a bit further. All the same, yum! Thanks Priya!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I made this. It tasted like tree bark. There were was too much coriander seed. I would suggest crushing it prior to adding onions and using olny one tablespoon. I used kale and took out the ribs. There wasn't enough liquid to blend the greens mixture. I love NYT recipes, but this one is not for me!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Unless you have a chef grade blender that will pulverize concrete, you will want to grind the coriander before starting the recipe.  Otherwise it makes a meal that has soapy crunchy bits with every bite- unable to avoid them at all.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Made this pretty much as written (used 3 bunches of whole spinach). Nice flavor. Not terribly time consuming to make. I used an immersion blender, but would use regular blender next time - spices had too much of a woody texture for my preference. Or I’d grind the spices finely before beginning.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I’ve made this three times, and the first two times I didn’t have coriander seeds so I used ground— and loved it. This time I made it as written and it’s barely edible. I put it in my food processor and it didn’t touch the seeds, so I’m chomping in whole seeds with every bite and they taste like pine sol. Bummer, but done the other way (with only ground) it’s excellent.

author: Priya Krishna
Priya Krishna
commentBody: @BHB try starting with about 1 1/2 tbsp

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I really wanted this to work because I love the saag feta recipe. I used mustard greens and it did not work: flavors didn’t meld together with the coconut and the texture was weird. I followed the recipe to a T.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: @Ruby ditto. I couldn’t have explained it better.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
This has become one of my staple recipes now.

For convenience, I use frozen spinach. I thaw it in the microwave and press it thorough to drain it. I like to double up on all of the ingredients except the coconut milk to make it more flavorful and fibrous. 

I also find that adding a bit of garam masala gives it an extra level of je ne sais quoi.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
Grind the coriander for a less chewy result.
Took the seed out of the serrano - could have left them in.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
Beware the coriander seeds. Unless you have a spice grinder or an industrial blender they do not break down and their texture ruins the dish.
I found the lime a little overwhelming but tempering it with a tiny bit of sugar helped.
Use tofu, not paneer. Paneer didn't really seem to fit with the flavor profile IMO.
One the plus side, this is super easy to make, especially w frozen spinach.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
We really enjoyed this dish. I used a large bunch of kale for greens - stripped the leaves off the stems & thinly sliced and sautéed stems first then threw in the rest after about 10 min. I also had a leek and threw that in (made it sweeter) with half an onion.  I used green sriracha for tang & spice/heat as I didn't have fresh chilis which was great. 
The only spice that I found a bit overpowering was the asafetida, I used 1/8 tsp and would only use a small pinch next time. but overall delish!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Made as written -whole family enjoyed very much -you can make the “paste” earlier in the day and then dinner will come together quickly later beginning at step where you add coconut milk so I liked that about this recipe as well

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I come from a Punjabi household and this saag just wasn't for me. Adding lime threw it completely off. The texture of the coriander seeds were off putting to say the least. I would not make this again.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: This is my first time have saag paneer and I enjoyed it! I used spinach and subbed halloumi for the paneer, and also some chickpeas. Don't skip on the cumin seed topping -- it really brings together the dish!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Mom of littles here: to simplify the spice manipulations, I put the cumin seeds in the oil ( butter) at the beginning, then onion, garlic and ginger and finally 1-2minutes with a lot of ground coriander and a fair bit of ground cardamom. I added garam masala also, then greens. I'm sure the toasted and home ground blend of spices is better, but this was delicious. I also made sure to blend for a very long time before adding tofu and... my kids loved it!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: 16 different ingredients for an easy weeknight meal, I dont think this one qualifies as simple

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Delicious! Lots of mustard greens at the farmers market right now. Next time, I think I’ll use slightly less coconut milk to keep the flavors a bit more concentrated and vegetal.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: @MadelineI just had an idea to use coconut cream next time, should be less watery!!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: This one was a total miss for me. Almost inedible. I used mustard greens and it was terribly bitter. Maybe I was meant to remove the ribs of the leaves? In the future, I will leave saag to the experts.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Same happened with me went with Mustard greens and it was "almost inedible'. I think the more successful reviewers went with spinach instead

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Against the advice of the author, I blended the mixture in Step 3 way too much in my high speed blender, and ended up with a baby food consistency. I should have listened to her, instead of to everyone in the comments talking about how the mixture is too chunky and the seeds/spices are too crunchy. I think I would have rather had crunchy pieces of seeds than baby food. Flavor was good.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
Followed the combined advice of commenters, served with NYT’s mixed sabzi recipe and some Trader Joe’s naan. Success! To summarize from my experience:
1. Shell cardamom pods, then dry toast with whole coriander seeds. Grind both before cooking with onions.
2. Used J Kenji’s method of prepping tofu in his vegan saag paneer. I found TJ’s high protein firm tofu to work very well here as it is a great texture for this recipe
3. 2 bunches fresh spinach   immersion blendworked fine (no liquidy issues)

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: This is a go to dish for me. It’s wonderful with firm tofu and frozen spinach so it uses pantry staples. I have made with whole and fresh ground spices. It’s delicious both ways.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Yuck all around. We love Indian food, but this was a big disappointment. Lacking in flavor overall, but the flavor it did have was not enjoyable - slightly bitter from the mustard greens and spices that never blended well.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: For the amount of spices, the flavor was underwhelming. I opted for spinach and tofu, so it’s possible those accounted for a surprisingly bland dish.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: To make this a vegan recipe, I added cashews which seem richer and more flavorful than the tofu option. Five stars!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
Freeze, then thaw firm tofu before adding. It becomes more porous and soaks up more sauce. 

I used a mix of mustard greens, spinach, and nettles. Next time I'll nearly double the spices and try dry roasting without the cardamon pods. my blender made pod shards rather than pulverizing them.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Meh. I love saag paneer. I followed totally, using dried herbs and paneer. I added chili crisp as suggested, and dried red pepper flakes. Still bland and boring.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: So good and cozy! Next time, I would consider increasing all spices, ginger, and garlic - maybe even double! The coconut milk mellows out those flavors a bit. Don’t skip the coconut oil and toasted seeds!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Made it tonight. I forgot I was out of coriander seed and just had the ground but it worked. However, using an immersion blender on the cooked mustard greens did not work. There was not enough liquid to blend it (I don't have a big blender). So, I added the coconut milk and then blended. The rest came out tasty!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Update: I ate this today as leftovers for lunch and had rice with it. I wish I'd eaten it plain or with roti or naan instead. The rice took away from the flavors I thought.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I made this tonight with feta, 1/2lb mustard greens and 1/2 lb Swiss chard. Will definitely make it again! I enjoyed it with the coconut milk!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I made this with turnip greens and it was divine!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Delicious, but if you’re using an immersion blender, definitely recommend dry toasting then grinding the seeds in a spice grinder or mortar and pestle before adding back. My immersion blender didn’t grind up the seeds which made for an unpleasant texture.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Extremely unpleasant to bite down on the whole coriander seeds. Don’t make this without grinding the seeds first.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Blended the greens with 1/3 of the coconut milk to loosen the mixture. Worked like a charm and added the rest into the pot with the greens. Delicious!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: So delicious! I made a few changes... Used ghee for coconut oil and pumfu (ground pumpkin seed slab) cut and fried in ghee in place of tofu, frozen spinach, a touch more salt. Spices were very well balanced and tasty. Will definitely make again. Thanks for the recipe!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: FWIW, this dish was not to my taste at all. I'm sure it's a matter of personal preference, but the flavor profile just made me crave a good panang or green curry preparation. I made it with kale.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
This. Everyone loved it, veggie and non veggie alike. 
and i skipped or missed stuff and it was still delicious. 
I found the pepper the next morning rolling around the grocery bag, and didn't do the coconut oil/cumin seed finish because...hunger:)
i served it w Melissa clark's Shakshuka, some roasted 
za'atar cauliflower and Air Fryer Polenta 1/2 moons. 
my goodness.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Anyone tried this with kale? We have tons. I'd think I'd have to cook the greens longer, and with the note about NOT overcooking the greens, would that ruin the dish? I should I maybe blanch the kale first to parcook it?

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Definitely going to try a blender next time, rather than the immersion blender, the coriander is strong and chewy, and the ginger bites that didn't cook long and get blended are unpleasant for me. Otherwise, it's delicious!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: The texture was inedible with the cardamon pods remnants,the whole coriander seeds and the whole cumin seeds. Took over an hour prep - not 10 mins and was so disappointing. Using ground option for all three might make it okay.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Dinner guests who lived a long time in India said this tasted authentic and it was good but we felt that it lacked something. Felt like it needed more acid (lime) more heat (another whole chili pepper not seeded?) or a richer more concentrated base (less coconut milk?). I'll try it a few more times to improve it but it needs something else to wow us. Will also brown and crisp the tofu cubes a bit before soaking them in the dish...

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Delicious recipe, however, the texture of the whole seeds and cardamom pods was really off-putting (they did not break down with either the immersion blender or in the regular blender). Will make again, but will definitely dry toast the spices and grind them to a fine powder. Also added more salt, a pinch of sugar, an extra Serrano, extra lime, and some tomato paste (most of my saag recipes have tomato in them).

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Always loved saag paneer but never made it at home much because I figured the paneer was a make-or-break ingredient. Opening the recipe’s elements up to riffing and substitutions makes it super accessible. I made this with collard greens, queso de freir, and halloumi and the results got rave reviews. As others note, the spices lean woody — might experiment with different combinations next time.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: This was an incredible dish! Like others, I did some improvising because I ran out of coriander seed (used Garam Masala with extra cumin and fresh turmeric). I used Lacinato kale from our CSA along with extra firm tofu and mild, firmer feta. It was wonderful! Thanks to folks who suggested cooking the spices in a dry pan - worked perfectly. I'll be making this again and again and again. Thanks, Priya!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Delicious! Used paneer and softened it in warm water while prepping, as suggested in another user note. If you opt to use whole coriander seeds I strongly suggest you grind them first. We followed the recipe as written, and the coriander husks were a bit obtrusive in the finished dish.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: 5 stars without the asafetida, so I look forward to using it next time. I used spinach & haloumi, and will also try using a mixture of spinach and dandelion greens.  Also thought I had a chili pepper and didn't, so I subbed that out for some Indian-inspired chili paste.  If you are worried about that much coconut milk, I'd use 3/4 of a can before going for light coconut milk. Served on brown rice.  Washed it down with a non-buttery, unfiltered Chardonnay.  I'll be making it again next week.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: for some reason was very bitter could have been the Mustard Greens

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Failure in my opinion. Really bitter (and I like bitter!), soooo much coriander seed, grassy texture, zero creamy coconut flavor. It had none of the things I love about this dish in restaurants. I can add that I bought everything at Patel brothers Indian grocer; top quality spices etc.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: As others have said, I made this with mustard greens and it was pretty bitter at first, but after adding a good amount of sugar and additional acidity in the form of vinegar, the resulting dish exceeded spinach versions I have made in the past. Definitely worth a try, it just might take more adjustment at the end to get things to your liking.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
Using mustard greens with the recipe as written resulted in a terribly bitter flavor. I had to add a lot of honey and butter to try and balance it. Worked a little, but still, not very enjoyable.

It sounds like the people who gave this a more positive review may have used spinach?

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I like to use half mustard greens and half spinach for saag recipes...the flavor is a bit more mellow and not as likely to turn out overly bitter.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
Cooked this for my wife and a friend last night - they loved it, I did not. I found the toasted coriander seeds to be overwhelming - I felt like I couldn’t taste anything else. Not even the heaping amount of coconut milk could offset it.

I would use ground coriander next time (or process the spices as one commenter suggested). Also - mustard greens are quite bitter, would try spinach instead.

Last, the paneer doesn’t cook through enough IMO - you have to let it sit for longer than advised.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: A note about Asafoetida- I found it at a Korean owned grocery store. It was in the spice aisle but not next to the large selection of spices. I bought it in powdered form. It came in a white plastic container, not in a glass container. It has a strong smell due to a high sulfur content.  It when cooked, the flavor is akin to leeks and or garlic. I have not yet made this dish but wanted to shared what I learned.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Toast the spices dry and then grind or use ground spices

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
Second time was a success. I read all the comments. 

Made a lot of adjustments. I roasted coriander seeds, cardamom, two red chili’s and one cinnamon stick. I then ground them in a spice grinder. I added this paste to onion, ginger garlic plus Thai chilli mix that was sautéed with mustard greens. 
This mixture was puréed in a vitamix with coconut milk, salt, lemon juice and lemon peel. Then returned to pan with tofu. I finished with the ghee plus cumin seeds plus hing. 

This was a winner.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Are we not supposed to grind coriander seeds at all? They remained totally whole and unpleasant in my dish

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: This is a great dish! I’ve made it twice, I used a large bag of power greens, so good!! Please don’t skip the asafetida, also known as hing.  It’s a staple in many Indian dishes and a small amount is all you need.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: This was really yummy! I used spinach because it was what I had on hand and we loved it.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: My partner and head chef demands boxed coconut milk, which can be tricky to find on short notice, so rather than used canned, I asked the internet, which suggested substituting full fat greek yogurt and some coconut oil. Turned out great.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I have Priya Krishna's book and like many of her recipes, but not this one. The crispy shards of toasted coriander ruined whatever else was going on with the dish. I couldn't get rid of them even though I blended the mixture far beyond what the recipe calls for. The mustard greens were too strong for me as well. The recipe probably would be excellent with spinach and freshly ground spices.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I was a fool (or maybe just really tired) and did not grind the coriander seeds. The flavor is good but unfortunately my family won't eat it with all those whole seeds so it's gone into the compost.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: This was a creative solution to "what to feed gluten free and dairy free dinner guests."  It did come out well, though the coriander seeds were a bit overpowering and I'd cut them down to 1 T at most (I knew that seemed like a lot, but figured I'd try it).  The only thing I did differently was to press and drain the tofu before tossing the cubes with oil and a bit of turmeric. I then pan fried the tofu cubes until they were a bit brown and crispy before adding to the blended mix. Perfect!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I would highly recommend grinding up the seeds like many have suggested. The blender didn’t grind the seeds enough and make the texture slightly unpleasant. I might add a little more salt. Overall this was delicious!!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I only had black cardemom and used that. do not recommend, the whole thing tasted smoked. I will try again!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: This turned out really well after paying attention to several of the suggestions from other people.  Thank you.  I de-ribbed the mustard greens of which I didn't have enough, so supplemented with some organic spinach, and used only the cardamom seeds which I roasted along with the coriander, then ground to a fine powder. I then added the ground spices and onions to the oil, before following the rest of the recipe.  Special thanks to @Anjali.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I made with frozen spinach and it worked very well. Didn't need to squeeze out or cook for much longer and it wasn't too watery. I would use more ginger next time and would like to try with cashew milk or something more neutral; the coconut milk was delicious but became the main flavour. Would probably dry roast and then grind the coriander and cardamom as well.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Starting with the whole spices makes all the difference - this recipe is a keeper!  I've tried it with tofu as well as paneer and the tofu version is better, IMO.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: This was delicious!! I took some liberties. I didn’t want to purchase a package of cardamom pods just to use a small amount so I used garam masala in place of all the other spices (added better than bullion veggie flavor) and used coconut oil to sautée my onions, ginger, garlic and greens (used baby kale and spinach). At the last minute, I decided to add a can of chick peas. Butternut squash would also have been delicious. Served this over Jasmine rice and my family was VERY happy.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: The taste of this was so good. However, I followed the instructions exactly and unfortunately didn’t read through a lot of comments first. By toasting and cooking with whole coriander seeds and cardamom pods I ended up with a trillion sharp shards in the saag. I tried pulverizing again and again, to no avail. I ended up having to strain the whole thing just to have an edible dinner, which became a light green coconut soup. Definitely would change up the seed situation next time.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I loved this and will make it again, but the recipe didn't quite work for me as written. Usually I ignore the comment section and just follow the recipe, but probably a mistake this time! The bits of cardamom pod were really unpleasant and made it difficult to eat, so I had to blend until totally smooth in my Vitamix instead of a rough pulse. If you want more texture from the spinach, you could probably try grinding the spices first or scooping the cardamom out.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
I've done this a couple of times, once with mustard greens and spinach, and today with mustard greens and chard.  Both turned out great - I love the bite of the mustard greens!!!  I generally roast paneer or tofu cubes in the oven on parchment, tossed with a little bit of melted ghee, while cooking the onions etc. (saves time).
One thing I've noticed: the recipe for the sauce is usually twice as much as is needed for 8 oz paneer.  So I freeze half of it for future use.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Sad to report, this recipe is a short on flavor. A plus is it comes together quickly. Found it bland and missing that robustness of saag. Where’s the fenugreek? Way too much cumin seed in the first step and made the dish bitter. Not a repeat for us.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
I'm terrible at following recipes completely, but used it as a template and absolutely loved it!!

I used ground spices instead of whole as I didn't have the seeds (about double what the recipe said except not that much coriander) + graham masala, used spinach and cilantro as the greens, and pan fried the tofu with some tumeric while the greens were in the blender, then just barely warmed everything together again.

Will definitely make again with whatever greens I have, seems very versatile!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I was really looking forward to this, but something about the flavor is off. I hoped that sitting overnight would improve it, but something is still off. I followed the recipe exactly and used fresh spices, including cardamom pods and whole coriander seed. I made this with tofu. I blended the greens in my Vitamix, so it’s not a texture issue, as others mentioned. Maybe it’s the mustard greens, and I should try spinach instead? Maybe it’s the large amount coriander and/or whole cardamom pods? There’s a bitter, woody quality that I can’t put my finger on.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: It's the mustard greens. Try mixing only a small amount of mustard greens in with frozen spinach.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I wish I’d read the comments before making this. As other commenters have said, grind down your spices after dry toasting! Otherwise you’ll end up with a whole lot of chunky coriander seeds in the end product.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
We did not add chilis, and it was still quite tasty.  We used "High Protein" tofu and it cooked to a tenderness that matches paneer.  We would definitely enjoy eating this one again.

What happened to coconut milk?  I've tried several brands in the past few months.  None seem as rich as they used to, and many add emulsifiers.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: i make this over and over again and i absolutely love it. i use fresh spinach and i add a jalapeño instead of whatever kind of chile they recommend and i probably couldn’t find.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I'm second-generation Indian, and my aunties were all raving about this when I made it on Christmas Day. Best compliment ever!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I've made this recipe so many times now. I usually use one 10 oz pack of frozen spinach and one 5 oz bunch of collard greens or mustard greens. Perfectly balanced bitterness. I also use a whole lime's worth of juice for the extra acidity, which balances the oil in the tadka nicely.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: This was delicious! I didn’t have cardamom so used the coriander and equal parts powdered ginger and cinnamon. The final result is so good I can’t wait to find out what it’s like using the actual intended ingredient.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Whenever I cook with coconut milk at home - full fat, unsweetened - the dish ends up with no distinct coconut flavor. I've doubled up on the coconut milk and still no coconut POW - which I get in restaurants. I've resorted to coconut extract. That's the only thing that seems to work. Any suggestions?

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: @Mark Lynn Maybe try a virgin coconut oil in place of the vegetable oil? It has a much more pronounced coconut flavor than refined coconut oil. Just double-check temp level--I've never had it "burn," but I think it could.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Good, but lacking something.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I like to finish with tablespoon or more of crumbled dry fenugreek leaves, as I felt the same. Much better.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Made it with frozen spinach, ground coriander, and halloumi. Toasted chickpeas with chili oil, salt , and curry powder to use as a topping. It was really, really good. Like, shockingly good.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Really loved this.  Made with TONS of baby spinache (about 16oz) - but did not blend it (no blender).  I simply wilted it to death (not to brown, just a gorgeous green wilt).  Also did not use use seeds but rather ground spices (3/4 ground to 1 seeds).  So, kept it pretty simple with what I had on hand and will most definitely make again.  My carnivore husband with vegetarian/vegan negative disposition liked it as much as I and would like to have it again

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: This recipe demystified a very intimidating dish for me for sure! I used tofu and a combination of Dino kale (just the leaves no stem), spinach, and cilantro. Super tasty even though I skipped cardamom and I only had cumin powder instead of whole ones.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Buy (2) 11oz clamshells of spinach!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: This was unbelievably good. As an Indian, I have grown up eating traditional saag with spinach, but I much prefer the fresh, spicy taste of Priya’s recipe. Only changes I made: boiled the cubed tofu in water (per Wendy the food scientist’s advice on IG), and added thinly sliced onion to the tadka, the tempered/seasoned oil that get thrown in at the end. My mustard greens were older (5-6 days?), so it wasn’t as spicy as a fresh batch. I love this!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I just made this for the 6th, maybe 7th, time. It surprises and delights everyone. I use a stanless steel pan now so I can just tip it and do the immersion blender there, and I get to use my tiny fancy pan with a spout for the chhonk. My mother and extended family from Nashville approve of eating your mustard greens like this. That counts for something.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Serves 4? LOL! My late mom would have eaten every bite, scraped the bowl and asked, is that all you made? I know a lot of people love greens (myself included) but Mom LOVED greens!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I’ve made this twice now, it’s definitely a new staple! I used both a block of tofu and paneer and loved having the combo.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I was surprised by how dissapointed I was with this! I would highly recommend toasting and then grinding the spices - the husky coriander seeds were too crunchy, and I felt like the texture overall was off!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I would highly recommend first pulverizing the coriander seeds or simply using ground coriander. I followed the recipe as described (toast seeds, blend with spinach) and was left with many gritty shells in my saag. I had to overblend the spinach to a pulp to even partially reduce this, and even still found the texture very offputting :(

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
This one is in rotation with a lot of edits…Spinach instead of mustard greens (far too bitter), roasting and dry grinding the coriander seeds (max 1/2 or 3/4 of tsp)and cardamom (a few pods). I am not a fan of tofu, and paneer is too rich, so I use a Turkish white cheese (Bazarny) which doesn’t crumble and becomes super soft when cooked - less thank half the calories of paneer. Also a fistful of fresh coriander leaves for a bright green color. Rest of the ingredients are the same. I love the flourish of the coconut oil and spices at the end! 
i also added a 1/2 serrano pepper - deseeded.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Delicious and so easy! Priya invites variations with her recipe, and her book Indian-ish includes feta cheese, so I tried it out along with paneer. The feta paired well with the sauce and gave extra acidity which I enjoyed. I used a vitamix (I also blended it smooth instead of the “chunky paste” suggested in the recipe) and had no issue with the whole spices, but I can understand texture being an issue if you don’t have a high powered blender.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Coconut oil can be bad for the heart. Even though it adds a lot of body to the Saag. For a healthier option, I used Plain Greek Yogurt. It perhaps will not taste as good but is healthier. Also if you use yogurt the heat treatment has to be really low to maintain yogurt nutrients.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: @VSX Did you mean coconut milk?

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: “1 can of coconut milk”….😳 taking away whatever great the greens bring in?! I would sub it

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Loved the recipe! This is definitely not an authentic North Indian  preparation - so if that’s what you want, look elsewhere! I used half a can of coconut milk. I am from the south of India and we always dilute coconut milk in curries as thick coconut milk is just too rich. I agree with another reviewer that dry roasting and grinding the spices beforehand is preferable from a texture perspective. In South Indian cuisine, we do not use turmeric with greens as it discolors the final product. I used 3 Thai green chilies and I bag of frozen spinach. If using mustard greens, I would highly recommend a ratio of 2/3 mustard greens to 1/3 spinach at least if not 50/50. Will def make this dish again!!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Another miss by this author. Flavors were a little off putting with the coconut milk.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Wonder how this would work with kale? That's what I have loads of from my CSA right now.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Wow, this sounds delish. But have a look at the nutritional info. Says 40g of fat including 24g of saturated fat. If you make this dish be aware it’s not heart healthy. Maybe there’s a way to substitute some of the fats/oils to bring down the fats.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Has anyone tried this with kale?

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Curly kale would be a bit tough texture-wise maybe but lacinato kale or similar should definitely work well.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: i have a bunch of swiss chard i need to use - i wonder if that would work instead of the mustard greens/spinach?

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Yes it will definitely work and it will be delicious!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I love saag paneer and this coconuty version was delicious. I would really recommend frying the paneer cubes in coconut oil with salt and cumin seeds first. Adds nice crunchy texture and taste to the dish. Otherwise I made it as written

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I used chard, adding the chopped stems ahead of the leaves. The stick blender suggestion was genius, and this may be my default way of preparing saag paneers. Less time and cleanup.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I made this in a large cast iron skillet and covered it as I added the spinach (didn't have mustard greens). I realized I had no more coconut milk and so I added about 2.5-3 T. unsweetened coconut flakes and blended it with the spinach/onion/spice mixture and it came out great! Not as creamy as it would have with the coconut milk but still delicious. Thanks for this great recipe!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Fun and tasty dish. We did tofu and feta, and the feta was the winner for us

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Made pretty close to the recipe with kale fresh from the fall garden, some peas from the freezer, and an habañero pepper from the garden. Yum! I used immersion blender after adding liquid from coconut milk. Extra firm tofu. Ground cumin, sumac, and ground chili in the hot coconut oil to finish.  I really enjoyed it with brown jasmine rice. Partner liked it but not as much as I did. I'll make again when the vegan kids come to visit.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Thoughts on using collards?

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Collards are milder than Kale, so they should work great!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I made this to a nyT and it needed more salt and spice. A little Patak’s chili lime relish made it delish.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Delicious. A little heavy for me.  Next time would try using light coconut milk and halving the coconut oil.  I made with spinach and chard leaves and ground the spices

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: This recipe was delicious and adaptable. I just wanted some seasoned greens, so didn’t use paneer or tofu and also didn’t blend the greens, for which I used red mustard greens with stems finely chopped (stems added a minute before leaves) and a few brussel sprouts (leaves separated). Neither did I bloom the cumin, asefoetida, and chili in oil  at the end (sorry), adding them as is. Yet following the other direction produced something great that I’ll make again, maybe as savory pie filling.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I rejiggered leftover sai Bhaji by adding light coconut milk, more ginger and cayenne and pan browned tofu. Most excellent and flexible recipe. The coconut milk with the spicy greens is so key

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: This was incredibly good. I used extra firm tofu (trying to eat less meat/dairy) and it was delicious. Used baby spinach, and half a can of coconut milk.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
Very good! Now I am motivated to buy fresh spices. I bet it would be terrific, then. 
I have a technique problem - Fresh spinach needs to be rinsed. How do you dry it? It made my sauce very runny.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I really want to make this (I bought both tofu and paneer yesterday!) but I'm wondering if it will freeze well.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I made this last night and it is so very good!  I had spinach, some arugula and Swiss Chard for the greens. The only change I made to the recipe was to dust the tofu cubes in cornstarch and lightly fry them until they were crisp. They made a delightful contrast to the silky greens. This is definitely going into rotation at our house.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Loved it with tofu. I used probably too much ginger but the coconut (from the unfiltered oil) was as powerful as anything else. I used one bunch spinach and one bunch dandelion greens. Nice balance and not too bitter. Also used a couple serranos seeds and all that gave it nice warmth. Subbed leeks for onions. Looking forward to making again and again and swapping in different types of greens.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Made as written other than used swiss chard and lite coconut milk.  Was very good.  Added lemon at the table.  Would make again.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Really enjoyed the flavors! I baked the tofu with my favorite turmeric marinade until it had a good bite to it but before it became crunchy, about 40 minutes at 400 degrees. I had slightly less coriander seed than needed. In the future I would use the full amount but grind half of it.

author: Priya Krishna
