commentBody: We used "mature" spinach and essentially made the recipe as directed. Other than my partner biting into 2 out of the 3 cardamom pods which somehow survived the blending, we loved it!! Hearty but nutritious and nicely balanced flavors. Great weeknight dinner. Thanks Priya :)

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Great flavors!  I used mostly Italian kale and some arugula to get to the 1 lbs. specified for greens, and used firm tofu.  Delicious!  Good base recipe for improvisation. I have mixed feelings about the whole spices.  I liked some crunchiness, but might consider grinding them to compare.  Instead of using the immersion blender, which really didn't work that well with a large pan (i.e., shallow depth of ingredients), a stand blender might grind the coriander and cardamom better.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: This is my first review of a dish from NYT… it was DELICIOUS! I used Edible Hibiscus from my garden and leek instead of onion and still came out incredibly tasty… I also loved how fast came together Also I airfried the Tofu with a bit of cumin, oils and cornstarch to give it a bit of a crust… I did only half of the recipe and it was a very generous portion, I would say enough for 4 people.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Yum! Followed the recipe except doubled the paneer (16 oz), used two bunches of whole spinach and only had ground spices so used those. Came out great! Served over rice. Makes a ton; save room for leftovers.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: You are lucky you used the ground spices. The whole left residue in my mouth after every bite. I couldn't finish one small portion. I also used an immersion blender - I definitely don't recommend the combination of whole spices and immersion bender.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: This was delicious. I used a baby greens mix (chard, kale, spinach, bok choy, etc), light coconut milk, and half of a habañero pepper, because that’s what I had. It turned out great and was totally fine without the full-fat coconut milk. Served it over rice and with garlic naan from Trader Joe’s. Will definitely be making this again!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I made this dish last night with baby spinach and it was delicious. Next time I make it I might use half the tofu and half the coconut milk. It takes a bit of time for the tofu to absorb the flavor of the dish, but when it does…yum.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I did grind the coriander seeds and cardamom pods with my mortar and pestle before roasting them.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Used 2 8 oz bags of fresh spinach. Try 3 bags next time

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I made this and it was delicious. I did add some more lime and chilli than the recipe called for, and used rainbow chard from  the garden. Will definitely make again.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Melissa Clark’s saag recipe in Dinner in One is much better!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Made this today, followed recipe as written except I used ground coriander because that’s what I had and it was so delicious, I had to leave a comment to let folks know it’s a winner! I also recommend Sach Foods Paneer for this.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Shockingly easy, fast, and delicious. I used 2/3 baby spinach and 1/3 kale, ground cardamom, extra hing, plenty of cayenne but had no fresh chili. I skipped the sizzle thing and just threw a pinch of cumin seeds in at the beginning with the coriander. We used light coconut milk which was honestly too watery but oh well, and extra firm high protein tofu and boiled it in the sauce for a few minutes at the end. The immersion blender left bits and pieces of everything but it was still great.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I loved this with the exception of the woody coriander bits. We puréed it twice in our vitamin, but they lingered. So I would follow others’ advice to either grind or smash them prior to the addition of other ingredients. But I’ll make it again. And potentially increase the spice- I kept in the Serrano seeds but maybe I’ll try a Thai chili.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Have been looking for a vegan saag paneer for some time. This is terrific, though I would add less coriander, or part ground coriander, next time. I used only one third cup of canned coconut milk and no additional coconut oil. Tastes fresher that way.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I am shocked at how good this is - how much depth it has!! I used regular spinach and mustard greens… ground cumin and coriander since it’s what I had. Still great! Do not skip on the coconut oil on top. Absolutely phenomenal. Next time I will do two Thai chili’s, but if you don’t LOVE heat, one is beautiful. Slam dunk recipe.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
Flavors were great - one of the best saag tofus I've made in a while. Agree with others who added a bit of turmeric. Added a TB of white miso for umami to finish, an unorthodox addition that I've picked up along my saag tofu journey. Disliked the texture of the spices, will grind next time.

Used mixed greens (daikon, chard, fennel and broccoli stems - another addition from my saag tofu journey), and the whole thing came out incredibly wet? Worked out, but had to reduce liquid.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: This one was disappointing. The flavors were there but very mute. The only change I made was ground cumin instead of cumin seeds, otherwise I made it exactly as written. I see others love this so just our palates.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: The crunchy seeds made this inedible for my wife and I.  Really a shame cuz the flavor was good but I can't stand the sandy crunch it came with.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I had some roasted potatoes that I added along with the tofu and used fresh spinach.  I only had light coconut milk so lacked some richness. Would make again with full fat coconut milk.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
3 bunches of spinach is perfect
xtra garlic and ginger

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
Made it with baby spinach and it turned out surprisingly well. My only complaint (and this may be because of was 2 oz short of a 1 lb of spinach), is that it wasn't as gunky as I'd prefer saag paneer to be. I also didn't have coriander seeds but ended up using a smidgen less of ground coriander powder than the recipe called for and it worked well. 

I was very pleased, and perhaps would've attempted to reduce the water of the spinach as it sauteed.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Yum! Everyone in my family loved this. I doubled the recipe (to have enough for my hungry teenagers + leftovers for lunches); used a mixture of kale, arugula, and baby spinach; and used half extra-firm tofu and half halloumi. And served over mashed potatoes because my sons were practicing making them for an upcoming friendsgiving party they're planning to host. Wonderful!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Went with tofu in place of the paneer & substituted fresh baby spinach for the mustard greens, which reduced down quite a bit - will double the quantity next time (& there will be a next time- delicious dish!)

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Besides the whole seeds becoming gritty and weird when blended, the flavor doesn’t taste like Any Saag dish I have ever eaten. I used a cuisenart, maybe my vitamix would have been better. This recipe was run so many times in the past couple weeks I figured it must be amazing and was excited to try.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
Had the same issues as others with woody bits of cardamom pods which was unpleasant

Otherwise I thought this was easy and tasty! Great for a weeknight meal. I did think it missed a little salt/brightness. Next time I make it I might experiment with adding some feta or topping with pickled red onions, scallions? Idk

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I mostly followed the directions, using a 9 ounce bag of spinach because that's all I had. I added the chile powder in step 3 with the lime juice, serrano, and salt and omitted steps 4 and 5. This is a rich dish, and the additional coconut oil was just too much fat for my taste. This is really easy, quick, and flavorful. Pretty healthy, too.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I cooked this last night. We enjoyed this new (to us) recipe for tofu. Next time I will toast and then pound the coriander seeds and cardamon pods, as others have said. I will also try ground cumin instead of the seeds. The only heat that I used was ground ancho chili powder. It’s one I that doesn’t trigger my asthma, and at home I control the level of heat. I never order anything with chilis at restaurants. Overall though I loved the intensity of the spices of this dish.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: If using feta, add only just before serving. Unlike paneer, it melts.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Couldn’t find paneer or mustard greens so I used queso fresco and spinach. Spectacular. Leftovers next day were even better.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I made this with frozen spinach (thawed and squeezed) and it worked well. Also skipped the oil on top though I’m sure it would’ve been delish.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I made it as the recipe stated (chose paneer and spinach) and thought it turned out great. At first I thought the cumin and oil seemed unnecessary but it totally made the dish.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: This was yum. Next time I will toast the seeds and grind them. Even after using my Vitamix, there were hard woody pieces of coriander seeds in it. That being said, I still scoffed it down with some white rice. Going into my rotation. Oh, I used frozen paneer.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
If looking for a gluten-free asafetida (Hing) look very closely at the ingredients list.
Some are categorized as gluten free but on inspection contain wheat (e.g. Rani and Vandevi).
Probably not enough to anger a celiac response, but I thought it worth mentioning.
I love saag and will make this tonight!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Made as is, served over jasmine rice. DELICIOUS! Nice heat. Next time i would make sure I let the tofu steep a bit more.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I'll answer my previous question: no to collard greens. I used collard greens and kale (what I had on hand) and didn't get a smooth puree...the texture was a bit rough. It tasked good though.Next time I'll try chard and/or spinach/arugula. And I used Haloumi cheese, grilled first which was very good!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I couldn't find mustard greens (which I prefer for saag for the iconic flavor) so I used a mix of spinach and other greens. The coconut flavor overpowered this mix of greens. I also did not like the whole coriander seeds - they are fibrous and do not have the pleasant texture of whole toasted cumin seeds in the tarka (the final spiced oil step). I think the texture and maybe flavor of the tofu could be improved by a salting and light pan fry before adding to the spinach but that's more work.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Really well spiced. I made it as written, with spinach, tofu, and a Serrano pepper, and including the hing. I did add some flaky salt when serving.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Do not make as written. You'll be picking coriander shells out of your teeth for hours. Dry toast then grind or just use powdered. Using an immersion blender didn't really work either. I had to jack up the flavor significantly

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: After reviewing all the notes, I decided to go with the 2 32oz bags of frozen spinach. Don’t do it! Way too much!  It wouldn’t work in the blender or the food processor, and I ultimately didn’t use all of it. I have a tiny kitchen and it seems to have spinach everywhere. I also had to change the amount of spices. I think it might be good and I’ll try again.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Made it with mature spinach. Was great! Have tried to make saag paneer before, this version was the best one.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: This was a little bland due to the cocunutmilk. I prefer an authentic saag, it s a lot brighter.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I made it with paneer instead of tofu. A mistake - combined with the large amount of coconut milk it made the whole dish very heavy. Possibly much better with the tofu!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Used spinach and found it decent but a bit blah and lacking pizazz. Had some left over sauce rof and swirled that into the bowl which helped liven it up a bit.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: This looks really fun and interesting. But how do you get results from an immersion blender or regular blender when there's no liquid ?

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I thought this was really good, made according to the recipe. Hubs thought it needed something to brighten it. Maybe add more lime next time?

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Excellent! Though I'll definitely grind the coriander seeds first. I kept biting into whole seeds and getting little explosions of coriander as I ate, not in a pleasant way for me. Also microwaved the fresh spinach first, just enough to dehydrate and squeeze most of the moisture out before adding to the pot. Added a bit of garam masala at the end, plus more salt. The extra firm tofu worked really nicely. It softened up beautifully but didn't lose its shape.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: This was so quick and easy to make and so delicious. I used a mix of tatsoi and swiss chard from my weekly CSA box and otherwise followed the recipe as written. This will absolutely be a weeknight staple for us!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I saw that in today's Vertex puzzle game, this dish was featured!  First time a Vertex puzzle made me hungry.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: My husband made this. He used frozen spinach instead of mustard greens. It was a fantastic meal! We will make this again and again.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I used a blend of mustard greens and spinach, as well as a blend of haloumi and extra firm tofu. Worked great. I will definitely crush the coriander seeds next time and use far less of them. Fun and easy, pretty, and my ten year old ate it up!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I loved everything about this recipe. I have tried and tried to perfect a saag paneer recipe, but when I made this, I was like ohhhhhhh this is what it's been missing. The coconut milk adds a nice silkiness and depth. It was a quick recipe to follow, put together, and was really good leftover. (I used firm tofu instead of extra firm because it was the literal last package left at the store. It held up okay!)

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Used one carton thawed frozen spinach in addition to fresh. Added fenugreek leaves to spinach, garam masala to baked paneer. Using immersion blender caused spinach to splatter all over. Needs salt.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Use ghee instead of oil

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Made this almost exactly as written and was surprised that it did not pack more of a flavor punch. It had all of the components suggesting it would. Wonder if I overcooked my greens. Not a huge tofu fan generally, but especially did not find it added anything here. I’d go with paneer or even chickpeas if you’re looking to add plant based protein.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: This is going on my rotation! I made it as described with modifications to what I had on hand; kale, dry ginger and no pepper. I loved it and will make this again for sure.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Thrilled with how yummy and multidimensional this turned out. Finally sprung for asefetida and it’s worth it! Did half mustard greens and half spinach. Next time I will grind the toasted cardamom and coriander instead of leaving whole because of the texture, though perhaps I didn’t blend the greens mixture enough.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Very tasty.  Also a good way to use up leftover greens (I used mostly baby spinach and a little chard).  I’ll definitely make this again, but next time I’ll use tofu, as the very tangy and salty Bulgarian feta I utilized threatened to overwhelm the other flavors in the dish.  Also, I used preground spices, not seeds, as that’s what I had.  I like that Priya made this recipe flexible, with several different options.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I used ghee instead of oil and added the cumin seeds along with other spices at the beginning instead with coconut oil at the end. The flavors were amazing BUT it was a bit too rich and heavy for my taste even with the lime. Next time I’ll use half of the coconut milk or use the light kind. I also had doubts about the lack of liquid for the immersion blender, but the spinach lets some liquid as it cooks and I added a bit more water to help it blend easier🙂

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Having tried a similar vegan saag paneer recipe before and been disappointed, I was so pleased that this one turned out so well! I suspect a food processor didn't grind up the whole spices as well as a high-powered blender would, but I found I didn't mind it when eaten with brown rice, as it hid the texture. I used half dandelion greens and half regular (not baby) spinach and the taste was lovely. Can't wait to make this again!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Wonderful recipe.   I used Queso Fresco instead of Paneer.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I made this with spinach as I cannot eat kale.  As well I used Sach paneer, which was really good with it.   I used Sirārakhong Hāthei Chillies and upped them a bit.  It was a fantastic recipe - full of flavor!  Will definitely be making this more often.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Made as written. Even with the modifications that make sense, I don’t think I would make this again. The base flavors are just odd.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Not sure what to make of this dish. Followed recipe, using tofu instead of cheese. It tasted okay, but sadly just okay. Recipe calls for a 13oz can of coconut milk, which seems a lot, so maybe reduce next time? Perhaps it will taste better tomorrow. It sure sounded better than it turned out.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: This was delicious as written. Next time I will likely double up on the peppers to add a bigger kick as it was fairly mild.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: If serving with rice, toss a few cloves and a small handful of cumins seeds into the pot/rice cooker

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Loved this. The only changes I made were to add black mustard seeds to the seasoning at the end. I also added endive to the turnip greens after one commenter said it was too bitter.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I followed the recipe to a T, using a pound of baby spinach, and I thought it was fantastic. My VitaMix made short work of blending everything--maybe even too well. The finished product was a little soupier than I expected. I might add just a half can of coconut milk next time.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Definitely crush and grind the toasted seeds; otherwise you end up with coriander pods in your mouth. (The flavor is overpowering and soapy.) We also found we needed to add more salt.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: definitely need to grind the spices, or you’re chewing on woody seeds. good flavor though, will make it again! paneer would probably be better than tofu.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I found I had to add a bit more coconut milk to make the mixture the right consistency but this was sooo good and mostly made from things I already had on hand.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: My end result was not as pretty… kind of looked like baby food. But it was pretty good! I did end up adding more salt, but definitely play it by taste. Used spinach, paneer (soak if store bought!), and canned unsweetened coconut cream instead of milk since that’s all I had. I did thin it out a little since cream has less liquid. Otherwise followed the recipe, served over rice and used the other half of the lime to squeeze over as well as some garam masala.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: This was fantastic. I had to make a few changes because I was unexpectedly out of things - added 1/4 tsp extra cayenne since I didn't have the birds' chili peppers, caraway seeds instead of coriander, and a splash of orange juice plus a 1/2 tsp of sumac since I didn't have lime. I also took the suggestion to add turmeric and chili powder to the onions. Really, really good!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I've made this twice now, and I have to say, while the flavor profile is delicious, I don't enjoy chewing on the whole spices and blending doesn't completely get rid of that sensation. So, in the future, I'll grind them ahead of time. Second time, I didn't have enough spinach. Won't make that mistake again! LOL. Otherwise, I wouldn't change a thing.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: This was the hit of the party. I took another reviewer’s advice and used three boxes of frozen chopped spinach, which I thawed and squeezed dry. That made the recipe even easier!!! Also, I started with ghee, rather than coconut oil, since you use so much coconut in the overall product. The recipe is rich and delicious — the saag paneer I’ve been dreaming about all my life.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Delicious. Didn't make the coconut oil, but used the oil to sear the tofu before cooking. Also added the asafaetida and cumin in with the coriander and the chili powder in with the ginger and garlic.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Didn't turn out that great. Next time I would grind the coriander seeds so there aren't just seeds floating around in the saag. It was off putting. Also not super flavorful. Needed something else. Probably won't make again.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: This was delicious. I used mustard greens. Also, my store didn't have the peppers specified, so I substituted a red fresno pepper. Then it turned out I didn't have cardamom pods, so I used a pinch of ground. I would definitely make this again.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Just trying to figure out how this made it on the quick and simple weeknight menu with so many unusual ingredients. We're shopping at Meijer and Kroger here in the Downriver Detroit suburbs.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Just made this and ended up adding loads more spice as I didn’t feel it was that flavourful. When I added the extras was delicious though! Would recommend frying off the paneer first so it’s nice and crispy

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Whenever a recipe asks for fresh spinach to be wilted, I “pre-wilt” it in the microwave. A 2 minute blast on high will wilt 4-6 cups of chopped, washed & spun-dry greens, an additional minute or 2 will thoroughly cook them (depends on the type of greens). Not only is the reduced volume easier to work with, but then I have a choice about how much cooking liquid to use (there won’t be a lot in any case).

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Ooh I loved this, the spices and the coconut really toned down the mustard greens, yet it remained such a lovely bright green. What a treat after work tonight!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I used British Winter greens with whole coriander,  cardamom seeds and asafoetida in the tarts. It was fantastic. Heartily recommend.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: This recipe is great! A few things I learned: the advice to grind the spices before toasting is good. Out of laziness, I added cumin and cayenne at the beginning too, and was happy with the result. I used a very wide-bottomed pan, and even with adding the coconut milk before blending, it was still not enough material to use an immersion blender. I would suggest using a stock pot, or plan on using a stand blender. Delicious! Impossible not to have a second helping.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Like many others have said in the notes that I of course didn't read ahead of time - GRIND THE SPICES FIRST. I attempted an immersion blender, then a food processor, and then a regular blender and none managed to get the coriander and cardamom ground all the way up. The hard, medicinal bites of tough cardamom pod really made the dish pretty unpleasant overall, which was too bad because it might have been good otherwise.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
I swear this recipe is a work of art!!!

The flavors are sublime.  Having said that, I don’t think I would have loved it as much if I hadn’t made three significant changes:

First of all, I didn’t blend the initial mixture.  By the time I’d added the greens (Swiss chard, in this case; but, I don’t think that was important.) and the lime, chile and salt, the whole thing looked so incredibly beautiful and delicious that I didn’t bother to blend it.  I think this was a wonderful decision; because,y

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
I swear this recipe is a work of art!!!

The flavors are sublime.  Having said that, I don’t think I would have loved it as much if I hadn’t made three significant changes:

First of all, I didn’t blend the initial mixture.  By the time I’d added the greens and the lime juice, etc., the thing looked so beautiful and delicious that I decided not to blend it.  As a result, every bite was different and spectacularly delicious.  Also, I added pistachio nuts, which gave it a lovely crunch.  Used I sw

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I used Swiss chard and it was wonderful.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I really enjoyed this; the richness of the coconut mild offset the mild bitterness of the mustard greens. I ended up using  2 t. ground coriander, but otherwise stuck to whole spices where called for. Definitely add more lime juice and salt than called for, and don't deseed the chile since the coconut milk sucks up the heat. Also, a sash of coconut milk in the blender helps it puree more easily.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I like the original recipe in her cookbook, Saag Feta, much better. I have been making it for years and we love it in my house. I tried this version and was a bit let down with the diluted flavor. I may try using less coconut milk next time. It would be great for people who want something more mild though.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: The coriander seeds need to be ground otherwise you’re chewing on the little hulls throughout which is not appealing. I freshly ground cardamom which really made an impact. I used an immersion blender but the texture was grainy so would use the vitamin next time to make it smoother. It’s nice. Not wow but solid and quite easy.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: This was a flavorful recipe, but the hand blender did not work well; I highly recommend using an actual blender.  I ultimately just added the coconut milk, salt, chile, and lime to the spinach mixture, stirred once, put it all in my high-speed blender until just blended (not smooth), and then poured it back into the pot to heat with the tofu.  Served with lime wedges on the side -- a little extra lime at the end helped cut the richness of the coconut.  A lovely variation on classic saag!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Omitted the chili because of kids, and then added fresh salsa Verde to our individual bowls. Delicious!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I found needed much more salt, at least two teaspoons. And I wish I had ground the spices adding as my immersion blender didn’t get them.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Really tasty! One thing I find in this and some other NYT recipes is that they really seem to overestimate the ability of tofu to quickly soak up flavors. I used tofu and if using again in the future, I would definitely season it or give it a light marinade before tossing in. Other than that, thought it was great!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: First time commenting on nyt recipes.  This recipe rocks! I'd like to get down to how much canned or frozen spinach equals 12 cups raw, and I would always double the recipe because one batch is hardly enough for leftovers. I see someone below said 32 oz frozen equals 12 cups fresh, I googled and got this:   "1 pound of fresh spinach will yield 10-12 cups of torn leaves, which will cook down to about 1 cup. One package (10 ounces) of frozen spinach leaves yields about 1-1/2 cups after cooking. "

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Yummy - but not as flavorful as I anticipated. I used ground spices as others suggested - so perhaps they weren’t as fresh, and doubled the ginger and added an extra clove garlic but it still felt like it needed something. Extra lime juice and salt for serving helped. I’ll add turmeric next time and heaping teaspoons of cardamom and coriander.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
This was great and surprised by how quick it came together.  I used 2 bunches of kale including tough inner veins.  Halloumi was ok but added more salt than needed.  
Substituted garam masala for coriander.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Not sure what I did, or maybe the coconut milk was off, but this was pretty bland for me. Def needed more salt and the aromatics. I ended up putting in some veggie better than bullion, a tbsp of garlic powder, and microplaned another 1  inch of ginger directly into it while it bubbled away. Much better, had a beautiful rounded umami quality and the extra fresh ginger really helped bring out the other flavors, including the coconut. The tofu also soaked it all in nicely with the extra time in the

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Agree with some comments that whole coriander seeds were a bit unpleasant to encounter, would prefer to use ground next time. Otherwise enjoyed this warm and comforting saag, it was great with naan!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I’ve made this recipe a few times now and love it! I use fresh baby spinach and have had no issue with excess liquid — it has always cooked up pretty dry. I think dry roasting the spices and then grinding is a great idea that I’ll try next time. I do love the whole cumin seeds, though.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Couldn’t find mustard greens and didn’t want to automatically resort to spinach. So o went we collard greens. They did not become bitter like others complained about with the mustard greens. Followed the suggestion to dry toast and then grind the coriander seeds and cardamom pods. Otherwise made as written. A solid 4, maybe more. Sophisticated seasoning. Trying to think of what it would take to make it a solid 5.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Used ground coriander as I can’t source coriander seeds in Albuquerque. Delicious! Hubs found it almost too hot (I used 1 seeded Serrano pepper) but I found it fine (I added half an avocado to my bowl, as I feared the heat). The mustard greens were a bit bitter, so I am curious to try it with spinach. Had to use a food processor for the greens: not enough liquid was in them to allow a blender or immersion blender to work.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I typically press the liquid out of my tofu before cooking with it but since this is a stew - is that advised or necessary?

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: This dish marries South Indian coconut milk with North Indian saag, and I don’t think it works. Priya Krishna’s saag with feta cheese (in her cookbook) is genius and so flavorful. I like to make that with half paneer/ half feta and will stick with that.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Delicious! Followed recipe as written -- using fresh spinach and firm tofu. I dry roasted the cardamom and coriander then blended and added to onions as they cooked. Used less ginger (I find ginger can be overpowering). At the end I added 3 thai chilis chopped for extra spice. Next time I might add a little extra spice, like garam masala or turmeric.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I really did not like this, but I am not positive it was the recipe—I used some leftover fresh spinach combined with frozen spinach, and maybe the frozen spinach was just not good. I did think the coconut oil and spices really improved the final product. I wished that the tofu had some seasoning of its own because it was kind of bland in the sauce. The immersion blender struggles with spinach so a real blender might help.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Very tasty. I would use more than the 8 oz of halloumi it calls for, probably 2 packs, to get 4 servings, or else there isn't enough cheese. Otherwise, my only mods were to use fresh spinach and to strain the actual Cumin seeds out of the coconut oil at the end.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
This is perhaps the best recipe I have cooked using one of the NYT recipes and I’ve been cooking with you all for several years.
I used mixed “power greens” for the greens and Trader Joe’s lite coconut milk.  I only had about 1 T of coriander seeds so added some ground coriander to make up for that shortage.  It was delicious.  I wish I had started with coconut oil but instead I used canola.  Next time.  
It will not be too long before I make this again.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: 2nd time i made this...made exactly as directed (used fresh spinach) and came out GREAT! For me it waa easier to use the immersion blender and the consistency of the paste was perfect.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I love this recipe for it's tasty, fresh simplicity.  I use high-protein tofu in place of paneer.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Are there substitutes for the coconut milk if you are not a fan .

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I would use Malabar spinach to make this, I much prefer it to normal western spinach and it's much more common in India.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: << You’re better off using a few boxes of frozen spinach and squeezing all the water out with a cloth or paper towel and giving it a coarse chop.   If you use fresh spinach you’ll need a boatload and will be waiting forever for all that liquid to reduce. >>

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I have never added a note but felt this was deserving! Used pre-cut kale and it held up well to all the spices. Slightly broke the coriander pods to release the seeds before adding the onion. Used a red onion (as I was out of white) and a whole lime for juice. Don’t skip on the asafetida. Found it at the Asian market and it is SO unique! Cut everything up early and the dish takes very little time to pull together. Served with naan rather than rice. DELICIOUS dish!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Great dish that is so healthy, so filling and so easy to make and pair with whatever else you have. Brilliant.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I used a bag of spinach and a bunch of lacinato kale, only ground spices, and used a Serrano pepper. Other than that I followed the recipe and it was delicious, better than the local restaurants and exponentially healthier. I also dried the tofu really well and popped it in the oven for a few minutes to really firm it up before adding it.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Delicious. You can substitute 6 x 3/4 tsp ground coriander for 2 T coriander seeds. Use more than 1 pound of greens. Note extra firm tofu. Or try queso fresco.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: 1 tbsp coriander seed

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
I used fresh spinach (1lb).
Don't burn the coriander and cardamom in the first step. 
The saag can handle heat. Use two serrano peppers, don't remove seeds. 
12 oz paneer was perfect. 8oz paneer isn't enough.
Be generous with garlic and ginger. 
Would have been better served with rice AND pita bread.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Saag paneer is my favorite Indian dish, and I have always thought it would be too complicated to make it at home. I was wrong. This was delicious. I only had powdered coriander and firm tofu (I pressed it to get rid of some of the water). I used a 1 lb container of baby spinach that was just about to go back (planned to eat salad that week, but life be lifein'). It turned out wonderful. Thank you, Priya!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: This is NOT a good recipe. So bland and sour and even though I added extra coconut and garam masala and more garlic and salt, it never had the delicious saag taste from a restaurant. Also the lime juice made it almost better; it was bad.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
This is the best Saag recipe I have ever made. It was also the quickest. Win-win

The Chhonk or Tadka is the key in this recipe to giving it that creamy restaurant taste.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Now that I’ve made my saag paneer, it will be hard to get sad take out. This is an outstanding recipe.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I followed the recipe very closely except used iceberg lettuce instead of spinach, ground spices instead of whole, corn oil instead of coconut oil, goat milk instead of coconut milk, and cottage cheese instead of tofu.  It was amazing!   I fed it all to my mother in law and she ate it up like she hadn’t been fed for days

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: As with some other reviewers this one was a miss for me too. I struggled to get the greens (used mustard greens) into a thick paste with my Vitamix. There wasn’t enough liquid for the greens to catch the blade. So I put everything back in the pan and used an immersion blender with no improvement. In hindsight I should have added the coconut milk to the Vitamix. Also, it was above our comfort level in terms of heat with one Serano chili. Next time, I will seed it and cook it a bit.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: This was absolutely terrific.  Yes, Chelsea :-). Made with mature spinach; kept the whole spices.  Paneer.  Will definitely make again.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Can I highly recommend swapping regular garlic for pickled garlic - it is divine.  If using skip the cardamon, cumin seeds, coriander seeds and vegetable oil

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I followed the recipe 99%. I couldn't find mustard greens so I used 50/50 kale and spinach. Turned out great!!! I also used Tofu not paneer.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
It was a hit!
My immersion blender worked fine with the wilted spinach. Used more cubed paneer than called for. Next time, I'll use coconut oil in both steps and more garlic, ginger and hot pepper. I didn't care for the whole cumin seeds (I usually like them), ground cumin would be better, IMO. At the table, we added more salt   lime juice and some Tabasco. I will make this again with these modifications and use tofu for my vegan SIL.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
I used about 16oz frozen chopped spinach and it worked just fine, wasn't even fully thawed I just let it thaw and come to temp in the pan. 
The flavor was good, but I found myself wanting *more* - will up the spices   ginger next time. The spices in coconut oil really pulled it together, I found it pretty bland tasting throughout cooking. Maybe put the chile pepper in w/ the garlic and ginger to let it really get some spice in there, seems odd that it's added at the end.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Good recipe but I also wanted it spicier.   Frozen spinach would have been my a g good sub.   I used a mix of collard, kale & mustard greens.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Love this recipe - practically a pantry meal for me as I always have kale and tofu on hand (as well as all the spices).  First time making it, I thought it could use a bit more spice.  Second time making it, ended up adding a set of spice to the onion mixture that I use for my daal, which includes ground tumeric, cumin, and coriander.  Also added mustard seeds to the cumin seed coconut oil at the end.  Since it is just two of us at home, I look forward to the leftovers with Jasmine rice.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
Followed the combined advice of commenters, served with NYT’s mixed sabzi recipe and some Trader Joe’s naan. Success! To summarize:
1. Shell cardamom pods, then dry toast with whole coriander. Grind both until fine before cooking with onions.
2. Used J Kenji’s method of prepping tofu in his vegan saag paneer. I found TJ’s high protein firm tofu to work very well here as it is a great texture for this recipe
3. 2 bunches fresh spinach   immersion blender worked fine (no liquidy issues)

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: This background on asafetida (aka asafoetida) may be of help... https://www.bonappetit.com/story/asafetida-indian-spice

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I thought white rice wasn't the healthy type of rice.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Absolutely delicious. Only change is that I added all the spices at the beginning. Used kale and nettles for greens and paneer. The whole coriander took it to another level!!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
I followed the recipe to a T. Used paneer which I first softened in warm water then sautéed in butter to gently brown it. 

It was fantastic.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
Really good, needed more salt. Based on comments I did the following: 

I made this with a mix of fresh and frozen spinach, more than a pound. 

Dry toasted the coriander and cardamom, then ground and added to the onion saute. 

Used a Fresno chile , and for the powdered chile a combo of cayenne and gochugaru flakes. Id use all gochugaru next time. 

I will also try other greens combos to include some mustard greens but wary of just mustard greens. 

A keeper!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Absolutely delicious. Followed as written but took advice to soak store bought paneer in warm water and added turmeric and chili powder with onions.  Served with white rice and naan.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: So good! Thank you! I made this with dandelion greens (no mustard greens available) and it is fabulous. The perfect way to move from winter to spring.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: OMG yes! Used 2 one pound bags frozen organic spinach, 1 can TJ’s organic coconut creme, only ground spices except the cardamom which went in the Ninja, smoked paprika for chile (can’t do spicy) and added smidget of garam masala and Better Than Boulion organic paste. Froze half before adding brick of tofu, skipped oil on top. Even our cat wanted some.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I make this regularly, we always use tofu since we keep it on hand, it is cheaper, and honestly, I don't miss the paneer at all, it is a great substitute. I've made this with all kinds of greens, works with everything I tried. I do up the spices and toast them first.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I was really excited for this recipe, but for whatever reason, the flavor combo here was not for me. The saag with coconut was slightly sweet in a way that didn't meld super well for me. I did not have whole spices and used ground spices, and also used spinach instead of mustard greens. I'm glad I tried this but I think I'll just go back to a more traditional saag recipe.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
I hate to be the "I substituted things and it came out bad" person. So, we didn't really like this, but I blame myself.
-I used collards. Not sure if that was the problem.
-I used an immersion blender. I wonder if better blending in a food processor would have helped the texture.
-I had run out of fresh ginger. Dried of course is no comparison. I was hoping the flavor bomb of other spices would compensate, but I think the missing fresh ginger omitted an important layer.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I have never instantly wanted to remake a recipe more.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I couldn't believe how well this turned out! I made this after work in about an hour and it was an instant winner for my wife and me. I did not have the whole cardamom or whole coriander seeds but the pre-ground version worked just fine. The leftovers we also amazing for lunch the next day. I'm definitely adding this recipe to my weeknight meal rotation!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Delicious!  After reading reviews about a "woody" texture, my only modifications were to how I prepared the spices: I toasted the cardamom pods and coriander seeds for about two minutes in a dry skillet and then ground them with mortar and pestle (along with a dried thai chili because there were no fresh chilis at the market), adding them to the onions, garlic, and ginger once they were sauteed.  I also toasted and ground the cumin before adding to the hot oil at the last step.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Could I make it with Spigariello? Seems like a rich green like that would hold up well to the coconut milk and spices.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
Wow! This was really good.  Toasted and ground my spices before incorporating and used fresh spinach.  Also, made with paneer.  My first experience cooking with paneer. Soaked the cut paneer in warm water as suggested by another commenter.
Will definitely make this again.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Really tasty weeknight meal. Needed more lime right at the end. Sort of a lot of dishes - next time I’ll try adding the cumin with the other spices at the beginning.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: this was truly a really delicious & easy recipe. like other commenters said - i recommend blitzing all the spices in the recipe in a spice/coffee grinder until finely ground, then toasting them. i also added about half a bunch of fresh cilantro, & used serano peppers + the zest of a lime.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Love this recipe! Adding water to the wilted greens in required for them to blend in my (mediocre blender) but I don't mind having a bit of a looser sauce afterwards. Has anyone tried freezing it?

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Yes!  I always freeze half of the saag, and use one 8 oz package of paneer with each half.  Works perfectly!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Saag paneer is one of my favorite dishes, but I've never been able to make a truly great version at home. This recipe broke that curse. I opted for the tofu, and didn't skip the asafoetida. I'm already excited about the leftovers tomorrow.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I made this after watching the video on CBS The Dish recipes. I used shrimp for the protein. Excellent and fast! My first ever Indian dish. My family loved it.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Delicious and easy! I love the spicy mustard greens paired with creamy coconut milk. I will definitely make this again!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Is there anything lost by using coconut oil instead of vegetable oil, and then including the cumin and cayenne with the cardamom and coriander at the beginning? This is so close to an amazing one pot meal, and while I love the look of the orange drizzle at the end, for weeknights and leftovers it’d be great to skip the extra pan and steps. But I can’t help but think Priya separated this part for a reason

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I did the oil separately, but then mixed it in to the final sauce.  I think you could probably do as you are suggesting, but maybe add a bit of extra fresh cayenne right at the end?

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: This is seriously delicious and comes together pretty quickly. Use all of the spices the recipe calls for—it makes or breaks this dish. Use the best coconut milk you can find (I used Chaokoh). And substitute the mustard greens with whatever bounty you have available. I had a pound of swiss chard and collards and was worried the collards would be too firm, but they’re wonderful. Looking forward to leftovers today!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Really delicious! Dry toasted the coriander and ground in spice grinder then added to the sautéed onion. Used a huge box of “power greens” and used our immersion blender. Turned out great!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Would coconut oil be okay, or even better?

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I'd reduce the coconut oil topper at the end. It's too much for its host.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I couldn’t find mustard greens so I made this with a mix of dandelion greens and collard greens but it was so so bitter. It was inedible. I’m going to try again with spinach. The recipe looks so good I’m going to give it another shot!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Such a great recipe! Added to my regular rotation :) I used spinach, and butter instead of coconut oil since that's what I had, and it worked well. Only "negative" is that it didn't quite pack a punch flavor-wise as I was hoping. This could be because I used coriander powder as opposed to coriander seeds, which might have dulled the aromas? I also think another reason was that the paneer was bland and unseasoned; chaat masala came to the rescue in the end, so next time I'll jazz up the paneer!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I made this with Swiss chard since I have a ton of it in my garden, but otherwise I followed the recipe. It seemed really flavorful in the pot but somehow all of that flavor kind of deflated once I served it over the rice. I really wanted this to be a hit to help use up all the greens this summer, but not sure if I’ll give it another try.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Thanks for sharing this recipe - immeditately when I saw the video I was thrilled to try it out, as this was something completely new to me from the Indian cuisine. I am from Finland and over here Coconut Saag is very uncommon even in restaurants serving Indian food. I really liked the process of making this, using the blender and roasting the coriander seeds etc. The dish turns out so beautiful in its lush green color. I used baby spinach and it worked really well!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Did anyone use ground spices only and have good ground spice equivalents?

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I use frozen spinach and sometimes I sub halloumi for tofu. This is really good.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I found the whole seeds of coriander & pods of cardamom (I knew this before but apparently had to relearn it...) to be distracting and an unwelcome texture; will be toasting & grinding them next time. Also will use mature spinach after reading comments - my partner accidently bought collard greens which weren't quite right, sadly. Beautiful color tho! I did love the way this came together quickly and look forward to the next time I make it!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Recipe doesn’t say to grind the spices after toasting them.  First attempt didn’t turn out well.  Hoping 2nd will please the family

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I have a question about the fresh chili. I looks like you don’t cook it because it is added with lime juice and salt and then blenderized. Am I missing something?

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Amazing. Toasted and ground the spices, leek greens instead of onions (fodmaps), used equal parts mustard greens/ spinach, and it was delicious.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: This is not an "easy weeknight dinner."  It took an hour to prepare, and even with rice, it's not substantial enough on its own to be a dinner.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Perfect and delicious! Creamier than a traditional saag. Love the coconut milk!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Way too sweet!!! This is definitely the worst thing I’ve made from NYT Cooking. I was so excited to make this and I thought the coconut would be a nice touch but it was overpowering and made the meal taste like “dessert spinach”. Back to cooking regular saag for me. I haven’t commented on a NYT recipe before, but I had to come here for this one to say, yuck.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Did you use Unsweetened Coconut milk?   It will make a big difference.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: This was really good. We used beet greens because the garden is producing a lot of beets right now. Also a bit of arugula (from garden) to get some of the edge that the mustard would provide. I ground the coriander after toasting. No way my Cuisinart would have pulverized it enough. Quite easy to pull together.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Listen to the other posters who are wise when they tell you to grind the spices after toasting. The texture of the coriander pods ruined this dish for me. I’m not sure whether I will attempt again. Also this took me an hour to make, not 25 min.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: What if you can’t eat chili/peppers due to an allergy? Any substitutions? Would it still be good without the spicy oil?

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I made this earlier in the week and found it to be AWESOME!  I used ground cardamom, tofu, and spinach.   Definitely a keeper!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I was so excited to make this. I love toasted whole cumin seeds and have made Indian recipes with whole mustard seeds that were delicious. This, however, was not good because the whole coriander seeds overwhelmed and tasted musty/woody. I bought them just a few days ago from my bulk section. 🤷‍♀️

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
This was terrible, the taste of mustard green is so over Powering , you cannot taste coconut milk or anything else . 
Maybe we need to blanch and mix match

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I loved all the spices and how easy and fast this recipe was. I used half mustard greens and half spinach, the mustard green had a very strong flavor that I'm not sure I loved. Next time I'll go with spinach only.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: This was delicious, and FAST. Super-easy weeknight dinner, like 20 minutes using pound of frozen spinach. Paneer came in 12 oz package so just used it all, which worked out well since my chile was particularly hot - spice level would have been high without more cheese to mellow it. Seriously just scooping more out of the pot with naan - addictive! Definitely worth trip to Indian grocery store for hing!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Ratio of sauce:protein was way too high on sauce. Tasted fine, but far too many steps to make the end result worthwhile.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I made this tonight, and while the flavor was wonderful, we kept feeling like there were tiny pieces of straw in it. Very unpleasant.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Highly recommend. I have made this several times with fresh baby spinach and homemade paneer. I use the recipe exactly as written and it always gets rave reviews from guests. The oil with spices at the end is key - I got some asafoetida and I am obsessed, don't skip it. I also think the whole cumin seeds are great for a textural element, just make sure you cook them enough. I make this in a menu along with butter chicken, raita, basmati rice, and cardamom-orange olive oil cake for dessert.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Not for me.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
This is a pretty straightforward recipe which I followed exactly, using fresh spinach. Setting up your mise en place is critical, but makes everything go fast. The dish was restaurant-quality good!

A couple of changes i will make next time: 

1) I would crush the coriander seeds in a mortar after toasting. I thought there was too much texture; some might not care!
2) The dish was a little soupy for my taste. I would start with 1/2 can coconut milk and add more as desired.
3) I used a deseeded Serrano pepper and the dish was barely spicy, but very flavorful.

A winner!
2)

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: delicious & simple. i used frozen spinach & it worked just fine for me. i also roasted the tofu (seasoned w s&p & soy sauce) in the oven at 400 for about 30 minutes, flipping halfway thru before adding to the saag.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: This dish is easy and so tasty, especially the next day. I did a combination of paneer and tofu. Skipped a few items I didn't have in my pantry like the asefetida and the fresh green chili, used ground coriander and  cardamom too, but will buy the next time I cook this. I think this will be in my regular rotation. Nom!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: It was such a shame—the meal had the potential to be a refreshing take on the original recipe. Unfortunately, the use of whole seeds really threw it off. They stayed completely intact, and their texture and taste was quite off-putting when eating . Please consider realistic expectation for making the dish - use powdered spices or really powerful blender .

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Excellent. Followed the recipe/video to a T (no modifications) and used spinach, not mustard greens. As someone who has loved Indian food her entire life married to someone who also has, this was a hit.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I don’t have a particularly high tolerance for spice, but I found that adding an extra 1 or 2 Serrano peppers and doubling the cayenne to 1/2tsp made for a tastier dish. I do scoop the centers out of the chilis but try to leave plenty of seeds. Also use silken tofu and just dump the whole block in and break it up with the spatula - great way to make it feel like there’s cheese in the recipe while keeping it vegan!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Priya is the goat!! I used extra lime (because I love lime) and frozen spinach and it came out so delicious. Tofu makes a good paneer substitute for those who are dairy free / don’t have access to paneer!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Brown cubes of paneer in ghee and use in place of tofu

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Really good but use spinach!! Wayyy too strong with just mustard greens

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: This was easy and amazing! I doubled the cardamom though and skipped the peppers

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Use queso panela instead of paneer.  Just as good, cheaper and easier to find in supermarkets.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I used silver beet and kale and it turned out very stringy, wish there was more guidance on which greens to use/not use  Its also quite bland, if youre making this (not for a child) - I would recommend amping up the spices!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Prepared per directions. Used an emulsifier to blend. Would highly recommended. 👍

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I have a lot of fresh chard from a friend's garden. Do you think I could use that instead of mustard greens? Thanks for your thoughts.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I just did. And it was delish.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Loved Priya's video, could watch her all day and now purchasing "Indian-ish" her cookbook asap. Saag is my favorite dish, using mustard greens is a new spin that may be too spicy for me. I am a paneer devotee. And will use an immersion blender! Can't wait to make it.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: what to substitute for tofu?  Comments keep mentioning paneer or even mexican panela but there is no cheese in the original recipe? huh

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
@love greens
Halloumi!!!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: This was great, my only comment was that it needed a little sweetness to round things out a little. I added a little honey and sherry vinegar at the end, but next time I might add a small handful of halved sungold tomatoes with the onions so they could break down a bit and then get blended into the sauce for a similar effect.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: The taste was great, but I didn’t like the grittiness of the whole spices. I wish I’d read the comments first! Definitely grind them before adding to the sauce.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Could you explain what you mean by coconut milk? She covered the can in the video.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: "international" isle of grocery store will have canned coconut milk (and cream and lite milk).  Pick the milk (vs the cream etc)

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I don’t write too many comments, but I saw one that said using mustard greens made it too bitter. I made this last night with mustard greens and it was delicious! Not bitter at all! For what it’s worth, I also didn’t feel like finding/buying whole spices so I just used ground coriander (added after the onions cooked with the garlic and ginger) and it was very delicious! Would highly recommend and make again!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I wanted to try this, despite having no blender. This appears to be an be one of those times a food processor really isn’t a good substitute: Go with the recommended tool! It makes for a much smoother purée, judging by my results. The flavours are appealing and balanced. My sauce granular, rather than smooth. More pesto than saag.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Way too much coconut milk. Ends up watery and thin. Next time I would use half a can.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Thank you for this easy and delicious recipe. I was making it ahead to freeze for company, so stirred the coconut oil and spices in with the rest instead of drizzling it over the top. Will add paneer when reheating to serve. I wanted to mention, I used ground spices instead of whole (coriander, cardamon and cumin), adjusting the quantities accordingly, and the dish still tastes wonderful. You're right, the asafetida adds a wonderful zing.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Made this with mustard greens and it wasn't a hit with the family... but I liked it. I will try it with spinach next time.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I’ve made this recipe several times and every time it’s delicious! I’ve made it primarily with spinach but have used paneer, feta, and tofu and all are delicious (feta might be my favorite!). This is such a luxurious yet simple meal and scratches the itch when I’m craving takeout saag. And comes together super easily with mostly pantry items. Cannot recommend enough!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Why would anyone want to eat an entire pound of greens at one time?

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: My iron-deficient body thanks you. This one is definitely going in the rotation.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I sent my husband to the store for a box of fresh Spinach.  He came home with salad greens, as he didn't look at the not so fine print... I used it anyway, why not?  It turned out great! Haha! Who knew?

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Really delicious but needs salt. I didn’t put any in, rather I let each person salt to taste.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Where is the garam masala! Added 1 tsp at the end. Chef Yamuna Devi adds that and paprika.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I love Indian food, especially this dish.  I blanched the spinach before pureeing to remove the oxalates by pouring out the water.  I followed the recipe completely including the asafetida which I doubled finding that the entire recipe needed more spices.  The subtle hints of flavor just are not enough to replicate the full spicy loveliness of this dish when you eat it at an Indian restaurant.  I used my blender and wish I hadn't.  In the end it was like spinach puree.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I made this with tofu and also added some fried halloumi on top at the end. Absolutely delicious.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I like the coconut in this but I would prefer toasting and grinding the spices. Is it more authentic to leave them whole? Did not go over well in our house

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Followed recipe exactly and cooked with fresh spinach, but did not make the coconut oil mixture. That coconut oil mixture might have elevated it a bit. Solid dish.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: What do people think about using pre-washed power greens that are pretty tender and small. Do people think it would be without enough texture?

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I love Indian food but so far I'm a terrible Indian cook. I will try this again using less coconut milk and more greens. I put in plenty of spices, but they didn't seem to sink into the greens. I always season greens with vinegar. I think I'm going to try that here. I also think I put in too much cheese. I just didn't get the proportions right, although I followed the recipe closely.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Don’t use an immersion blender if you’re using whole spices! A good blender is needed to get all of the spices not chunky / crunchy in the final product.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: The recipe worked for me as is, with baby spinach. Beautiful bright green, wonderful flavor. Only needed twice the salt bc of tofu, and would crush the cumin seed before toasting. Otherwise great recipe, easy to follow for my first try making saag.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I made it again following recipe (first time I “did it my way”). It’s a winner anyway you make it. I only thing is, this time I used turnip greens. It was a bit bitter so I added a little sweetener (Korean corn syrup because it was on hand). It cut the bitterness and gave the dish a beautiful sheen. I served it with a Bengali pulao and Punjabi pappadums   Delish!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
Loved the video!
The coconut milk and heated cumin seeds completely overwhelmed what would have otherwise been delicious mustard greens.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I’ve made this recipe twice, once with paneer and the other time with tofu, using fresh spinach and subbing powdered spices for most of the seed suggestions. I would recommend doctoring it with some chili crisp—you could maybe even skip the chili sauce the recipe calls for. My take is that paneer is a lot better than tofu, because the latter is too bland and stands out compared to the delicious spinach sauce. Perhaps the tofu could be marinated in soy sauce or salted to better effect? In any case, it’s a great recipe!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Thank you Priya for this recipe! It’s in regular rotation in our house. I use fresh baby spinach, and the vibrant green color is so wonderful. I use a Vitamix and have not had the issues with unpleasant texture from the spices. I do tend to amp up the spices, ginger, and garlic a bit. For such a flavor-packed, gorgeous dish, it comes together pretty quickly and easily. I also love the video with Priya and her cookbook!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Very weird tasting dish.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: This turned out great! I didn't deviate from the recipe at all.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: needed more salt and double amount of spices, also would cut the tofu in smaller cubes

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: This sounds delicious, but has anyone figured out a way to make it simpler? I detest cleaning the blender, and having to heat the coconut oil separately just means another thing to wash. What if I used frozen spinach (as others have suggested), defrost it and chop it up first before adding?

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Question: How well does this “keep”?  Can I make ahead and keep warm on the stove and serve a couple of hours later with just a little reheat?

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Any suggestions for replacing the cumin? I really want to make this (with tofu), but my wife simply cannot abide the smell of cumin. Sometimes I use coriander seeds as a substitute, but I know how key cumin is to this dish.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Made with a packet of frozen spinach ( what I had on hand) and added a bit of chopped shrimp, easy and a keeper of a recipe. So healthy and quick, yum

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I had a bunch of greens (spinach, bok choy, kale) from my CSA that I needed to use and then this recipe popped up. Perfect timing! I wasn't sure how it would all turn out, but when I tasted the "chunky paste," I said (out loud, to myself), "ooooh, this is GOOD!" I will make it with paneer next time, and add a dash of turmeric and more cayenne at the end. Surprisingly light, given the coconut milk and oil. I served over Basmati rice. Will make again!!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I make a similar Saag all the time but always with frozen spinach. I see mustard greens in the store but never knew what to do with them. Despite my loving bitter flavors, the greens were way too bitter even for me. Almost inedible. Wow. Maybe the NYT test kitchen used a different variety. I’m not a fussy eater. I love aggressive flavors but I have to say never again to this recipe.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Delicious! I used kale, spinach, cilantro, and radish greens. Great way to use up a bunch of miscellaneous greens. I added a touch of honey to offset some of the bitterness. Agree with the recommendation to grind the whole spices before toasting - my blender wouldn't have been able to blitz them effectively otherwise. The tofu works surprisingly well in lieu of paneer.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: This was really delicious.  But yes, crush the spices.  My immersion blender wasn't up to that task, and there was a bit too much woody, crunchy stuff.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Really tasty and really healthy with complex flavors. Will definitely make again.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Do you think this would be good with 1/2 baby spinach and 1/2 collard greens?

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I followed the recipe, except I didn’t have asafetida and subbed ground coriander for seeds. It was good, but not great. I think it needs more lime (at least one whole lime), and I will also try half mustard greens and half spinach. The mustard greens were just too much for us.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: While I tend to always make a recipe as written first time out, I sure wish I'd read the comments for this one. The flavor was delicious, but I spent most of the meal trying to discreetly pick whole coriander seeds off my tongue. Two tablespoons! I should've known better. Next time will either toast and finely grind, or simply use ground coriander (but less, maybe one tablespoon in each case). Otherwise, this is a keeper. (I did top with a sprinkle of salt and more lime juice. Yum.)

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I used fresh spinach and otherwise followed the recipe. Too watery and not enough spice flavor. I agree with others that the whole spices make it gritty. My husband didn’t finish his portion. I wouldn’t make this again.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I think I must have done something very wrong. Mine did not turn out like all of yours. I used 3 lbs of mustard greens instead of 1 lb but I did not think it would have that much effect.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Cumin and Cashew Yogurt Rice Recipe
commentBody: This is thayir sadham, a comfort food in most south Indian homes.  I find basmati rice, which I love in other recipes, not as appropriate for this dish as a neutral-flavored long grained rice. Instead of ghee, I use Indian sesame oil, omit the cashew nuts, and add a pinch of mustard seeds while roasting the cumin seeds.  The asafoetida is a must; it introduces a complex depth to the rice.  I add a sprig of curry leaves to the seasoned oil before adding it to the rice.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Cumin and Cashew Yogurt Rice Recipe
commentBody:
This is not only Southern Indian comfort food, its also classic end of the meal dish to eat.

Couple points: #1. For a soft pudding like consistency its important to cook the rice to be soft with extra water. While its still warm, mash some of the rice grains(1/2 cup). #2. Various fresh veggies are added in moderation, like red onions diced small, carrots shredded, pomegranate seeds. These are added for flavor and visual appeal ;-)

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Cumin and Cashew Yogurt Rice Recipe
commentBody:
I appreciate the author notes with the recipes, and this one especially. When I told my family what I made for dinner, they initially looked dubious. When I told them a South Asian culture would call this comfort food, they immediately opened up to it.
I’m not South Asian, but it’s easy to see why it’s comforting. 
We won’t overdo this in our rotation. But I will certainly roll this out on some cold winter day when the pantry is bare and we just want a good bowl of something fast and satisfying.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Cumin and Cashew Yogurt Rice Recipe
commentBody: I recommend adding the "traditional" ingredients that are mentioned, as well. My son is allergic to cashews, so we add peanuts instead. Just as delicious.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Cumin and Cashew Yogurt Rice Recipe
commentBody: Love seeing Thayir Sadham (Yogurt Rice) on NYT cooking! My kids love it and it’s so easy to make. Truly a South Indian, Tamil, comfort food. I grew up eating with long grain rice but prefer with basmati rice -it doesn’t absorb as much of the yogurt so it’s a little thinner. Literally just rice, mixed with yogurt, salt and tempered. I use a bit of oil, then throw in black mustard, dried red Chile or two, urad dhal, curry leaves from Indian store. When mustard pops, add & top with fresh cilantro

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Cumin and Cashew Yogurt Rice Recipe
commentBody:
Thayir saadham is classic comfort food! Had a bowl almost every single day of my childhood. At its simplest it’s just plain cooked rice mixed with plain yogurt with some salt, and this is it at perhaps its fanciest.

An in-between simplified version of this calls for the same cooking preparation, but use of just ooruga (jarred pickle - what North Indians would call aachar) as a topping instead of the crunchy goodness offered here.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Cumin and Cashew Yogurt Rice Recipe
commentBody: Re: adding meat, I would say that, like any cooking, you can add anything you like. Reading this recipe, I think the key ingredients are the rice and yoghurt, with spices and nuts stir fried and added on top of the rice. But if you wanted to add other meats and veggies or different nuts or spices, you could undoubtedly do so. If you want to stick to an Indian tradition, you might consider lamb or chicken. If you add beef or pork, you'd be really straying. But these are personal choices.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Cumin and Cashew Yogurt Rice Recipe
commentBody: You can of course add anything to anything, but if you add animal protein to this Indian vegetarian dish, you are basically making it something entirely different.  Sometimes it's ok to just enjoy a vegetarian dish in its simplicity.   Just saying...

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Cumin and Cashew Yogurt Rice Recipe
commentBody: This is lovely!   South Indian curd rice is what it is.  The traditional way is better, as described in the posts by readers of Indian origin.  To which I'd add mine:  it is incomplete without a spoonful of Indian lemon or mango pickle, which you can get in Indian grocery stores

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Cumin and Cashew Yogurt Rice Recipe
commentBody:
Re: asafoetida:
Asafoetida is the dried latex exuded from the rhizome or tap root of several species of Ferula, perennial herbs of the carrot family.
I'm not sure if one needs to avoid if one has a latex allergy ( I would) but it is useful to be aware of its origin.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Cumin and Cashew Yogurt Rice Recipe
commentBody: Vixen, you may want to fry them in the ghee with the cashews and the chile to release flavor if you want to add the curry leaves

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Cumin and Cashew Yogurt Rice Recipe
commentBody: Math is not my forte....3 cups of cooked rice is NOT the same as cooking 3 cups of rice. I hope it is a great comfort food as we will be comforted all week!

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Cumin and Cashew Yogurt Rice Recipe
commentBody: If you want grated zucchini or carrots, should they be added raw to the rice? Or cooked separately for a short while in the saucepan with ghee/unsalted butter, before the cashews, etc. are added?

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Cumin and Cashew Yogurt Rice Recipe
commentBody: This was really yummy. I cooked some minced shallots and minced carrots in the pan I had cooked the nuts in until they were al dente. It was delicious!  We had it for breakfast, as a protein rich alternative to eggs. Definitely on the make again list!

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Cumin and Cashew Yogurt Rice Recipe
commentBody: @Katherine—this is a great idea! I’m much more of a savory breakfast person and I need a break from eggs. I’ll definitely give this a try.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Cumin and Cashew Yogurt Rice Recipe
commentBody: Definitely a comfort food for most South Indians like me! A few tips: rice should be somewhat overcooked (add more water for cooking than normal) and mashed when still warm; add a mixture of milk and yogurt to the rice, the milk prevents the yogurt rice turning sour; the yogurt rice should be a loose mixture, adjust liquid accordingly; include mustard seeds in the tempering, an absolute must! Finally, the accompaniment for this rice is a lemon pickle (could be store bought)! All set now!

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Cumin and Cashew Yogurt Rice Recipe
commentBody: The consistency of the rice is really important. It needs to be soft and mashed up a bit before adding the yogurt, because you want the rice to absorb the yogurt flavor. Also, as another post mentioned, long grain rice is preferable to basmati rice, and the asafoetida is a must . I usually add the ginger in with the chile in the hot oil. Americanized versions include adding some sour cream or buttermilk. Either will enhance the flavor.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Cumin and Cashew Yogurt Rice Recipe
commentBody: God forgive me, I used jasmine rice instead of basmati. The cumin, cashews, serranos and   ghee are a remarkably flavorful combination. If I was doing it over again - and I really might - I would make a bit more of the cumin and cashew mix.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Cumin and Cashew Yogurt Rice Recipe
commentBody: In India is this eaten at room temperature like a rice salad? Or is the finished dish rewarmed before it's served?

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Cumin and Cashew Yogurt Rice Recipe
commentBody: Room temp

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Cumin and Cashew Yogurt Rice Recipe
commentBody: This is my family’s comfort food. I would offer a few suggestions. We make this dish with sona masoori rice, cook the rice with water, milk and ghee. Mash the rice with a little milk before adding the yogurt. Sautee some chopped  cilantro along with the cashews. We have dadhojanam bar where I make this rice and serve toppings in individual bowls so that ppl can customize. Toppings include finely diced cucumber, pomegranate seeds, lime and mango pickles, papads

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Cumin and Cashew Yogurt Rice Recipe
commentBody: @ps sounds delicious— love the idea of offering options to customize! Thanks for this!

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Cumin and Cashew Yogurt Rice Recipe
commentBody: Yogurt is also very protein-rich.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Cumin and Cashew Yogurt Rice Recipe
commentBody: This was a surprise hit! I might add peas next time? Served w/ TJ’s samosas.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Cumin and Cashew Yogurt Rice Recipe
commentBody: Is this served at room temperature? I don’t see where it gets heated.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Cumin and Cashew Yogurt Rice Recipe
commentBody: This is a wonderful dish! So easy to make. Next time I will add a little less yoghurt and grilled zucchini!

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Cumin and Cashew Yogurt Rice Recipe
commentBody: Perhaps a silly question, but would you add the grated veggies to the rice before cooking or after?

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Cumin and Cashew Yogurt Rice Recipe
