commentBody: Add veggies after, like grated carrot, cucumbers, finely chopped red onions Etc.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Cumin and Cashew Yogurt Rice Recipe
commentBody: Has anyone made this with brown rice? I try to avoid white rice but this sounds so yummy!

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Cumin and Cashew Yogurt Rice Recipe
commentBody: Yes, certainly. You may want to make the brown rice mushy and cook it in a pressure cooker.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Cumin and Cashew Yogurt Rice Recipe
commentBody: This can be easily made vegan with vegan yogurt and some vegan sour cream. Grated carrot and finely diced cucumber can be added. Curry leaf and hing (which the British with condescension have called something to show off their imperialist arrogance) will greatly enrich the taste. Tempering with finely diced green chillies and mustard and perhaps some urad dal (in peanut oil rather than ghee)  makes this heavenly. The hing must be added to the hot used to temper.  Cashews may be best avoided.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Cumin and Cashew Yogurt Rice Recipe
commentBody: anyone made and served with this hot rice?

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Cumin and Cashew Yogurt Rice Recipe
commentBody: yes, and it was delicious!

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Cumin and Cashew Yogurt Rice Recipe
commentBody: What happens if you use non-fat yogurt?

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Cumin and Cashew Yogurt Rice Recipe
commentBody: I used Chobani non-fat and it worked perfectly well. Like many commenters, I would have preferred either more rice or less yogurt, but it was really good as it was.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Cumin and Cashew Yogurt Rice Recipe
commentBody: I subbed pistachios for cashews, cumin powder for seeds and candied ginger for fresh and it was still delicious.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Cumin and Cashew Yogurt Rice Recipe
commentBody: I loved this dish. I used less yogurt, and I sauteed onions, carrots and edemame in with the butter (didn't have ghee), cashews and spices. It was so good. Probably not very much like the original recipe, but I would never have added yogurt to rice, and it turns out, it's fabulous.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Cumin and Cashew Yogurt Rice Recipe
commentBody: A note to those with diabetes or carbohydrate intolerance, this has 122gm of carbohydrates. Yes, it is yummy and adding extra nuts may reduce the glycemic index.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Cumin and Cashew Yogurt Rice Recipe
commentBody: Decrease yogurt amount by half.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Cumin and Cashew Yogurt Rice Recipe
commentBody: I found the flavour of this dish to be wonderful but it was a little wet (yogurt liquid comes out when plated). I used regular 3.5% fat yogurt, not greek yogurt. What type of yogurt works best? Regular or greek. I also used brown basmati rice which may not have been as absorbent.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Cumin and Cashew Yogurt Rice Recipe
commentBody: Made this as written, only with nonfat yogurt, which I bought out of habit. It is excellent. Next time, I will add more rice (or less yogurt) and double (at least) the cashews. Important: I learned that if I'm using butter, I cannot get the butter hot enough to brown the cashews and cumin without browning the butter. Next time, I will brown the cashews in a dry pan, then add a touch of oil to cook cumin seed, then add the butter, chile, etc. And I like the idea of adding some curry leaves.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Cumin and Cashew Yogurt Rice Recipe
commentBody: I followed the recipe closely (minus asafetida, plus some chopped raw green pepper from the garden added at the end) and found this to be underwhelming, perhaps because I did not have asafetida, or maybe I should have reduced the amount of yoghurt. I kept thinking it was missing something.  When I try the leftovers tomorrow, I’ll add cooked garlic and shallots to the mix and perhaps add some additional crunchy veggies too.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Cumin and Cashew Yogurt Rice Recipe
commentBody: I had the astifetida and still found it missing something.  I may heat some up with my breakfast and add a fried egg.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Cumin and Cashew Yogurt Rice Recipe
commentBody: Comforting indeed! I used the introductory comments as well as reader comments to do a little improvisation.  Rather than making ghee, used Julie Child's astuce of butter & oil together to prevent burning.  Spices were: cumin seed, black mustard seed, powdered curry leaf, ground fermented white pepper, and a bit of black lime. I added chopped green beans and shrimp to make the meal more substantial.  I dry-toasted the cashews in a cast iron skillet for a few minutes before adding to the pot.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Cumin and Cashew Yogurt Rice Recipe
commentBody: This recipe is good, but you need to quadruple the cashews. A quarter cup is like 5 cashews. Also it does not take 5 minutes to make.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Cumin and Cashew Yogurt Rice Recipe
commentBody: This is great! I thought it would be kind of bland but it had a lovely kick once it was all mixed together.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Cumin and Cashew Yogurt Rice Recipe
commentBody: Vegan here! So for creaminess and tang, I subbed 1 can of coconut milk and some lemon juice instead of the yogurt. I don’t have Southern Indian food nostalgia but this easy comfort food recipe has already created nostalgia of its own.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Cumin and Cashew Yogurt Rice Recipe
commentBody: So funny!  This sounds wonderful and I'm going to try it.  BUT... I've had a small jar of asafetida in my spice cupboard for many years, since a friend and I had a Indian cooking weekend.  Do I have it now?  Of course not.  I tossed it, a month ago, when doing a big kitchen pantry clean out.  LOL.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Cumin and Cashew Yogurt Rice Recipe
commentBody: I find it somewhat humorous with a pinch of agitation that a recipe that takes 5 minutes to prepare and 5 minutes to cook calls for 3 cups of cooked rice taken to room temperature. This cannot be done in 5 minutes…..

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Cumin and Cashew Yogurt Rice Recipe
commentBody: Liked it but found a whole serrano and a full tablespoon of cumin seeds kinda ovrrwhelmed the recipe serving size. Yes, I'm Irish  okay?

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Cumin and Cashew Yogurt Rice Recipe
commentBody: I actually have a curry leaf plant. Any suggestions on how to use it in this dish?

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Cumin and Cashew Yogurt Rice Recipe
commentBody: Add to the ghee after cashews are browned.  Or after cumin seeds.  Just to get a little "fried" and add their flavor to the ghee. It is better to not let them brown.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Cumin and Cashew Yogurt Rice Recipe
commentBody: Oh, you're lucky! Curry leaves are part of the spices tempered (fried) together. Add them after the cumin, make sure they don't burn, and they become part of the spice mixture added to the yogurt-rice. Enjoy!

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Cumin and Cashew Yogurt Rice Recipe
commentBody: Chiffonade a few leaves like basil and add them to the ghee either instead of or alongside the cumin seeds. Personally, I would use both. Idea for others: we get fresh curry leaves from a local spice store and keep them in the freezer. They last about six months.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Cumin and Cashew Yogurt Rice Recipe
commentBody: That's a LOT of yogurt! Okay by me, but my husband is doubtful. Nonetheless, going to try it. Sounds like this dish might resemble a risotto. Is that right?

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Cumin and Cashew Yogurt Rice Recipe
commentBody: I wonder about the amount of yogurt also. Did you make it?  What did you think?  Thanks.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Cumin and Cashew Yogurt Rice Recipe
commentBody: Can you add some protein to this dish?

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Cumin and Cashew Yogurt Rice Recipe
commentBody: The nuts are protein, but I'm sure it would work with chicken or shrimp?

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Cumin and Cashew Yogurt Rice Recipe
commentBody: There's protein in yogurt.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Cumin and Cashew Yogurt Rice Recipe
commentBody: Greek yogurt is a complete protein. Cashews are also an excellent source

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Cumin and Cashew Yogurt Rice Recipe
commentBody: Although I can look up asafetida, it would be helpful is you explain what part it plays in this recipe.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Cumin and Cashew Yogurt Rice Recipe
commentBody:
From my experience with Krishna Lunch at the University of Florida, it appears many Hindus and Buddhists do not use alliums, such as onion and garlic. I have found they use asafetida as a pungent substitute. It’s aroma is also quite pungent, but it’s an interesting flavor.
You could probably substitute onion and/or garlic if desired.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Cumin and Cashew Yogurt Rice Recipe
commentBody: I only have non-fat greek yogurt on hand. Will the recipe still work?

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Cumin and Cashew Yogurt Rice Recipe
commentBody: Do you need to use that much yogurt, or can you do a lighter coating? Lactose intolerant, but really want to try this!

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Cumin and Cashew Yogurt Rice Recipe
commentBody: You can use Kitehouse almond yogurt which is excellent.  I'm lactose intolerant too and this is a great substitute for yogurt

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Cumin and Cashew Yogurt Rice Recipe
commentBody: I would like to make this, but don't have curry leaves or a local source for them. What is a good substitute? I did not find one in the Times substitute ingredient list. Thanks so much.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Cumin and Cashew Yogurt Rice Recipe
commentBody: If you don't have curry leaves, just omit. There's really no substitute that I can think of. You can just add some chopped coriander leaves at the end and mix it in. Thats easier to find. Do not put the coriander leaves in the hot oil though. It will get a bitter taste.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Cumin and Cashew Yogurt Rice Recipe
commentBody: My kids' favorite. It's a very simple dish and great to see it on NYT Cooking :)

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Cumin and Cashew Yogurt Rice Recipe
commentBody: Would this be freezable? I've never tried to freeze yogurty dishes before but it sounds like it could be a godsend on a cold night.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Cumin and Cashew Yogurt Rice Recipe
commentBody: I don't think this would freeze well.  The consistency would probably not be too pleasing once it thawed.  Also, this is meant to be eaten cool / room temperature, something like the way one might eat potato salad or pasta salad.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Cumin and Cashew Yogurt Rice Recipe
commentBody: Do leftovers from this recipe reheat well the next day? Or not so much?

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Cumin and Cashew Yogurt Rice Recipe
commentBody: No need to reheat - this is a cool / room temperature food.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Cumin and Cashew Yogurt Rice Recipe
commentBody: I didn't have asafetida and added several cloves of sliced garlic but must confess that I wasn't very impressed, found it spicy which was fine but lacking in body, just quite dull.  Plenty left over so I'll definitely add red onions tomorrow and may even try some of my home-made pickled jalapenos in the mix!

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Cumin and Cashew Yogurt Rice Recipe
commentBody: Can you make this with vegan plain yogurt?  Cashew or coconut based?

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Cumin and Cashew Yogurt Rice Recipe
commentBody: Is the yogurt meant to be room temp as well?

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Cumin and Cashew Yogurt Rice Recipe
commentBody: I always use hot rice (freshly made or reheated) and yogurt from the refrigerator. This results in a dish that is not hot or cold. More like room temperature, which makes it perfect for hot summer days.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Cumin and Cashew Yogurt Rice Recipe
commentBody: The cashews cooked and became chewy but didn't brown. I didn't have fresh ginger or garlic, so I used ginger and garlic paste. it was good but under spiced somehow. Also I am more a sweet lover, so since I made a huge batch for a potluck, I am going to concentrate the topping in the leftovers that I freeze, take a good amount of the plain yogurt rice below, add cream, coconut and splenda and make a sweet rice pudding. Two out of one.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Cumin and Cashew Yogurt Rice Recipe
commentBody: This reminds me of really good cottage cheese. I like it very much either hot or cold. I will make more of the topping next time.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Cumin and Cashew Yogurt Rice Recipe
commentBody: What a delightful dish!  Served for brunch to curious friends who tripled dipped!  Added some small cubed potatoes that were browned in a bit of butter then bits of the last summer tomatoes around the platter’s edge for a pop of color. The cashews and crisped cumin seeds added a lot of texture.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Cumin and Cashew Yogurt Rice Recipe
commentBody: I only had the general categories of ingredients for this recipe available and it mocks up really well. I used all in the same relative amounts and the same order of preparation. The inspired result will be used as a small side to boost flavors and put some surprise in packed lunches. So GOOD!

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Cumin and Cashew Yogurt Rice Recipe
commentBody: Add the tip of a teaspoon of cilantro chutney if you don’t have fresh cilantro. I didn’t have asafetida but I did have curry leaves, which are perfect for this. Also, I can confirm that peanuts are a good substitute for cashews here.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Cumin and Cashew Yogurt Rice Recipe
commentBody: I made this with brown basmati, since that was what I had, and it was just fine. I doubled the ginger and used toasted cashews, not liking cooked cashews.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Cumin and Cashew Yogurt Rice Recipe
commentBody: How is it with brown rice, long grain or basmati?

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Cumin and Cashew Yogurt Rice Recipe
commentBody: I fried some raisins along with the cashews. Yummy!

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Cumin and Cashew Yogurt Rice Recipe
commentBody: Made this the other night and found it bland.  I used plenty of fresh ginger but wonder if sauting the ginger would have helped.  Would make half the rice next time.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Cumin and Cashew Yogurt Rice Recipe
commentBody: If it was bland it was probably undersalted.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Cumin and Cashew Yogurt Rice Recipe
commentBody: Delicious but recommend using about half the amount of yogurt and add more cashews.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Cumin and Cashew Yogurt Rice Recipe
commentBody:
I added some chopped red onion and it was delightful! Would salt the cashew, pepper,
Cumin, ghee mixture rather than just salting the rice as well.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Cumin and Cashew Yogurt Rice Recipe
commentBody: Very quick if ingredients are on hand.  Not sure what it's supposed to taste like,  but our version was very tasty.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Cumin and Cashew Yogurt Rice Recipe
commentBody: This recipe is so incredibly simple, tasty and beautiful.  Thank you!

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Cumin and Cashew Yogurt Rice Recipe
commentBody: Make it easy on yourself. Use any rice other than basmati. It is delicate, so it does not take well to folding and does not have enough starch to give pudding like consistency.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Cumin and Cashew Yogurt Rice Recipe
commentBody: My mom used to always add halved grapes. Delicious.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Cumin and Cashew Yogurt Rice Recipe
commentBody: This went over well. Like others, I threw I. A few curry leaves and wished I’d done some mustard seeds. But good, quick, and worth doing again.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Cumin and Cashew Yogurt Rice Recipe
commentBody: Tasty, easy, filling lunch! Be careful not to mince your chile too fine as I did - it ended up cooking completely before the cashews were close to being toasted. I also added a small splash of water to the yogurt and rice to make it a little looser and more rice pudding-like. Will make again!

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Cumin and Cashew Yogurt Rice Recipe
commentBody: This was a tasty dish if not particularly pretty to look at. I doubled the cashews, doubled the cayenne because I did not have a Chile pepper.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Cumin and Cashew Yogurt Rice Recipe
commentBody: I used one cup less yogurt and it was plenty. Another cup would have made it soupy. I’m not sure why I don’t see others posting this.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Cumin and Cashew Yogurt Rice Recipe
commentBody: Can you please share more about adding carrots and zucchini? Would they be cooked or raw?

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Cumin and Cashew Yogurt Rice Recipe
commentBody: Same question from me. I cooked cabbage and carrots with the Serranos and cashews.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Cumin and Cashew Yogurt Rice Recipe
commentBody: Maybe I’m missing something, but I can’t figure out if this is to be served hot, cold, or somewhere in between. Help!

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Cumin and Cashew Yogurt Rice Recipe
commentBody: @Peggy the rice mixture is room temp or cool, and the spice-cashew mix is hot. Hope that helps!

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Cumin and Cashew Yogurt Rice Recipe
commentBody: What about the ginger? The recipe doesn’t mention it

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Cumin and Cashew Yogurt Rice Recipe
commentBody: This just wasn't my jam.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Cumin and Cashew Yogurt Rice Recipe
commentBody: My rice was made with light coconut milk. I was also about a cup short of yogurt, but I didn’t miss it. I’m still very much disinterested in long ingredient lists and spending hours in a hot kitchen. This was easy to pull together in under 30 minutes. (That includes washing, boiling, and cooking the rice.) I like the crunch of the topping against the cool yogurt rice. I’ll keep this in my back pocket for hot summer days.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Cumin and Cashew Yogurt Rice Recipe
commentBody: for added level of depth and texture eat with potato chips, both flavored and classic work well. experiment with different flavors to see what you like best!

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Cumin and Cashew Yogurt Rice Recipe
commentBody: Made this at least a dozen times but adding corn tonight was a new hit! Had a couple of ears kicking around so I shaved off the kernels and cooked them up in the ghee before the cashews. So good!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
When adding cardamom pods or whole cloves when I cook I always count how many I
put in and try to find them before serving.   Works most of the time.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Me too! The kids make a game of finding them all.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Add thinly sliced 2-3 cloves of garlic with the sliced onion. Garnish with fresh tomatoes, diced or halved if using small ones.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: This was delicious! I prepped the vegetables ahead of starting the dish, as it comes together quickly.  The only modification I made was substituting low sodium vegetable stock for the water in the recipe. I served with the NYT’s recipe for Cumin Lime Shrimp with Ginger, along with naan and a side of cantaloupe and berries.  Will definitely make again.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: We enjoyed this delicious well spiced dish. Paired with a protein it is a full meal

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Make sure to add some pickles on the side (mango, sour) for a kick.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: This was a great recipe. Very easy and very flexible to make. Only took about ten minutes. I used leftover brown rice , butter instead of ghee, cumin powder because I didn't have seeds,  green cardamom, red pepper flakes because I didn't have the chili and left out the potatoes. Next time I may add chick peas. I cracked the peppercorns and cardamom.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Does one pick the whole spices out of the completed dish? Do they dissolve?  Could one grind the spices first and then sauté them?

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: They don't dissolve. You need to set them aside while eating.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: You get a different flavor if you grind the whole spices

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I picked out the bay, cinnamon, and cardamom pods, which did not dissolve.  However, the whole cumin and peppercorns were not really noticeable in the finished dish.  I think that if I took the cardamom seeds out of the pods, they might have softened a bit - not sure though.  I did use whole seeds in a recipe once where they disappeared, but I think that that recipe cooked them much longer.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I have not made this yet but I intend to, and I plan to put the peas in only in the last few minutes off heat as I think they'll be very overcooked otherwise.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Really delicious! Added some tomato paste and minced garlic after cooking the onions which added even more depth of flavor

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Good, belly filling, not terribly exciting. Benefited considerably from a spritz of lime at serving time. Adding some chunks of protein would be nice, too.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I also used butter instead of ghee (which I did not have). And I separately pan fried some tofu, and just added it in after the dish was done. We ate it with yogurt and chutney.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: My family enjoyed this!  I used sweet potatoes and cauliflower, as suggested in the Intro.  And I used a cup of broth instead of water.  Otherwise, I followed the recipe, and it came out great, with just the right amount of kick.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: If you use brown basmati instead of white plan on double the cooking time, maybe pre-cook. Otherwise, delicious.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
Four stars because I made it with a ton of modifications, so no idea how the actual recipe is:
Bloomed 1/8 tsp hing (asafoetida) in ghee to start, omitted onions; used all the whole spices plus 3 cloves, 3 green cardamom, Four-Pepper blend (green white black red), 1/2 tsp ground smoked black cardamom, 1/2 tsp Black Urfa Chili, 1/2 tsp ground Prasad curry leaf; sauteed one fresh green garlic plant; small bunch swiss chard chopped instead of green peas; 1-2 T fresh lemon juice. Delicious.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Maybe it’s the quality of my spices…but this filled my house with such a delicious aroma and then tasted like absolutely nothing.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: We enjoyed this, but it certainly does smell more fragrant than it tastes. Before serving, I added probably 2 to 3 more large pinches of salt to amp up the flavor. With a little sriracha, it worked for us. Recipe halves easily for two people, with one serving of leftovers.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: This was good but not as flavorful as I’d hoped for. Made as is (including Serrano).

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Add two chicken thighs, sautéing first in ghee and then adding back in to simmer with rice. Use a bag of frozen mixed vegetables in place of carrots and peas, for convenience.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: How would you adjust the process to use brown basmati rice?  Is that possible?  Is the best approach to cook the rice until it only needs 15 more minutes and then add it to this recipe?

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I doubled the quantities and followed the recipe as written. Sadly it turned out mush!  Next time I will used already cooked rice and add to the vegetables at the end. Also, it’s not just the cardamom pods that have to be picked out. There are lots of peppercorns that can be a surprise to bite into.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Can oil or lard be used instead of ghee?

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: You can definitely use a neutral oil instead of ghee. The taste will be less rich. You could add a pat of butter to the finished dish to get a richer taste.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Made with 1/2 med cauliflower, 1 C carrots, 1 C peas, a large jalapeno and a white fleshed sweet potato; all other ingredients and proportions as per recipe. Very good but recommend larger chunks (1") and cutting saute time for sweet potato to no more than 2 minutes. I cut in 1/2" cubes and sauteed for 4 minutes and they were pasty by the time the rice was cooked.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: This is a great template. I didn't have carrots, so used a lot of potatoes I wanted to use up. The whole spices are hard for Americans to get used to, but you generally just don't eat them, or if you accidentally eat a peppercorn or cardamom pod, no harm done, just a surprise burst of flavor! I also didn't have a hot pepper, so it was very gently spiced. But we made up for it with condiments!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
Thank you for this. 
Which condiments did you use?

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Condiments: Yogurt. Chopped green onions. Any green herb (like cilantro). Walk through an Indian grocer or the “Ethnic” aisle at your grocery store and grab the little jars of chutney or pickled condiments.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: @Lauren Tamarind chutney and mint or cilantro chutney are great. At an Indian store, but I can even find them sometimes at the regular grocery store here in Vermont…

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Could you use brown rice?

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I did and found I should have pre-cooked it or doubled the cooking time at least.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: How do I adjust this recipe for brown rice?

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
I found that even with the lovely whole spices this didn't taste like much. It's possible that my spices (though not old) aren't the best quality? 

I used Diamond Crystal kosher salt and I found that the recommended amount wasn't nearly enough for my taste. Given that they mentioned Morton, perhaps the recipe was developed with Morton, in which case I understand it might be best to use double the amount of Diamond Crystal.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: The taste was not very robust in this recipe. Had to salt and spice it up. Didn't have the cardamom and cumin seeds though.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: We really enjoyed this one pot dish! Lots of texture and color on a weeknight and leftovers were delicious. I added half a block of cubed tofu during the final stirring before adding water. I might hold the peas back and try adding them later since they didn’t stay vibrant. Served with Greek yogurt and Priya’s excellent Green Chile & Cherry Tomato Pickle which added spice, brightness, and richness.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Fabulous.  Perfectly seasoned, I served it with tandoori fried chicken and it was great.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Very flavorful and delicious! I misunderstood the concept of "weeknight staple in Desi households" to mean quick, which this recipe was not for me. It's not difficult but there are multiple steps and you need to allot the time for them - soaking the rice, tempering spices, sauteeing onions and pepper, sauteeing the potatoes, etc. Those multiple steps also build and concentrate delicious flavor. I served with recommended yogurt - perfect!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Bad experience with this one. The rice was undercooked and the potatoes were over cooked despite following the recipe.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I added a cup of cubed, frozen paneer (household staple for me) and subbed the peas and carrots for a cup of frozen zucchini and peppers. Served with Cilantro chutney, which it definitely needed.  The paneer was a great addition- I think it would have been quite bland without it.  Will definitely make again.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: This has been a weeknight staple in our home now for a few years.  It’s easily adaptable for whatever veg you have on hand.  We like our yogurt with lemon juice and grated garlic.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I liked the mix of spices in this dish. They provided a strong flavor kick. I have another recipe for pulao that I like that is milder and it was interesting to compare the two. I ate the whole peppercorns but wasn't sure if I was supposed to. But I figured the other whole spices were just meant to flavor the dish and not to eat. I enjoyed this recipe and will definitely keep it in my rotation.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: This was wonderful, great vehicle for leftovers too

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: My supermarket didn't have whole cardomom pods, can I use ground?

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: If I use brown rice, what’s the timing for cooking, as it will take longer than the basmati rice?

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: When I sub brown rice I partially cook it first.  Instead of just soaking, gently simmer the rice for 15 minutes, then add to the dish at Step 5 and proceed as written.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Easy, but the spice combination was different and delicious.  Next time I'll be braver with the chiles.  Whole peppercorns took some getting used to, but I ended up liking them.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Do you separate the seeds from the cardamom pods?

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: No. Throw the whole pods in. You fish them out while eating it. Warn your guests.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Oh, my goodness, I just made this following the recipe exactly, with one exception.  I had no ghee, so used coconut oil.  It is pretty and it is delicious!  Thank you for this, I will be having it again. Yes, I picked out the big pieces of spices.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Used coarse ground peppercorns and chili flakes

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
I, too, thought shrimp was a good addition. Saute in garlic oil briefly before adding.
Rather than trying to pick out whole spices, cook in cheesecloth (bouquet garni). Stir a couple of times during cooking.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Is the purpose of the chili, slit in half, solely for flavoring/heat? Does one eat it? Personally, I'm tempted to just slice a small jalapeno into half moons, as we always have jalapenos....

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Crazy delicious. It’s already been requested for next week!  Used a bag of frozen mixed veg, as suggested.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: This recipe was really delicious!  The spices added a ton of flavor.  A dear friend from southern India made this dish for us a month ago, but I have to confess that this version is better!  I won't tell her, though!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: so yummy but i would recommend making the rice with veg broth. we also added tofu/chicken and it was incredible

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Instead of water I used Orrington Farms vegan chicken broth base. Used half a Serrano chili. Delicious!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: This turned out wonderful. Used 1/4 tsp ground cardamom and 1/4 tsp black pepper for the whole spices. Skipped the chile because we can't handle heat. Added 3 cloves minced garlic in with the onion. Used veggie broth instead of water for the rice. Added the peas (thawed) once the rice was done after the 15 min, then recovered and did the 10 min rest time. Added pan fried paneer cubes. Definitely serve with yogurt.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: This is so yummy! I didn’t have a green pepper which i think would make a huge difference. It’s delicious, easy to make, and a good dinner. Will be making again!!!!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Do you think shrimp would be nice in this?

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: you forgot the ginger-garlic paste!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Add cut green beans and small florets of cauliflower for a even better taste.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Can you make this ahead of time and serve it at a picnic?

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Use Chilli available from indian store for more authenticity. add string beans along with carrots.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: This was delish! My husband isn't as fond of Indian food as I am but he loved the spices in this dish. I also used left over brown rice, added at the very end. In addition to the one potato and a few carrots I added half a head of cauliflower since it was in the house and needed to be cooked. I will make this again with whatever vegs I have on hand, plus this great mix of spices

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: added more ghee and put carrots in with cubed sweet potato covered and cooked on low a few minutes otherwise potato and carrots would not be cooked enough. also less salt. think risotto, similar. excellent flavors.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I didn’t have any peas but two handfuls of spinach worked. Is there a way to make it as flavourful as possible without the loose spices in it?

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I ADORE this recipe! So simple and delicious. I make it frequently and it is my go-to potluck dish.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Absolutely delicious!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: How much ground cardamom might be appropriate instead of a pod or two?

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Noting multiple comments about the dish being bland, I added minced ginger and garlic, as well as garam masala and coriander, before adding the potatoes. Good flavor. Wasn’t thrilled by the potato, I’ll substitute cauliflower next time.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Added 1 can chickpeas for protein which was good. As per some of the reviews, I used broth in place of water, and added garlic and ginger. I used brown rice and simmered it for 15 minutes before adding but it was still a little chewy, next time I will use already cooked brown rice & reduce the amount of liquid. Was not a fan of the whole peppercorns. Overall very tasty though & will make again!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Heresy, I know, but this seems like a cake best made with a boxed cake mix. :)

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I was thinking the same thing!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Nothing fancy, no doubt. But fresh, simple, quick, needing few ingredients and likely to be a hit with children and those tired of complex baking land longing for something light and not overly sweet or rich. Cover it with fresh lightly sweetened whipping cream and a mix of pfreeze-died strawberries and toasted sliced almonds for texture. I'll be at that table with a hot cup of Earl Grey tea.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Made this today for our picnic. EVERYONE loved it. Best compliment was: "This is right out of a 1950s Lutheran Church potluck supper." Trust me that's a compliment! 😀

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Have been making this for 40 years after it was featured in some ladies magazine my mother got. It was for Xmas.  Make a layer cake - box of course!  Make jello with half the water you normally do. Poke holes and do one red, one green, frost with whipped cream. My brother still says Xmas isn’t complete without it!  Gourmet? No. Pure delicious nostalgia?  Yes!!  A

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Im wondering how well a homemade cake with jello that needs refrigerated tastes? Homemade cake fresh from the refrigerator is not soft and fluffy but heavy and dense. . A box cake  ( yes i said it) is probably a better option.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: But what is the texture like? Is it like a Tres Leches cake—super moist? Is it jiggly from the gelatin?

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: @Harry Stylish more like a tres leches but less liquidy (depending on your experience with tres leches I suppose).

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I haven’t made a poke cake since the 1980’s!  It’s a cool refrigerated treat that my kids loved and thank you NYT for reminding me. Try mixing flavors of cake and jello - we liked lemon cake and lime jello.  And, if you are feeding adults, this isn’t a bad option for Jell-O shots either.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
Do you mean adding alcohol to the jello for the cake?
I'm older and have never made jello shots. ;-)

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
1 (3 ounce) package Jello gelatin
1 cup boiling water
1⁄2 cup cold water
1⁄2 cup vodka
- Add boiling water to jello powder and stir until dissolved. Add the cold water and vodka.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Holy Mackerel!!!! I now know what's wrong with my Jello shots - not enough booze. Thanks!!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Overall, a great recipe that we'll make again! But next time I will check the cake at 35 minutes or so. At 45 minutes the bottom and sides of the cake were a little too browned. Another note, it looked like the full recipe of Jell-O would be too much and so I didn't use it all, but next time I will use all the Jell-O so that there's lots of pink color/strawberry flavor in each piece.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: WON'T MAKE AGAIN. Experienced cake baker here: Recipe is more like a pound cake [delicious on its own] but a poor choice for poking with jello as it is very firm and a bit dry refrigerated.  Bake time is way off: I set timer for 44 minutes and it was nearly burned at the edges.  60 minutes would have made it inedible.  Whipped cream required refrigeration but cake was much improved at room temperature. .. Since I don't use boxed mixes [softer cake] I can see a poke cake isn't for me.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Made it per recipe for 4th of July and it was a hit! It’s a perfect larger gathering or potluck share - wonderful desert for those that enjoy a lighter sweetness level treat. Dense cake so a little goes a long way, but nicely juxtaposed w fresh whipped cream and in season strawberries.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
My favorite dessert — one my grandma used to make.

Only thing I’d add: the topping should be one container of cool whip, mixed with an instant vanilla pudding mix. Add strawberries after spreading on cake.

It’ll change your life!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Second time I've made this... I put some sliced strawberries in the holes before adding the J-ELLO... it's a nice addition.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: What do you use to make the holes? I used the handle of a wooden spoon, but seems like you would need something bigger in order to fit the strawberry slices?

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Need to go all the way with this throw-back. Frosting: one box instant vanilla pudding thinned with 1 cup of milk. Fold in a container of cool whip. Frost and chill.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Simple, easy and the teenagers that hang around my house request it again & again.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Exactly - my MIL makes the best version of this using a recipe from 40-50 years ago. It calls for white cake mix, and Dream Whip (!) for the frosting. While I’m not a fan of boxed mixes, I’m also not above having seconds of her strawberry jello cake.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Make this even easier.  Use a box cake mix. Add some extra flavorings. Whipped cream topping

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: A homemade refrigerated cake is usually dense and heavy. Can you have an unrefrigerated homemade cake with jello? I don’t think so. A box cake is probably a better option.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: My daughter and I had so much fun making this and it was a huge hit! Thank you Priya and April.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I much prefer to use a white box cake mix for jello poke cakes. My favorite is to use orange jello and then put a few drops of orange extract in the cool whip.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: My mother has made a cake like this one for years. Try a creme diplomat (creme patissiere with whipped cream) for the topping!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: An excellent super moist cake. Kids loved it. Very easy to make

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Based on the feedback, I just used a plain boxed cake (yellow cake from trader joe’s) and made my own whipped cream. I didnt need all the strawberry jello. But it turned out lovely for my daughter’s summer bday. I used some fancy sprinkles to add some pizazz since it was a bday cake. Very simple.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: As others have said, the bake time on this recipe is way off. It only took 35 minutes for me and the result was a moist, but fully cooked cake. Otherwise, it came out delicious!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Accidentally used twice the amount of jello and it was delicious! Very moist like a pound cake, not jiggly like jello. Baked the cake for 45 mins exactly. Added the whipped cream at the end right before serving.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Really wanted to make the full recipe-- have met April Anderson and frequented her bakery-- but  I took the comments to heart and did this with a boxed cake mix & my kids. Followed the directions here as much as I could, and decorated it like this one. Easy, delicious, and tons of fun.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Made this Saturday to take to a dinner party because I didn’t feel like spending the entire afternoon on something fancy, and gotta say: This was easy, delicious and a huge hit, as written.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: This cake was known, in our family, as Aunt Leona’s Cake. My great aunt made this cake for every event and party. And, growing up in the Midwest, it was made with boxed cake, strawberry Jello, and Cool Whip. I’d give this slightly elevated version a go.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I smile that we are asked to “patiently” cream the butter and sugar.  I’ll do some yoga before I start on this yummy-looking cake!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: It’s a simple cake and is a nice easy dessert for summer. Nothing fancy, sure, but we don’t always have to have fancy. And kids will love it too!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Whipped almost a quart of cream with about a quarter cup sugar. Was definitely more than the three cups called for but I’m glad I did it!  Piled it high in an extra tall pyrex baking dish with a lid to keep covered in the fridge. Garnished with both blueberries and strawberries because it didn’t look like enough strawberries to me. Was even better on day two!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I tried it with a mix because that was all I could find in gluten-free. I also used the alcohol recommended by another commentor. I used  Cherry Kirsch. It was a very festive birthday cake. But next time I’m going to try again with the recipe cake to get the right crumb for the Jell-O to make a better pattern Instead of just flooding the porous cake.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Most disappointing- was very dry and more biscuit-like. Followed directions exactly and guests were kind.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
Having read notes, prepared with white cake mix. The jello mostly dispersed throughout the texture of the cake, and I worried about result. However, this was a project with school aged grandchildren so we pressed on. Two cups of cream yielded a good amount of cover, which we covered liberally with the sliced strawberries.
The end result was a mostly pink cake, but it was light and flavorful, that we served at a big family dinner. I would prepare it again as a project with the children.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Disappointing results... jello soaked into cake and once cooled the texture was heavy and dry. Perhaps if jello was chilled until soft set and then spread to fill the holes the finished cake would be less dense.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Cake came out over cooked and flat, maybe an inch high… 9*13 pan seems too big. I agree to lower cook time, edges were burned. Switching to box cake mix for my remake since I don’t have time to start over!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: We all felt like this cake was wayyyy too heavy.  Not my favorite NYT recipe.  :(

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Based on others’ helpful notes, I baked this for only 35 minutes, and the cake still came out dry and dense. Definitely did not live up to the jello cakes of my youth! Steer clear.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
I had high hopes for this cake delivering on a strong strawberry flavor and it didn’t. It was a good tasting shortcake like dense cake but the strawberry flavor from the jello pokes just didn’t come through. I wonder if i left the cake out of the refrigerator for at least an hour before serving though would’ve helped. 

I am glad i tried this cake but i will make other strawberry cakes before making this again.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Used a box mix and plant-based gelatin. Did not like the result, which was dry. Also, the gelatin did not travel well down the poked holes. Whipped cream almost saved it. Won't make again.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I thought this would be something like Tres Leches cake.  It was not.  The jello didn't even work like I thought, and did not give it a punch.  The whipped cream and fruit on top saved it.  Surprisingly bland.  If you try to turn this into jello shots, you are wasting good vodka on this cake.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I made this for a church event. Everyone raved about it! The jello made the cake even more moist and frosting with cool whip kept it from being overly sweet. Wonderfully light summer desert!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I’ve been making this for years with a Gluten Free box cake (King Arthur). It makes for a moist, soft, delicious cake. Usually I make it for the 4th of July and use fresh blueberries and strawberries/raspberries to turn the top into a Flag or a rainbow of fresh fruit for a Pride flag. Always a big hit!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
I have to agree with others in

1. Texture - akin to pound cake. Quite dense
2. Flavor - just fine. Nothing great
3. Ease - you could probably use a box cake mix and get a better result.

In the end, I probably won’t make this again. Not awful but didn’t make me want to go back for more.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I did this, but used 4 bananas & banana pudding total instead of using strawberry. I did not find this recipe to be dry like others have said. Like others said, it may be easier to just use a boxed cake mix and do milk instead of water.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Don’t cook as long as recipe says. Shoot for 35-40 minutes instead.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
I’m not sure why so many people are complaining about the texture of the cake. I have had it made with box cake and it was good, but too sweet for me. I thought the texture of this recipe complemented the jello well - everyone at my July 4th party said the texture was their favorite part!
I made it with half the sugar and I frosted it with unsweetened, whipped heavy whipping cream. I didn’t have my 9x13 pan on hand so i made it a layer cake with 2 layers (9” round pan).

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I made this last week. I am 75% sure it was user error, but the jello seeped into the cake, making it unpleasantly squishy.  There is something off about the cake itself. I made homemade whipped cream and added bergamot extract to it. That in itself was delicious, with some thinly sliced strawberries on top.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Does anyone have a substitute for the gelatin?

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I made this with my 4 and 2 year old. The cake itself was so easy and fast to whip up. They loved poking the cake and pouring in the jello. And it was honestly so yummy! I wasn’t sure what to expect as I’ve never had a poke cake before but I was really pleasantly surprised. We did cut into it before we were supposed to so I can only imagine it will be even better tomorrow.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I made it with a Duncan Hines yellow cake mix labeled "super moist." The whole cake slurped up the jello. I was intrigued by the frosting÷topping. it tasted okay and had a nice consistency, but will it hold up for more than a day? I think this cake is best made in anticipation of a crowd because i am not sure it will be good after a day or 2. I'll not likely repeat this recipe as it tasted a bit off. Could it be due to using a cake mix? All the ingredients in the convenience foods of jello, instant pudding, and cool whip? I don't know. It's the next day and I'll try the cake after a night in the fridge.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
I made this with a boxed cake mix for a work pot luck. I added a box of banana flavored pudding to the cake mix and made it with butter rather than oil. Did everything else according to the recipe. 
Coworkers went gaga for it!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
To follow up with Alex’s comment about erucic acids toxicity to the heart. 

There is an approved brand of mustard oil but tastes lighter.

Original studies are from the 70s on rats, yet still a massive world population cooks with mustard oil daily. 

I would be more concerned about lead and arsenic levels in plants and grains or mercury in fish before worrying about using mustard oil to make this recipe.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
Couple of tips about this dish which we frequently make in a bengali home. 

Soak the mustard seeds for a couple of hours
Avoid a food processor, over grinding will make the mustard bitter
We dont use onion
Wrap in banana leaf if possible and not in foil
You can steam the fish instead of baking
Lastly, i have grown up eating food cooked in mustard oil

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: There are seemingly complex issues related to healthful or harmful effects of using mustard oil in cooking. I have not had time to evaluate in sufficient depth, so I've stayed away from it. For instance the FDA has an import alert covering its use as a vegetable oil (https://www.accessdata.fda.gov/cms_ia/importalert_89.html). Would be good for the NYT to reference some material on this issue if posting a recipe in which the oil figures as a prominent ingredient.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
The taste enhancement that mustard oil brings to a recipe calling for it, far outweighs serious health impediments caused by erucic acid. It will be consumed in very limited quantities and as a cook who uses mustard oil my recommendation for those, who have some reservations, is drizzling  a little on top of the cooked item instead of cooking in it. 
One other point to make is, mustard seeds when ground can be bitter so it is best to add salt and green chile together with the seeds.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I was curious about the mustard oil "debate" as I really wanted to try this recipe. I found this write up by Nik Sharma of SeriouscEats helpful https://www.seriouseats.com/mustard-oil-guide. He suggests one of the same brand s that the NYT does in the Tip section at the bottom of the recipe. Problem solved if you wanna shell out for one of the safer options. Can't wait to try!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
Tasty!  I used hake, which is firmer and tastier than cod (and was on sale!).  Made as directed, except that after 15 minutes in foil the fish seemed underdone - so I broiled the fillets for 5 minutes to brown them.  Nice.  Easy preparation, too.

(Mustard oil is readily available at Pakistani and Indian groceries in Brooklyn, and probably at Patel, though I haven't looked at the latter.  But readily available and inexpensive.)

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Cooking with aluminum foil is toxic- use banana leaves or baking paper please!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Baking paper at 450 degrees is putting an awful lot of trust in the accuracy of your oven's temperature. If you're set on cooking in paper, you're better off decreasing the temp a bit and adjusting the cook time to compensate. Or just cook all four fillets in a baking dish with a cover, though the time may need to be adjusted up a bit.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: This is actually inaccurate.  Cooking with aluminum foil is safe.  Please be careful about spreading misinformation.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: One other thing to note is this recipe, traditionally is cooked wrapped in banana leaf. I think parchment paper might be a better option to foil.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
Weighing in on the mustard oil debate - it's worth it to know that every mustard oil warning (whether in the U.S. or India) has paved the way for genetically modified oils to enter the market  - in the 1950s it was canola in the U.S. and in the 1990s it was foreign soy imports into India.  Something to consider if you're looking into the mustard oil research.

Also, this is a very typical (and delicious) Bengali fish dish and the mustard oil is essential to it's sharp and delightful flavor :)

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Delicious and unique flavor!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Really delicious recipe! Great for mild flavored fish when you’re feeling adventurous. 😋

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
Wow! I am really into spicy food and I enjoyed this, but too spicy to serve to the unsuspecting. As it turned out here, it couldn't be called "mild".
On the other hand, I was thinking that I would take care to more precise next time I make it (and that I should keep quiet until then :-) ).
I will try Souradeep's suggestion to steam the fish parcel.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Delicious recipe—-great for mild fish when you’re feeling adventurous!!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: My husband and I love this dish! He’s Indian, I’m not but we both love spicy.  I make it with a neutral oil because I think the mustard seeds are sufficient for that flavor.  I cook it in my Le Creuset Dutch oven because it’s easier than wrapping each piece in foil and I like that it contains all the delicious flavors.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: This recipe looks delicious, but I thought mustard oil should be heated to the smoking point to improve its flavor.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Thank you for referring to the FDA alert on mustard oil. I agree that it would be important for NYT to weigh in.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Super flavorful! Didn’t use mustard oil but only because I couldn't find it. Olive oil subs perfectly well

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Or just use something else “earthy” like avocado oil, for such a small amount, just basically binding the paste together?

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: FYI, read the full NYT article as it does mention US and EU warnings re mustard oil.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: 10 minutes in a °450 oven was too much for us. Rockfish filets would have been good at 7 minutes. Also, I'll amp up the spices next time too. Maybe add some cumin to the mix

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Please DONT toss the fish and paste with bare hands. Ingredients in the paste are too harsh for delicate skin and cuticles. Either manage with a spoon, fork and brush or wear gloves.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: It’s a great recipe that yields really uniquely flavored, tasty fish! That said, the flavor is capital-m Mustard, so if you’re not into that, maybe sub mustard oil for another fat. I personally wanted to balance the sharpness of the mustard with more fatty sweetness - maybe I’ll add coconut milk next time?

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I like this recipe. Thanks to the folks who added about the purity of mustard oil. With the mustard oil, I leave the mustard seeds, and chile powder. If you have a serrano, that's legitimate heat and flavor. Feel like the spicy ingredients are overkill, such that you wouldn't be able to decipher the actual spice. I like to add curry powder, and tumeric as recommended.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I got drunk and made this when my fiancee wasn't home and got all these stains on my undershirt when I ate it (I spilled on myself) and it was so good and I don't even care.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: @Dan_Dags I’m sorry but years later my wife and I still LOL when we read this. I swear we make this recipe just so we can laugh at this pitch perfect  comment 😂

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: @Dan_Dags just read this, gave me my only belly laugh in too long of a time.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: This 4-serving gutbuster clocks in at over 1,100 calories per serving. I lightened it up by omitting the bacon, using half-and-half and 93% ground beef. I easily divided it into 6 servings with a salad on the side.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: @Cassie W. I used 1% milk and omitted the process cheese and it was still quite rich. I may cut down the bacon next time. Very tasty, tho.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
Made as directed, following others' suggestions to cut down on the hot sauce. Delicious! And worked very well using gluten-free macaroni.
Although after spending a good hour and a half in the kitchen -- on a recipe inspired by a convenience product, of all things -- my teen son and I decided it would more accurately be titled "Hamburger Complicater."

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: @kimball99 We agree! It’s so, so tasty but YES to calling it Hamburger Complicator. We’ve now adopted your term. For what it’s worth, I’ve found a very similar-tasting and MUCH simpler recipe (link below). Whether you use it or not, thanks for giving our family a laugh and a new entrée title https://spicysouthernkitchen.com/stovetop-sausage-mac-and-cheese/

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: @kimball99 uncomplicate it. That’s what I did. Fast and easy and still delish. Added a can of petite diced tomatoes for color and added flavor.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Why couldn’t you just cook the bacon first? There’s no need for 1/4 c of oil if you use the FIVE STRIPS of bacon and drippings to cook the onions and garlic, and then the ground beef. I cannot see throwing away added oil when you have the bacon drippings?!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
@KMR
I think because the recipe is using the finely chopped raw bacon as a pork addition to the ground beef. You don’t want the bacon crisp and cooked. You want it to meld into the other meat as you make the “sauce”?

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: @Justine I think about this comment often and it changed how I thought bacon had to be cooked l

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: @Ryguy I’ve seen this in Italian sauces with pancetta and it was hard to do at first but the way it cooks down along with the flavor is amazing

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I used 1/4 cup of mild buffalo wing sauce (Noble Made) and subbed a head of steamed cauliflower cut to 1/2 inch florets instead of the macaroni. Leave out most of the broth (used about a TBSP), combine the cream, hot sauce, cheeses and broth, then mix in the already steamed florets. Makes an amazing lower carb option.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I honestly don't know why anyone would make a cheese, cream, pasta, bacon and beef dish and then try to reduce carbs and fat. I followed the directions exactly and not only drained the fat off but dumped all the beef etc onto a paper towel in step 4 and then continued. 1/4 c. of hot sauce sounded like a lot to me too so I waited until all the ingredients were together and added some sriracha and red chili flakes a little at a time to taste. This homemade version is SO. GOOD.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Made this strictly by recipe tonight for dinner. Yeah, time consuming. Yeah, plenty of stage cooking. Yeah, heavy meal. But what a payoff. Absolutely delicious!  Kept all ingredients as written and... I dunno what all these comments about “too spicy” are about.  Used Franks Original RetHot and it was perfect! A lot of white folk Wonder Bread palates up in here. This is perfect winter comfort food, and I thank you for the recipe!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: @RickMac did you use reg paprika instead of hot? Or maybe your brand of hot paprika runs mild. Mine has a spice level similar to cayenne…it added a LOT of heat right off the bat.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: To those who don’t have to count calories/fat/etc., I salute you!—but I just couldn’t face my trainer if I’d made as written. (1/4 c oil to sauté a single onion?) So: 1 T oil, lean turkey instead of beef, no bacon, low-fat milk instead of cream, 3 slices of Gouda instead of 5 slices of American (bc American cheese gives me flashbacks to the elementary school lunchroom). I’m sure it wasn’t as good as the recipe as written—but it was delicious nonetheless!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: @KSE try ground bison. It’s much leaner and renders very little fat.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
On the basis of your recommendations (including nix the hot sauce for small kids thank you) I’m going to put together the cooked components for my harried daughter to keep on her freezer. For one of the days the toddler is hiding things in the trash can, the 5 year old just got busted at school for kissing a girl, and the tween is annoyed by it all. 
Mom will only  have to nuke the parts, cook pasta and serve. Thank you nyt you’ve never steered me wrong.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I’m just here to read the “I made it exactly as written but turned it into something else entirely” posts.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: @James Gravely. I agree. I just get aggravated. Too many calories?  Don't make it. Can't eat gluten?  Don't eat it. Didn't make the recipe as written?  Your aunt makes a recipe you like better?  Go make it. Don't write about what you did. Why are we so compelled to throw shade on these recipes?  Just move on. Go write your own column. Now I'm hungry and I'm going to make this recipe again and love it. Thanks Priya.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: We don’t love spice so we didn’t use hot sauce. I used 1/2 paprika and 1/2 ancho chili powder instead and it was delicious!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
Divine. Not remotely spicy even with 1/4 cup Tapatio; sheesh, have you guys ever eaten vindaloo in your lives? This is just a pleasant kick. I think quantity of vinegar the hot sauce provides is necessary as well. 

Really important to deeply brown onion and reduce liquids fully at each stage. I used diced pancetta instead of bacon, and I added a couple teaspoons of English mustard with the paprika, plus several dashes of Worcestershire.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I used 1/4 c of Tapatio and it was a bit too spicy even for spice-lovers. Very, very tasty comfort food, but would cut that hot sauce in half to avoid the inferno.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Thanks for this comment. I took your advice and used 1/8 cup of Tapatio and it was perfect. Just enough to make our noses run, but not so hot that we couldn't have a second helping.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: We are fans of spice but wish I had reduced hot sauce by a 1/3 so that folks could adjust to taste when served.  (I used just a touch more than 1/4 cup of Frank's) Made this primarily for our elementary school aged family members and it was a bit too spicy for them.  Adults loved this elevated comfort food recipe.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Whew! Heavy duty! Delicious, but whoa. Heavy and a lot of work, so it might be a one-time dish for us. Subbed GF pasta, pancetta for the bacon, and added 2 tablespoons of Cholula based on reviews that 1/4 cup was too spicy. The Cholula made this, though - yum! We also don't love American cheese, so we just used shredded cheddar and it was PLENTY cheesy.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: A lot of comments about "too spicy!".  Use common sense. There are hot sauces that are very hot and others much milder.  If you're cooking for little kids or people with sensitive palates, use less or none. You can always serve it on the side for the fire-eaters.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
Insane! This adds animal fat mouthfeel on top of animal fat mouthfeel and then doubles down again. Too much, unless serving 8 instead of 4.

A few mods that made it more like a rich but approachable dinner: use milk instead of cream; use less bacon. Maybe a bit less cheddar. Add 2 tbsp tomato paste before adding wine. (No-fat richness!) Use more pasta (11-12 oz). Use sweet smoked paprika instead of hot to control heat. Essentially the same recipe minus the gut-busting and heart attack.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
I’m heartbroken reading of the corners cut for the sake of sparing a calorie or two. If y’all wanted a lean cuisine….

Doubled the batch but only upped the liquid by about a cup or so. 4cups broth, cream per recipe and a few generous splashes of half and half as I only had 1c cream. Also used mini farfalle so I could really shovel this down. Added a few splashes of Worcestershire just after wine evaporated. Used Valentina hot sauce as it’s basically ketchup and was perfect. 

Go big or go home!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Thank you for referencing the frustrating calorie and fat adjustment posts. They make me so sad. And I say this as someone who lost 40 pounds and is very careful about food. Somehow, even if I never make these recipes, I don't want to know that someone omitted something wonderful.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I made this per the recipe instructions and actually found it to not be spicy enough by a long shot. If you enjoy spicy food like I do don’t be afraid of adding in more hot sauce (I used Frank’s). I would not use bacon at all next time (the texture was not pleasing, maybe crisp separately) and would increase the amount of paprika and hot sauce. Don’t be afraid to make it your own! Served with the maple/soy sauce/balsamic grilled broccoli also on the site and found that to be a great combo.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: This recipe was great, especially since it’s a one pot meal. I made to with a few modifications: only 2T oil for the onions; used a couple of paper towels to soak up the oil; and used 1/4 c shredded Monterey Jack cheese instead of the American cheese slices.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: For those wondering why anyone would cook bacon in a 1/4 cup of oil, remember that the source of the recipe is the person responsible for the Shake Shack menu!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Made this tonight and used only about two tbsp of chalula hot sauce instead of the called for amount (cooking for toddler too).  I also omitted bacon just because I didn’t feel like fussing with it. This turned out great. As promised, satisfied that craving for my childhood memory of hamburger helper that actual hamburger helper wouldn’t have satisfied. This is a great recipe and I’ll definitely make it again.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Wowed by the end result, refined cozy in a bowl! I cut the hot sauce (Frank's) to two tablespoons and it had the perfect balance of heat. Good lazy recipe to do intermittent chores/netflix binging between steps.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Enjoyed this. Appreciated the notes. After caramelizing onions, I added chopped leftover veggies: 1/2 green and 1/2 red bell peppers and a couple stalks of chopped celery.  Made it more colorful. I used ground turkey breast mixed with meat from a couple of sweet Italian sausages. Felt I would miss the tomato so I added 1/4 cup of ketchup to the pepper sauce (yes, ketchup!). Didn’t have any bacon or American cheese at the time. Used gruyère & the cheddar. Also added oregano. Yummy!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
I loved it! 

Decadent winter dinner treat. My only change was to cook beef and bacon *first* and then drain all fat (quick rinse of beef in hot water removes residual fat). Bacon crisper up. Then cooked onion/garlic and added beef/bacon back in at that point.

Other than changing the order of cooking the meat I followed the recipe exactly and thought it was just delicious and a perfect January comfort meal.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: So I bent the rules a little - don't be hatin'. If you're willing to dirty an extra pan, you'll save some time & grease. While you're caramelizing your onions, fry the bacon in a separate pan. When it's done, drain the grease from pan #2 & brown your ground beef in the same pan. When your onions & garlic are ready, put in the bacon & beef, & because they're drained, you'll save yourself the trouble of getting rid of an oil slick. Otherwise I cooked as directed. My menfolk swear it's got crack.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Thank you for this recipe. Delicious. Like many of my generation, I ate a fair amount of the Helper franchise in the years after my Mom returned to the workforce in the early 70s. This recipe is layered, time consuming, has the potential to be a gut bomb, and finishes with American cheese!  I thought of my late Mom, my siblings, and the love I feel for my own family the entire time I was cooking this.  I may never cook this again, but I hope I will always remember dinner tonight.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
Comfort food to the max!
Make sure you drain as much fat as possible (tends to develop a slick)

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: At our house, we called this Train Wreck.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Should also have said: with lean ground turkey, there’s no fat to pour off (and no fond to deglaze), so if I did this again, I’d skip the step with the wine.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: We loved it! I left out the bacon because I was lazy and figured it would save some time. Used 85/15 to compensate a little. Ended up using cholula for the hot sauce and maybe used like 4 tablespoons? Wish I measured! The paprika and hot sauce lend a lot to the flavor of the this dish. Only issue I had was I used rotelle instead of macaroni - took a little longer to cook so I had to add more liquid (cream because, again, lazy). Would definitely make again!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Delicious! Added a can of cannellini beans to the leftovers and liked it even better that way.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I followed everyone's notes, reduced hot sauce (used  two tablespoons of  chalula), and reduced heat after adding white wine to dry out meat. I also found that the amount of oil plus ground beef proved to be very greasy, so I drained it out using a meat drainer and also used paper towels to take out grease. I had to cook the meat in the white wine for longer than 10 minutes, more like 12.  Came out great.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I have made this recipe exactly as written four times.  The problem? Nobody wants me to cook anything else!  The first time I looked through others notes - people who actually made the recipe before commenting - and used Cholula, which like others said delivers less heat and more flavor.  Disagree with one who said the 5-lb deli blocks are the same as the individual wrapped "singles" - not necessarily.  I recommend Land O Lakes from the deli, not packaged slices - two different products.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Made it as written except that I reduced the hot sauce (I used Sriracha) to 1/8 cup and that was hot enough for the family.  Rich and delicious.  Perfect for an ice-cold winter night.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I don't like the vinegar taste in hot sauce, so I love your idea of using Sriracha. Thanks!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Awesome! Subbed the ground beef for bison to make it leaner, and added shallots as well. So good!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I made recipe as stated. I was craving camp food (don’t ask. ). It was disgusting.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: With an added 12 minutes to the cook time, I don’t see how the wine adds anything to this dish. Just slows you down

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
I tried this with rice today, as we're in the midst of moving and most of our pantry is in boxes. It came out great.

Once I got to Step 6, I added 1.5 C short grain white rice and:

- 1/2 C heavy cream
- 1/4 C Franks Red Hot
- 2.25 C chicken stock
- Bay leaf
- 1 tsp paprika

Stirred, brought to a boil, then simmered on low for 15 minutes. Stirred 3/4 C cheddar cheese and 4 slices of American (maybe use 3 next time).

Straight into the belly.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
This was perfect on a super cold Minnesota night. I used ground turkey and turkey bacon so my wife would eat it (no red meat for her). Conveniently there was no fat to drain. 

We like spice so I also added a little cayenne powder. 
As others said you have to use deli American cheese slices. I happened to have white American slices around. 

Yes this is not the healthiest but when you were  just out in subzero temps clearing snow off your driveway it’s exactly what you need!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I’ve made this recipe many times and always alter the ingredients but this time I outdid myself. My cheeses vary too, tonite white cheddar, Gruyère, and a little Gorgonzola. Had a few super ripe tomatoes, crushed them in there, and added a diced red jalapeño, Worcestershire, smoked paprika and….a dollop of Dijon. Didn’t have chives and used scallion instead. The best version of this super nostalgic hug in a bowl, by far.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Sounds delicious!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I’ve made many less time consuming and less expensive dishes like this out of necessity during hard times. Use any protein, seasoning and short dry pasta you want, use 2 to 2 1/2 cups liquid to 8 ounces pasta. I use (gasp) cream of soup instead of heavy cream, whatever’s hand. I’ve used smoked sausage or andouille sausage with a can of rotel and homemade Cajun seasoning. Ground beef and mushrooms with Worcestershire and paprika, etc. Remove from heat and stir in cheese or sour cream.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Careful with the hot sauce. Used 1/4 cup of Louisiana Hot Sauce brand. My Indiana relatives would find this almost inedible - but we're in Louisiana, so we can handle it.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: This sounds like it would taste great finished in the oven, in a buttered casserole dish, topped with panko breadcrumbs. May as well go that extra step since you're making it from scratch anyway.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
“Serve immediately,” Krishna writes — and might as well have added, “or die in the stampede.” It’s that yummy! 

That said, like most stews, it gets even better after a night in the fridge: the slightly soupy consistency hot off the stove becomes a thick sauce. I prepared as directed, except for using the Xiaoshing cooking wine and cavatappi noodles I had on hand. Will make again and again.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: This recipe is definitely for stoners. It is insanely delicious.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
This dish is a gutbuster. I enhanced it by doubling the bacon, adding Velveeta, increasing the heavy cream, and using the fattiest ground beef I could find. And you know what? I'd do it again!

If you want healthier options, why bother even reading this recipe??

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I crisped the bacon first and cut it into very small slivers, but not sure it made a difference since it mainly added flavor more than texture. I used 4 tsp of Frank's and it was kicked up enough for us. Otherwise followed the recipe as written and it is very very delicious comfort food.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: This is really, really good comfort food. You can do so many substitutions on this and it's still great. We used beyond meat, chickpea posata, took down the hot sauce a little, used 2% milk and you know what? It was still delicious! This is not health food, but it's not trying to be. You can make some common sense swaps to lighten it up and you will still really enjoy this bowl of comfort. Will definitely make this again, but not too often because it is decadently delicious.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: This is now a family favorite!  I use buttermilk instead of heavy cream because we prefer things a bit more sour.  Delicious!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
It’s a tedious recipe but decadent, delicious, nostalgic. Better than the box stuff (that holds a place in my heart) by far while still scratching that proverbial itch. 

People falling over themselves to hate on this recipe miss the point & up-turned noses about “acceptable” ingredients is consistently predictable from folx who are going to manage to find fault with anything they personally find beneath them. You want delicious hamburger helper? This is a win.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: 5 stars

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: We made one change:  My wife was a little worried about too much heat, so I used 2 Tbl of Frank's hot sauce (regular) and 2 Tbl of Worcestershire sauce.  It was excellent.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: This is a family favorite! It’s decedent and soul soothing. A special treat to be enjoyed occasionally and looked forward to. Recommend doubling the pasta, it makes quite a bit but frozen leftovers reheat well. The perfect hot sauce for this is vinegary to cut through the fat. We like Yucatán Sunshine for this and won’t us another brand. I do also use half and half, and don’t think it loses anything for it.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Made this with impossible beef, no bacon, 1/2 and 1/2 instead of heavy cream. Thought it turned out delicious!  I paired it with a side salad to complete the meal. I used 1/4 cup of hot Valentina and it was perfect! Not to spicy and tons of flavor!  I didn’t have chives so I topped with sliced green onions. I can’t wait to make it again, this batch didn’t last long.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Like many other commenters, I omitted the oil by gently frying the bacon first, removing it and then throwing the onions in the fat. No need to drain excess oil this way. I added celery, and used light thickened cream and the Australian “Mexican cheese blend”. Stirred in some frozen baby peas at the end. Still tasted fabulous — a great winter warmer and honestly very satisfying to pull together.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
Ah yes, American Cheese with the complexity of a Bolognese. 

Follows by Jello mold with the complexity of Crépes Suzette.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: FINALLY, a recipe that gets real about carmelizing onions. "Let cook until the onions turn light beige in color and begin to caramelize, 20 to 25 minutes."

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Use 97% lean beef, fat-free Half & Half (yup), whole wheat pasta.  I would also partially render bacon, drain, then add to keep the wonderful flavor.  Smaller servings are always a good idea.  But, yumm.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I swapped cultured cashew cream cheese for the heavy cream and used cashew cheddar as the only cheese, then chickpea pasta for a gluten and dairy free dish.  This came out as an A  version of a favorite childhood comfort food!  Also added a healthy pinch of chili flakes and followed the advice of a reader and rendered bacon first, using that fat to sauté the onions and garlic.  Will for sure be making again!

author: Priya Krishna
