commentBody: It just didn’t do it for me. Just over the top. And I love rich food but it feels like a mishmash and so.much.work. Won’t be making it again.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
Was not pleased with the taste result. Am not sure if it's the hot sauce (Cholula) or the wine or the smoked paprika... or something else. 
Usually a casserole like this one tastes as good or better the next day. However, the taste didn't improve and I'm actually putting the uneaten leftovers in our Bio Bin. Since I live with two 20 somethings who hoover up almost any meat based leftovers, the casserole results weren't acceptable.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I used canned tomato purée in place of the hot sauce, no bacon, and whole wheat noodles... so delicious!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: This really is a great recipe and it was easily converted to vegetarian. Use Beyond Meat for the ground beef and 2 tsp of JD's Bacon salt for the bacon. Then prepare as above. It shaves off quite a bit of fat, cholesterol and sodium.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
Regarding the bacon: I blitzed the raw bacon strips in a food processer (so it was very finely chopped), then added the raw ground beef to the processor bowl and pulsed a few times to mix, then transferred the combined meats to the pot for browning. That way I side-stepped the "issue" of bacon texture (as many commenters have noted).

My personal preference is also to use 4 strips bacon/ lb ground beef.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I made this earlier today so it’s done and just needs a warm up before dinnertime. I had to taste it and I was dying. It’s like the ultimate in good childhood memories, warmth and a hug from nana on a big spoon. Hubby walked in the door after a work out and said it “smells so good in here!”  He had a bowl. I had to put it up so he wouldn’t eat it for a snack. I used soyrizo because I’m vegetarian and didn’t add the paprika or hot sauce. There’s just no need when using the soyrizo.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Yes, this was labor intensive.  Yes, you should just fry the bacon first, remove the bacon and leave the fat (don't need more oil), and add the bacon back when the beef is brown.  But it's just not that good, not worth the trouble.  Sorry.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Made with ground turkey--not even a full pound. No bacon, cut back on the oil. So far we've gotten five good servings out of it with a serving or two left over. 1/4 c Cholula gives it a significant kick. This is really yummy and because of the heat, you can't just shovel it into your mouth. I'm finding it wonderfully satisfying without being the gut-buster everyone seems to be complaining about.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: It would be nice for a change to someday see a photo of food that doesn't have a spoon or a fork sticking out of it. Even just one such photo would do. Then I would never mention it again. I promise.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Followed the recipe to the letter but instead of making hamburger helper I made swordfish salad. Came out great brah

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Instructions fine but does not taste good.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: OMG I made this as directed except lessened the amount of hot sauce to 2 tablespoons… it’s flippin’ amazing! A bit of work, but definitely worth the effort (and calories!)

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Made this as written and we loved it!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Ok full disclosure, I do not come from a hamburger helper home and am not really familiar with what it should taste like, but I felt like for all this time and calories there should be more of a payoff. Don’t get me wrong, it was really good…I did not find it even remotely spicy with 1/4 cup of hot sauce, in fact I just found the whole thing a little bland, white bread. I wanted more…something I couldn’t put my finger on. My partner loved it. I only liked it.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
In the words of my "eat anything" teen:  "this is very mid"
Agreed.  If you're in the mood for Hamburger Helper, save yourself the time, energy, and frustration for spending so much effort on something that is not anywhere near as good as the box on the shelf (which we haven't eaten since the kids were little but remember it as being better than "edible).  Made this as written (including full 1/3 c Crystal hot sauce)  but won't again.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
I’m sorry … call me a heathen whilst I wear my wife-beater t-shirt and underwear, unshaven for five days and a tasting hangover.

But if I want Hamburger Helper, I want it in the time it takes to boil water and cook pasta, not five times that and a dozen trips back and forth to the pantry and fridge.  

Splurge on the quality of ground beef????  Sure, absolutely!

It takes considerable willpower not to finish the whole pan’s chemical-laden atrocity … I love it!!!!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: My family thought this was excellent for a homemade Hamburger Helper - much better than my other recipe. I didn’t have any white wine but kept everything else the same. Thanks for a wonderful family comfort meal recipe.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: This was wonderful. I used a full lb of pasta and the full quarter cup of hot sauce (cholula). I love spicy things, and I could see how if I had used the half lb of pasta called for it would be pretty spicy. Keep in mind if you use double the pasta you’ll need to increase the volume of liquid. I added about another 3 cups or so of water/stock/cream as I went along. I skipped the bacon and am glad I did; didn’t miss it. Love the full volume of chives at the end.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Let's make this easy for everyone.  If you don't already know how much spice/heat you prefer, you probably shouldn't be including spice/heat in anything you cook.  This isn't difficult.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Good if time intensive recipe. BUT, the hot sauce is a misstep for me. It overpowered the dish with the taste of vinegar. Next time I would substitute the hot sauce with chili flakes to add heat.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Made as written. Next time would not add bacon since it overpowered the flavor and is not at all in the OG..? Tasted good but not like hamburger helper. Probably not worth all the effort. Won't make again and will just buy the box, I'm sad to say.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: This was disgusting and I threw the whole thing away.  What an awful waste.  The bacon was rubbery from being cooked with the beef, and it was hard to drain out enough fat.  It was repulsively greasy.  I made the mistake of using Tabasco for the hot sauce, which made dish tasted heavily of vinegar.  Worst dish I’ve cooked in years.  The packaged Hamburger Helper has got to taste waaaay better than this slop.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Made it with fake bacon and walnuts and mushrooms instead of meat to make it vegetarian. I also used vegan creamer and it worked out great. Super tasty and filling. Would omit spicy smoked paprika since the hot sauce is enough. Definitely a comfort food for chilly nights.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: This was good, but there's absolutely no reason it needs so much hot sauce. That's absurd. If you love the people you're serving it to and you don't usually dare people to eat the food you cook, I would say use no more than a third of what's recommended here.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: My 3 year old has proclaimed this the best ever Mac n cheese. High praise!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I'm surprised, but I really didn't like it at all, and it seemed greasy in spite of using extra lean ground beef and draining it well over paper towels on top of a wire mesh basket. I'd fortunately used only 1/8 c of hot sauce, which was plenty spicy - but wished I hadn't put in the paprika, because it didn't add a pleasant flavor at all. I ordinarily like spicy food, but found nothing about this dish that I liked. I hate to be so negative, but this flavor just really didn't work for me.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Had to have something comforting for election night here in NC. I did turkey bacon and beyond beef. Dont  buy cream milk was fine. Just enough Valentina to be able to smell it maybe a tablespoon and this was correct - right spiced for me- doctored at the table with chilies by the fella. Kids could have handled it but didn’t try it. It is a democracy after all - if we can keep it.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
As the son of a CIA trained gourmet chef growing up in the 60's, my absolute favorite dish (a birthday special when I could ask for anything) was American Goulash, which I thought my depression era dad had just coined for want of another name.
But lo and behold, it's been mentioned as such since at least 1914.
Aside from the lack of any tomato, this dish looks very similar and from the comments here, is just as comfy!
Bon apetit!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Why would I spend 1 hour 15 minutes to create this? But then, I am not a fan of Shake Shack, so perhaps that is why.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I love this recipe, but in no way is a recipe that requires carmelized onions and takes an hour and fifteen minutes of active work a "weeknight" recipe. Signed, Mom of a Toddler Who Made This on a Sunday Night

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Made this a second time but made several mods. Used maybe about a tbsp oil to carmelize the onions and just a pinch of salt. I omitted the bacon and used 93/7 ground beef.  Added about a teaspoon fresh thyme along with bay leaf. Used 3.5oz of cheddar cheese (thats all i had) which I grated and omitted american cheese slices. It came out much better than my first time when I followed the recipe exactly.This was slightly "lighter" and less salty than 1st time.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
I made this last night, but didn’t have elbow macaroni and ended up using rotini. I followed the suggestion to crisp the bacon first and cook everything in bacon fat in order to cut down on fat. I also subbed Greek yogurt for heavy cream. It was amazing!

The hot smoked paprika was a little bit intense in terms of smokiness. When I make it again, I’m going to use one-quarter the amount of hot smoked paprika.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
Substituted prosciutto for bacon. 
Substituted half and half for cream. 
Didn’t add any hot sauce because I have a child. 
Used cheddar and Parmesan instead for the American cheese. 
Added Worcestershire sauce because it needed an umami kick (I’m assuming because I didn’t use bacon). 
All of this was done because I didn’t have the exact ingredients. I can’t compare it to the original but what we had tasted great!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
Excellent dish.  I left out the wine (and drank it instead).  Freezes great--just defrost or cover with foil and heat on 250 for 30 min until defrosted by fork test.  Increase to 325 until hot.
I made 2 casseroles for daughter-in law (who has twins age 2)  Twins loved it.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Never posted a comment here before but I wanted to offer some praise and advice for this recipe. I passed on it a couple months ago after reading all the people grousing about its richness/fattiness but made it tonight with a few common sense tweaks that got the entire pot down to around 2000 calories. I used Carbe Diem elbows, 93/7 ground beef, four slices of thick bacon, and skim milk with a dollop of yogurt instead of the heavy cream. Rather than frying the onion in oil, I rendered the bacon first and fried the onions in that fat. I added sharp cheddar and some Muenster (it’s all I had) and left the recipe alone otherwise. Even with these tweaks, it was a masterpiece—and felt very indulgent. Don’t sleep on this one!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Replaced the processed American cheese with hand-grated local cheddar and ate it in my hot tub. RFK approved.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Just made this with 1 pound of Beyond Beef - came out great!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I think you'd be better off making mac and cheese or bolognese. This recipe is rich without being that flavorful, and was quite greasy, even though I drained off the fat in step 4. It also lends itself to overcooked noodle leftovers with a texture that's difficult to resurrect into something appetizing. It was a fun idea, but no thanks!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I made this as written and it was tasty but VERY rich. It provided way more food than four people could eat. I might lighten it slightly if I made it again and I think it could benefit from a wilted green being included just to cut some of the fat. I did have to cook it for quite awhile, more than 20 minutes, after the cheese was added to get the sauce to thicken.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Better as a side than as a main dish.  My family of four agreed that the first few bites are very good, but as a main dish it turns very heavy very fast.  And we omitted the bacon and subbed soy crumbles for the beef.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: This was delicious. I’ve never had hamburger helper before, but I made the recipe as written, used Impossible “meat” and omitted the bacon and it was BOMB.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I love this recipe, it's so good!  I made it as is the first time and it was great and then make a few changes the second time to accommodate what I had.  It's good with whole milk instead of half and half and whole wheat pasta is great!  The second time I omitted the hot sauce because I used a a spicy american cheese.  Both times it was great and also fun to make!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Vegetarian variations: we used the 12-ounce packet of Impossible in place of a pound of ground beef, and 2-3 dashes of liquid smoke instead of bacon. Sourcing hot paprika was more challenging than I expected, so I used regular and kept the hot sauce (Frank's Red Hot) closer to the 1/3 end of things. Would it be better with deli-sliced American cheese? Probably, but Kraft Singles got the job done, and we have now made this multiple times. The chives really do add to it, don't skip 'em!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I’m obsessed with this recipe! Used vegetarian crumbles and skipped the bacon… like a told a friend who I shared some with “it’s vegetarian, it is NOT health food.” Super rich, creamy, exactly what I wanted.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
I used Impossible meats (beef and sausage) in substitution. I took out a whole cup of broth from the recipe--almost 4 cups of liquids seemed wild. 

It was perrrrfection! It's going into the pasta dish rotation and thank you!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I made this a lazy way but sauteing the beef and the bacon together, draining most of the grease, sauteeing the onions and garlic briefly in the remaining fat, adding all the other ingredients and simmering, adding the cheese at the end. I used half and half instead of cream to redeem myself a smidgeon. I used 1/8 c. of Frank's hot sauce. It was quick and absolutely delicious.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I made this exactly as written because I thought it sounded delicious. Besides why even use a recipe if you aren't going to follow it? It turned out as a huge gloppy greasy spicy mess. Very much a waste of time and ingredients.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Hate to admit I can’t compare against the original, but this felt overly rich and greasy. Flavor was good, but for the effort and nutritional value, I think I’ll pass on making this a second time.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
We really love this recipe but one thing about it is kind of obviously improvable: 

Cook the bacon first until rendered, then take the cooked bacon out and cook the onions, beef, etc... in the bacon fat, then add the bacon back in at the end. You get more flavor and don't end up trying to brown your beef with the far slower-cooking bacon. 

I've done it this way every time I've made the recipe so I can't comment on the as-is formulation, but with this tweak, everyone loves this dish over here.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Oops, should clarify further- I leave out the bacon and use vegetable stock and Impossible “beef” to make this kosher and it’s still delicious.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Start frying bacon first. Then add onion when almost done. So bacon will be crisper

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Holy cow! Literally! Way bigger than the sum of its parts. Seems like a Frankenstein recipe, but trust it. I lightened w/o bacon, defatted with wine poured all juices into a fat separating meas cup before reducing, used 1/4 heavy cream and 1/2 almond milk, beef stock. I forgot to re-read comments about reducing stock. Oh well. Also used beef stock. Humble looking when done but freakin amazing.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
I took heed of the comments. Living in Europe, I haven’t had any hamburger helper for ages, so that’s a good enough excuse for trying this.  

To reduce the ridiculous amount of fat:
1) Use a wok
2) Brown the onions without oil for about 5 minutes, stirring only occasionally. Then add salt and pepper and about 1 tablespoon of oil. Cook another few minutes. The beef fat on top of that is enough.  
3) Skip the bacon (time saver), add a teaspoon of smoked salt. Same flavor, less calories. 
4) Combine cream with milk. Or just use milk.  
5) You only need about half the cheese.  Really. (This is coming from a cheese addict). 

What is missing? Vegetables. I found that this could sparkle with broccoli, peppers, carrots, etc. mixed in. And I needed to add lemon juice for more acidity.  

You might need more fluid (broth, water, or milk) towards the end, so the noodles don’t burn before becoming al dente. 
You can substitute a scant teaspoon of garlic powder instead of using fresh garlic. (I had run out if fresh garlic).  

All in all I spent about 45-50 minutes cooking. It made enough for two mealtimes for three adults.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Fabulous... I used gochujang for the hot sauce. A new weeknight favorite in a hurry/

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
1/4 cup of Chalula ruined the meal. Next time I’ll use 1-2 tablespoons maximum. I wish the recipe had been more clear about that. Hamburger helper isn’t spicy at all (or nearly this salty) so I don’t really understand why that line of the recipe doesn’t suggest that it is highly optional. Instead the recipe seems to imply it would be season-less without it. 

I made a massive pot of cheesy meat that my children won’t be able to touch… what a waste.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Perfectly good omitting the bacon, excessive oil, and the wine. Just sub the wine for some extra broth. Add some umami seasoning as well.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Another calorie lightner is to use veggie crumbles in place of cow meat. Could add smoke as a flavor to avoid the piglet.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: This can easily be veganized by subbing vegan crumbles (or sausage) for the beef and sun dried tomatoes for the bacon.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
American cheese — in the NYT cooking section.  Pierre Franey is rolling in his grave.  
Hamburger Complicater indeed.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: American cheese (unlike cheddar) contains an emusifying agent called sodium citrate. In mac-and-cheese recipes, which this one essentially is, it helps keep the sauce creamy and gooey without breaking.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I could only allow myself about 2 tablespoons of this delicious sounding but astoindingly high sodium and calories meal.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I am overriding my earlier post. Yes the onions burned and the directions should not have allowed. However. I hate hamburger helper. I LOVE this hamburger helper. The trick is to use white wine vinegar. Mmmm

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: American cheese is often ultraprocessed. What would be a good substitute?

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: 2 slices of crumbled cooked bacon is plenty. Skip the American cheese and substitute a bit of mild and aged cheddar.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: We don't use heavy cream (I'm Italian) nor the bacon (I'm Italian) or the American and Cheddar chesses for the same reason.   We also use vegetarian cumbled "meat"   We cook the pasta separate and use the pasta water in lieu of stock/cream.   Lower fat, and better taste.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I've made this many times.  It has evolved to two strips of bacon, ground chicken, double the pasta, 1/2 and 1/2, and all the seasonings.  Freezes well.  All round winner.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: We have a spice-adverse family so skipped the hot sauce, subbed old bay for paprika because it’s what we had. Skipped bacon to make easier / save cost. Also added a bunch of spinach toward the end of the pasta cooking to make me feel like there was some healthy component to this dinner! Overall delicious.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I always use Impossible Meat, shredded cheddar, and velveeta (I'm GenX, I can't help it!). This is one of our favorites, so I always double it. That way, we can freeze the leftovers.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I subbed tomato sauce for the hot sauce and used just 1 teaspoon of smoked paprika to make it more kid-friendly. It was a hit! I think next time I might omit the bacon. Leftovers reheated well with a little chicken stock added to the pan to prevent scorching.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Would have been perfect as written (although I did appreciate the cooking reordering of the ingredients)! I did throw in a can of fire-roasted tomatoes, which we loved! I can't way that it made it better, since I didn't make it exactly as the recipe states, but it was wonderful!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: This recipe is fantastic. Yes, it's rich, but it's hamburger helper. Want to make it healthy? Sure, sub in a leaner meat, different cheeses, milk not cream, whatever. But I reckon it's best if you make it as is. If you have a normal spice tolerance, no, 1/4 to 1/3 C. hot sauce is not overdoing it at all. It comes out to a hot side of mild/low side of medium-ish heat level, and it's fantastic. Take the plunge--it's worth it.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Made this with ground turkey and no bacon, heavy cream or American cheese and it was STILL flavorful and rich. There is something essentially yummy about this combo that is not only about the fat.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
Read comments about how complicated it was. Not sure why, but then simplify the recipe. It does require exact anything. 

Leave out the bacon or use real bits, if that was the step that turned it more laborious. 

I made in one pot with ground Turkey added sherry instead. Real bacon bits, pre-shredded cheese, chose up American. A can of petite diced tomatoes for flavor and color. The pasta gets thrown in the mixture so no extra pot. 

Make it easy. It’s a very versatile and forgiving recipe.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Sorry to be a buzz kill, but just buy a box of Hamburger Helpet and  eat in 30 minutes.  If you’re going for a quick, easy meal you aren’t necessarily looking for a high tone dinner.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: This recipe produced a dish even more disgusting than the stuff from a box. I recommend serving it only to people you especially dislike. The odor lingered in the kitchen for more than two days.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I simply do not understand the comments complaining. Yes, you can adjust the cheese, cream ratios, etc. However, out of all the meals I make for teenagers for an after school program, this is by and far their favorite meal. It may not be for you, but hungry high schoolers will absolutely gobble it up.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Do the bacon separate. Even pouring the grease off, too much is left clinging to everything.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I’ve bought cheese in the US before, like in Vermont, but I have never heard of « American cheese. » Had to google it!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: A touch of tomato paste and a splash of Worcestershire sauce ups the umami nicely.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: This is delicious, but very salty. I’d make it with water instead of stock and refrain from adding any additional salt next time

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
This stuff is insanely good. Just right to cook after Christmas, before your resolutions kick in!
Based on other recommendations, I used only 3 Tbsp Cholula hot sauce -- resulting in super-flavorful, not terribly hot, sauce.  If I wanted significant heat, I'd add the additional Tbsp, or I'd leave it up to my guests at the table.
I might even add a veggie with the pasta; perhaps chopped cauliflower or shredded carrot.  Tonight I just served it with steamed broccolini alongside.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I made something like this and called it Pizza Noodles.  1 pound of ground meat, pizza seasoning, tomato sauce, and 1-2 cups of shredded cheese along with 3 cups of dried elbow macaroni. Kids loved it

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: This made a lot more than 4 servings for us. More like 8. I used just a few dashes of hot sauce as it was for kids. Very good! Will make again.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Everyone really liked it in our household. Personally the smoked paprika quantity was a little heavier that I liked and I will probably halve it next time or substitute with something else (regular paprika?). Also, I had too much liquid by the time the pasta was ready so I ladled out some liquid before it was done, but I enjoyed this being a one-pot meal regardless.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Try reducing the heat, and making sure you fully reduce at eat point before the chicken stock is added.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I’m all for hamburger helper, but American cheese = ewwwwww. Any recs for a substitute? Would just a regular cheddar work or does it require something with a different consistency/composition? Thanks :)

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: yes, the american cheese from the *deli* is what is meant to be used here due to its amazing ability to melt like a dream. the recipe isn't calling for Kraft American "Cheese" slices. it makes all the difference, trust me :)

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
Am cheese is often used because the additives that give it its rubbery texture also make it melt without getting stringy and will help the cheddar in this dish remain creamy too.
I worked in a deli in college; the Am cheese sliced off a 5 lb loaf is exactly the same as the individual slices in a cello pack.  Either will work, and it depends on how much you want left over after making this recipe, but you really shouldn't skip it.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: To Rachel: Whether from the deli or Kraft singles, American cheese is a processed "cheese product". It's the same thing. Yes, it does melt nicely  but it is highly processed.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Made with Beyond Meat (kept the bacon, there was leftover rashers in the freezer), didn’t drain off the fat.  half and half for the cream, for the hot sauce we used a combination of harissa, sriracha and gochujang cause that’s what we had on-hand. tasty! funnily, never had a hamburger helper from the box before. considering trying it now, lol!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: This is a great recipe! Just made it tonight and it was delicious. The only thing I would make differently for next time - instead of 8 ounces of pasta (about 2 cups of dry pasta) I would make it with 16 ounces of pasta (about 4 cups of dry pasta). There was too much sauce leftover for me so I cooked more pasta and added it in. It’s perfect and great for meal prep on Sunday!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Sorry - I meant 3 cups of pasta! Not 4! 4 would be too much.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Delicious comfort food for a cold rainy day. I used ground turkey to lighten it up and smoked gouda instead of the American cheese. I also limited the hot sauce because we are spice wimps. This will be on regular rotation at my house. Yum!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: This was delicious. Doubled the recipe for a large group, using three times the amount of pasta (6 cups dried) called for. I also used half-sharp paprika and half the amount of hot sauce as serving to kids. Next time I will add a can of diced tomatoes - needs a little acidity to cut the richness. Served with a simple salad dressed with lemon, evoo and shaved Parmesan. Very happy group!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Comfort food at it's best.  I did decrease the hot sauce as recommended by others.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Isn't this what my mother called American chop suey? Omit the spices and the cream, add in a can of Campbell's Cream of Tomato soup and you've got it, babe!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Made this last night and it was seriously good. Doubled the recipe & cooked the pasta separately so it wouldn't turn to mush. Reduced chicken broth to 1 cup and could have left it out completely. Let it simmer for a while to reduce. There was a significant amount of oil floating on top - used 90/10 ground beef and drained it, so perhaps it came from the cheddar & cream. Used sweet paprika and added hot sauce to our own servings (I'm a wimp). Definitely a do-again!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: My general rule it to always cook the first time as written. This time, I should have taken the comments more seriously. I used a 1/4 c of tabasco as written. Way too spicy for my taste. I would think less than an 1/8 would property spice up this dish. Also- after it sat on the stove in the Le Crueset braiser covered up on the stove, it got super greasy as others have mentioned.  Maybe due to the cheddar? Overall, very rich and tasy, but would be so much better without so much hot sauce.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I substituted some diced salami for the bacon and used   a mild french cheese plus some cheddar and mozzarella and added a can of petite diced tomatoes: (cos hello I hamburger helper). Oh and BTW 16 oz (more broth /water) macaroni. Those substituting veg for pasta are in the wrong recipe. Hey it was enjoyable.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: A super rich and delicious comfort food. I omitted the hot sauce completely after seeing previous comments and it was still very tasty.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I guess this turned out about how I expected, it was tasty but it does mimic a boxed food and there's only so much to like about that. I added broccoli and highly recommend that. Also do not skip pouring of the grease. There is a lot. I made it with banza noodles and that worked fine. Overall it was good, a fun thing to try but not a keeper for me.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
Haha! Before Hamburger Helper there were recipes using Campbell's condensed soups and Velveeta cheese homemakers passed around. There were even charts to follow that adapt a basic formula for creating one-dish meal casseroles with a protein, vegetable, and carb in a sauce. 

Ground beef, condensed tomato soup, simple seasoning, Velveeta, macaroni. I was in heaven when my mother made this.

There are infinite ways to riff on this recipe that aren't so chef-y, ingredient- & calorie-heavy.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: The funny thing is that at end of the day it tastes like... Hamburger Helper.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Used 2 % milk instead of cream, 10 oz penne instead of Elbows. Needed to add little more (1/2c) stock at end as penne sucked up alot of liquid when cooking.  Only used splash of hot sauce to make more kid friendly. Used 2 cups cheddar since its what I had on hand. All above worked great.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Since there are a million different versions of hot sauce out there, and having no idea what the author used, I went with trusty Tabasco. Having read the reviews, however, I added only 1 tablespoon.  My family commented on the heat - but in a good way. It's discernible but not in the least overwhelming. I'm adding this to my cheat night rotation.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I used 93% ground beef and pre-cooked bacon to cut down on the amount of fat.  To lessen the heat, I decreased the amount of hot sauce to 1/8 cup and used added tomato sauce for the remainder.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Has anyone done this in the instant pot and has the adjusted version for that?

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: This just wasn’t that good

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: This is absolutely heavenly.  I did use only 1/2 tbsp hot sauce because I am cooking for my husband and myself and my three kids who do not appreciate heat the way I do, and it was still AMAZING.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: We made this exactly as written (except we skipped the chives, the one green thing, ha ha ha) and used 1/4 cup Frank's for our hot sauce, and it was so ridiculously good that we all just confessed to each other how much we've thought about it since! I would consider skipping the bacon. Not to be teetotally, but just because I wasn't sure it added that much. But I might be wrong.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Deadly, but worth it. Make sure you use yellow/orange cheese. I used white, which looked awful. I was in a hurry and neglected to read the recipe carefully. I made numerous missteps which were fortunately salvageable. Next time will be much better.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Instead of hot sauce, I substituted a few red pepper flakes.  That gave it just enough kick.  I also did not serve immediately because we were waiting for someone and the flavor improved.  Delicious and all enjoyed.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I tend to like my bacon in larger chunks so I threw those in early to get a little crisp and then used that to sauté the onions with only a little added oil. Also, this easily made six servings to make it a little easier on the waist.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Trying it tomorrow with an eye towards (a) how it keeps/reheats and how much can be pre-prepped & finished for a really quick weeknight meal.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I made this last night and after it had been sitting for an hour it had really set up. You might make it in advance and gently reheat for your dinner. We all loved it!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I made this - it was quite wonderful and addictive.  I omitted the hot sauce as I didn't want a vinegar flavor to mar the taste.  I browned the bacon as the onion cooked and then added the ground beef.  Its a keeper!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Thanks to everyone's notes. This is superb! I did make sure to give us small portions and left the rest in the kitchen so we wouldn't be tempted by seconds. Used 3 tablespoons of Frank's, and it was perfect. I am somewhat embarrassed to say that this is my recent favorite recipe!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
greatly enjoyed this with some slight modifications.

cavatappi instead of macaroni, regular paprika to help with spice, and no bacon to help with the calories. 

the real secret ingredient? a hit of rice vinegar to brighten it up and add a bit of funk. game changing. 

was doubtful it would come together when i added the pasta but a high boil brought things home.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: 1/4 cup hot sauce (we used Tapatio) was a bit much, I think - there's a slow burn heat that lingers. And I am regretting not taking the time to drain the fat off (Step 4), as the finished product was a little greasy. Having said that, it really was delicious! Rave reviews from the family. Not an every day kind of meal, but good stick to your bones comfort food.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Honestly, next time I would just cook some ground beef and add it to leftover Mac and cheese that I make in my Ninja Foodi. If it isn’t meaty enough then I might add the American cheese. This was too much effort for something not nutritious.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Beyond burger would work well in this. I've used it in everything that I used to use ground meats for and it's wonderful. At first, made me a little guilty that I was enjoying the meatiness!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Regarding the concern over calories, I recommend taking a page out of Kenji's book and using evaporated milk instead of cream.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Way more than four servings. We had it for SuperBowl dinner and it was a big hit with the kids. I might try half and half next time to lighten it up a bit.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: We made the recipe exactly as listed (except with less hot sauce) and it hurt our stomachs. There was literally a pool of oil at the bottom of our bowls. I suggest reading other recommendations and cutting down on the cream (or using half-and-half) and reducing the cheese. That being said, my 2- and 6-year olds loved it.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Made exactly as specified in the recipe and it is completely delicious. Total success, obviously not for everyday consumption.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Sweet mother of GOD that's good (says the Brooklyn Jew). Note that you can drop the American cheese and add a couple of teaspoons of sodium citrate instead, sticking with cheddar or whatever other cheese you'd like. I stirred in a big pile of spinach right before serving to add color and a plausible-vegetable component, then fed it to a kid just back from rowing practice - suffice it to say it's now going into steady rotation.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Only changes made were: used 1/2 onion diced small (cooked quicker!), diced precooked bacon and added after hamburger was cooked + a little bit of reserved bacon fat, and about 1 TBSP Sriracha for hot sauce.  Tasted really good.  But the more I ate, the less I liked it. ?  Oh well.  Don't know until you try it.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I made this as written except I did not add hot sauce -- -we put it on the table at serving but it was ultra-rich and wasn't necessary. I've never had hamburger helper but my husband did as a kid, and my daughter never eats any two ingredients together, everything MUST be deconstructed,  (and that includes pizza) but everyone really liked it. It's not health food, I mean, it's pretty gross, but I had it with a glass of good red wine, absolutely no vegetables and would totally make it again.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: For a vegan version, I replaced the chicken broth with vegetable broth (made with 3 cups of water and 1.5 tablets of vegetable bouillon), the heavy cream with full-fat coconut milk, and the ground meat with Impossible ground meat. I also replaced the hot sauce with 2 tbs (or so) of tomato paste and skipped the bacon. Seasoned to taste and topped it with "Follow your Heart" (vegan) parmesan cheese and voila! The final result was fantastic.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Loved this! Lightened it up with ground turkey and Greek yogurt to replace heavy cream.  Still tasted delicious.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
Didn’t change a thing, and it’s delicious!!
However, my family loves spicy food. And this definitely has a kick. I would lower the amount of hot sauce for guests who don’t love spicy food.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I thought the order of steps in this recipe was strange? Why not cook the bacon first, remove from pot, and then saute onions in the bacon fat? Adding the bacon along with the ground beef made it difficult to render. Plus, then you don't need to add canola oil.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
I think cutting raw bacon is the absolute worst. I did it & would skip it next time
Didn't have hot paprika 2t pap + 1/2t cayenne
This recipe doesn't tell you to season the beef when you cook it. PLEASE season the beef while you cook it
Cover the pot when cooking the noodles, too much water evaporated during this time
I would use a full cup of cream 
Used 1/3 c Franks red hot This dish IS NOT SPICY
Garnished with a dollop of sour cream and it made it taste more hamburger helper-y 
dish = average

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I'm not sure the bacon was completely necessary. I was not convinced this recipe would hit the mark but it does and elevated to meet my adult flavor expectations. Add some frozen corn at the end to kick the childhood nostalgia up an extra notch.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Used gochujang paste, (2T), rather than hot sauce, added bag of shaved Brussels sprouts from trader Joes, half n half rather than heavy cream.  Also used bacon fat for half the oil at the start for the onions. Was really rich and good.  Liked the cauliflower substitution earlier...

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Is there a vegetarian version of this recipe? Cause it looks so comforting and worth trying!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: When a dish like this  like calls for a small amount of bacon I often use leftover bacon grease in place of the oil. Just a couple of tablespoons here. You get the flavor without the fuss.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: This is delicious, I couldn’t bring myself to use cream but it was perfect with whole milk.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Absolutely fantastic!  I eliminated the American cheese, just to lighten it up, and that was a good call...plenty rich without that ingredient.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: BIG HIT WITH THE KIDDOS!!! (I subbed in half n' half for heavy cream, and reduced the hot sauce to 1/8 cup.)

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
Listen to the comments about the hot sauce. My husband loves spicy food and I read the comments and lowered to 2T. That was PLENTY spicy and next time would use less. 

Also agree that 1/4 cup oil is too much for the onion. 1T was plenty. 

It was really good and would definitely make again.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I thought this was delicious and so did my son.  It was very forgiving.  I subbed the following items because it was all I had, ground turkey, half and half instead of cream and all grated "mexican" blend cheese, I didn't have American.  I'm sort of a baby when it comes to hot sauce but used a couple good squirts of sriracha and it was perfect for me,  Everything else I did exactly like the recipe.  Next time I might try impossible "beef" for my vegie kids.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: This is definitely a method rather than a strict recipe...I was intrigued to see it since we have been using our own very similar version of this technique for years, usually with more of a southwestern slant, but there are infinite variations.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Only difference I would do is cook the bacon with the onions, rendering the oil unnecessary and make for crisper bacon!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I did that too! I think it really added to the overall flavor.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Delicious as written, but easy to treat this as a guideline recipe and add or change if you want/have different ingredients.  Every combo is its own delicious creation.  No ground beef, then try ground turkey, chicken or sausage.  Use Beef stock if you don't have chicken.  Don't have smoked Paprika, try a spoonful of smoky BBQ sauce.  Use any allium you have, I actually prefer scallion.  Don't have wine, try 7 oz. water with 1 oz. wine vinegar.  It's an easy comfort dish to personalize.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Obviously, you’re not going to cook this weekly, or even monthly. But as a once in a great while comfort food dish, make exactly as is. I used 1/4 cup Sriracha and followed all other instructions. Divine. Oh, and def use American cheese from the deli, not the plastic Kraft slices!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: This was so incredibly good. My husband and I both struggled to stop eating. I am really glad I read the comments before cooking, especially about letting the onions cook properly and adjusting the hot sauce. I was feeding children, so I did Cholula and used less. It was plenty for me. I let the meat cook long enough to get crusty so much so that I actually didn't have much fat to discard. Used a LeCreuset Dutch oven to cook, perfect size and perfect heat retention.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Subs to dial back the heat, because children: smoked paprika for hot, and 2 T Cholula hot sauce. I added 2 t of apple cider vinegar and more stock to account for the liquid and tang missing from not adding the full amount of sauce. I had some pinot noir 'aging' in the fridge so I used that instead of white wine. It's rich, yes, but SO tasty. The recipe served 5 people for dinner, there's easily another 3 servings. I also cooked the bacon 1st to get it crispy- that really added to the flavor.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Delicious. I cut the hot sauce and hot paprika in half, and it was plenty spicy! Added a dollop of light sour cream on top. Might try plain tomato sauce instead of hot sauce when we are looking for something mild in the future. Love one-pot meals- this one is a keeper.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
This is so delicious! Yes, time-consuming. Yes, fattening. But what did you expect? You saw the ingredients, and hamburger helper isn’t something I’d ever consider to be healthy anyway.
Made as written. I used a scant 1/3 cup of Frank’s Red Hot and still think it could be spicier but I suppose it’s a matter of tolerance, i.e. weak palates! 
More than 4 servings if paired with something green on the side. If you completely changed the ingredients you’re not really reviewing the recipe mmkay?

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I never had hamburger helper growing up but this was *amazing*. Used less oil for the onions (around 2Tbsp) which was plenty. The bacon I got was really fatty and with 80/20 beef I had to remove over a cup of grease halfway through in order for my meat to brown. Used only a teaspoon of hot sauce for my husband who can't eat spicy things, but loved the smokiness of the paprika. A warm, delicious, comfort food recipe!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I made this for Election Night and used half & half instead of heavy cream and used a mixture of cheddar & Colby cheeses as well as the AM cheese. It was a hit!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: If there was ever a time to eat your feelings, it's now. If there was ever a recipe to help you do that, it's this. Soooo good. Between me and my husband, four servings quickly became two with a little left over. Used milk with cornstarch to thicken instead of cream. Didn't have American cheese so added a bit more cheddar. Used homemade fermented hot sauce (ok, sorry, I'm bragging here) that took it to another level. So much smokey, cheesy, meaty goodness.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: This is one of the few recipes in NYT that I did not like at all.  It is time-consuming to make and entirely too rich. If you have any digestive or coronary problems I would let this one go!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
I never had Hamburger Helper. And if it tastes anything like this I’m not missing anything. My son and husband said it tasted like a bad cheesesteak. (We are from Philly.) My son even put ketchup on it. Ew. 

I followed the recipe except for a little less hot sauce. Didn’t like the slimy mouth feel and it tasted overwhelmingly of American cheese. 

My mom used to make a homemade one-pot beef-a-roni. It was fabulous cafeteria food and significantly fewer calories and time investment.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I will start by saying that I don't eat ground beef. I will then say that this recipe is astonishingly good, even without. I made this for myself and my son, cooking the beef in a separate pan and stirring it in to the pot after i scooped out my portion. I omitted the bacon (I just didn't have any on hand), but otherwise followed the recipe. We differ from those who found it too spicy. We went for the 1/4 measure of hot sauce and thought the flavor disappeared. We'll do 1/3 next time!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I made this using soy-chorizo instead of meat for a vegetarian version - and it was AMAZING! I halved the oil in the beginning, and used a mixture of cheddar, gouda, and pepper jack for the cheeses (just what I had on hand), and I will definitely be making it again. The soyrizo worked perfectly in the recipe.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Loved this recipe! I’m a vegetarian so I omitted the bacon and used Impossible plant based meat for the ground beef and my meat eating boyfriend still loved it. I like spicy food, but 1/4 cup hot sauce was a bit too spicy for me so next time I think I would do 1/8 cup hot sauce. I love how well American cheese melts, but I really think you could use any cheese you fancy. Definitely recommend!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Husband loved it, I thought it was just okay, but I ate it as I focused on my impending death so I just wasn't in the mood (waiting on some test results). Subbed Beyond Beef for the meat, used just 8 oz. Made up for the rest of the pound with roasted butternut squash. No bacon. Used 1/4 cup Frank's Red Hot sauce, had to use more on my plate to remind myself I'm still alive. Plus my hubbie doesn't like hot stuff (myself excepted). I'd like to try the leftovers after I get the test results back.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I hope you're ok!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Maybe it was the negative test results, or maybe the flavors just had more time to meld, but the leftovers were delicious.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: It was good the first night, but makes quite poor leftovers as the heavy cream turns to grease when reheating.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
We’re vegetarians. While omitting the bacon I kept the reminder of the recipe intact. Subbing in a 12 ounce brick of Impossible Burger for the pound of beef. Consequently, calculator app open and everything was multiplied by 0.75. 
It was still kind of bland and colorless. I’m thinking of adding frozen peas at the end just to inject some color. And since my husband thought it was terrific, there will be a next time.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Everyone likes this.  Eliminated the bacon so no need to drain fat, saving time. Used 1 and 1/2 lb ground beef.  Took 45 minutes to make tops.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I omit the cheese at the end all together. I think the recipe is great without it! Delicious, simple and great for leftover lunches during the week.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Turned out great but very rich, which was expected, but maybe a bit too rich for my taste. If I make again, I'll cut out the bacon and some of the oil and use half and half. I used 1/4 cup hot sauce and thought it provided the perfect amount of kick. As others mentioned it is labor intensive but nice that it is truly a one pot dish.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Made this with a few changes - I had 16 oz of pasta so did 4 cups broth, a pint of cream, 1.5 lbs of ground beef, 8 slices of bacon, 2 cups of cheddar, 6 slices of American cheese, and two tablespoons of sriracha. This was just super delicious - it took about two hours so I would have started it sooner but omgoodness it was just great

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Just finished up a vegetarian version, made with Beyond ground meet. Delicious, and actually a pretty fast weeknight dinner, especially since the pasta cooks in the sauce. I used a mix if cheeses from what we had on hand--cheddar, parmesan, and some pepper jack. Worked great. I think next time I'll also stir in some frozen peas so that I feel like I'm eating some veggies.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: used impossible beef, excluded bacon and american cheese. maybe it's my fault for making those alterations but this was unpleasant to eat, imo. my family loved it.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: There is zero reason to cook the onion and then cook the bacon. Cook the bacon and onion together, add the garlic, then the ground beef. Then pour off excess fat and proceed.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I cook the bacon first (like it to have some more crispy bits), then I cook the onion and garlic in the bacon fat. This way the 1/4 of oil is not even needed, and no need to strain off the really flavorful bacon fat (used Gunthorp farms this time), I try to use a really high quality variety from my bacon-of-the month club.  I omit the hot sauce and add it at the table- my kids don't love quite that much spice, but love the dish.  Otherwise I follow the recipe and it's a weeknight favorite.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I must say, this is just not worth it.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Followed this recipe exactly as is except used 1/2 the onion amount (fiancé doesn’t like them) and used Kraft American slices and sharp cheddar. Wasn’t too spicy and turned out awesome. Obviously is an indulgent meal but what do you expect from the culinary guru of Shake Shack! Was wonderful for a once in a while occasion

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I liked this but I didn't quite follow the directions. I made it because I didn't want to go to the store and had a limited selection of dinner possibilities. We don't eat meat, so I skipped the bacon and used non-meat burgers, which we think are very good. I also used vegetable broth. I used Crystal, which is milder than most hot sauces.  I only had cheddar -- no American. Next time, I'll skip the wine. Even though it cooked a long time, it gave a sour taste I didn't like. No salt or pepper.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: As anyone made a vegetarian version with tofu or meat substitute ? Asking for a friend 🤣

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I used Impossible Meat, omitted the bacon, but used chicken stock. It was divine. I imagine you'd get the same result with one of those veggie chicken stock substitutes.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Made this as directed, substituted beef with veggie beef and bacon with smoked tofu. Didn't have american cheese, put in more cheddar. Only had mature white cheddar. Was delish!! Didn't have yellow onion, put in 4-5 shallots.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Cooking the bacon with the ground beef leaves an end product full of fatty, flabby bacon and for a Jack Sprat sort like me it was close to inedible. Made for a big Sunday family dinner and the general reviews added up to meh. Way too much work for that kind of feedback so, one and done recipe for this guy.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I omitted the meat and cream and used soy crumbles instead—So a lot less caloric and also vegetarian. I’d reduce hot sauce and salt next time but regardless, this was delicious!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Needs the pasta amount DOUBLED

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I followed the recipe very precisely and found it extraordinarily greasy. It was also rather unappealing on the plate. There are probably better uses of your time and ingredients. Mac and cheese and a burger on the side with some nice, acidic salsa?

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Did you drain the pot after the meat was browned?  I drained it thoroughly and it was not greasy. I like to use ground beef from the butcher's counter as it is usually less fatty than prepacked ground beef.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: There is none higher as my Double wide never smelled better.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Made this with Beyond beef and added a can of whole tomatoes (rinsed and chopped), skipped the bacon and was out of smoked paprika so used regular instead. Used Frank's Red Hot for the hot sauce and ended up with a super delightful vegetarian version of this. There are a million ways this could be tweaked. Next time I might throw in a handful of peas towards the end. Consistency improved as it cooled, at first it seemed like too much sauce, but like most one-pot pasta dishes it thickened up.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
I baked the bacon and saved it to crumble on top at the end so it wouldn't get mushy. 

Also I definitely think it needs the full amount of hot sauce. We used Texas Pete and the vinegar helps cut the richness from the cream and cheese.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
This one’s a one-note gut bomb. I fried the bacon first, and reserved it until the end. I drained all the bacon fat and as much of the ground beef fat as possible. But the dish still was heavy. The processed cheese product helps with the sauce’s texture, but adds nothing else of value. We ate it with green beans, and agreed it desperately needs veg. 

One other thing: it did not taste better as leftovers the next day. 

Not with the calories.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Loved this! I followed the recipe as written except that I subbed Beyond Beef and it came out great. It was really rich and satisfying. I froze two portions!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: It tasted like Tabasco. Barely edible.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I don't know what possessed me to make this recipe. I'm a good cook, I followed the directions precisely except I reduced the hot sauce and garlic. Ultimately the dich came out as it should. I made a poor choice. I found it  to be disgusting and a total waste of time and recourses. Maybe I'm not a Hamburger Helper person.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Only adding this note as it is more current. Looking today for some one pot ideas and comfort. Snowing and my husband just had joint surgery. I pretty much followed recipe but as he had a spice restriction used less than 1/4 cup of Chilula and regular paprika. It was great and everyone loved. I do love reading the other comments. We also had been gifted some American cheese so that was easy. I would make this again for comfort.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
this recipe was the worst i’ve cooked on here. i don’t believe for one second that priya - who i normally love - eats this food. the end product is fatty, cheesy, melty slop. it is truly reminiscent of cafiteria food and not hamburger helper. 

bacon, wine and american cheese is a combo i could never imagine together and for good reason.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: The smell that your kitchen gets when you are sautéing the garlic, onions, beef and bacon is like nothing I’ve ever experienced.  To those who made a reduced fat version- brace yourself.  I have made this a few times and FORGOT to drain ANY of the fat from the meat. And we are the whole pot! Sorry not sorry! It’s a forgiving recipe and to say it’s delicious is an understatement.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Served this for dinner tonight. I doubled the recipe to stretch this out over the week. We have a 5 year old so I used 8 oz of plain tomato sauce in lieu of hot sauce and I subbed the hot paprika for Hungarian, added a dash of smoked paprika. In accordance I then seasoned with salt and pepper as I went, checking flavors throughout. I did not drain the grease, I never do. It makes a mess. My husband and I added numerous generous dashes of hot sauce to our dishes. Will make again. Insanely good.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I made this vegetarian by leaving out the bacon and using a pound of Beyond Meat and vegetarian chicken-style broth. 1/4 cup of Cholula hot sauce added a good amount of spice. My gluten free elbow pasta was done in 7 minutes. Used a couple of minced scallions for chives, and had only 3 slices American cheese; it still was plenty cheesy. This made 6 servings.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: My family loves this recipe.  We call it bougie hamburger helper.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: This was so delicious! Flavor is built by getting a mailard reaction out of each addition of ingredient (caramelize the onions! Sear the meat!) and it’s so worth the time it takes to complete this dish. I prepped and measured all my ingredients beforehand, so the actually cooking part was pretty idle and it ultimately made for a laidback kitchen experience. I absolutely recommend this very special treat of a meal!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Don’t make this if you’re worried about the calorie content. This is a delicious, heavy meal that is a new comfort food favorite for me. Delicious, however I would drain it a lot more than suggested due to how greasy it can end up. Also don’t use all the hot sauce, as it ends up overwhelming the dish. I would make this again on a rainy, down day to bring me right back up!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Used ground turkey instead of beef, half and half instead of heavy cream. Omitted the bacon. It is SO delicious.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: This was really awful, beige in colour, , no flavour, took an hour to cook and my husband wouldn’t eat it. Sorry, most things here are good, this would be better made from a store bought packet.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
For Instant Pot:
- Follow Steps 1-5 as-is, using Saute on IP.
- At Step 6, slight sub with ~2C broth (instead of 3), ~1/4C hot sauce and 1/2C heavy cream.
- For Step 7, stir to combine as recipe states, then cook on High Pressure for 4 mins.
- Quick release, return to Saute, add cheese and adjust seasonings.

I prefer making it on the stovetop, but this saved about 15-20 minutes of cook time for me and still tasted delish.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Absolutely delicious! Hate when people write in saying they subbed this for that, and that for this making it a completely different recipe, BUT...being a vegetarian, I used beyond meat, a bit of meatless bacon and a TBLE spoon or so of tabasco (not a fan of a lot of heat and didn't have any other hot sauce in the house). Brought me right back to my Hamburger Helper childhood, only better!!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Can use vegetarian mince, but add a tablespoon of marmite to correct umami.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I cooked the bacon first so I could use the grease to sautée the onions and garlic. Also helps to cook alone in order to get it nice and crispy. I also used 2-3x the garlic and added Dijon mustard.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: One small change: rend the bacon as the first step, take the crispy bits out with a slotted spoon, then use the bacon grease in the pan to coop the onion and garlic. Throw the crispy bacon bits back in at the end. No sense in wasting good bacon.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: We’re kosher so I skip the bacon and use Beyond Beef, leaving everything else as is. It’s a crowd fave.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I have made this more than once. Hot sauce makes it taste nothing like Hamburger Helper. I substituted Worcestershire Sauce for the hot sauce. Brought me back to my childhood.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Extremely rich, as you might expect. Still delicious, but I would make it with a veggie side :)

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I failed to read the comments before cooking and added 6 tablespoons of Frank’s Red Hot. It wasn’t too hot to eat, but the sauce did seem to drown out the subtleties of caramelized onion, white wine, bacon, smoked paprika and bay leaf I had just spent an hour building. Next time I’ll use less. Still very tasty.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I will add that the recipe makes a lot more than 4 servings. I’m not fussy about calories or fat, but this dish is so extremely rich it’s hard to eat more than a small amount. It left me craving some broccoli. I’d probably pare the recipe down a little next time.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I should have added to my own comment that I only put about a tbsp of Franks and just served with the bottle for people to add. The flavor was truly nice with the white wine, paprika and bay leaf in the cooking. I agree that it makes quite a lot but it reheats nicely in the microwave.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
This was extra delicious, the cup of white wine reduction and bay leaf truly made the taste into Hambourgeois Helper. 
I used all the cheeses, it's rich so I served with a crunchy iceberg lettuce and red radish salad and a side of asparagus as well.  Worth the time spent, I will be making this again for sure.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: What is American cheese? Is this the same as Kraft singles? I’ve never been able to figure this out. Can I just use more real cheddar instead?

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: There is no way you can make this in 1.5 hours. Unless you have a sous chef...

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I made this using beyond beef and a vegan bacon, still heavy but also very satisfying.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Still not a fan boxed or homemade. I gave it an honest try though. It is best served right away doesn’t seem to look that appetizing after sitting out for a bit.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
Recipe note in paper:
Use beefless ground beef 
Skip white wine
Use cornstarch slurry instead of cream
Just use cheddar cheese

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Too much work for a basic mac and cheese with  some ground beef thrown in.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Have made this several times and its delicious, though you have to chuckle at adding wine and bay leaf to a hamburger helper recipe. Only changes are to reduce hot sauce to 2 TBSP (Frank's) and throw in a head of cut up broccoli with the pasta.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: This is fantastic and not really as much work as some others have suggested. It’s delicious and makes so much. Question from a Canadian- can someone define “American cheese”? I used a couple of pieces of block Velveeta along with shredded cheddar.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: American cheese is a processed cheese usually made from Cheddar or Colby.  Velveeta would be close, but I think Velveeta incorporates maybe Swiss?  They both melt more smoothly than a proper cheese.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
I cut the smoked paprika by at least half and it could have been less. Probably doubled the garlic. 
Subbed white wine for extra chicken stock and a splash of whit wine vinegar. 
Had half and half so used that in lieu of cream. 
Eyeballed the hot sauce, Frank’s, probably 2 Tbsp. 
Didn’t have American cheese so stirred in a 1/4 cup of plain yogurt to up the creaminess at the end. 
Forgot to add the chives, but plenty of leftovers so next serving.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: This was really good! I used smoked paprika and a bit under an 8th c Franks Red Hot for the hot sauce. I also added an extra c of water   1/4 c of heavy cream in order to increase the pasta to 10 oz. My young children genuinely enjoyed this. This could feed a family of 4 for 2 days. I served a half portion of this recipe with a large portion of mixed vegetables. One kid mixed it all together & loved it. This one is a keeper!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I made this recipe exactly as instructed my first go around and can report that I wouldn’t change anything. Some recipes are just meant to be comfort meals and not be healthy, just my opinion! Definitely get some quality sliced American cheese from your deli section, not Kraft or Velveeta singles. It will make all the difference. I too was worried about the hot sauce being too much. I used 1/4 cup of Frank’s original and it was perfect.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
People can say what they want about this recipe - about how it is hard - about how it is too much - etc etc. 
But what is missed is how perfect it is in every way - comforting, delicious, flexible. Make it how you want, with what you’ve got! It’s even good if you totally mess it up! Use whatever noodle, don’t add hot sauce if you don’t want, use whatever cheese, season to taste. 
Ok, had to get that off my chest. The end.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
solid. i added extra pasta (or maybe was measuring wrong-8 ounces didnt seem enough)

followed recipe except used red peper flakes instead of hot sauce due to what i had.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
I made this twice. My picky grandson asked for it the second time. 
First time as written. Too rich. Second time left out cheeses and less cream. He loved it. Calling it hamburger helper is a bit misleading, I think.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Extremely greasy and unnecessarily complicated. Wouldn't make this again.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: brilliant - cook ahead (apart from the pasta) and finish off on the dinky gas ring in the camper van

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Made this in the middle of summer like a psycho. It was a crowd pleaser. I added chopped kale and highly recommend.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
Made after reading suggestions. What I incorporated: 
Grind meat and bacon so are finer. Beef didn't work for me so I cooked on griddle and chopped as it cooked Made bacon in dutch oven then removed, chopped in chopper. Ground a bit too much. Next time less chop or leave out. 
Sauteed onion and garlic in bacon fat, then deglazed with white wine, a flavor must, THEN added beef & bacon and followed the rest of recipe. Added more pasta. Forgot suggested diced tomatoes, adding to leftovers.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I recently tried out what I'd rename the "Homemade Hamburger Helper - Cheeseburger Macaroni" recipe. The outcome was impressive, with flavors even better after a night in the fridge. The cheesy, comforting goodness truly surpassed the classic store-bought version. However, the hands-on cooking time was around one and a half hours. While the end result justified the effort, pondering whether the extra time was worthwhile becomes a consideration. I will keep both the recipe and the "box" on hand.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Brown the meats first, then use the drippings to caramelize the onion.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Has anyone made this with plant-based 'meat?'

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: We vegetarianise it by using vegi bacon and vegi meat crumble and man does this thing taste delicious!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Hum, bacon drippings, Kentucky Gold!  Mamie always cooked the bacon first, then the onions no added olive oil.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: How could I make this meatless?  Sub eggplant and mushrooms for the bacon and beef?  Or tofu?

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I'm going to try this with a brick of Impossible and use smoked paprika when the onion is sauteed. And replace the chicken stock with veggie stock.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I was so excited. But pick your hot sauce carefully. I used Tabasco and it was just too acidic. And then I read the calorie count. Sigh. It’s sadly going down the disposal.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Very rich. Unlike many here, I really thought it needed much more tang and heat, but we just added extra hot sauce separately to each serving to each person's taste.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
Made this last night for our family of five. It was enjoyed by all, from kids through grandma. Made it mostly as written, although like some below I skipped the oil and cooked the bacon first. Then took it out with slotted spoon and cooked the onions/garlic in the bacon fat. Added the bacon back in after the beef was browned. 

We did not find the dish to be greasy at all. I used the "folded up paper towel with tongs" approach to getting rid of almost all of the fat after the meat was browned.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
Super tasty!!!
Takes a lot of time to prep the ingredients as well as cooking time, so would not recommend for a weekday meal, unless you start early!
Worth the wait though, I made a version with gluten free noodles and it turned out really well! 
10/10 recommend this recipe.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: This is basically a bolognese sauce with cheese added. To get rid of a good deal of the fat:  Cool and then refrigerate after Step 6.  Scoop out grease when it hardens and then continue with the recipe.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I left out the hot sauce and everyone just added it to their own serving to suit their taste.   I’m not familiar with American Cheese, thought it was referring to Kraft cheese slices so that’s what I used.  It was delicious.   I will definitely make this again.   PS What is American Cheese?  Asking for Canadians.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: A quick note on the hot sauce in this recipe.  Many comments refer to using Frank's Hot Sauce for this dish.  Frank's has a staggering amount of salt in it.  A much better choice is Tapatio - half the salt and better flavor, in my opinion.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Toast some panko bread crumbs in the drained off oil.  Adds a great textural component.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: love topping my pasta dishes w panko and finishing in oven:))

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I was motivated by the one-pot recipe, but as indicated by others, it was time consuming. It was an active >1.5 hours in the kitchen and the eaters indicated it was tasty. Definitely a very generous "4 servings".

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
Regretably not a success in my household.  Made per recipe - only thing we changed was using 1/2 & 1/2 instead of heavy cream (because that's what was in the fridge).  Had to reduce the hot sauce to 2 Tbs (no snarky comments about white bread palates, guys, we're in Texas where hot sauce is HOT.)  The flavors just didn't blend very well - in fact my husband suggested leaving out the cheese and adding some tomato.  (I think at that point we'd essentially have a 
Bolognese).

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
I made this today. It came out more like elbow pasta helper, not hamburger helper.  
Wasn’t crazy about the final flavor.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Although my husband and I choked this down for dinner one night, we threw the rest out. If I had to guess, I'd say it was the combination of the hot sauce and the American cheese that we found disgusting. I followed the recipe exactly as written and won't make it again, but if I did, I would definitely omit those two items.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: This is a great recipe. I used olive oil and crushed basil instead of Bay Leaf. I used two teaspoons of Harissa and which gave a great flavor. Used pancetta instead of bacon. Used ancho taco seasoning instead of paprika. Delish.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: This tasted like the 80s, but better.  It was a bit too heavy for us --- I'd probably skip the American cheese next time, but I probably won't make this again.  We used Impossible Burger here, and it was just fine.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
Next time will use 2 cups water/broth, not 3, with the 8oz of pasta. Much too soupy. Tastes great.
Leaving overnight so pasta will absorb liquid.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Great recipe! May I suggest: cooking the bacon, if using, in the oven and adding it in. I just love crispy bacon.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Can this be frozen?

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: My question too!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
That’s a quote from my husband after his 3rd helping. This is definitely not something we’d eat every night but it hit the comfort food spot. 

Cooked the bacon in strips first and sautéed the onion and garlic in the fat. Other than that (less hot sauce and subbed milk for half the cream) I followed the recipe but cut the time down by cooking over slightly higher heat. Delish.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: This is totally delicious and something I've been making to take as comfort food to friends.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: So delicious but also made me hate myself. I made this nearly as written except that I used much less hot sauce (about 1 tbsp) and there was plenty of flavor -- and it was still kid friendly. This dish is so heavy that even for my monstrous family of of huge eaters this was definitely more than 4 servings, probably more like 6. You will feel like utter sin and want to fuse with your sofa afterwards, but you'll enjoy every bite.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I was more than disappointed with the results. First, the dish turned out very greasy which I suspect resulted from the melted cheddar cheese. Second, there are many different types of “hot sauce”.  I used Louisiana Crystal hot sauce which I had on hand which has a vinegary base and it added way too much of a vinegar taste to the dish. I was really looking forward to a luxurious comfort food dish and ended up with a greasy, gloppy mess.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: The Hamburger Helper and Shake Shack mentions seem downright misleading after making this dish. Luxurious, rich, complex and filling seem most appropriate. Oh and did I forget to say filling. I went Full Monty with the specified ingredients...heavy cream, broth, "real" American cheese, wine, thick cut premium bacon...no calorie concerns. Opted for the min hot sauce recommendation using Cholula. Deep flavors, very cheesy, very filling. At least 6 servings out of this...for a hungry crowd.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Not fast. Not simple. Not edible.  I made a mistake and swapped the bacon for pancetta, and that one swap resulted in the only meal I've ever thrown out after tasting.  Great description, but not worth the work for a creamy, dense, mush.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
I love this recipe but make it much healthier with following tweaks:

Add chopped red or yellow bell peppers with the onion and garlic 
Use whole milk or 1/2 and 1/2 instead of heavy cream
Use 2 cups chicken stock and 1 cup water (instead of 3 cups stock)
Add 1 or 2 cans chopped tomatoes
Leave out the American cheese - just use the 1.5 cups sharp or extra sharp cheddar
Serve over fresh spunach or with steamed broccoli

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Yes. We loved it. Made it as written (except reduced hot sauce per our taste - we like spicy food a lot, but weren’t looking for our hamburger helper to be too spicy. ⅛c of Tapatio added a nice little kick and the needed acidity without being overpowering). Next time  I will start with the bacon as another reviewer suggested to avoid discarding extra oil.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: For the most part, followed the recipe. Skipped the smoked paprika because I didn’t feel like paying the high price for a small jar. Didn’t add all the hot sauce as i added some diced jalapeños and Korean red pepper flakes. It was easy and very delicious!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: We made this last night on a whim because we had all of the ingredients on hand. Completely forgot to add the wine and omitted the hot sauce. It still turned out great! Decadent comfort food that really hit the spot on a chilly night. Everyone in the family loved it. We will definitely be making this one again.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Not sold on this. For the calories and the amount of effort/cook time, this should be excellent. It is not. I followed the recipe almost exactly (slightly less cream and significantly less hot sauce, neither of which is my issue), and it is just unnecessarily heavy and slightly off? In order to adequately render the bacon, the beef becomes incredibly over-cooked, and I think that might be part of my issue. Next time, I’ll just make a good Mac and cheese.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Lived up to the hype for sure. So satisfying. Best part of this recipe is that after a day of pre-Christmas cleaning, errands, and gift wrapping this tired Mom sent the recipe to her teenage kids and they made it!!!
