author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Whole picky family loves this. I take the meat out and return it at the end with the cheese for better texture.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: This was delicious but it was uncomfortably rich, and i wish i had divided into 8 servings with a nice, crunchy, lemony side salad for balance.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I only use Impossible sausage. Add Worcestershire, liquid smoke & a few other tiny tweaks. It’s in our monthly rotation of dinners !

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
I found it too rich with bacon and cream and extra cheese.   I would reduce the cream or replace with broth (who would reduce cheese??) and precook the bacon to eliminate the grease. 

The flavors were fabulous, though. Definitely worth tinkering with to your liking.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: It’s delicious. My trick to cut some calories is to use a can of evaporated milk instead of cream, you won’t notice the difference, it’s just as creamy. Also, precook the bacon and no need to add oil, even when you drain most of the fat.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I had leftover porcupine meatballs which cut down on cooking time, used some old red wine, skipped the bacon, and used sour cream since I didn’t have cream. Yum

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: This might be the best recipe on the planet. I use whole milk, for no reason other than I don’t often have heavy cream and I don’t want to not have half and half for my coffee. I often use random cheeses — comte, Gruyère, sometimes truffle Gruyère — because that’s what’s in my fridge. Sometimes I only make 1/2 the recipe bc I’ve made a couple of smash burgers with my ground beef and have 1/2 lb of ground beef left over. Oh — once I used dry vermouth instead of wine. It. Doesn’t. Matter. This Rca

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Used turkey instead of beef, left out the American cheese, added more hot sauce, perfect West Coast version. Everyone loved it.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Carameizing the onions is a key step.  Take the time to do it!  In fact, there are several steps in this recipe that, while straightforward, take time.  Don't cut corners if you can help it.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I make this kosher by using Impossible Beef. Such a crowd pleaser for my large family.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: This is by far my favorite comfort meal. If I have had a long work week or family drama its the best. DONT COUNT CALORIES just enjoy. I did sub the ground beef for turkey, everything else was the same and delish!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I replaced the pasta with finely sliced cabbage to cut down on the carbs. Out of this world!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I used the "what do I have in the pantry (and fridge)" method and used ground turkey instead of beef, subbed non-fat Greek yogurt for the heavy cream, and used 1 1/2 cups of shredded Mexican cheese blend and half a cup of Pecorino Romano for the cheese. I also added a quarter cup of hickory smoked bbq sauce just to give it a little smokier flavor. It was great! Next time, I'll render the bacon first, as others have suggested, and sauté the onions, garlic, and ground meet in the rendered fat.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
This was comfort food personified! If you’re looking for low cal or low fat, keep scrolling. Very rich & tough to resist going back for seconds. I had to forego the bacon since I didn’t have any handy, and cut way back on hot sauce. Not an uber-hot fan, and a Tbs of sriracha was perfect. 
This is a keeper!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: This was certainly work intensive for a dish named Hamburger Helper. I followed the recipe except to omit hot sauce. The hot paprika did its job. We didn't like it, unfortunately, and after all that work.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Celiac and vegetarian household so used La Veneziane elbows and Gardein “be’f” crumbles. 1/4 c Crystal hot sauce gave it heat and vinegar kick. Great comfort food dish. Definitely closer to 6-8 servings when paired with a salad or veg on the side. 17 year old gave it a 5 star thumbs up!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Made a vegetarian version with Morningstar bacon, Beyond ground meat, and veggie broth. Added the "bacon" near the end of step 1, about 3-4 mins before the garlic. Added the paprika in step 2. No fat to drain. Had some leftover pasta sauce (approx 3/4 c) that I added while the wine was reducing - used a little less broth to compensate. Light cream, not heavy. Added baby spinach before adding the cheeses. Excellent!!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I followed this very loosely as this is literally a heartstopper of a recipe. Used ground chicken, no bacon and no cream. But adding the hot sauce was a fantastic recommendation which brought all the oomph needed. That’s the one step I would recommend following, albeit in a lesser quantity as your palate dictates.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I've been making this mostly dairy free by subbing non diary milk for the cream and pumpkin puree for the cheese. Tastes superb! Sometimes i shave in a little pecorino (I can tolerate a little sheep dairy).

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I followed the advice of some others and used ground turkey, 1/2 and 1/2, and no bacon. It was plenty rich with these changes. I thought it was delicious and will definitely make it again.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Agree with all the commenters omitting the bacon - not just because of calories/grease, but because I found it overpowered the flavor.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: You should not use seed oils. Why not use olive oils instead?

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Adding to many vegetarian suggestions: skip bacon (if really wanted, can replace fat with a bit of butter or oil), replace chicken broth with lapsang souchong tea and veggie/mushroom stock cubes and beef with veggie crumbles of choice.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: This was great comfort food!  Especially during a cold winter day. I did not use that much oil maybe a tablespoon and then I cut some of the fat off of the bacon and fried the onions with the small amount of oil. I made sure most of the fat was gone using a strainer.  I didn’t use as much hot sauce (1 T) and I teaspoon of regular paprika.  I did use the prepackaged Kraft American cheese and grated mild cheddar.  It was delicious!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I burned the onions even though I cooked for five minutes less than the minimum suggested. I have no room for error when I gave two kids and basketball practice and appointments.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I've never had a pound of leftover ground beef, so this is a non-starter, but it probably tastes great.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Doubled macaroni and liked the proportions. To compensate, upped water/broth to 4 1/2 or 5 cups. Used a mix of 1/2 and 1/2 and cream, a few tablespoons extra due to extra macaroni. 2 cups cheddar. No bacon. It honestly looked a lot like the picture. I wouldn’t like it as much with just 8 oz. pasta!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I made this as directed and was bitterly disappointed.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: My arteries started tightening the more I read. Yikes!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Made it as written with Valentinas hot sauce (Valentinas is not very hot but is vinegary - no problem using a quarter cup. Crystal is another good brand here, Frank's would be a bit hotter. Tabasco would be too hot in my opinion). For the many comments about this being too fat laden and caloric, this isn't the recipe for you. If you're looking for alternative to American cheese, try Monterey Jack or Colby. If you don't have cream or it's over your budget, a roux oas thickener would work

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: American cheese in not a thing in my house, but - remembering fondly the recent “Million Dollar Macaroni” recipe — I substituted cream cheese. Maybe because gluten-free macaroni is necessary in my house, cooked as written it lacked dimension. Before serving I doctored with garlic powder and dried thyme. It was fine, but not really worth the time, complexity, and embarrassment of telling a guest I was serving Hamburger Helper.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Super Delicious!  I added 3 cups of frozen mixed veggies (corn, beans, carrots - microwaved to remove chill) and added towards end of cooking, shortly before noodles were done.  I didn't use hot paprika because I have a few tender palettes to feed, but did add hot sauce.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: 100% better than any prepackaged product!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Added 8 oz. of chopped mushrooms to the onions to add some nutrition and stretch the meal to more portions...and reduce the amount of fat overall. The 1/4 cup of oil can easily be reduced a bit. Simply cut the fat from most of the bacon, then no need to drain fat.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: My friends and I used to make a pared down version of this for our kids in the 80's we called Tony Marzetti -- or something like that. So good yet so bad for you...

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I think it was called Johnny Marzetti. I grew up eating it all the time!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Anyone tried this with bison instead of ground beef?

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I made this with what I had on hand in my house, so these were my substitutions:  cooking sherry for wine, half-and-half, smoked paprika, ground turkey. I wanted a little tomato flavor, so I added one can partially drained petite diced tomatoes, and used torchiette pasta. Delicious! I would definitely make this again.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Made this today for football food.  Everyone loved it.  Based on reader comments, I made the following changes: 2 strips of bacon, lean ground beef, one can of rotel, 1 cup of 1/2 and 1/2 instead of cream, double pasta and 4 cups broth.  Cut back on hot sauce.  Added diced carrots to 1/2 onion sauté.  Will make again but will probably try ground turkey.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Simply delicious!! This ended up being one of my pregnancy cravings and it hit the mark every time!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I think this is a really great recipe, with a few changes that stay true to the original vibe: get some veggies in with the addition of diced carrots, chopped spinach, chopped kale, etc., and opt for 2% or whole milk instead of cream.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: This was a resounding success! Made it for three kids, ages 5, 9 and 10, and they all came back for seconds and thirds. Reduced the amount of hot sauce, though, to keep it child-friendly. Will definitely make it again!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Why on earth did I neglect to read the comments before pouring over 2 ounces of Tabasco into my Dutch oven. Sadly I have been duped by another tasty looking video on the gram, and made a fool. Biggest problem with this recipe is it’s not balanced well and there’s too many flavors - white wine is a nice touch but I didn’t need a whole cup, and I coulda ditched the bay leaf too. Lessons learned.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: This is the kind of recipe that's making me consider keeping my NYT Cooking subscription.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Substituting 1 can of chopped tomatoes for some of the liquid really punches up the flavor.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: This is the NYT recipe I’ve made the most. We don’t eat meat often in my house, but when we do this is what I want.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: This was delicious. It’s the ultimate comfort food. I wouldn’t want to eat it often but if your best friend loses her job or has a break up, make her a pot of this.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: We love this recipe in my family, but there are DEFINITELY fussy, unnecessary steps that do not make it more delicious. You absolutely should brown the bacon separately if you prefer not to spend an hour browning meat. Don't bother caramelizing the onions separately, just do them with the beef. Add the garlic any time while browning the beef. Even with these changes, we still call it hamburger complicator! We use imported orchiette that cook for like 20 minutes, it's amazing.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
I agree with the rest of the posters that you can’t cook this recipe as is. Nutritionally, it’s way too fatty and takes way too long to cook. Sorry, Ms. Krishna! That being said, this is a good idea and is going into the rotation. 
Here are my suggested changes:
Don’t use all that oil. Don’t use the bacon. Use whatever meat or soy crumbles you have on hand. Instead of just onion and garlic, add an equal amount of small diced onions, carrots, celery, and jalapenos. (I keep a homemade mirepoix in the frig that I chop up weekly). Don’t use sharp paprika, just use regular and fry it up with the onion mix and garlic to bloom the spice. Don’t use cream. Instead, use 1/4 brick of neufchatel or whatever cream cheese you have on hand and melt it in. Don’t use all pasta. Instead, use half pasta and half chopped cauliflower.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I saved this in my recipe box some time ago, and I pulled it out tonight to use some ground bison I had on hand, and I had fancy bacon. The flavors were amazing, but I made a flub by not using low-sodium chicken broth, so it was mighty salty. I didn't salt at the end, and I only used a smidge with the caramelized onions - the fancy bacon and my broth together were just too salty. Totally my bad! Use low sodium broth or unsalted stock if you make it. I'll try it again!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Did it exactly as written and was sooooo fatty. My mouth was covered in a layer of fat the mouthfeel was so uncomfortably fatty like eating butter. Disappointing because it took so long to make and i was craving hamburger helper. Next time ill just use the box even though I wanted to skip all the weird chemically ingredients. Oh well

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: @Ld can just cut down on bacon- use lean beef, half half or whole milk!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Delicious. Skipped the bacon.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I have made this dish a few times now. Mostly this recipe is for when I’m going into winter and will be working less(there for less $$). It freezes well. And reheats in oven well if I don’t cook the pasta too far. I also love that I can make it a day ahead if we have company coming, with a big salad to help it all move down it’s a crowd pleaser. I do use less hot sauce ( I go for franks original), also I substitute a combination of smoked paprika and regular. My husband is lactose intolerant so I use whole lactose free milk thickened with veg oil. Or a lactose free sour cream if I have it. Works great for the creamy feel. I also just add cheese to my own dish. I don’t use American cheese, I know it melts great but I just can’t get past the fact that it is not real cheese. Cheddar alone is wonderful in this dish. I also don’t use the oil to cook the onions. I brown the meats first remove them to a bowl then add them back and complete the recipe as fallows. Without all the accumulated fats at the bottom. Thanks for a great dish that is comfort food to the max!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: @Russ Wilcox you made a completely different recipe.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Ultimate comfort— as satisfying as a bolognese but easier. I used 1/2 the bacon and 1/2 the smoked paprika because I don’t love the smokey flavor. It was perfect. Didn’t need to drain fat. Probably because less bacon and good quality beef.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Agreed its way too much hot sauce

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: This was real good. I followed some other commenters edits and cooked the meat first and then added the onion to that. I had trouble rendering the fat though from bacon when it was mixed in with the beef. So what I'll do next time is cook the bacon to render the fat, remove it, add the onions, and then add the beef to that once the onions are done and proceed from there, adding the bacon at the end to preserve its crispness.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I don’t know about everyone else but my half and half (which I subbed for the cream) separated when I stirred it in. It would have been much better to just stir in a drizzle of cream (or even sour cream or yogurt) at the end, with the cheese, after it had cooled a bit.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: We make this a fair bit. Once every few months. This time I didn’t have hot sauce and I added about half a cut of marinara sauce. I think that made a superior albeit different dish. More like American chop suey.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: First offf I have to say I made the recipe as written. Yeah, it's tasty. Yeah it's high in calories. Yeah, it's time consuming. I can live with all of that. Did I LOVE it? No. Between the bacon and the cheese I found it too salty for my taste. Perhaps next time I will eschew the bacon.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
This was soooo good. Kids loved it! Here are my changes:
-1/4c olive oil
-12oz pasta (to fit our family 1 adult, 3 kids (7,4,1) + leftovers
- 1/2c white wine vinegar mixed with 1/2c water to replace white wine
- 3 tbsp of tomato paste instead of hot sauce
- 3 tbsp cream cheese instead of american cheese

Next time i would omit the cream cheese, felt like it dilutes the flavour but maybe it adds the necessary creamy texture. I'd add parmesan with the cheddar.

Everything else exactly the same!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Way too much fat in this dish to enjoy.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: This was a lot of fuss for the finished product. It was overly rich, with an unnecessary amount of high fat ingredients that cancelled each other out. Our family agreed, it wasn’t worth the time and effort.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: This was delicious!  I deleted the bacon (because I forgot to buy it).  I used very lean ground beef, and minimal oil, which just consisted of enough to brown the onion.  I thought the spice level was wonderful, but I did switch from hot paprika to just smoked paprika.  I also used half and half in place of the heavy cream.  This will be made again this winter!!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Labour-intensive and overly rich, but absolutely worth it.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
I’m starting to really look at the nutrition info before trying or saving any recipe.  I was thinking I could make this with TVP or crumbled tofu instead of all the meat, but the entire recipe would need an overhaul before I would consider reasonably heathy.

1343 calories; 92 grams fat; 39 grams saturated fat; 2 grams trans fat; 36 grams monounsaturated fat; 9 grams polyunsaturated fat; 62 grams carbohydrates; 4 grams dietary fiber; 9 grams sugars; 55 grams protein; 1646 milligrams sodium

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: @B Dawson So don’t make it, then.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
Four thoughts:
1.  You really want to brown the beef well.  A good fond on the pan, and very well browned beef.  
2.  A full pound of noodles is a value add.  Add an extra cup of water/ stock mixture 
3.  If you use homemade stock salt the beef (assuming you don't salt your stock).
4.  If you use really good homemade stock, mix 50/50 with water or the pasta won't cook properly.  It needs some water that it can absorb.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Pfffft to low fat and lean when sheer comfort is necessary for many reasons, once in a while.  For me, low fat is sad food - much like decaf coffee… why would you ruin such divine perfection ?

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I’m considering making this recipe. It looks so good. I know it’s best to make a recipe the first round as is, but, I’m going to replace the bacon with liquid smoke. I find that liquid smoke is a great substitute for anything that requires that smoky pork like flavor.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I love reading the comments as well as the recipes. They give me an idea about how I might want to adapt the recipe to my personal tastes. I don't think the comments take anything away from the creator of the recipe. I like the helpful hints for cutting calories.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: This recipe tasted good, but seemed like a waste of perfectly good wine, and I didn’t see the point of caramelizing the onions either. Ignore the comments about it being too spicy, it’s not.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Solid B recipe. My husband is avoiding spice so subbed part worcestershire sauce for the hot sauce and did not care for the flavor. I did not grow up with hamburger helper so did not have the nostalgia factor going. I did put it into a casserole and topped it with buttered panko, and baked it for half an hour, which I think helped improve it some.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: This recipe needs a new name, as its current one does not do it justice.  A few suggestions: Homemade Hangover Helper. Mid-Winter Love Bomb. Eat on Repeat. 😋

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Made this with the only modification from the comments to add tomato paste.  I would definitely make this again, it was delicious.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
I'm going to continue to praise the comments on everyone's adjustments that make the recipe perfect for them. That's true cooking. That's how recipes work. That's what your nana did!

I'm a vegetarian but I still enjoy something like this. Obviously I'm going to change the recipe. 

I love all the comments! keep them coming.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I have been stuck in my house for four days due to a snow storm so I had to sub cream cheese for heavy cream, sherry for white wine, and leave out the bacon. I’m having a hard time thinking of how to phrase this without swearing: that might be the best thing I’ve ever eaten. Maybe it’s the cabin fever but wow that is a winner.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I never had hamburger helper growing up, but our family were not fans.   flavor is mostly from the heat/spice and we missed vegetables--just a bad pick on our part.   Also it took quite a lot of stiring for a long time.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I had high hopes. I was disappointed. You literally could have added a little wine and cooked bacon to a box of Hamburger Helper and it would have tasted exactly the same. I’m glad I tried it, however I won’t be making again. Glad many did like it on here though!!:)

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
If I'm being honest I mostly used this recipe as a base and modified the heck out of it. In spirit what I made was the same, but my process was pretty different. My kids gave it a 10/10. The wife and I both thought it was very good, albeit pretty heavy. My mods below:
Cooked the beef first, then the bacon setting each aside when done; drained off all but a few Tablespoons of fat and cooked the onion as described in the fat; added the wine and added the garlic when reduced; added half and half in lieu of heavy cream, with about 1/2 cup of broth, paprika, bay leaf and brought to a simmer; cooked double the pasta on its own in salted water; strained pasta and combined with all other ingredients.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I used beer instead of white wine because it was all I had, then I used a combo of other cheese because I usually don't keep American cheese. I also used protein/fiber low carb bowtie pasta. I had a little guanciale in the freezer that I used instead of bacon, and then used Frank's red hot (the amount suggested) and it was perfect level of seasoning. Very very good and I appreciate how adaptable it is.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Since this is a riff on classic hamburger helper, which is designed to make life easy, are there any thoughts out there about which of this can be prepped ahead of time and kept in the freezer? Do you think up to and including step six? What about freezing the recipe while the chicken stock and cream have already been added?

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: @Lisa D I'd be concerned about freezing and reheating the cream (it can separate and become grainy). I wouldn't add it until ready to finish & serve.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I will make anything Priya tells me. Because of her, I always stop at Patel Bros. when visiting family in Pittsburgh. Always come home with packets of fresh Curry leaves. Use the American cheese. It melts well. That’s all.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Absolutely a crack recipe! One for the books. I used Crystal Garlic hot sauce and if you are cooking for kids or spice intolerant adults, go with 1 TBLSPN only. I also subbed egg noodles for elbows, but could see using super high quality pasta if you want to fancy this up.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Tasted like my childhood. Definitely not a quick dish but I shortened the time by cooking pasta separately and adding to the meat sauce. Used regular not smoked paprika and left out hot sauce due to children but used a lot of Crystal hot sauce in my bowl.  Everyone liked it although my son who has never experienced Hamburger Helper thought it was bland even with hot sauce added.  I disagree (and he still ate two bowls of it). Great winter comfort dish that would serve a crowd.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I used to dress up Hamburger Helper back in the day, trying to make it tastier: this is All Of That!  Used beef stock instead of chicken stock because beef, very small amount of hot sauce until after cooking/tasting, when I added some sriracha because....that's what I had.  Also used 1 lb pasta, totally fine. Takes time to make (no way to do this in 1.5 hours) but TOTALLY worth it.  LOVED it.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
This is delicious, although I lightened up on a few ingredients (3-4 strips bacon, ~3/4 cup shredded/American cheese blend, a little less than 1/4 cup Frank's Red Hot) and it was plenty rich.

Might up the hot sauce a smidge, otherwise it's a perfect dinner on dark, cold winter nights like many of us in the States are experiencing now.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Used 1 T hot sauce for 1/2 recipe, and it was plenty spicy.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I used pancetta instead of bacon.  Next time I’d cook the pancetta separately form the onions so it crisps more. Otherwise this was an amazing comfort food meal. I’ll be making this again for sure.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Joe is my soul mate.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: This was delicious!  I like the idea of half and half instead of heavy cream, and I will cut down the hot sauce to 1/4 cup-you can add more to taste at the table.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Way behind - my mother cooked this in the 30's - not quite so spicy but definitely the same.  We worked so this was a fast get together meal. And for southwestern Iowa a bit exotic.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Appreciate nutritional information - a hit or miss with recipes featured. Essential for Type 2 diabetics. If listed, you can modify it from the getgo and maintain the dish's integrity.  Many thanks!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Why chicken stock with beef as the meat? Perhaps beef stock makes more sense.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Beef stock adds a weird taste sometimes, I usually use chicken stock on beef recipes too.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Tasty, but a little greasy.  Really drain meat.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Sorry I wasted some excellent bacon and ground beef on this recipe.  Not a keeper.  Bitterly disappointed.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Hah! That's tellin' them in true New York style, Joe.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I made this recipe with faux ground beef and sans the bacon.  It was delicious and without all those nasty ingredients from the actual Hamburger Helper.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: How much cheese is 5 slices of American cheese? Ounces?

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Venison works well with this recipe.  Very low fat and delicious.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
I’m not crazy about the bacon cooking the same time as the beef—it wasn’t crispy and I’m not a fan of small pieces of bacon fat.
Also, used 1/4 cup of Tabasco to later be told that Tabasco is hotter than hot sauce.
It was a little too much.  A lot of fat, and a lot of heat, and yet not a lot of flavor (other than Tabasco)

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: SOOO good, but left out hot sauce because some of of can't take heat.  This was much better than the package we had sooo many years ago .  I don't know why it says to add american cheese, coz I rarely do, but it came out great!!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I used quorn mince to make it vegetarian and it was absolutely delicious.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: This was a hit. I used a couple of minced hot peppers plus a few splashes of Cholula sauce, extra sharp English cheddar plus a bit of Monterey Jack to help the consistency, and scallions instead of chives, because those are what I had on hand. Like Pierre Franey's Macaroni and Beef casserole, I suspect the tastiness depended largely on the quality of the cheese, because the heat level was noticeable but still in the background.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Was happy with the spice levels but the recipe took quite a while.  Next time I'd like to substitute pancetta for the bacon.  Or maybe Italian sausage for both the beef and bacon.  Has anyone tried that?  And I don't want to think of calories.  :-)

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I made it exactly as written, except that I used rotini and I listened to people here and reduced the hot sauce (Frank's) to 1/8 cup.  I'll use more next time. It hardly had a kick at all.  It was cheesy and delicious.  I wonder how it would be with a bit more wine.  I love the taste of wine in sauces and it wasn't really detectable here.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Ok, I'm cooking for a young family here and I halved the paprika and added 5 dashes of Cholula and it was plenty flavourful with a little kick.  I also added a bit of extra cream to mellow it out a bit.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: This was very flavorful.  Every component came through in the final dish.  Good for a football game or a cold winter night. I used 1/4 cup of hot sauce and that was plenty - and I like spicy food.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: End result was fairly tasty but way too fussy and time consuming to be worth the effort. I wonder at how much this recipe would actually lose if you simplified some of the steps. Would suggest frying bacon separately so it actually has some crisp - when cooked as is the texture is not appetizing. My kids are totally spice adverse so I omitted the hot sauce and did a few dashes of smoked paprika - the flavor was kid friendly BUT the color won’t look as appetizing. Tomato paste may help fix that.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I used cooked bacon, so the double cooking gave it some crisp.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: This recipe took way too long to prepare compared to the payoff. Very average and very high in calories. Not worth the time or the calories.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: We doubled the noodles to spread out some of the heaviness. This meant 2 more cups chicken stock, 1 more cup of cream. Fantastic turn out. Likely lighter because of extra noodles, but now it will go much further. Would recommend this to anyone with kids who want a kick to their Mac n cheese/avoid processed hamburger helper.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: This was a huge hit in our house. I diced the bacon very small (1/8 inch maybe) so it blended in with the beef. Used a Goya brand hot sauce and it was not spicy, just had a nice tang. For the American cheese, I did 100 grams of velveeta - I didn’t have any issues with it being greasy as it cooled down like other commenters mentioned. Too time consuming for a weeknight but was an excellent Sunday night treat. We will definitely make this again!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: We lightened this up by using ground turkey instead of ground beef, replaced some of the noodles with cauliflower florets, and used about 3/4 of the cheese called for.  It was still very decadent and delicious! (We roasted the cauliflower florets in the oven and stirred in at the end.)

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Decent dish but not worth the effort

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: The hot sauce is essential!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Just an ok recipe. Really heavy. From a flavor standpoint, it was rich but lacked complexity. My family's reaction was "good on a cold winter's night but would not request it again." Just "meh."

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Were you expecting complexity in a recipe for homemade Hamburger Helper?  It's meant to be simple comfort food.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I think the concern about the listed calories per serving is misplaced.  If four people can get outside all of this at one sitting they are either gluttons (so don't care about the calorie count) or having been doing extensive physical labor (so can well afford it).  My family got about 8 servings out of it.  Unfortunately, I thought it was fairly uninspired, so will probably not make it again.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: This is EXTREMELY salty—I wouldn’t add any extra salt until the end. It’s also very oily as others have mentioned. Subbing the bacon might be a good idea

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I doubled the recipe. I used 1 pound of ground beef and 1 pound of beyond beef, 1/4 cup hot sauce and 1/4 cup Worcestershire sauce, and gluten free pasta elbows. It turned out well. My 15 year old liked it. I think it needs something but not sure what. Tomato paste maybe?

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Is the recipe calling for yellow or white cheddar cheese?

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I don't think the color of the cheddar matters.  Or the color of the American cheese, for that matter.  I used white American and yellow cheddar.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I made this almost as written.  I didn’t do the bacon because I forgot to buy it, wasn’t crazy about the bacon idea in the first place. I reduced the amount of oil per comments by others.  I used a super lean ground beef so I had no fat to drain. Other than that made as written. I started with 1/4 cup of hot sauce but ended up adding more, but I like spicy.  Bought the chives but forgot to add them.  I like this dish a lot, probably would not make it again for myself because of the calories.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: How much, oz/gm, does 5 slices of American cheese weigh?

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I doubled the pasta and added splash of milk to keep enough liquid in the pot to boil all the noodles. Agree that the bacon texture isn’t great, but the rich bacon taste is heavenly. Maybe crisp it up separately next time, as others have suggested. Used paprika but not hot sauce due to anti-spice child; we added hot sauce individually later and it really pulled the whole dish together. Will definitely make again.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Delicious as a sinful dinner treat. I used 2 tbsp of Tabasco and added more as needed while serving.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: We like some heat but not a lot, so I used a fraction of the hot sauce. Using what I had on hand...half-and-half instead of heavy cream, 1-1/2 tsp smoked paprika + 1/2 tsp Trader Joe's Chipotle Smoked Red Jalapenos instead of hot smoked paprika...subbed gluten-free elbow pasta (cooked 12 min) and it turned out great. Satisfying and thirst-making in an ice-cold Coke kinda way. Next time I will try adding a vegetable - like peas or greens - at the end. Yum!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I got 7 servings out of it.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Also add vegetable (peas?) and use half and half

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Use less oil

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
Awesome and hearty mid-winter evening meal. Went by the recipe, didn't change a thing. This is the usual go to: follow the recipe as written. Afterwards, twerk it a bit if necessary. 
Spiciness varies with mileage, so dose accordingly.  
Will keep this in rotation til spring. But definitely will be bring it back next winter.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I made this yummy dish and it was so easy, my husband loved it!  I used all the ingredients exactly, but did slightly less hot sauce (Frank’s) and only used about 3/4 cup of the shredded cheddar.  I don’t think the additional cheddar slices are even necessary! One comment about the bacon, next time I’ll try and crisp it a bit first, so it’s not so fatty.  I ended up removing most of the bacon pieces once the ground beef had been flavored with it.  But great  recipe, can’t wait to make it again!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I know it’s annoying to see a review with several substitutions, but we used what we had on hand and it was fantastic. I can only imagine how delicious it would be if I’d had all of the ingredients. I didn’t have onions, bacon, ground beef, or heavy cream, so I subbed ground turkey for beef and half-and-half for heavy cream, and skipped the onions and bacon. We have a toddler so I only used 2 TB of Frank’s Red Hot, and smoked paprika instead of hot paprika. Rich and indulgent, gobbled by all.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Wow - this is great! Brings back my single mom's 1975 Hamburger Helper dinners in a great way! Made do with ground turkey, cream cheese instead of American, low fat cheddar, and milk instead of cream and it was still delish! Can only imagine how good the full fat version is. Texas Pete's hot sauce and smoked paprika really made the difference. Also used a few extra bay leaves and garlic cloves as well as a dash of cinnamon and cumin for a middle east twist.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Made it with some leftover egg noodles I had. Omitted hot sauce but wanted it to have a tiny bit of a kick so I added a few dashes of cayenne pepper.  Yum!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Delicious. But too many steps and takes too much time to cook. May not make this again because of this. Used half of hot sauce.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Satisfied w mods I made based on reviews and dietary restrictions (no pork or beef): ground chicken for beef, 2 tsp of chipotle powder for paprika (and of course no bacon), 1 cup of frozen peas when melting cheese, milk for heavy cream, less cheddar (just eyeballed the amount), no hot sauce. There was no fat to pour off from the meat and I only used 1/4 cup white wine to deglaze. It was plenty spicy w chipotle powder only and while not a light meal, not overwhelmingly heavy. Would make again.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I made this weeknight friendly by just sautéing the onions until translucent and skipping the wine. I also left out the bacon. Tasted really good still! I’d skip salting the onions next time, the hot sauce is plenty salty. I used Frank’s.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Made some modifications to lighten it up, ground turkey for beef, milk for cream, and no bacon. Added a few tablespoons of chipotles in adobo in addition to hot sauce, and a a couple handfuls of kale. Delicious!  Perfect for after a day out in the snow!!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: This recipe is not worth the energy. You constantly have to be stirring to avoid burning. It didn’t even taste that good. Too meaty for my liking.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: This is a bit labor intensive. But, as a child of the 80’s, nostalgic comfort food is beef, cheese, macaroni in some shape of form. This nails it. I’m not very familiar with Hamburger Helper but this is just a bowl of goodness. Will keep in mind for colder weekend nights but likely won’t make too often due to time (~45 minutes and have to stay close to stove)

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I used ground turkey instead of beef, Swiss instead of cheddar, good sharp white cheddar slices instead of American cheese, and handmade trecce dell’ orto pasta..... Fantastic.  Definitely would make again in this way.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I substituted ground turkey instead of beef, canadian bacon instead of bacon, half and half instead of cream, didn't have any onions so I used minced. It was great, loved the flavor. I had smoked salt so added a dash of that but don't think I really needed to. I want to try it with the cauliflower instead of elbow macaroni but my husband doesn't like cooked cauliflower :(

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
I adjusted the hot sauce to suit my family’s taste. I used a block of American  I had from the deli and Colby that I had on hand. i used 5oz  pancetta as that was in the fridge. For the dry wine, vermouth, and a tsp if white wine vinegar at end of cooking,  after cheese, to finish. 
This dish was very well received by all of my family! I think the cooking time for me was an hour, but also had some great leftovers.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Made exactly as described with two exceptions. I had 6 slices of bacon left, so used all 6 instead of 5. I used 3 tablespoons of Sriracha sauce in response to the comments about the heat. Next time, I will add another tablespoon, to 1/4 cup, but reviews were good at the table.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Really good and quick

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
I followed recipe exactly, used 1/4 C of Tabasco. I asked my husband, who puts hot sauce on everything, to please taste before adding anything.  The dish was perfect for his palate!  I'm not averse to spice, but thought so much heat gave it a bitter taste. 
Did not feel the recipe was difficult to put together, as some have indicated. Directions for timing were perfect.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Used 1/4 c Uncle Franks hot sauce

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I halved this to avoid eating 4 servings of cheesey beef in one week (I live alone). This may have made it simpler/faster because it took me more like an hour. I loved the flavor but not the texture of the bacon in the dish. Next time I would remove the lardons and crisp them separately, them re-add at the end. I skipped the American cheese, adding more cheddar instead. I was out of wine and used dry sake instead, used whole milk instead of cream, and used Sriracha for the hot sauce.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Changing my original rating of 4 to 5. When I first made and reviewed, there were leftovers waiting. Then, they were eaten (not by me) and I only had 1 bowl of this. Been thing of it it since. Made again. So easy to use what’s on hand and is DELICIOUS (veggie stock for wine & beef stock, no bacon, whole milk instead of cream). Two adults and two kids are very very happy

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Excellent comfort food! I cooked the bacon separately. Got it real crispy and added it at the end with the chives. I added two spoons if bacon grease to my ground beef. Didn't have white wine, used a Spanish hard apple cider. 2 cups chicken stock, 4 cups water. Used Queso de Papa instead of American cheese. Doubled the recipe and used an entire bottle of Cholula. Just the right amount of heat. Make this, but I think adding the crispy bacon at the end is key!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Made it a couple of months ago.  Very tasty. Didn't change a thing except left off the chives. My partner took leftovers to work and re-heated it for lunch.  People kept asking things like, "What is that?  It smells wonderful."  It's on the menu again tonight!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: This is simply great. Thank you Priya. And your Mom. We have to “social distance” d/t virus threat. This recipe is helpful and delicious. Cooking in our home is the plan for the near future. Your recipes are a guidepost. Thank you many times over!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Wow!!! This was absolutely delicious!!! We subbed 1 tsp. sweet paprika & 1 tsp. cayenne for the hot smoked paprika and then added 2 tsp. Cholula instead of the 1/4 cup hot sauce.  It was absolutely perfect!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Wow - this.is.amazing.  Used way less oil to sauté the onion.  Doubled the recipe except for the ground beef and to this "flexitarian" there was still plenty of meat.  As many other suggested, I reduced the amount of hot sauce (Frank's) so my toddler granddaughter would eat it.  Definitely a keeper of a recipe!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I didn’t have hot smoked paprika, so used slightly less hot paprika and added 1/2 teaspoon smoked paprika. Otherwise, followed the recipe (using stock, not water and Frank’s Hot Sauce). I loved it. Obviously, it’s not an everyday recipe given the calories. If you don’t like heat or the fat content, move on or adjust.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
Ohhhh man. Is this classy? No. Is this healthy? Absolutely not. Is it so incredibly comforting and wonderfully delicious? YEP.
I did not add the bacon- don’t miss it. 
I added the full 1/3c hot sauce and thought it was perfect amount of zip. 
This easily is 6 servings, as opposed to 4.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Hey this is really good. Took the advice of other reviewers and added 2 Tbs hot sauce, Irish cheddar and fontina and arugula at the end. Also used grass fed beef for the rich Omega 3 benefits.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: left out bacon, little less oil. 1/4 c Sriracha - just enough spice for spice lovers. used half&half. 4 slices AC. sooo good.  takes time, yes. but delicious every once and a while.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I used an approximate 4 to 1 ratio of half and half to cream instead of full cream and I didn't use bacon. I didn't have hot smoked paprika, so I substituted with a mild paprika and chipotle hot sauce (chipotle peppers are apparently sort of a substitution for hot smoked paprika). Because it already had chipotle hot sauce, I only used a bit of cholula. My partner diced up raw sweet onion to serve it with. It was fantastic and the onion really made it.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Agree with Cassie. (Tastes great. NOT less filling) . Also worked in some sautéed mushrooms .

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I used 2c chicken broth to start with and fresh pasta instead of dried. Added about 1/2 c more chic broth later on.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I collapsed cooking time by using less oil + less fatty meat --> less fat to manage --> moved right into the liquids stage and completed in 30 min

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: This recipe is absolutely AMAZING. Thank you for creating and posting it. I used coconut milk instead of heavy cream and Hot Mama  - Fermented Fresno Chili Hot Sauce. Truly incredible and satisfying. Thank you.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I love this recipe so much. Feels like home when you know you are a thousand miles from being able to be there.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I know I'm being dense but how is this homemade Hamburger Helper.  I've never used that, but I always assumed it was just a lot of seasonings and flavorings and regular uncooked macaroni that you cook, add to your own sauteed ground beef with the seasonings.  Never understood why it was touted as convenient, but certainly this recipe doesn't duplicate it? It's a pretty standard way to prepare ground beef, seasonings and pasta, tarted up with wine and cream.  But standard nonetheless?

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I used ground turkey and it worked perfectly and cuts the fat a bit too.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Used a combo of (total 1 C) gouda and cheddar, subbed about 1 tsp Momofuku spicy salt for the hot sauce and used 1 tsp of a good smoky paprika. Had half-and-half in the fridge so subbed that for the heavy cream Added chopped kale at the very end. This was a "use what you have in the pantry" recipe and it was delish. Not hot, with a little spicy kick.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: This is delicious and an everyone’s thumbs up winner.  I donMt add the bacon, but so far no complaints...

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Delicious! Eat your salad first. Once you start eating this, it's hard to stop! I used:  extra sharp cheddar and white american cheese; 1Tbs. Cholula and 1 Tbs. Frank's Red Hot Original, Andean Dream gluten-free elbow macaroni, Applewood Smoked Country Bacon, low sodium chicken broth, sunflower oil to sautee, 85% lean beef, and Noilly Prat extra dry vermouth for the dry white wine. Smoked bacon was the secret ingredient. Added hot sauce to taste. Definitely kid-friendly with just 1 Tbs. Cholula

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Classic dish begging for on-hand substitutions. I had a pan of bacon drippings from lunch available and use some of it for the onions. Because I no longer had the bacon, used some slices of prosciutto. Didn’t have heavy cream - used a milk and Greek yogurt combination, and happened to have Kraft American slices which were actually the perfect creamy texture. Didn’t have hot paprika but I did have smoked Hungarian, and the final dish was absolutely delicious.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Made this tonight and it was wonderful! I had hot sauce that was hotter than usual so I only used a small amount figuring I could always add more. I also used very lean ground beef and as a result didn’t have to drain any fat. It was a big hit!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Easy on the hot sauce. You can always add more. Cut it in half, especially if your guests aren't inclined to spicy food.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I made this as is except I left out the hot sauce for my picky kids. It was good. But there was too much meat to pasta ratio. Next time I would use a whole box of pasta and a little more chicken stock.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I just made this last night, and it was delicious. For the two of us, it was just enough for two dinners. I read many of the comments and followed some of the suggestions. To cut down on time, I sautéed the onion and garlic together until softened (only using a tbsp or two of oil, and just for maybe ten minutes) then followed the rest of the recipe. I completely omitted the hot sauce (my husband's stomach can't even take a drop unfortunately), but it was still a really nice one-pot meal.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Excellent.  Reduced hot sauce, and cheese because of health issues.  this will become a regular.  And thinking about ways to change the flavor profile........just for variation.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Had to make this again 2 nights later because it was such a huge hit. Cooked an extra cup of pasta separately and added it in with the cheeses, the 8oz of pasta wasn’t enough for us. Will definitely make this again, even my kids love it.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I mostly psid attention to previous notes, but still tried to remain somewhat true to the original. I did no t add salt pepper, and I didn’t have a bay leaf. I also only added about 1tbsp hot sauce. It was good, just far too rich/fatty. Next time I’ll use no additional oil, drain/pat the bacon/ground beef dry (cook separately)and use half & half or even milk.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: It took more than 3 cups of chicken broth to make this saucy enough and to cook the pasta. I used more like 4.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: This is so decadent and delicious. I made it after a particularly bad day because of 2020 and it was perfect after a good ugly cry in my car. Skipped bacon and used half and half instead of heavy cream and it was totally worth the impending lactose intolerant cramps.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Good solid comfort food.  Lightened it up by using 1/2 turkey breast 1/2 90% lean beef, and using evaporated skim milk for the cream. I did not have wine handy, and had to sub Guinness beer. Will try with wine for sure next time.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: This was absolutely delicious. I didn’t have hot sauce (because I don’t really ear hot things) and I put a little more pasta in. Perfect for a cold winter night!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: This was delicious and comforting. Decadent for sure. And VERY flavorful. But almost too rich. As loyal as I am to Team Bacon, I may leave it out next time. It will be just as delicious without it, but maybe a little less heavy.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: This is delicious! I love the idea of Hamburger Helper and this homemade, gourmet version is AMAZING. I had all ingredients on hand so I didn’t make any substitutions but I added sautéed mushrooms. Great “sweatah weathah” dish!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I used whole milk rather than cream to cut some of the calories, and still achieved a creamy sauce, thanks to the cheese.  This is a hearty dish that my husband loved!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Used way less oil and about half the amount of each cheese. Used a couple tablespoons of hot sauce but definitely could have added more.  Very tasty and rich! Delicious!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Good and fun but extremely rich (to be expected, just like real Hamburger Helper, I suppose). Love that it is a one pot meal. I would use just water, no broth next time-- because it was too salty! I would also recommend using more pasta than it calls for (12 or even 16 oz) as the sauce was so flavorful.  I didnt use American cheese and used 1/2 & 1/2 and it was still very rich.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Super rich, fatty, high calorie, and packed with flavor. I’m sure anyone would realize this prior to preparing. A couple notes: My pasta was still undercooked (hard) at the prescribed 9 minutes, and a lot of the liquid had already burnt off so I added about a half cup more chicken stock and let cook for another 3 minutes. This was perfect. I used Dave’s creamy garlic hot sauce which was a nice flavor, but the recipe would probably be good without hot sauce , or substituted with chilli flakes.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Unreal!!!! Everyone could not stop talking about this. I went in for the full caloric experience and it was just perfect!!! I got a lot of long faves when they heard hamburger helper for dinner - but this isn’t your boxed mix!!! We used 1/4 cup of Siracha and it was perfectly zingy.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: A nice weekend project, agree with the commenter who called this Hamburger Complicator, nonetheless it’s an easy, riffable stove top casserole. Used penne instead of elbows and needed a bit more liquid so added some leftover coconut milk. Didn’t bother with the American cheese. Also forgot chives, which I think would be great. Quality smoked paprika shines here. As does hot sauce with flavor, not just heat. Next time, a cream substitute and chives. Watch the serving size. It’s rich.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Dumb question here, but is the ground beef in the pot after browning and while boiling the noodles? Are you boiling the beef too or adding it back in with the cheese once the noodles are finished?

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: My interpretation was to leave it in the whole time.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: splash of white wine vinegar at the end to help cut the richness plus add depth/balance

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I followed the recipe for the most part, listened to those who mentioned to really drain the fat and I also added some more noodles - it was delicious! I wish there was a way to make it a bit faster. This is the type of comfort food you need in cold weather.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Added a red pepper with the garlic, some frozen peas in with the pasta, ditched the bacon, wine and American cheese, added gruyere instead. Needed more time for the pasta and was running out of liquid so added a cup of water. Served with a quick salad and only a cup of pasta. The two of us will have so many leftovers. Never mentioned when to take out the bay leaf?

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: My family loves pasta, so I added an extra cup of chicken broth and used about 12 oz of macaroni. It came out perfect! The spice was just right for my young kids with 1/4 c. Cholula and 1.5 t. sweet smoked paprika plus 1 t. New Mexico chile powder. Everybody wanted seconds (and thirds). Much more satisfying than the Hamburger Helper of my childhood!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: This is an interesting take on a classic '80s taste. To me it was missing a beefy flavor note. I'd like to try it again without the bacon, and substitute beef for chicken broth, add a bit of tomato paste and reduce the hot sauce.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Oh, and the hot sauce I used was Texas Pete. It’s definitely got a kick at 1/4 cup. If you prefer something a little milder I would go with Frank’s.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: This is yummy. A little smoky flavor from the paprika, a bit of heat-but not too much-from the hot sauce (I used Frank’s). Way better than the box, but just as comforting.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I added the chopped fatty part of the bacon (2 thick cut strips) to the pan first, rendered out some of the fat on medium-low, and cooked the onions in that instead of adding extra oil. It worked out well with little fat left to drain at the end. 1/8th cup tobacco was not too spicy, but a little too vinegary— would probably try a different hot sauce, or combination, next time. This was tasty and fun!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: subbed ground dark meat turkey instead of beef and gluten free macaroni....still incredibly heavy meal.  I would not include bacon next time.  The smokey flavor was overwhelming.  I agree that the American cheese (from the deli) makes a big difference and adds a nice creamy texture.  I added about 3 Tbsps of Cholula and it wasn't spicy at all.  Would make again, but really want to lighten it up.  We couldn't move after eating it!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: This dish was absolutely amazing!  I did reduce the spicy-ness by using regular smoked paprika vs hot but did add the hot sauce and a dash of ground red chili pepper. The heat was perfect. Mild and warm. The American cheese is what makes this dish and give it a creamy flavor. The white wine also gives the meat a delicious and tender taste. Be sure to finish it off with the chives.   The ingredients of this dish come together so beautifully!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Canola oil is just awful for your body. Use animal fat instead. Substituted with a less processed but melty cheese and it worked wonderfully.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Agreed-- I never cook with canola. I used avocado oil for this.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: This Hamburger Helper is no help to me.  Prep time? Over 30 minutes.  I like the addition of wine and 1/2 and 1/2.  You don't really need American Cheese (who buys that stuff?) Cheddar is fine.  Cut the bacon-just more time in prep.  Get the dish on the table, people are hungry.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: For a really smooth, cheesier version, make a cheese sauce in a saucepan over medium heat by whisking together the heavy cream and 5 grams of sodium citrate.  Bring to a simmer, then reduce the heat to low and slowly whisk in 2 cups of grated cheddar cheese (eliminating the American cheese) until smooth.  Stir cheese sauce into the hamburger/pasta mixture after the pasta is cooked.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I made a couple changes... I cooked the bacon first and used that fat to cook the onions. In future, I think I might add some mushrooms then just for some veggies. My hot sauce was sriracha cause it's just what I had... used about two tablespoons. Didn't have hot paprika so I used smoked paprika with some red pepper flakes. I skipped the American cheese and just used two cups of medium cheddar. I think in future I might add some frozen peas or corn to get some veggies in there. Otherwise... yum.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: This recipe and idea was a no brainer to make - being stuck home in these times - I am constantly looking for something interesting to cook for dinner. I cut back on the broth to add by 1 cup, as well as the hot sauce as the leftovers will be for my grandkids for lunch.  But I added all the cheese it called for - OMG yummy!  I will make this again, and again.  Oh, I used the chickpea pasta Banza.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: As per recipe except turkey and no bacon, and only a few squirts of sriracha. Really needed comfort food for us overseas, prior to election chaos! Recommend.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: My good god, this was delicious. I followed the recipe closely, using only 1/4 cup of hot sauce and using swirly macaroni for the pasta. I liked Hamburger Helper as a kid and this was a perfect grown up(ish) version.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
Very heavy. Eliminate the oil at beginning because you drain it off anyway. 
I enjoyed cooking it. It was a cozy meal to make on a cold night.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Use turkey, leave out bacon, use half/half, use deli AM. cheese or Monterey Jack, dial down hot sauce

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Substitutions for improved nutrition: Omit the bacon, but cook the ground meat with 1 Tbsp bacon fat, and add a half tsp liquid smoke along with the other liquid flavorings. Switch to ground turkey. Switching the cream to half & half, and the processed cheese singles to 3 ounces fresh mozzarella. That all reduces the calories to 724 per serving, and the sodium is "only" 1136. Cauliflower for some of all of the pasta would be even better.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I added broccoli & used Cashew milk instead. It was seamless

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Perfect election night comfort food. Omitted bacon and cheese, substituted chives with parsley. Took it next level by stirring in a generous amount of Heinz ketchup in the end, as an homage to my Dad's "Bolivian Chop Suey." (Although I think he just bestowed that name because he's Bolivian and not because they cook it this way.)

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Cook bacon first, remove, cook onion, garlic, ground beef. Omit wine. Do not salt. Use 2 cups broth and 2 cups water. Add 8 oz canned green chilies, 1/2 tsp cumin. Next time try adding a can of rotel tomatoes and chili. Great cold weather comfort food.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Phenomenal recipe. Made it as stated with two exceptions: added Worcestershire and seasoning to the ground beef, and cooked the bacon separately on the stovetop, adding it in at the very end to avoid overcooking. I can see how this could get funky, fast and would avoid a vinegary hot sauce. I used wellshire sugar free dry rubbed uncured bacon, Sauvignon blanc, 1/3 cup tapatio, horizon organic american singles, Whole Foods brand sharp cheddar, and banza macaroni. Delicious!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: This is the first meal I've ever made where my 3-yo toddler asked for seconds and ate ALL of it. Wish this meal was healthy enough that I could justify making it on the regular but, still, it's a delicious indulgence once and again. (Note that I used a fraction of the amount of hot sauce called and would do the same next time.)

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: We have dairy allergies in my family so I subbed the heavy cream with soy creamer (it's the heaviest non-dairy creamer I've found that isn't flavored or sweetened) and the cheese for daiya cheese, 1 1/2 C of "cheddar" daiya and a few small handfuls of "mozerella" daiya cheese (to equal what I thought might be 5 slices of american).  It's actually pretty flipping good!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Is there a wine pairing for this dish?  And is there a suggestion for how to serve it?

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Followed almost to the letter except the hot sauce (thanks to the other commenters!). Two tablespoons was just enough for a slight kick. It was perfect. My new favorite recipe for a cold night.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I was preparing this dish while on a weekly Zoom call with friends.  It brought up stories of "Hamburger Helper" from the box and I repeatedly told them this was going to be way different and IT WAS!  The bacon and the hot sauce are the key elements.  Delicious!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
I subbed bison for ground beef and chickpea pasta for regular, also seared 8oz baby Bella mushrooms and steamed 5oz baby spinach which I added at the end with the chives for extra fiber. Did only 3 slices of American and added 1/2 cup grated parm. It turned out great! I should have read others notes and cut the hot sauce but we're happy with it! 

A 1 cup serving (super filling and plenty for me!) comes out to 542 cals, 25.8 carbs, 30 fat, 34.3 protein.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: "Fancy Nancy" Hamburger Helper for sure! Browned the bacon first, saving some fat to brown the onions. 90/10 ground sirloin. 2 tbsp Cholula hit about the right heat note for us. 1 cup of wine seemed like a lot of liquid, so I used 1/2 cup and drank the rest while finishing the dish. Used a shredded Mexican cheese blend because that's what I had on hand. Scallions instead of chives for better flavor and more crunch. A rave review from my beloved.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Made as directed using Frank's hot sauce and 1/4 cup was perfect, it allowed us to add more at the table to suit individual palates. Using American cheese slices allows the dish to develop a creamy texture so when you add the grated Cheddar it blends beautifully. It's VERY rich, could easily cut back on the fat content of the dairy to suit your families taste.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
Make this for something comforting, delicious, unctuous, all the good words. I never had hamburger helper from the box but I know it can’t be this good. Some minor changes:
Ground turkey instead of beef (what I had in the freezer)
Sweet paprika instead of hot (what I had on hand)
3 slices bacon instead of 5 (b/c it was extra thick and smokey - this was a good decision)
Subbed some of the cream with whole milk - concerned it might split but it didn’t; might try all milk next time
Amazing!!!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: This was a HIT!  Yes, more work than opening a box and adding a mound of ground beef, but the flavors melded beautifully and it was a joy to eat.  As others mentioned, I added the spicy stuff to taste as it progressed, until it hit my personal sweet spot (well, heat spot, I guess).  Mise en place took a bit of time, so this isn’t a harried weekday meal, but worth making when you have a few extra minutes.  Leftovers were just as good!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Don’t use too much hot sauce. Much less than recipe calls for. Don’t use Kraft singles. Get American cheese from deli

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Comfort food heaven. Added a red bell pepper with the garlic. Maybe mushrooms next time too. Increased total quantity by half again to feed a crowd. Used half the cheese called for, subbed half and half for the cream, and just a couple tablespoons of hot sauce plus 3/4 tsp of ancho chili powder. Serve with hot sauce and it was a dream.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I added broccoli when I added the pasta. I used a whole 13.5 ounce box of whole wheat elbow macaroni. I omitted the bacon. I used beef bone broth. This made 6 huge servings.  Delicious

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Winner! I followed the tip of a previous post and switched the pasta to cauliflower. Even my 13 year old son loved it. We all had thirds.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Does it take time? Yes. But great things do. And I am grateful to be able to make such a thing and enjoy it, rather than sucking the joy out of it with our American way of making food “bad.” Make it on rare occasion and enjoy the hell out of it. Adapted to what I had on hand, using whole milk and the last few slices of a smoked havarti instead of American. Used Cholula hot sauce, went with a scant 1/4 c. Ate a full serving slowly and with great relish.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Subbed Beyond Meat crumbles for ground beef and it was delish. Kept the real bacon. Following others’ suggestions, reduced the hot sauce while cooking for less spicy palettes.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: So so so good. So so so HOT. Second recipe in a row from this app that has been WAY too spicy. Should have read the comments, third will be a charm. The garnish is incredible. Also used a blender to puree and wow, yes. Make this. Add like 2 tablespoons of the curry and hold on the chiles in the garnish (because you can always add more of both) and voila, ENJOY.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I used the entire 12 oz package of gluten free elbow noodles and it turned out great. I used Between 1/4 and a 1/3 cup Frank’s Red Hot and it was the perfect amount of spice and flavor. Split it into 6 servings and served with a simple side salad.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Enjoyed making this dish and loved that it was made in one pot. The velvety texture is so good. I am a little on the fence about the hot sauce, I think I would prefer chili flakes or more of an Italian flavor profile. Mushrooms would be a great addition to this dish. I always like to follow the recipe the first time and then adapt to our tastes for next time. This is legit comfort food.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I like comfort food, but this dish was too heavy for my tastes. 1/4 cup of oil plus fat from bacon and ground beef. I drained all the fat according the recipe, but there was still too much in the end.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I added canned diced tomatoes instead of the hot sauce.  Great.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: We made this with fresh venison instead of the beef and it cut down on the grease factor. Also added 2 fresh jalapeños from the garden, and reduced the hot sauce to about 1/8 cup. Overall, delicious! But definitely a comfort food dish - we made this on our first 40 degree fall day (in Georgia, this is pretty chilly). Also, do not make this if you are looking for a quick dinner. It took about 1.5 hours of hands-on time.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I love this dish, but I personally prefer to leave out the bacon because I don't like the texture and lower the amount of hot sauce to preference. I also just do a drizzle of oil rather than 1/4 cup. I can't imagine needing that much. I also use pretty lean beef so it doesn't have as much fat. Overall the dish is great and super customizable!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: This was delicious. I cooked it just as the recipe described, but omitted the hot sauce--everyone added it to their liking once it was served. It's very easy to enjoy! Don't think twice about making it--just go for it.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
I liked this dish but recommend the following changes to keep it as flavorful as possible while reducing the otherwise unpleasant fattiness (and keeping the taste as similar as possible to the original):

-- Only 2 TBSP oil instead of 1/4 cup
-- No bacon
-- Drain the ground beef thoroughly
-- Use 1/2 and 1/2 instead of heavy cream
-- Relace 1/2 to 1/3 cup hot sauce with 2 TBSP Cholula (Hamburger Helper was not a spicy dish!)

I also recommend making it the night before to let the flavors marry

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
Just like I remember, except it still tasted great after the second bite! Cook the bacon separately, that way you can get a nice crisp on it (or leave it out). 
I added half a bag of frozen string beans (the skinny ones from TJ's) for a truly one pot meal!.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I added extra pasta and some Trader Joe’s umami seasoning. I also dialed back the hot sauce so my kids could eat it. Delicious, but very rich. Served with salad.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Gut buster or not, this was totally delicious. Used pancetta instead of bacon & reduced hot sauce slightly. Otherwise followed recipe as is.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: This was delicious albeit a bit on the heavy side. I did mix in some frozen spinach which worked well.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: We loved this, yes, it’s like 8 different days plus some carbs, so don’t make it if that’s not what you want.  I didn’t use all the oil, subbed tomato paste and red wine for white wine, everyone loved it

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: This was yummy comfort food. I did not have bacon or cream, so I used evaporated skim milk.  Still was plenty rich and creamy.  For the leftovers, I added more pasta, as well as mushrooms, spinach, peppers, and some more onion.  I liked it better that way and will definitely use a full box of macaroni and add veggies when I make it again.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
I never had Hamburger Helper, but this tastes like a spicy bacon cheeseburger pasta.

We used half the bacon because we have double-smoked bacon and didn’t want it to overpower the flavor. We used the full 1/4 cup hot sauce and I don’t think it was too spicy, but the pasta was pretty flat - just cheese and hot sauce.

I had a ladle full and that was enough. It is super filling - I felt gross after eating it, to be honest. Probably wouldn’t make this again.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I thought this was excellent. It's not difficult, but requires patience and attention. The only ingredient alteration I made was the use of Sargento shredded cheeses and no use of american cheese slices. Preparation-wise, I browned the burger separately and as the bacon was cooking in step 3, and added when the bacon was done. Was great as a leftover. Short video on my prep - https://youtu.be/9SWvtZ_huns

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I’m not sure how I feel about this. It gives me a spicy chili cheese dog vibe and I think it’s because of the bacon. I feel like the bacon is unnecessary and I’ll definitely be omitting it when I make this again. I used the 1/4 cup of hot sauce and I find this to be too hot for hamburger helper. I’ll be reducing it to at least 1/8 cup next time.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: The bacon got totally lost so don’t bother. I feel like the meat to pasta ratio should be switched, one pound of pasta to half a pound of meat. Or at least one to one. I don’t think it needed the cream since the cheddar and American emulsion made it creamy enough. I subbed some of the water for a can of tomato sauce and used franks red hot sauce. Overall fine base recipe but I wish the editors and testers had done more work to finesse it.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I tend to add the bacon to the onion and garlic mixture ahead of the ground beef, so that when the ground beef is added, the bacon has had a chance to crisp up a bit in the mix.  I also use only a 1/2 cup of heavy cream and more stock or milk to lighten this up.  It is a family favourite!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: This is great! Definitely a “treat yo self” meal. Highly recommend using Valentina’s Salsa Picante if you don’t want a ton of heat but you want the flavor. Then sub with a habanero hot sauce for spice to taste. May add in a can of diced tomatoes next time.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Made as directed except cut down on hot sauce. Spot on comfort food. I use grass fed everything so it’s not the fat that bothers me but the carbs. Love the idea of using cauliflower as a sub for the Mac and will try that next time.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Yummm!!!! Great dish. Definitely dont need the half and half, and leave out hot sauce if family doesnt like spicy things. Bison to make it a little healther :)

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Followed recipe as is. I would add more pasta and less meat next time. A little too meat heavy for me

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Made this (almost) as written, but doubled the pasta and added a few extra cups of water, switched the heavy cream out for whole milk, and used Colby instead of American cheese. Sooooo decadent and delicious (if indigestion-inducing).

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
This is a HEAVY rich pot of comfort food, and I am glad I took the time. This is probably closer to 6-8 servings. 

I used ~1/4 c Crystal hot sauce, and more would have probably been good too. As another person noted, the vinegar in the hot sauce is key. 

I didn't have American cheese but cheddar-only still produced an ooey gooey cheese outcome! 

I'll definitely make this again next time I have to feed a large group... or the next time current events requires me to need a vat of comfort food.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I used the full 1/3 cup of Cholula Original hot sauce, which is one of my favorites.  Adds a lot of flavor without making the dish spicy hot.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
I subbed heavy cream for oat milk (because it’s what I had), used light vegetable broth instead of chicken stock, and cut the cheese in half. 
And it was still SO rich and SO good! 

Served with a spinach side salad, this was a delicious dinner. Next time I think I’ll just use one slice of bacon to get that little flavor without the fat. Definitely recommend this and modifying it as you desire—I think it’s hard to mess up.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Loved it. Although I’m sure it’s perfection as is, I tweaked as follows and absolutely went nuts for this dish. 1) I used regular paprika instead of smoked 2) In addition to the American sliced cheese, I used a blend of “Mexican cheese” blend and cheddar cheese and then I topped the entire dish with cheddar at the end. 3) I used a full pound or elbows instead of half. I also pre cooked my noodles just by mistake instead of boiling them in the sauce and was still very happy. 4)half cup of hot sau

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Use bigger pasta and a milder hot sauce just 1/8

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: This is a glorious meal for a cold day. Simple. Terrible for you. Delicious. A sure winner.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: 02/07/2021 - made this with TVP. 4oz dry mixed with about 2cups hot veggie stock. Make as recipe directs. Turned out like usual.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Delicious.  I left out the bacon and reduced the 1/4 cup oil to 2 tablespoons so I didn't have to drain the meat.  It took 45 minutes to complete.  Serve small portions, it's rich!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
I've made this a few times - a few ways I've tried to make it a bit healthier:

Added veggies via 1/4 C tomato paste, 1-2 C chopped spinach, 1-2 C chopped mushrooms.

Doubled the chicken broth and amount of pasta.

Omitted bacon or used just 1-2 slices (or used up a few remaining slices of prosciutto instead)

Also, I thought the 2 tsp of smoked paprika was overly smoky... so I swapped 1 tsp for regular paprika.

Like others, I used quite a lot less hot sauce to accommodate my husband's palate!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Can this me made ahead?

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: This is a rich, comforting dish, that reheats nicely (there are only two of us, so left overs are inevitable). And as proven by the pandemic pantry, adaptable.  Shelf-stable heavy cream isn't really that great for whipping - it is good for sauces, soups, and casseroles like this. One night I had bacon, no ground meat, and a little over a pound of cremini and button mushrooms; chopped these really fine and let them brown and crust with the bacon, the rest of the recipe as written.  Play is good.

author: Priya Krishna
