commentBody: I used 1/2c monterey instead of Am cheese (which I find a little off-putting) and it turned out beautifully creamy. Also used an Indian paprika/turmeric spice blend which was. Nice and have it that fluorescent cheese colour from my childhood

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I made this last night. It’s not difficult to make by any means, just time-consuming.  I made it exactly as written, using 1/4 C of Texas Pete Hot Sauce.  It was delicious, not too spicy (even for my husband), but boy was it salty! We both love salt. This was just too much. I think next time I’ll use low-sodium chicken stock and cut the bacon by half.  I only added the smallest pinch of salt to the onion. I’ll cut that out next time, too. Tasty comfort food, not a healthful entree.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: This is so delicious! Definitely a new favourite.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Leave out bacon

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Very tasty, but rather rich for our taste.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
This 4-serving gutbuster clocks in at over 1,100 calories per serving. I lightened it up by omitting the bacon, using half-and-half and 93% ground beef. I easily divided it into 6 servings with a salad on the side.

To those who don’t have to count calories/fat/etc., I salute you!—but I just couldn’t face my trainer if I’d made as written. (1/4 c oil to sauté a single onion?) So: 1 T oil, lean turkey instead of beef, no bacon, low-fat milk instead of cream, 3 slices of Gouda instead of 5 slices

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: But I’m going to have to let my pants out tomorrow. And as someone else noted, this is really “hamburger complicator.” Not difficult, just time-consuming for what it is. But a great melding of flavors. Amazing twist on comfort food.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: This is delicious! Definitely indulgent, but oh so satisfying. This also reheats very well, which makes it a great Sunday night recipe that will take you through the week.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: We made the following adjustments: very little oil, no bacon, no wine (sub homemade chicken stock), 3 tbsps franks red hot sauce, added frozen peas at the end. Was gonna put in frozen corn but didn't have on hand. This recipe was Made to substitute and adjust and still be amazing. It was soooo good.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Good winter comfort food

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Cook the whole thing on high and do it much faster

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I find this doesn't need the bacon but the heavy cream, white wine, and two different types of cheese are a nice touch. I consider this to be too fussy for a frequent weeknight meal but it's so delicious that it has made multiple appearances at my house. Don't rush cooking down the liquid or you'll end up with runny sauce!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Delicious! Ultimate comfort food. I took reviewers suggestions and added the bacon to the onions, and added mushrooms too. It was a little soupy so i doubled the shredded cheese, used Cooper Sharp American from the deli for the American. It ended up being too spicy for me so added a little sour cream (why stop there?) and it was perfect! I will reduce it to 1/8 cup next time (Used Beast Feast Maine Gourmet Hot Sauce). I can see making this again on a snow day!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I love this dish. Can have it for lunch or dinner. I use egg noodles instead of elbows and no bacon or hot sauce. A little less cheese but make sure to use the cheese slices! Adds something special to the dish. Still amazing!!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: So delicious. Warm up leftovers gently as it tends to split.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I used this recipe as part of cleaning out fridge prior to a trip. I used 14 oz. of ground venison rather than beef and it made it so I didn't need drain off any fat. I also used some leftover cooked penne which I added after the elbows were cooked. I also used a Mexican cheese mix rather than the American cheese. It all came together great and was pleasing to everybody.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Delicious.  Subbed Turkey for ground beef, rest as directed. It’s even better as leftovers.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: The only thing I changed was using ground venison. It was incredible. It felt decadent to eat. I wouldn't scale back on the ingredients if you're trying to lighten it up; cut the portion in half. It's filling. A little goes a long way.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Three in a big handful of chopped mushrooms and used only the processed cheese, huge hit in my house

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Can this be made with ground turkey?

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: The fat draining sounded a little onerous, so I crisped the bacon and cooked the aromatics and beef in its fat; added it, along with roasted mushrooms, at the end for texture and oomph. Also, I was cooking for wimps, so I did 2 tbsps hot sauce, 2 tbsps white vinegar. Next time will add msg!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: This recipe sounds like a fun feed!  But I am always daunted by the  instructions to “serve immediately.”  Why?

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Consistency with these one-pot meals is really a trick, it always looks like too much sauce, then as it cools it rapidly absorbs more and congeals a bit. Once it has cooled past a certain point, it can become quite stodgy so it's best to start eating your meal within a few minutes of pulling it off the heat so that it is enjoyable the whole time.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I recently discovered the great flavor of smoked bacon for my tomotao-based bolognese sauce. Just one slice, finely chopped, completely transforms this sauce. Keep the rest simple, add a finely-chopped habnero for an even-keeled kick, and you have a real winner. My teen son attempts to eat the entire container in a day.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: This is delicious as written. We've subbed cauliflower rice in for half of the elbow macaroni and that wasn't bad. We make this once a month or so because it hits all the comfort food notes and feeds a crowd nicely.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Made exactly as directed and it’s excellent. It makes a ton of food, however, so expect to have delicious leftovers for a few meals.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: The change I would make in the process the next time is cook the bacon with the onion when the onion was almost done so that it would be crispy. The changes I made because of what I had on hand were ground turkey, red wine, half and half. I also used only a tbsp of oil and 1/4 cup water for cooking the onion, worked just fine. Will make this again.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Really good, hearty and complex flavor for a ‘hamburger helper’. The making of fond in a couple steps was key to this recipe’s flavors. I added 1/4 cup hot sauce and it wasn’t that hot. Next time I will add a bit more.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: This is one of my all time favorite comfort food dishes. I make it with a few adjustments. I reduce the cheese a bit…4 slices of American and 1 cup of cheddar. I use 3 tablespoons of hot sauce. I reduce the oil to a tablespoon and always use lean ground beef. It’s so good. I think 6 servings is more accurate than 4 servings.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Omit the bacon!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Only if you don't like bacon!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Can this be made in a slow cooker?

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
The only change I made was regular smoked paprika instead of hot (not available in my area). I did a 1/4c of Cholula and in my opinion it was the perfect amount of spice. I followed the recipe to the letter and this came out great.

Everyone who says this is too spicy really has no palate for spice. I can’t go hotter than a jalapeño or serrano and this is no problem for me.

Also, there’s no way this is a 4 serving dish; it’s more like 8. I’ve had leftovers with my wife for 3 days with this.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Wow -- great comfort food recipe. Very satisfying on a cold night! Cut hot sauce in half as suggested. Regarding fat/carb/calorie content, it is super rich -- so I chose to have a small portion along with a salad on the side. Can't wait for leftovers tomorrow! BTW, NYT Cooking has filters for "Healthy", "Low-Calorie", "Low-Fat", "Low-Carb", "Low-Cholesterol", etc. Homemade Hamburger Helper not tagged under any of those, as we'd expect.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Incredible comfort food!! Yes, a lot of steps, but if you prep ingredients in the morning, it makes it very easy. Used 1/4 cup of Frank’s Red Hot, which was a bit more spice than I would like. but not enough for hubs and kid. Used sharp cheddar and 7 slices of deli American cheese. Cooked pasta for 11 minutes rather than 9.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
Listen, this isn't a diet site folks. This is a dreamy recipe with far less bad stuff than the boxed variety. Make it as-is the first time, then play with it. It is really good on paper, but your personal preferences will make it brighter and more satisfying. We changed all of the cheese to a more flavorful sharp cheddar, adding small portions to allow it to melt before adding more - preventing clumping. We've tried muenster and some others too.

This is a great wintery day feel-good recipe!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Krystal Hot Sauce - it is simple and bright tasting and not too hot. I would use Krystal in this recipe and it would be perfect.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I used 1/4 cup Frank’s Buffalo hot sauce and could’ve used more. Next time I’ll top it with some homemade sourdough, Italian seasoned croutons for a finishing touch. Overall, delicious and just what the doctor ordered on a cold and rainy day!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I keep bacon fat in the frig, added a tablespoon plus to brown onion and garlic..works like a charm, plenty of flavor without wasting bacon.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I am in Canada and there is no such thing as American cheese what type of cheese would be a good substitute for American cheese? Thanks for any or all response.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Extra cheddar will work just as well

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: The American cheese has coagulants in it that will make the sauce emulsify and not break and go grainy on you. If you don't have access to American cheese (Kraft singles will do the trick), then consider adding a few tbsp of a cornstarch or potato starch slurry to help emulsify things and prevent a grainy disaster.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: So good! Made this with ground turkey and chickpea pasta, also subbed in white wine vinegar (diluted with water) for white wine. Thoroughly satisfying meal.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I made this tonight.  It was great.  I love one-pot meals.  I had most of the ingredients on hand.  I substituted 1/2 and 1/2 for the heavy cream.  I did not caramelize the onions.  Instead I  cooked them over low to medium heat for about 10 minutes.  I was able to get this dish on the table in less than 40 minutes.  It is much tastier than box hamburger helper (going by my memory since it's been years since I've tried it.)

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Okay, so this is so delicious!!  I used ground turkey instead of beef and panchetta instead of bacon, just based on what I had available in the house, and it was so delicious!!  Seriously,  though,  this is eight servings easy. And I used a little less than 1/4 cup of Frank's hot sauce, but it was not hot at all.  Not sure what folks are talking about with the whole "too hot" business.  I think the 1/3rd cup would have been fine.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I always like to try a recipe the first time as written. That being said, I used cheddar Jack I stead of cheddar because it was what I had on hand. We used the 1/4 cup hot sauce in hopes our 14 month old might be able to have some too. I personally think the 1/4 is delicious but would love to try it with more. I also think baby girl will like it as it’s not slap you in the face spicy. Just a nice warning. Can’t wait to try it again

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I would cook the bacon ahead to ensure some crunch and also control the fat added. I also cut back on the hot sauce and would replace some of the cream with sour cream in the future to add a little kick. Comfort food for damp and cold days.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Made as-is except reduced hot sauce slightly and had to sun a whole milk-sour cream mixture for the heavy cream. It’s delicious for a casual dinner on a cold and rainy night, and gave me an excuse to open a bottle of white. Those are the best recipes.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: This was delicious.  Because we made it for our entire family (two kids), we didn't add the hot sauce, and it was still extremely delicious.  I cringed when I saw American Cheese, but, it gives it that extra creaminess.  Perfect for a "night before Halloween" dish.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: It tastes like the hamburger helper I grew up with. It’s very heavy. I feel like it needs tomatoes or something acidic to break up the heaviness. I like it. But I kinda feel gross about it. I did add in some spinach for some type of vegetable.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Gouda is a great substitution for the American cheese.  My daughter and I loved the dish.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
Add a couple of teaspoons of English mustard with the paprika, plus several dashes of Worcestershire.
Cut Frank's hot sauce to 2.5 tbsp.
Increase pasta to 10 oz.
2 tsp. smoked paprika and 1 tsp Aleppo pepper.
Cook bacon first, remove when crisp, then cook onions in bacon fat.
Add bacon back in at the end with the chives.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I haven't made this yet, but why would the recipe call for chicken broth instead of beef broth?

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Chicken broth has a richer flavor than beef broth. Also, beef broth can have a metallic taste. But make it with beef broth if that's what you think should be used.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Cauliflower is a good addition

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: No bacon, half a cup of half n half, no wine, person- specific hot sauce and no American cheese. Still pretty yum.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: tasty but lots of ingredients and work

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Maybe use the boxed Hamburger Helper to save yourself some effort.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
Wow! Just made it when I did not want to do burgers with some ground beef I needed to use. This is super rich and delish. Not a quick recipe (onions alone took 20 min!) but worth it. Glad we have leftovers.
Only mods were: only 1T sriracha; no wine around so used 3/4c water with 2T white wine vinegar; GF pasta; smoked not spicy paprika. 
Oh, and used a 12” pan and the pasta was not consistent as it was hard to keep submerged, so add a lid while the pasta cooks but still stir frequently.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
Maybe slightly less bacon? 
This recipe is so much more than hamburger helper, and we all loved it. However it was extremely-above-board-flavorful and the fatty aspect of flavorful was above our expectations. Maybe one or two less slices of bacon will deliver similar results? Next time I’ll try that and let y’all know :).

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: LOVE this recipe!  Spicy comfort food.  However I’ve had multiple people not want to try it because of the name.  So I’m renaming it Spicy Hamburger Mac & Cheese!!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
I'm not a wine drinker, so I skipped the white wine and after I drained the fat went straight to the chicken stock/hot sauce/heavy cream stage. It still turned out great.

Bacon is annoying to chop up and takes forever to cook compared to onions & garlic, so I'll probably leave it out next time.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Well....That was delicious. definitely heavy American comfort food but it did the trick tonight.  loved the hot sauce. used half and Half and not heavy cream.  Surprised at how much we liked it.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I made this exactly by the recipe, and it was very good except for the strange flavor from the smoked paprika. I would make again using either less smoked paprika or subbing regular paprika. Otherwise a good comfort dish I'll make again.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: This was delicious! The wine and chives made this dish. I simplified by boiling the noodles (doubled to 16 ounces noodles) separately and added in a handful of bacon crumbles. I added mushrooms and celery to the onion/garlic saute. Came together fast and easy, great weeknight meal - and the left overs will taste even better for lunch tomorrow.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I've made this a couple times and don't see any reason to alter the recipe as it is quite delicious.   My partner took leftovers to work, heated it up in the microwave and took it into a lunch meeting.  Everyone wondered what smelled so heavenly.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: First time I made it was very tasty but WAY TOO RICH for me. I tried to make a "healthier version" (reduced fat by tweaking bacon, cream, cheese etc) but it was definitely not as tasty, not worth the effort. So much for this one, on to all the other recipes yet to be tasted!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I was very impressed with the smoky flavor of this recipe. It’s so delicious and I haven’t tasted anything like it. We used Franks red hot and got the spicy paprika. I did use a little less oil for the onions based on other reviews. I used avocado oil. I would definitely make this again but it’s not light healthy recipe you want every week.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Sooooo good. Used ground dark meat turkey because I don't eat beef. Absolutely delish.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
Such an elevated “Bolognese Mac n cheese” yet so humble. I found this to be a bit saucy so I added more  pasta totaling 12oz instead of the 8…which came out perfect. 

I would recommend cutting the recipe in half if this were to be prepared for two people.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
The recipe worked well with Beyond Meat ground beef (and actual smoked bacon); texture was fine and it eliminated the issue of too much to be drained.  Just need to adjust the timing a bit: did not need to cook the Beyond Meat ground beef for 12-15 min; and the wine evaporated (or was absorbed?) much faster than 10 min.  

Also rendered the bacon fat first, removed the bacon, and cooked the onion in the bacon fat.  Still needed to add about 1 tbsp of vegetable oil to help cook the onion.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: This recipe is great. I haven’t been eating meat because it’s expensive, but I think it’s still good without it. It might not be as rich and fatty, but it’s still yummy. I also added chopped green pepper and used Marsala wine instead of white wine. I’m a huge fan of spice, so when I make it I use at least 1/3 cup franks hot sauce. Delicious!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I just finished cooking this at 9:19pm. Lol. I don’t eat beef so I used ground turkey. I also added garlic powder, onion powder, dried mustard, and thyme to the sauce. I subbed mozzarella for the American cheese because that’s what I had. I also used cavatappi because I prefer it to macaroni. It came out really delicious.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I made exactly, except I forgot to add the Papkrika and I am glad as I found the smoky flavor from the bacon was just right.  I made a double batch for the homeless youth that I cook for and I believe they will love it.  It has the 3 things they always want in a hot meal, meat, pasta and cheese; but lasagna gets pretty old, so this new twist should keep them happy

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Too rich for my cholesterol which is why it tastes so good. I too reduced bacon to 3 slices, drained off all fat and decrease the elbows to under 2 ounces.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Very delicious and very nostalgic. Definitely has a very pronounced bacon flavor so make sure you choose one that you like to use in this dish. We left the hot sauce out entirely because of our spicy food-averse children and added it to taste once all was made.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
Mods:
No bacon
Used red wine bc it was open
7 cups of water with some better than bouillon instead of 3
Forgot paprika 
Used full box of large shells instead of half box of elbows
Used franks hot wings sauce 

Too half the time allotted and was delicious

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: This will never be a healthy, low fat meal but it is delicious!   Make it healthier by using low fat ground meat, substituting ham for the bacon, skip the American cheese, use less cream & cheddar.   I halved the hot sauce and used  smoked paprika.   Yummy!   Don’t ask me why I made this in July!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
Made tonight.
No bacon or wine (hubby doesn't partake), but otherwise stuck to the recipe.
I like this. It brings back childhood memories but all grown up.
Next time I might try different cheeses, and reduce the oil at the start. Or maybe even cook the onions alongside the beef.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
In a family of 8 we called this noodle burger and we quite surprised when Hamburger Helper picked this up…though our family recipe differed slightly:
Fry hamburger til brown
Boil elbow macaroni, drain
Combine in noodle pot
Add ketchup, stir
Dinner in the table.
Family favorite.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: The recipe shows the perfect way to add fat to your meal.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: The rich, robust flavor of this dish is wonderful, but it’s absolutely swimming in oil and grease. I made it exactly as directed, but if I was to do it again, I would have used only a tablespoon or two of oil in the beginning, and patted the oil out of the meat rather than just draining it from the pot.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: This was amazing. Very rich!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I followed the directions pretty close but cut back on a few things. Reduced the cream down to 1/2 cup and went with 1 cup of shredded cheese, 4 slices of American. I add the hot sauce a little at a time working up to the 1/4 cup to dial in the heat. Excellent flavor and very delicious!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: It's good but greasy. Leftovers particularly came out very greasy

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I enjoyed this dish. The bacon didn’t seem to add anything other than additional fat, so I’d leave it out. The key, I think, us to really allow time to let the onions and meat get deeply seared. Great flavour. I might add mushrooms next time!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Does not reheat well. Too much grease. Bleh.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Add a tablespoon or two of gochujang, a teaspoon or two of gochugaru, and top with chopped kimchi and chives. Do half of the hot sauce requested. Out of this world delicious

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Made tonight, was trying to use up the ground beef in the freezer and the onion heading for the door.  I don't think it was quit cold enough outside yet for this one but delish.  Subbed sriracha and some gochugaru for the hot sauce (didn't have any in the fridge????) and skipped the American cheese for some shredded mix. Used a colored pasta that made the whole thing sorta brown....  But it was good!  Feel like I need to go walk some of it off though...

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Are you to use pasta that has already been cooked, or do you cook the past in the liquid?

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: You add uncooked pasta and cook it in the liquid.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Made it to directions and regret using the bacon. Too salty. Would have been perfect without bacon.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: If you’re a real cook, there’s a container of rendered bacon or pancetta grease in your fridge. Skip the chopped bacon entirely and sauté the onions and garlic in the hoarded fat that you know you have. Drain the cooked ground meat well, maybe even give it a quick hot rinse in a mesh strainer. Presto, you have eliminated from this dish a ton of fat that you will never ever miss.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
Public service announcement:
“Hot sauce” in this recipe doesn’t mean Tabasco or other kind that you’d normally use very sparingly. It means the less-concentrated type of sauce used for Buffalo chicken wings. Ray’s works perfectly.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Really good. Can be greasy so recommend very lean ground beef and trimming fat off bacon

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: This comes out way way too salty. I’d omit the bacon (wasn’t necessary and will reduce salt) and use low sodium or no salt chicken stock and/or substitute water for half the amount of stock

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I recommend making this exactly as written (only tweak I made was to omit the bacon since my husband is pork-free). Yes, it's a gut bomb, but it's a perfect meal to indulge in maybe once per year!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
So delicious! I’ve made this twice. The first time I cooked it alongside the original Hamburger Helper and this one is definitely better (maybe that’s obvious but I’m saying I think the effort put in for this recipe is worth it)- warmer color and a bit more spice. 

I didn’t measure but it still turned out great. I also didn’t want to buy $8 bacon so I used some leftover bacon grease which I think helped the flavor without going through all that. Definitely a good hardy dinner!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: We used turkey bacon & impossible burger and added 1/2 head of finely chopped broccoli. We will be adding a ton more broccoli to the leftovers. The 1/4c hot sauce was crazy hot! And we like heat- added sour cream to balance that out.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: So this wasn’t spicy at all. I used franks hot sauce. A bit too vinegary to be honest. I followed the recipe exactly

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: This was a revelation. Made exactly as written, using a quarter cup of sriracha. Nice even heat. Served it with a simple green salad and some bread.  Not something you can eat all that often, but it was a delight. And love that it uses only one pan/pot.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Oh, and I skipped the draining the fat step. Because, why bother?

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
I think the title throws people off and turns people away. It is time consuming. It is heavy. It is delicious. The only change I make is crisping the bacon first, using that fat to begin step one, and adding the bacon back after browning the beef. 
A more accurate title may be "Meaty One-pot Mac and Cheese"
We make it semi-regularly through the winter and lasts our small family at least 2 meals.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: SO GOOD! Don’t skimp on the paprika. The first time I made this it took me a long time but after making it 3-4 times it’s super quick now! Bacon, for me is optional.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Used red wine as I didn’t realize we were out of white. Worked well - made a rich sauce.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: It was good but a very rich recipe. In my opinion so much oil was not needed to cook the onion and beef. I also found the amount of paprika overwhelming. Only used a little hot sauce

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
I buy butcher section bacon, not the thin stuff you can see through. If I follow steps the bacon is still chewy. What I do is render the bacon on med high first for 5 minutes. Use the bacon grease in lieu of oil and then follow directions as normal.
As others I use much less tapatio hot sauce (1/8th cup) and then provide more to add later for your own palate.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: This was incredible! I made a few changes to the recipe. For some reason I thought 5 pieces of bacon was too many, so I only did 4 and crispied it up, took it out and added it at the end. And, seriously, macaroni does not do justice to this. I used cavatappi. Game changer, People! Everyone lucky enough to eat this was fist pumping and hip bumping. I will for sure make it again. Maybe for Christmas dinner.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: So delicious! And relatively easy. I skipped the bacon, used half-and-half instead of heavy cream, fusilli pasta, regular American cheese, and red rooster hot sauce. Loved!!!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: This is our comfort food go-to! Good, hearty food after a day of shoveling the neverending Eastern Sierra snow. Don't alter the recipe, just make it as directed, eat, and enjoy!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
Made this instead of homemade pizza for a mess of 8 yr old kids over for a Saturday supper after realizing I didn’t want to spend the night throwing dough while all the other parents drank wine. A couple parents warned me their kids don’t eat anything that’s food. Well, apparently this isn’t food: plates came back to the kitchen clean. 

Used beef broth, not chicken because that’s all I had, and got a few things out of sequence towards the end but seems like a very durable recipe. A new go-to!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: This is perfect.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: For presentation purposes and, and because a crispy top is always good, cover the top with some parm and - if you like - bread crumbs, and stick it under the broiler for a few minutes.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
We made this as directed as part of a "meatfest" we were having with friends. It IS decadent, and not something to eat every day. Our house smelled like meat for 2 days! But it WAS delicious and easy to make. Just takes some time. 
I might make this for our next camping trip, so we can just reheat and serve for an easy and satisfying dinner, when those extra calories are needed! 
Serve it with a salad to feel better about the caloric intake. ;)

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Question- I don’t have wine around- should I buy some just for this? What’s a good pantry sub?

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Thats a tough one, just use more stock or water or if you have it 1/2 apple cider/grape juice and water.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Made with Banza chickpea pasta, no bacon, half n half instead of heavy cream, and half a head of chopped cauliflower (added right before liquid). Did not drain fat. Came out great! I might do a little less paprika next time, pretty smoky flavor hit.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I’ve never had hamburger helper, so I’ve nothing to compare it to, but this is good! Stick to your ribs! Sumptuous! Almost luxurious! I’ll make this again but prob only in the coldest cold of winter or for a potluck—def for a potluck! It’s just too sinful to make on a regular basis. I did add more broth and less half and half, and my paprika was smoked but not hot. I prob did not use the entire amt of hot sauce. What a great change from chili! Great for tailgating!! Yum! Kudos to chef/creator!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Do you think you can freeze this?

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Where does one find American cheese outside of America? Canadian wants to know! Doesn’t exist here…I guess I drive over the border?

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: It is just regular Kraft slices or Velveeta

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I think American cheese is included for creamy emulsification purposes. You could substitute with Cheez Whiz (I do this in other recipes with great success) or Velveeta. From reading through other responses, it looks possible to leave it out entirely or simply add in more shredded cheese of your choice. Happy cooking!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I added more hot sauce, more cheese, and used fancier noodles than suggested (full of gluten). Super delicious. Don't make this every day and you don't have to worry about the calories or fat or salt or whatever. Add a mile to your run or a few extra sets to your lift if you're worried about it being unhealthy. Eat a salad tomorrow.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: HOLY CALORIES!  Big fan of Skinny Taste recipes so had to cut some calories and fat somewhere in this delicious recipe.  Subs-4 slices of reduced fat cheddar and 4 slices of Horizon American, 1/8 cup oil, 4 (not 5) slices of bacon and omitted the wine.  I couldn't force myself to sub out the heavy cream.  For 1 serving: Cal 742, Fat 51(OUCH but go low on fat tomorrow), Carb 23, Fiber 1.5, Pro 44.  This will be my only meal for the day.  Worth it!!!!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: This is a flavor bomb. Super creamy and yummy comfort food. The end!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I added spinach after the final step to make myself feel healthier and add a color. It was a nice addition and wilts very quickly.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: This was so good! I omitted hot sauce and used dried chives. Labor intensive.  Tons of calories. Comfort food.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I opted for a really lean ground turkey. I skipped draining the fat because there was hardly any to drain. Even with the bacon, there wasn't much. I subbed the smoked paprika for regular paprika + chipotle chili powder & it came out delicious.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
The six year old said he loved this one. And he's been picky. I used 1 Tbsp of Tobasco. Delicious! I also used 1 cup of milk & 2 Tbsp of corn starch instead of cream. For oil, I used 2 Tbsp. I also didn't use any salt or pepper--there was enough flavor without it. 

I cooked it ahead of time and kept it in the fridge. I heated it up green onions and chopped cherry tomatoes.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: End result, very yummy. Made it easier and slightly healthier by browning meat in one pan, bought pre diced onions, browning onions in another pan w 1 tsp oil. Drained meat and added onions and meat to my dutch oven. Saved time and fat calories by doing that. I subbed 2 Tbsp Franks Hot sauce, 2 Tbsp worcestershire sauce and did 1 tsp dark chili powder 1 tsp smoked paprika. Could have been spicer but served with red chili flakes. Also subbed GF pasta. Everyone had seconds.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Brings back fond memories of a dish we referred to as "train wreck" back in college more years ago than I want to admit (and wistful memories of having the metabolism of a 20-something). Cut back on the hot sauce and smoked paprika. Pure comfort food. Totally worth living on salad for a few days to atone for.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: What a great recipe-super comfort food, or for a cold evening meal.  Since the introduction mentioned being a "Bolognese"-type sauce I decided to follow that method and use finely diced carrot and celery with the onion, sautéed in bacon fat (didn’t use bacon), added a mixture of ground beef & pork, also smoked paprika and really good hot sauce.  For the pasta, I had cavatappi noodles which also made it more special. Looking forward to the leftovers!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Delicious! I made some changes because I'm following WW. I used ground turkey instead of beef and half of the bacon. I disliked both the taste or texture of the bacon, so I would omit it entirely the next time. I also used half and half instead of cream and, following another person's tip here, substituted half of the pasta with cauliflower. I used Pasta Carba Nada pasta. I used all of the hot sauce (Tapatio)! I like heat, but I could see why a lot thought it was too much.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Priya never misses. This is great. I only made one change - no bacon - and it still came out great. I highly recommend taking the time to go through the steps to get the depth of flavor. I like the addition of hot sauce as well for that little tingle on the palate. I used sriracha. A lot of this is just watch and wait so even though there’s a lot of steps, it’s not really that labor intensive. If you’re looking for easy, you’re prob better off with the box, but this is worth it!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: So, so good. I love the suggestion to use the bacon drippings to sauté the onion. then the extra oil is not needed.  Will do that in the future. Also agree that 1/4 hot sauce is enough — pass more to guests if needed.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: made this last night -whole family loved it!  next time would use ground turkey instead of beef and pancetta instead of bacon.  loved the depth the white wine added.  will absolutely make again.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I followed the recipe with some minor tweaks to suit our tastes.  Used a fair amount of paprika, chile powder, and allspice since I was omitting hot sauce and the recipe says that is most of the flavor.  I always use whole milk instead of cream unless its critical.  I also scaled the cheese back because it looked too cheesey in the pics.  Man was this good.  Will make again.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Made it exactly as the recipe the 1st time, however I used Tapatio for my hot sauce. Big mistake, it came out way too spicy. Next time I will wait to add the hot sauce at the end a little at a time, I would reduce the amount of bacon, since I used thick slices. I would also add mushrooms & top with some panko bread crumbs & broil for 5 minutes to get a nice crust on top.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: One of the best I've made from NYT! Switched around the steps like some users suggested and cooked the meats first, then sautéed the onion in drippings. Used a full pound of pasta, so doubled the chicken broth/water and added a splash more cream. Full pound of pasta is the way to go! Still rich and delicious, but much less heavy!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Easy to make this a *bit* lower in fat & calories.   Use less bacon, fry it up separately & drain on paper towels.   Drain ground beef/onion mixture of most of the fat.  Use much less cream & more stock.   Use less cheddar & no American cheese.   Serve with a salad.  It’s still amazing & we get about 7 servings from it.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Very umami, rich flavor - kids liked it. A bit rich for adults.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Use 1-2 tablespoons Tabasco maybe add to tasting as you go just before adding the cheese.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Loved the suggestion to start with bacon and skip the extra oil. It was super delicious and, surprisingly for such a rich dish, didn't give me indigestion at all.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: 5/15/23 tried it again, good, but not spectacular. Used the wine this time.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Made basically as is but subbed the ground beef for ground turkey. Accidentally added an extra 1/2 cup of shredded cheddar bc measured wrong, and omitted 2 of the american when I realized. Still tasted great, I might use less cheese overall next time.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Delicious!  I didn’t measure the oil or hot sauce, hoped to to add less.  My kids don’t like spicy and they still loved it. Next time I’m going to try with ground turkey.  As is it’s great!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Need to double the liquids for a whole box of pasta. Enough meat but need double sauce and spice probably

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Works great with ground pork and subbing all the cheese for cheddar jack!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Made this last night.   I omitted the bacon but otherwise followed the recipe.  Used Murder Hornet Hot sauce in the blood orange agave flavor.  I was afraid it would be too hot but as promised it brought tons of flavor.  Used double the garlic.  My family devoured this.  Even the heat shy 8 year old.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Cooked as directed, except reduced the hot sauce by half.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: We make this often, with a few changes. I cook the onion in a big spoonful of store bought bacon grease, and skip the bacon. Usually green onions instead of chives. I don't have to drain, but I wouldn't be able to tell you the day percentage of our ground beef as it's from a neighbor. 1/3c of Frank's is perfect for us.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: At my house, we call this beefaroni. My husband once called it hamburger helper, and I promptly corrected him. Quick, cozy food on a cold night.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: My family has been making a similar recipe for 50 years - I call it "Mom's casserole". I boil & drain the macaroni, combine with a bacon/ground beef/onion/celery/mushroom mixture prepared in a manner similar to this recipe, then I add about 3 cups tomato sauce and grated cheese, & bake it all for an hour.  Next time I will improve my version by using the seasonings & method of this recipe - thanks!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Ok, so the most important thing to know is that I’m just an ok cook. Not great. Many who read NYT cooking are fabulous chefs. I’m not one of them. There just wasn’t a lot of taste here, except greasy. Maybe that’s due to it being really hard to drain the fat out of a Le Cruset Dutch oven. Just saying… if you’re not an amazing chef like so many here, you might want to stay away from this one.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Ok, an important thing to know about removing grease from a deep pan: fold a paper towel into 8ths, push the meat away from a side of the pan, press one of the edges of the folded paper towel into the pool of grease and let it absorb the grease. Toss the paper towel. Repeat as necessary. Fat is where the flavor is, but you are correct: no one likes a dish with pools of grease. With garlic, chicken stock, hot sauce, cheese, chives, and paprika, this dish should not be lacking "taste."

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I would make again for a kid and adult friendly meal. Some comments mentioned it being greasy, so I halved the bacon and used ground turkey. That did the trick. I also added some green peas as other suggested, which were a welcome addition. I included some provolone I have been meaning to use up, which gave me the idea to include Gouda next time to increase the smoky flavor.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
OOPS, someone used cauliflower, not broccoli. "I used 1/4 cup of mild buffalo wing sauce (Noble Made) and subbed a head of steamed cauliflower cut to 1/2 inch florets instead of the macaroni. Leave out most of the broth (used about a TBSP), combine the cream, hot sauce, cheeses and broth, then mix in the already steamed florets. Makes an amazing lower carb option."

Also, I wouldn't add the pasta. I'd cook it separately so that means less liquid in the Sloppy Joe.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Does this Freeze?

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Recipe reminds me of the America's Test Kitchen skillet baked ziti except - American, not Italian. And re why cauliflower and not macaroni - some people are cutting carbs due to blood sugar levels, not watching fat or calories.  Good alternative to doughnuts. This would be a treat!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I am not a big beef eater, but every so often I develop a yen. So I made this! I left out the bacon, but otherwise followed the recipe, using Frank’s hot sauce, which I think has a good flavor and isn’t too hot. I have no idea what the original Hamburger Helper tastes like, but my visiting son thought this was great. And my carnivorous craving was sated for another several months.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
I haven't had ground beef in the house since my two sons left home over ten years ago.
I do not care for beef at all.  
But ground pork is very tasty in so many dishes.  Try Picadillo.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Has anyone tried this with ground turkey? Recipe sounds yummy…..but i try to limit the amount of beef I  consume.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: After sneaking a peek at the nutritional information, I can see why this is good for a potluck supper, where most will be taking only a spoonful of this and several other offerings. I'm certain it will be delicious, but I will tuck this one away for when I need a to please a crowd of folks whose health concerns I don't have to take into account when cooking for them.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: To reduce the amount of time to make this recipe, you could start boiling a pot of water to cook the pasta separately while starting on the rest of the dish. If the sauce is too thick, then add some chicken broth to it before adding the pasta and cheese.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Beats store-bought hamburger helper any day of the week!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I love the subtle pairing of American with Cheddar cheese. It's that kind of nuanced understanding of flavor which separates fine cooking from ordinary fare!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I cooked this several times. Great recipe! I liked it more on the spicy side, but that’s just me. But. If you don’t like the caloric values: feel free to skip over to another recipe, for goodness’ sakes. No one proposed this as a weight watcher supper.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Delicious but spicy as directed. Be careful with kids.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: The apple wood smoked bacon I used from Whole Foods completely overwhelmed all other flavor.  I will use regular bacon next time and cook it separately first.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
Thank you for the perfect recipe that checks so many boxes….

Indulgent: Did you run a marathon today or maybe just get your kids to school and picked up kinda on time this week? You’ve earned these calories! 

Nostalgic: Did you grow up in the ‘80’s when convenient boxed dinners were actually pretty aok or maybe just went to college in the ‘90’s when that’s all you could afford? You deserve this upgrade! 

You’ve been there and done that but this is next level to it all. Check the box and do it

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: This recipe is amazing, and I have almost no notes, but I made it with a longer cooking pasta than elbow macaroni because that’s what I had and in retrospect I would have added more liquid. In my situation my pasta had a 10-12 minute cooking time on the package and most macaronis come in at about 8 minutes. I had to add about 1/2 cup of water to get it fully cooked.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Also, as others have mentioned I would cook the bacon first to render out the fat (but not get it crisp) and then cook the onions in that (adding a little oil or butter if necessary). I feel like it’s easier to brown the beef when it’s just beef and you probably won’t need to dump out extra fat in that case.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I would swap bacon for pancetta or omit all together. I mistakenly used smoked paprika for regular....be sure to watch that! Otherwise, it was a hit

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
It is definitely a comfort food type recipe.  Made exactly as written.  We did not find it too spicy.

One of the notes below talks about the American cheese and it is definitely worth it to go to your deli counter and buy some American cheese (again NOT Kraft slices).  The meltiness of the American cheese makes this recipe very creamy.

I will likely not make this again for awhile, but I've put it in my virtual recipe box to pull out for a night of comfort food.

Thank you.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I made this today where I am living in Spain.  I had to make a few adjustments to the cheeses, found cheddar OK but had to find an American cheese substitute that worked well. Also, couldn't find cream, half and half, nor crema, so subbed Philly Cream Cheese. Yes, it is everywhere.  I used gluten free pasta and Frank's as some had suggested.  It turned out fabuloso!  Family says it is one of their new favorites. I want to sub in the cauliflower for the pasta next time.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: To much sodium

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Great recipe. Took me about 1.5hrs because of the wait time on the onions and bacon cooking down. I subbed ground turkey, browned the bacon for 5 min then added the turkey and cooked down for 15 mins, added onions for 10 mins then garlic, then deglazed with wine after 20 mins. Waited 10 mins for wine to cook down then added stock, cream, hot sauce and spices. Added pasta then Added in cheeses after 8 mins. Turned out fantastic and garnished with scallions. Serving for at least 6-8, not 4!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
Between the stock, bacon and cheese, there is plenty of salt! Don’t add more.  Too salty for me and my family.  I’ll add more pasta next time to stretch out the saltiness!  
Heavy cream is not needed. Neither is the added oil.  I added a Tbl of oil to sauté the onions and garlic.  I also used half & half instead of heavy cream.  Next time I’m using skim.
Should you simmer the final mixture with the cover on?
In other words,I’m looking for a better recipe that is less salty and fatty.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I wish I had heeded the notes about being judicious with the hot sauce a bit more carefully. I added less than a 1/4 cup, but it was still too hot for me, although my husband loved it. Speaking of my meat-loving husband, he didn't make a peep about my substituting ground turkey and smoked turkey bacon (a whole lot less fat and less sodium than regular smoked bacon). As others suggested, I used half and half. Even so, my husband gives the meal a 5 out of 5. Next time, fresh thyme!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: This was delicious...awesome adult comfort food. I substituted turkey bacon and whole milk for the heavy cream. I will make it again.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I made a vegetarian version of this by using beyond beef and skipping the bacon. Used 1/3 cup Cholula (I like spicy) - very tasty and filling!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Recipes like this that call for American cheese: don't use the wrapped slices, use cheese from the deli--they are NOT the same, despite what another commenter here asserted. A recipe for stovetop mac and cheese from Cooks Illustrated explains the difference: https://www.americastestkitchen.com/recipes/9474-simple-stovetop-macaroni-and-cheese (you have to have a membership to access it here, but it gives the full explanation of the difference, and why it's important not to use slices).

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Wine choice and omitting hot sauce can impact the quality of the overall flavor of the dish. Even if the family is averse to spicy, sort out what can be done instead of the hot sauce to add depth of flavor.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I agree with the comment about cooking the bacon first and then will cook the onions, garlic and beef. Otherwise the onions and garlic tend to burn while browning the meats.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: My only tip: MORE BACON! I had seven strips left out of a pound and used them all. Deliciously smoky!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Made this tonight. First reaction: this is a salt bomb. If you add up the sodium in the ingredients, you're going to get some impressive numbers. I added zero salt in the beginning and the recipe was still over the top salty. So be forewarned. Otherwise, it's good, a darned sight better than the boxed stuff for very little additional work. Someone commented that this is complicated, so I assume they've never made the boxed stuff. Except for sauteeing the onions, takes the same amount of time.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: The best drunk food I’ve ever eaten sober. It was *delicious* but it did make me feel like a potato lol. Even AFTER I omitted the bacon, american, & used oat milk in place of the heavy cream (just what I had on hand), this tasty tasty dish put us to bed lol.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Ok I made this exactly as directed and it was delicious—salty, creamy, smoky, carb filled goodness! Will I make modifications the next time? Yes I will. I’ll cook the bacon first and then sauté the onion in the drippings. I did 1/4 cup of the hot sauce and for us it wasn’t enough so I’ll be a bit more heavy handed next time. We like heat! I will also add salt at the end—with bacon, cheese, and hot sauce I felt like I didn’t need anything extra. Time consuming for sure but for us worth the effort

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: This was fantastic. I made minor changes. I skipped the bacon. I puréed spinach and added since there were no veggies in sight. I loved the spice with a 1/4 cup sriracha. The whole family enjoyed.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
My girlfriend, who is a more or less vegan (based on her theory that anything that tastes good is both unhealthy and probably morally wrong). Fortunately, she's not judge-y and allowed this dish in our kitchen.

Just one substitution out of expedience: beer instead of wine, but I feel that it was in the spirit of the thing. I also used rotini instead of elbows.

I loved it. She admitted to loving it as well.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: It was amazing! I used turkey bacon and ground turkey, Velveeta for the American cheese ingredient (don't judge, I live in the midwest), added a squeeze of tomato paste, and did not add any hot sauce due to picky kids in the house, but my husband and I enjoyed adding some on the finished product. Super delicious, and worth the effort. Can't wait for leftovers today!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I made this with minor modifications to accommodate dietary restrictions and loved it. I discovered, however, that the leftovers the next day were even better. Next time I'm going to double the recipe in order to freeze some.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Recipe came out great! I made it in about half the time suggested by using a deep 12” cast iron skillet and first smashing the beef on it a la a huge smash burger and caramelizing on both sides, then removing and doing the aromatics, then deglazing with white wine and stock, and using egg noodles instead of macaroni which cool twice as fast. I also pushed the cream inclusion until the pasta was almost done and then wrapped up with the cheese and hot sauce. So tiny tweaks and I made this in 30min

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I made this but felt the Tabasco sauce amount was a lot. I added 1ounce to the pound of beef. I also used cellentani pasta instead of elbows. I also didn’t add the bacon.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Son tried it today & he said I made the right call on using 1 ounce of Tabasco sauce to the beef.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
I cooked the bacon first in a separate pan and added it much later in the recipe because we like it crispy.
Definitely thought it was too rich.  Will use 15% next time.  
I might cut down on the cheese a bit as well.
We used sriracha for hot sauce.  Will use Frank's next time.  
Great comfort food.  Will certainly make again in the dead of winter!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Absolutely decision!  But a lot of time and a lot of ingredients.  The end product was worth while, trying it at least one time.  I will make the gravy with peas and some other veggies.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Not a hot sauce fan, so I leave that out.  And I do a 1/2 and 1/2 mixture of hot and smoked paprika.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: This dish was very very rich. I would prefer a lighter version without so much fat.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Great for teenage boys. It was too strong or intense for my guys so I took the left overs And added a lot more noodles. Next time I'd definitely triple the noodles...but hey, I'm feeding an army of working teens and logger husband.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Did anyone else’s cream curdle?   Actually I used evaporated milk and it still curdled a bit.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Leave out the paprika next time.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Will not add the hot smoked paprika next time.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Can I bake in aluminum pan instead of cooking on stovetop? I need to transport to a friend. Thanks.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: The cheddar measurement being in cups, I figured about 100g. Added a little gruyere as it was on hand vs American cheese. Used ground sirloin. Next time I'll cook the bacon before the beef so it crisps up a little more. I will still cook the onions as described so they slow cook nicely. I drained the meat onion mix on a paper towel and wiped the Dutch oven clean before proceeding. Used Cholula as my hot sauce and 1/4 cup was just on the edge of too hot (but hubby loved it).

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: A lot of work for a disgusting looking, but mildly palatable dish. Directions were followed exactly, except that the pasta cooking phase required about 3/4 additional chicken stock to get the pasta to al dente. I found it to be very heavy and seriously unattractive on the plate. I'll stick to basic mac and cheese or a meatloaf in the future. Don't recommend.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I made as written. The sauce was a bit too thin. I made with extra sharp cheddar. Next time I would also add blue cheese crumbles for extra flavor as there is blue cheese in the original Hamburger Helper.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: This is insanely rich. Almost too rich.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Divine. Lucious. Comfort food extraordinaire. Don’t hurt your brain. Make as is and enjoy.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I always use recipes as a starting point/suggestion, but I stuck pretty closely to this one even though I did make some changes. Turned out great! Will be making again. I did: cook bacon to halfway point then add onion & garlic. When onions are translucent add in the beef. I did 1/3 c cholula, but will cut back on that next time. I used half & half, and did 4 cups beef broth instead of wine & chicken broth.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: This recipe is the bomb. I would make this for entertaining easily.  Key is taking the time to do each layer: developing the crust, reducing the white wine, etc.  I used whole milk to shave what little fat calories I could, white sharp cheddar, and only about a tablespoon of hot sauce (Aardvark brand).  Love the one pot aspect too.  For what it is-a souped up Hamburger Helper-I give this 5 stars for comfort food level deliciousness.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: This was super yummy… I will say though, I feel like I really craved some veggies in it. Like broccoli or peas. My husband really liked it and agreed, when I make it again, I will add something green.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I had a lot of fun with this one - and isn't the recipe written beautifully? Cut it in half for two people, which shortened cook time considerably. Cooked bacon first and the onion in the drippings. Drained beef mixture on paper towels and wiped the skillet, as others suggested. Used two tablespoons plus a few shakes of Cholua hot sauce - will use less next time. Reaching al dente past took longer than suggested, and I did need to add a bit more broth, but was a really lovely dish to cook.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: It was delicious. I skipped the bacon and made a couple of healthy substitutes so not as calorie dense: Greek yogurt instead of heavy cream, parmesan instead of american bc thats what i had, and probably half the amount of cheddar. husband loved it.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Made this with gusto! Grated, measured stirred and, just like Frankenstein's Monster, it arrived alive!  My wife and I dug in. I almost cried when she exclaimed that it was the best dish I ever made as surely it was not and just as surely I will not make it again; see the subject line above. This dish has no agency nor a future with me. Tasty yes!  Balanced yes!  Attractive check! Yet the hamburger with onion, washed with cheeses, just did not appeal. My wife was hungry!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I made this as directed. Very good, substantial meal. Makes 4 very ample, filling servings. You could stretch this meal out with smaller portions and serving with a side salad. But plenty of food on its own. Loved the flavor. I used 1/4 cup of Frank's red hot sauce, which was just enough touch of heat for me. I used a white wine from Portugal (a cup for the recipe, a cup for the cook!), Silk non-dairy heavy cream (it worked fine) and Barrilla gluten-free elbow macaroni. I would make this again.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: By the way, this definitely took me more than 1 hour 15 minutes to make. If you are going to make this recipe, give yourself ample time to prep and cook. Took me, from beginning to end, about 2 hours.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: This was so delicious. I will be making it more in the future.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Amazing recipe. I went lighter on the fat and drained the ground beef well, otherwise as written. Comfort food at its finest

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I didn't use the bacon and substituted non-fat greek yogurt for the heavy cream.  Still very good, but not the wow type of delicious that I bet it would be with the bacon and the cream.  I bet my version will get better as it sits and all the flavors have more time to come together.  Simple recipe and I enjoyed making it.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Cooked the bacon and beef first, and then caramelized the onions in the oil left over rather than adding oil in the first step!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
Excellent recipe!  I increased the meat by approximately 25%.  For grated cheddar I used old cheddar.  

Being a Canadian, I could not find “American cheese” slices, so I substituted “Swiss cheese” slices.  I’m not sure how different it would have tasted, but I was pleased with the result.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
I have made this about four times and it is by far mine and my husband's favorite homemade cheeseburger mac recipe. By my fourth time, I now skip the oil, cook the bacon first and remove, caramelize the onions in the bacon fat, cook the beef and drain it well, use two cups of stock.
If you don't use the American cheese the cheddar will not melt and stay creamy; the American cheese has stabilizers that you need to make this sauce creamy -the sauce is not a bechamel.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: If the year 1978 had a flavor, it'd be this recipe. I drastically lightened up the recipe and made it in 30 minutes. Used ground chicken, no bacon and cooked the onions in 2T olive oil for two minutes before adding in the meat. Used smoked paprika ( NOT hot smoked) and used half and half. Also used less hot sauce, as I can't tolerate spice. Ooey gooey creamy.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Too much bacon. Skip this or reduce by half at least. Other than that, it has all the makings of hamburger helper.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Heck yeah. My husband made this for our anniversary dinner (he would have made me anything, imagine his confusion when I said homemade hamburger helper lol) and it was decadent comfort in a bowl for sure. We did not have bacon and we swapped the cream with creme fraiche but other than that the recipe was followed as written, including the hot sauce quantity. The hot sauce and it’s accompanying tang is necessary in my opinion. It definitely hit the spot!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I made this as written, except that I used buttermilk instead of cream, and I crisped the bacon first in order to use the fat as the medium to brown the onions. For hot sauce, I used 1 ounce of Tabasco, and 1 ounce of Cholula. For those who don’t love spicy foods, I suggest you start with 1/2 oz of Tabasco and taste. And all you have to do is read the recipe to know this isn’t a diet dish, but I think it makes more than 4 servings, and yes, it is pretty tasty.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Used vevan ched shreds in place of all cheese and dairy free yogurt plus almond milk to make a heavy cream substitute. I will cut bacon down to 2 or 3 strips next time as that flavor was a little too intense for my taste. Otherwise it was quite delicious and will absolutely make again!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
Made it just as directed - end result was great
Very enjoyable and a solid amount of leftovers
Used whipping cream cause I couldn’t find anything with higher fat.
Will make again

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Second time I’ve made this.  No salt needed when cooking the meats.  I used bison to reduce the fat.  Only used 2T hot sauce.  You can easily halve the cheese.   It’s yummy but high calorie.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Can this be frozen?

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: A winner!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Add 2 cups of hot sauce instead of one

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I really like this recipe- and if you really want to live más, use Taco Bell Fire sauce as your hot sauce (they bottle it now) I also add cumin and garlic/onion powder. I don’t find this recipe that spicy, but I like spice so I usually just dump the hot stuff in. I’ve also done this one with Cholula and a haberno sauce I friend made. It’s pretty perfect comfort food, definitely an occasional thing as it is very dense

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I agree with KMR; cook the bacon, remove, sauté onions, then add the garlic and then the beef.  If you sauté the onions in oil and then throw out the oil, out goes all of the flavor that infused the oil.  Complete waste of flavor and oil.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Instead of regular bacon strips, I prefer European bacon (also called shoulder bacon or butcher’s cut bacon) - has nice smoky flavor and virtually no fat.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
This is our all-time favorite NYT comfort food recipe. 

It does not pretend to be nor should it be treated as a health food. However, I do agree with another commenter that the use of oil is silly when the bacon fat is right there. So I do the bacon first, drain off a little, and then caramelize the onion and brown the beef in the fat. I toss the bacon back in after draining the fat from the beef. 

Other than that small change this recipe needs no edits. It is delightfully indulgent.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Great recipe, as is, for those that like the idea of Hamburger Helper but homemade. Next time I would use a hotter sauce than Franks - would have elevated it beyond a homemade clone.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I made this a month ago and am still thinking about it. It was so incredibly good. Rich and complex- but it walks up to the line of being too much so and stops before crossing. The leftovers were just as delicious as when freshly made, and I generally hate leftovers. Don’t change anything as you make this.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I had to add a good bit of salt and pepper at the end.  I also started with one-third cup hot sauce.  Next time I will increase it.  I had to add more at the end. I would also saute the bacon completely before adding the hamburger.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
Excellent comfort food! I used the prescribed amount of Cholula hot sauce (I buy it by the liter, LOL) and I also added some chopped hot green peppers in at same time as the bacon and beef. I reduced the amount of bacon to just two slices, and I ended up just using cheddar, didn't have any American cheese on hand. Finally, I used whole milk homemade yogurt instead of cream.

For such a simple recipe, this really delivers... will definitely be making it again!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Very heavy and one dimensional but not too bad on a cold night. Made it with 1.5 oz of crystal hot sauce and 2tsp of smoked (not hot smoked, didn’t have any) paprika and the spice level was quite tame. Used 5 slices of deli Swiss as I didn’t have American and I think it would have benefited from American cheese’s creaminess. Better than the hamburger helper I remember from the old days.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I was yearning for this comfort meal from childhood! Because of what I had on hand, I omitted the bacon and hot sauce, added red pepper flakes, 1 can of fire-roasted diced tomatoes, reduced water by 1 cup (due to the addition of tomatoes), and used 2 cups of cheddar/gruyere combo for the cheese. It came out perfectly and wasn't all that much work. Having everything prepped before cooking makes it easy. Would definitely make it again with my modifications.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I made this last night and it’s a new family favorite. I  only used about a tablespoon of oil because I had decided not to caramelize the onion. If you plan to take the full 25 min, you will need more oil or else they will burn; I also added a diced red bell pepper for a little sweetness. I used 3lb and ground beef and 5 strips of bacon. Drained everything WELL. There will be a lot of grease. I didn’t have American so I used shredded cheddar and mozzarella. I also added ketchup.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Deep and flavorful. Way better than out of the box with a similar cook time. I played around with the scaling, went heavier on the seasonings, snuck in some MSG, and used the bacon fat to cook the onions.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: What a fun dish! Didn't have American cheese and it was still lovely. Used shredded chicken from a rotisserie bird, still lovely. Used a few tabs. of Chinese chili-garlic sauce instead of hot sauce, just lovely. This dish is a good framework for a stovetop macaroni casserole inspiration! Thanks for this.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Skipped the bacon as we didn’t have any, used stock instead of the wine (also didn’t have any), and used cream cheese in place of the American cheese (again didn’t have any). Honestly, probably would either use cream cheese or even sour cream or Greek yoghurt if making in the future as we liked the extra zing it gave the dish. The SO loved this.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Made this for dinner last night, it was fantastic. My husband was gushing over it, will definitely be making it again. I was able to cut the time down to 45 minutes by racking my heat and using a thick cast iron pan that retained the heat. Will definitely be making again.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Took a while to make but my family loved it.  Will definitely make again (on a weekend).  The only change I would make is to cook the bacon before adding the meat.  This would allow for the bacon to cook through all the way and the chance to pour off some grease.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I hate wine, so I skipped step 5. Added a few tablespoons of tomato paste at that time for a bit of richness. My grocery order came with half & half instead of heavy cream, so I used that. I only added a few good splashes of hot sauce, knowing folks could add more at the end if they wanted it. I cooked the bacon first and added the onions to that, omitting the oil entirely. Perhaps should have removed the cooked bacon until later and just used the fat. Still turned out great!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: You could modify this recipe multiple ways to make it healthy, but honestly if you want to do that maybe look for something different - healthy - to cook. A little bit of decadence here and there isn't going to kill you.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I’m a huge Louisiana or Texas Pete hot sauce fan, love spice and love this recipe. I may still be partial to the other NYTC fancy hamburger helper recipe with the Italian sausage mostly because it adds greens… maybe some of you who didn’t look at the ingredients and realize this isn’t the “healthiest” recipe should try that one!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Really tasty - i would put this in the “comfort “ food category.  I would not add any salt at all until the very end - it’s pretty salty already!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Fantastic revamp of comfort food

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Would make again but pull way back on the hot sauce and smoked paprika. I was also confused by the instruction to "dice" the onion into 1/2 inch pieces. Dicing is cutting into 1/4 inch squares. Well, it didn't affect the final product too much.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Quick caramelized the onions with 1 Tb butter and substituted ground pork as it was hanging out in the fridge.  Used only 1/2c. wine since that was all that was left in the bottle.  Omitted the hot sauce, and ended up with about 1c. of grated cheese after combining the odds and ends in the cheese drawer.   Followed the recipe as written otherwise, and had an excellent lunch on the table in about 30 minutes.  Next time I think I’ll add in a couple of shredded zucchini, and cut back the cream…

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Fantastic! I kept to the instructions the first three times, and it was delicious. The fourth time, (so I could pretend it was slightly nutritious) I added a 16 oz. can of diced tomatoes after browning the bacon and before adding the beef. Even better!!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: If you’re on a diet skip this. If you think you can make it taste good by cutting corners, don’t bother. I double it up, using a full pack of bacon, a 2lb bag of cavatappi pasta. Sometimes i buy the 1/2 pork and 1/2 beef from the store and that’s great as well. Cook the bacon with the onions and you can get a better brown on the beef. Get the pan screaming hot before you add the wine. Also I’ve found Valentina is the best for this hot sauce wise. Makes for a richer consistency

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I’m not usually one to suggest changing recipes and making them healthier, I love a rich dish. But this recipe might be the exception. It was like a brick in my stomach, and after the work of making it, I didn’t wanna feel gross like that. I’d make some of the changes people have suggested and swap out the heavy cream, use rendered bacon fat instead of adding more oil, maybe even add some peas or something. It will still be very delicious and indulgent but won’t make you feel straight up ill.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: 1/4 c hot sauce is too much, it robs the flavor. Couldn’t taste the beef or the bacon. Disappointing. Would make again with only 1 T hot sauce. I used Tobasco. Might have been passable with Chiloula.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: This is a keeper. I omitted the canola oil. Instead I browned the bacon, drained it on paper towel, then used the rendered fat to sauté the onion. Used 1/4 c Texas Pete and it was just the right amount of heat for us.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Successfully subbed low fat sour cream for the heavy cream. I also upped the pasta to 12oz because I was cooking for a few people and found it plenty saucy and decadent. I think you could easily stretch to 16oz pasta.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
Kids really like it! Even my more picky child. I made a few modifications to speed things up and for my kids 

1. Crisped the bacon first then removed from pan
2. Used bacon grease to cook my onions and garlic (used half a large onion) 
3. 3-4 dashes of hot sauce for the kiddos. Hubby added more to taste once done 
4. Added cheese slowly and tasted as we went. We did end up using the full amount but you may prefer less. 
5. Used a “shellbow” noodle. It’s a bit large and could hold more sauce

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: This is absolutely delicious and the perfect winter meal, but honestly, I’d make this all year round. Make it and enjoy it on the couch with your comfort show- you won’t regret it!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I love this dish and make it as directed, but it ends up a bit too loose for me. Next time I will use 1lb of pasta and add additional chicken stock if the mixture looks too dry while the pasta cooks.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
I cook the bacon separately, draining it on paper towels and pouring off (or reserving, if you like) the excess fat and then browning the beef in that.  This can be done at the same time as the onions are caramelizing in the pot you intend to serve the meal in, to which the cooked and drained meat can be added later.  Otherwise, I find the dish to be a little too greasy.  

A note: last time, stuck for white wine, I substituted a cup of beer. It actually works really well.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: @Chris what kind of beer? I assume something light? I keep Stella Artois on hand.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: This recipe is so life-changing that my friend took a picture of the finished product, printed it, and put it on her fridge.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I added WAY less hot sauce, Midwestern lol! But it was great! Way better than the boxes I remember from college 25 years ago!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I thought the bacon made it too rich, and took away from the flavor. If I make this again (unlikely), I will skip the bacon.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I cook my bacon first and then the onion in the bacon fat etc.... then then add the bacon back last so it stays crispy.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: i enjoyed making this dish, but 1/4 cup of hot sauce might be a lot... i like it hot and spicy that was not the issue, i used Tabasco brand hot sauce and i think the Tabasco is too Vinegary. I will try again sometime and use a different hot sauce.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: You can lower the calories of this dish by using much less oil to saute the onion, and also if you put the browned ground beef in a colander after it’s cooked and wash it under hot running water.  This removed most of the fat.  It also removes some of the flavorful gelatinous proteins, a situation that can be mitigated by dissolving a teaspoon or so of low salt Better Than Bullion with a q

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: ……Quarter cup of hot water and mixing that with the beef mince.  It’s an acceptable substitute and cuts both calories and fat.  Personally I would also leave out the bacon, which contributes to the huge per serving calorie count.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I wish I would have read the comments. I thought 1/4 cup hot sauce would be enough of a kick, it was way too much. My husband and son loved it tho. Too hot for me.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I made this a second time using ground turkey and 2% and it's just as good as written. Great recipe!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
Sweat the bacon first and don't add oil. 
Didn't have white wine open so I used a half a can of brut cider. Used the higher end of the hot sauce suggestion, and used 1% instead of cream because I had it. Worked perfectly fine and was delicious.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: The bacon AND the paprika made for a very strong flavor. I would probably eliminate one in a future recipe.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Love making this for a cozy crowd-pleaser. Doesn't need the bacon. And hear me out... add a couple tablespoons of ketchup - it really makes it taste like the childhood original. You could also do some tomato paste and sugar/honey, but it's easier, and when I don't add it, it feels like something is missing.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Can this be frozen?

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
Cooked with 90/10 Bison, no bacon, 1/3c cholula, added Worcestershire. And cheddar based on what we had.

Delish

author: Priya Krishna
