commentBody: holy wow. this sent me to another plane of existence. and no it definitely did not need the bacon, also benefits from an especially tangy/spicy hot sauce since it’s veryyyy rich

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Delicious, but too heavy for my husband and I and too spicy for the kids.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
The first time I made this I made it as directed, but I had a few problems with it. 1. While the bacon is here for flavour and not texture as another commenter noted, personally I don’t think anyone likes biting into flabby bacon. And 2., as someone who grew up in the American Southwest, when I read “hot sauce”, I reached for my trusty tapatio — adding 1/4 of a cup resulted in too much heat for this dish, as another commenter noted. In spite of that, it was tasty, but difficult to eat very much of. 

When I told my dad about this, he, in all his culinary wisdom, informed me that when a recipe is from the north east, “hot sauce” usually means Frank’s. 

So the second time I made it I rendered out my bacon as step one and cooked the onion and garlic in the bacon fat (in line with several comments), and used Frank’s. I didn’t have any yellow cheddar so I used a mix of English cheddar and Gouda, and I added a couple dashes or Worcestershire as another commenter recommended. The result was a creamy dream with just enough heat to warm us up from the first cold, rainy night of the season. 

Absolutely fantastic, will be making again with these notes.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I'm not sure why this recipe is so popular.  The flavors don't really go together, and the taste of smoked paprika is quite overpowering.  I had to add tomato paste and extra sriracha just to make it palatable.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: This is a splurge night...like Halloween, for example. Your friends will love it! -Oct 31, 2024

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I have made this twice because my family disliked this so much the first time I made it, I was sure I had done something wrong and wanted to redeem myself. Nope. Made it again. Not good. So heavy. Such an odd flavor. So much work. I really don’t get the hype.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: This is so salty, do not add any! Also, cut the stock by half, or use water. So many ingredients add salt to this dish. It's very tasty tho

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: It was whatever. The idea of that much hot sauce is hilarious - I omitted completely because I was cooking for kids. In the end it was a lot of work for something nobody loved.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Hello all! I made this with my S.O. It was a 10/10 dish. Absolutely killer. BUT what we did was cooked the bacon but didn’t re-add it do the cooking process, we didn’t want the bacon to be gummy and loose it’s crunch. We used it as a garnish instead! This is the way it should be done, the variation in texture elevated the dish very well!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I’ve made this recipe with my adult kids several times.  Each time in the winter.  We enjoy the richness and simplicity.  One change for us is reducing the amount of hot sauce and pasta.  I wish all my  cooking efforts came out this well.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I never had store HH so I can't compare the two, but I'm sure if I don't like this one (which I really didn't), I wouldn't like the store one, either. I thought it was just "meh." When my 19yo son and husband don't like a dish like this, that's all I need to know.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: My mom is gluten free, so used chickpea pasta and it was delicious! Really make sure you char your ground beef for maximum flavour.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: This provokes dry heaves just thinking about Hamburger Helper. Perhaps it was because my Italian mother didn't read English well when reading the directions on the box, or perhaps because she raised us eating ragu and various risotti as well as her homemade passata di pomodoro.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: We call it “Johnny  Marzetti”  in Ohio/PA

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Remove the bay leaf?

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Preferably before you add the cheeses. Seems to be missing from the instructions.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I leave out the American cheese, go lighter on the bacon, add a little extra cheddar and use half-and-half.  It isn't quick, but my husband loves it.  The only reason I gave it 4 stars is that it is time consuming.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I diced up a carrot to add to the onions as a mirepoix. Also added 1 tablespoon Worcestershire sauce to the beef while it was cooking. I cut back on the hot sauce using only 1 tablespoon of cilantro HankSauce. Also added about 1/2 cup of Comte that I had laying around. The depth of flavor was amazing. The macaroni picked up some brown from the sauce so if you’d like a nice bright white contrast as pictured in the recipe it might be good to cook the pasta separately. Go easy with the salt.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: So good. I had to use what I had. That meant evaporated milk instead of cream, no American cheese and no bacon, and gluten free noodles. Yummy.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I’ve never eaten hamburger helper before but this sounded so good. It turns out it was not for me. My kids were not fans either.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: 1 hour 15 minutes to prepare. Now we know why Mom loved the 20-minute prep time for this back in 1970!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Just checked the nutrition facts on the boxed version. 321 calories, 12 grams of fat. This one is ridiculously over 1000 calories and 90 grams of fat. I also think the serving size is enormous. I get that this is no diet dish, but it grosses men out enough that I would never even make this.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Inspired by reading the ingredients for this dish, I worry about this trend where high-fat meals are regularly offered here at NYT Cooking. We have an obesity epidemic that afflicts the entire nation. This dish is swimming in dangerous fats. Bacon, two kinds of cheese, hamburger, heavy cream (!), and oil. One person here said it clocked in at 1,100 calories per serving. Are we going for Cheesecake Factory levels of bad fats now.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: @Deb if you don’t like the recipe, keep scrolling and find something that suits you. Variety is what makes life interesting and that includes eating food that doesn’t conform to a certain diet plan. I’ve never understood why people post complaints about food they haven’t cooked.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: My husband and mother in law laughed when I got excited about this recipe.  Yet I made it anyways.  I loved hamburger helper as a kid, and I was curious for this gourmet version.  It is delicious, even as I took some liberties to make it slightly lighter.  (Ground turkey, light cheese, etc).  It is now a staple for us to make as a road trip comfort meal!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Cut down on the hot sauce if you are like me. May take some time in the kitchen, but it is really satisfying on a cold winter day.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Made with 1/4 cup of Tabasco. Far too hot for my family, which loved spicy food.Straight into the trash.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: This was really good. I took some tips from the some of the other cooks. I cooked the bacon down first, removed from the pan to drain, then added ground beef (leaner cut) with the onions and garlic. I didn't need to add much avocado oil after that, then cooked as directed. My husband cannot have lot of sodium for health reasons, I used low sodium center-cut bacon, low sodium chicken stock, did not add additional salt (just pepper), and it was perfect. Deli American and cheddar cheese.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I loved this recipe! I swapped the heavy cream with milk and butter mixture, the macaroni with fusilli, and a few tablespoons of olive oil with the bacon. Went with white American cheese from the deli / but would choose a totally different cheese like Gouda if I could make this again! 9/10

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: One way to reduce the fat from the hamburger is to cook it in water in a skillet I know it sounds weird but this is a trick from Chef Ric Orlando and it works.  Drain off the water and continue with the recipe.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I’m going to add a can of fire roasted tomatoes to this next time. The taste was good but it needed a little something. I think the tomatoes will put it over the top!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I wanted to love this, but it was too heavy and too spicy for me and my kids.  I might try again, but would cut out hot sauce and bacon and add some crushed tomatoes.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I suggest moderating the hot sauce. But you be you

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I left out the bacon and only used 1/2 c cream. I also cooked the meat first, took it out and drained off the grease. I then used a small amount of olive oil to sauté onion, then garlic, adding the cooked meat back in. I get “comfort food” but all that fat was not needed, in addition to cream and cheese! I would make it again, however would add less broth…pasta was ready before all of the liquid was absorbed so I had to boil it out before adding in the cheese. I think some Worcestershire would be a good addition too.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Added a bit more spice with dried green chilies, chili powder, garlic powder, onion powder. Creamy and delicious.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: This is a great version of exactly what it is - savory, cheesy, comfort food on a budget. It’s obviously not a diet food, so you’ll need to make changes if you’re counting calories. As far as the difference in opinion on spice levels go, my guess is that it has to do with the hot paprika. Not sure how much variation in spice level is common for hot paprika, but the brand I have runs HOT. I’m pretty down with spicy food, and even a teaspoon of that stuff changes a recipe dramatically. Subbing reg paprika for hot would absolutely account for the differences in opinion on spice. Anyway, I added the recommended amount of the hot paprika,   one glug of the hot sauce. It made for a moderate level of heat. Satisfied me without overwhelming the rest of the family. I think it would be really good with diced jalapeño added in the early stages, and next time would probably do that   hot sauce to taste, while subbing reg paprika for the hot.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Followed directions to the “T”.   Bleech. Just because it’s in my beloved Times doesn’t make every dish wonderful.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Skip the bacon. Double the pasta. Decrease the cheese slices. Also skipped the chives. This recipe was FIRE.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Wow, just wow - that's what my teenagers managed to utter after tasting this divinity. Yeah, it may be too much for a calorie conscious adult, but if you want to see your endlessly hungry kids happy, this will make their day. Used less oil than what it says in the recipe, poured in a couple of splashes of white vermouth instead of white wine (since I didn't have any lying around), used regular cream (don't have heavy variety available here) and maybe half of the grated cheddar. Fantastic!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: This was perfect for my picky kids on a cold night. They don't do spicy so I subbed a mixture of tomato paste, apple cider vinegar, ancho chili powder and salt. Also used non-hot smoked paprika. It's delicious.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Tastes good but too many steps!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I made this for my two young daughters and my wife. Took more than an hour to cook, tasted disgusting and made all of us feel like we were going to die after two bites. It quickly became bean taco night instead.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Omitted the bacon and used 90/10 beef. Otherwise made it as asked. absolutely delicious and comforting.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: 20-25 minutes to cook onions? Is that correct?

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
@Marjorie

Properly caramelized, this is the correct timing.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I used Louisiana hot sauce instead of a Tabasco or Cholula, paired with the rest of the white wine- it was a great spicy cheesy dish!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I made this exacyly as written.  It definitely feeds more people than stated.  Its very rich. If i make again, i would use half the oil to cook the onions and omit the bacon completely. I would also omit the cheese slices.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Although my husband and I grew up during the 70s and saw many Hamburger Helper recipes, we have never eaten Hamburger Helper. Our teen daughter thought this looked interesting so I cooked it. My husband and daughter thought it was ok but could not bring themselves to eat the leftovers before they went bad. I don’t eat meat but thought it looked disgusting. There are so many things you can make with the ingredients. Why mix them together?

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I highly recommend using ground bison in place of the beef. Much leaner, fewer calories. 1/4 oil seems excessive. 1T worked fine.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: What kind of cheese would work as a sub for american cheese? fontina?

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: My husband and I loved this, however it was way too spicy for our kids. I’ll reduce the hot sauce next time.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Didn't use the bacon.  Used ground turkey, and fat free half and half instead of cream and shredded cheddar (no slices).  I really liked it, my family thought it was ok.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Used ground turkey and extra chicken broth instead of white wine. Didn't have any cheddar so I used extra white American cheese.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Let's lighten up this nostalgia. Omit most of the oil, and bacon. Fry up the onions in a couple of minutes, use ground chicken, use half and half instead of cream, and use One tablespoon of hot sauce and just reguajr smoked paprika. I had it on the table in 30 minutes following these tips. Tasted like 1978

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I love this as a base recipe and I don’t say this as a critique! I used minimal oil to caramelize the onions and only used a couple strips of bacon. I also use venison because that’s what I had. I didn’t have heavy cream so I just puréed 3/4 of a cup of 2% cottage cheese.   I have the Penzey’s smoked paprika, which is extremely strong so I only used about a third of the measurement called for with the smoked paprika and the rest. I use regular sweet paprika. Subbed sharp cheddar and parm.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Absolutely delicious. I did trim the fat a bit by using 93% lean grass, fed beef, Canadian bacon, chopped finely, instead of bacon strips and half-and-half instead of heavy cream. I also used whole wheat elbows to get a little bit of fiber. Otherwise I followed the recipe exactly. Perfect for a cold snowy afternoon/evening!  Have a glass of red wine and a salad on the side.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I added mustard powder along with paprika and a squeeze of lemon at the end to brighten it up.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I cut down on the bacon and hot sauce.  It seems fine with less.  Even so, i thought the bacon a bit much.  Otherwise, i followed the recipe without difficulty and produced a dish that is amenable to children.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I thought this recipe was a bit "claggy" - an English term meaning too sticky/too....something. I wanted to like it, but I just thought it was too much cheese, bacon etc. but I did like the spice level. I don't think I will make it again - I agree with a comment below that the caloric wallop of this recipe was too much for me to handle!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Thank you for the new term, usually we use the words greasy or rich but they don't quite hit right.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: So good it’s sinful. Don’t look at the calories! Just trust that she is RICH. I also cut the hot sauce in half just because I’m personally not a fan. And I used low sodium everything because holy cow, folks… but definitely making it again after I finish a cleanse.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: This is good but it’s way too much hot sauce and i like heat but more importantly you can’t possibly add the bacon raw with the beef unless you want it to never be crispy which is gross. Cook it when uou start the onions separately to crispy it up and then also you don’t have to drain grease later if you just remove it from the pan with a slotted spoon.  Then delicious!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I didn’t grow up eating hamburger helper but have always been curious about it.  I made this with a few modifications and it was delicious.  To reduce the fat, I used ground chicken, less cheese and 1% milk (it’s what I had on hand).  For the cheese, I used up odds and ends, 1/4 cup cream cheese, a heal of Manchego and four slices of American cheese.  With the use of seasonings, chicken stock and wine the flavor was full and not lacking, the texture velvety.  I topped it with micro greens and a side of green beans.  Such a satisfying dish.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Excellent and a more complete meal with a bag of frozen peas thrown in!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I love this so much - but omit the hot sauce and add 3/4 small can of tomato paste.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Argh, forgot to say, you don't need any fat, especially if you cook the bacon first and use whatever you need to cook your beef and onions. Our Midwestern family with a middle schooler only gets one teaspoon of hot paprika and one dollop of hot sauce. Honestly the original recipe was really spicy for me, but I'm a level 2 spicy person.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Added tomato paste and less paprika

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Has anyone ever added sautéed mushrooms?

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: We always save our bacon fat and for this recipe, it is great to use that rather than oil to saute the onions because it gives you that nice smoky taste which makes it unnecessary to cook and add bacon. This dish is delicious with bacon but also good without.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
Followed the comments for advice. Did not need to add oil to this recipe at all.

 I like to brown and drain my meat first and set it aside. Cook the bacon, add the onion, garlic, and follow the recipe otherwise. I thought it was good and the family liked it, however, I plan to tweak the recipe some more. I did not add hot sauce bc my kids wouldn't eat it otherwise, so it needs something else to compensate, IMO. Not sure what yet, will have to work on it.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
I took the time to mise en place all of the ingredients beforehand, and it was a waste of time. This recipe seems made for just in time cooking. Also in the spirit of Hamburger Helper a substituted a cup of beer for the white wine. 

It turned out delicious!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I saved this recipe long ago and finally made it for dinner. Woof, it's waaaay too rich. That was a one and done for me!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: 1 pan!!  first onions, added garlic then added ground beef!  Then cause I’m really really lazy- I started grating the cheddar - got bored and just cubed it! It’s fine- it all melts.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I made this and thought it was pretty awful. First time I ever felt that way about a NYT recipe. (And yes, I did read all the comment first; I always do.) When I make a big pot of something, I usually bring some down to my building's desk person, but I was too embarrassed to bring him this. If you want a ground beef, cheese and pasta casserole, there are so many other (edible) recipes. This one even smelled weird.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Skipped hot sauce and added some ancho chile powder & regular paprika to the smoked paprika. The flavor profile was pretty good, but the dish is just far, far too rich.  With so much salty bacon plus the heavy cream and the fat from the beef, this was so fatty and salty that I couldn’t make it through a serving. I drained out as much oil as I could but it was still like shoveling in Crisco.  My teen boy thought it was amazing, however, so if you want to make some ultra-high-calorie teen chow this would probably be popular.  But if you don’t have the metabolism of Michael Phelps or a 14 year old boy, think twice.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: We love this, it's absolutely a weekend treat given the work. I've made some adaptations recommended by others, such as browning the bacon first, and I don't feel caramelizing the onions is worth the time. I just cook until they're soft. We're very Midwestern, so I do just a dash of hot sauce and like 1/2tsp of the hot paprika. It's absolutely great! What hamburger helper wishes it were!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Starting with some chopped pancetta (thanks Trader Joe’s) with a little olive oil then the chopped onion, pre-crushed frozen garlic (thanks, again, TJ) before adding beef and a good stir of prepared pesto (hey, TJ) with the rest of the stuff, much faster. (Easier to pre-cook the pasta separately, BTW, and stir in at the end.)

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: instead of heavy cream, I used milk powder.  probably not as tasty but kids didn't miss it

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Making this tonight and it was about 45 min without bacon and added tomato sauce I had to use up. Can’t wait to see what he thinks of this. Classic childhood.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: @Jenski H ok so the verdict was this was really good and I found it easy and tasty without bacon and less oil and cream. Lots of paprika and hot sauce. We only had cheddar and put a bit on top. My son liked it very ver much. A keeper and fun to make. Play with it, the spices are all key and added also a parm rind and bias in the freezer.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Measurement are off I think, it made a soup for me.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Delicious, but so time consuming. There’s gotta be a way to condense this. Especially for a recipe that’s I’m sure was originally meant to be easy. All the ingredients I believe were necessary though.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: if you're going to change the ingredients to reduce the fat and calories, don't even bother making it. It's called Hamburger Helper for a reason!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Can this be frozen?

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: What can I substitute for the wine?  All tips appreciated.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: This is nothing like the dish my mother and grandmother used to make, which was baked and browned on top. Sauteed hamburger and onion got mixed with a can of tomatoes and elbow pasta (I don't know if it was raw) in an oven proof baking dish, topped with lots of real cheddar cheese, and baked for 40 minutes or an hour or so. The cheese, macaroni, and tomato formed a thick, browned, and tasty crust on the top. Inside was the soft and bland but appetizing ground beef, onions, and macaroni.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
I've probably posted about this one before - but just wanted to add a few notes.
Use ground round - less fatty.
I skip the bacon and just use 1/4 cup of fat (I store in the refrigerator).  And though I love chives, I usually skip them.
I agree that it's an artery clogger - but for a couple of times a year I'm willing to chance it.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: This was delicious with a few adjustments, I used ground turkey (all I had in the house).  I used an additional teaspoon of paprika (sweet). I used half the cream and very little oil since the bacon grease provided a lot of oil. I will definitely make again.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I’ve made this twice now. The first time with bacon and the second time without. I do prefer it without the bacon. I also used half and half instead of heavy cream, omitted the American cheese, and used a shredded three cheese blend. This is a delicious recipe, but very heavy, so I will make it on occasion.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
This was delicious! The whole family loved it. 
I have a very picky two year old. My fear every time I cook dinner is that she’ll hate whatever is I’m making. She ate every last morsel. A win!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: added pre-sauteed zucchini & mushrooms and microwaved green peas (other veggies would work too); adding them at the end.  Also used 1/3 cup cream cheese and a bit more stock to increase creaminess to incorporate extra veggies.  Very Delicious.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
This was delicious! I cut the amount of sliced cheese and used whole milk over half and half. I also used olive oil and butter to cook the onions and garlic. And beef. 
It really hit the spot and was so good!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Yuck. This wasn't just rich, it was heavy, greasy and salty. I have no idea why folks like Shake Shack, so I guess I should have just skipped this one. This recipe wasted one evening for making it and most of the next day feeling bad from eating it.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: This is so good. I didn’t have any red wine so I just used an extra cup of chicken stock and it worked just fine

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Great recipe, doesn’t really need the hot sauce IMO, made it with and without, the taste without the hot sauce was a better IMO

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: This recipe is a winner!  I made it without hot sauce and used crème fraiche instead of heavy cream because that’s what I had on hand.  Absolutely delicious anytime, and especially on a cold winter day!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: As a childhood fan of HH who tried it again as an adult and found it very lacking, I've been making something similar for years. I don't use bacon, I use cheddar powder instead of actual cheddar cheese, and I use half and half instead of cream. Eliminate salt and add 1-2 tsp no-msg Chinese chicken powder and defs use chicken stock. It boosts depth of flavor even more. Instead of or with hot sauce, add chopped jalapeno at the end to give it crunch, color and spiciness. Also add tbsp of mayo.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: It was okay- pretty easy. A bit gummy. I probably will not make again

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Followed the recipe and family loved it.  Got about 6 servings.  It's a calorie bomb though.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Made it tonight. Didn’t have dry white wine (subbed 1/2 cup apple juice and 1/2 cup white wine vinegar), didn’t have heavy cream (subbed Mexican crema), and just did 3 tablespoons of Cholula rather than the 1/4 cup of hot sauce. It turned out really good!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: It was disappointing. I won’t be making this again. Meh, for my husband and I but glad others enjoyed it.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I made this with only a 1/4 cup of hot sauce, and the heat, while "frisky" was not at all over the limit for me.  What I found overwhelming was the smokiness.  Too much, especially with the plethora of Paprika.  I am tempted to omit the Paprika altogether.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Made this and enjoyed it. Used homemade chicken stock and added dry mustard powder. Used medium shells as my pasta. Delicious!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I used a Trader Joe’s brand hot sauce and it was WAY too hot. I even halved the original amount. Came together perfectly but seriously messed it up with the hot sauce. Would not add next time or just a splash.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Very good recipe. Family loved it both times I made it.  It freezes well. I did leave out the wine.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
Have made this a bunch of times. A family favorite. My advice is simplify. The added steps aren’t necessary. 1 pot recipe. 

I like larger elbows but San Georgio (sp) were too large and not as good as other brands (but the only one left in grocery before the storm). And, I added the whole box since I get tired of recipes that use almost but not quite a box. Added extra liquid etc but not sure I’ll do that again. 

Adds that I always:
Worcestershire sauce
Diced tomatoes
This time:
White beans
Can of low sodium V-8, I keep on hand to soups

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: @Andrea V. and a great side dish too!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Favorite family recipe! Have made several times as is but gluten free. And have also tried with no bacon and ground chicken for a non red meat eater. Not quite the same but still very good.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Probably my fault for using all-natural, organic EVERYTHING, but I felt it had a weird texture. Tasty as all get out, but the sauce was somehow like, sandy?? It is heavy, and I wish I had read others' comments about lowering the amount of hot sauce before I made it, still, I think with a few modifications, this could be great! However, it took over an hour and didn't replace my love for original Hamburger Helper, so... up to you whether you find it worth the effort.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: @Lynn_P you need the emulsifying agent in American cheese or you’ll break your sauce. That’s why it tasted sandy.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: This was a solid B. My husband is avoiding spice so added part Worcestershire sauce with the hot sauce and did not care for the flavor. Did not grow up with hamburger helper so I'm missing out on the nostalgia factor.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: My wife and I gave this 4 1/2 stars (however, we couldn't do a half, so 4 stars), We reserve 5 stars for what we really love. Since we are vegan, I veganized the recipe and it turned out great. I will definitely be making this recipe again.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
Subbed a tube of pork chorizo for bacon
Used EVOO, Frank's Buffalo hot sauce and regular paprika
Added a sweet orange bell pepper, after cooking onion for 15 minutes and cooked both together 10 minutes. Used paper towels to soak up oil. Pasta took way longer than 9 minutes, probably 15 to 18. Then took off heat and waited 30 minutes before adding cheese and chives. delish!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Never posted a comment here before but I wanted to offer some praise and advice for this recipe. I passed on it a couple months ago after reading all the people grousing about its richness/fattiness but made it tonight with a few common sense tweaks that got the entire pot down to around 2000 calories. I used Carbe Diem elbows, 93/7 ground beef, four slices of thick bacon, and skim milk with a dollop of yogurt instead of the heavy cream. Rather than frying the onion in oil, I rendered the bacon first and fried the onions in that fat. I left the rest of

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
For people complaining about the nutrition of the dish. Fat is good for you, your brain is 60% fat. Don't need to shy away. Just use natural fats and your golden. Lard, Butter, and Grass-fed beef is a superfood.

it's the sugar that's killing you, and this dish has none.

So indulge!

Woo!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: These cookies should be called “billion dollar cookies”because they’re SO RICH! These will cure any chocolate craving to the core. Easy too!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Cholula is a great hot sauce to use for more flavor than heat. I omitted bacon cause I didn't have any, used 1 shallot and 1/2 a yellow onion, 3/4 cup sherry instead of white wine. came out great! didn't have any fat to pour off without the bacon. added a half tsp or so (glug) of Worcestershire to compensate for the smokiness I lost. seriously good. also made extra fancy with bronze cut casareccia instead of elbows.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Yummy recipe. Definitely took me back to the days of eating hamburger helper. I pretty luv followed all the steps but cooked the bacon first with the onions, that created much more flavor. I also used chicken and beef stock. Didn’t have any chives but I’m sure that would have made it pop a little more.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Made as directed, with some changes. Instead of oil, I started by cooking the bacon, removing from the pan, and using that fat to sauté the onions & garlic. I also cut back on hot sauce, used tabasco, and tasted as I went. Lastly, I used 1/2 & 1/2 instead of heavy cream. It was very rich and tasty, definitely stick-to-your-bones food! If I make it again, I'll still cook the bacon first, remove from the pan, but will drain, and then sauté the onions & garlic in the oil to reduce the greasiness.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: My picky eaters wouldn't touch this. If you included a splash of white wine and a little hot sauce during the boxed stuff preparation I think it would be very hard to tell which was which in a blind test. I don't think that stuff is great and unfortunately this recipe wasn't any better.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: It was okay.  To be fair, we had to make too many adjustments to make a realistic review -- we don't eat pork, gluten-free, and went lighter on the fatty ingredients.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Decided to give this recipe a try after seeing Sydney making it in The Bear season 4 and salivating like a dog while watching it. As a European, I did not know this recipe beforehand. It is comforting, perfect for the Swiss winter, my partner and I gave it 4 stars and added it to our recipe rotation. I subbed planted bacon and used oven baked tofu in lieu of ground beef. Kept the rest as is but we did run out of hot sauce while making, which to us was disappointing because we love hot dishes . Nevertheless: « délicieux! »

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: The hubster loved it! I added a bag of frozen broccoli & cauliflower florets to add some fiber. Not sure that will compensate for the saturated fat, but... it was Saturday night.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I just made this and followed the recipe word for word. It was delicious. Make this for yourself today!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: The first time I made it as instructed, very fussy, very good. Today, I was in a hurry so I sautéed some bacon with onions, garlic,and some ground beef seasoned with whatever was on hand. I deglazed the pan with wine, poured in broth, gluten free elbows, some leftover veggies (and simmered until the pasta was done. I stirred in some grated mozzarella. So delicious. You absolutely cannot go wrong if you start with this as your base recipe.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
I left out the bacon and added some diced tomato. So I didn't make the recipe as written, and maybe that's why I didn't think much of it. It's nice, and simple enough, and I like it uses one pan. Not sure what the wine added.

Thanks to everyone who warned agaist usig so much hot sauce.

I don't think I would make it again.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: This is really good. Follow the recipe. It's a dead easy two beers. Enjoy.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: this was pretty good but honestly im not sure worth all the work it was to make it

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
I wanted to meal prep this, so I did some changes but it turned out great!! 

I added about 5 cups mixed frozen veg before the onion and drained liquid before adding the beef. 

I also didn't have bacon so I didn't use it, but it turned out great!! the added veg turns it into almost 6-8 servings depending on how hungry you are!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Tastes like something a drunk dad invented while the rest of the family was out of town. I love big bold flavors, and spice, but there is way too much going on in the sauce. Couldn't even taste the beef or pasta, let alone the chives. Cut back on the bacon, halve the hot sauce and paprika (add some fresh or dried chile instead), consider bechamel instead of cream, and maybe add some chard and/or chickpeas for your cardiologist. You could even flip the ratio of meat to pasta.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Also, there is absolutely no need to cook the onions in 1/4 c. of oil. You need no more than a tablespoon, two if you want. And you could just cook the bacon first and sauté the onion in that fat.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: This dish is a trip back in the time machine to family weekday meals around my childhood dinner table.  The twist is a kick of heat and a smoky bacon hint that elevates.  Excellent!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Made EXACTLY (ok, I got precooked bacon because i didn't want cooked bacon stink in my house, and I used cavatapi - which is like elbow mac in a screw) as published. I really liked it. I used Frank's hot sauce & Sargento american cheese slices. l worked well. I enjoy the vinegar nuance of Franks  - while my partner hates vinegar hot sauce - but I was the one cooking...LOL.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
I halved the recipe and it still made about 3-4 servings. 

It's good. A bit of work tho. 

Didn't change anything. But I wish I'd remembered to add a little tomato paste and Worcestershire sauce as someone suggested.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Winner!!!  So good, so tasty, so guilty-pleasure-yyyy.  My kids loved it, I loved it, the dog tried to love it.  I went by the book, but used milk instead of heavy cream.  Otherwise, chefs kiss.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
James Gravely

"I’m just here to read the “I made it exactly as written but turned it into something else entirely” posts."

I'm here for these comments.  Everyone has unique tastebuds and every chef will adjust a recipe to make it their own.  That's what cooking is.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: It’s rich but satisfying!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Made this tonight but I did not have any heavy cream so I substituted cream cheese. That worked perfectly and added a bit of a tang as well.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Is this originally called Slumgullion? …like Mrs. O’Connor made in ‘It Happened on 5th Avenue.’

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
My family loved this. Per my usual, I made a few modifications.

We always use Red lentil pasta. Simply because we prefer the taste.

I also chopped a head of cauliflower and added to the meat mixture. Since the volume was bigger, I added the entire pint of cream. And I used broth paste and water. Finally, Kirkland (Costco) Sweet Mesquite seasoning blend.  Finally, just shredded cheese. With all of the seasoning, the kids didn't mind the cauliflower and it extended the meal by quite a bit.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: good news - if you don’t realize you used maple bacon until after you’ve put it in the pan, it still turns out really tasty

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Love this recipe,  but I'm curious - what does the wine step really add?  To be fair, like everyone else on here, I took the recipe as suggestion and not instructions, but I did do the wine step, I'm just not sure why or what it adds.

author: Cathy Lo
commentBody: We order this at Cruz del Sur every time we eat there - it's divine! - and then binge on making it at home, substituting daikon when I can't get jicama, the best fruity or lemon olive oil you can find (we keep on hand for just such dishes) for the avocado oil, and adding a sprinkling of pomegranate seeds if I have them. Hugo adds delicacies from time to time as well - the tiny lime halves we once found in this salad were a revelation. He keeps the plant right there in the backyard!

author: Cathy Lo
commentBody: Trader Joe’s carries jicama wraps, already thinly sliced.

author: Cathy Lo
commentBody: This is exceptional!! You could top with grilled shrimp and have a complete, delicious summery meal.

author: Cathy Lo
commentBody: Tajin is very popular in Mexico.  Also good on orange slices as a snack!

author: Cathy Lo
commentBody: Wow! This salad is so, so good. I doubled it and served on a big platter for 8 people at a dinner party. What worked for me was composing the layers as directed but saving a few bits of each ingredient, then tucking those bits into the into the leaves on top. I transported it like that (uncovered) to the party, then drizzled with dressing and sprinkled with Tajin just before we ate. I didn’t need to double the dressing; I dressed it well and there’s still a boatload leftover.

author: Cathy Lo
commentBody: This is a good base recipe for a great salad.  I mixed all together vs. plating it individually in parts.  I did not like my jicama so I used a hard pear instead (a green apple would also do well).  I might add some honey to dressing to improve it.  Did not use pineapple, but orange, mango and green tart pear instead.  Has promise.  Goat cheese would be yummy in as well.

author: Cathy Lo
commentBody: Excellent as written, using cilantro, dill and microgreens!  I forgot to put the avocado on top, as noted in the introductory paragraph but not in the ingredients, which would have made it even better.

author: Cathy Lo
commentBody: If you can’t get jicama try water chestnuts

author: Cathy Lo
commentBody: Chile piquín, the idea behind the now ever popular Tajín, is widely used in Mexico also in vegetables and sold by vendors on the streets like cucumbers, carrots and jicama. They are cut into vertical slices, bathed in lime and Tajin. Lots of children also have this in their lunch boxes. Use it also over mango or watermelon! I cannot live without it – I carry a mini one in my bag and been known to give as gift to my farmers market coworkers…Oh and on ramen or consomé, also great.

author: Cathy Lo
commentBody: What a beautiful salad! Clean, fresh, bursting with flavor. Made it largely as written. Used butter lettuce and julienned it, fresh dill and basil for herbs. Great accompaniment to a rich pasta dish.

author: Cathy Lo
commentBody: I couldn’t find ripe pineapple so used mango instead. Delicious.

author: Cathy Lo
commentBody: Made according to the recipe except, I used basil and mint as the fresh herbs. We really liked the mint addition. This is an excellent salad!! Very light and refreshing, but filling. I finished it with Cyprus Black Lava Salt. Goes very well with the fruit!

author: Cathy Lo
commentBody: Sounds wonderful, but I am wondering...couldn't you substitute and nice big avocado for the 1/2 cup of avocado oil in the vinaigrette?

author: Cathy Lo
commentBody: A whole avocado would change the dressing from a vinaigrette to a creamy dressing. Would probably be very tasty but would fundamentally change the recipe. Happy experimenting!

author: Cathy Lo
commentBody: @Bobbi I used a small avocado in the dressing and it was divine!

author: Cathy Lo
commentBody: Any substitution for cucumber?  Husband finds them bitter.

author: Cathy Lo
commentBody: try peeling them first - or getting the thin skinned european ones! Much less bitter!

author: Cathy Lo
commentBody:
In addition to Laura's advice, you can also use a spoon to scrape out the seeds in the middle, then slice into crescent moon shapes.

I love cucumbers but as I've gotten older, my stomach has griped at me when I've eaten them.  These suggestions have greatly helped in that department.

author: Cathy Lo
commentBody: This salad is so good and I make it all the time. Full of flavor and great textures.  I cut the jicama into matchsticks.

author: Cathy Lo
commentBody: Loved it. If it’s in season try adding chopped up mango and fresh corn, even better!

author: Cathy Lo
commentBody: Add fried currants to salad, Miscio, basil, cilantro and mint, did toasted pumpkin and a spiced pumpkin version since I had on hand, nex time adding the sliced avacado on top. Very refreshing

author: Cathy Lo
commentBody: I couldn't get a hold of the peppers so used jalepenos. Worked great. This was a HUGE hit at a party where I doubled the recipe and prepared it as told but on one large wide bowl/platter.

author: Cathy Lo
commentBody: Amazing balance of flavors, this was such a fresh and delicious salad! The balance of acidity was just perfect, and I think a little fresh avocado on top would have been lovely.  You could easily make this an entrée by adding a little protein. We had it as a side with enchiladas, so it was a perfect relief to a heavier meal.  And absolutely beautiful! Will definitely be making this again.

author: Cathy Lo
commentBody: This salad has A LOT going on! Amazingly balanced. 10 /10!!!

author: Cathy Lo
commentBody: This salad was time consuming to make but so worth it.  Super simple salad and full of flavor.  My partner loved this salad so much that we made this salad again the next night. No alterations needed - recipe is perfect as is.

author: Cathy Lo
commentBody: I've been having trouble finding jicama (which I love) in NYC. Any suggestions?

author: Cathy Lo
commentBody: Kohlrabi is a good substitute, but that can also be difficult to find.  I think both have specific seasons and aren't really available outside them.

author: Cathy Lo
commentBody: I think a very crisp, peeled apple could substitute here.

author: Cathy Lo
commentBody:
For Fen:  Try English or hothouse cucumbers (the ones wrapped in plastic). and do the other suggestions that were sent to you:

Hot House cucumbers are classified as burpless which means they have thinner skins, are sweeter, and are believed to be easier to digest

author: Cathy Lo
commentBody: First time, followed recipe exactly- delicious and beautiful, party favorite. Second time added variations- thinly sliced strawberries, shaved mini sweet peppers, substituted  spinach for gem. Added shrimp or salmon. Summer perfection!

author: Cathy Lo
commentBody: I used daikon instead of jicama. Really well received by a crowd.

author: Cathy Lo
commentBody: What a great mix of flavors!  It has it all - sweet, salty, heat, onion, crunch, herbs.  This was my first time using tajin & followed the recipe as written.  The salt in the dressing is over the top for my palate. I expect it will be plenty salty from the tajin alone. I look forward to making this again. And soon!

author: Cathy Lo
commentBody: Is it better to use roasted/salted seeds or raw seeds?

author: Cathy Lo
commentBody: @Kitty Maldonado- I used toasted/salted because it was what my store had available and it was very good. I was cautious about adding salt elsewhere but it wasn’t an issue for us.

author: Cathy Lo
commentBody: Our guests, including a 4 year old, loved this and asked for more. We ruined the dressing, maybe by using the wrong kind of salt? It was too salty to bother trying to repair, so we used oil and vinegar, which worked just fine. Took forever to prep without a mandolin, but it was a very big hit.

author: Cathy Lo
commentBody: Really good addition to any Mexican themed meal- light and tropical

author: Cathy Lo
commentBody: Like many others I opted to sub mango for pineapple and added avocado and pomegranate. I was pleased to find that the leftovers (although not as pretty) were delicious two days later, and would in fact recommend preparing the dressing in advance to let the flavors meld. I also liked the texture and flavor of the semi-pickled onions in my leftovers, so I’d dress the onions ahead of time and add before serving. I would also like to add shrimp to make this a luncheon salad.

author: Cathy Lo
commentBody: This was a hit - people loved it. Made as written and presented on a tray.

author: Cathy Lo
commentBody: This was delicious: bright and fresh with lots of contrasting flavours of sweet, savoury and spicy. BUT it was incredibly time-consuming to make, about two hours. Loads of prep and chopping.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: How would this recipe be different if I use whole grain brown rice?

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I have used double the amount of water that you have of rice for whole grain 'brown' jasmine & basmati all my life. 1/2 & 1 cup or 2 cups & 4 cups always double amount of rice used. Though I prefer steamed on stove top the water content is likely correct. Whole grain, I think, in Micro would takes somewhat longer than white I believe just as it does on stovetop.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: For fluffy rice, my Larousse cookbook from several decades ago said to cook rice like pasta, i.e., add to boiling salted water, cook until done, drain, add butter & serve.  The rice never burns, you don’t need to time it. it cooks in much less time than regular stove top methods and you can add vegetables like broccoli and asparagus to the boiling water for a quick side dish.  Faster than using this microwave method.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I tried it with brown rice. Increase water to three cups for 1 cup of rice and increase cooking time to 30 minutes. Don’t know the wattage for my microwave so others may need to make different adjustments.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Be very careful with timing if you halve or quarter this recipe. I cut it down and ended up with burned rice and the smoke alarm talking to me about it!:))

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
No you can't use the same timing to make less or more rice.

No you can't use the same recipe to make brown rice, short grain rice, etc.

This is a recipe for ONE CUP OF LONG GRAIN WHITE RICE.  It will not work without alterations for anything else. Other grains and quantities might require different timing or water ratios or microwave power levels. Either you are going to have to figure them out on your own or find another recipe but YOU CAN'T USE THIS RECIPE FOR ANYTHING ELSE, so stop asking.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: If you constantly need to check microwave every 1-2 minutes after the initial 15 minutes to check when it's done, it seems like a lot more work than the stove top method which is pretty darn easy (bring rice to boil, turn heat down to simmer for 15 minutes, turn off stove top, wait 5 minutes).

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: @Rob Pgh correct, how this is possibly better or easier than the stovetop method is a mystery.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
@Rob Pgh

I think you just do this once to set the recipe to your specific microwave. After that, just set it for the full time you needed right away…

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I am also curious about using long grain brown rice.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I confess that I find cooking rice on the stovetop to be foolproof. And it gives me the chance—if I want—for some crusty grains on the bottom. And I don’t rinse the rice, even for arroz con pollo.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I made this tonight and it was perfect. I added a tsp of salt and a tsp of butter and cooked it in a medium soufflé bowl. I just served it with no extra pots to clean! Wow!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I'm also curious about adapting this for brown rice

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Microwave rice is a lifesaver when the heat index is 112, my air conditioning is already working overtime, and I can't bear to turn on the gas stove or oven for as long as it takes to cook rice.  For those asking about brown rice, just ask our friend, Google.  Those instructions already exist.  :)

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: What utter nonsense.  I tried to cook a small portion of rice in my ge microwave.  I set it for full power and 15 minutes as in your article but got called away.  At some point there was an explosion rupturing the glass bowl, burning the rice  and spewing burnt rice all over the interior of the microwave! Pfui!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: You undoubtedly adjusted the rice and water amounts for a smaller portion, but should also have adjusted the cooking time for a smaller portion. It doesn't take as long for a small amount to heat to boiling and cook. If it boils dry, it might cause the result you got.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
I have cooked rice in the microwave for about 30 years now just like this. I'm delighted to see microwaves getting more use. It is so much faster and it's nearly impossible to scorch the pan or the rice. Microwaves are also more energy efficient. You can even use the cooking pot  as the serving dish.

Thank you for this recipe and spreading the word and the technique.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Will definitely try this method - but if I double this, is the rice-water ratio the same? And how does it affect the cook time?

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: The article containing the recipe said to just double the water you would normally use for the stovetop method.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: It did NOT say to double the water you’d use for the stovetop method. It said to use twice as much water as rice:  1 cup rice to 2 cups water.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: That is probably a safe recommendation for basmati, but "sushi" and other kinds of rice require different ratios.  The basmati will require less water in the pot if it has soaked for long.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: For short grain white rice like Nishiki or Kokuo Rose, it only takes 15 minutes stovetop...but interested in doing microwave brown.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Can someone explain to me why all this rinsing of rice? Is it just to reduce “stickiness”? If so what’s so bad about stickiness? All sorts of things are good sticky. Some not even food!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Also helpful for removing the arsenic, which can be high in rice grown in contaminated groundwater. Here's one study: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/16876928/

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: @Kevin, thanks for sharing the NIH article. Gee, apparently only 28% of arsenic is taken away by washing rice before cooking.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: @Kevin Yes, which is why I cook my rice (usually brown) like pasta, in a large pot of boiling water. When it’s done to your taste, just drain it.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Simple and fast - rice turned out great.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I preheat the water for a few minutes in the microwave, add in and stir the rice, cover with plastic wrap, microwave at 50% power for 15 minutes. Fluff with a fork. Perfect every time.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I guess, I don’t really have a problem with making rice in a microwave, I think I just take issue with the implication that using a stove top is so onerous. After rinsing the rice and bringing it to a boil, it takes 10 minutes on simmer to cook it. Yes, you have to babysit the rice while the water comes to a boil, but we’re talking 5 minutes top. Also, in terms of cleaning, just add water to the pot and let it soak for a few minutes and it’s done.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: My cooking technique differs for white jasmine rice. In a pyrex glass bowl, I add 1/2 tsp. salt and 1 TB. of olive oil, cover with the glass top, program to cook on high for 5 minutes (to boil) and 14 minutes on low. Works in my microwave.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Tonight I made this exactly as written, and I came out with a solid block of overcooked rice.  No bueno.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Basmati rice was gorgeous! Don't think I ever ran my microwave for this long, so that was the only strange thing about this recipe. 15 minutes and voila. Next time, will try to add some masala to jazz it up. Rather than conical, I did what another commenter did and used a porcelain soufflé dish. Makes sense to use a flat dish. Another commenter disputed cutting recipe and wrote that it burned. Maybe smaller dish and less time .. or maybe just a smaller dish would resolve?

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: After many years using a gas range with no complaints, I recently started using an electric cooker and I am in LOVE with it!!  Perfect rice every time.  I almost always eat brown rice and I would be a little bit scared to keep the microwave going for 50+ minutes.  Not sure why, but the thought makes me very wary.  Adopt this method if you like, but I see no need.  As they say these days, "You do you, boo!!"

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Color me amazed. This worked perfectly for me! I have a low-wattage microwave, so I followed the instructions exactly (used Jasmine rice, added 1Tbs butter and a pinch of ras al hanout) and at 20 minutes, it was perfectly cooked. I was able to make it in the morning while getting kids ready for school, etc., and then pack it for my own lunch. I thought my rice cooker was the bomb, but this will be my new go-to for fast dinner side dish.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Can't get enough of this. Mixed in some slightly sweated grated ginger and garlic (as well as its oil) before putting rice into the microwave, perfect ginger-garlic rice.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
This recipe changed my life!  10 minutes before rice is done, I put salmon filet(s) on top of the rice and continue cooking per instructions.  Both come out sublime.  I top the salmon with shredded cabbage, maybe a tiny sprinkle of white balsamic or rice vinegar and shredded packaged cabbage.  Sprinkle with sesame seeds if you have them .So proud of myself.
Made brown rice with this method using 3 cups water for 30 minutes.  Perfection.  Will try this with black pearl rice next.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
You don’t have to keep checking every 1-2 minutes. Only if your rice is mushy after the recipe’s timing.
Everyone, please read the recipe a few times before criticizing how it doesn’t work
Smile we need all patience available in this world.  If you don’t have any just sit and breathe deeply

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
Rice cooks wonderfully in an Instant Pot or other pressure cooker. (Rice Cookers are pressure cookers, too.) Even old-fashioned stove top pressure cookers, though you need to keep your eye on them and use a bit more water.

1 cup white rice
1 to 1.25 cups water 
3 - 4 minutes at high pressure
3 for Basmati

1 cup brown rice
1 to 1.33 cups water
15 minutes 

These guidelines scale, since the IP needs only a half cup of water to cook. To scale up, just be aware of the Max Fill line.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: This works well, but then you have to clean everything up. BTW, my rice cooker is not a pressure cooker, but a steamer. I much prefer the microwave method with almost no clean up time afterward.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
I think there is confusion in that the newspaper article said to use twice as much water as called for in the original recipe.  Whereas the Microwave Rice recipe provided simply says use two cups of water for one cup of rice. Perhaps the author would speak to whether to use twice the original recipe”s water, or use twice as much water as rice.
And I also want to know about brown and sushi rice please. Thank you for this useful article and recipe.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I made a half batch in 10 minutes in a small microwave.  The rice came out a fluffy and perfect!  This is a great option for my office where I do not have access to a stovetop.  Pair with quality canned tuna or a few vegetables from the corner market.  Easy lunch!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I’ve used the microwave to make weeknite risotto for decades.  And indeed the hands off, perfect every time quality make at extra wonderful!  I’ve thrown peeled shrimp on top at the last minute for a seafood version, I’m going to try Mr Pang’s chicken idea for a one-dish meal.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Has anyone tried this with brown rice?

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: This does not seem to me like an improvement on the stove method. Both require the same amount of potchkying before cooking and neither involves stirring. Rice cooking away on the stove provides a sense of jokiness that the microwave doesn’t. What’s the benefit?

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: My microwave was not happy to have boiling water for such a long time. It shut itself off.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
Used this technique for years. Works great. I find brown rice takes about 2x as long as white. 
PS - also a revelation: polenta in the microwave - cheap, easy, delicious! Google it.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
brown rice-
Used 3 cups of water instead of 2, and it took more time. Worked.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I have used this method for years for brown rice. I always start with 1 cup of rice to 3 cups of boiling water and cook it for 25minutes . Check it for doneness and  add more water and time if needed. For short grain organic brown rice, I always need to do that. I also use this method for rice pilaf or risotto (always with brown rice). I first sauté a chopped onion in olive oil with  a pinch of thyme and some crushed garlic; then I add the brown rice ( not rinsed) to cook its starchy coating, th

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: All the rice varieties should have microwave instructions on them.  I’ve cooked my rice in the microwave for years.  I have a microwaveable bowl with a covered I got from a at-home kitchen products company (not sure if I should publish a brand name).  It’s perfect!  There’s an inner lid that has a hole in the center to vent & a top lid fastens on with a “lock” on both sides.  My rice cooks perfectly every time.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Water is expensive in my city. My Chinese neighbor taught me to use the preliminary rice-rinsing water to water plants, indoor or out.  She maintains it's good for the plants.  Seems to work!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Woudl this work for sushi rice?

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I have used this method for years for brown rice. I start with 1 cup of rice to 3 cups of boiling water for 25 min. Then I check and add more water and time if needed. I even use this method for brown rice pilaf or risotto: first sauté a chopped onion in  olive oil with a pinch of thyme and some chopped garlic; Add  rice to it ( not rinsed ) and stir a few minutes to cook the starch. then add boiling chicken stock and a little white wine. Transfer to a microwavable dish and cook 25  min or so.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I bought a microwave rice pot that came with instructions for all types of rice. It has been fantastic and goes into the dishwasher for cleaning.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: My rice cooker only takes up counter space when in use. I use it for more than just making rice. I use it for cooking many other grains, steel cut oats, quinoa. It's brilliant for making yogurt and functions like a slow cooker. I wouldn't trade making rice in a rice cooker for making rice in the microwave.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I was confused, because rice cookers have been around for years now and make perfect rice without even that last bit of checking for doneness in this recipe. Maybe this is a emergency recipe for if your rice cooker breaks down?

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Yes please share tips for brown rice. Anyone?

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: What’s the rice/water ratio and timing for brown rice?

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Brown rice can be very variable. I succeeded with my current supply of brown rice by increasing water to 2 and 3/4 cups per cup of rinsed brown rice, then cooking at 80% power for 20 minutes in my 1200 watt microwave, using a vented lid on the microwave pot to keep water from boiling off too fast. Then resting covered for 10 minutes. The result was acceptably tender brown rice with no crunch. Next time I will soak the brown rice in tepid water gor 20-30 minutes before rinsing.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: This method takes 5 -10 minutes longer than steaming on the stovetop, which also requires no monitoring and only one stir is allowed.  No method for brown rice, either, which takes 40-45 minutes on the stove, so possibly an hour in the microwave.  I admit I haven't tried this recipe - no microwave...

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Echoing comments by Nick and Laurie:  Does this method work for brown rice? Would I use the same recipe as for stove-top and rice-cooker brown rice?

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I thought I would be clever and make this for a single generous serving using a third cup rice and twice the water.  Don't know what went wrong but it burned after 12 minutes and was a solid cake of rice nothingness.  I tried it again, smaller amount of rice, a pat of butter and more water.  And nuked it for about 6-7 minutes.  It turned out great! So, lesson is: for smaller servings, less time.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Do all this in a pressure cooker and it takes 7-8 minutes.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I was stunned that this really is a good way to cook rice. I wanted to test the method as well as have some rice for today's stir fry.  I found it as advertised; will I use the method all the time no, but in a pinch, I shall pop the bowl into the microwave.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I usually cook mine in the oven after toasting it briefly with melted butter on the stove top. 20 min in covered le Creuset pot at 350°. Sort of takes care of itself.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Thank you Lionlady!  I read the article in the Times about this recipe and that wording just says "... double the amount of water."  Both my wife and I assumed that since it cooked in the microwave without a lid that this meant to use twice the amount of water as usual.  Not so.  Usual water to rice ratio and you are good to go.  Wish I read this before making my first batch!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
Microwave:
1c Rinsed rice
2c Water
Cover and zap for half an hour at half power.
Fluff with a fork when it's up and cover back up.
Eat whenever, it'll be perfect.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Tried this as written and had a lovely layer of rice on top and bottom  with no leftover water - and a solid, rock hard puck of uncooked rice in the middle that the nice rice was stuck to. Added another cup of water and cooked more, and a bit more rice was edible, but all in all, it did not work for me.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Is using a Instapot easier?

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I have an 1800W microwave.  I used 1/2 a cup and cooked on high for 6 minutes.  The rice was ok but nothing close to my rice Zojirushi rice cooker which takes an hour to cook white rice.  Great in a pinch, though.  Next time, I'll try cooking on a lower power so the rice has more time to soak up the water.  Might use a little more water, too.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I adapted this from a recipe for barley pilaff in the the Silver Palette New Basics cookbook. Two cups of water and one cup of washed rice. I use Trader Joe's brown basmati rice from India (the TJ's brown jasmine rice from Thailand has somewhat more arsenic). I use a glass Pyrex covered dish. Cook for 5 minutes on high and 25 minutes on medium. There is sometimes a small amount of boiling over when cooking with the cover on for the whole time. The wattage of my microwave is 800.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: How did I never think to do this? 15 minutes and it’s perfectly cooked and fluffy. I usually use an instant pot, which I then have to clean. I’m convinced. My new favorite way to make weeknight rice.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
I don’t know that I will try this again. I had basmati rice but only about 3/4 c. I rinsed it. I added about 1.625c water from the tap so it was cold (who has room temp water just hanging out?) I put it in the 950w m-wave for 20 minutes. At about 19:45 seconds the machine noise changed. I think it was too hot. The rice was over cooked. Stiff and rubbery. There was so much moisture I couldn’t see what was going on. 

I’ve never scorched rice on the stove. At least my m-wave is cleaner now.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: easy hack. this way you don't have to babysit the stove and is a useful tool for making healthy dinner. Thanks NYT!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Get an Instant Pot! Rinse, and add water 1-1.  Timing depends on the kind of rice *only*. There is no judgment or monitoring required. The results are identical every single time. Clean-up is the same as for the microwave method: one bowl.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I cooked rice perfectly on gas ranges for most of my life, then moved, and started using an electric range.  I found it extremely difficult to try to make a lot of rice based dishes on an electric ranges. Utter failures most of the time, until I bought a Zojirushi rice cooker and never looked back. Easy perfect rice every time.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Same problem as Richard. Tried half the amounts. Pyrex bowl exploded while I was sautéing scallops to go with the rice.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
"It is so much faster..."

2 cups jasmine rice takes 25 - 30 minutes in my rice cooker that I do not have to babysit at all.

This method is not significantly faster, and requires your attention.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Tried this in my microwave at 15 minutes.  15 minutes later I had a dried and hard hockey puck of rice, ready for my trash bin.  No good at all.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Check out the Sistema microwave rice cooker (about $15 on amazon). Speeds things up by using steam. Very even results, especially helpful for brown rice that takes forever.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: A friend from Poland taught me how the farmers made rice back in the day. I start in the morning, before heading out to work. After I’ve boiled the water and added the rice, and returned it to a boil, I take it off the stove and wrap the pot in a blanket or two. The rice slow cooks all day. I come home to it and whip up whatever goes with it. No wait. Best of all is the sensation that someone is home in the kitchen all day preparing my supper.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Rice needs to be rinsed because of ate arsenic content it gets from the soil. Always rinse and drain it.  Google it.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Well there is an easier way to make microwave rice. Use twice as much water as rice, then hit the ‘rice’ button on your microwave. Works every time.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Who’s burning rice on their stovetop? Less than a teaspoon of oil in the water prevents sticking to the bottom of the pot. While I like microwaves, this recipe isn’t a timesaver nor does it improve the flavor of the rice.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: This method takes as much time and fuel (or more) than simply boiling it on the stove. Just put the pot in the dishwasher.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Game changer for quick weekday meals.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Brown rice 18 min

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
I have made this several times (each time with 1/2 cup of Basmati or Jasmine rice). I use the same amount of liquid that I would use for cooking that amount of rice stove top.
I am about to make 1/2 cup of brown rice in the microwave. I will report back.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
Using a 1300 watt microwave, I cooked 1 cup brown texmati rice for 25 minutes & let it sit in the microwave for 5 minutes. The rice turned out fine. I live a few hundred feet about sea level.
25 minutes is shorter than the 45 minute stove top cooking time.
The parboiled rice you can nuke takes 1.5 minutes for 1 cup. But, you create more plastic waste per cup of rice in the process.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I just bought a cheap microwave rice cooker (about $10) that came with recipes/times for all kinds of rice. It works well and has the same recipe as here for long-grain white rice.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: For a while, I was buying "Asia's Best" Jasmine rice. The package had instructions for both microwave and stovetop prep. I had never heard of cooking rice in a microwave, so I tried it. As I recall, I cooked 1 cup of rice with the amount of water specified, uncovered in a shallow 1-qt French soufflé dish. I  wasn't expecting much but it turned out PERFECT.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Cooking rice in the microwave makes pulling dinner together so much easier. I never worry about it scorching, having to stir it, etc. Magic!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
This is much better…
Prep your (white) rice by rinsing, 
Add 1 part rice and 2 parts water to microwave safe casserole dish that has a lid,
Give the rice a quick stir (so it’s not compacted),
Microwave on high, lid on, for 4-6 minutes (goal is to get water boiling)- you’ll need to modify based on amount of rice and wattage. 
Then, microwave at 40% power for 13-15 minutes, let rest 5 minutes and it’s ready. Keep the lid on throughout-

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Just moved house. The gas hasn’t been turned on. Felt like eating rice but didn’t want to order a takeaway. Followed the instructions, added butter, checked at 10minutes, again 10minutes later and eureka, 25minutes later, I had perfect rice. Wow!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: A cup of rice, two cups of water, butter and salt. Cover. Fur minutes on high, 13 minutes at 50%. Perfect every time

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
Third time on this recipe...cannot make it work.  Each time reducing the cooking time in a 1000 watt microwave.  Far_too_dry, even at 16 min.  Will try once more, reducing the "microwave standing time" to half the recommended 5 min.

NYT is my go-to for recipes.  If anyone has suggestions, these are welcome.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Works perfectly every time.  Useful for me as I have a tiny kitchen and no room for a rice cooker.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
I CANNOT believe this that cooking columns, shows, etc. are still devoting time and space with tips on how to cook rice. Folks, there is this amazing device known as a rice cooker. I got got my first one, a Panasonic (that’s how long ago it was) when I lived in Japan. It still works (love Japanese quality!) but now I’ve upgraded to a Zojirushi. 
Any rice cooker eliminates guesswork and the rice is perfect every time. Some rice cookers keep rice warm for a considerable amount of time.
There are other foods to fuss over…. Rice needn’t be one of them.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Some small kitchens would be better without an addition just for rice.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Thanks for the new method. We have cooked rice like pasta for years (plus I am not a fan of single-purpose kitchen gadgets). Start with plenty of water (a couple of quarts at least) and bring it to a boil. Put in your rice and boil away. It doesn't have to be covered. Cook for 10 min for most white rice and 20 for brown. Once the time is up, strain it (we use a wire mesh strainer). That's it - ready to eat. It's always fluffy and tender. This doesn't work if you want sticky rice.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Honestly, stovetop rice is already so simple and foolproof once you nail it. I fear that the 15 minutes of steamy air inside my microwave will cause problems over time.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
We have been using this techniques for over 30 years. Our way is 1 cup rice, 2 cups water, 12 minutes in microwave. It should look a little wet. Fluff it with a fork and cover it with a lid and leave it alone for 5 minutes for the rice to absorb the rest of the moisture.

The rice - water ratio depends on the quality of rice. We always use basmati.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
We use basmati, rinse and use double the water just as described but:
1. Let the rice soak in two cups of water in a bit oversized bowl for half an hour to one hour
2. If in a hurry, soak in two cups of a lukewarm water for 10-15 minutes.
3. Rice should look bright and solid white.
4. Then start microwaving it uncovered for nine minutes
5. At the end of nine, cover it and do another minute
6. Let it rest covered for five minutes
7. Gently stir it to make it fluffy.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
Dave 
My Indian friends soak their rice for about 20 minutes before cooking, which hydrates it and produces a fluffier rice. Has anyone else heard of this?

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Why rinse the rice?

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: The "flour" resulting from milling of the rice will combine with water and make the rice sticky.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Rinsing rice has been mentioned in this paper & elseware as importent for one's better health. Also removes the superficial starch that makes rice stick together in clumps.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Wondering if any modifications needed for short-grain rice?  This sounds wonderful and faster than my rice cooker!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Will this work for short grain rice?

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Use Anyday Cookware for making rice in the microwave. 25 minutes on power 3. Best rice ever, every time!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I always cook my rice like this, it is the best way to cook rice!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Please explain what to expect with brown rice. I assume it will work but just take more time. I am not supposed to eat white rice.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: How important is starting with "room-temperature" water? If you don't have time to let the water stand around to come up to temperature, how long should the water be heated in the microwave?

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: It would be good to have directions about cooking other types of rice in the microwave. A follow up article?

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I have cooked rice for years in the microwave using a corning glass dish with the cover on, no condensation on the interior of the microwave and still comes out great! Also add a bit of salt and a knob of butter.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Do you need to adjust the cooking time in the microwave for less or more than 1 cup of rice?

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: This method here DOES require babysitting. Here's another: Do Step I.  My Step II: To 1 cup of basmati rice, add 1 3/4 cups of cooking liquid in a saucepan. Bring to boil. Lower heat and cook for 5 minutes. DON'T PEEK! Then, let it sit, COVERED for 15 minutes (and go about your business). Finally, fluff the rice with your fork. Bingo!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: How is this different than cooking it on the stovetop for twenty minutes?

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I cook mine in the oven, easy and just as good, only 25 minutes.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: It takes me a few minutes less time to cook white rice on the stovetop. Besides not having to swish out the pan, this doesn't seem like much of a time saver!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Is the 1:2 ratio consistent for all rice, or should I follow the package directions if a short-grain rice calls for a 2:3 ratio?

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: This works well. For brown rice, first cook uncovered at 100% power for 10 minutes, then cover it and cook at 50% power for 20 minutes.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Long time stove top loyalist here, but I'm tempted to try this method. Do you need to soak the rice at all before microwaving? Basmati typically requires soaking.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: There's no need to waste 15 gallons of water to rinse rice if you just soak it for an hour or so then give it a quick rinse.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I don't normally rinse my rice, especially long grain. Is there a reason behind rinsing when cooking in microwave?

author: Priya Krishna
