commentBody: Two thoughts: I have to use sometimes as much as 3:1 water to rice ratio in a microwave. Especially if it’s uncovered. And why do you recommend rinsing rice? What is the benefit and is it necessary? Thanks

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: just made this, so easy perfect rice, TY!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Brown rice?  One way to find out, just be prepared to throw out one or two batches.  You probably won't have to, maybe just use more water.  Brown rice is resilient.  On the stovetop, I cook it like pasta, using lots of water, cook it (25 min), drain it and let it steep (10 min), covered.  It's always perfect.  I may need to do the experiment in the microwave, just to see what happens, but I don't think it will buy anything.  I only use white rice for risotto now.  Brown rice is so much easier.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I don't usually rinse my basmati rice before cooking, is that a critical portion of this working or can I go ahead without the rinse?

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I have been cooking rice in a microwave for years. I actually add boiling water to the rice, 1:2 and let it cook for 13 mins - it is fluffy and perfect every time.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: My foolproof method: mix rice with lots of water. (no need to wash rice) Bring to a boil and simmer until just tender. Drain rice and replace in pan, covered, off heat and allow rice to steam for 15 minutes. Boiling time for white rice is about 15 minutes. For brown rice 28 minutes.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Do you cover rice when putting it in the microwave

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I always cook rice, white or brown, in the microwave using the following method: use the ratio of rice and water recommended on the bag, add salt and a little butter or oil if you want. Start by boiling water in a tea kettle, measure your rice, boiling water and seasonings into a covered microwave-safe container. Microwave at 30% for the amount of time which the rice bag recommends that the rice be simmered. E.g. for brown rice perhaps 45 minutes, and for white rice perhaps 20 minutes.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Check out Jamie Oliver’s method for fast cooking rice on the stove top. You can cook rice on the stove top in 8 to 10 minutes, once water is boiling. And… timing is the SAME for any amount of rice. 2 servings or 40 servings. The rice is fluffy and perfect every time.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I tried it with a quarter cup of rice and a half cup of water. After 5 minutes, it needed more water. But when I reset for another 2 minutes,  it made my microwave (only 2 yrs old, 900 watt Sharp) microwave shut down. I’d go cautiously with this recipe. It saves no time. I suggest rinsing rice, boiling on the stove top for 5 minutes in double the volume of water, draining, and then steaming the rice for 5-10 more minutes. Perfect every time.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I would also like to sub brown rice.  Would you provide directions for both brown basmati and long grain brown (if there is a difference)?

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Maybe I am lucky, but I have never had a problem with stove top plain rice.  Could be because I am using a pot with heavy cast-iron bottom in stainless steel on an electric stove.  Also Have never rinsed the rice.   So I tried, following exactly,  this and it worked just as good as stove top, and took about the same time.  Still don't know why the rinse.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
Burned rice.  Big stink.  Moderate damage to Pyrex bowl.  
Any other fancy ideas?

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Followed amount of rice and water. Rice was stuck to the bottom of the dish because needed more water. Added another cup of water (3 cups total) and cooked for another 10 minutes. I think these proportions should be ok.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Followed recipe 15 min at 1250 watts. Serviceable for a busy night but would not serve it to anyone.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I found that while the grains were quite individual, the rice was not fluffy and lacked that rich texture. Perhaps I needed more water?  It a fan.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Would this technique work for Indian style rice pilau where you saute (in another pan obviously) some ingredients like onion and then add spices like turmeric, cinnamon etc.  to the rice. Mix in with rice and then microwave? Would stock be okay?

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Please tell me how to adapt this method and measurements  for brown, black pearl and wild rice.   Many thanks

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Why is it necessary to rinse rice.  I have been cooking perfect unrinsed rice on the stove top for years, all varieties.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
Rice takes up arsenic more than any other grain. I like to wash (rinse) the rice thoroughly, changing out the water several times.

Almost always, I boil rice in lots of water until it is partially done, then sieve, rinse, and steam the rice until it is cooked completely.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: If I want to make half as much rice, does it take less time? Or should I stick to the 1:2 rice:water ratio and keep the cooking time at 15 minutes?

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: My microwave has variable power settings so I always put the rice and water into a covered dish, microwave at full power for 3 minutes and then at power level 2 or 3 for 12 minutes. Leave it covered for a few more minutes and then fluff with a fork. It always turns out perfectly fluffy and done.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Wow! It really works!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I use 90-second packaged microwave rice that can be found in many grocery stores, supermarkets, etc. There are a plethora of options to choose from, the rice comes out perfectly, & best of all, no threat of exploding glassware! And cleanup is as simple as throwing away the bag the rice came in!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I always use 1/8 of a cup less water than the 2 to 1 ratio when cooking rice. I did so with this recipe in my 900 watt microwave. Cooked the rice for 20 min on high. it was perfect.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
I just tried this with Jasmine rice in an 1000 watt microwave, it turned out awful.  I used a Pyrex round container with one cup of rice and two cups of water, 20 minutes.  The rice stuck to the glass.  It was hard, undercooked, and very dried out.  So I added two spoonful of water, stirred it around, another 5 minutes in the microwave, still didn't make a difference.

All I can say is that I wasted a cup of good rice, and kitchen smelt like something had burnt.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I've made this twice now. First time with too much water, thanks I say to wording in the NYT article, and the second time with supposedly the right amount of water.  Neither time worked out well.  I think the key is to pick a power setting on your microwave so that the rice cooks in about 20 - 25 minutes without boiling the water dry first.  My microwave is about 1200 watts and boils it dry in 15 minutes well before the rice is cooked.  So I presume I need to set it to about 60% power.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: This method did not work for me. My daughter and I had great expectations. The rice was dry and tasteless. Help! I want ti love this.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: How much water? all the recipe says is rice

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Read the recipe!  Step 2 says how much water!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Been doing this for a couple of decades. The only difference is I split the time in half. The second half is cooked at 50% power. It can be done !

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I’ve made basmati, jasmine and other rices in the microwave for a few years and it is consistently good. I’d never go back to stove top. But I use less water than this recipe (I use 1.5 cups of water to 2 cups of rice).

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I only eat brown rice because it is healthier. Cooking it this way is more complicated than on the stove top, and takes longer.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I cooked white rice for 15 minutes in my microwave and it came out perfectly cooked. Last night I tried to cook brown jasmine rice in my microwave. After 15 minutes it wasn't cooked through. I had to add water and cook it another 10 minutes, and then, more water and 5 minutes. Finally, I gave up because I needed the rice to go with the rest of the dinner.  It was never cooked entirely.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Would this work with brown rice, too?

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I tried this with basmati. After only 15 minutes, the water was absorbed, but the rice was a little more al dente than we like. I added another quarter cup of water and cooked 2 minutes more. I am going to try again with the proportions below and then experiment some. The rice was very good and the clean up was easy. I added salt at the beginning.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Wittie has excellent advice…cooking rice using the pasta method. This is the only way I cook rice, white or brown. Bring to a boil 10 cups water, add 1 cup rice, lower heat to maintain boil, cook until done, then drain. This has the added benefit of reducing the arsenic content of the rice. Yes, you read that correctly. Don’t worry, it is a very small amount and not something to stress about. Let’s face it, much of the world eats a lot of rice. But I figure if I can reduce it, all the better.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Rice is very easy to cook on the stove and doesn't require constant attention. Just put one measure of white rice (any variety) in a small pot with two measures of water (e.g., 1 cup rice, 2 cups water) plus a little salt. Bring to boil, then reduce heat to simmer, cook 15 minutes; turn off heat and allow the rice to sit for another 15 minutes. Fluff and serve.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: If your Microwave oven has a "Rice" setting, use it. Every Microwave oven we've owned had a "Rice" setting and it comes out perfect every time. BTW, we only eat Basmati.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: As a Californian, I can’t in good conscience run the water through a sieve of rice for a few minutes until the water runs clear. I’ve stopped rinsing rice, and I’m here to tell the tale.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I have had a microwave rice cooker for many years.  I put the cover on, cook on high for 4 minutes, cook on low for 15 minutes.  Leave in the microwave for a couple of minutes.  Stir and viola.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Ha, about 5 years ago my oven/stove broke and I searched for ways to make rice in the microwave but somehow did not come up with a good result. Thanks for sharing this. For everyone who had problems or other questions about this technique, this is a good start to give info for our own experiments. Right?

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: For the people asking about brown rice, I find the instant pot to be perfect. if you google, you'll see where people have tested different water/rice ratios and timing to get it just right.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: For a long time, we have been cooking rice like pasta and we will never stop. No need to watch it per se or for the rice to absorb the water. Unless you want sticky rice, this is foolproof: Put a "large" amount (enough to fully engulf the amount of rice you want to cook) of water in a pot and bring it too a boil. Pour in rice and continue to boil for 10 min, maybe with a stir or two (20 min for brown rice). Strain through a colander (best if it's a wire mesh type). That's it. Perfect every time.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
I cook "Brazilian" syle rice (fluffy, speparate grains) in the microwave after i left Brazil and no longer had a cook. 
Brazilians usually soften a little onion or garlic in oil, then add the dry rice (basmati is best), then add the rice and stir it till the grains are coated with oil, add the water and simmer till done.For the microwave, one cup of dry rice, i do the same in microwave. After adding water i put in on high for 5 min. (level 10) then medium (level 5) for 9 min. Fluff and serve!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: @David C. Morris, who never rinses his rice. I hope you know your rice grower and processor well. It's my understanding that a lot of noxious pesticides are used to grow rice, which could be one reason so many rice packages caution consumers to rinse the product thoroughly before cooking.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I have found that my Thermomix cooks rice perfectly.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I use a 2.5 quart Staub cast iron ‘pot’. I usually cook 1 cup of white rice with 1.25 cup water. I do rinse rice in metal strainer. I usually add some butteIr to the pot, heat, then stir in rice. After a bit, I add the water.  Bring to a boil, place the lid and immediately turn burner to lowest setting & set timer 14 minutes. After 14 minutes, turn off heat & set timer another 14 mins.  Remove lid & fluff. Perfect rice ALWAYS.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I have cooked this twice with excellent results. No more stove top, sticky clean up mess in the pan for me. I used jasmine rice and rinsed the rice several times until the rinse water was clear.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I’ve been making basmati rice in the microwave for ages.  You really have to experiment with the time and water.  Start with 8 minutes and keep checking, adding a minute or 2 at a time.  Also, you need a large enough bowl so that the water/rice doesn’t spill over into the microwave.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: the 'stickiness' of rice is a preference.  the longer the rice, the more aged the rice, the less stickier it will be - especially if rinsed before cooking.  basmati will not be sticky, in my experience, even if not rinsed.  rinsing also removes any small bits of extranneous vegetation that sometime accompany rice.  if you want sticky rice, i have no words of wisdom for you.  if you want rice where each grain is distinct, then this method looks like it might be for you - and me.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
First rule of cooking (or heating) anything in a microwave is to NEVER walk away and leave it unattended. If you need to leave the microwave, STOP THE COOK -> hit cancel. 

If you've had a problem with how the rice comes out when following the directions, make sure you're using long grain rice - you can't swap different types of rice for this one recipe as it is specific. Two cups of water may be too much for the brand of rice you're using. Check the package for the amount to use.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Try it without all this rinsing and see if you can tell the difference. What a colossal waste of water!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
Brown rice was the Tip at bottom of recipe
Tip
 You also can use this technique for brown rice. Follow the instructions above, using 2½ cups water for 1 cup long-grain brown rice and cook for 20 to 30 minutes.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I use 1.5 cups water to 1 cup rice (slightly less if rinsing first) in a 4-cup Pyrex measuring cup; microwave on high until boiling (3:15 or so in my microwave) then stir & cover (I use plastic wrap & cut slits.) Turn down to 20% power for 15 minutes. Perfect every time. For softer rice or brown or exotic rice, use more water and cook longer as needed. TO DOUBLE RECIPE: you will need to increase the time needed to boil but staying with 20% power for 15 minutes should be fine.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I believe I read through most of the notes but I don't think I saw reference to the question I'm most curious about - why a 2-1/2 to 3 quart container for 3 cups of ingredients?  I realize that as the rice boils, liquid will rise in the bowl, but is such a large bowl really required?  Seems like a good use for a 2-quart batter bowl if that would work.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: See pampered chef for their microwave rice cooker. 1 cup rice/2 cups liquid (I use chicken broth). 5.5 minutes full power-10.5 minutes 50% power. Let rest. Perfect.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
Ordered a plastic cooking pot for cooking rice in the microwave oven.  I followed the directions but I reduced the rice from a cup to 1/4 cup (I was cooking only for myself).  I adjusted the cooking time down but not enough.  Nearly melted the plastic pot.  Ordered another plastic pot, cooked another pot of rice with reduced cooking times, same disaster...
Have gone back to using a tried and true double boiler with wonderful results.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I have used a similar method for years, BUT cover the dish and set on high for 5min to come to boil, then decrease power to 3-4 (out of 10) for required time--45 min for short grain brown rice.   Running the micro for 15-20 min results in a disatrous boil-over!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I still get gummy rice when I use this technique.  What works for me is 1.5 cups jasmine or basmati rice rinsed.  Then add 2 1/4 cups water and some salt. Cook covered on high power for 5 minutes, then stir, cook covered (I leave the lid slightly ajar) for 15 minutes on .5 power.  Let the rice sit covered for 5 minutes.  This is a reliable microwaving alternative. So far, the rice is feathery with distinct grains.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: This method did not work for me - rice was undercooked and stuck together.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
2/27/23

Amazingly easy. Came out a bit stiff and hard, probably cooked too long at 20 minutes. 

15 minutes might be more perfect next time.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
2/27/23

Amazingly easy. No cleaning two dishes.

At 20 minutes, rice came out a bit dry and sticky. Next time worth trying 15 mins for full recipe.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Let me revise my previous note. This recipe doesn’t multiply or divide well, and depends on precise timing and attention at the end of the cooking process. Since convenience in rice cooking means it can sit, ready to serve, as you finish preparing the meal, this has to be a gimmick at best.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
Does anyone have experience using a lesser amount of basmati or jasmine?  I usually use 1/2 or 2/3 of a cup.   

Otherwise I'll try it at the cup size. 

I love the look of the cooked rice in the photo.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
I did the full recipe approach and it turned out terrific.  Not that stove top is difficult, but the texture of the MW rice is better.  I'll add a bit of salt and butter next time.

I stumbled on the hint to reheat rice in the MW using an ice cube.  "... pop an ice cube on top of the rice before you reheat it in the microwave. Rather than melting, the ice cube helps to steam the rice and it will only take a minute to produce hot, ready-to-eat rice."   better than MW alone!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: For great basmati rice - 1 cup rice (no rinsing) lightly sautéed in 2 Tbsps ghee. Add 1.5 cups warm (from tap) water. Cook uncovered on high to just boiling. Add a bit of salt (about 1 tsp kosher), stir, then turn down to a simmer, put a clean folded dish towel or about 3 layers of paper towels on top of the pot, then the lid. Cook for 15 minutes, turn off heat and let sit 10 minutes. Gently fluff with fork or spoon. Ready to serve!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Great if you have the spare burner and not needing to pay attention elsewhere. Used your method for years. This microwave method actually produces better basmati rice with zero attention and no stove.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I cook rice in the microwave frequently, using a very similar path -- 180 grams of rice, 480 grams of water, a little salt and a splash of vegetable oil. My microwave goes to 900 watts, and I cook it for 7 minutes at full power and 14 minutes at half-power in a covered glass measuring bowl. Works fine every time.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
I do brown rice, 6 minutes high power, then  12-15 minutes 50% power depending on how powerful the microwave is, 
Easy enough to do the second phase short, then check if it’s done. If you see any umabsorbed water, it isn’t done. Then do another minute or two and check again. Only takes a time or two to see how long it takes in your microwave.b

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I cut the recipe in half (not reading the comments), and after 15 minutes it was a hard disk of undercooked rice. I added more water and broke it up with a fork. Cooked it another minute x 3 checking water content after each minute, added a little butter and it was great! Reconstituted, resurrected rice! I’m looking for a reason to abandon my rice cooker in my small kitchen. This worked!!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Followed recipe ( for once!) and it came out splendidly!  Our new and only method.  Thanks.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I'm using supermarket Sunrice Instant Rice Cups - a few different Rice varieties. 40 secs in microwave. Am I missing out?

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Great recipe!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I have been doing this for years. 1 cup basmati rice / 2 cups water. I never rinse and 13 minute in the microwave covered. Let sit 5 minutes. Perfect every time!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Beware - this may be too much for your microwave. Twenty minutes continuously on high burned out my 3 year old microwave! Rice was good though.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Tried this. It works but I don’t see the point. It doesn’t save time, it doesn’t produce a better result and you have a bowl of sticky rice bits to clean instead of a pot of sticky rice bits.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Boiled over and made a mess.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: It works really well!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: This is a fraught method for cooking rice. I thought I would try it this evening, and started with 15 minutes of cooking time. It was not done at 15 minutes, so I added 3 minutes to the cooking time, and it completely dried out, and became hard. I added some more water, and the heirloom pyrex bowl I was using shattered into a thousand pieces. Thanks for nothing, NYT... I'll stick with my rice cooker!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: 10 minutes is sufficient.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I cook rice like pasta…works great.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: This worked perfectly, following directions, ingredients and amounts exactly as written. I find making rice on my electric cooktop to be finicky. I’m doing it this way from now on.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I did a bag of boxed rice in 10 mins in the microwave in a 2 quart bowl filled with 4 cups of water. I don’t eat much rice but this seemed fine.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: It worked well

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I've done this twice using the steam/simmer Setting on my microwave. It took a little experience to determine how much time is needed but the rice comes out fluffy and the pot barely needs cleaning. I use a 2 1⁄2 quart CorningWare pot that is over 50 years old. This is much better than my rice cooker because there is no scrubbing.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Works surprisingly well. Did it w jasmine rice yesterday, after realizing the rice cooker was lent out. 22min

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Very disappointed as I wanted to keep from using gas burner in summer.  Since after 15 minutes, the rice water exploded in the microwave and I had to cook further, this was a BIG MESS and in no way, easier or quicker than stovetop method.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Did you cover the bowl? Don't cover the bowl. Works brilliantly if you don't cover the bowl, at least with a 1100 Watt microwave. And no cleanup of the bowl to speak of. Crazy easy.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I use boiling water and cover my bowl everytime...I have never had an explosion.  Been making microwave rice for many years.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: This works perfectly if you follow the instructions exactly. And it's really helpful if you've already used up your stove's burners doing other things, or need to pay attention somewhere else in your cooking project. The stove doesn't turn itself off after 15 minutes, the microwave does. Richard of the explosion probably covered the bowl. The first time I made this, I did what I always do and soaked the Basmati rice; way too mushy. Following the instructions precisely works perfectly.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: 75g washed basmati rice. 150 g water. 8 mins. 800W microwave. Perfect

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
I had about 8 HUGE collard leaves, and the "1 tablespoon" of filling wasn't going to cut it. Thankfully, scaling up the filling worked great, didn't have to modify any timings etc.

Also, if you have extra filling you can easily cook up in microwave with 2x water to filling. I had 3/4 cups left, 1.5 cups water, 12 minutes in microwave, turned out SO good.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I made this with brown rice and the 2 1/2 c. of water in the tip. Took about 23 minutes in my 1000 Watt microwave. Came out perfect.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
I LOVE this idea - but my results so far with brown rice varieties were unimpressive.  I wonder if an overnight soak in water might improve the texture of microwaved brown rice?  
I emailed Lundberg Family Farms asking for their best guidance on microwaving brown rice - but I'm not holding my breath for their reply.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I would not soak the rice. I suspect you need to cook it longer. Mine always comes out perfect. I use a store brand, nothing fancy.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I have made microwave for many years.  But I prefer to use boiling water and cover with plastic wrap.  This cuts the micro time to 10 minutes.  I also add a pat of butter to the boiling water before starting.  Sometimes I sub hot chicken broth for water too.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Tomato and Parmesan rice. 800 watt microwave, 16 minutes, 1 tea cup of white rice, two tea cups of water, 3 tea spoons of olive oil, half a tube of double tomato paste. In the last two minutes add butter (25 gr) and 6 teaspoon of Italian seasoned Parmesan.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: The results are exactly as stated here: fluffy, separate grains. As per always, I will chill the rice to reduce its glycemic load. See under "resistant starch."

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: My very inexpensive rice cooker can cook white rice in 24 minutes. It’s foolproof.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I’ve been using a Zojirushi 6-cup rice cooker that I picked up at a Goodwill store over ten years ago for under ten dollars. Plug it in, measure out the rice, add the water and 20 minutes later, you have perfect rice. New Zojirushi cookers are expensive, but you can pick up other brands for under $20. No over-cooked or under-cooked rice ever because of physics: when the water is fully absorbed by the rice, the weight changes and the pot clicks off from cooking and changes to ‘keep warm’.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
Everyone asking about brown rice, it says at the end of the recipe:
You also can use this technique for brown rice. Follow the instructions above, using 2½ cups water for 1 cup long-grain brown rice and cook for 20 to 30 minutes.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I have an 1100 watt microwave. I simply follow the recipe that came with it to cook brown rice. I use the simmer setting, but if your microwave doesn't offer that setting, simply follow the directions above. I usually make 2 cups at a time so that I can freeze some for fried rice or a quick dinner later on. A 2.5 quart square CorningWare pot is ideal and clean up is a breeze. Perfect rice in about 20 -25 minutes, better than my rice cooker, which usually has browned pieces sticking to it.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Slightly scorched rice at the bottom of the (stovetop) pan is the BEST part of the pot!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Be sure to let the rice rest in the microwave after the power has shut off.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Followed this recipe and ended up with an inedible rice brick. Total waste of time (and rice).

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Much easier to track.  I also made it with vegetable broth.  The one note I have is that the water or broth really has to be at room temperature or this doesn't work.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
Two cups brown rice, 5 cups water: 37-39 min

Two cups white rice, 4 cups water: 17 min

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Is there any reason you can't use chicken broth instead of water?

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
This works perfectly with white jasmine rice for 15 mins in my microwave, but one cup of rice is barely 3 servings in my house.  

I successfully scaled up to 1.5 cups rice and 2.75 cups water for 18 mins.  It's slightly al dente the way I like it.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Have an 800 Watt Microwave. Cooking for the minimum 20 minutes produced mushy jasmine rice!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I ended up with an inedible mess!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
I've never had trouble cooking rice properly on the stovetop, but decided to try this. My microwave is, I think, 1200W. When I follow this recipe exactly, it is perfect. When I tried to scale down the recipe for 1/2 cup rice, no go; I think too high a portion of the water was lost to steam.
Chilled the leftovers in the bowl (2 Q was big enough), covered; they came out nicely. Soaked the bowl with cold water, and any residual starch cleaned out easily.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I cook a premixed long grain and wild rice combination. I use a half cup of rice, and a full cup of water - plus a few tbsps. Takes 13 minutes (1000 watt). I like that I can leave the timing to the oven. The rice doesn't seem to suffer if I'm not there to pull it out right away. I place a glass bowl with a lid on a dinner plate, with a paper towel in-between to catch any water 'boil-over'. Going to try it without the lid and see if that helps. I rinse and soak the rice before cooking.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: My water tends to spill out from the bowl. Any idea why?

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I've been microwaving rice for years, since I had a microwave with a rice button that gave me the idea about 20 years ago. My approach is slightly different. 1 cup rice and a bit over 1.5 c water. I cover the casserole with plastic wrap and poke a few holes in the wrap, this helps keep it from making a mess. In my current microwave it's 4 mins on high, 13 minutes on power 3, but the time after the initial 4 mins depends a lot on your microwave.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Sorry, if you ONLY use your instant pot to do rice and boiled eggs, you'll be happy you bought one. It's perfect every time.  Along with chick peas and other dried beans.  The money you save on those will pay for the instant pot.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Perfect brown rice in 20 minutes in my 1000 microwave. Thank you!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Microwave ovens don’t heat evenly; that’s why they have turntables, but even with those there can be hot spots.  Rinsing for several minutes might produce rice that isn’t sticky, but it also wastes a lot of water.  This has the advantage of not cleaning the pot of the rice cooker, but is a lot more wasteful in general, since it requires as much time to cook; microwave ovens use a lot of electricity, probably comparable to the rice cooker.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: It takes 20 min on the stove and 20 minutes in the microwave. Why would anyone do this?

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: The problem I have is that the water boils over and leaves a big mess. What am I doing wrong?

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: use a rice cooker

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: C'mon folks! If Edison had given up after the first pot of exploded microwaved rice, we wouldn't have the light bulb! Keep trying! Recipes aren't challenges, they're opportunities. The chicken shouldn't be standing in front of the stove.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Bring one and 1/4 cups water to boil. Add one cup rinsed rice. Bring back to boil then turn down to low for  20-25 minutes. Remove from heat and let sit. Perfect every time.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I have done this for years.  My Kitchenaid MW has a rice setting as does an older whirlpool one.  I use the rice setting for white long grain, and for brown or brown basmati, I use more water plus a longer cooking time.  I always cover, and it works FANTASTICALLY… no attention necessary.  I will not cook rice another way anymore.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I do slightly differently. Large bowl, same measurement water and rice.  20 minutes at level 4. Works for me.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: better than my cheap rice cooker and easier to clean up after

author: Jesse Szewczyk
commentBody: i used to work at a pastry shop in wellesley ma, right next to the library and we used a ton of pastry cream. the owners told me that the recipe would go to the grave with the family matriarch, who was the irascible pastry chef. one day i was taking out the trash and i noticed quart sized containers of heavy cream. almost all the cream we used came in pints. so i fished one out of the bag and found twisted up inside it an empty food service size pouch of jello instant vanilla pudding.

author: Jesse Szewczyk
commentBody: Best part before making…reading the notes!

author: Jesse Szewczyk
commentBody:
For years I have made a whiskey cake that everyone (even foodies) loves which uses 1 box yellow cake mix, one box vanilla pudding, .75 c. oil, .75 c. water, 4 eggs, vanilla. Mix it up, bake at 350 in a greased bundt pan for about 45 minutes. 
While hot and still in the pan, prick with a fork and then pour over a combo of heated .5 c. whiskey, 4 tbl.  butter, .75 c. sugar. So easy.

author: Jesse Szewczyk
commentBody: I'm trying this!  Especially since reading about the whisky cake, and wondering if I could just use a boxed mix.

author: Jesse Szewczyk
commentBody: I am old enough to say that my mom made the one with a box vanilla or white cake and instant pistachio pudding.  Whenever I make a bundt cake, there is a box of instant pudding.  It is essential to those of use who grew up in the 70s.

author: Jesse Szewczyk
commentBody:
@Knitter 215
Mmm-mmm!! And the marbled chocolate butterscotch Bundt had both varieties of jello pudding mix. Sweet childhood dreams were made of these!

author: Jesse Szewczyk
commentBody:
Just made this—delicious!
-Used a carton of egg whites instead of trying to separate 9.
-I always use grapeseed oil.
-Skipped the ricotta cream topping.
-Used the zest & juice of one lime for the glaze, which was plenty. 
They’re not kidding about the pan size. I have an 8 cup Bundt which is usually fine for recipes calling for larger pans, but I failed to take into account how much the eggs would add to the rise! It overflowed onto the baking sheet but I just trimmed the edges and it was fine.

author: Jesse Szewczyk
commentBody: Using pistachio paste (Sicilian or from California) in addition to or instead of the instant pistachio pudding mix improves the taste without compromising the texture.

author: Jesse Szewczyk
commentBody:
For Americans living in the UK:
   When I moved to Britain in 2010 I could not find any instant pudding mixes except Bird's instant custard which didn't work & I personally think it is vile tasting. Finally I discovered packages (called sachets in the UK) of Angel Delight and it worked!!  I have a recipe for chocolate Kahlua Bundt cake that calls for 4 eggs, 16 oz. of sour cream, and among other ingredients, a pkg. of Jello chocolate instant pudding mix; Angel Delight does the trick!

author: Jesse Szewczyk
commentBody: Pudding mix adds moisture to scratch cakes and improves their rise. Even a couple tablespoons makes a difference if you need the cake to keep for several days.

author: Jesse Szewczyk
commentBody: The ingredients in instant pudding mix are sugar and cornstarch, aren't they?  (along with a bunch of artificial flavors & coloring).  I'm no Jello snob-one of my favorite desserts ever is the Five Layer Dessert which uses Jello Instant though I draw the line at Cool-Whip. Melissa Clark uses cornstarch in her poundcake recipe, and it really does improve the texture.  So I bet it's not the Jello so much as the cornstarch.  Wonder what the other bakers think.

author: Jesse Szewczyk
commentBody: @EllKayCee @EllKayCee Instant pudding uses modified corn starch. It can be bought on its own as Instant Clear-Jel. It has different properties than cornstarch.

author: Jesse Szewczyk
commentBody: I used this recipe as inspiration with yellow cake mix. Therefore not this recipe really at all. But it came out awesome. Here's the recipe: 1 box yellow cake mix; 1.25 c sour cream or plain yogurt; 1 box pistachio pudding mix; 5 eggs separated (whip the whites in a separate bowl to stiff peaks with 1t salt but no sugar, put the yolks in the cake mix); 1t almond extract; 1/2 c warm water; 1/3 c vegetable oil. Fold in the whipped egg whites with 1 c chopped walnuts. Bake at 325 for 1 hour.

author: Jesse Szewczyk
commentBody: You lost me at instant pudding.

author: Jesse Szewczyk
commentBody: And yet, the recipe that inspired this, box pudding mix and all, was on the menu at Momofuku and at its current residence is considered by some to be one of the best desserts in New York City.

author: Jesse Szewczyk
commentBody: Thanks for letting those of us who might be curious to try the recipe if it's worth trying.  Very helpful.

author: Jesse Szewczyk
commentBody: Instant pudding has a modified cornstarch that activates with cold liquids. It’s not the same as the type that must be cooked. Consumers can purchase this its own, as Instant ClearJel; I had to order it online. You can make your own instant puddings with it, or use it in fruit pie fillings. It’s the same as the recently popular idea of tangzhong (=cooked flour paste) in bread. One article on the science on pregelatinized starches in cakes is at https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/309 .

author: Jesse Szewczyk
commentBody: My Nana and mom made this cake, but topped with a mix of whipping cream and more pistachio pudding mix.

author: Jesse Szewczyk
commentBody: Absolutely LOVE this cake.  Worth the work and love that goes into it.  The ricotta cream paired beautifully with it  Will be making this again.  I wasn't sure whether to use salted or unsalted pistachios, opted for the salted and it was great!

author: Jesse Szewczyk
commentBody: The cake is moist but honestly, the lime glaze was so overpowering is was difficult to taste any pistachio flavor at all, just lime.  And I didn't even use all the glaze. The technique seemed overly fussy for the result, too.

author: Jesse Szewczyk
commentBody:
Grew up with a version of this cake that my mom used to make, except hers had chocolate chips studding the cake along with the pistachios, and no glaze or sidecar of ricotta - a simple dusting of powdered sugar to finish the cake with a little extra pop of sweetness & visual contrast.  
Will definitely give this version a whirl to compare...

author: Jesse Szewczyk
commentBody: Made it according to the recipe.  You do need the large bundt pan.  Cooked exactly 60 mins and it turned out of the pan perfectly.  Big hit at our house. My glaze was too thin, but I think that was a good thing.  And I just sprinkled some chopped nuts on top - not even close to 1/2 cup, though.  Batter was lick the spatula good.

author: Jesse Szewczyk
commentBody: @ Barbara regarding the egg yolk comment: I agree whole-heartedly with your sentiment. I hate food waste. I frequently cook with egg whites. Here is my recipe for the leftover yolks (from Cook's Illustrated). Make a syrup of 2 parts sugar to 1 part water and stir it into the yolks (using ¾ teaspoon syrup per 4 large yolks). Defrost and use in place of beaten yolk. Desserts, like flan and madeleines using egg yolks frozen in this way are nearly the same as those made w/ fresh yolks.

author: Jesse Szewczyk
commentBody: 8 yolks=>pot de crème. Yummers!!!

author: Jesse Szewczyk
commentBody: Sorry to say the return on investment here (both in time and ingredients) was very low. Bland and disappointing. Definitely won’t make again.

author: Jesse Szewczyk
commentBody:
Well, I made this over-hyped cake the other day, expecting wonderful pistachio flavor and excellent crumb.
My reaction after several bites?
“Dump it in the garbage can.”

author: Jesse Szewczyk
commentBody: I've made this cake twice.  The first time, I followed the recipe exactly, and like many reviewers, thought it could use more pistachio flavor.  The second time, I added about 1/2 cup of lightly sweetened pistachio paste to the milk, yogurt, vanilla mixture and cut the sugar to just under 2 cups.  Cake looked and tasted great and now the pistachio really comes through.  Will be making this again and again (in part because I bought a 7 lb can of pistachio paste!)

author: Jesse Szewczyk
commentBody: You can buy separate containers of egg whites.

author: Jesse Szewczyk
commentBody: Nine egg whites? In this economy?! 😂

author: Jesse Szewczyk
commentBody:
In the middle of making this (about 40 minutes left in the oven) and I gotta ask: were the gram measurements way way way off when compared to the cup measurements? 

Was in the middle of measuring out the sugar when I saw I’d have to grab a much bigger bowl to reach 528 grams. Forgot the exact grams when I switched to a cup but 1 cup was more than half of where my scale was currently at, and yes: just checked my scale and it seems all good. Anyone else?

author: Jesse Szewczyk
commentBody: @T A it was the same for the flour, measuring cups yielding much much more grams, I took out probably 1/3 cup and then just used the scale for the rest. It’s currently overflowing in my oven so we’ll see what happens.

author: Jesse Szewczyk
commentBody: When I was a child, my mother made pistachio chocolate bundt cake similar to Mr. Pinsky's mother, but with a chocolate cake base. Likely it was Duncan Hines chocolate cake mix. It was a family favorite. Thank you for publishing this version!

author: Jesse Szewczyk
commentBody: One can also use pistachio paste in the glaze for more of a "pistachio flavor".

author: Jesse Szewczyk
commentBody: To not waste egg yolks, purchasing a carton of egg whites is a saver!

author: Jesse Szewczyk
commentBody:
This is very delicious, though as the baker I wished for a cake with a drier crumb and less oil. I skipped the whipped ricotta because I was toting the cake, and frankly did not miss it at all.
The topping pictured uses probably less than 1/3 the amount of pistachios called for in the recipe. I used all that was called for and completely covered the top in pistachios! Everyone loved it.

author: Jesse Szewczyk
commentBody: "if you've never been a fan of bundt cake....." I don't understand this intro.  Bundt is a shape of cake, not a type of cake like pound cake.

author: Jesse Szewczyk
commentBody: This cake was a hot mess for me. Too much batter to even fit into my regular sized Bundt pan, for starters! Very specific and detailed directions, which I liked...but too many ingredients and really: NINE egg whites? The batter was really heavy for all those egg whites...must have been to more-than one cup of canola oil. Plus, the glaze was enough for two cakes. I was really hoping the taste would be all that. It wasn't. I could taste the artificial pudding mix flavor & the ricotta cream? Boring

author: Jesse Szewczyk
commentBody: Do not use liquid egg whites from a carton. My cake was a disaster. It fell, was dense, and went into the trash.

author: Jesse Szewczyk
commentBody: People liked it. Not so much work. Used boxed whites and they didn't peak but cake turned out fine. Salted nuts. 2 cups sugar, 1 cup oil. No glaze but dusted w confect sugar and put lime zest in the ricotta cream. Double the cream for the whole cake. Pistachio paste would have made it better. 60 minutes was almost overdone in my oven - I'd check earlier. Do use full 12 cup pan.

author: Jesse Szewczyk
commentBody: this is a kind of an interesting hybrid of an angelfood cake and a pound cake, IMO. But I do like it. Next I would like to try this with lemon pudding and poppy seeds. Also maybe vanilla.

author: Jesse Szewczyk
commentBody: Visually the cake was quite spectacular. Unfortunately, the artificial pistachio flavor of the pudding significantly downgraded the taste of the cake. Will probably not make it again.

author: Jesse Szewczyk
commentBody: This was very easy to bake and tasted delicious, albeit very rich. Next time I’ll add raspberries, maybe some citrus zest, to cut the sweetness. But I’ll definitely make it again

author: Jesse Szewczyk
commentBody: The recipe rose above the cake pan.  A little weird, but okay.  The ricotta was grainy and didn’t add much.  The lime glaze was perfect.  I wonder if a drop of pistachio flavoring would push it too much?  It seems heavy, a little oily.  I definitely used the eggs whites in a carton, just took a little longer to hold its shape.  Delicious.  Not too sweet.  I used roasted and salted pistachios, because you didn’t say not too.

author: Jesse Szewczyk
commentBody:
My cake was an absolute disaster because it stuck MISERABLY to pan.  But it tasted good (more vanilla than pistachio), it had a great texture, and the ricotta cream was very delicious.  Add chocolate chips for a cannoli cake?  I much prefer Smitten Kitchen's cannoli pound cake. 

Also, the carton of egg whites was the best idea.  Thanks to all for suggesting.

author: Jesse Szewczyk
commentBody: Left off the glaze per other comments and just sprinkled some powdered sugar on top. Cake was just ok considering the amount of work, a little spongy,closer to angel food cake consistency,which makes sense considering all the egg whites. Whatever you do don’t skip the ricotta cream! I was tempted but it’s delicious and makes the cake!

author: Jesse Szewczyk
commentBody: Made this over the weekend and it was pretty good! I left out a cup of the sugar - my German mother in law rails against the sweetness of American cakes, and typically adds only 1/2 of what’s called for and for the most part, that works for me. It turned out great, still plenty sweet with the addition of the glaze and the instant pudding mix. The only thing I would do differently is maybe find a pudding mix without the green coloring? Gave it a kind of other worldly green look…lol. Still yummy

author: Jesse Szewczyk
commentBody: This is unbelievably good. Don’t skip the ricotta cream - makes great even greater. I splurged on the vanilla paste, and it made a big difference in the cake and the cream. We thought the whole combination of flavors with lime, vanilla, and pistachio was outstanding. The cake is soft, dense and moist, and the crust caramelizes to chewy perfection. Baked for 60 minutes flat, and it was perfect.

author: Jesse Szewczyk
commentBody: Made a 1/2 recipe as too big for mixer. Used King Arthur Fl meringue pdr for egg whites. 1 T pdr   4 t water = 1 egg white. Worked perfectly. Lime glaze overwhelmed. Ricotta crm was unnecessary. Cake was tender and close crumb lovely but lacked flavor, for the lime. Try again with a van. butter cream glaze only.

author: Jesse Szewczyk
commentBody: I never add my input/reviews, but I had to for this recipe as this cake was phenomenal! Friends and family loved it, not just for flavor but also looks as it’s a very pretty cake. I followed the tip of using egg carton egg whites and left out the ricotta topping, so those were the only modifications. 10/10

author: Jesse Szewczyk
commentBody: I add Monin pistachio syrup, before and after cooking in the oven. Clients prefer it this way.

author: Jesse Szewczyk
commentBody: I use Monin pistachio syrup in the batter and as a soaking liquid when the cake is cooked

author: Jesse Szewczyk
commentBody: This is my “ go to” want to impress cake recipe. Whenever I make it, there is never a slice left & people rave about it forever… they have told me I can sell it! I have it down to a system now…1. I measure everything out before I start 2. I make one bunt & one small square to keep at home 🤩 3. I follow the directions exactly ( the low end of the baking time) …. Fabulous!!!!

author: Jesse Szewczyk
commentBody:
Never canola oil. There’s no such thing as a “canola.” Never even think of using it again.

Instead, delight in using avocado oil, say, Primal Kitchens? Nutrients, mild flavor, high burn point.

author: Jesse Szewczyk
commentBody: From the Univ of Missouri Extension: Canola is a European crop that migrated to America, with a stop in Canada along the way. In fact, the crop we call canola is actually a type of European rapeseed that was developed by Canadians, using conventional plant breeding, into a food crop providing healthier and safer vegetable oil for human food use. The name “canola” derives from the phrase “Canadian oilseed low in acid.”

author: Jesse Szewczyk
commentBody: Here's a fun, easy idea for the egg yolks: salt-cured egg yolks: https://www.splendidtable.org/story/2018/02/01/salt-cured-egg-yolks I remember the first time I made them I was wondering what to do with all the egg whites!

author: Jesse Szewczyk
commentBody: When I see a recipe with a packet of Jello pudding, I don't expect a fussy recipe. Multiple bowls of mixtures, multiple steps, time consuming. 1st cake is in the oven, & I just saw my bowl of yogurt, milk & vanilla paste on the counter.Ugh!!!!! I am an experienced baker, and am frustrated.I tried egg whites in a carton, and they never developed thick peaks. I made a second batch with real egg whites, and it worked.Do not add the flour and canola oil at the same time.They clump together.

author: Jesse Szewczyk
commentBody: I baked it 2 standard loaf pans, one for me, one to gift!

author: Jesse Szewczyk
commentBody: Tasty, but the batter overflowed out of the bundt pan once it was in the oven. Like, a lot! The recipe should definitely have a warning about this.

author: Jesse Szewczyk
commentBody:
First of all, don't use egg whites sold in a carton. They don't whip up like the recipe says. I learned the hard way. 
Secondly, this cake is pretty good but the batter is a bit of a hassle - making the marshmallow mixture, all the mixing bowls required, and the strange measurements. I can't say the end results were any better than a more basic recipe. I don't think I'd make this again.

author: Jesse Szewczyk
commentBody: I love the end result, but went on an emotional roller coaster while following the recipe - after 60 minutes of baking (with a beautiful golden brown top and the toothpick coming out clean), I let the cake cool and flipped it over only to find that there was a portion totally uncooked! I think it's an issue with my oven (though I rotated halfway through), but anyhow I ended up having to add 25 minutes of baking time to get the cake fully baked.

author: Jesse Szewczyk
commentBody: Been wanting to make this cake for quite a while and finally tackled it. Definitely use a sheet pan underneath bc it overflows — I have a 12 cup Bundt and it still was bubbling over. The texture of this cake seemed more like an angel food cake vs. a traditional Bundt. It has a tighter crumb so not as much mouthfeel and texture (which I prefer). It’s also pretty sweet, not that subtle peek of flavor that I wanted. Not sure I would make it again but it was a good experiment and my crowd seemed to enjoy it!

author: Jesse Szewczyk
commentBody: I  am not a fan or yogurt, Greek or otherwise. Can I substitute sour cream for this ingredient?

author: Jesse Szewczyk
commentBody: Yogurt and sour cream are generally interchangeable in baking, but I'd bet serious money you'd be unable to detect any difference between them in the finished cake.

author: Jesse Szewczyk
commentBody: I use sour cream in my recipe.

author: Jesse Szewczyk
commentBody:
A recipe I treasure from my mother, who was a terrible cook otherwise, is an orange-mace-wine-pudding mix cake that is not only fool-proof (hence my mothers success) but delicious and very rich. It also uses cake mix (please see “fool proof” above)

Don’t dis the pudding mix.

author: Jesse Szewczyk
commentBody: Sounds fantastic. Please share that recipe!

author: Jesse Szewczyk
commentBody: Looks amazing—but what do you do with all those egg yolks?

author: Jesse Szewczyk
commentBody: Make creme brûlée!!

author: Jesse Szewczyk
commentBody: You can buy egg whites by the carton which eliminates the need for leftover yolks. Or if you don't want to go that route You could also make hollandaise, homemade mayo, creme brulee, custard, ice cream, google recipes for leftover egg yolks and I'm sure you'll find something.

author: Jesse Szewczyk
commentBody: Are the pistachios salted or unsalted?

author: Jesse Szewczyk
commentBody: Either is fine.  The important thing is that they are toasted.

author: Jesse Szewczyk
commentBody: I don’t have a bundt pan.  Could the recipe be tweaked for a loaf pan? It looks awesome.

author: Jesse Szewczyk
commentBody: Used a standard size Bundt pan and it overflowed big time.

author: Jesse Szewczyk
commentBody: Instant pudding ingredients are mostly sugar and cornstarch, with small amounts of additives, preservatives, stabilizers, etc.  I'm surprised that chefs can't figure out how to add the required pudding-mix ingredients without all the other dreck.

author: Jesse Szewczyk
commentBody: Reminds me of Watergate cake that was popular in the ‘70s. The frosting was also made with pistachio pudding.

author: Jesse Szewczyk
commentBody: Any recommendations for gluten free flour?

author: Jesse Szewczyk
commentBody: What is pistachio pudding mix and where do you find?

author: Jesse Szewczyk
commentBody: I have the same question as it isn’t available in Europe.

author: Jesse Szewczyk
commentBody: It’s instant pistachio pudding mix made by Jell-O. Maybe Amazon? Jell-O Instant Pudding & Pie Filling, Pistachio, 3.4-Ounce Boxes (Pack of 4) https://a.co/d/bIhARcc

author: Jesse Szewczyk
commentBody: Never having used pudding mix in my life I’m intrigued by this recipe. I’d be curious to know what kinds of additives it has, and amounts of sugar, salt etc. And, is the pistachio flavor from pistachios or chemicals? These are important questions these days.

author: Jesse Szewczyk
commentBody:
The “friend” who outed herself says pistachio pudding is a must but also pistachio paste — which is not listed as an ingredient in this version of the recipe. 

How much pistachio paste would one use? And at what step would it be incorporated? I’d really like to try this recipe and want to ensure it tastes like pistachio, a flavor I came to love as a kid in Tehran.

author: Jesse Szewczyk
commentBody: I can't speak to the pistachio paste, but a good quality pistachio extract would boost the flavor without compromising the structure of the cake--I'd start with a teaspoon.

author: Jesse Szewczyk
commentBody: @ Maria holt: my exact thought!

author: Jesse Szewczyk
commentBody: What do you do with 9 leftover egg yolks?

author: Jesse Szewczyk
commentBody: Sounds like a more sophisticated version of Watergate cake from the ‘70s.

author: Jesse Szewczyk
commentBody: I happened to have 9 egg whites in the freezer from previous recipes that called for yolks, so this was a perfect recipe. Be sure to thaw them out in advance! I don’t know that I would have made it otherwise. The texture in the end was a bit chewy, but that might have been because I used the “convection humid” setting on my steam oven. It works great for banana bread, but maybe it was too much for the egg whites.

author: Jesse Szewczyk
commentBody: Sooooooo ... can you use custard powder instead, as with quite a few cake recipes?  (Because  what on earth is pistachio pudding?)

author: Jesse Szewczyk
commentBody: CC I think the chocolate chip addition was maybe a nod to Spumoni an under appreciated flavor profile!

author: Jesse Szewczyk
commentBody: The best part of this recipe is the ricotta cream and the icing.  The cake does not have much flavor without these.  Too much work for the end result.  There are Much more tastier recipes for bunt cakes, without the waste of yolks.  Yes, you can find uses for the yolks if you are ready to use them right away.  More work. If not, they can be frozen. The icing and the ricotta cream are quite good to use on other cakes in the future.

author: Jesse Szewczyk
commentBody: This one is a keeper. Followed the directions exactly (weighing) and the cake is moist with a contrasting crisp edge. Happened to have cannoli cream and used that for dolloping.

author: Jesse Szewczyk
commentBody: My old stoneware Bundt pan from Pampered Chef was obviously not large enough as this cake completely overflowed the pan while cooking. After about an hour, a toothpick came out clean but the cake still seemed liquid-y so I cooked for another 10 minutes. By this point, the cake had settled a bit in the pan, but in hindsight it probably could have cooked for a few more minutes. I'm not sure the whipped ricotta added much flavor-wise so I might skip that if I make this again.

author: Jesse Szewczyk
commentBody: I looked at this recipe and saw nine egg whites and said, No thank you.

author: Jesse Szewczyk
commentBody:
Today I made lunch for 10 Habitat for Humanity volunteers and I baked this cake (no whipped ricotta or glaze) and Melissa Clark's oatmeal cookies for dessert. This cake got rave reviews especially from my husband. Later in the day, our 7 year old grandson had a piece and he said it was delicious!  
I can only imagine what a lovely dessert this would be with the whipped ricotta and glaze. 
Note:  I used a 12-cup Bundt pan which wasn't big enough for all the batter the recipe makes.

author: Jesse Szewczyk
commentBody: I'm prob same age as the mother who inspired this. I also used pistachio pudding for my Bundt cake but it was marbled... half chocolate was stirred in. But thanks for reminding me. I think I'll dust off my Bundt pan and bake this! was maybe 40 yrs ago!

author: Jesse Szewczyk
commentBody:
Made this today. Considering the number of ingredients and the amount of work involved, the cake was just ok. 
Also if I make it again I won’t garnish it with flaky salt. It left an unpleasant sweet and salty taste in my mouth.

author: Jesse Szewczyk
commentBody: My mother's pistachio bundt cake -- made with a cake mix and instant vanilla pudding was my favorite cake growing up in the 70s. I often requested it as my birthday cake, instead of a 4 layer chocolate cake (also good) that she made from scratch.

author: Jesse Szewczyk
commentBody: Fell very flat..I followed the recipe exactly. Probably won’t try again… 9 egg whites!

author: Jesse Szewczyk
commentBody: People love to complain: This cake is DIVINE. It has the perfect crumb and an intense flavor that does not taste artificial. The recipe was easy to follow and our cake came out beautifully at 65 minutes. The ricotta sauce is heavenly and should not be skipped (splurge for the vanilla bean paste--it will make that much of a difference). My only complaint is that there was WAY too much glaze, which could easily be halved. Contrary to others' thoughts, we thought the lime flavor was just right.

author: Jesse Szewczyk
commentBody: Use butter and flour instead of the cooking spray, or put the spray on right before adding the batter. This cake is delicious!! A total keeper. Make sure the glaze is thick enough, but if it's not, it's still great. Soaks in like a rum cake. Will definitely make this again, and I'm not a big baking fan. It's a nice impressive cake that feeds a lot of people. Didn't make the whipped ricotta. This holds its freshness for a long time. Make ice cream with the yolks!

author: Jesse Szewczyk
commentBody: Used gluten free all purpose flour and it came out reasonably well, despite that fundamental change. I found it did retain more moister (as that flour often does) so it browned up more on the outside than I would have preferred while trying to get it baked on the inside. In future I would reduce oven temp and increase baking time given my flour shenanigans. On the whole though, I really wasn't a fan of the lime glaze with the pistachio flavors, and that's my reason for three stars.

author: Jesse Szewczyk
commentBody: This cake was absolutely stellar. Several guests said it was the best cake they ever had! I accidentally purchased non-fat Greek yogurt, so I added a tablespoon of butter. I would wholeheartedly disregard the comments about the glaze as it was an absolute must to complement the flavors of the cake. I didn't use it all and so it didn't overwhelm the flavors.

author: Jesse Szewczyk
commentBody: Used a carton of egg whites instead of separating eggs. I beat them according to the instructions, added the sugar, and beat on high as recipe stated. Mixture turned out soupy. Then I used actual eggs and separated them. Perfect results! Just an fyi.

author: Jesse Szewczyk
commentBody: I found this a bit disappointingly dry and spongy and not super flavorful. The no yolks aspect deprived the cake of moisture and richness that the pudding mix didn’t make up for. I guess if you love Angel food you might enjoy it but I don’t think I’ll make it again.

author: Jesse Szewczyk
commentBody:
Don't let anyone tell you not to make this!  It is delicious.  I left the lime glaze off after my daughter asked "Do Lime and Pistachio go together??"  and I skipped the mascarpone. We had it with tea and coffee at breakfast or as an evening snack after dinner. 

One important note -- if you use carton egg whites you'll need to beat them for a LONG time!  Mine were organic ones from Whole Foods and they took 15 minutes. But, they'll get there if you are patient.

author: Jesse Szewczyk
commentBody: After reading all the notes on this recipe I was a little nervous to try it.  I read thru the recipe and all the notes 3 or 4 times before I finally dived in and I'm really glad I did.  I used 5 real egg whites with 4 carton egg whites and had no problem getting the whites to stiff peaks.  I made the whipped ricotta the day before so I didn't feel like I had a stack of bowls in to wash, but honestly I wasn't a fan of it.  There was alot of batter so I split it between a Bundt pan and a 9 inch ca

author: Jesse Szewczyk
commentBody: This came out beautifully. Based on others comments about Bundt pans being too small and given the number of egg whites being akin to an Angel food cake, I used an Angel food cake pan, thoroughly greased. No overflow at all and came out of pan easily. Took 70 minutes. Also, only used 1 cup of oil in batter. Otherwise, no changes. Next time may reduce sugar by 1/4-1/2 cup. And definitely only need half the glaze. Enjoy!

author: Jesse Szewczyk
commentBody: Good lord how big of a bundt pan can there be? I’m just watching it bubble over like mint lava. $$$ yikes.

author: Jesse Szewczyk
commentBody: A fancier version of Watergate cake, which the Wash. Post came out with in the 70s. Yeah, there's nothing like the pudding.

author: Jesse Szewczyk
commentBody: I made it for Easter and it was loved by all. I used full fat Greek vanilla yogurt. Thank you for this delicious recipe

author: Jesse Szewczyk
commentBody: Oil shortfall update: 1/2c was not enough. Cake was acceptable for just us but not to serve others. I’d suggest somewhere between 1c and 3/4c if others are finding it too oily.

author: Jesse Szewczyk
commentBody: My son has a dairy allergy, so I made this with soy milk and Cocojune coconut yogurt. I’m not a fan of glazes or frosting, so we just ate the cake plain and it was delicious. I’m at 8500’ and didn’t otherwise adjust the ingredients. I don’t have a large Bundt pan (just a regular one), so I filled it about 2/3rds, and was able to make 9 cupcakes with the remainder. Cake cooked for 48 minutes, cupcakes for 20.

author: Jesse Szewczyk
commentBody:
I really hated this cake.  It tastes super artificial.  There's no way that they are using Jello instant pudding in the restaurant...maybe My-T-Fine brand pistachio pudding  tastes less like chemicals?  
I had a million frozen egg whites so I don't lament the waste from a financial stand point.  However, I find this cake inedible and it's huge.  Even my 9 year old hates the after taste.  
Mind you, I love strawberry Quick and Doritos every now and again, but this is nasty.

author: Jesse Szewczyk
commentBody: I very much appreciate that the recipe writers have listed most of the ingredients in grams, (thank you!!!) but oh how I wish the egg white amounts would be given in weights, too. Especially if you are using egg whites from a carton. Soooo much easier (and more accurate)  to pour and weigh on a scale. This is especially true for cake recipes, where small changes can yield differences in the end result.

author: Jesse Szewczyk
commentBody: I have an old delicious recipe for a pistachio bundt from the early 80's that involves yellow cake mix, and the pudding.  This recipe seems a lot more complicated, wink wink.

author: Jesse Szewczyk
commentBody: @Nancy I was reading the recipe thinking the same thing! It would be totally different from this recipe and apparently quite offensive because of the boxed cake and pudding mix but I bet it would be good!!

author: Jesse Szewczyk
commentBody: This was excellent! Made the cake as written, but subbed sour cream for yogurt and skipped the glaze and whipped ricotta. Served with raspberries. So good!

author: Jesse Szewczyk
commentBody: This cake was a hit. I brought it to my Book Club brunch and it was the most adored sweet on the table ( and there were many ). I had made homemade raspberry curd, so I had an abundance of egg whites....270 gm. The only change I made was to add some Amoretti brand Pistachio butter (extract) to the cake and also making a cream cheese glaze instead of the lime glaze, and it was perfection. Moist and delicious without tasting artificial or overly sweet. I didn't make the whipped Ricotta this time.

author: Jesse Szewczyk
commentBody: Instant pudding mix? With all of the bad additives and chemicals? NEVER. I added freshly crumbled Brontë pistachios and used corn starch in place of the pudding mix, which has zero real pistachios. What an odd item for a recipe.

author: Jesse Szewczyk
commentBody: can you freeze this cake?

author: Jesse Szewczyk
commentBody: This is my absolute favorite NYT recipe! I have made this cake several times over the past year and all of our family and friends have loved it. I have a pampered chef stoneware Bundt pan and it fits perfectly. 100 stars!

author: Jesse Szewczyk
commentBody: I have gotten so many compliments on this cake. Three changes I've made: I add extra pistachios (more finely chopped) into the batter; I use lime zest to garnish in addition to the pistachios, and I don't use all that sugar in the ricotta-- just a tablespoon. I think that a more savory flavor in the ricotta brings out the richness of the pistachios.

author: Jesse Szewczyk
commentBody: So what do you do with the ricotta mixture, I’m missing something?

author: Jesse Szewczyk
commentBody: Right at the end the recipe states to serve the whipped ricotta on the side.

author: Jesse Szewczyk
commentBody: My friend just made this for a pot luck and everyone loved it! She didn’t mention the pudding mix until the cake was gone. We were surprised!

author: Jesse Szewczyk
commentBody: I confess, I am the friend.My recipe is much more Ladies Home Journal than New York Times, but nevertheless delicious. The pudding is absolutely essential for taste and moistness as is pistachio paste.

author: Jesse Szewczyk
commentBody: I’m not a fan of canola oil. Any suggestions for alternative oil?

author: Jesse Szewczyk
commentBody: Anything neutra tasting should work, a light olive oil for example. The canola isn't likely used because of any flavor it gives the cake but because it won't give any extra flavor to it. I imagine an avocado oil would work fine too, coconut maybe?

author: Jesse Szewczyk
commentBody: I’ve been making a version of this cake since 1974. Yum

author: Jesse Szewczyk
commentBody: Ok now I need a recipe calling for 9 egg yolks.

author: Jesse Szewczyk
commentBody: Tiramisu

author: Jesse Szewczyk
commentBody: How about making ice cream with some of the yolks?

author: Jesse Szewczyk
commentBody: Grocery stores sell cartons of egg whites.

author: Jesse Szewczyk
commentBody: The recipe stipulates that the pistachios in the topping should be unsalted, but what about the pistachios in the batter?

author: Jesse Szewczyk
commentBody: Thank you @ Raindrop for the excellent and informative comment

author: Jesse Szewczyk
commentBody:
This brings back fond memories of the beginnings of the internet. Prior to browsers I'd explore info via the rec. newsgroups including rec.food.recipes & rec.food.cooking.

Somewhere in there was a copycat Cinnabon Sticky Bun recipe. The secret? A package of Jell-O Vanilla Pudding mix. I make this whne a family gathering involves brunch.

author: Jesse Szewczyk
commentBody: Itʻs in the oven now. Yes, the egg yolk issue is key. It would be nice to include in the description of the recipe that this is an opportunity to use the yolks!

author: Jesse Szewczyk
commentBody: 9 egg whites? It will cost $30 to make this cake! What’s a good use for 9 egg yolks?

author: Jesse Szewczyk
commentBody:
Anyone have a substitute for ricotta ?  I detest it and find it useless. And I'm part Italian. 
Also, those 9 eggs are trouble right now. I can afford them, but refuse to buy. You know, supply and demand...

author: Jesse Szewczyk
commentBody: The ratio of Dishes to Delicious is difficult with this one… after all that, it was just pretty good.  And the whipped ricotta definitely feels unnecessary.  I’ll keep this cake as one that should be made a day ahead as it’s much better after resting overnight. Breakfast cake— YES please!  And as others noted, important to plan ahead to use for those precious 9 egg yolks!

author: Jesse Szewczyk
commentBody: Anybody try this recipe reducing the amount of sugar???

author: Jesse Szewczyk
commentBody: You can reduce the sugar up to 25% without changing the crumb texture, but more than that will definitely work against the texture and moisture.  Try King Arthur's sugar substitute for baking.  The best.

author: Jesse Szewczyk
commentBody:
Boatwif-on a narrow boat somewhere in England- 
I would like that recipe please!  🙂

author: Jesse Szewczyk
commentBody: KK and CC loved your comments. Yes had to make its delicious. I’ve done it before with just more instant pudding mix and whipped cream as topping. The cake is great the recipe toppings were very good but for regular home baking I might do it 70’s style with the classic topping. I work on a time and fuss vs. payoff equation. A winner.

author: Jesse Szewczyk
commentBody: Pistachio flavoring is one of the most artificial flavors and tastes nothing like real pistachios..unfortunately it is in the same category and cherry flavor.  Some original flavors just cannot be captured and pistachio pudding mix is one of those...

author: Jesse Szewczyk
commentBody:
@ Andrea what do you do with the pistachio paste in addition to the instant pudding? 
Thank you,

author: Jesse Szewczyk
commentBody: Has anyone tried this but replaced the whole milk with skim, the whole fat yogurt etc with low or no?  I’m always looking to cut back on fats…..just wondering…..

author: Jesse Szewczyk
commentBody: Don't cut back on fats when baking. Find other ways to do that, if you must. If you're eating dessert, admit it, and enjoy it, or don't bother.

author: Jesse Szewczyk
commentBody: The cake is beautiful and very tasty. However, it required 5 bowls of varying sizes, plus the food processor and the electric beaters, numerous measuring cups and two sets of measuring spoons along with whips and spatulas. I don’t work outside the home and am retired so have plenty of time but the mess was considerable and will not be making again. Also used “Reddit-eggs” which are all egg whites as I didn’t want to use 9 eggs at the cost of eggs. They did whip to soft peaks but would not whip.

author: Jesse Szewczyk
commentBody: Does anyone have ideas for an alternate glaze?  I know it's not exactly making the same recipe, but I've just never much cared for lime.  I do, however, love pistachios.  They are among my favorite things.  I just know I wouldn't enjoy the cake as much with lime flavors.  I can make a simple vanilla glaze and I'm sure it would be fine, but if anyone is feeling creative with complimentary glaze ideas I would love to hear them!

author: Jesse Szewczyk
commentBody: A classic icing is confectioners sugar and milk or cream, with perhaps some vanilla bean. I like organic confectioners sugar for topping because the anti-caking agent is less gritty.

author: Jesse Szewczyk
commentBody: Frist I baked as writ, it was good, a little too sweet.Children enjoyed it. Glaze was a little thin. Second time I skipped the yoghurt, added ricotta in a similar proportion to the milk mixture, used 1/2 cup oil, and only 2 cups sugar to egg whites. Did my own measurements for the glaze. Much better and lighter result. Would like to try Bird's custard for a vanilla and almond flavour.

author: Jesse Szewczyk
commentBody: Does the leftover cake need to be kept in the refrigerator afterwards?

author: Jesse Szewczyk
commentBody: How is this best preserved afterward—does it need to be refrigerated or can t be kept at room temp?

author: Jesse Szewczyk
commentBody: Does this need to be refrigerated afterwards?

author: Jesse Szewczyk
commentBody: No. But you can freeze it easily.

author: Jesse Szewczyk
commentBody: I needed to make this cake low sugar. I used Truvia cane sugar blend as a directed on the package(1 1/4 C). Followed the rest of the recipe using cartoned egg whites. I put it in the pan and ate a big spoonful of batter.  Meh. But then I remembered that I had not added salt. Then I noticed the bowl with the yogurt, milk, and vanilla still sitting on the counter. So I scraped the batter out of the pan added in the missing ingredients. Baked 45 min. Perfection!

author: Jesse Szewczyk
commentBody: Excellent recipe. The ricotta whip was delicious. The only ingredient I substituted was ponderosa lemon instead of a common lemon in the glaze. Definitely recommend.

author: Jesse Szewczyk
commentBody: I got very excited about this recipe until I checked my local supermarket (Wegmans) and saw that both brands of pistachio instant pudding they carry, Jello and Royal, use artificial pistachio flavoring and genetically modified ingredients. Ugh. I will look for pistachio paste instead, as a couple of people suggested. What else do I add to get the texture that I'd get from the instant pudding?

author: Jesse Szewczyk
commentBody: Nothing will give you the same texture, but you will have much better flavor.

author: Jesse Szewczyk
commentBody: Any sub for no nuts?

author: Jesse Szewczyk
commentBody: This cake stuck to the pan and broke apart. Any advice? I usually don't have that problem. I just stuck it back together and drizzled on the glaze & nuts. It was delicious. I appreciated the advice about buying egg whites in a carton.  I had to beat them for close to ten minutes to get peaks.

author: Jesse Szewczyk
commentBody:
Made this cake today. In my humble opinion there’s no need for garnishing it with flaky sea salt. 
It left an unpleasant sweet and salty taste in my mouth.

author: Jesse Szewczyk
commentBody: This cake has an amazing texture     - 2 things I'd do differently  next time.  1.  folding the ingredients into the egg whites requires a LONG spatula if  you use a common stand mixer with a tall bowl - the standard length one I have was too short and made it harder than it needed to be  and 2. I cooked it in a dark metal bundt and the crust of the cake was pretty thick/chewy - I'll try a lighter metal pan next time (unless anyone else has other suggestions).

author: Jesse Szewczyk
commentBody: This is a fabulous cake and is well worth the effort. We made it as stated and though we had difficulties getting it out of the “top rated” Bundt pan, we enjoyed it a lot!

author: Jesse Szewczyk
commentBody: Could some or all of the unused egg yolks be substituted for some of the oil?

author: Jesse Szewczyk
commentBody: Not really.  It will make the cake less moist and make it seem "tighter" in texture.

author: Jesse Szewczyk
commentBody: Has anyone yet tried substituting olive oil for part or all of the canola? I was going to make this for a birthday celebration today but discovered I was almost out of canola oil.

author: Jesse Szewczyk
commentBody: What do you do with 9 egg yolks?

author: Jesse Szewczyk
commentBody: I used egg whites from a carton and had trouble getting them stiff enough to peak. Cake sank a little after it cooled… but still delicious.

author: Jesse Szewczyk
commentBody: I made this cake the other day. The cake by itself is delicious so is the whipped ricotta. However, I did not like the lime glaze, especially with flaky salt over the cake. It was rather off putting to my taste anyway.  Next time I will only sprinkle powdered sugar before serving. The cake tasted better the next day and the day after that!

author: Jesse Szewczyk
commentBody: Cake is delicious, glaze is delicious, ricotta is delicious (but unnecessary.) Cake keeps well at room temperature for about a week, keeps tasting better. Look up soy sauce pickled eggs on this site (Sam Sifton) and eat the yolks over rice. Yum.

author: Jesse Szewczyk
commentBody:
I use a lot of yolks and always have leftover egg whites on hand - they last for a very long time - and am always looking for recipes to use them up.

Used the wrong size pan, resulting in overflow and a cake that was crunchy on top and very dense inside. Do you think this cake can be made in an angel food cake pan? Or can the overflow be baked in a regular cake pan?

Going to try this when I have amassed 9 egg whites again and hoping for a better bake. Definitely NOT buying another bundt pan.

author: Jesse Szewczyk
commentBody:
Thank you to everyone for the notes. Always a highlight! For people not getting the same stuff peaks from egg-white cartons, while avoiding the cost of 9 fresh egg-whites (despite the great suggestions for how to use the yolks):
When making cakes at home, we often do a mixture of whole eggs and separated, whipped egg-whites folded in. A trick for this one? Use a couple of fresh egg-whites and the rest from a carton. Maybe a tiny pinch of cream of tartar at the end? That always works here!

author: Jesse Szewczyk
commentBody: Just made this recipe and it was excellent! Everyone loved it!

author: Jesse Szewczyk
commentBody: I don't see where the whipped ricotta comes in. You don't mention how it included. Is it just "plop some on, if you like" or is this a necessary part of the dish? What's up with this, please? It doesn't seem necessary so...?

author: Jesse Szewczyk
commentBody: Has anyone tried to 1/2 the recipe for a smaller Bundt pan?

author: Jesse Szewczyk
commentBody: ok this cake was an absolute disaster for me.
