SOME of the issues are definitely mine, but i think some of them are issues in the recipe. for starters, i realized too late my bundt pan is more in the ballpark of 6-8 cups instead of 12, so i split the batter into two different pans, one a bundt and one a loaf tin. i should have reduced the cook time to account for this error, but i forgot, so they were slightly over baked! now: this recipe needs to call for a better way to grease your bundt tin.

author: Jesse Szewczyk
commentBody: i greased my tin as per the recipe and doused it in cooking spray, but neither my loaf nor my bundt survived the removal from their tins. this cake's texture is very silky and fragile, and if i make this again, i'll probably put it in a different tin, potentially one that is springform, so the removal process is a little less violet. my cakes were a little stickier than i think they should have been, potentially from the over baking?, so this also contributed, but again the cake is VERY FRAGILE.

author: Jesse Szewczyk
commentBody: finally, i didn't include the pistachio bits inside the batter because my roommate doesn't like when there are large nuts in baked items. however, both my cakes fell badly. were the pistachio pieces partially structural? or was there something wrong with my whites or baking powder? my bp is new and my whites got to hard peaks. the overall taste is great, but i'm confused about my result.

author: Jesse Szewczyk
commentBody: Too much batter for my bunt pan so it overflowed and then collapsed. Couldn't get it out of the tin so just dug in with a spoon. Still tasted great! Although it is a VERY sweet cake and I'm normally not the sort of person who has a problem with that....

author: Jesse Szewczyk
commentBody: I used two smaller Bundt pans since the recipe makes a lot of batter. The cake slumped and was gummy in the middle despite baking for longer than instructed and having a nice color on the outside. I wish it would have tuned out because it was tasty.

author: Jesse Szewczyk
commentBody: My third time. This time I omitted the pistachio mix for the same measurement by weight of Birds, and added almond extract as well as my vanilla paste. Omitted the oil entirely. Added a little less flour, maybe more like 2 1/2c. Sugar I only added 1 c to meringue mixture. So far it's baking and rising well. I'm hoping for a less dense and sweet cake. I made a glaze with 2 1/2c powder sugar, vanilla paste, almond extract and whole cream to thin.

author: Jesse Szewczyk
commentBody: directions seem contradictory: adding sugar before egg whites are well whipped keeps them from forming firm peaks...

author: Jesse Szewczyk
commentBody: I don't understand the enthusiasm for this cake.  The pudding mix (and the rather odd marshmallow base) gave it a soft, somewhat spongy, texture.  More importantly, the cake has almost no pistachio flavor.  You'll have to add extract or a pistachio paste to make it really worthwhile.  I won't remake it.

author: Jesse Szewczyk
commentBody: I think I followed the directions closely and it turned out just great. I did used kosher salt instead of flaked salt. Made it for a book group and it was a hit- the taste of the cake and the whipped ricotta.  a winner!!

author: Jesse Szewczyk
commentBody: This cake exploded in my oven. It overflowed the pan and left a mess in the oven. Smelled good though. Unfortunately the middle of the cake was still raw. I tried baking it some more and turned it into a brick. My husband took it out of the trash and put it on top of the pilaster by the driveway. Birds ate it all and we are now beloved by the crows. I’m going to make it again but in 2 Bundt pans.

author: Jesse Szewczyk
commentBody: Me too.  I don't make many bundt cakes, so I didn't realize filling it up that much meant it was 100% going to overflow (I have a normal sized bundt pan).  I put a sheet pan underneath to be safe (thank goodness), but that hindered the bake I think because mine too was still very jiggly after 75 min.  I baked for 20 more after removing the sheet pan (all the overflow was done) and it finally came out.  In making again, I definitely would use 3/4 the batter at the most or two bundt pans.

author: Jesse Szewczyk
commentBody: This is an amazing cake!  I've only made it twice, but I learned:  1) It is a lot of batter, even for my largest bundt pan; I  had around 3/4 cup excess batter.  I made a mini loaf cake out of that.  That worked, but then do check the cake after 35 minutes so you don't over bake;2)  I doubled the chopped pistachios in the batter - much better. (3) Ricotta topping is fine but I preferred scoops of good quality vanilla ice cream (4) I buttered/floured cake pan, to be confident in release of cake.

author: Jesse Szewczyk
commentBody: I never write but wanted to note that I followed the directions and it was truly awful. Greasy, oddly unflavorful. I feel bad about all those eggs.

author: Jesse Szewczyk
commentBody: Way too sweet.

author: Jesse Szewczyk
commentBody: Can raw pistachios be used in the cake? I can't find roasted unsalted ones. Going to use roasted lightly salted for the top.

author: Jesse Szewczyk
commentBody: I thought about using salted for the top, but I used roasted UNsalted nuts in the cake and on top. All the pistachios are grown in California, and we can easily find shelled and unshelled, salted and unsalted nuts. I bought unsalted unshelled nuts for this recipe.

author: Jesse Szewczyk
commentBody: I followed the recipe very carefully and took the cake to my office. They're still talking about it. However, between shopping for the ingredients (pistachio pudding mix) and carrying it 20 miles on my train commute, I probably won't oblige my cohorts anytime soon. The cake was very good, and was exactly as described.

author: Jesse Szewczyk
commentBody:
This cake is delicious!
-it does use a lot of bowls
-used egg whites from the carton, they took a bit longer to whip
-the whipped ricotta is divine, don't skip it
-cut sugar down to 2 cups

author: Jesse Szewczyk
commentBody: Made this using 2 small tube pans- gave one as a gift- other for a book group along with the fabulous Tunnel of fudge cake by Shirley Corriher (in NYT : 12/28/2004, a staple for me) - both were hits and recipes requested. I used more pistachios in the cake and made less glaze- thanks to the advice seen here. Also served with pistachio ice cream, but most liked it plain.

author: Jesse Szewczyk
commentBody:
Overflowed my massive bundt pan and stuck so bad it came out in pieces (yes I greased). The picture looks like a much denser crumb and no green food coloring from the pudding mix, I'm not sure how they got that between the generous amount of baking powder and the egg whites.. 
Tastes alright, VERY sweet, looks like garbage!

author: Jesse Szewczyk
commentBody:
Absolutely wonderful.
My bundt pan is only 8 cups.  Third time I made it forgot to use the reduced ingredient amounts I figured out using the gram measurements.  Note that the additional batter fits into a 6” pan.  And also note that it is better to prepare every ingredient before starting. Not my usual approach but for this cake it is far less stressful.
Incredibly good cake!!

author: Jesse Szewczyk
commentBody: I was excited to try this cake but came away a bit disappointed. Cloyingly sweet and bland with no real pistachio flavour. I didn’t realize the pistachio pudding mix would give it a toxic green hue (not very appetizing nor does it look like the photo).  A lot of ingredients for a “just okay”cake. It made one bundt cake (regular size) and eight mini bundt cakes.

author: Jesse Szewczyk
commentBody: I really wanted to love this cake. But after the many steps of mixing an array of ingredients, this tasted like a combination of lime, powdered sugar and flat grainy frosting. Cake Doctor cakes are better than this. And always flour the pan.

author: Jesse Szewczyk
commentBody: We grew up with this cake in the 70s!  And always loved it!  You cannot go wrong with this cake!

author: Jesse Szewczyk
commentBody: My mom’s version, still one of my all-time favorite desserts, uses a cinnamon-sugar mix to coat the well-greased Bundt pan. OMG, is that good.  And that’s all you need for a topping. She also made a cinnamon-sugar-nut streusel that she added after pouring in about half the batter.  And with Duncan Hines butter cake mix.

author: Jesse Szewczyk
commentBody: Also a hot mess for me. I used TJ's boxed whites, which absolutely would not whip, after 30 mins of beating. I had to dump those eggwhites and sugar and start again with real, separated eggs (painful in this age of expensive eggs.) They whipped fine and we were so excited to see the cake rising beautifully in the oven....until the whole thing fell dramatically.  Could not get it out of the well-oiled pan, left it in to cool. Not pretty but reasonably tasty. Wish I knew what I did wrong!!

author: Jesse Szewczyk
commentBody:
You know how you to the garage to get something and notice something needs doing and it leads to doing 16 other things? 

This was the same. Made it as written. Knew my Bundt was too small so made 4 Bundt-lettes too, that only took 1/2 hour. I will have to see if I like them better then full-size due to the crunchy outside vs amount of inside

What to do with the yolks? Ending up making my first challah (4 yolks) and prepped potato croquettes that I’ll make tonight with the other five yolks).

author: Jesse Szewczyk
commentBody:
If you don't plan to eat this entire cake within about 12 hours of icing it (it is a large cake!), don't sprinkle the flake salt onto the wet icing. It will melt and leave unattractive pockmarks.
I had fun with the unusual techniques, and found this a quick and easy recipe (once the 9 eggs are separated, that is) with minimal clean-up (add both the milk & yogurt to the same measuring cup and stir in the vanilla to save a bowl). Thought the cake was just meh, though (my husband loved it).

author: Jesse Szewczyk
commentBody: Shoot, forgot to add that I made this GF as I am celiac. Used Krusteaz GF AP Flour. I find it give the best texture of the GF flours I’ve tried.

author: Jesse Szewczyk
commentBody: I didn't use the glaze or the ricotta, so this review is solely for the cake. My only complaint is that the instant pudding mix turned the cake into a ghastly pastel green, but the cake itself was very good. I decreased the sugar to 2 cups and it was still a little too sweet. The cake is very hard to mess up due to the pudding mix--it has a texture reminiscent to boxed cake mix in the best way. The color is so distasteful I would only recommend this if you can find yellow pistachio pudding mix.

author: Jesse Szewczyk
commentBody: Nate (below) had great notes... I also had a too small Bundt pan. I had measured it and it was just under 1 inch short of being deep enough (it must be a 10 cup vs a 12 cup pan?). But I had placed a sheet tray with parchment paper to catch the drips which really helped. Thank you Nate!

author: Jesse Szewczyk
commentBody: I have had a package of pistachio pudding on my counter for weeks to remind me that I wanted to make this cake.  Made according to the directions, without the whipped ricotta. I used carton egg whites, which worked fine, and I liked the cake's texture.  My dinner party guests happily ate it.  However, I agree with other reviewers who found the flavor lacking.   I will make this one more time using pistachio paste along with the rest of the ingredients, to amp up the pistachio flavor.

author: Jesse Szewczyk
commentBody: Wondering if anyone has used other pans besides a bundt pan for this and how they have worked out? Thank you!

author: Jesse Szewczyk
commentBody: This cake has garnered amazing raves from good bakers. Bought egg whites from the supermarket, as some notes suggested.  No ricotta filing but yes to the icing.  No to the pistachios for the finished cake my Bundt pan is only 8 cups.  The first time I figured the ingredient list with 2/3 of each ingredient.  2nd effort forgot.  Had enough left over to fill a 6” cake.  It worked.

author: Jesse Szewczyk
commentBody: Carton of egg whites did not peak for me. Had to switch to separated egg whites. Maybe I had some fat in the first batch.

author: Jesse Szewczyk
commentBody: Egg whites in a carton are pasteurized and will not whip.

author: Jesse Szewczyk
commentBody: Yummy, subtle & complex special occasions cake. The instant pudding mix does seem foodie unorthodox but it yields a moist cake with good crumb. Great use of our freezer’s stash of frozen egg whites. The runny lime glaze (should of used more confectioners sugar) and ricotta cream dressing send this right over the top. Will make again for a special occasion.

author: Jesse Szewczyk
commentBody: Enjoyed this cake very much without the ricotta topping or glaze. I cut the sugar to 2 cups.

author: Jesse Szewczyk
commentBody: Oh no. I just put the cake in the oven to look over and realize that I only added 1/2C oil to the batter.  I forgot the rest as I was hand folding. Crud.

author: Jesse Szewczyk
commentBody:
Swapped sugar with 1/3 brown sugar 
Swapped yogurt with 1 cup butter 
Swapped canola oil with crisco

author: Jesse Szewczyk
commentBody: I used a 4 cup pyrex measuring cup for the glaze which poured easily onto the cake.

author: Jesse Szewczyk
commentBody: Love this recipe. Followed the recipe closely and the only substitute was lemon for lime. The texture and flavour was outstanding

author: Jesse Szewczyk
commentBody: Made this cake and it was very good.  Wondering what would happen if you doubled or at least increased pistachio pudding.  Thinking it needs a bit more pistachio flavor.  Wonder if more pudding would mess up recipe results?

author: Jesse Szewczyk
commentBody: This was really tough for us. A Bundt that holds 12 cups is insufficient. The glaze is overly lime-y and covers up the cake. If we were to do this again we would drop the glaze entirely. And we would use a regular pan or make muffins. The Bundt was basically a disaster in slow motion. The cake had a great taste though.

author: Jesse Szewczyk
commentBody: FYI I only have a 10 cup fluted bundt instead of the required 12 cup, but the recipe as written makes exactly enough to fill a smaller bundt (~2/3 to 3/4 full to avoid overflow) and a full 6 count tray of mini bundts. Baked all at the same time and the minis were cooked in 25-30 mins and the larger bundt was ready 10-15 mins later 👍🏻

author: Jesse Szewczyk
commentBody: This is a fairly involved recipe that results in a nicely flavored, well textured cake. I omitted the ricotta sauce, as I wanted a cake that would (and should) stand on its own merits. The fresh lime juice glaze seemed like a good topping, but, as with other commenters, I found the tartness of the lime not to enhance the pistachio flavor, in fact, I found it an odd combination. If I make this cake again, I might try the juice of an orange instead. I also will omit the salt flakes on the glaze.

author: Jesse Szewczyk
commentBody: Add 5-6 drops off green food coloring to milk mixture to create pleasant green color of cake

author: Jesse Szewczyk
commentBody: For fun I added two drops green food coloring to the glaze. I skipped the ricotta whip after reading other commenters. The cake was a hit with my pistachio loving friend who I made it for.

author: Jesse Szewczyk
commentBody:
This is a very nice cake, but it is very sweet and you have to enjoy "fake pistachio" flavoring to really enjoy it.  If you would like the pudding, you will love the cake.  I also found the glaze recipe to yield too much glaze, half the recipe would give you result shown in the picture.  

I wonder if anyone has tried chocolate pudding mix in a cake?

author: Jesse Szewczyk
commentBody:
1.Regarding The nuts -think about how much time it would take to shell them. Do it in advance to make workflow easier 

2.It’s a lot of batter- use a larger mixing bowl when doing last step

3.Cream- recipe could be doubled ! It is so delicious!!

4.Glaze- if you want to cut the sugar then cut the lime juice too

Summary: delicious, beautiful, moist, unique

author: Jesse Szewczyk
commentBody: Huge hit.

author: Jesse Szewczyk
commentBody: i have made this twice. a trifle labor intensive but a solid recipe & fun to do. have found both times that the whipped ricotta is a bit of an overkill, feeling like too much of a good thing. highly recommend serving as smaller slices - this cake is kind of full-bodied to over-the-top bodied.

author: Jesse Szewczyk
commentBody: Why 9 egg whites, it sounds excessive. Some times with cake making less is more. To me, too many ingredients.

author: Jesse Szewczyk
commentBody: Frosting far too thin

author: Jesse Szewczyk
commentBody: I really wanted to like this cake - it sounded delicious from the description. But the flavor was rather artificial, and the cake tasted like it was made from a boxed cake mix. It didn't taste like pistachios, except for the ones sprinkled on the top.

author: Jesse Szewczyk
commentBody: Has anyone made this cake in a large cake pan? I'd like to split it like a layer cake and frost it with whipped cream. Has anyone done this? Thanks in advance

author: Jesse Szewczyk
commentBody: I’ve made this cake twice now and had a similar issue both times. The first time I used a large Bundt pan, and the second time a large round cake pan. In both cases, it took forever to bake, about an hour and a half. The second time, the outside ended up pretty burnt while the inside was still delicious. I vaguely remember the first one being similar, not burnt, but definitely overbrowning. I even had to cover it with foil so the bottom wouldn’t burn before the center was done. I’m not sure what I’m doing wrong. Any tips or pan recommendations?

author: Jesse Szewczyk
commentBody: I had a similar issue the first time I made this, but I think it was due to overfilling my bundt. I have a fancy Nordicware bundt pan and it is just a touch smaller than the standard. When I made this a second time, I only filled it about 2/3 and it came out beautifully!

author: Jesse Szewczyk
commentBody: FYI, Google says to substitute 9 large egg whites using a carton of liquid egg whites, use 1 1/8 cups (270 mL) or 18 tablespoons.

author: Jesse Szewczyk
commentBody: @Tina that happened to me too! I thought it was my silicone Bundt pan since it's happened before.

author: Jesse Szewczyk
commentBody: The instructions should be broken in to more steps especially Step 4 which is more like 4-7. Also it took the addition of many more tablespoons of sugar before the glaze even stayed on the cake, let alone made a good presentation.

author: Jesse Szewczyk
commentBody: I made this a few days ago.  Nice taste, but too oily.  I did use canola oil.  I would like to try this again.  Has anyone tried this with less oil?  Or maybe a different oil?  Hoping to hear soon so that I can retry this recipe.

author: Jesse Szewczyk
commentBody: This recipe was a great way to use up the egg white collection in the freezer (from all the  various cake/cookie recipes calling for extra yolks).

author: Jesse Szewczyk
commentBody:
Several people suggested egg whites from a carton to simplify the recipe. 
I am sitting here with whites that won't whip up and just learned that carton egg whites are pasteurized, which damages the protein structure and inhibits whipping...

author: Jesse Szewczyk
commentBody: This cake is so divine. I'm a little irritated by how good it is because it is a little fussy to make (including sourcing pistachio pudding mix), but well worth it. Having been to Claud in NYC many times and had the cake at the source, I'm so happy that it is nearly identical. The crumb is really the star here. The last time I made this, I used the icing from Bon Appétit's pistachio bundt cake, and I loved the two together. Just a personal preference.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Hey folks hurling judgement at strangers for using a mix: I’m a “scratch or die” baker but hands down the cookie I bake that sends people into orbit starts with a yellow cake mix and I’m fine with that. The most important ingredient in any recipe is love.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Totally agree!!  Please share your yellow cake mix cookie recipe... ;)

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I agree, no judgment. It's just that in this case I made it this way and from scratch, and the latter really was a lot better. For one thing, this version is just flavored whipped cream and I much prefer a pudding.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
@diane Agreed! I just listened to a podcast (She’s My Cherry Pie) with the current owner of Magnolia Bakery who walked listeners through her recipe for Magnolia’s famous Banana pudding.

When she bough the bakery, she wanted to revamp the Banana Pudding recipe by making it with homemade vanilla pudding. She brought the revamped recipe to her staff to taste and provide feedback, and everyone yelled, “No!” Everyone loved the Banana Pudding made with the boxed Jello-O brand (only!) vanilla pudding! So, mixed pudding mix it is!

She also mentioned that the version for the cookbook is slightly different (more creamy) because the ratio of ingredients differ slightly from the huge vats they make at the bakery. She wanted bakers to be able to make the recipe using just one box of wafers, one can of sweetened condensed milk, one box of pudding, etc.

Can’t wait to make this! 

She also said she sometimes doesn’t layer the pudding— she just throws everything into a bowl and saves. Little pudding to spread in the top for a party! 
(Podcast pods

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I worked at Magnolia Bakery over 20 years ago. We just made the vanilla pudding mix according to the instructions on the box (i.e. with whole milk). Not sure about this whole sweetened condensed milk with water thing. Sounds overly sweet.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: @Clark it’s actually published in magnolias official cookbook

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: @Clark Back when it first opened, I remember the banana pudding being less sweet than when I tried it years later. When I made it at home a couple of years ago, it was also too sweet. Now I make it with whole milk and I think it's what I remember from way back when.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
Here are the cookies that start with a yellow cake mix! :)
1 brick of cream cheese
1 stick of unsalted butter
1 egg
1 tsp vanilla
1 yellow cake mix 
Powdered sugar

Preheat to 325
In a stand mixer with the paddle, cream the butter and cream cheese until smooth and fluffy. Add the egg and vanilla and mix to combine. Add the cake mix and mix until just combined. Form the dough into 1” balls and roll in the sugar. Place on a baking sheet. Bake for 10-12 minutes. They should be gooey and not brown.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: @diane thank you so much for this recipe. Saw this yesterday and baked this today and everyone love it! So gooey, like a cake in form of a cookie. Yum! Thank you! 😘

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: This is the classic southern banana pudding served at holidays.  I do not understand the need to make the pudding with condensed milk.  I think it makes it too sweet.  I just make the pudding with milk as called for on the box.  And I prefer the cooked instead of instant pudding.  And it helps to coat the sliced bananas in lemon juice.  They stay fresh longer and the lemon adds a nice zest.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Tried it with Biscoff cookies instead of vanilla wafers - next level!!!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
This recipe is often requested by friends and family. I have made in trifle bowls, mason jars and even in pie form (my husband is a banana cream pie fanatic). It is always consistently good and could not be more simple. I always like to crush 5-6 cookies and sprinkle over the top to add a little crunch right before serving.

I do not feel the need to do the first step in a stand-mixer, but definitely for whipping the heavy cream. A hand held mixer would work just as well.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Toasted, ground pecans, with a little butte,r and salt in lieu of vanilla wafers makes it gluten-free, and maybe better.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: How many pecans, please?  Thanks.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: @Bob Weiss Pecans and butter sounds amazing! What a great idea.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: My friends go wild when I make this dessert for get together! I don’t bother with a stand mixer for the first step. The water, condensed milk, and pudding mix can be easily mixed by hand. Also, i only use half the can of condensed milk and it is still plenty sweet!!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: @Charlene. yes 1/2 of the recommended eagle brand condensed milk they give vanilla pudding a turn in the mouth which combined with bananas really peps it up I would certainly avoid the banana cream pudding mix we’re making parfait cups for a party.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: In remembrance of my grandmother, we put egg white meringue on the finished pudding, which was then browned with a small kitchen torch: it was delicious!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: @Joe if you don’t bake your meringue, you are eating raw egg whites.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: @Frances Duquette Davis. In-shell pasteurized eggs are readily available for safe consumption (USDA reports 0.0% chance of salmonella and other harmful bacteria) for recipes calling for uncooked eggs

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Why a stand mixer? Wouldn't a hand-held mixer with whisk work?

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: @jane yeah what’s the deal with always instructing us to use a space-hog? I don’t have room for a standup mixer so I’ve always used my trusty hand mixer. Please stop making it sound like we have to go buy a $400, counter-hogging appliance! Can you just say “mixer”??????

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I totally agree with Diane and I am anxiously waiting for her magical cookies that start with yellow cake mix!  Thank you NYT for including recipes like this (and the pistachio Bundt cake from last week). These recipes are part of American history and have just as much validity as high brow recipes. I love both.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Unfortunately, Nilla wafers are much smaller than they used to be, which is a shame. One wafer used to cover the bottom of a muffin tin, making them perfect for individual cheesecakes (a popular recipe in the 1970s & 1980s). Not any more.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Interesting catch! I wonder why.  So they can reduce the calories/carbs of a "serving size" of X cookies for diet-conscious consumers without reducing the number of cookies? So they can reduce the actual contents but keep the same amount of cookies?

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I too worked at Magnolia (circa 2000-2001 Hi, Clark!) and this is EXACTLY how the banana pudding was made. With cans of sweetened condensed milk and water. I was a baker (not a counter kid) so I made the pudding day in and day out. Though to give due credit, it was the dishwashers who would make the pudding base (instant pudding mix w water and canned sweetened milk). I’m a purist from- scratch baker, but I think this banana pudding is absolutely delicious. Don’t knock it till you try it!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: And, not to mention the fact that Magnolia Bakery’s own website lists the ingredients as—wait for it—"Heavy Cream, Sweetened Condensed Milk, Vanilla Instant Pudding, Water, Vanilla Wafers, Bananas."

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: What a fun recipe for a crowd. I added 1/2 tsp salt to the condensed milk mixture and about a teaspoon of vanilla to the pudding. After putting the pudding together, I took the leftover wafers and toasted them (one layer at 375 for about 10 minutes), broke them up roughly and mixed with roasted pecans to make a crunchy topping for people to serve themselves. The toasted wafers were so good that the next time I make this, I will toast all the wafers first.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Decades ago, I worked as a pastry chef at a popular local restaurant. The Boston Cream Cake was much loved. I used a package of instant vanilla pudding made with heavy cream as the filling. It was the perfect texture and taste. No one ever suspected this “secret” ingredient!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: This is the banana pudding I grew up with and it is delicious. I don't eat a lot of sweets and the sound of banana pudding (or pudding in general) never sounds all that appealing, but then I take a bite and am amazed I don't dream about it - it's so good! And a hand mixer works just fine.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: After reading in the notes that some found the recipe too sweet, I halved the recipe and made 1/2 with condensed milk and the other with whole milk, per Clark’s note. The whole milk version had a thicker more velvety texture. Both were delicious however all were in agreement that the less sweet version without the condensed milk was yummier.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Double the bananas! This is delicious and comforting in all the right ways!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
Two things I learned shopping:  Pay close attention to Jello Instant vs Cook And Serve pudding - I accidentally bought the cook and serve.

Also, at Kroger-brand stores (QFC, Fred Meyer, etc), Nilla Wafers brand boxes aren't actually the real thing.  They are an artifically flavored, stale imitation.  Had to toss them and get the real thing (made by Nabisco) at Safeway

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I have noticed a difference in the texture of whipped egg whites and whipped cream when I use my Kitchenaid stand mixer vs. my Kitchenaid hand mixture.  I get more lift from the stand mixture.  So it does make a difference but if you only have a hand I am sure it will work fine.  I make the recipe on the box of Nilla vanilla wafers and it’s terrific too.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I added a tablespoon of rum and extra vanilla. I also used only one cup of cream (all I had) and substituted one cup of sour cream instead. It didn’t taste sour but added an extra dimension to the pudding. I recommend it!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I made this pudding exactly as instructed and it came out amazing. We ate some right after the setting period as well as a day later; both were delicious, but we found the day-old pudding even tastier and creamier. I read it wouldn’t freeze well, but figured there was no harm in trying - we froze some in small mason jars and they actually thawed really well and were still delicious.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: can't believe snootys here.  who cares if a mix is used. i've been a professional cook for 20 years. can make puff pastry to melt your heart. BUT, i'm not snooty enough to forego using frozen puff paste, or a box cake mix. oof, my timer just dinged. TIME TO TAKE OUT MY BOXED BROWNIES FROM THE OVEN

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: For anyone playing along in Australia: I made this using 250g sponge finger biscuits (like for tiramisu), Cottee’s instant pudding mix, a 395mL tin of condensed milk and 600mL thickened cream. The products were slightly different quantities to the recipe (the biggest difference being the cream, where the local product was 100mL shy of 3 US cups), but it turned out great. If making again, I’d refrigerate it for longer as the sponge biscuits didn’t get entirely moist and cakey in 5 hours.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I make this a couple times a year since it is my daughters favorite dessert.  However, I have found it is much too sweet when made as directed and so I use about 2-2.5x the whipped cream and it tasted SOOO much better. Interestingly we went to magnolia the last time we were in nyc and bought the pudding, neither of us could get past a couple of bites since it just tasted soo overly sweet after having had the version I make at home.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Used a hand held mixer and it came out exactly as described. Absolutely wonderful. I’ve had the original and this tastes exactly the same. Super easy great dessert.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: The pudding is way too sweet. I may throw it out. The flavor is disgusting and is reminiscent of trashy desserts from a supermarket. I’d rather make (better quality) pudding and add some bananas.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I make this exact recipe every year for Christmas—it’s become a family tradition. It isn’t a light & airy as Magnolia’s, but it’s pretty close. There are never any leftovers. It is a delightful dessert. A hand mixer does just as well as a stand mixer, from my experience.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
I love real vanilla, but here the taste of artificial vanilla just seems right.
A couple of years ago I tried to make a Boston Cream pie that tasted just like ones I’d eaten in the past, and though my final version was good there was something about it that just didn’t taste right.
I’d used real vanilla.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I make this recipe only I use sour cream instead of sweetened condensed milk. And no water. Follow directions on the pudding box. It is so much better with the sour cream. Not quite as sweet. Trust me.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: As tasty as this is, nothing is as good as the original recipe from the back of the Nilla wafer box

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
I split this between one dish with bananas and without and, I preferred the one without. 

I added a tablespoon of vanilla to the whipped cream & used cold milk instead of cold water because with all the dairy, it made sense. 

This concoction gives the pudding a rich & creamy mouthfeel unlike any other pudding. The vanilla wafers give it a depth of crunch and adds great stability to the dish.  I'm eager to try it out on other pudding flavors.

Warning: it's super filling gut buster of a desert

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: This pudding is fantastic.  And anyone who likes banana pudding is likely to say this is the best banana pudding they've ever eaten!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Yes Jane that would work just fine. A handheld mixer is the same as a stand mixer in its output (just more tedious for the user).

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: This only passes as banana pudding to people who did not grow up eating scratch-made vanilla pudding layered with Nilla wafers and just-ripe bananas. Once you have that taste imprinted, this will come off as horribly artificial. No doubt quicker to pull together though!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
The popularity of this recipe, especially the use of instant vanilla pudding mix, baffles me. It's a cheap fake of the classically divine southern dessert made by cooking up some genuine vanilla pastry cream using fresh eggs, whole milk and heavy cream, and preferably a vanilla bean. A billowy topping of toasted meringue seals the deal. 
I'm appalled by instant pudding mix and its abhorrent, chemical vanilla flavor with hints of petroleum. 
Make the real deal-follow Shirley Corriher's recipe.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: @Mlynn hi I googled Shirley and couldn’t find any recipe for banana pudding

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: @Mlynn as a southerner, I’ve made the original scratch version since I was about ten years old. I never ever considered using chemical-tasting boxed pudding mix (and certainly not banana-flavored - horrors!) but made the oh so simple vanilla custard that was always printed on the vanilla wafers box with the required baked meringue topping, using the whites from the pudding yolks. Every other southern cook I knew did the same. I recently stopped in at a Magnolia Bakery and tried theirs, and it was such an overly sweet disappointment with poor consistency and hardly any vanilla wafers (or whatever they use).

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: It's a great recipe - made it several times to rave reviews. I've swapped out and used strawberries and Oreos to change it up.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I do not believe for a second that this is actually the recipe they use at the bakery! It is super heavy/fatty feeling, and way, way too sweet!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Julia Child's standard pie pastry used Crisco along with butter. If she could open her mind to various ingredient possibilities, I think the rest of us can.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I finally made this and admit that’s it’s excellent. My only regret is that I don’t have a magnolia bakery near me so I can just buy the amount I need. This recipe can easily serve 10 a lot for my household of two. I think though that I prefer the version made with an egg custard or to use this recipe in a pie version

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
I literally got a marriage proposal from this recipe today!

I said no.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: This was very good. I had no problem with soupy. I halved the recipe and it still was plenty. Perhaps six or eight servings? The only thing I would change if anything, is to add additional vanilla. Wonderful.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I’ve never understood the hype around Magnolia’s banana pudding. It has a bad after taste because of the instant mix made in large batches. A true banana pudding is made with a homemade stove top custard, not an instant mix!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: My apologies but I have to chime in with those saying they make it the old fashioned way. Eggs are separated, yolks are cooked with milk, sugar and vanilla and the whites make a meringue that is browned in to oven. I love banana pudding but never order it in restaurants due to the overly sweet prefab taste.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Not an artificial vanilla fan- no judgement, I eat canned cheese. I used Jello instant lemon pudding(leftover from my 92-year-old  MIL's annual holiday lemon sour cream coffee cake baking spree-another  delicious  pudding mix recipe),Biscoff cookies(per another's  suggestion), ditched the condensed milk,and made pudding according to the box. Added a hefty amount of vanilla and rum.  It is a good recipe to riff upon, if one chooses. No shame in the instant pudding game.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
This dessert is death by cream and death by sugar. With banana as a vitamin excuse. It has no subtlety whatsoever. After a few bites we were completely bloated. 
Serving it with yoghurt made it more palatable. 
I think I knew in advance reading the recipe, but as NYT was promoting it, I thought it might not be as bad. I was wrong.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I read through the comments and skipped the condensed milk and used the cooked instead of instant pudding. I made it for a friend's birthday party. She's from the south and it brought tears to her eyes. She also ate three servings! Two of my guests couldn't tolerate dairy so I made a vegan version for them with coconut oil whipped cream.They nearly cried too as they hadn't had banana pudding since they were kids. Great recipe!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I’ve been making this recipe for at least a decade and it’s a definite crowd pleaser!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I have had Magnolia's banana pudding many times and enjoyed it. It's too sweet but the occasional small serving with a black coffee can be lovely. I am surprised they'd release the recipe to reveal it as a pedestrian assemblage of cheap, artificial ingredients. This is not a crime, but the prices they charge might be. Further still, there's nothing particularly precious going on here.  Tinker with abandon. Maybe cool whip is better than whipping cream yourself. Try adding more bananas.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I have made this many times as it’s my husbands favorite.  I have a neighbor who flambés his bananas. I still like mine better.   I made my own banana extract and add a little to this.  It’s wonderful.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: A significant contribution to American culture. Jaw dropping and delicious dessert! Followed the recipe to a T.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: The pudding was a total flop- way too runny, never setting in the fridge. Chucked it and bought another box of instant vanilla and made per box instructions. Perfection- just the way pudding should have been made. Otherwise, the rest of the recipe went smoothly. My family just loved it! So rich and delicious! Good for a crowd.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: My dad loved banana pudding-when he had cancer in '99 he needed all the calories we could stuff in him, and this is the way I made banana pudding. It was decadent-so good. He didn't eat much but he did eat it. Nothing wrong with using a mix!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: My husband is from Mississippi, and he loved banana pudding as a kid.  I made this as written, and it was so cloyingly sweet, I ended up throwing it out.  Gag.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Thought it was ok. Why not use banana pudding mix if you’re gonna call it banana pudding? I’d consider this more of a parfait considering you’re just layering fruit in with some flavored whipped cream - and that is a LOT of whip cream for a parfait. Worth trying but probably won’t make again.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Nope. This is just too fluffy. I much prefer Sam Sifton’s version.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: The pudding sat overnight in the fridge, covered but never thickened. It was very watery still. I’m going to try again with the package instructions and use milk.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Oh, this was soooo good. I mixed a few suggestions from here on mine, including using sour cream in place of some heavy cream (I had run out...), plus a 1/2 t of salt to mitigate the sweetness of the condensed milk. It was delicious, and will be made again.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: IF you have no means by which you can whip the cream (house packed up, alas), I used a fork for about 10 minutes then gave up and just mixed the cream into the cold and set pudding mixture (with the sweetened condensed milk and water) AND it came out just divine.  Silky.  So, if you can't whip, just mix.  All good.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: While the magnolia Banana pudding is tasty. Do yourself a favor go to the grocery store buy a box of vanilla wafers cook the recipe on the side of the box, it’s the best! Sometime for variation add strawberries with the bananas!!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I have an allergy to dairy and gluten, but always wanted to know what this tasted like since it is a HUGE hit in my family, so I replaced the condensed milk with condensed oat milk, heavy cream/whipped cream with a homemade coconut whipped cream, and used Kinnikinnick GF vanilla wafers and WOW! It was heavenly! I know I can't accurately judge because of all the replacements for crucial ingredients, but this truly was something special!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Did the wafers soften enough? I’ve read it’s necessary to dip GF cookies in a liquid before using in an icebox cake, because they don’t soften as readily as wheat flour cookies do.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
Served for Xmas dinner dessert. I don't normally use mixes and chose this recipe for nostalgia and ease. 
Positive: Easy to make
Negative: Taste was underwhelming. Just tasted like a big bowl of whipped cream. Very little banana flavor-or pudding flavor eventt hough I did add vanilla extract per others comments. Nothing like what I remember Magnolia Bakery Banana Pudding tastes like. Should have used more bananas, less cream and definitely add more vanilla.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I  agree. Much ado about nothing.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I made this recipe as written with the exception of how I included the Vanilla Wafers. Instead of placing the cookies in whole, I crushed them and sprinkled the crumbs as layers. Having eaten the pudding from Magnolia Bakery several times where the cookies are placed in whole, my family said they preferred it the way I did it where the cookies were crumbled. I put the pudding in individual ramakins and the crumbs made it easier to eat while also giving it a nice texture.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
I use the Jello banana cream pudding mix and replace 1/4 c. of the ice water with bourbon.
People lose their minds over this dessert, it's so good.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: This is not a recipe. This is putting cookies in a bowl of pudding.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
I made this recipe. It was fairly mediocre. 

If you desire true delicious banana pudding, head to the South, where it originated and where even middling cooks know how to whip up a delicious batch.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Here in New York, we have busy cooks. While they may even be middling, they don't have time to whip up every batch from scratch.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: For those saying the pudding is too sweet:  it needs to be too sweet to balance w/the unsweetened whipped cream. When you add water to the sweetened condensed milk,  you are reconstituting the milk. So you end up with sweet milk. The heavy cream isn't sweetened at all so when you combine them it's perfect. It's no different than adding pudding made with milk to store bought whipped topping like Cool Whip,  etc.  If you taste the pudding before you add to the whipped cream, it will be too sweet.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: The pudding was so watery and never set overnight. Did anyone else have this problem? Going back to the store and buying another box of vanilla and this time using the package instructions. Milk with pudding and that’s it.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I like to quickly dip the cookies in either rum or espresso if I'm feeling spicy.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I was worried about it being too sweet, so based on another comment, doubled the whipped cream. Not sure that was the right thing to do...however, I did make myself a bit sick licking the spatula and bowl after assembling the concoction. So take that for what it's worth...

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I never liked banana pudding due to the vanilla wafers.  Last night was a friend's birthday and his favorite dessert is banana pudding, so I found this recipe and the suggestion to use biscoff cookies instead of the traditional vanilla wafers. It was a huge hit and easily fed 14 people with several going back for second helpings.  One of his gifts was a bottle of high end rum.  A few folks drizzled the rum over their second helping which was a great addition.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Made this for Christmas Eve dinner dessert. Doubled the recipe. Added chopped walnuts to the banana and wafer layers. Also lined the sides of the trifle bowl with bananas and wafers. As an amazingly delicious extra I made a bananas foster sauce with brown sugar, butter, cinnamon and vanilla. Lightly coated the sliced bananas with it. Used crushed wafers and chopped walnuts as the topping and drizzled with the remaining bananas foster sauce. It was absolutely incredible. My guests raved about it.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Yumm! I love banana's foster!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Mash up a ripe banana and add it to the pudding. It gives it that “from scratch” flavor.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I’ve made this recipe at least four times. For family, for parties, and just now for a potluck. People *love* it. My advice: halve the recipe. Really. It’s perfect as is (or with gluten free cookies if you need to), it’s just always too much. Next time, I’m dividing the pudding into two, and going from there.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: instead of thinning out the condensed milk with water, i thinned w/ evaporated milk and used more evaporated than condensed! added vanilla extract to whipped cream and a smidge of flakey salt. so good. the salt is a must to balance everything out, trust me

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I tried it using whole milk as per the pudding instructions and it turned out too milky. Maybe it just got over whipped, but the pudding part didn’t seem to taste of anything but milk. Even adding in a table spoon and a half of vanilla extract to the pudding and a splash in the whipping cream, it still was very faint. Next time I would stick with the recipe and use the water and condensed milk mixture for the pudding.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: When I read a recipe and it has a can of something, a box of something and a packet of instant something, it’s a huge turn-off. I like cooking, not assembling pre-marde processed stuff.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
Things I changed: I used 2/3 of condensed milk, I added a bit of vanilla, I soaked the wafers in milk while assembling (just because I made it the same day of
The dinner and I didn’t want the cookies to be crunchy) I sprinkled crushed biscoff on top when serving. I got comments like “this is better than Mangnolia’s, its less sweet” i guess because I didn’t put the whole Can of
Condensed milk.  You really need to let it sit on the fridge for 6 hours, it makes a difference.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
For all of the rest of us who don’t care about banana pudding, I keep being surprised how frequently the food app republishes this dessert. 

I detest instant pudding, so there’s no way I’d try this anyway. I can’t get past it to be willing to try this recipe. 

I just don’t get why it keeps coming up.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: 4,450+ reviews is why it keeps coming up. I love instant pudding: Hershey white chocolate is my favorite. I make it several times a month.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Banana pudding made with instant pudding mix instead of home made vanilla pudding stirred in a double boiler? “you cannot be serious”.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: For those that say it’s too sweet. Did you make it? 3 cups of cream really cuts the sweetness. It feels decadent but not sweet (I’ve only tasted the filling) waiting for the dessert to chill for my Galentine’s evening event !

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Edit for my post above. My guests absolutely loved the pudding and took cups of it home. This is a keeper

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
We’re a bit old fashioned in our household and my hubby insists we make it all from scratch. The bananas, cream and pudding are straight forward but I want to share my secret hand written nannas recipe for nilla wafers here for anyone wanting to go the extra mile.

Preheat oven to 350°F (175°C). In a bowl, mix 1 ½ cups enriched wheat flour, ½ cup granulated sugar, ¼ cup corn syrup solids, ½ tsp ammonium bicarbonate, ½ tsp baking soda, ½ tsp salt, and ¼ tsp xanthan gum. In a saucepan, melt ¼ cup hydrogenated palm oil   2 tbsp soybean oil, then stir in 1 tsp soy lecithin, ¼ tsp mono- and diglycerides. In a small bowl, dissolve 1 tbsp dextrose, 1 tbsp high fructose corn syrup, and 1 tbsp artificial vanilla flavor in 1 tbsp water. Combine everything into a dough. Roll thin, cut into small rounds, and bake for 10-12 minutes. Cool completely.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I swapped the nilla wafers for biscoff and added cinnamon and a half ounce of spiced rum YUM

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Just made this with gf cookies and froze it (after letting it sit in the fridge overnight). It’s literally blue bell ice cream. I felt it was pretty sweet at fridge temp so figured it would be good as ice cream and it definitely works… oh my god I’m gonna eat this whole thing 😂

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Thank you for this comment! Made it at the end of the day half brain dead for two people today - was shocked when I realized how big it was so realizing I can freeze it and turn into ice cream is huge. Can't wait!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Made as directed - was not too sweet, huge hit.  Served in individual glass jars.  This is now my go to banana pudding recipe!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: The pudding part tastes like weakly flavored whipped cream. I made a homemade vanilla pudding in less than 10 minutes. Blind tasting by friends and family confirmed that the homemade version was better. The uncooked cornstarch in this recipe, which acts as a stabilizer to the whipped cream so it stays whipped, left a floury taste. Avoid uncooked cornstarch as a stabilizer. I use instant Clear Jel, which is modified cornstarch that prevents cloudiness and doesn’t taste floury.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Instant pudding mix? Seriously? Why not just pick up something in the prepared foods section?

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I've made this twice exactly as written and it's a perfect dessert. Today I went rogue by not whipping the cream and instead, I put everything into an ice-cream maker. I added the Nilla Wafers in the last minute. Divinely indulgent!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I make this all the time from Jennifer Appel’s book and never use a electric mixer of any kind. You don’t need it.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I want to make this for my mom‘s 80th birthday but she was recently diagnosed with pre-diabetes. I’m wondering what it would taste like if I used sugar, free cookies, pudding, sweetener? Or not my ideal but there are so few foods she can/will eat, and this might be a nice treat.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: you'll never know until you try it.  If she's accustomed to artificial sweeteners, then she might enjoy it with the sugar-free version.  Give it a whirl.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: A fair amount of the sweetness comes from sweetened condensed milk. There are a few recipes out there for condensed milk, which use various alternative sweeteners. This is one. https://thebigmansworld.com/sugar-free-condensed-milk/. Evaporated milk might work, but it isn’t as thick. You can leave the cookies out altogether and use more bananas instead. Or your mom can treat it as a special occasion, and just eat a small portion.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: There is a lot of sugar in the sweetened condensed milk, too. But if you take an after dinner walk that will offset it!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: This is a winner every time, even that time we used Trader Joe’s chocolate Cat Cookies instead of Nilla’s.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
My hand-mixer broke, so I whipped the cream by hand. In fact, the entire recipe can be made without an electric mixer 

BTW, This stuff is divine!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Perfection! Tooth rattlingly sweet and just like it tastes at the store. What’s not to love?

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Diane wrote almost exactly what I was about to mention (you said it, sister!).  I always keep boxes of Nilla wafers in my home overseas, - along with the other dry ingredients in this recipe - for a last-minute "emergency dessert" with sliced frozen bananas.  (yes I'm getting gnarly!). Seeing "my" recipe here - particularly noting that it was not altered from it's 1950's pedigree - I smiled.   It is hard to not tweak this recipe to make it "better",...but seriously don't. It's always a pleaser.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: First had this from Magnolia Bakery in Grand Central. I was smitten. I don't usually go for the processed stuff (condensed milk, instant pudding, wafers), but this is always an easy, impressive, and well-loved hit for large gatherings.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I have been making this recipe for years. I actually use 2 packages of vanilla pudding as that gives the pudding a bit more flavor in my opinion. Otherwise it seems a bit overwhelmed by the whipped cream.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I have this recipe, but it's a bit different and it the best thing ever! Mine uses 1/2C of sweetened condensed milk, 1 3/4C milk, and 3/4C powdered sugar and 3 bananas. Everything else is the same and the same instructions. Love my recipe.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Is the 1 3/4 cup of milk instead of the ice water?

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Thanks to those folks who pointed out that a hand mixer is adequate for making this recipe. I don’t bake nor cook enough complex recipes to justify a stand mixer and am perplexed when a recipe begins with those words. My next question is about the quantity here. It makes more servings than we would ever use. Not sure if it’s possible to pare this one down for just 4 or six servings. Any thoughts?

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Karen - you ask about the possibility of scaling this down to serve 4 or 6 instead of the 12 to 16 the recipes says it will feed.  I think this would be very easy to reduce by 50%.  Just cut the water, condensed milk and cream volume measurements in half.  Then use your kitchen scale for gram weights for the pudding mix and cookies to reduce those by half.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
I appreciate Diane’s comment that love is the most important ingredient, so thank you for that. 

I detest instant pudding, so it bums me out that two recipes have shown up in this app recently that feature it as an ingredient. It is NOT my nostalgia food. Ho hum, I’ll have to come back another day.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I, too, do not like instant pudding WHEN MADE AS PUDDING. But I have another recipe that uses it in combination with other ingredients and there is no unpleasant raw or grainy bits. Given the relatively affordable cost of these ingredients, it might be worth a try. Good luck.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Never use boxed commercial vanilla wafers! They are so easy to bake from scratch people and will make a world of difference in the end, trust me.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Can you recommend a good recipe for vanilla wafers?

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Jill, A good substitute for vanilla wafers are all-butter shortbread cookies. You can use Walker shortbread or Pepperidge Farm chessman or Dublin cookies. All of the them are basic all-butter shortbread.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Love this recipe! Next time, though, I will throw in more banana and a fun twist that my grandpa used to do with his banana pudding: a layer of creamy peanut butter sandwiched between the wafers and then layered into the pudding. Delicious!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: This is a variation on the classic banana pudding of my youth. I made it and it came together much more quickly than the scratch version I usually make, so I will save this for when I am in a hurry. (I mashed an extra banana and mixed it into the pudding.) Someone suggested using whipped evaporated milk instead of whipped cream. Sorry, but nothing beats whipped cream! The store was out of Nilla wafers, so I used the Trader Joe’s version, and they were delicious.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: While I’m sure this is delicious, it’s never going to be “best” using instant pudding and sweetened condensed milk, sorry not sorry. The best banana pudding is made entirely from scratch other than Nilla Wafers (or making your own shortbread for it). I only ever use Patty Pinner’s recipe from her “Sweets” cookbook.  Take the time to actually cook a pudding stovetop from scratch and thank me later.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: My goodness.  You get the Sententious of The Year Award and it's only January.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I agree with you. It takes 5 minutes to make a delicious pudding from scratch. And having made banana pudding following this recipe and just making a regular vanilla pudding, the latter is better. This pudding is basically flavored whipped cream.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I felt the heavy cream made it so much blander in flavor- just felt like way too much of it.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: My immune system goes haywire (yes, it's diagnosed) with artificial vanilla and instant pudding. No judgement against people with normal bodies. I can make pudding, but what do you substitute for the wafers?

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Not sure what I did wrong, but the condensed milk mixed with cold water and the vanilla pudding mix never set. I kept it covered and refrigerated overnight.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Shared some with a friend who just texted me saying, “I’m not joking when I say that pudding would be part of my last meal on earth.” So yeah it was pretty good.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Yes, please share your cookie recipe, Diane :)

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I am certainly not a banana pudding connoisseur or big fan but was interested in the addition of whipped cream with instant vanilla pudding. however I found it rather insipid compared with traditional cooked puddings. family members loved it.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: This was delicious but made too much for just me, so I froze the leftover in a casserole dish. It is delicious as a banana-cookie ice cream.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Sorting through the notes and wanting a smaller batch, I used half the can of sweetened condensed milk, which is easily hand whisked in a bowl with the pudding, 2 cups of heavy cream and 2-3 large bananas. It was a total hit. It does need about 4 hrs to set and get adequate banana flavor.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I second Diane’s note from 2 weeks ago, as I bake from scratch..best ingredients..experienced cook and baker, etc.  Instant pudding has no bad ingredients and I fondly remember banana cream pie from childhood, so I needed to try the recipe.  My adult family loved it.  Made  with whole milk instead of condensed milk and layered with extra banana.  Delicious and perfect sweetness.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: It looks similar to Paula Deen's "Not Your Momma's Banana Pudding", except different cookies (Chessmen)and cream cheese instead of heavy cream. Wonderful!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Made with GF vanilla wafers and it turned out delicious! Everyone loved it! Will definitely make again!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: This recipe turned out really good! I followed the advice in the notes to make pudding using regular milk instead of condensed milk/water. It would gave been way too sweet with the condensed milk. I also substituted heavy cream with half and half. Pudding was delicious and enjoyed by everyone, individual portions are the best IMO. My husband thinks it's a perfect breakfast too 🤔

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Hi! We tried to make this but the pudding/ice water/condensed milk mixture never firmed. Any ideas for the future?

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Probly too much water.  Just make the pudding as per instructions on the box, and add the whipped cream once it is set.  Less sweet and much better.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: This recipe is excellent.  I'm not sure why people are complaining about the sweetened condensed milk and pudding making it too sweet- it's definitely not overly sweet.  I typically categorize bananas as "baby food", but this recipe is to die for!  The texture, flavor and sweetness is all perfect as written!  It's also super easy.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Just served this to a bunch of picky kids and discerning adults, everyone loved it. Was so easy (and inexpensive!) to make and received very solid reviews. 10/10 would make again.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I could not get the pudding to set! I tried but it was soupy. I’d recommend (as others have here in the comments) to make the pudding section as per the package instructions. Otherwise I’ll stick to Sam Sifton’s banana pudding, which is my family’s favorite!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Adding merinque and baking to brown changes the texture of the bananas.  For my tastes the meringue is not a positive addition to a great Southern dessert

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: This is a fantastic recipe, though I agree with others here that the use of sweetened condensed milk makes it crazy-sweet, and following the pudding box instructions and using regular whole milk results in a dessert that is still plenty sweet and delicious.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I followed the advice of the Magnolia Bakery baker and used the instructions on the box of instant pudding (i.e., no condensed milk/water/sugar, just whole milk) and it was wonderful.  Have ordered and enjoyed the Magnolia Bakery product many times, and this NYT recipe is every bit as good.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I loved this recipe! I noticed a lot of people saying it was too sweet but I made it exactly as directed and I really disagree with that, I thought it was perfect. Incredibly easy too. I used the classic nilla wafers and if I make it again I think the only thing I’ll change is using a bigger cookie, but it was still really good. I made this for my family and everyone loved it (ages ranged from 2 to 69) :) thank you Priya!!!!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I promise you this is just as delicious without the sweetened condensed milk.  Just make the pudding as described but with milk, not water. Fold in the whipped cream etc…Let’s the creamy banana flavor shine.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: We love Magnolia's banana pudding...so were happy to try this and definitely (after 4 efforts) feel extra bananas and extra nilla wafers make this extra tasty!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: MC - Magnolia ships this pudding frozen. It definitely freezes well.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I don’t understand all the griping and controversy in the comments. The first time I made this pudding I followed the author’s instructions and the result was delightful. Each spoonful delivered a gorgeous cloud of flavor and texture. It’s a classic.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: This turned out great! I was worried after the pudding mix step because the flavor is so artificial. However, after adding the whipped cream (I only used two cups), the flavor was right. I used “tea biscuits” instead of nilla wafers because that was the closest thing I could find in Quebec.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: If you must use sweetened condensed milk, use the fat free version— not for calorie counting, but because it tastes less sweet than the regular condensed milk.   But pudding made with whole milk is better and produces an airier dessert.  Layering with shortbread cookies like Pepperidge Farm’s chessmen butter cookies (in lieu of vanilla wafers) gives this dish an extra star.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Not sure why the word "pudding" appears in the name. To me this was lightly banana-flavored whipped cream. I followed the recipe to the letter but was very disappointed. Maybe I did something wrong, but I don't know what it would be. If you want a bowl of whipped cream for dessert, this is for you.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I agree. It tasted like mildly flavored and overly sweet whipped cream. Why bother when a basic vanilla pudding can be made from scratch in 5 minutes and tastes so much better?

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: This is okay, but not as delicious as Maida Heatter's banana pudding.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: The best recipe for banana pudding came from the original Nilla Wafers (before the company changed their recipe for the cookie) and incorporated a cooked from scratch custard, not the instant pudding.  Then it was topped with a meringue and baked to brown the meringue swirls.  The instant pudding recipe with today's version of vanilla wafers is nothing close to the original.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: The first time I made this as written and thought it came out tasting like lightly flavored whipped cream. This time I used milk instead of water, banana cream flavored instant pudding, and only 2 cups of heavy cream with an added tsp of vanilla (I had artificial and real and used artificial for this recipe). We preferred it this way so I’ll continue making it with these adjustments. Easy enough recipe for my 4 year old to help make!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Hi all. I made this recently for a bring-a-dish event and got some good reviews. One woman told me this was exactly the way her mother made it. I also receive critical points from good friends and fellow foodies, and I agree. Too much whipped cream and not enough bananas. There was hardly any vanilla pudding taste, and it was really, really sweet. Next time, I'll cut the sweetened condensed milk and heavy cream in half and double the bananas. Just my personal preferences. But it's worth trying!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I think the texture and taste is better following box pudding instructions. Skip the condensed milk.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I made this with  the sweetened condensed milk and whipped cream as instructed, just too rich and sweet.  Of course we couldn't stop eating it, but everyone had a stomach ache afterwards.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I'm not surprised it was rich. A friend of mine gave me a recipe to make banana ice cream and you don't need an ice cream maker. He used bananas that were very ripe, some almond butter and maple syrup and it was nice. I'm gonna make some myself.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Made this and it was delicious but a little soupy.  So I ran it through my ice cream maker without the cookies and it was absolutely divine.  Topped with roasted graham cracker crumbs (baked with butter and sugar).

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Sub Pepperidge Farm's Chessmen butter cookies in lieu of Nilla wafers to take this over the top.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
I really can't stand recipes with instant pudding.  Did anyone ever read the ingredient list? 

Sodium Phosphate, Tetrasodium Pyrophosphate, Salt, Mono- and Diglycerides, Artificial Color, Natural and Artificial Flavor, Yellow 5, Yellow 6.

It is pretty easy to make cooked pudding.  I would not follow this recipe.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Use whole milk, not condensed. Coat banana w lemon juice

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: The comments here are too funny.  If you don't want to use mixes, don't make the recipe!  I also am a mix skeptic but followed the recipe exactly (although cut in half). This was absolutely delicious.   Period.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I made this as directed and it is amazing but it made a lot for just 3 people so I froze the majority of it and it's still really good! Maybe more of a frozen desert vs. pudding but still a good option if you can't eat it all at once.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Made this several times, and agreed with many it was too sweet. The simple swap of heavy cream for condensed milk did the trick. While I love from scratch recipes, if you’re pinched for time or equipment (say you’re camping), you can use pre-made vanilla pudding and premade whipped cream. Heresy I know, but it works when you’re in the middle of nowhere with nothing but a cooler and a single propane burner.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: This dish is EXCELLENT as written. Let it sit so the cookies get soft - that is the key!! Also I used a bit more banana than called for. YUM.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: This is the EXACT recipe from the Magnolia Bakery Cookbook.  So unless they're lying, this is how it's made.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: NYT-s sometimes you get it right and sometimes you don't. Nothing iconic or special about this recipe. It's too sweet and Magnolia Bakery acts like this is something special. Ripe bananas, vanilla pudding, vanilla wafers, heavy cream- done.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I made this recently with vegan ingredients. Husband is vegan for medical reasons and I am severely lactose intolerant. I used condensed oat milk and Silk brand heavy cream. The vanilla wafers from Whole Foods are vegan. Turned out wonderfully! Really creamy and delicious.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I made this dish for a cookout at my parents' house.  I chose this recipe because it's a cinch to throw together without a lot of fancy ingredients that might be challenging to find in my parents' rural community.  I, too, am a "scratch or die" baker (thanks, Diane!) and the idea of using a packaged pudding mix meant that my expectations stayed pretty low, but this pudding took me back to happy days as a little kid eating "nanner puddin'" at church potlucks.  It was perfect!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Highly recommend throwing leftovers in an ice cream maker

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I love this recipe but feel that there is too much whipped cream which I felt lightened the pudding texture too much. It was light and airy rather than a denser pudding consistency. At least for me.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I have not, and will not, be making this. Any banana pudding that starts off with a box of pudding mix does not taste like real banana pudding. Trust me, I've had a lot of short-cut banana puddings made with pudding mix and they just don't cut it.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Trust me, it's your loss. I'm an unprocessed food / scratch baker and I couldn't stop eating this.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I made this for our warming center when it was -24 degrees (-55 with wind chill) and despite it being a cold dessert, I saw mouths hold bites in their mouths and savor it. It’s good and a soul warmer with the blended whipped cream. I highly recommend this. We served this with Kim Severson’s Red Beans and Rice recipe.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
I followed the suggestions of some of the previous commenters and mixed the vanilla instant pudding mix with whole milk (per the box's recipe). 
The pudding turned out delicious, but I would certainly recommend letting the assembled pudding sit overnight so that the wafers can get soft. I tested the pudding right after assembling and it was delicious, but after a night of absorbing moisture in the fridge, the wafers were immaculate. Will absolutely be making again.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: A big sweet and the assembly and wait limit usefulness in my house. Flavor is good but I don’t expect repeat. The King Arthur banana muffin is on repeat here

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I substitute the vanilla wafers for pecan sandies or traditional shortbread cookies. I find it cuts the overall sweetness and gives a better body to the pudding.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I have bought too much at their Westside store to admit and made it here at home with great results.  Earlier this month we were in town for the weekend and I bought an over priced frozen pint at LGA on the way home. It was just not the same, with no discernible cookies and much less banana flavor/ texture. Turns out, the frozen pints are made with banana puree. Yuck. I prefer slightly crunchy identifiable cookies and visible slices of banana.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: If you have some left around the holidays, substitute egg nog for the heavy cream.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Just follow the directions on the back of the Nilla wafers box and you get the nice meringue topping. Adding sweetened condensed milk isn’t necessary.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Paula dean has a recipe on the food network called Not Yo mama’s Banana Pudding. It uses pepridge farms Chessman cookies instead of vanilla wafers. Delicious.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Lesson learned from my Georgia mother-in-law:  If you use the "cooked" pudding mix, you have the advantage of being able to make the pudding (with just regular milk, per the pudding box instructions), layer it with the wafers and bananas ("one day past ripe"), let it set a while until the wafers get soggy, then eat some of it while it's still WARM.  Top it off with chopped pecans and whipped cream, OR do a meringue if you're feeling ambitious.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: The only thing I ever learned to make from my Southern Granny was Banana Pudding. Making pudding from scratch is super easy- just milk, egg yolks, sugar, a pinch of salt and vanilla extract simmered in a saucepan, then a few heaping wooden spoons of flour to thicken- then poured over the vanilla wafers and bananas layered into the bowl. You use the egg whites to make the meringue. I’d never ever use boxed pudding mix. However I do use premade vanilla wafers just like my Granny did.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: My son makes it with Pepperidge Farm Milano cookies. NEXT LEVEL!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: “Refrigerate until firm, at least one hour, or overnight” was misleading for me. 3 hours later the pudding was nowhere near firm. This is probably best started the day prior with the refrigeration happening overnight. Sigh.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Way too sweet. I could not eat more than two bites. I STRONGLY suggest making the pudding as directed. Leave out the condensed milk.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Just made using whole milk for pudding per package instructions and two cups (not three) of heavy cream. Delicious and no issues. I think three cups of cream would have diluted the pudding too much; as it is there’s a very modest pudding flavor  — mostly general lusciousness. Used Trader Joe’s Speculoos cookies (two packages), which gave a nice bit of spice.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I made a vegan version using coconut sweetened condensed milk, vegan heavy cream, vegan pudding mix and vegan cookies and wow it was incredible. I followed the recipe exactly using the vegan alternatives and it turned out perfectly.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Pudding mix remained liquid after 12 hours.  Used Jello brand, which was only 3 oz.  Heated with added cornstarch to thicken.  Did not have Nilla wafers.  Used ginger snaps which was a close as I had.  Crumbled graham crackers on top.  Result was okay.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I had some leftover salted caramel and drizzled it over the cookies between layers, otherwise I made it as instructed. It's absolutely delicious! I did not find the sweetened condensed milk to be too much. Personally, I think eating it about 24 hours after assembly is even better than 5 hours later- the bananas really have a chance to work their flavor through the whole dessert!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: No, just no. The original banana pudding is the best. This is a poor imitation of an excellent dessert, it is too sweet and not enough banana or wafers.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I have the same recipe as Diane, though one of my favorites starts with brownie mix. It’s supposed to have chocolate chips and raisins, but I don’t like raisins.  I do, however, love cranberries, so I swap that. Some white chocolate chips adds complexity to the flavors.  I also bake mostly from scratch, but when I put together 2000 cookies in a few weekends between thanksgiving and Christmas, the shortcut recipes help me out a bit, along with the tried and true, totally from scratch ones.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Banana Pudding was my dad’s favorite. My mom would put a merengue on top of it sometimes, with caramelized peaks…beautiful…

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Did this for husband’s birthday. Used only milk, not sweetened condensed. My husband, who has a huge sweet tooth said “I like that it’s not too sweet”. Felt like banana flavor came through better. I, who does not like anything puddings-y sans chocolate, thought this was heaven. Also did with GF vanilla wafers, worked fine.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: My favorite treat at the West Hollywood Magnolia Bakery! Jello instant vanilla pudding mix? Wow.
