author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Boxed pudding mix and boxed cookies. Chemical aftertaste and not actual cooking.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Anyway to convert this recipe to Vegan?

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Use Vegan ingredients!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: This is a recipe from the Eagle Brand cookbook. I’ve been making this for years and have never been to the Magnolia Bakery.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
@Luci

Makes sense that it would be an Eagle Brand recipe. I used banana pudding mix by accident instead of vanilla. It has stevia as the sweetener, which with the condensed milk is very sweet. The cream is just whipped with no sweetener and it came out about right once assembled. Next time I will look for a pudding mix without sweetener. Or make it from scratch.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
Oh good heavens. I'm going to have to repeat what I said two years ago"

"My apologies but I have to chime in with those saying they make it the old fashioned way. Eggs are separated, yolks are cooked with milk, sugar and vanilla and the whites make a meringue that is browned in to oven. I love banana pudding but never order it in restaurants due to the overly sweet prefab taste."

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: By all means keep making banana pudding your way. I'll make it according to this recipe because my family loves it.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: This recipe nipped my craving for Magnolia's banana pudding right in the bud! Very happy to find a delicious dupe. I followed the advice to not use condensed milk and I think that was the move since I had very ripe bananas and everything seems to get a bit sweeter after I let it sit overnight. One thing I had an issue with was with getting the pudding to firm up in the fridge. Mine stayed liquid even after waiting 5 hours, so I ended up heating it on the stove to get it firm.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: If you’re looking for an easy and delicious dessert that will be a showstopper at your next party, this is it. I was conflicted on using the sweetened condensed milk after reading the reviews, but I decided to make the recipe as is and I’m so glad I did. People couldn’t stop raving about this pudding and it was all gone by the end of the night. I used a deep 9 x 13 ceramic dish and it filled up well.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I was hesitant to use a boxed pudding mix, but went with it. The results were easy and delicious. I have made this receipe now with chocolate pudding and macerated stawberries, too. Don't worry about the base being too sweet, you will be whipping a large amount of unsweetened whipped cream into the base.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Delicious! I made the pudding exactly how the recipe says and it is yummy ya’ll.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Hello all - baker from Ireland and have never heard of instant pudding mix, what is it? Is it like a creme patisserie?

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
@Emer
It’s a box mix that contains artificial flavoring and coloring, as well as dextrose and other chemicals.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Sort of like Bird's Custard Powder --

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: As a southerner who grew up with homemade banana pudding at every family gathering, this recipe hurts my soul. I’m sure it’s delicious, but this ain’t ‘nana puddin’.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
I shave chocolate to cover the pudding .

it's one of my rotation desserts along with the Basque Cheesecake on NYT   a simple fruit crumble with fruit and walnuts   Flan Caramel.

If there are leftovers I usually have them for breakfast!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: this was heavenly!! made a double batch for our BBQ and i'm so happy that there was some left over the next day ahaha. It was creamy, light, and not too sweet. I'm still dreaming about it and conniving a plan so that i can make it again. Thank you!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: made this for Thanksgiving. this is without a doubt the best banana pudding I've ever tasted and a general good technique if you want to make a lot of decadent pudding at once. I'm going to be only making this recipe for the rest of my life. thank you magnolia and nyt

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Magnolia Bakery's Banana Pudding was an excellent, easy to prepare recipe for a crowd at Thanksgiving.  You really need to be able to serve it to a lot of people as I had so much left over.  We kept the portions small because the delicious pudding was quite sweet.  I would love to figure out how to make a smaller batch, but dividing the pudding mix and the sweetened condensed milk would be a challenge.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: This is amazing just the way the recipe reads!  I layered it once and next time put everything together in a big bowl and stirred it up and it was so much easier and just as delicious. Rave reviews both times.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: made pudding according to package instructions and assembled as the recipe says. so delicious, easy, and not too sweet at all

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Fantastic! . . . But it makes an unbelievable amount.  It’s enough for twenty people and we’re not tiny eaters.  I’ll make again but halve or quarter the recipe unless i have a crowd.

author: Priya Krishna

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Anyone know why this recipe uses water instead of milk? With instant pudding, milk would seem to be an essential ingredient, so there must be a reason for using water instead.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Water was used to thin out the condensed milk.   Condensed milk is also sweetened as well as evaporated so there’s too much sugar in that recipe.   Use plain cold milk, and instead of whipped cream whip a can of ice cold evaporated milk to soft peaks.  OR Just use half as much whipped cream with a Tablespoon of icing sugar and a teaspoon of vanilla extract and just use the whipped cream as a topping and not folded into the pudding.  No one needs all those extra calories or sugar.

author: Priya Krishna

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I just had to Google the ingredients of instant vanilla pudding mix. Pretty simple: sugar, cornstarch, mono & diglycerides, natural and artificial flavor. I guess I wonder if there is some magic or if you could just add some of these ingredients to your recipe and get the same result.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: No magic. Mono & diglycerides don't affect the taste. They're added as emulsifiers, thickeners, and to help the ingredients blend together. Uncooked cornstarch leaves a pasty floury taste, which is somewhat hidden by the massive amount of whipped cream, but it acts as a stabilizer of whipped cream or whipped egg whites--so it stays whipped. I prefer other ways to stabilze egg whites or whipped cream to avoid that taste.

Like another reviewer, we substituted heavy cream for the sweetened condensed milk and it's perfectly sweet this way. It turned out beautifully considering all other elements are already quite sweet (pudding, Nilla, bananas). Makes a great big batch!

Suspend your disbelief and doubts about puddin mix and follow the recipe.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I've tasted hundreds of banana puddings and, over the years, I've made at least a hundred huge dishpan sized banana puddings. I reject the Nilla method as that wafer has no flavor and disintegrates into a mushy mealy mess. By far the best method is to make thin vanilla sponge cakes (about the thickness of pancakes) and mix the pudding with hot half-and-half which slightly cooks each layer of very thinly sliced bananas giving the pudding silky texture. A bit more work and worth it.

author: Priya Krishna

Very easy and I liked that this recipe uses all the ingredients as packaged, rather than a half of this or that. I got more than 12 servings out of this and found that it easily keeps a couple of days, covered, in the fridge. Thank you.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
I found this to be too sweet with the sweetened condensed milk. The quantity is also massive, which is good if you are making a whole trifle, but less ideal if you were planning on individual servings.

Next time I would: Make pudding with whole milk, potentially cook and serve instead of instant, and layer the pudding and whipped cream (or even god forbid cool whip?) separately rather than combining them.  That way I could get less quantity too.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: This is awesome as written and so many people raved about how good it is. Not too sweet at all. The water and the heavy cream are perfect to reduce the sweetness of the milk and pudding. I'd say you are going to need a stand mixer for the second part. Makes a huge amount of blended pudding.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Simply the best banana pudding I’ve ever had!  After reading the reviews I too was afraid the sweetened condensed milk would make it too sweet, but that wasn’t the case at all, particularly with 3 cups of heavy cream mixed in. My only addition was to add 2 teaspoons vanilla to the mix.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Just to see how it was, I used banana pudding instead of vanilla. Plus, I cut the recipe in half. Now, what to do with 1/2 can sweetened condensed milk?

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Has anyone tried graham crackers in place of nilla wafers? (I realize this is not traditional but I have them already…)

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I loved this but I agree the condensed milk makes it overly sweet. I will make the instant pudding as per the box instructions next time. Also, it makes a huge amount of dessert and is very filling, so you can definitely make at least 16 servings. I may cut down the recipe in half to serve 6 next time. Perhaps chocolate shavings on top may enhance the flavor.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
I’ve had Magnolia’s Banana Pudding more than a few times living in NYC. From my best guess, they use leftover stale cupcakes/cake instead of Nilla wafers which gives the pudding its uncanny buttercream richness. 
Also, when I make Banana Pudding I opt for Whole Foods brand instant pudding. Whip up some homemade whipped cream from heavy cream (instead of chemical CoolWhip) with a touch of sweetener, ripe bananas & let it sit in the fridge all day to transfer the banana flavor around. 
Yum!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: This is so sweet it is almost inedible. I’ll go back to the original recipe!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Used soy milk 1 1/2 cups, and Califia heavy whip. Will try different vegan heavy whip as it didn’t thicken as much

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Not sure if I did something wrong or what, but I ended up with almost a gallon of the pudding and whipped cream mix. So, maybe if you're considering this recipe for a time when you're not feeding an army consider halfing it. Maybe even fourthing it. It is delicious though, made according to directions exactly.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: The first time I saw this recipe, I was scornful, but it was fortititously printed in the NYT last week, and with very little time to pull together a birthday dessert for my son, I decided to go for it with banana pudding mix.  Everyone loved it - and it was this easiest thing ever, and so quick.  I didn't do the fancy trifle assembly; just layered it with banana slices and crushed fortune cookies (I forgot to buy wafers), in parfait glasses, right before serving.  Will definitely make it again.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: The recipe is great as is and not too sweet at all IMO. I like to try changing things up sometimes, so I used instant banana pudding for "MORE banana flavor!" and borrowed from another recipe by tossing the cookies in browned salted butter and adding chopped peanuts on top too. I might try a chocolate sauce drizzle.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Delicious and easy! Made it as written except I squeezed a little lemon juice on the bananas to keep them yellow. Put it in a trifle dish and pressed some banana slices and nilla wafers against the side of the dish to make it look pretty. I served it for a cook out and it was a perfect fruit dessert after all the grilled meats. Everyone loved it!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Love, love, love this recipe! My husband isn’t a banana pudding fan, but he loves this version! I grew up on my Mom’s traditional, cooked pudding version. This is a nice change. Love both!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I didn't love this. I like box pudding just fine, but mixing in the whipped cream just made it blander. Layering on fresh bananas and crumbled cookie was... fine. It was fine. But I wouldn't make it again and can't recommend it.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Cloyingly sweet with the sweetened condensed milk. Best to make the pudding as per package instructions

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: What was the adaptation? An additional layer?

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I didn’t have a mixer, so I used a blender for everything! Knowing I can whip heavy cream in a blender is a game changer - I just checked the blender every 20 seconds or so until I got to stiff peaks to make sure I didn’t over mix it.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: The proportion of filling (cookies and bananas) to liquids is not favorable in my opinion.  The recipe yields too much pudding that is less fun to eat.  I have had the original magnolia version which has a silky pudding texture with more air.  This recipe as an adaptation of the original does not quite land the texture.  Also, which brand of vanilla wafer? Nabisco nilla is just less flavorful than it used to be.  There is better use of your time & ingredients.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I make this all the time, but the one substitute I do is I use the banana cream pudding flavor instead of the vanilla flavor!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Has anyone tried to make this with chocolate pudding instead of vanilla?

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I have been making this for years, although I layer the filling with alternating layers of graham crackers and bananas and call it an ice box cake. It is my most requested dessert. Try it. You won’t be sorry!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: My dad made banana pudding very often when we were growing up, and it was always looked forward to and eaten quickly. This is in New Orleans. I never heard of adding whipped cream, and when I have it with that, it tastes mushy and wrong. This is a simple, cheap, three-ingredient dessert for children made of Nilla wafers, instant pudding, and sliced bananas. That's why when I moved to NYC and people only referred to banana pudding as Magnolia Bakery banana pudding, it sounded laughable.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: We have Sellands here in Sacramento who makes a similar dessert except with crumbled graham crackers instead of vanilla wafers. Outstanding!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I like ginger cookies from Trader Joe’s.  Would they work in lieu of the vanilla wafers?

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
What do you mean by "would they work?" Will they taste the same" No. Would the cream still whip? Yes. 

Do it!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I used Biscoff cookies and they were delicious.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: My wife’s regular craving when pregnant 24 years ago was this banana pudding.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
one and one half cups cold water.  here i dissolved 1/3 cup of dark brown sugar in
about 1/2 cup water.  add water to make one and one half cups. let this sit in icecube bath
until the liquid reaches "ice cold".  (about 15 minutes).
for the heavy whipping cream add 1 tblsp powdered sugar per cup of whipping cream.
i used a variable speed stick blender (immersion blender) to reach "soft peaks", 5 minutes or so.
i used banana flavored jello pudding mix and very ripe bananas.  best ever.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: i don't get it. This is vanilla pudding with bananas on top, not banana pudding.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Think about it some more. You'll eventually get it. Focus on a hot fudge sundae: vanilla ice cream with hot fudge on top.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I don't have a standing mixer. Will it work with a hand beater?

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: You could even beat it by hand, using a whisk to whip the cream and a wooden spoon to fold in the pudding mixture. It will still "work."

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: If you are a Southerner be forewarned, this isn't your grandmother's banana pudding. It is a nice pudding, but quite light compared to either of my grannie's banana pudding recipes. But on a hot and humid Southern night, it will certainly hit the spot.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I made this for Christmas. (I’m a Southerner; what can I say?) Wow!!! I have never had banana pudding this good. I was delighted I could say I made it. Delicious!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Hello, I am in Portugal, and hard to find Nilla wafers, I was thinking of ladyfingers as a replacement, any thoughts on that? I've also read that galletas Maria (from Spain but we also have them here) work. Any ideas? Thank you!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I believe that lady fingers are too absorbent and will get mushy really quickly.  A cookie of some kind will get soft, but not all mush. Not having, unfortunately, ever been to Portugal, I am not sure what to recommend, but a hard cookie is required here as it gets soft in the pudding during the refrigerator phase.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I use lady fingers (the ones used for tiramisu) and it is delicious!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
I made this with a few adjustments. Overall I followed the recipe except I substituted Biscoff cookies for the vanilla wafers and added some dollops of Dulce de Leche for a riff on Banoffee pudding.  
This was a huge hit but I found it slightly looser than I would have liked. I think next time I will add more cookies (I don’t think I added a full 11 ounces) and I will beat the cream a little more before adding the pudding mixture to firm things up. 
Serving this in a trifle bowl made it pretty.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: The packaged cookies listed here are not available in Canada, so I make them from scratch - I had been making the pudding from scratch as well (it's easy) but the box mix works too.  Did not find this too sweet but I would like the pudding a bit firmer.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Has anyone made this with less whipped cream? I really want to make it, but I can't bring myself to use 3 cups of heavy cream.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: @Mo the whipped cream keeps the mixture from being too sweet.  I accidentally used 1 cup 1/2 and 1/2 with 2 cups heavy cream.  Result not as thick as it should have been, but still delicious.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Question: so many other banana pudding recipes call for whipped topping in place of whipped cream. I figured the homemade whipped cream (without added sugar) would have tamed the sweetness?? Im trying to decide between this and another recipe.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
Maybe some were along the line, sweetened condensed milk was confused with evaporated milk - hence the “too sweet” comments.  Lots of people don’t understand the difference.

I often use evaporated milk, diluted a bit less than 1:1, when cooking.  Puddings, chowders, quiches and cheese sauces all benefit.  It comes in a low fat version too.  Now that I don’t keep cow’s on hand, it saves recipes that fail when using plant based milk.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Has anyone made this with graham crackers instead of Nilla wafers?

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Can I add pureed bananas to the mix rather than slices?

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I’ve made this multiple times both with “real“ pudding and instant. Both are really good, so for me it’s a matter of how much time I have. No one has ever turned their nose up at it. The one major change that I do always make is to replace the vanilla wafers with Biscoff cookies. Looks really impressive in a trifle bowl!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: My brother- in -law from Mississippi absolutely loves this pudding. I am so glad to have this pudding recipe for family get togethers. Even family members from New England love but it too

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: As my husband's carb-counter I have to pay attention, and I'm somewhat dubious of your assessment of this as providing only 1 carb per serving. But then perhaps the 12-16 servings this makes are only a couple of spoonfuls. Sure sounds yummy!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
Hi!
I am in Sweden and have never heard of a vanilla pudding mix. What can I substitute with and also; what is yellow cake mix? Hoping for answers;-)

commentBody: I must have been sleeping … I just notice the scaler to scale the recipe down by half or take up higher.   I’m definitely using this scaler.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: For those of you who have made this...how sweet is it? I love banana pudding but find it's usually just too sweet for my tastes nowadays. I'd probably opt to make a less sweet vanilla pudding from scratch just to cut down on the sweetness, but I'd love the idea of a mix shortcut!!  Thanks.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: @High Altitude Baker I honestly don’t think it’s too sweet and neither does anyone who’s had it! Try it, it’s really delicious!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Diane I would love your recipe for the yellow cake mix cookies please!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: It’s just way too sweet to enjoy- would reduce by half next time. If there is a next time. Must add more bananas.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: We love this. Simple available ingredients, easy and incomparable. My daughter recently made it using Biscoff biscuits in place of vanilla wafers and it was delightful.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Would this work with another fruit? I'm off bananas, per doctor's orders.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Thank you for this suggestion - we can't get Nilla wafers in the UK and this was a delicious substitute! @Trevor

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: 1h was definitely not enough for the pudding to firm up enough; I tried mixing it into my whipped scream at the 1h stage and it punched the air out of the cream as it was too liquid. :( Would advise refrigerating pudding for much longer, and not using the mixer whisk to mix it in, but folding it in by hand.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Sorry, I just don’t get it. The Original Banana Pudding recipe on the Nilla Wafers box is what we always made.  It seems 100x better than the overly sweet concoction at Magnolia Bakery.  At least give that a try if you haven’t already.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
I loved banana pudding since I was a kid and could make it alone (with milk).
No I live in France and need a substitute for Nilla wafers but not the bi??? Cookies someone suggested.    Any of you know what I could use please?

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I've made this multiple times ... and everyone goes nuts for it!! A fan favorite among our friends and family!

author: Cathy Lo
commentBody: We made ours with sweetened condensed milk instead of sugar. You still have to warm it through in a skillet. You can also add pistachios to the mix. Delicious

author: Cathy Lo
commentBody: Made these using frozen shredded coconut, which came out like a sort of sticky hash at first. We spread it out on parchment and left it to harden overnight, then blended it with a dash of soy creamer and mixed in ground almonds and pistachios, plus lots more fresh cardamom. Rolled them into delicious ladoos, thanks to troubleshooting with an Indian mom!

author: Cathy Lo
commentBody: This was a disaster. The end result is a large puddle of  coconut in sugar syrup. There is nothing to form from a liquid with coconut in it. The good news is that it tastes good so I suppose it could be used as a coconut sauce.

author: Cathy Lo
commentBody: Like others, disaster. At medium low for 15 minutes, I had a pot of sugar and coconut. But the recipe said cook until the sugar melts and bubbles, so I used that as my guide. 20 or 25 minutes later, the sugar finally melted but by then the coconut was incinerated. After 25 minutes of cooling it was down to 180 degrees so I waited longer, knowing it was ruined. By the time it finally cooled, it was concrete. Maybe will try another time with condensed milk, like others have suggested. Sigh.

author: Cathy Lo
commentBody:
Priya, Jayasri, HELP! All I made was a mess…and I’m an accomplished home cook. The sugar bubbled but never totally melted with the coconut in pot as one does for other candy recipes. Definitely nothing like your Indian-ish cookbook or other online recipes that turn out nicely. 

I finely grated fresh coconut and used a “natural” granulated sugar that is slightly golden, which I assumed would add a bit of jaggery-like complexity. Perhaps traditional white sugar would have performed better?

author: Cathy Lo
commentBody: Have made these with jaggery that I shredded and frozen coconut. Takes a while to evaporate water out. They were gobbled up at Rakhi fast. Check out Swashti’s recipe for step by step. Easy and super tasty. Will try this variation. Key is to evaporate the water out and they will bind. It took me 25 min  to do so.

author: Jesse Szewczyk
commentBody: Using slivered blanched almonds or shaved raw almonds for the topping makes for a nicer presentation - it's also easier to eat.

author: Jesse Szewczyk
commentBody: Has anyone made this with a homemade caramel? I want to avoid corn sugar based candies.

author: Jesse Szewczyk
commentBody: @Jeff yes. I’m not sure if I failed or if it was the caramel, but the squares were difficult to cut. In fact the whole recipe was a disappointment to me: the almonds fell off, I made the base too soft. Just an overall bummer.

author: Jesse Szewczyk

author: Jesse Szewczyk

author: Jesse Szewczyk

author: Jesse Szewczyk
commentBody: Many people who celebrate Diwali live in the West and have a varied palette. Variety is the spice of life!

author: Jesse Szewczyk
commentBody: This did not set at all for me. I measured the almond flour by weight and followed instructions precisely, but the dough turned into a mushy mess that couldn't be cut. Worse still, the caramel topping did stiffen, so it couldn't be cut without the bottom layer oozing. I used King Arthur almond flour.

author: Jesse Szewczyk
commentBody: Agree with Jeff, I don’t want to use store bought candies? Has anyone used home made caramel?

author: Jesse Szewczyk
commentBody: I just made these with home-made caramel, cooked to the firm-ball stage (243°).  The caramel part came out fine; the almond flour/milk base is impossible.  Following the recipe directions exactly, it's the texture of very soft Play-doh, and extremely bland. Impossible to cut in squares, much too messy. I've rolled them into balls and put them into tiny muffin paper cups.

author: Jesse Szewczyk
commentBody: Can you use almond milk instead?

author: Jesse Szewczyk
commentBody: Has anyone added flavor to it with extract or spices?

author: Jesse Szewczyk
commentBody: Used 1/2 tsp pre ground cardamom, would use at least 2tsp.  However if fresh ground 1tsp would be enough I think.  Used slivered almonds. Make sure weigh ingredients (I didn't sugar or milk) or will end up with fudgy melty texture..which is similar to another kind of dessert.  A sprinkle of salt may help bring out flavors as sweet prevails. Overall enjoyed by many at Diwali party!

author: Jesse Szewczyk

author: Jesse Szewczyk

author: Jesse Szewczyk
commentBody: Mine didn't turn out.  The almond flour/milk mixture was way too soft.  Something's wrong with the ratios.  Per the recipe, I used 2 cup whole milk, 200 grams of sugar and 671 grams of almond flour.

author: Jesse Szewczyk
commentBody: As many others have said before me, the batter simply doesn't firm up. Not even after spending hours in the fridge. It has the consistency of barely cooked polenta. Tasty, but impossible to eat without a fork.

author: Jesse Szewczyk
commentBody: Can we substitute cashews for almonds (on top, not the flour)? I'm also interested in a dairy-free and corn-syrup free option as others have requested.

author: Jesse Szewczyk
commentBody: Instead of unsalted almonds, I used roasted with sea salt. After all, caramel and salt are friends in the wild! Sliced or slivered almonds, toasted, with a nice sprinkle of fleur de sel is what I’ll try next (since that probably would be prettier).

author: Jesse Szewczyk
commentBody: Help. I added a teaspoon ground cardamom and it is delicious, but the caramel top is hard and the Almond Burfi is too soft  to cut. what is the texture supposed to be like?

author: Jesse Szewczyk
commentBody: A follow-up to my earlier response to a question about using my home-made caramel: having posted that the base was impossibly soft, I will add that a day later, the caramel topping was "melting" from absorbing moisture from the soft-paste bottom layer.  Either the proportions in the original recipe for the base were way off, or the method was wrong for getting the mixture to be solid enough to cut.  By contrast, after a day, my caramel that wasn't used for the topping was not runny at all.

author: Jesse Szewczyk

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
Had all the ingredients on hand so I made these yesterday and took them to a meeting last night. Beautiful colour, a delicious riff on blondies, raves all around. If you love the combo of pistachios, cardamom and saffron, you're all set. Surprisingly, despite the amount of sugar + white chocolate, they weren't over-the-top sweet as I expected.
I freshly ground the cardamom -- and it took more like 4 hours for the frosting to set in the fridge. Will definitely make again.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I made them to share with friends from the neighborhood. They were a hit. But if I make them again, I will change a couple things: (1) I thought they were WAY too sweet. Even though I cut the bars in tiny portions, I thought they were a little overpowering. I'll skip the white chocolate next time, and (2) a half teaspoon of saffron is too much, given the price and overpowering taste. I'll cut that down -- maybe an eighth of a teaspoon.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I made these today.  Love the bar, but the frosting never set.  I would definitely cut the amount of milk or add it “as needed” for a spreading consistency.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
I absolutely loved this treat. Reminded me of Diwali sweets. Made a couple of changes after reading the reviews, used salted pistachios, 3/4 cup sugar and 1/4 cup white chocolate chips. DIVINE!
Cannot wait to make it again.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Delicious, but just way too sweet. I didn't even add the white chocolate! The cardamom overpowered the pistachio, so I would cut that in half next time. The frosting was excellent though, wouldn't change anything there. I did not have issues with the frosting setting.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Made these today, some have said they were too sweet, but I didn’t have that experience, probably because I used lightly salted roasted pistachios as that’s what I had on hand!  Honestly, I think that’s a better choice- nutty texture and really cut the sweetness -  all around, an unusual and tasty treat!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: For a sweet cake that size I use only one-half cup sugar.  Plenty sweet.  Plus you have white chocolate in the batter and a sweet frosting.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
Not sure why other people complained. These are excellent cookies. The pistachio flavor is divine. I have a couple of notes. I did not have white chocolate chips; so I cut up a bar of Callebaut white chocolate into tiny pieces, more like shaved chocolate. With this technique the chocolate didn’t sink and blended throughout the cookies. 
They did need an extra 5 minutes to bake and the frosting stayed a little gooey. But they are still fantastic.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: WOW....I'm in the process of making these.  Pan has been in the oven 30 minutes and they still are not done.  I have no idea why.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: 1/2 the sugar, salted pistachios, added orange zest to the batter. Astounding

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
This was so uninteresting. Not one of these ingredients shines; making it an uninteresting and uninspired muddle of flavors. 
Perhaps orange zest or orange blossom water in the frosting? And I would  go way heavier on the cardamom in the blondie. 
Not a repeat.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: rock solid. used roasted + salted pistachios, which ended up balancing out the sweetness of the icing; keep the white choc chips as-is, gives a little sweetness to the bar -- great recipe

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: These were divine, but I made a few slight modifications. Add 1 tsp of orange zest to the frosting to give a hint of citrus/acidity, and balance out the sweet and salty combo. I also halved the amount of dark brown sugar in the batter since the frosting was already plenty sweet. I also used lightly salted roasted pistachios as that it all I had in my pantry and the flavors still turned out great. These did not last long in my house - a wonderful take on blondies with some of my favorite flavors!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Disaster! The chips sank to the bottom and crusted.  The frosting was not very good and you cannot taste the pistachios.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Frosting disaster!! The recipe calls for room temperature cream cheese. I ignored the instruction "room temperature" and wondered why oh why my frosting looked like undercooked hard boiled eggs...turns out, if cream cheese is cold, it won't mix properly and have a lumpy consistency.  Making this on Thanksgiving wasn't the smartest play either. The blondie portion, was fantastic. Will make again with a simple butter cream frosting.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: What a treat! I had roasted and salted pistachios, so decreased the salt to 1/2 tsp. Baking in an 8x8 pan took 40 minutes. These were perfection and reminiscent of carrot cake with cream cheese frosting. Ten out of five stars

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Disappointing. The bloodies were too sweet. The frosting did not set well. Definitely not a tribute to kesar burfi. The cardamom is too overpowering. Pistachio and saffron are more than enough. Sugar overwhelms the taste buds and then dulled by excess cardamom. Big dud. Would cute down sugar by half and advise to use a pinch of cardamom.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: This was one of the driest cakes I’ve ever baked. The texture was tough as old boots and it didn’t taste of anything at all, despite the pistachios. The frosting was golden and delicious and could be potentially used for something different, like basic sponge cupcakes.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
Topped with rose petals and as per previous comments, also added orange zest to the icing.
Int he day if making, these were a 4. For breakfast the next day… 5

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: These were delicious! I only used a cup of sugar and added about a teaspoon of orange zest to the batter. Outstanding! No frosting needed.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Terrific taste,  but brace yourself for a sugar bomb.  You can definitely cut the sweet ingredients in half. I would still leave in the white chocolate though. Tasted it, so did my guests, and I ended up giving the remainder to my neighbor so that it wouldn't sit in my house.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Without reading the comment section, I forged ahead with this interesting dessert and, as I am want to do, doubled the cardamom. I'm not sorry and stick to my belief that most people just don't get how good a flavour cardamom is. However, pretty as it is, I might scrape off the icing. It just makes it too rich for my liking. In fact, I'd go so far as to suggest mixing ground saffron with icing sugar and sprinkling it on top, forgetting the cream cheese and yet more butter. Next time . . .

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Hmm, just realized my suggestion about powdered sugar and saffron wouldn't release th saffron flavour. Amend to: saffron-fused cream or water glaze—no butter or cream cheese.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: These were fab! A total hit at a dinner party. Didn't find them too sweet at all. The one piece of advise I took from other comments was to halve the amount of saffron milk. The icing never would have set if I hadn't. Still had the taste of saffron and great color.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: We really enjoyed this! Not too sweet to our taste. Really love the texture of the cake and cardamom flavor with the texture and taste of the saffron icing. Will definitely make again. Thank you, Priya!!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
I made this and LOVED LOVED LOVED them! My spouse didn't like them at the first bite but then...he had seconds. I didn't change a thing, even though I really don't like white "chocolate" but it worked.

For some reason I don't have a 9" square pan so I used an 8" pan and they took quite a long time to cook, and honestly, I took them out of the oven when the toothpick was kind of moist (too soon!) because the edges were getting too brown. After a couple of hours in the fridge, they were DIVINE.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
I had to bake this for nearly 45 minutes.  It was also extremely dense and very sweet. Is this supposed to be a heavy baked good?   When I have such a spectacular failure despite following directions exactly, I have to wonder what happened.  Between the saffron and  pistachios, it was an expensive recipe.  

On another note, the amount of frosting yielded was at least twice as much as necessary:  had I used it all, it would have been 3/4 “ deep.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: These were delicious! Per others’ suggestion (and because I didn’t have white chocolate), I skipped the white chocolate chips. I love cardamom, so freshly ground it and probably added more like 1/3 tsp to the batter. Indian grocery stores often have saffron in larger quantities, and more affordable.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: This was FANTASTIC. I weighed my ingredients and omitted white chocolate because I just think white chocolate is gross. I ground cardamom pods using a mortar and pestle as I didn’t have power. My guests RAVED and have demanded it be in regular desert rotation.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: white chocolate IS gross but somehow it worked in this recipe, which I and my family loved.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Absolutely adored these, and so did my company! Following some other notes I cut the sugar down by 1/4 and it came out to the absolute perfect sweetness level. I personally felt the saffron in the icing was overpowered by the cream cheese, but it was still a lovely treat overall.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
They took longer to bake than the recipe suggested. Otherwise, they were delicious. Of course, I didn't read the notes until after I made them. I think they would have been fine without the white chocolate chips. Not sure what I did right, but the frosting had no trouble setting. It was awfully rich though. Next time, I will spread less frosting on the cake. 
For those who already know about saffron in desserts, this was a real treat. And for those who don't--welcome to the club!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: this is easily one of the most delicious desserts i have made in years, all in my house who enjoyed it concurred. i approached the frosting a little differently: brought cream cheese (used some made by my local market which was super delicious) butter and milk to room temp, added ground saffron then powdered sugar and whisked. as such it set up more or less like a buttercream. served immediately. kept nicely in fridge.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I made these because they sounded so interesting and I love cardamom and saffron. I did not have a problem with my frosting setting but I do think the bars are a bit sweet and I did not even add the white chocolate ( just do not like it) My sweet tooth husband loves them as is but I think if I make them again I will use half the saffron and maybe a little more flour to make them a little less sweet.  I’ll see if that works. Definitely a recipe to work with to make it more my style.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I know this is probably not honoring the original intent of the recipe, but I never put just one spice and I was worried this much cardamom would just overpower the pistachios. I used 1/2 tsp ground coriander, 1/4 tsp each mace and cardamom and it was a much warmer blend. I also used roasted, salted pistachios and salted butter and reduced the salt to half accordingly. It worked out beautifully. The frosting on the other hand is just confusing and still hasn’t set 3 hrs later. ?

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Found lightly salted, roasted pistachio and those were good. Cut brown sugar down to a generous 1 cup. Grated a white chocolate bar and stirred that in. Only needed 15 seconds in the microwave for heating the milk/saffron. Mine turned out yummy.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I ground the inside seeds of a some cardamom pods, with a mortar and pestle and used a little less than the recipe called for.  It was so much fresher than the ground cardamom I had in a spice jar.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Delicious! This was a hit with my husband and my son. I cut the bake into 16 bars, which made the perfect size treat for us. The intense saffron flavor rounds out the spicey sweetness very well, and the white chocolate chips give a nice crunch to the texture. I'll definitely make this one again.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
Delicious! If anyone is looking for a THC-vehicle-of-a-recipe, this one works well. I added the reclaim from the water pipe for dabbing weed directly to the butter when I melted it for the base. It incorporated really well, and the cardamom helps to ameliorate the “weediness.” 
 I also made mine gluten free using coconut, white rice, and oat flours. The pistachios ground with the flour helped add substance and it baked nicely. I made the cream cheese/saffron icing but left half sans icing.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: It’s a good start for an unusual blondie recipe. But, I couldn’t taste the pistachios. I love the taste of cardamom, so I could see just adding cardamom to a regular blondie recipe to make a much cheaper version (pistachios = $$). Like others, it took a very long time for the batter to cook & the frosting never set. While I loved the color, the saffron flavor didn’t really stand out, and saffron is also really expensive. Next time, I would make cream cheese icing with vanilla and/or citrus.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
Loved these! I did make some recipe adjustments based on what I had. I mixed walnuts in with the pistachios since I didn’t quite have enough of them. I also didn’t have quite enough butter for both the bars and the frosting, so I subbed some of it in the bars for Greek yogurt. The swap worked! I used the full amount of sugar because I didn’t want to go overboard with the swaps, but I could’ve used less. 

I’d add a little more cardamom but I am a big cardamom fan here so that could just be me!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I only had half the confectioners' sugar needed for the frosting and it turned out perfect- sweet but not too sweet. I would definitely make this again!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: With all the sources of sugar, I was afraid it would overly sweet. For blondies, only used 3/4 cup brown sugar and left out white chocolate chips. For frosting, used white chocolate chips in place of powdered sugar. Left out butter and milk for richer cream cheese flavor. Delicious!!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Very delicious! I used half the frosting.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I have made at least ten batches of this fantastic recipe and every single person loves it. I’ve even created a recipe for ice cream with most of the same flavors (except buttercream). Easy. Flavorful. And surprising. Love it!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: About 30g sugar per bar if portioned into 12 bars.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: 10g molasses

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I just made these to bring into work - it is 30 Celsius outside so the frosting melted a bit but my colleagues in the newsroom were crazy for them! One colleague said ‘this is the best thing you’ve ever made’ and I make 3 cakes a week to bring in for people… so I’d say they were a success.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: RAVE reviews from the book club. I will make again, but next time will sub turmeric for the saffron. It gets lost in all the other flavors.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Haven’t made the frosting yet because they’re so good on their own! I fine chopped the pistachios (dry roasted, unsalted) vs. food processor, same with the white chocolate so it blends better - also toss the chocolate into the flour bowl, it’s less likely to sink if coated in flour/baking powder. Agree with others that 3/4c brown sugar and 1/4c white chocolate is perfect. Easy and fun flavor combo - next time I’ll make the frosting OR use a little orange blossom or rose syrup on top.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I will admit that my wallet cried a bit at the sheer amount of saffron called for, but I loved these. They were a huge hit and had a really pleasant flavor and texture.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I routinely replace the white chocolate chips with sweetened coconut flakes and it shines. Also I use a whipping cream and saffron topping instead of cream cheese - add a pinch of powdered cloves. It is a drier cake and therefore transports great - I sent it to NYC from SF for my sisters birthday!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
I made 1.5 batch in a 13x9 and it filled it out perfectly. I used light brown sugar because that's what I had but only the original amount of white chocolate chips (1/2c) because I didn't want them to be too sweet as others said. The blondie was so good. I didn't find it too sweet. Certainly not when compared to some brownies. Although having made these twice now I really don't feel they need the white chocolate if you want to leave that out. 

I don't really like cream cheese frosting. I just don't! So I made a small batch of Swiss meringue buttercream and heated 1/2tsp of loosely packed saffron (which I lightly crushed) in with the egg white mixture, then strained it out before whipping. I didn't use the whole batch on the blondies, just piped little dallops according to personal taste. So lovely and impressive. Would definitely make again.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I would love to make these but I have celiac. Could I substitute gluten-free flour successfully?

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Can I substitute almond flour for the pistachios, and if so, how much would you recommend (in cups).  Thanks!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I wonder if you could substitute turmeric for the saffron?

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Definitely not!  Saffron has a flavor to it as opposed to turmeric which is mostly used for color.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: You can often find another flower that is used to offer color in the place of saffron that does not add a flavor. If you’re just looking for that beautiful color I would look for fake saffron be careful with annatto however because sometimes that adds a flavor you’ll have to try but I’m sure you can find something to make that color that isn’t food coloring or expensive saffron

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I know, I know completely different recipe, but wonder how these flavors would work in a lemon bar form without lemon.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I don’t like white chocolate can I use Semi - sweet chocolate chips?

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Really enjoyed these! The tangy, cream cheese frosting works nicely with the sweeter, almost butterscotch-like blondie. I probably used slightly less saffron than the recipe calls for - the color and flavor is really lovely and I would definitely use the full amount next time. I love anything with cardamom and would consider adding more of that as well. Don’t skimp on the chopped pistachio topping!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Made this recipe today and it came out beautifully. The recipe worked perfectly as written, but be careful with microwaving the saffron and milk. Depending on the power of the microwave, 30 seconds could be too long. Everyone loves these blondies!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Beautiful, delicious frosting, but the blondie was a miss for me. Insanely salty - perhaps user error - and seriously undercooked, despite extending cooking time.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Quite easy and a real hit with everyone. Suggest letting icing set up for more than the 2 hours suggested but otherwise a real winner.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: The blondies are among the best I've ever had, although I substituted dark chocolate for white (just don't like white chocolate).  The saffron in the frosting overpowered the cardomam and pistachio, so next time I will cut that back by at least half, maybe more.  Also, the frosting seemed to lack some depth.  I added a little bit of vanilla and that seemed to help.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I skipped the white chocolate chips and they were wonderful. I think the chips would have been too sweet. I used cream in the frosting and I was able to pipe cute little circles on the cute squares and sprinkle with pistachios. The frosting is almost like shrikand- awesome.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Beautiful flavor. Delicious as-is. But next time I will use just 1/4 tsp of saffron. It's a little strong (and I LOVE saffron).

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I loved the combination of flavors in this dessert. My partner was slow to warm to the saffron frosting, but came around.  The Blondies are terrific with or without the frosting, due to their  intense pistachio flavor.  Can also be made gluten free with almond flour instead of regular.   I am certain to make these again.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Was SO intrigued to try this combo of flavors but adapted the heck out of it. Made it more cake-like (more flour, leavening, less butter), used roasted unsalted pistachios and subbed pears for the white chocolate (allergies). Made a light buttercream with saffron. A big success. Thanks for the inspiration!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I will cut down on the saffron next time, maybe half as much. You just don't need that much and it's EXPENSIVE! Took longer to cook, maybe 6-8 minutes. I could barely taste the cardamom so will add more next time.  Frosting needed more powdered sugar to set, that made more frosting than I could use so next time I will make less frosting. The color is amazing and they tasted amazing the next day.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I did lightly toast and flour raw pistachios before processing.  Frosting was made with soy milk and thinner than pictured in recipe. A huge success! What beverage would go well with?

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: These remind me of Butterscotch Brownies from The Joy of Cooking.  That said, they did not really need the frosting, but were delicious and I will make them again.  I would bump up the cardamom, and consider some whole pistachios on the top, and skip the frosting.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
I made these, and they are as pretty as they look in the picture.

They don't have a strong flavor, either in the blondie or the frosting.  Next time, I might add more cardamon, or some other spice to perk them up.

Texture of both is very good.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: This is a travesty of the original recipe… it’s not okay to Americanize everything.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: This comment doesn't make sense. No reference to "original recipe" in the notes; only references to traditional flavors/ingredients. Blondies arguably might be "American" anyway, so adding other flavors to them doesn't "Americanize" them.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: These are dense and heavy.  I agree about cutting the saffron just a bit—not to adjust the flavor but to tone down the color. My bars looked like Halloween treats.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Based on other comments, I drastically reduced the brown sugar in the blondies to 1/3 cup, and the sugar level ended up being great - the sweetness didn't overpower the pistachio and cardamom. I also added an extra 1/4 tsp cardamom, because it's my favorite flavor. Mine ended up baking for 33 minutes. I halved the frosting recipe, and that ended up being the perfect amount to complement the blondies.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: This is a delicious, wonderful cake. I skipped the white chocolate chips, but now I can see that they would add texture to this dense, moist cake. 25 minutes was enough here - the recipe does say it will be pale with a moist crumb when you test it. I also only made half the frosting, but I think I would make the whole batch next time - this cake is thick and a thick layer of frosting feels like the right match. Might use more cardamom next time, too?

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
Another amazing recipe. 5/5.
I reduced a few things:
- 210g brown sugar
- 65g white chocolate
- 0.25g saffron

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Did not have pistachios on hand, but was heading out to a mountain ski trip and really wanted to have some different dessert.  Use hazel nuts, and no chocolate chips - and they were delicious.  Increased the cardamom, because it is a spice I love.  Had no trouble at all with the baking or the frosting setting up - found this to be one of the easiest and tastiest desserts for a quick pull together.  I didnt have whole milk, but I did have heavy cream and it worked great.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Made this recipe today. Didn't make any adjustments, though I may have been a wee inexact on the brown sugar (some of it stuck to the canister and I couldn't get it out and then it came out in one lump etc.). However, the cake itself, while tasty and moist, was very flat and not particularly brownie textured. Any ideas what happened?

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I made these with the suggested changes (no chips, less milk) and they’re perfect ! So good. But It makes a bit too much frosting.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I made these the other night. I substituted gluten free flour for the flour (because I have celiac) and they took much longer than the bake time, but once they were out of the oven, I lifted the parchment paper out of the pan to cool in the fridge and it was much quicker than if I had left them in the pan. I also substituted semisweet chocolate for white chocolate because I am not a fan of the white. They were amazing. I will definitely make them again.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Crushed the chips so they wouldn't sink to the bottom.  Cut  the cardamom by 1/8 tsp as I find it can be overpowering.   Baked 25 min. and that was probably too long as my bars were sort of dry. The flavor of the bars was interesting but didn't note any one particular flavor stood out. Didn't like the icing at all, neither the flavor or the sweetness.  Scraped it completely off the brownies.  Probably won't make again as just ok.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Yes its very sweet and it’s very rich but the flavors work- and it is very reminiscent of Indian desserts. I used only a tablespoon of milk for the icing based on the reviews and let it sit in the fridge while the blondies cooled. I wouldn’t skip the white chocolate as the milky flavor reminds you of a milk-based Indian sweet. Was a hit with friends and family.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Followed others’ tip to halve the brown sugar, and the bars came out very dry and crumbly. Dominant flavor is pistachio, so I recommend adding more cardamom if you like that spice.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
There was about twice as much icing as I needed for my 9x9 pan of bloodies.

I am not much of a baker but this recipe worked well.  I love the flavors!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
I read the notes before I made them, used 210 gm sugar in the bars.  I am glad I did!  Sweet enough.  The frosting was a bit sloppy, so added what confectioners sugar I had.  Put in fridge.  Nibbled.  After three days these morsels transformed into a divine concoction.  Icing firmed enough.  Base softened enough.  Flavors melded.  Reminiscent, indeed, of Indian sweets.  

A stellar creation.  Must share what's left, or I will eat them all myself.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Made these. They were so good. Substitute roasted, salted pistachios for raw pistachios because I couldn’t find raw pistachios. These still tasted great! The icing was my favorite part. May try it on a carrot cake.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: We did not love the cream cheese frosting. The saffron adds a savory note, a little cheesy, which did not pair well. Scraped it off and topped with chocolate ganache, which worked well with pistachio/cardamom combo.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: The frosting was perfect, but the bars were way, way too sweet.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
Amazing taste - two modifications:
 - used sweetened coconut in place of white chocolate chips as the store didnt have any
- used whipped cream in place of cream cheese as we are not cream cheese frosting fans here

Reminded me of shrikhand - a western Indian dessert made with hung yogurt , sugar, saffron and cardamom

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Made with some changes they were so good and got better over next couple days.  Didn’t pack brown sugar, used salted roasted pistachios cause I had them, and replaced the white chocolate with 1/2 cup coconut cream added to the wet ingredients. Came out more like a dense cake than blondie maybe do to changes.  Used confections sugar only to taste in frosting probably less than 1/2 of recipe amount.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Somehow these were massively underbaked - and became a mildly expensive miss. The flavors are great - but given how costly the saffron and pistachios are, I think I’d rather bake something that will absolutely knock it out of the park with fewer spenny ingredients. The saffron frosting is pretty stunning tho! Good luck.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I loved this.   It doesn’t matter if it sinks in the middle because it’s chewy like brownies anyway.  That was too much icing for mine.  I think next time I would put a tiny bit of vanilla in the icing.  I will make it again!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: This was a sublime dessert bar. The taste profile was completely surprising to my “western” palate. We sampled our first bites right away, and the frosting was light and fluffy. We will see how cooling it changes the experience.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Hey Chef McCormick.  Why the hate?  I thought these were wonderful as is, but your suggestions of orange zest and extra cardamom will probably push them over the top. Definitely will repeat and give your suggestions a try.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: very good - good to take out

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: My friends and I thought these were too sweet. The amount of sugar seemed to dominate any nuance of cardamom or saffron or pistachio which were the intriguing flavors of this treat for me. I might try again with 3/4 brown sugar in the blondie and skipping the white chocolate. I’d also leave out the butter in the frosting and only use 1/3 confectioners sugar for a stronger cream cheese tang to balance the blondie.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I loved these however the frosting was a bit thick so I used more milk. The blondies alone are the BEST!!! My family and kids went crazy. We enjoyed the saffron frosting but I made more of a glaze and it was a hit. I love saffron but we all felt the frosting was a bit much. I now make these with a glaze, and garnish with pistachios and when I can get them edible flowers. Always a hit.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: These are so good!! I’m desi and I’m loving the South Asian flavors in this. I’m pretty sure saffron cream cheese frosting is my new favorite thing. I forgot to set a timer when I put the blondies in the oven so I guesstimated 19 more minutes but then they needed like 15 more minutes than it called for! I think I had these in the oven for like 30-45 minutes in total which was confusing and I’m not sure why they took so long; the blondies were still super jiggly and not passing the toothpick test

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Great. Not too sweet. Very pretty

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Going to make this, looks delish.  Wondering what i can use instead of flour (wheat-free) – almond meal too nutty/thick.  Will do a cashew frosting, from comments the reco frosting is a bit rich.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: A hit at Friendsgiving! The frosting was a bit on the softer side so add time for extra cooling if needed.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I took a lot of liberties with this recipe, and it turned out pretty tasty. Reduced the sugar in both the frosting and the blondies. Also reduced the cardamon in the blondies so it didn't overpower the pistachios. Semi-sweet chocolate chips instead of white chocolate chips because I had them and i think white chocolate is gross. Suffered from saffron sticker shock, so skipped it and added cardamon and turmeric to the frosting.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: The family loved these. I cut the sugar down to 150 grams and tossed the white chocolate chips in the dry mixture before adding to the wet, but otherwise followed the recipe. Perfectly sweet and will make again!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: These are amazing.  Worth the time to complete all the steps.  I cut them into 16 pieces and that was a very nice dessert size.  I added some fresh sliced strawberries on the side when served.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: This is a waste of ingredients. I rarely write reviews of recipes wanting to give the benefit of the doubt to both the writer and allow for user error. But this recipe is a failure of quality control/testing. I rarely find it necessary to pre-grease/butter dishes from anytime as their butter proportions usually provide plenty of moisture to easy my baked goods out of whatever dish. But

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
I didn’t even make the frosting because the bars were already far too sweet. My wife and I both think the butter and sugar listed is higher than it should be. The bars came oily and cloying.

I made the recipe by weight for the ingredients that had weights listed.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Absent white chocolate, could I substitute a little condensed milk?

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Really great recipe. I followed everything except the white chocolate. The recipe turned out wonderfully, moist, balanced in sweet and salty.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Made these and shared with several neighbours. They were a hit. Reduced the amount of sugar by a bit and they were still sufficiently sweet. The saffron was not prominent due to other competing flavours, but still gave a lovely colour. I had no issue with the icing setting. Will make again!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: yes these are so good. used roasted and salted pistachios.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
I made these for a birthday party and they were a hit! 

My main modification was to use a 40% cacao milk chocolate bar chopped up into chunks rather than white chocolate. It’s the only way I’ve found to avoid soy lecithin. 

I think the frosting was perfect with this chocolate swap. Not too sweet!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: This got raves.  I think the frosting could be a touch less sweet, but it's pretty great as it is.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Recipe calls for 9” square pan, but photo looks like 9” x 13,” which shall we use, please?

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Hi, is the pan size 8” square? The photo looks like 9 x 13” Thanks!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Absolutely loved these. I couldn't find raw pistachios so I used roasted and salted and just omitted the salt in the recipe. The frosting set quickly. I stored them in the fridge and they were delicious with an iced coffee on a summer morning.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Didn’t like this one; the half tsp of saffron was way too strong. More like a very loosely packed half tsp or quarter tsp would be appropriate. The blondies themselves were solid but didn’t like the frosting at all, likely wouldn’t have liked it much even without the overpowering saffron

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Brought this to work for my coworkers, and everyone who wasn’t turned off by the saffron loved them! (You can’t please everyone - and probably shouldn’t try.) I went down to an 8x8 because it’s what I had, but it did double the bake time for me. Despite my fears, they turned out fine following the toothpick test. I was told they tasted even better the second day, as the saffron had time to suffuse the frosting.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: These were fantastic as written! I almost didn't include the white chocolate chips, but I'm glad I did. I didn't find them too sweet at all. The flavors and the spices balance out the sweetness very nicely.

author: Priya Krishna

The 3/4 teaspoon kosher salt required for the filling is too much - it resulted in the filling being way too salty. author: Priya Krishna

I used 00 pizza flour. Needed to add over a half cup more and the dough was still a bit sticky. 
I added shredded coconut and cardamom powder, going in an Indian direction with the filling. Really successful and will make again with other fillings.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
The dough turned out perfect for me. When pressing it out with your fingers, just make sure the dough ball is lightly floured so it doesn’t stick. 

For the filling, you can sub any nut and/or seeds. I roasted and roughly chopped cashews, almonds, pumpkin seeds and pistachios (keeping within the 75g range total). 

The dark muscovado sugar added a nice depth but it tasted different from the hotteok I’ve had in Seoul and LA Koreatown. I think regular brown sugar might be sufficient.

author: Priya Krishna

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: What's the difference between "sweet rice flour" and ordinary rice flour as found in Indian shops? Does it make a difference in this recipe?

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: They're very different texturally. Rice flour is ground from standard long grain rice, and sweet rice flour is ground from glutinous rice (like the sticky rice served with Thai salads). There's also a difference between sweet rice flour from Japan, which is from short grain (what's used for mochi/butter mochi), and from China and Southeast Asia (long grain), but it maybe doesn't matter here. But to the overriding question, I've seen this recipe with all AP flour too - it'll just be less "chewy."

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: These were excellent!  Just a couple things I noticed.  I could not get two T of stuffing into each pancake, one T was max.  With that said, they were plenty sweet.  I associate pancakes with butter, so instead of veg oil I used ghee (aka clarified butter). I thought it worked perfectly.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
I used creamy oat milk for the dough instead of milk and I used coconut sugar, dark brown sugar, and maple sugar for the filling along with cardamom, ceylon cinnamon, and some orange juice. 
Absolutely delicious, I would love to try an ube filling next time!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: If you want to do a vegetarian version, omit all the meat/fish and instead add some Liquid Smoke, as well as a roux.  Both of these items will help to enrich and thicken the liquid, give it a more appealing, deeper brown shade, and a nice smokey smell.

author: Priya Krishna

For a vegan version use better than bullion un - chicken bullion paste and Goya Ham seasoning, both are vegan. Eliminate the fish, ham and smoked turkey wings. Also add a dash of smoked paprika.

It’s delicious.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I made this with smoked turkey necks and pork necks and used Old Bay as seasoned salt. Don’t think you have to but I painstakingly took the meat off the bone after boiling them which makes the final product a little easier to deal with, but it took a while. This is DELICIOUS! I had fewer collards than called for which was fine because that meant more of that onion puree to sautee them in to make them richer, and I saved half the broth to make this again! Yum

Any ideas about salt adjustment especially if not using meat?

Great technique for preparing the greens, yum.

author: Priya Krishna

author: Priya Krishna

author: Priya Krishna

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: "Smoked Cajun or regular turkey" is smoked turkey.  Cajun or not Cajun.  But smoked.  Necks or wings are suggested for this recipe.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Is the cooked meat taken out after final cooking and removed from the bones then returned to dish?

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Couldn’t find a ham hock! Sliced up a pound of bacon instead. Added a splash of apple cider vinegar at the end. Used old bay and lawry’s seasoning salt, definitely went heavy on those. Oh. My. Lanta. These are bomb.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Maybe no bullion cube?

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
There is simply NO Need for all this meat. Greens are a vegetable! No healthy southerner would eat this. 

Add 1/4 cup of smoked ham OR 3 strips of bacon, or a half of a smoked sausage (Cajun if you like it). 1/2 chopped onion, 2 tbsp vinegar to soften the leaves and smooth the flavors. Add 1-2 tsp of Cajun season, red pepper flakes if you like hot, or salt and pepper if you don’t. 

Liquid smoke is fake. If you are vegetarian, add mushrooms for richer flavor. Simmer: two hours. Instapot 30 min.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: This is a Liberian dish, as the article says. In Liberia, we use all different types of meat--chicken, fish, ham, beef--with our greens. We identify our food by the vegetable, not by the meat, because it is assumed that most of our dishes will have all different kinds of meats in them. If you are looking for a "healthy southerner" collard greens, go find a different recipe.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I am 81 years old and have searched for these flavors since someone served me collards when I was 27. I didn’t have ham hocks and used just smoked Turkey. I used a jalapeno and maybe it could have used two. A breeze to make in my Instapot. A million thanks to Thalmus.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I made several modifications: I used boneless skinless thighs, Better than Bouillon paste instead of the bouillon cube and to make the stock, and cooked for 25 minutes in a 400 degree oven. That said, it was fantastic. Heat with one habanero was perfect. I used 4 thighs which was a little skimpy: will use more next time.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Re @Adele M — The “MSG MYTH” has been debunked. Unless you happen to have an actual allergy or serious sensitivity to it, MSG is now shown to be fine in moderation, like most spices and flavorings. Doesn’t have long-term effects either. Eaten by spoonful on its own, may cause a mild headache, but definitely not the scary thing I was raised to believe. Read up, free yourself! :) (But as I said, if you have a particular sensitivity, avoid it.) Also wanted to let other readers know.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I followed the recipe precisely and it came out bland and very oily. You need to discard most of the chicken fat before cooking the onion mixture. My habaneros were starting to get soft so they may have lost their punch, but there wasn’t enough of the seasoning mix to really coat the chicken. Next time I want spicy chicken, I will make a piri piri recipe instead.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Yeah, even having not made it 1/4 tsp each of those spices seems like very very little for that much chicken and sauce?

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: For the record, Liberians love oily, spicy food. The “base” for most Liberian recipes is pounded hot pepper (sometimes with onion), Maggi cube (or Vita), and some kind of oil - yellow oil, red oil (palm oil), and/or rendered fat from meat or poultry. For this recipe (which is very similar to the classic Liberian Chicken that you can order at the Royal Grand Hotel in Liberia’s capitol, Monrovia), I doubled the habañeros, minced (not blended) the veggies, and used leg quarters. Came out great!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I made this as written and it was anything but bland - I initially was afraid I wouldn't be able to stand the heat. The addition of some salt (largely absent in the recipe) brought out some complexity and made the heat enjoyable.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
Obviously none of our erudite contributor have eaten chicken in Liberia.

The only objection I have is the use of a habaneros in lieu of the tiny super hot peppers used in West African cooking.

Otherwise, if you want piri piri--go to India.

May explain texture/taste issues: I'm going to try original. Has anyone else, to compare?

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I know I'm late but I would imagine they mean mix with a whisk or wooden spoon (as long as they combine completely I think the tool doesn't matter as much) since egg yolks have too much fat in them to foam when whisked, like egg whites do.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: The original recipe called for 2 cups of sugar! (Priya Krishna really cut back on the sugar in this recipe.) I always use a scale and the gram measurements; the volume measurements are often approximations of the more accurate gram weight. So, no, you did not use too much sugar.

author: Priya Krishna

After baking one cookie, which was like a weird little dough ball, I checked the comments and added 2 t. of baking powder, a bit more lemon juice about 1 T and some egg whites maybe 1T. These cookies weren’t at all what I expecting

They were tiny but taste great and will be good with tea/coffee time. I will try the NYT lemon meltaways next..please give us more lemon recipes!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Can ordinary cardamom be used as a substitute for black cardamom?

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Yes, you can substitute black for green but not vice versa entirely. The difference between the two lies in potency, black tends to be “smoky,” so it adds depth to savory dishes as this pudding draws towards.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
I don't see why not. 

Black cardamom is hard to come by unless you have an Indian grocer nearby.

author: Priya Krishna
