author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I live in Austin and eat at Nixta almost weekly. This is the best rice pudding I’ve ever had. Can’t wait to try making it myself. Wish you had more of their recipes. Best tacos in town and that’s a high bar.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Had no freeze-dried strawberries, but did have freeze-dried raspberry powder.  Works great!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: This looks amazing. Anybody have suggestions for a vegan version? Maybe it’s not possible to come close.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: The "original" Persian recipe of sholeh zard is vegan even without modification. This take on sholeh zard is very different from the original so I don't know that you would find the original recipe as enticing, but I think both are delicious in different ways.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: This looks good, but it’s far from sholeh zard. I see a few people asked about vegan/no egg options? Traditional sholeh zard doesn’t have any dairy or eggs. jasmine rice, salt, sugar, saffron, unsalted butter, cardamom, rose water, ground cinnamon and slivered almonds for topping. You can Google the recipe - so yummy and a more traditional texture (this one looks soupy).

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I think the description freely admits it's a "take on sholeh zard" that borrows some Mexican influence and replaces the traditional ingredients with others.  I'm sure both versions are delicious.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: FYI, butter is a dairy product

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Black cardamom is very different from green cardamom. Alton Brown describes the flavor as “Like somebody put a menthol cigarette out in a cup of dark, sweet coffee into which one drop of pine resin had fallen, and some dirt...only in a good way.”  That said, subbing in green cardamom would likely be good.  Just different.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: If I use rosewater instead of the strawberries, how much should I use?

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: People seem to respond differently to rosewater. I love it but until you are sure what your diners feel about it, i would start with perhaps 1/2 tsp. If everyone likes it you could increase it a bit up to 1 tsp.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: 1tsp is a lot! A few drops will be MORE than enough! Rose water is very intense.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Wow this was great!  Due to my location and pandemic-related supply shortages, I could not find the black cardamom or the strawberry powder so that got left out.  But incredibly I found fresh turmeric root, so I trimmed a knob of it and let it infuse along with the cinnamon stick.  Like another, I had raspberry powder on hand, which worked wonderfully.  I prepared layers of the rice pudding, raspberry powder, and chopped pistachios in a tall slender glass and it looked so lovely.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: This sounds delicious.  The instructions don't indicate when the cinnamon stick and black cardamom pod are removed.  Before the rice is added to the milk or does it all cook together and only removed before serving?  I guess if the pod doesn't open up and release the seeds when cooking, it could be either.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I left them in till stirring in the egg yolk.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
Hello,

First of all, this looks delicious. Second, Arroz con Leche is not exclusively Mexican, I'm Venezuelan and I grew up eating it too and it is because it is a Hispanic recipe, and the original recipe is from Spain and France, it arrived to The Americas during the Colony.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Desi is correct, "arroz con leche" certainly comes originally from Spain where it is usually very simply perfumed with cinnamon and pieces of lemon peel. (You cook the lemon peel with the rice, but you sprinkle the cinnamon on top.) A lady I used to work with in Spain would often make it from leftover, already cooked plain boiled rice. It's a great way to use it up.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
Quite delicious, but:
It doesn’t matter in how many cups of water you cook the rice.
Use different/any pot for the rice while you start Step 2 in Dutch oven. No reason to use the same pot for two steps and make the whole procedure lengthier.
Use more milk, more heavy cream and more sugar!
Black cardamon? No, just cardamom. Even ground one is delicious.
Strawberries powder: not great. I’ll use only cinnamon next time.  
Much tastier after refrigeration. Rice pudding ought to be eaten cold.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I’m persian and for those asking yes you can use tapioca or barley or regular basmati (that’s been soaked). No eggs, the sugar and starch will set the pudding. You can use black or green cardamom. Every family makes Sholeh Zaard differently but the base concept is the same. And yes I get this is a riff. Always slivered almonds and pistachio dust on top with some cinnamon (light dusting).

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: OMG i didn't even look at the context for the recipe until your comment, yes I HAVE had this! Nixta is amazing and really has something for everyone. I'm obsessed with rice based desserts and theirs is truly unbeatable. It has some flavors reminiscent of kheer, but is very much it's own thing.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I don’t want to be “that girl”, but can you substitute tapioca for the rice?

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: For the rice pudding? lol

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I live in Austin and we eat at Nixta almost weekly. They make some of the best food in the city and are good people. I love this rice pudding. Best I've ever had. So glad to have the recipe and to try it at home. thanks!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: This dish absolutely has nothing to do with what Iranians call Sholeh Zard, All it has in common with the Persian recipe is the name and almost nothing else. I don’t think it is right to use the name of a foreign dish then complexly butcher it .

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Delicious, but absolutely not authentic sholeh zard! If you're interested in the real thing I'd recommend Najmieh Batmanglij's recipe.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: My husband made a dairy-free version of this recipe today with coconut milk & coconut cream.  It works!  The cinnamon, strawberry & pistachios are really important and really good!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Greetings and thanks to Mrs. Mardanbeigi for the recipe for the sholeh zard. I am an Iranian living in the city of Parand near Tehran. It's been a long time since I've eaten sholeh zard, you know it's very delicious, whether you want to eat it plain or add cinnamon or saffron to it, it's enjoyable and very delicious. I hope that one day I will be able to make sholeh zard because everyone's cooking is very different. hope to see another recipe from you in the New York Times. I wish you success.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: For folks looking for freeze-dried strawberries, check your local Target. In my store, you can find them in the nuts/salty snacks aisle.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: OMG, if you use ANY Turmeric, forget about a whole tablespoon of it, you have to throw the whole thing out.  I would be very interested to know who has tried it with turmeric and found it edible.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Strawberries? No, hard no. I grew up with this delicious pudding, and you need a dusting of cinnamon. Strawberry will change the flavor profile of this cardamom, saffron, rose water delight.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: One of my all time favourites. Surprised to see you recommend it. So little of our unique cuisine is known, since the Ayatollah Regime made Iran a pariah state.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: very very delicious!! I skipped the egg yokes (out of laze) and it was still creamy. This is now my favorite rice pudding recipe. I didn't pay attention to serving size - for 2 ppl I'll half the recipe next time. For everyone saying its not authentic Sholeh Zard, they say explicitly in the description that its a fusion of Sholeh Zard from one chef's childhood and arroz con leche from the other chef's childhood... a delicious rice pudding together! I'd love to have Sholeh Zard one day too.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Is it safe to eat those 2 egg yolks if they are stirred in after the pudding has cooled to warm?

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: A vegan version might be coconut milk, coconut cream, aquafaba and vegan butter. Not sure it would get the same result, tho

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Can you add the egg yolks while the pudding is hot to let them cook some to avoid salmonella? Sorry to be overly cautious but will be serving this to grandchildren.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I added them while it was hot. it worked but I had to mix quickly and keep stirring, stirring, stoirring! because otherwise, it looked like I would have a fried egg in the pudding.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Anyone have any thoughts on substituting the arborio rice with basmati rice? Would the recipe still be workable, even if it tastes a little different?

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I have successfully subbed jasmine for arborio in other recipes and plan to do it for this one as well.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Gabriella you ask for thoughts on using basmati in place of arborio rice.  Since arborio is a short grain rice that is starchier than the long grain basmati it would likely work to use basmati here for the flavors but the texture of the dish and the consistency would be different.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Any thoughts on how many green or white cardamom pods to sub? I love cardamom but I am not about to buy a third variety for this.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Hi Marie, 6-8 pods would work fine. Giving them a light crush to open them up will infuse more flavor into the pudding. I like to put in only cardamom seeds because biting into a tiny but zingy seed adds extra deliciousness!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I forgot the vanilla and it was still so good. The spoonfuls that had both the strawberry powder and a flake of salt were almost explosive! The one purposefully change I made was that I tempered the eggs in instead, because I am giving a few servings away, and since one family has small children wanted to make sure the egg was fully cooked just to be on the safe side. And I almost used less butter, because I was nervous that the pudding would taste buttery, but I followed the recipe, and yum!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Delicious. Made according to recipe, put in sealed bag in sous vide bath at 82 c for 5 hours. No dried fruit, but fresh raspberries. Lovely

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Delicious! Made it with regular cardamom as I forgot to get black cardamom at the AliBaba market in Portland ME. It is amazing i preferred it warm but was great any time. I also could not find dried strawberry alone so subbed a dried apple strawberry mix( a nod to Maine apples i thought) i shared it with many friends who all found it lush. Will most certainly make again and again and again....

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Is the raw egg yolk necessary? When I made this wonderful pudding the consistency seemed fine without the egg yolk or does it add something to the flavor etc.?

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I don't think the egg yolk is necessary. It was rich and yellow without it.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I tasted this before and after the egg yolks and it's great even without them. Maybe a bit richer, velvetier texture with the eggs.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: This pudding is awesome. bought saffron &pistachios at a mid eastern market in bay ridge and the strawberry powder on Amazon. used 3 green cardamon pods instead of 1 black, thinking maybe that would help make up for the fact it wasn't a black cardamon pod as the note on the recipe indicated a stronger smokier taste. the green pods worked fine. was on the fence about adding yolks but been trying to stay true to recipes the first time I make it and I'm glad I did. everything about this is perfect!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Wow.  Made as directed - such a unique flavor.  It was pricy to get all the ingredients, so I made it for a special occasion.  Had no trouble with it; found it very easy to make.  Fantastically delicious!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: The rice pudding is well worth it alone; and I would do much less of the strawberry and pistachios;   I did 50% less on strawberry and it was still over powering the rice.  While it wont look as pretty; maybe an alternative is to let people spoon on the toppings to their liking.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I don't think should be called sholeh zard, but rather given its own name (or even just called a rice pudding) given it's only similarity is some base ingredients. Sholeh zard is a very specific dish, and this is far from it.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
I have made this many times and love it. Two notes: 
1. I divide it into 8 servings. It is rich and flavorful and 1/8th of this is plenty satisfying.
2. Tried it with coconut milk instead of milk/cream for a friend who can't have dairy. Left out the butter (coconut milk has plenty of fat) and it was delicious. Not as coconutty tasting as I thought it might be.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: WOW this was good. Amazing texture and flavors. Although the ingredients were a bit of an investment, now we have enough to make this again a few times more. It was perfect to make for guests - the rice cooked while we ate dinner, and by the time it was cooled and assembled everyone was ready for dessert.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Thoughts on substituting/leaving the egg out? Have an egg allergic daughter.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: You could probably use a little corn starch, if it needs thickening but it may not affect it.   I'm also severely allergic to eggs too that it's usually the white that's the problem so I can handle desserts with just the yolks.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I haven't tried this recipe yet but I have tried making an eggless custard that used cornstarch in place of the eggs. It won't taste quite the same as with eggs but it's still tasty. I'm not entirely sure of the proportions but I'd try a tbsp of cornstarch in place of the 2 yolks.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: My guess is a great compromise in texture. The eggs help thicken and add richness. You could try a cornstarch slurry to thicken but that will add nothing in the way of silkiness and richness. It would probably work but won't be the same product.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: What is the difference between Persian saffron and saffron?  Also, what other dishes call for black cardamon?

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Rajma (Kidney beans) is an option that uses black cardamom. There is a recipe at https://www.chezshuchi.com/Rajma-Kidney-Beans-Curry.html. They are called big cardamom in this recipe because that is a direct translation of the Hindi word for them, badi illaichi.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I love this recipe, already, I am a complete devote of rice puddings in general. Could this recipe be made just as easily, though replacing a couple of drops of pomegranate molasses, instead of the black cardomon and saffron, unavailable to me right now, but easily within reach at any Middle Eastern Stores within my immediate reach?

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: The substitution you suggest will change the flavor and character of this recipe, but it should still taste OK.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: A better choice might be to use whatever sweet/savory spices you DO have in your pantry. Mace or nutmeg? Fennel? Tumeric? Ginger? Even rosemary? You're looking to add aroma and intrigue, while providing some contrast against the sweet richness, so anything aromatic that shows up in both sweet and savory dishes will serve.

author: Priya Krishna

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I have not cooked this specific recipe, but i have made Sholeh Zarde before. I would say that plant-based milk should work OK.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: regular Sholeh Zard doesn't have any milk -- I would go w rice milk, almond or coconut milk -- the coconut milk will take it in a completely different direction taste-wise though. Rice milk makes the most sense....

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I'm a chef who has made vegan rice pudding for some years now using almond, oat and coconut milk. If using coconut milk, be careful not to use the canned versions, buy the cartons as they are more diluted & less 'oily'.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: This was amazing. Ordered black cardamon online and it was totally worth it. Love that smoky flavor. Didn't have cinnamon sticks so I added some powdered cinnamon during the milk heating stage. Ground the freeze dried strawberries in my coffee grinder, thought they added a great touch with the pistachios. Such an interesting, fun, pretty, and delicious recipe!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I found this to be really rich even though I substituted oatmilk for regular milk, coconut cream for heavy cream, duck eggs instead of chicken eggs, and used a generous pour of rosewater instead of dried, ground strawberries. Next time I'll omit the eggs and use began butter and see how it goes. Overall, delicious and hard to put down. Thank you for sharing this!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: The freeze-dried strawberries and Arborio rice, really differentiate this rice pudding dish from others I have eaten or made. The freeze-dried strawberries give a nice, tart taste that break up the sweetness of the pudding and the Arborio rice keep their shape and don't become mushy after being cooked. It was a great dish to make for my mother's birthday after being separated due to the Corona virus.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Why is it necessary to pre-boil the rice? Is this one of those things that seems pointless but otherwise the rice won't even soften?

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: The rice needs to be preboiled to eliminate surface starch, otherwise the grains would clump together and the pudding would not obtain the desired homogeneous consistency.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: i thought the consistency was pretty thick, not loose at all.  i subbed raspberry powder to keep it from being cloying.  result was a complex meld of flavors - and just the right touch of sweetness.  not something i’d make every day, but worth the effort when i do.

author: Priya Krishna

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Tradition with a twist. I just made this, as we Haitian-Americans do every year (more than a few times a year), smells and tastes divine. With pandemic shopping and produce from local farms, I made some substitutes: an acorn, butternut squash and a sweet potato, a ham bone for the beef (yummy blasphemy!), NO bouillon cube (yuk!), kale instead of cabbage, and leeks (green and white) for more flavor. My maman's soupe joumou had the best doumbwyes-dumplings, 1/2 tsp baking powder makes them light.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I've seen another recipe for this soup use pumpkin.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Butternut should be a fine substitute for calabaza.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: You might be able to use butternut squash. Being Haitian, it would taste a lot different to me but I think the texture would be right.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I live in the Pacific Northwest. If you can find Kabocha Squash, it’s truly the closest to Calabaza that I’ve tasted.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Interesting, but I think there's a step missing.  The other versions, including the Wikipedia account of it, I found put the raw squash into the soup in your step 5 above, remove it when cooked, puree it and put it back in.  Pureeing raw squash might work but is unconventional.  I know that Wikipedia is the home of all falsehoods, but still ...

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
When I don’t find a good piece of dark yellow calabasa, ( pale ones are not good with the soup), I simply add a can of pumpkin purée. 
Also, I don’t go through the trouble of peeling, cooking then puréeing the squash. I scrape the seeds and hair, then drop it in the soup. After about 30 minutes, I pull each piece with tongues, and put it on a plate. 
With a fork I mash and scrape the soft flesh, put in back in the soup, and discard the skin. I work with one slice at a time.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: We made this tonight for Halloween dinner. It wasn't the fastest thing we've ever made...it took a bit of time and both my husband and me chopping (for a while!) but our six year old and twelve year olds sons ate two bowls after trick or treating and loved it. We used a black futsu squash and steamed it first and then mashed it with a potato masher. We also served it with extra epis in a dollop on top and a couple of thin slices of Serrano pepper. Great to try something new!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: There is a good deal of prep required for this soup but well worth it.  I had to cook it much longer than prescribed and was a bit more generous with the thyme and salt.  I used a habanero pepper and allowed it to stay in the soup the whole time since we love spice.  It never burst and the soup was only mildly spicy.  It got RAVE reviews!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: As a Haitian American who is normally home w/ma mere on Jan1, I found this easy to follow for my first time making on my own..mainly help w/proportions, etc. This was very easy to follow & authentic. As someone who is a member of my local CSA and has a small herb garden at home, I used whatever I had on hand. I had both kabocha and butternut squash, but decided on butternut. Also used turnips, carrots, cabbage, thyme, parsley, bouillon, scotch bonnet, and spaghetti. It was beyond a hit! Thanks

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Delicious! Used kabocha squash from the garden which I roasted and then scooped out before the purée step. This is a time intensive meal for all the chopping, but worth it! Plus we have leftovers for lunch all week because as others noted, it makes a lot. Served with a squeeze of lime and a bit of hot sauce.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I am wondering if this food come from heaven?

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Acorn or butternut squash would be good substitutes.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: My family uses butternut squash and it is delicious. They also use whatever pasta is available.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I wouldn’t think the addition of dumplings would be necessary at all... there’s enough starchy carbs to go around with the potatoes, squash, turnip (sometimes included), and pasta. I’ve never seen my Haitian relatives add dumplings before. For the noodles it’s more common to use regular spaghetti or tubed pasta. Still looks like a good recipe though!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I use butternut squash and it’s as good

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Calabaza is the generic name in the Spanish language for any type of pumpkin.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I would suggest sautéing all the veggies first and then adding your water. That way the squash breaks down naturally and there is no need to blend it separately. I know this is heresy, but I made a vegetarian version and it was absolutely DELICIOUS!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: This recipe was overflowing in my 6 quart stock pot. This recipe can be easily halved, and still feed an army for a week! Looking forward to it tonight, after hearing so much about it recently.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Just be aware that "water" is not among the ingredients for the dumplings, it is listed in the text.  It might not be obvious to all.  That kind of thing occasionally trips me up.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Following up on ‘traditional with a twist’...Having vegetarian friends and family, I make this meatless, just because it’s so good. The calabaza takes time to cook so def add it 1st; no blender needed, pull it out to mash with a potato masher or fork. The only reason my family adds the dumplings (which is for chicken soup or bean purée and def not this soup) was that my father loved them so much. I still count them as I plop them in the soup knowing that a few will disappear before dinner.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
No dumplings in soup Joumou( sacrilege!)

Dumplings is for Bouyon which is quite different

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
According to "Fine haitian cuisine" book by Mona Cassion Ménager
Either acorn squash or combination of half acorn squash and half regular pumpkin can substitute for calabaza squash

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Kabocha or butternut squash will do. Add 1/2 tsp baking powder to dumpling mix.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
Ingredients
1 small onion, coarsely chopped
1/2 green bell pepper, coarsely chopped
1/2 red bell pepper, coarsely chopped
1/2 yellow bell pepper, coarsely chopped
6 scallions, coarsely chopped
6 garlic cloves, coarsely chopped
1 cup coarsely chopped parsley leaves with tender stems
1/2 cup olive or canola oil
6 basil leaves

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
Epis (Haitian Seasoning Base)
This blend of onions, scallions, garlic, parsley, bell peppers, and other spices is the foundation for most Haitian dishes, and many Haitians have it in their refrigerator at all times. Even in recipes that do not call for it specifically, it can often be added. Basil brings a freshness to this version; you can also add thyme. In Haiti, a mortar and pestle is used to mash the ingredients together, but a food processor or blender makes it come together much faster. M

commentBody: I agree with one reader - bouillon cube! Not used back in the days. Yuk x 10.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Made this yesterday and shared with family - they all loved it. Lots of chopping - I think next time I'll do it stages. In the end I didn't make the dumplings - will add those to the leftovers for tomorrows dinner. One statement in the article is certainly true "Use your biggest pot."

commentBody: Calabaza or Kabocha squash can be found at Asian markets as well. At least you can where I live (Raleigh/Durham area).

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I made this and found it a bit bland. The dumplings were heavy, even though I added the suggested baking powder. But I froze what was left and reheated adding Worcestershire and additional pickled jalapenos and it was yummy. Saving this because the epis sauce is a wonderful marinade and addition to soups

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I’m telling u (as a Haitian woman) soup joumou is not spicy in the least. You can add scotch bonnet and the squash’s sweetness will mask it.

commentBody: Kabocha squash is a very satisfying substitute indeed. Also you don't need to use the bouillon cube if you have left over rotisserie chicken

commentBody:
Making this now--can't get the right kind of squash, so subbing butternut. Can't get a Scotch bonnet pepper, so subbing either serrano pepper or habenero sauce (advice on this?)  Otherwise, making exactly as instructed.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I didn't use bouillon cubes because the reviewers said not to--I used "better than bouillon" instead. I didn't make dumplings because how many different starch sources do you need in one soup? The meat was amazing but the soup was bland. I won't make the soup again, but I WILL prep beef this way in future.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
This is a delicious recipe! I followed it almost exactly, substituting 2 pounds of tiny whole potatoes for the russets. This was a timesaver. 

The one aspect that didn't work well was pureeing the raw squash. Maybe I didn't puree it finely enough, but I ended up with tiny bits suspended in liquid rather than a smooth-textured soup. Still tasty, but next time I'll cook the squash separately before pureeing and adding to the soup.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: @Bunny I use beef flavored (vegan) better than bouillon when i need a plant-based version.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: The best thing about making this was learning about Epis. I can't wait to riff on Epis-first thing I'll try is subbing roast garlic for fresh.  I loved it as is but my batch is very garlic forward.  I kipped the vermicilli and the dumplings were not a pleasant addition. Maybe I didn't roll them thin enough but mine ended up really doughy and floury tasting. Another sub I made was using a habenero (no scotch bonnets to be found) and it added a really nice undertone of chili heat to soup.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I've had this soup may times in Haiti. I do like the dumplings. What is missing in this recipe is the ever present plantains which are cooked but hard and dry in every Haitian soup I ever had. I see why they left it out here. They have little appeal . That said , its odd to have dumplings and pasta, but that's the way it works in Haiti. Some foods for the masses are cheap , starchy and delicious.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
Very good recipe. Improvise with whatever fruits, vegetables and crunchy stuff (tortilla chips, kettle potato chips, cheese curls,Chex work well) are around: it's the sauces/condiments that make it chaat. 
  The term "raita" (see Wikipedia - etymology from "ra-ee", Hindustani for mustard seed) strictly refers to spiced, salted yoghurt (optionally diluted) PLUS something else: chopped/grated vegetables - (e.g., cucumber, like tzatziki) or deep-fried chickpea-flour pearls ("boondi").

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: My family would do this all the time during summer when I was growing up. My mother always offered sprouted lentils and cooked chickpeas. These add heft and protein and take it from being a not so healthy snack to a real meal.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
Just saying:  all the items listed under 'crunchy base' can be found at your nearest Indian store, or online.  
Or experiment with tortilla or corn chips, various types of grocery store crackers, Chex Mix, etc.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Save the fruit for a fruit chaat (except for a pomegranate seed sprinkle on top).  Could also skip the fresh ginger -- it's so sharp!  Add legumes -- garbanzos (black would be even better) or whatever you have on hand.  And a chile-garlic chutney as well (in a pinch, use a sambal or whatever utilitarian one you have in your refrigerator).  It's about texture and awakening every part of your tongue with different flavors.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Chaat is served in all kinds of different ways so I would say use your imagination. Some recipes have you scoop it up with a fried pita triangle but it also comes served in a newspaper cone you just scoop out with a disposable spoon.

Definitely leave out the fruits for a fruit chaat (maybe except pomegranate seeds as another tip suggested) and layer like you would layer a plate/tray of nachos. You could also simply put all of the components out in separate bowls and let each person customize assembly to their own taste.

commentBody: I know folks always complain that the prep time in NYT recipes is way too low, but this one takes the cake. 10 minutes??? How many hours would it really take to roast and chop all the vegetables and prepare 3 different chutneys (much less hunt down all the ingredients that most folks don't happen to have on hand)?

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I would add pani puri to this mix…. It’s kind of like the soup dumplings of chaat, but cold and refreshing.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: The mango pulp isn't essential: before canning, it was available (homemade) only between Apr-Aug, and so most recipes omit it, and are either non-sweet or substitute smaller quantities of an unrefined sugar like jaggery. Adjust the quantity/variety of chili (green cayenne cultivars are ubiquitous in India) to heat preference, ditto for leaving the seeds in or out.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I second Mr. Nadkarni's comment re. mango pulp. A non-sweet cilantro-mint chutney makes for a fantastic layering spread inside a grilled cheese and tomato sandwich. The addition of ground peanuts or peanut butter is another good variation to spread on bread or crackers.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: A dear Indian friend makes sandwich filling with the chutney(minus mango pulp).  She combines it with mashed potatoes, and then grills the sandwich in a skillet with ghee.  Scrumptious!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: A question: this chutney, which has 5 Serrano chiles, is called a "Cilantro Mint Chutney". But the chutney that is labelled as a "Green Chile Chutney" only has 3 Serrano chiles. Shouldn't it be the other way around, since this one has more chile?

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: The mango is absolutely a must. I got unusually spicy peppers - I added apple cider vinegar since acidity cuts spice, and it ended up perfect. Easy and versatile!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: sublime. Cilantro and mint were meant for each other, and the hot peppers gave just enough emphasis.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
It’s a mixture of rock salts and spices often used to sprinkle on as a finisher in Indian snack foods. It is salty, funky and sour, with some smoky cumin notes. Can be bought at any Indian store or online. 
‘Chaats’ are a category of Indian street snack foods that are so named because they make one want to lick the plate clean. ‘Chaatna’ means ‘to lick’.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Use the channa dal dry. Not soaked

author: Priya Krishna

I made a second batch, and ground the dal after toasting, as Drew suggested, and added the ground dal to the rest of the ingredients, and it blended well.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: To anybody that's made this, did you find it superior enough to a storebought, jarred green chutney containing chaat masala to justify the work of making it?

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Can parsley be subbed for cilantro without compromising the taste too much?  Be honest.  I can take it.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I used Indian tamarind concentrate (Tamicon brand) instead of a block of tamarind. 3/4 cup concentrate and 3/4 cup water worked out well. It was done in about 5 minutes

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
I see that my comment form yesterday has not  been   posted. Why not? There is no water listed in the ingredients and yet we are told to boil an assemblage of sugar, dried fruit and spices. So how much water?

I love imli (tamarind) chutney and would love to make it myself. I currently buy it online and some brands are thicker, some thinner, but all list water in the ingredients.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: The tamarind and dried fruit have water that will come out when cooking. The recipe states "add water if needed" when blending - it's your discretion to how thin you want your own chutney to be. If too thick, maybe try a tablespoon at a time :)

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Thanks to the author for inspiring me to assemble a chaat dinner, but this tamarind chutney recipe is deeply flawed. You can't complete this recipe without adding water to the tamarind, etc., as it cooks. Add 2 cups of water when you add the tamarind to the pan. You can add more when processing to thin the chutney, but you must add water when you cook the tamarind. And, beware of seeds in "seedless" tamarind: they stick to the blade of your food processor and cause the blade to flip off its post

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: This is the third time making Tamarind Chutney, and the first time using Chaat Masala with it. Brilliant! It adds a complexity to the flavor beyond tart and sweet. After doing the tamarind block once, never again, just use concentrate, Desi/Indian grocery stores have many options.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Something went wrong with this recipe. I had to add probably 3 cups of water to get it to a consistency where it would boil, instead of just burning. No water came out of any of the dried fruits. My tamarind block said it was seedless, but it definitely wasn't and I had to run this through the strainer three times so no one would chip a tooth. It ended up delicious... but I don't know how much of the recipe I actually followed after I had to amend course so many times to salvage it.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: FYI for a 1 pound brick of tamarind pulp, you would add 2 cups of hot water. If you are using concentrate, 3/4 cup works well with 3/4 cup water.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: This recipe (and the recipe in Maneet’s cookbook!) does not include the necessary instruction to add water to the tamarind/date mixture to boil. Otherwise you’re just “boiling” solid ingredients. I’m taking another commenter’s advice to add 2 cups of water!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Loved this. It's very thick, but I can thin as needed. Subbed an extra 1/4 cup of golden raisins since I had no dates, anise for the fennel, and used 3/4  cup cane sugar since I had no jaggery or brown sugar, and added 2 tablespoons of pomegranite molasses for depth. Also added a splash of water during cooking to avoid scorching.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: This chutney is so much better than store brands.  I start with the dried seed pods which, after the outer shell is removed, need quite a bit of soaking before the seeds and tough fiber strings can be removed. This requires about 2 cups of water per pound, so the pulp is a bit thin (about the consistency of melted butter) before the dates and raisins are added. This makes the end consistency just right.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
This recipe is a bust! It’s so thick before blending that it’s hard to imagine how it could coat a spoon…without adding liquid it’s just a gloppy mess! 
We had to add SO MUCH WATER just to get it to any sort of pourable consistency, and ours looks absolutely nothing like this picture. 
I feel like I wasted all these ingredients on this recipe. First NYT recipe fail and I will return to my other tamarind chutney recipe from now on.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Kaddu (Sweet and Sour Butternut Squash) Recipe
commentBody:
I thought this was a really good recipe! Sweet, sour, spicy. Yum! I used mustard seeds instead of fenugreek. I ended up having to cook the squash about 8-10 extra minutes. And did 1/4 tsp of regular chili powder and 1/4 of cayenne. I like spicy but think it would have been too spicy with a full 1/2 tsp cayenne. 

Next time I think I’d add a little crunch with either some chopped roasted peanuts or fried shallots!

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Kaddu (Sweet and Sour Butternut Squash) Recipe
commentBody: This turned out amazing! The flavor was incredible! Only substitutions were using acorn squash instead of butternut, and I used 1 can diced tomatoes instead of fresh, since the ones at the store were looking a little sad. I am going to make this again and again!

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Kaddu (Sweet and Sour Butternut Squash) Recipe
commentBody: This is an excellent recipe. If you're preparing this for Navarati, skip the onions and tumeric, which are not allowed during the fast. You can also include green chiles, mustard seeds, cumin seeds as well as tamarind, which are all allowed.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Kaddu (Sweet and Sour Butternut Squash) Recipe
commentBody: Just gorgeous! Rarely do I have everything for a recipe on-hand, but I did today. Taking the convenience route with pre-cut organic squash and diced tomatoes, it came together fairly quickly. The squash cubes were much larger than the recipe specified, so the cooking time was longer, and I stirred everything a few times to be sure the squash was cooked. PS: if you grind several peeled ginger roots in a mini-processor and freeze it in 1 tablespoon clumps, you can do recipes like this in a flash.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Kaddu (Sweet and Sour Butternut Squash) Recipe
commentBody: This was delicious, I’ll be making it many more times. I cooked the squash a while longer, ~45 min on medium low till it was soft and partially broken down and saucy. Used canned tomatoes, no other changes. Really delicious, the flavors are bright and multilayered, balanced, substantial but not heavy - beautiful.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Kaddu (Sweet and Sour Butternut Squash) Recipe
commentBody: I agree this is a delicious dish. I didn’t find fenugreek seeds, so I used 1/2 tsp ground fenugreek that I added with the turmeric. @Kyra, I used 1/2 tsp cayenne as recipe calls for - not too spicy. I used local, organic yellow cherry tomatoes from the farmers market, and maple syrup in place of the brown sugar. Don’t leave off the cilantro. It definitely adds something to provide the full umami flavor. The 25 min. is just cooking time. W/ peeling, chopping, etc, this took a good hour to make.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Kaddu (Sweet and Sour Butternut Squash) Recipe
commentBody: My Mom makes this every year for Diwali. She used pumpkin which is a nice way to deal with non-carved pumpkins you may have gotten for Halloween. She cuts them into large chunks, leaving the peal on. No onions, or tomato, but adds in green chilis and powdered coriander. So tasty!

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Kaddu (Sweet and Sour Butternut Squash) Recipe
commentBody: This was a fantastic "yellow" dish to replace our tired sweet potatoes with marshmallows for the Thanksgiving feast. I prepped the squash and onions the day before which made cooking a breeze and used canned diced tomatoes (15 oz) instead of the roma tomatoes. I cooked it down to soften it (30 min) but left the squash with some firmness. Even more delicious the next day as a breakfast side with eggs!

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Kaddu (Sweet and Sour Butternut Squash) Recipe
commentBody: Cooked exactly as directed but in an Instant Pot. 30 minutes and it was perfect and delicious.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Kaddu (Sweet and Sour Butternut Squash) Recipe
commentBody: I make stovetop squash and sweet potato dishes all the time -- but here's a tip, chop them up and throw the chunks in a steamer basket for five-ten minutes before adding to the saute pan. This softens them up and makes them more amenable to mixing with the other ingredients. I find it hard to believe you can cook raw squash this way in only 10 mins!

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Kaddu (Sweet and Sour Butternut Squash) Recipe
commentBody: Delightful mix of sweet, sour and  spice. To add protein, added drained can of chickpeas and fried in oil before adding fenugreek seeds. Used a little less than Tablespoon of  brown sugar and subbed plum tomatoes and grape tomatoes for Roma. Came together quite easily in wok. Served over quinoa. Definitely will make again and again.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Kaddu (Sweet and Sour Butternut Squash) Recipe
commentBody: Allow time for the prep; I wrestled with the squash for a while! Excellent flavours, even though I had to sub mustard seeds. No coriander so topped with parsley and green onion. Served up with toasted naan, mango chutney and a few nuts. Happy Thanksgiving to American cooks from up in the North!

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Kaddu (Sweet and Sour Butternut Squash) Recipe
commentBody: I found that leftovers were great in salads.  I used baby spinach, cubed avocado, and goat cheese, with olive oil and Japanese rice vinegar, and a glop (however much you want) of the squash. Interesting flavors - I may make the recipe in the future just to have On hand for salads.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Kaddu (Sweet and Sour Butternut Squash) Recipe
commentBody: Adapted a bit based on what I had on hand. Used Panch Puron Indian spice mix, 14 Oz can of fire roasted tomatoes, drained. Smallish squash so reduced everything accordingly. Very adaptable recipe. Loved it. Will keep in rotation.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Kaddu (Sweet and Sour Butternut Squash) Recipe
commentBody: A different way to serve butternut squash. The mix of spices come together nicely. However there is no way butternut squash will soften in 15 minutes. It took me closer to 40 minutes.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Kaddu (Sweet and Sour Butternut Squash) Recipe
commentBody: Made this twice. Used more of every spice and no brown sugar. Loved it!

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Kaddu (Sweet and Sour Butternut Squash) Recipe
commentBody: Add some garam masala at the end to give an extra layer of flavor

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Kaddu (Sweet and Sour Butternut Squash) Recipe
commentBody: Outstanding depth and complexity of flavor and very easy to make. 1/2" cubes of squash easily cooked in the time suggested. Used canned tomatoes; otherwise made no alterations. (I did use asafetida, which is always good.) Served with quick flatbreads; would also be good with rice.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Kaddu (Sweet and Sour Butternut Squash) Recipe
commentBody: This was an eye opener, we also used a combo of mustard seeds and fennel seeds, its really tart, sweet, savory, its everything at once!!

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Kaddu (Sweet and Sour Butternut Squash) Recipe
commentBody: I served this with Tourtiere and cranberry sauce for Boxing Day brunch. Everyone loved it and marveled at how well it went with Tourtiere and cranberry sauce! It's already on the menu for next year.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Kaddu (Sweet and Sour Butternut Squash) Recipe
commentBody: Soooo good!!! I made it with pumpkin instead of butternut squash, as we had a huge pumpkin from Halloween that we never got around to carving. Recommended the recipe to a friend who didn't have fenugreek - you could probably swap out the fenugreek and brown sugar for maple syrup (she taps her own!) and arrive at a similar flavour.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Kaddu (Sweet and Sour Butternut Squash) Recipe
commentBody: Replaced fenugreek with yellow mustard seeds. Cut the brown sugar by about half and felt it was sufficiently sweetened. Delicious!

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Kaddu (Sweet and Sour Butternut Squash) Recipe
commentBody: I made this as a side for Thanksgiving 2021. There were lots of vegetarians at the table. It was very, very good. I should have cut back a bit on the cayenne pepper, as it was pretty spicy for the guests and yet they kept eating it--seconds all around!

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Kaddu (Sweet and Sour Butternut Squash) Recipe
commentBody:
This was outstanding!  As others have mentioned, I substituted fennel seeds and mustard seeds (50-50 mix) for the fenugreek seed.  I enjoy spice, so used the entire ½ teaspoon of cayenne.  
This will be on frequent rotation and was a nice change for Thanksgiving this year.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Kaddu (Sweet and Sour Butternut Squash) Recipe
commentBody:
Excellent as written. My vegetarian wife says to put this in the regular rotation. I replaced part of the brown sugar with dark maple syrup to pick up the fenugreek.

It'd be relatively simple to sheet-pan this one too.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Kaddu (Sweet and Sour Butternut Squash) Recipe
commentBody: This was absolutely fantastic. I have several other squash recipes that I love, but this was new and fresh.  My husband said he loved it too (that's a real endorsement!).  Using fenugreek made a lot of difference in the flavor--don't skip it!  I used kabocha squash, which made it deeply flavorful.  And the cilantro at the end made it pop.  This will go into my "keepers" list for sure.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Kaddu (Sweet and Sour Butternut Squash) Recipe
commentBody: Made this for Thanksgiving. Excellent, and my guests liked it too.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Kaddu (Sweet and Sour Butternut Squash) Recipe
commentBody: So delicious! I had half a butternut squash left over, so I halved this recipe. It was really easy and turned out so good. I didn't garnish with cilantro and it didn't seem to be wanting.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Kaddu (Sweet and Sour Butternut Squash) Recipe
commentBody: I’m hardly an expert but ... asefetida is a powdered seasoning often used in Indian food.  Uncooked, it’s pretty odiferous but when cooked, it adds the flavor of onions/leeks/garlic.  Google it for more info.  I’ve purchased it at my local Indian market and use it when called for.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Kaddu (Sweet and Sour Butternut Squash) Recipe
commentBody: Not a whole lot of "local" Indian markets around.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Kaddu (Sweet and Sour Butternut Squash) Recipe
commentBody: There are a lot of online Indian grocers. You can search reddit for recommendations of reputable ones to help narrow the field.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Kaddu (Sweet and Sour Butternut Squash) Recipe
commentBody: Help! The fenugreek seeds burned almost immediately. Then I luckily checked on the squash and it was starting to scorch. Not sure what I did wrong, I followed the recipe exactly. I ended up having to add some water to keep it from scorching further. It turned out great, a little spicy for some of my fam but I loved it that way. Just some hints on why the 2 issues arose would be helpful!

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Kaddu (Sweet and Sour Butternut Squash) Recipe
commentBody:
One doesn't use mustard seeds instead of fenugreek. Lot of recipes call for both, as well as cumin seed. At no time one of these is used as a substitute for another.
The other day (before I saw this on NYT) I made a version of this, just using squash, with cumin, mustard, urad dal as seasoning, with a pinch of salt, citric acid thrown in. Was quite good. Of course, cilantro, onion and ginger add another level of spiciness.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Kaddu (Sweet and Sour Butternut Squash) Recipe
commentBody:
1. Cooked squash (acorn) for 10 mins.  Not soft enough so added a little bit of chicken broth, covered, and cooked for 10 mins. more.  Squash was done.  I used half the cayenne, and added a little chipotle.
2. Used 1 15 oz. can diced tomatoes instead of fresh; winter tomatoes have no taste. Then added a heaping tsp. of peanut butter and 1 T. Honey mustard (used this as a substitute for fenugreek).  Topped with a few peanuts for crunch.
3.  Loved this true comfort dish!

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Kaddu (Sweet and Sour Butternut Squash) Recipe
commentBody: Came out great. Didn't have tomatoes so sautéed about 1 TSP tomato paste before adding squash. Used maple syrup and molasses, and added lime zest at the end. Loved this method of cooking the squash--got carmelized a bit on the bottom with just leaving it alone for 10 min in the pan covered.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Kaddu (Sweet and Sour Butternut Squash) Recipe
commentBody: This was truly delicious. My squash took a little longer to cook as I was not quite assiduous enough when cutting it into 1/2-inch pieces. The dish does not take long to prepare as long as you have a sous chef who will peel and cut up the squash. A food processor is a must, too. I am a vegetarian so I don't mind the time spent preparing a dish of this quality. If all vegetables tasted this good, more people would eat them.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Kaddu (Sweet and Sour Butternut Squash) Recipe
commentBody: Fenugreek is an important component to this recipe- don’t skip it! I also think the asafoetida is a necessary ingredient. This was deliciously spicy and sour: just right. Prepping does take some time, but if you get everything chopped and ready beforehand putting it together goes quickly.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Kaddu (Sweet and Sour Butternut Squash) Recipe
commentBody: Note that asafoetida is almost always sold already mixed with wheat flour (which is not always obvious on the package), so you can't use it if you're avoiding gluten. I've seen it in many Indian recipes with the indication "optional," so it must not be considered essential.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Kaddu (Sweet and Sour Butternut Squash) Recipe
commentBody: This was the most popular dish this Thanksgiving (along with the braised collard greens recipe on NYT). Every bite was delightful.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Kaddu (Sweet and Sour Butternut Squash) Recipe
commentBody: This is an unbelievably delicious recipe.  I would give it 6 stars !  Since I don't care for things too spicy, I reduced the cayenne by 1/2.  The leftovers were delicious cold.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Kaddu (Sweet and Sour Butternut Squash) Recipe
commentBody: I made this tonight and it is incredibly flavorful.  Yummy.  I added a little sour cream on top which kicked it up a notch in my opinion.  I served it over a brown basmati and wild rice blend my husband had bought.  Will make this again.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Kaddu (Sweet and Sour Butternut Squash) Recipe
commentBody: This was our Covid Christmas breakfast. I served it with wilted radicchio on the bottom of the plate, then the squash, then fried eggs. Truly wonderful holiday morning meal, and then we ate fresh grapes and oranges while sipping coffee afterwards.  I skipped the asafetida but actually had fenugreek seeds in the cupboard, so for the most part, I made recipe as written. It's a keeper.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Kaddu (Sweet and Sour Butternut Squash) Recipe
commentBody: I absolutely loved this! Served with oven-roasted bone-in chicken thighs marinated in buttermilk and rubbed with a blend of garam masala, cayenne, and turmeric. The one adjustment I had to make was adding a few tablespoons of water before covering and simmering the squash—I think it would have burned a little without it. My burners are a little unpredictable, though, and my onion seemed a little dry, so it may have just been me! Anyway, I loved the dish and will be making it again very soon.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Kaddu (Sweet and Sour Butternut Squash) Recipe
commentBody: Delicious. Added a T of tomato paste at the end.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Kaddu (Sweet and Sour Butternut Squash) Recipe
commentBody:
This was really delicious. It was a welcome change at Thanksgiving, but we will make it throughout the year.  I altered it in five minor ways:
1.) tempered 1T minced fresh kadi patta (curry leaf) plus some whole smaller leaves with the fenugreek seeds in the beginning.
2.) fried the ginger before adding the onion.
3.) used kashmiri chile flakes instead of cayenne.
4.) used a can of diced tomatoes since the fresh Roma looked drab.
5.) cut the sugar in half.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Kaddu (Sweet and Sour Butternut Squash) Recipe
commentBody: this was sooo good!!

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Kaddu (Sweet and Sour Butternut Squash) Recipe
commentBody: I thought this was delicious.  It was also easy to make.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Kaddu (Sweet and Sour Butternut Squash) Recipe
commentBody:
Don't miss trying this gem! Served it over brown short grain rice.
I didn't have fenugreek seeds so I subbed in mustard seeds. Did not have access to asafetida either. In addition, I did not use the cilantro, since it is not very popular in my family. Even with those changes, the dish is exquisite! It's simple, nutritious and the flavor is wonderful!

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Kaddu (Sweet and Sour Butternut Squash) Recipe
commentBody: Delicious!! The sweet and sour is a great combo with the sweetness of squash. Used mustard seed, in place of fenugreek, half of a jalapeño, and cherry tomatoes instead of romas. My squash had to cook longer than the 10-15 minutes (maybe 30) and I added small amounts of water for steam. Otherwise, the dish came together easily and had loads of flavor. Served with rice.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Kaddu (Sweet and Sour Butternut Squash) Recipe
commentBody: ho hum. not at all flavorful

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Kaddu (Sweet and Sour Butternut Squash) Recipe
commentBody: I love this one! I got better on day two and three. I used Kashmiri chili powder and wow, the heat is beautiful paired with the ginger!

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Kaddu (Sweet and Sour Butternut Squash) Recipe
commentBody:
4/15/2022
I replaced the fenugreek with mustard seed, the brown sugar with maple syrup and used Aleppo pepper for the chile powder.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Kaddu (Sweet and Sour Butternut Squash) Recipe
commentBody: Perfect as is. Didn’t think the squash would tenderize so fast in zero liquid but it did. Amazing. Once the prep is done it’s very fast.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Kaddu (Sweet and Sour Butternut Squash) Recipe
commentBody: This recipe is delicious! I was delighted that it uses the fenugreek seeds I have but rarely use in my own Indian cooking. Like other commenters, I was dubious about the added sugar but trusted Priya and went for it, and I’m glad I did. The extra sugar plays off the lime juice and creates a new flavor profile. I was also dubious about the tomatoes, but they, too, are necessary. Recipe made exactly as written, except for some curry leaves I had on hand. Can’t wait to make this for Thanksgiving!

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Kaddu (Sweet and Sour Butternut Squash) Recipe
commentBody: I’m afraid this one was a rare dud for me. I was pretty faithful to the recipe. I just didn’t love the flavor and texture.  If I were to try to tweak this recipe (I do love fenugreek), I might roast the squash cubes ahead of assembling the curry. I think a little caramelization might help.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Kaddu (Sweet and Sour Butternut Squash) Recipe
commentBody: Made this last night with an array of other Indian inspired dishes.  My kids and I loved it...btw, kids are 29 (haha).

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Kaddu (Sweet and Sour Butternut Squash) Recipe
commentBody: This is amazing!

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Kaddu (Sweet and Sour Butternut Squash) Recipe
commentBody: It’s amazing!

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Kaddu (Sweet and Sour Butternut Squash) Recipe
commentBody: this was amazing! I used only a small amount of squash so I quartered the spices et al. I used a regular tomato instead of Roma. I've no idea what asafetida is. Anyone?

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Kaddu (Sweet and Sour Butternut Squash) Recipe
commentBody: Have you tried asking Google?

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Kaddu (Sweet and Sour Butternut Squash) Recipe
commentBody:
Hella good flavours.  Yum on the spices
The eggplant I purchased happened to be 3 lbs and it was perfect

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Kaddu (Sweet and Sour Butternut Squash) Recipe
commentBody:
Eggplant...hmmm.

An interesting substitute for the butternut squash that this recipe is based on.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Kaddu (Sweet and Sour Butternut Squash) Recipe
commentBody: really good!  followed recipe exactly, it was a big hit!

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Kaddu (Sweet and Sour Butternut Squash) Recipe
commentBody:
Flavor packed and delicious. multi steps are a lot to do and worth it. 

First time I used fenugreek seeds and GLAD I did. Taught me how to add a new flavor profile. 

I had half the squash (1#) and I used 1 potato to add bulk. Still I used full recipe of rest of ingredients including plenty of lime juice. Delicious.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Kaddu (Sweet and Sour Butternut Squash) Recipe
commentBody:
acorn squash instead of butternut, and I used 1 can diced tomatoes
Mustard seed instead of fenogreek

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Kaddu (Sweet and Sour Butternut Squash) Recipe
commentBody: Is there a substitute for fenugreek seeds?

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Kaddu (Sweet and Sour Butternut Squash) Recipe
commentBody: It’s really worth seeking out fresh fenugreek.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Kaddu (Sweet and Sour Butternut Squash) Recipe
commentBody:
This was sensational. Everyone needs more ways to cook winter squash and this is so different from the usual and so interesting. 
When recipes use fresh tomatoes I would like to see the canned equivalent, because COVID = living from the larder, and in the winter, canned tomatoes are better than wooden Romas. I used 2/3 of a 15 oz can of diced tomatoes.   I suppose Ro-Tel diced tomatoes with chiles might also work.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Kaddu (Sweet and Sour Butternut Squash) Recipe
commentBody: Excellent and different. Worked with bottled lime juice and no cilantro garnish but would like to use fresh lime and the cilantro if possible. Next time try to serve with a non-tomato based protein main dish.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Kaddu (Sweet and Sour Butternut Squash) Recipe
commentBody: Love love love this. Just did not have the asafetida. I think I will include this as my orange element for our two person Thanksgiving meal.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Kaddu (Sweet and Sour Butternut Squash) Recipe
commentBody: Would pumpkin work as well, or is it too watery?

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Kaddu (Sweet and Sour Butternut Squash) Recipe
commentBody: Trying this in the slow cooker so will likely look much different. Also using canned tomatoes. I did not have asafetida but did add garam masala.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Kaddu (Sweet and Sour Butternut Squash) Recipe
commentBody: I had two problems with this recipe, using the recommended timings the squash broke down. And the sugar amount was OTT. Far too sweet for me. Should recipe read teaspoons?

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Kaddu (Sweet and Sour Butternut Squash) Recipe
commentBody: I read that mustard seed and maple syrup could be a substitute for fenugreek so I used mustard seed and maple sugar(instead of brown). Tasted very good and sweet and sour to me. Had Trader Joe’s umami seasoning so added a tsp for good measure.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Kaddu (Sweet and Sour Butternut Squash) Recipe
commentBody: I saw this recipe recently on Priya's low-waste Thanksgiving feast video. WOW! This dish is amazing. I only had fenugreek powder so I used a little less than 1 tsp to slurry in with the turmeric and I tempered whole fennel seeds (maybe 1/2 tsp?) at the beginning instead and loved how that came out. Also used canned tomatoes... because quarantine. Will make this again!

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Kaddu (Sweet and Sour Butternut Squash) Recipe
commentBody: Too sweet for me.  Cut sugar in half or altogether!

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Kaddu (Sweet and Sour Butternut Squash) Recipe
commentBody: Fantastic flavour. Loved it.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Kaddu (Sweet and Sour Butternut Squash) Recipe
commentBody: Do you think Kabocha squash could be subbed for the butternut?

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Kaddu (Sweet and Sour Butternut Squash) Recipe
commentBody: In my opinion all winter squashes are fairly interchangeable (except for spaghetti squash which is its own slightly weird thing), so I would say to use whatever is available. They do vary somewhat in how dense they are and therefore in how long it takes to cook them.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Kaddu (Sweet and Sour Butternut Squash) Recipe
commentBody: Really enjoyed this! I didn’t have fenugreek on hand and omitted the brown sugar bc it honestly was sweet enough without. I added some water to keep the squash from sticking to the bottom of the pan and ended up with half of the squash mashed and the other half pieces.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Kaddu (Sweet and Sour Butternut Squash) Recipe
commentBody: Very good, but spicier than I like it. We had 13 guests, and some like it hot so liked this dish.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Kaddu (Sweet and Sour Butternut Squash) Recipe
commentBody: Was fine but sort of ho hum. Didn’t add heat and also the salt was low. Maybe if those were fixed, it would be better. Also, cook the squash 10 mins longer.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Kaddu (Sweet and Sour Butternut Squash) Recipe
commentBody: This was not bad - but can't take the place of the most delicious way to cook this squash, which is roasted in a hot oven with just some oil, salt and pepper so that it gets a bit caramelized.  That works alone and as an addition to certain other recipes.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Kaddu (Sweet and Sour Butternut Squash) Recipe
commentBody: May need to cook at higher temp at first to soften squash

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Kaddu (Sweet and Sour Butternut Squash) Recipe
commentBody: I had cauliflower on hand and added a couple of cups. It was a great addition to an already delicious recipe.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Kaddu (Sweet and Sour Butternut Squash) Recipe
commentBody: Yum!  Really delicious recipe (1st time use of fenugreek seeds bought months ag). Used 1 vs 2 lb veg:  1 honeynut squash, plus 1/2 sweet potato. 3/4 teaspoon fenugreek, 3/4 teaspoon gochugaru (I don’t have any cayenne), ~1 tablespoon dark brown sugar (no light), ~1 cup of diced Gardener’s Delight cherry tomatoes from the garden, and rest of ingredients as written.  Garnished with the cilantro and diced Serrano chili. Lovely meal with dal, injira, and a vino verde.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Kaddu (Sweet and Sour Butternut Squash) Recipe
commentBody: The optional ingredient - asafetida - is essential to certain types of Indian cooking. It is easy to obtain online or from South Asian groceries.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Kaddu (Sweet and Sour Butternut Squash) Recipe
commentBody: Will the dish freeze?

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Kaddu (Sweet and Sour Butternut Squash) Recipe
commentBody:
This was easy and delicious! I skipped the oil and dry toasted the spices before sautéing the onion in a bit of water. I subbed the fresh tomato for a can of diced. I put everything in a small pot and cooked it in a Sun Oven until soft. 

Will make again!

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Kaddu (Sweet and Sour Butternut Squash) Recipe
commentBody: So delicious! I didn't have fenugreek, so used fennel seeds. Also, used canned diced tomatoes instead of fresh roma. The squash took longer to cook than the recipe says. Very delicious!

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Kaddu (Sweet and Sour Butternut Squash) Recipe
commentBody: This is SO good. And pretty for company. I added chopped roasted peanuts as another post suggested and think the taste and crunch are essential. Also followed another post by steaming squash in advance for 10 mins. Made the rest of the cooking go quickly.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Kaddu (Sweet and Sour Butternut Squash) Recipe
commentBody: Loved it. I could only find pumpkin but it was delicious. Honestly I could have gone even spicier when I tasted it so I served with some green pepper flakes.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Kaddu (Sweet and Sour Butternut Squash) Recipe
commentBody: This was easy to make and delicious but with 1/2 tap of cayenne ours was tongue tingling hot. And we like spicy. I would use less next time but some yogurt mixed with lime juice, salt and pepper tamed the heat.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Kaddu (Sweet and Sour Butternut Squash) Recipe
commentBody: In the video for this recipe, the author seems to cut and cook the squash unpeeled.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Kaddu (Sweet and Sour Butternut Squash) Recipe
commentBody: Yes. I followed that example when I cooked it but I was using a long-stored squash and the peel was quite noticeable when we were eating it. In the future I will either use a fresh/younger squash or peel it.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Kaddu (Sweet and Sour Butternut Squash) Recipe
commentBody: Amazing! Put a fried egg on top

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Kaddu (Sweet and Sour Butternut Squash) Recipe
commentBody: I love this recipe - one of my all time favorite ways to prepare squash. Similar to others I sometimes have to cook the squash a bit longer, and often use canned diced tomatoes especially during the winter.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Kaddu (Sweet and Sour Butternut Squash) Recipe
commentBody: I only had two small honey nut squash, so I added in chard, which worked really well. I loved this.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Kaddu (Sweet and Sour Butternut Squash) Recipe
commentBody: Delicious!

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Kaddu (Sweet and Sour Butternut Squash) Recipe
commentBody: The video shows that she does not peel the squash at all!

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Kaddu (Sweet and Sour Butternut Squash) Recipe
commentBody: Yes, though we found that when we followed her model and didn't peel the squash, we ended up eating tough squash skin which wasn't fun. Next time I make it, I'm definitely peeling the squash!

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Kaddu (Sweet and Sour Butternut Squash) Recipe
commentBody: Made this for Thanksgiving as suggested in the video. Easy, interesting, and delicious! Flavors I haven't experienced for a long time. Probably need to find more uses for my fenugreek and asafetida.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Kaddu (Sweet and Sour Butternut Squash) Recipe
commentBody:
Didn't have fenugreek so substituted maple syrup and omitted the the brown sugar as well.
Also, substituted canned tomatoes without the juice for the fresh tomatoes. 
Also substituted Korean gochugaru for the red pepper flakes.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Kaddu (Sweet and Sour Butternut Squash) Recipe
commentBody: It added a nice spiciness to the usually rich and bland American recipes at Thanksgiving. It was also quite easy after prepping the ingredients. I coudl not find asafetida, which is too bad. It also was a nice touchstone for conversation during the dinner.  I will make this again!

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Kaddu (Sweet and Sour Butternut Squash) Recipe
commentBody: In her video, I noticed she does not peel the skin from the squash?? Is that correct?

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Kaddu (Sweet and Sour Butternut Squash) Recipe
commentBody: A little more labor intensive than butter nut squash on a sheet pan but well worth the extra effort! My vegetarian daughter loved it! Depth of flavor has everyone asking what did you put in there? Used cherry tomatoes which provided a good color contrast. It’s on repeat!

author: Ritu Krishna
recipeTitle: Indian-ish Nachos With Cheddar, Black Beans and Chutney Recipe
commentBody:
For tamarind sauce:
Heat ½ - 1 tsp ground cumin in the bottom of a dry pan until fragrant, about 30 seconds, 
Add ¼ cup each tamarind paste, date syrup/maple syrup, juice of ½ lime and a pinch of salt. 
Simmer over low heat until heated though.

author: Ritu Krishna
recipeTitle: Indian-ish Nachos With Cheddar, Black Beans and Chutney Recipe
commentBody: How do you make tamarind sauce from tamarind paste? Is it as simple as soaking some of the paste in hot water and blending?

author: Ritu Krishna
recipeTitle: Indian-ish Nachos With Cheddar, Black Beans and Chutney Recipe
commentBody: I *think* you can use a ratio of 1 Tbl paste to 1/2 c water, microwaved until it can be mixed (1 min?) You might then need to tweak the spices - add a little ginger, sugar or brown sugar, cumin, and Garam Masala, if you have them, as the store brands seem to have variations of these ingredients (almost always at least sugar and cumin).

author: Ritu Krishna
recipeTitle: Indian-ish Nachos With Cheddar, Black Beans and Chutney Recipe
commentBody:
2 tbsp  seedless Tamarind, soaked 
1½ cup Water
1 tsp red chili powder or paprika
1/2 tsp coriander powder
1/4 tsp cumin powder
4 tbsp Jaggery 
6-8 Dates, seedless 
Salt to taste 


For Tamarind Chutney
In a pan add strained tamarind pulp, water, , jaggery, dates, salt, and boil it for 15-20 minutes on medium heat.
Once boiled, grind it properly using a hand blender and add chili powder, cumin powder, coriander powder . Mix well .let it cool down completely. Store  it in the fridge .

author: Ritu Krishna
recipeTitle: Indian-ish Nachos With Cheddar, Black Beans and Chutney Recipe
commentBody: It was good, but maybe too good.  We ate too much and felt sick afterward. If you make it, have more than two people or control yourself.

author: Ritu Krishna
recipeTitle: Indian-ish Nachos With Cheddar, Black Beans and Chutney Recipe
commentBody: I LOVED this! The cilantro chutney would be great for all kinds of other recipes. My changes: beans are not my friend, so I cooked skinless, boneless chicken thighs in a crock pot with a masala simmer sauce from Trader Joe’s until it easy easily shredded. I had no time to go get tamarind sauce, so made a combo of the maple syrup & some sweet chutney. No cumin seeds, so I used ground cumin in ghee. I look forward to making it with tamarind sauce, but this is definitely a keeper. So much flavor!

author: Ritu Krishna
recipeTitle: Indian-ish Nachos With Cheddar, Black Beans and Chutney Recipe
commentBody:
Amazing! A flavor sensation! I added pickled jalapenos because I can't make nachos without them.
I had never used date syrup for tamarind sauce, but found some at the Indian market. This was by far the best tamarind sauce I've ever made: Heat 1/2 - 1 tsp ground cumin in the bottom of a dry pan until fragrant, 30 sec., add 1/4 C ea. tamarind paste and date syrup, juice of 1/2 lime and a pinch of black salt. Simmer over a low heat until heated though.
These are the best nachos EVER!

author: Ritu Krishna
recipeTitle: Indian-ish Nachos With Cheddar, Black Beans and Chutney Recipe
commentBody: SH in SC + all: It's easy to make friends with beans with the help of kombu, a seaweed that mediates the hard-to-digest sugars in beans responsible for the toot factor. Eden brand canned beans are cooked with kombu before canning, or you can get packaged squares of kombu (I like Pacific brand) from Amazon or Whole Foods. You rinse off a square and pop it into your slow cooker or bean pot for the duration of cooking; it first gets a bit slimy and then cooks down and disappears into the recipe.

author: Ritu Krishna
recipeTitle: Indian-ish Nachos With Cheddar, Black Beans and Chutney Recipe
commentBody: Made using papadums instead of tortilla chips!!

author: Ritu Krishna
recipeTitle: Indian-ish Nachos With Cheddar, Black Beans and Chutney Recipe
commentBody: If the tamarind sauce is a reference to "mithi chutney" used in Indian chaat dishes (likely given the green chutney), then its made of dates and tamarind + spices. That is work and those ingredients may not be everyday ingredients that some may want to purchase for a single dish. An easy substitute would be apple sauce + lemon juice + cumin powder + red chili powder + salt + water to thin to desired consistency. Most Indians who eat mithi chutney regularly cannot tell the difference in taste.

author: Ritu Krishna
recipeTitle: Indian-ish Nachos With Cheddar, Black Beans and Chutney Recipe
commentBody: These were the BEST nachos I’ve ever had. You think you’re nachoed out and then this comes along. We had no black beans, so we cut up boneless chicken thighs and pan fried them first in chilli powder and cumin. Accidentally put chutney and tamarind sauce on each layer before baking, so they were a bit soggy (which I kinda dig anyways), next time one giant layer and sauce on after. It’s true about the chhonk! Don’t miss it! These will definitely happen at our next gathering, thank you NYT.

author: Ritu Krishna
recipeTitle: Indian-ish Nachos With Cheddar, Black Beans and Chutney Recipe
commentBody: Replace the black beans with channa masala and enjoy an even more amazing nacho. Love it. It was an experiment after having leftovers from take-out, but now I just order an extra side to make this with.  Also, the scoop-shape chips work exceptionally well-- a sort of pani puri.

author: Ritu Krishna
recipeTitle: Indian-ish Nachos With Cheddar, Black Beans and Chutney Recipe
commentBody: I’ve made these nachos three times and they are always a hit, especially with the teenagers. 4 cups of cheese made it too greasy. I’ve used half the cheese and prefer it that way.

author: Ritu Krishna
recipeTitle: Indian-ish Nachos With Cheddar, Black Beans and Chutney Recipe
commentBody: Pomegranate molasses is an excellent substitute for the tamarind sauce!

author: Ritu Krishna
recipeTitle: Indian-ish Nachos With Cheddar, Black Beans and Chutney Recipe
commentBody: Do it just like that. Forget yogurt; go straight to sour cream. Forget it being an appetizer; eat it for dinner. Really.

author: Ritu Krishna
recipeTitle: Indian-ish Nachos With Cheddar, Black Beans and Chutney Recipe
commentBody:
My sister's tamarind chutney recipe:
Lemon sized ball of tamarind soaked in hot water (or 3 tsp of concentrate)
½ cup dates chopped and soaked in hot water (rehydrated in hot water)

Blend the two ingredients above and then strain.  Should yield about 3-4 cups of liquid
Add 
½ tsp ginger powder
½ tsp cumin
½ tsp red chili powder
1 tsp salt
3  Tbsp Jaggery or brown sugar

In a sauce pan on medium-low heat, reduce to 2 cups or so.  Taste and adjust.  Should be thick syrup.

author: Ritu Krishna
recipeTitle: Indian-ish Nachos With Cheddar, Black Beans and Chutney Recipe
commentBody: Just made these at the cottage! SO good. Someone even said “best nachos ever.” We did the maple syrup variation on the tamarind sauce and it was the perfect hit of sweet with the tart chutney and spiced chhonk. Recipe is perfect as-is and easy to follow. Thank you!

author: Ritu Krishna
recipeTitle: Indian-ish Nachos With Cheddar, Black Beans and Chutney Recipe
commentBody: We made this last night, I am from India so when I came across this recipe, I was thrilled. We always have tamarind sauce and cilantro chutney in our fridge.Whenever I am following the recipe for the first time, I follow it exactly. And Next time when I make Nachos, I will remove extra salt, season my tomatoes and onions with chat masala and lemon juice also add some jalapeños . and chicken of course!

author: Ritu Krishna
recipeTitle: Indian-ish Nachos With Cheddar, Black Beans and Chutney Recipe
commentBody: Chamoy sauce (found at most grocery stores in central TX, not sure about nationally) also works well in place of tamarind sauce-- hits the same sweet/sour/spicy notes.

author: Ritu Krishna
recipeTitle: Indian-ish Nachos With Cheddar, Black Beans and Chutney Recipe
commentBody: These were unreal. I would actually order nachos at bars if they tasted like this. All nachos will be compared to these from this day forward, and they will all fall short.

author: Ritu Krishna
recipeTitle: Indian-ish Nachos With Cheddar, Black Beans and Chutney Recipe
commentBody: A hit at neighborhood potluck. I thought the cilantro chutney would be too spicy for kids but they didn’t notice. The recipe makes a huge quantity of the chutney; however. We barely used half of it. I couldn’t find tamarind sauce or date syrup so I used date nectar. It was watery. I think maple syrup would have been a better alternative.

author: Ritu Krishna
recipeTitle: Indian-ish Nachos With Cheddar, Black Beans and Chutney Recipe
commentBody: Less chips! Double beans

author: Ritu Krishna
recipeTitle: Indian-ish Nachos With Cheddar, Black Beans and Chutney Recipe
commentBody: OMG this so tasty.  I had never used date syrup but happily found a bottle at a local market and have tamarind paste in the refrigerator.
