But heck, we're just Ohio rustics.   We don't use ghee, we use wax.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: use browned butter instead of ghee for nutty flavor

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Yes, please add the quantity of coconut oil to the Ingredients! We were setting out to make these & realized there’s no quantity listed - we don’t want to end up with too-oily dipping chocolate!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: It's 1 tsp, per the newspaper recipe.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Like Nicole, I could not get the mixture to form into balls either. Added the extra ghee and everything. The mixture wasn’t even remotely close to being solid enough to hold any sort of shape. Just a bowl of slightly spiced peanut butter. Eventually gave up, what a waste.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I've similiarly found the mixture difficult to roll into balls. (Too powdery before I added extra ghee, but much more made them greasy and sloppy.) Do not despair! It's my fourth time making them and I think I've perfected it: I squish the semi-powdery mixture into misshapen lumps, only very loosely approximating balls. I freeze for ~1 hour. I then roll into spheres just in time for chocolate-dipping. They've been worth some trial-and-error!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
I cooked this yesterday night. It was not easy to make the balls . I had to freeze the paste for 1 hour in order to be able to shape them.
The milk chocolate was too runny and after freezing again I needed to cut out the edges that formed in the bottom of the balls. It´s delicious, I will try to perfect it next time, by using less ghee and less coconut oil.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Yum! My family of Indians from Ohio thought they tasted like a perfect, rich fusion of buckeyes and laddoo (not huge laddoo fans). Like others, despite following the proportions to a "t", the initial peanut/jaggery/ghee blend was too soft (consistency of peanut butter), not moldable; adding additional peanuts did not significantly thicken. Put blender jar in freezer x1-2 hours then able to form into balls! Rest was easy. Used semi-sweet chocolate instead of milk chocolate, and sprinkle of salt.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: For the non-Indians laddoo is also used as an affectionate term for someone a little plump and lazy, like my 85-lb dog Bruno. Someone on the big side but ultimately harmless. Emphasis on affectionate!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Ahh yum -- my favorite for flavor are ladoos (laddoos, laddus -- so many way to spell these delectables) made with roasted chick pea flour.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: These were delicious! I tried basic peanut butter balls and these cardamon ones. These were way better. Be careful though, I almost killed our little Cuisinart grinding peanuts. It was hard to get these to roll into balls, so I just squished them into triangular tubes (closed my fist on them) and I think as long as you smooth out the finger marks, they look fine. It was 100% a good choice to sub melting chocolate for melting chocolate + oil.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
Native Ohioan here. Thought these were delicious and the textural issues in the other comments are easily overcome. My main issue is that the recipe only made 10 Midwestern-sized buckeyes, which is about a single serving around here. My mom would take a jar of peanut butter and a bag of powdered sugar, alternating between the distress of a mixture which was too powdery and then too loose, until suddenly she had an actual mixing bowl full of buckeye mixture. That was about the right amount. Otherwise, I love the use of whole peanuts and the jaggery flavor is delicious! I will make these again, and I might explore some flavor additions with the chocolate: orange zest, vanilla beans, chili powder, etc. 

PS When you have your mixture and it’s too liquid for forming balls, just dollop spoonfuls onto wax paper and freeze them for like 10 minutes. When you get them out you can easily roll them into balls. Then proceed with the recipe. I only had a block of jaggery and tried to grind it into a powder with little success. The buckeyes would be more evenly sweetened with powder so I’ll look for that next time, but it’s fun to get pops of sweetness too, so it worked out.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Is there a substitute for ghee?  Unsalted butter?

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Thank you for this recipe. It brings back memories, as my mother (from Pittsburgh) always made the more original version for her grandchildren.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
Recipe needs an edit or two. Is it use ghee or coconut oil?

Or is the coconut oil an additional ingredient.

You do not define what the laddo mixure is.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Directions for the laddo mixture is in the first paragraph.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I could not get the laddoo mixture firm enough to form balls.  It was always too liquidy.  I tried adding more ghee to firm it up and eventually gave up.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: What is jaggery and where do you find it?

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Jaggery is an Indian sugar, sometimes called Gaur. You can find it at the Indian store or on Amazon! Brown sugar can be substituted, but you may find it sweeter

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Happy failure as I tried to use peanut butter (it has seized up and thought it might work in this recipe). Since my mixture was too liquidy, I heated it up (rationale: evaporate some water). When this failed, combined it with rolled oats to make a peanut oat ladoo. Delicious still. The flavors are really good.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: Dear NYT cooking Eds, recipe sounds delicious. One request: can you please advocate a different marination technique, one that doesn't require adding yet another chicken-juice soiled, never-recycled zip plastic bag to our trash? Couldn't the chicken be tightly wrapped in parchment 'en papillote' and left to marinade in the fridge in a bowl? Or, simply marinated in a small bowl and covered snugly with a cut-out parchment blanket?

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody:
The bowl you suggested (just large enough to hold the chicken+marinade should do just fine - this is what Indian cooks used in the pre-plastic era. Use your hand to massage the mixture before covering (with cling wrap or parchment) and refrigerating.
  Variants of this recipe use about 1/2 cup whole-milk regular yoghurt as part of the marinade.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: I never use a plastic bag to marinate—just a glass dish with a lid. Make the marinade in the dish, add the protein, massage a bit, and cover, flipping the protein a few times during the marinating period.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: I'm SO glad someone has brought this up.  Thank you, PB!  Many recipes now call for marinating in plastic bags, and it is such a wasteful and harmful practice. Plastic waste is literally choking our waterways and killing our wildlife.  Please, please, don't advocate or use this method of marinating.  A glass bowl, preferably with a lid that can be washed and reused over and over, is so much more responsible and works equally well.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: I actually made this dish and it was everything I hoped it would be exceeding my expectations. Thank you soooo much! The lectures on plastic bags were so redundant without any “thanks” to PK for the hard work of securing this delicious recipe!

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: @Diane M -but why do people think (at least they seem to) that keeping recipes is charming in some way?  I never understood the point.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody:
@Diane M -
The plastic bag discussion is about an *improvement* to the technique, NOT a criticism of the recipe.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody:
A little soap and water and the zip bag is ready to marinate your steak!!
Don’t knock it till you try it.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: I do this all the time -- wash, dry, and re-use zip-lock bags for marinating and everything else. I put a the tiniest bit of white vinegar or even bleach in the soaking water, to help cut grease. The only time I throw out zip-lock bags is when they get a hole in them and, cared for lovingly, that can take years. Cheers!

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: Exactly.  I regularly re-use ziplock bags.  But then I also drive my cars till they have over 300,000 miles...

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: Agreed. I picked up some reusable silicone baggies on Amazon-they're available in large sizes that would do the trick here.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody:
I confess I haven't made this recipe (yet), but I'd like to weigh in on the marinate-in-a-plastic bag question.   I use my trusty CorningWare shallow baking dish with lid all the time for recipes that call for marinating in a plastic bag.  It's always works out fine - just stir once or twice, depending on how long you need to marinate.  
And I agree: it would be nice if recipe-writers didn't recommend the plastic bag all the time.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: Make it. It’s lovely. We are having it tonight for company. In the oven at 450

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody:
Made as written, delicious. I always use cilantro stems in a marinade, reserving the leaves for garnishing. 

I’ve also adapted the marinade for bone in, skin on thighs/legs by adding 4 oz pineapple juice to the marinade. Marinate overnight, remove from marinade and then grill the thighs/legs.   The addition of pineapple juice helps the skin to char and crisp beautifully on the grill. Very pleased either way.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: @mosaicYes, thighs bone-in skin-on always over breasts.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody:
Trader Joe's has a newish product called Amba Mango Sauce, comes in a tough bag with a cap, refrigerated. Contains water, mango, green mango, sunflower oil (not much), garlic, salt, spices, turmeric, paprika. I'm using about 2 t in this recipe instead of mango powder and leaving out equivalent volume olive oil.
I've used it as a marinade, with other stuff, for beef and pork, very good. On the interesting but delicious and unusual taste spectrum.
Bag is bright mango color!

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: Thanks!

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: I cooked this in my oven alongside some fingerling potatoes I was baking. A 400-degree oven for about 25-30 minutes. I used bone-in breasts sliced into three pieces each. The marinade is very tasty. An easy and delicious dish. Good for week-night meals.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: I used a pyrex covered dish, it worked fine.  No plastic was released into the environment and the chicken soaked up all the marinade.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: ...you're awesome!

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody:
Wonderful flavor. Ended up marinating for 2 nights. I have an electric stove, so had to leave on low for about 30 minutes before chicken was finally done but still very moist and tender. Reduced pan juices and poured over chicken and veggies.
Served with roasted cauliflower, onions and sweet potato chunks, topped with slivered almonds and raisins.
Husband pronounced it a great success!

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: This was super tasty.  Since I didn't have two hours to marinate, I settled for an hour at room temp.  Plus, I added some yogurt to help tenderize the meat.  The chicken turned our moist which is hard to do with breasts.  Served with pitas, mango salsa for heat, shredded cabbage with cilantro and lime, and yogurt sauce with some lemon juice, salt and pepper.  This is a do again recipe and very versatile.  It could be used for stand alone chicken or as a taco filler.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: Editor: Add "it to pan". Do you mean the marinade or just the chicken? If the latter will it generate enough liquid for steam?

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: just the chicken. The next sentence tells us "cook in batches if necessary"

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: My guess is that “it” refers to the chicken, not the marinade.  If the marinade was added, I don’t think you would get the chicken cooked lightly golden underneath.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: Based on what they said it is just the chicken. You remove it from the bag, shake to remove excess marinade and then add to the pan.  So idea is to get rid of a lot of marinade at this point.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: My spouse made this with half the amount of chicken (2-person household) but full marinade - it was _the_ best thing we’d eaten in recent memory. Cooked it again later that week, same result. Couldn’t get enough, so he tried a third time, but used a full amount of chicken (leftovers!) with again, full marinade, not understanding that the amount of marinade would make a difference. Came out bland with an unappetizing texture. Advice - double the marinade and this recipe will blow you away.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: The marinade was very good, but I wouldn't use the cooking method again.  I would cook on a sheet pan at heat for 425 for either boneless breasts or bone in breasts (my preference) to 165 degrees.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: The picture shows a sauce. Can you tell me what’s in it?

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody:
It's a Riata
1/2 cup plain yogurt
1/2 cup chopped seeded English hothouse cucumber
2 tablespoons chopped fresh cilantro
2 teaspoons chopped green onions
1/4 teaspoon ground coriander
1/4 teaspoon ground cumin

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: It is obviously a raita. You can easily find a recipe for one with yogurt and mint and maybe cucumber.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody:
Looks like a Raita
1/2 cup plain yogurt
1/2 cup chopped seeded cucumber
2 tablespoons chopped fresh cilantro
2 teaspoons chopped green onions
1/4 teaspoon ground coriander
1/4 teaspoon ground cumin

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: Excellent marinade.  I substituted boneless, chicken thighs last week and followed PB’s recommendation to use a bowl rather than a Ziplock bag.  Perfect!  Tonight, I used the same marinade for shrimp and grilled them.  Another winner.  Tofu next week?

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: This was FABULOUS! and, very easy. I made it exactly according to the recipe and the chicken was full of flavor and perfectly cooked. Served this with Samin Nosrat's Basic Yogurt Sauce. Our sides were roasted cauliflower and steamed Rapini.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: This was delicious! Made as written but used sumac instead of dried mango powder. Used the leftover herbs + lemon to make a yogurt sauce and served with spiced potatoes and peas. The marinade is fantastic—I want to try it grilled.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: St your suggestion, I used sumac. Just happened to have some.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: Anyone tried this on the grill? Looks like this would make my house reek for days.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: Chicken marinade is delicious! I used thighs, marinated overnight, and grilled them and it came out perfectly. I would add a little more salt next time. Both my husband and 5 and 10 year old raved. The NY Times Mango Tamarind Chutney recipe from David Tanis is a perfect accompaniment! Do look it up! I also served with raita and roti.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: This was fantastic! I marinated for 2 hours and made the recipe as written. I'll definitely be making this again. I'd like to try this technique with other marinades too. Served with Samin Nosrat's basic yogurt sauce, naan, and Bobbly Flay's roasted turmeric cauliflower.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: Delicious marinade. My chicken breasts from the farmers' market  (1 lb total - 2 halves) were not fully cooked after the recommended saute/simmer/steam times. Even with a minute in the microwave to finish they were still super juicy and flavorful. This recipe is a keeper- next time I will pound the chicken breasts to flatten them a bit. Looking forward to slicing the leftovers over a green salad with mint and cucumbers.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody:
Marinated Cx thighs overnight.  Decrease garlic to 5 cloves( although I love garlic, 8 cloves seemed too much. Cooked chicken on the grill.  Did not shake off excess marinade. OMG!!!!!!! Excellent and great reviews from my guests.  Served it with a mint yogurt sauce found on NYT, naan and sauteed spinach with cumin and tumeric roasted carrots.
You can order the  dried mango powder on Amazon if you can't find it locally.  Use it! It adds a unique and pleasant flavor!
My new go to chicken recipe!

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: I started using Stasher silicone bags. They are also dishwasher safe :-)

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: Super tender and juicy chicken breast cooking method. I pierced the meat with a knife for more marinade distribution, and flattened it through a bit of pounding with my fist before letting sit over night in the fridge. I think I'll use this cooking method with other marinades as well.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: I made this tonight; my youngest son, 17, says this is the BEST chicken I have ever made and the best he has ever had.  I only had 1.5 hours to marinade - it was still really great  and easy to make.  I did not add the oil to the marinade and it still turned out so would likely leave this out again in future. Thank you to you and to your Aunt Sonia for sharing her recipe!

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: I've made this and a variation many times. Great recipe. The amchur/mango powder adds a bit of tang and sweet sour-ness. I've added paprika and a touch of yogurt to the marinade for elevated texture and as a tenderizer for the meat. Also, serves very well as kabobs instead of strips and with a cucumber raita (whipped european style plain yogurt + shredded cucumber + salt + pepper + chopped mint leaves)

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: 35 minutes, my eye!  Took me about that long to make the marinade from fresh inredients!  I'm sure I'll like the final dish, but the time estimate is way, way low.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: I marinated  chicken thighs for 6 hrs and flavor was very strong. Also I roasted them at 425F for 50 min. Great !

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: HUGE HIT! The marinade was fantastic - although I ended up putting the chicken on a rack suspended over a 9X13 glass pan and just roasting it in the oven at 425 for 25 minutes. Made a huge batch of raita to go with it, and my husband raved about it. Leftovers were delicious the next day.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: I cooked this today but with boneless chicken thighs (breasts are usually dry), also with amchur. It was delicious. I would add a little more salt than what's called for.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: Where did you get the amchur?

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: You can get amchur (they spell it amchoor) from thespicehouse.com

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: @Carol Burlap & Barrel has powdered Amchur. It’s become one of my go-to umami boosters, along with their tomato powder and black lime.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: Have you ever tried baking the chicken breasts instead of cooking them on the stove?

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: Good idea:  reduce extra marinade and serve with chicken.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: They Cayenne kind of overpowered the rest of the flavors. But I second what a couple other commenters have recommended: the leftover marinade makes a great pan sauce (I added a little butter and cornstarch)

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: After reading all the arguments against using a plastic bag to marinate the chicken (actually I stopped after 10), I put all the ingredients in a dish. It was immediately apparent that the mixture was not a paste.  Then I put it the food processor. That didn’t work either. I ended up putting the mixture in a plastic bag as the recipe said and was able to smash it just fine. The  chicken was great. Cleaning up was not. Moral of the story: Follow the recipe first.  Save the planet another way.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: I've been using quarantine to lose some pounds so always looking for chicken breast recipes - not my favourite part of a chicken! I made this without the oil (see losing pounds) and it is life changing. Juicy, tender, flavourful. I feel like I broke my diet somehow it's so delicious. Thank you thank you. PS no plastic ziplock is needed. A covered bowl does the trick.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody:
I usually marinade my proteins/meat in a shallow Coring casserole dish and top it with a silicone cover.  I followed this recipe but used 8 skinless boneless chicken thighs, added 1/2 cup of full fat yogurt (as suggested by a fellow commentator), then grilled over charcoal. Roasted green beans and pita bread rounded out the meal.  It was delicious.
Basmati rice and roasted okra could be another combination.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody:
Additional note on spice scale:
My husband who preferred “mild” at most Indian restaurants thought the amount of spices in this recipe were just right but my son who usually requested “medium” would have liked a bit more spices.  The next time I make this dish, I think I will just split the difference.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: This recipe is fine but there is a Middle Eastern chicken on NYT Cooking that is infinitely tastier that has a similar base but uses abundant herbs to really brighten up the chicken. It’s a favorite on rotation at our house and this recipe reminded me of that just not as good. Try that one!

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: I wonder how these would turn out cooked sous vide and then quickly seared?

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: Made this without the amchur, let the chicken marinate overnight, and cooked as directed. It was delicious and moist with lots of flavor. I served it with steamed spinach and cauliflower rice garnished with cilantro, mint, and slivered almonds. My beau and I gobbled it up.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: I use plastic bags regularly for marinade. All you need to do is wash it with soap and water, let it dry over the end of a large handle and reuse it. I never throw them away.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: This is one of our favorite recipes! We put the chicken in thin pita wraps, top with feta, chopped cucumber, red onion, cilantro, mint, and a cilantro yogurt sauce made with yogurt, olive oil, cilantro, salt, lime and cumin. Delicious!

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: What do you do with the excess marinade? I would saute it, reduce  it  and use it as a garnish

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: Terrific, easy, and DELISH. The low & slow approach produced succulent texture and the spice marinade created its own jammy sauce. Sliced breasts into fillets for portion control so I needed only 7 minutes on low and then only 5 minutes off heat. Keep the faith. While this technique seems odd, I was delighted by the taste and texture. Served w/ my favorite rice: https://cooking.nytimes.com/recipes/8742-herbed-basmati-rice?action=click&module=Global%20Search%20Recipe%20Card&pgType=search&rank=

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: I confess, I had no dried mango powder, I read about the possibility of tamarind powder and at least had a sense of the flavour.  So I used one of the mangoes from off my tree.  It was absolutely beautiful.   I also did make slits and slide some of the marinade in as suggested.  I was doubtful it would cook through but the cover and steam with that little bit of liquid was just right.  I had made a cilantro dressing (greek yogurt/light sour cream) and prayed that would work with it and it did.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: This was spectacular. I was crunched for time so marinated for the minimum two hours, but it still came out very tender. I followed the cooking time instructions exactly (15 minutes covered with the heat off cause I was using bigger pieces of chicken), and it was perfectly cooked. I added a small sprinkle of salt at the end, which I thought was needed. A huge hit with dinner guests.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: Mildly spicy, delicious. Early Sunday I spatchcocked a chicken, set it in a shallow pan and added the marinade ingredients. I sliced the garlic instead of chopping. I threw in whole mint and whole cilantro leaves. Otherwise I followed directions, omitting mango powder. I rubbed all on to chicken, then marinated for 8 hours in the refrigerator, turning once. My husband grilled it. A keeper.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: wow this was very tasty! marinated in my glass pyrex :) ran out of coriander about half way, but I imagine it being overwhelmed even with the full amount. Garlic and ginger are very potent (but so delicious). I also cooked the chicken in the entire marinade (rather than shaking it off) because I hate wasting all of it when it adds that extra flavor!!

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: I've made this about ten times -used all kinds of chicken and grilled it - used sumac instead of mango powder.  Makes a mess of the grill but totally worth it.  Have marinated for an hour and for several hours and overnight.  Incredibly delicious, time after time.  Thank you so much for this wonderful recipe.  I'm making it right now for tomorrow.  This time I'll marinate in a glass bowl and stir it around a few times.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: Plastic bags? Mix marinade n breasts in a suitable casserole then microwave: the results r great - marinade forms a nice juicy sauce. Micro in minute segments til [nearly] done; if a breast micros unevenly, cut across the thickest part, then put the 2 thick ends against the casserole sides, (since the middle of the dish tends to micro faster), and give it another 30 secs to finish. Just had some last night...can't wait to try this combo... also, no  plastic bags to pollute my kids future!

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: Made this tonight, let it marinate for 5 hrs in the fridge, added a little buttermilk to the marinade. My boyfriend said he loved the flavours, and that it turned out delicious. The time wasn’t enough to cook the chicken breasts we had, but they were very large (.4kg each), so it ended up taking around 25 min, and I kept the heat on low (gas stove).  Will definitely make again!

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: Made quick work of all that dicing with a mini-processor, adding oil/lemon juice after first blitz. Reduced coriander from I TB to 1 tsp but still overpowered a bit. Followed cooking instructions. Chicken does not brown after only 1-2 min/side. Concerned any longer would turn garlic bitter. Chicken was tender/tasty, but doesn’t look as pictured. That’s ok when sliced, used in a wrap/tort, but wouldn’t want to serve it as is. Looks a bit anemic w/out enough caramelization.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody:
To be honest, this recipe is NEVER enough. I have to double it, because leftovers they are divine if there are ever any.
The first time I made this I felt so bad that some of the marinade was left, so I threw a few handfuls of green beans in, swirled them around and put them on top of the whole casserole. Weird? Maybe, but delicious for sure. Thank you, Priya for prying this amazing dish from your auntie. It's a treasure.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody:
Well.  We loved this and served it with our quarantine must-eat-today asparagus and mini-peppers (both cooked in foil in the oven).  Sorry to the
anti-waste folks.  We do try. 

But...oh, the mint.  Oh, the red pepper.
Oh.

Hubby wrapped his seconds in a tortilla.   This chicken, peppers, asparagus in one forlorn tortilla.   SO delicious, but a lovely dipping sauce of yogurt and cucumber....?

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: I used boneless, skinless thighs, substituted sumac per other's suggestions, and grilled the chicken.  Served with Ottolenghi's basmanti rice and chickpeas (mixing cultures, though it does use curry powder), over finely chopped greens from my farm share with a dollop of yoghurt on the side.  No mortar and pestle so the marinade went into the miniprep.  A meal fit for a king!

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: Tasty. Very good grilled. Because the marinade does not penetrate the chicken and chicken breasts have no flavor, I used chicken thighs. Great combo of spices. I made some separate from the marinade to serve as dipping sauce. Very good. Tried with raita, but surprisingly wasn't that great.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: This may be considered cooking heresy, but I cooked the breasts in the marinade, simmered until the chicken was cooked through, and the liquid had reduced a bit. It was fabulous over white rice. I also marinate using a bowl rather than a plastic bag

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: Loved this recipe. Since the marinade needs to be combined into a "paste", I got the idea to put the ingredients into a mini food processor instead of squishing them in a plastic bag. It worked fantastic and covered the chicken easily  so I could marinate it in a glass dish. Next time I'm going to skip the mincing and chopping and put it all in the food processor at the start.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: This is a perfect spice combination and FWIW I like to use diggi mirch (a pepper blend, pretty hot) that I get an my Indian grocery. And to echo other comments--the lecturing on just about everything, including plastic bags--is tedious (although sometimes it's so outlandish as to be enjoyable).

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody:
What is the sauce on the side

Thanks

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: I wondered the same. I made a raita, it's cooling in fridge now. Getting ready to cook chicken soon. Looking forward. https://www.epicurious.com/recipes/food/views/cilantro-yogurt-sauce-51154890

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: The sauce looks like raita:  yogurt, shredded cucumber, coriander leaves.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: Made this last night - 1.5 pounds of chicken thigh and 1/5 pounds of chicken breast. Made yogurt sauce, cucumber salad, and frozen garlic naan to go with. The marinade smelled amazing and in the future, I will grind the garlic and ginger instead of chopping finely so that they make more of a paste. This is going into the regular rotation for me!

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: I followed the recipe except added all the marinade to the pan after browning the chicken.  I had 3 breasts weighing a bit more than 1/2 pound each.  I cooked it for 10 minutes and let it sit for the maximum 15 minutes.  The chicken came out moist and flavorful.  We loved it!

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody:
I made this dish as is, except I didn't have amchur and I baked the chicken breasts at 425 degrees for 30 minutes with the marinade in the baking dish.  It came out delicious!  Served it with Tzatziki from Trader Joe's on the side, wild rice medley and the watermelon, tomato and feta salad from the NYT.  Excellent summer dinner.  

And, I marinated the chicken in a bowl :)!

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody:
Really good. Ordered amchur online to make it, then had the chicken with garam masala smashed potatoes. I didn't have fresh mint so I actually added a drop or two of mint extract to the marinade. Which didn't ruin it! Could've used some more heat, but still very flavorful.
Only other differences: I minced up fresh turmeric into the marinade and baked the chicken at 425, in lieu of the skillet route.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody:
Raita
1/2 cup plain yogurt
1/2 cup chopped seeded English hothouse cucumber
2 tablespoons chopped fresh cilantro
2 teaspoons chopped green onions
1/4 teaspoon ground coriander
1/4 teaspoon ground cumin

Use bowl instead of plastic bag.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody:
Thank you for this wonderful recipe. The first time I made exactly as directed, and Oh-My-Sainted-Aunt! it was delicious. Next time I made it, I had an easy hand with the cayenne. I shouldn’t have messed with perfection. 
This is now on regular rotation and my family’s life is all the better for it.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: I've been making Priya's chicken for several years now and it is a staple that I return to again and again. So simple and ridiculously flavorable and moist! I prefer boneless, skinless thighs and I put them in a cast iron skillet, throw it in a 350 degree oven with the convection on for approx. 30 minutes. Moist, golden and delectable.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody:
Delicious, but didn't the Times just do an article showing that marinades don't work?  (It gets in at most a half cm.)

You'll get the same quality dish if you just brine the chicken in salt, and then slather on the sauce right before you cook.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: I wouldn't wash and reuse a ziplock bag that contained raw chicken. An appropriate size glass bowl, etc. is a better option. I do rinse and reuse all my ziplock bags--except for those that had some sort of protein, onion (or anything that spoiled) in them. Rinsing will also confirm that bags are pin hole free.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: Made this tonight, to the letter (fresh lemon juice, mint and cilantro, amchur etc) and marinated it all for about 4 hours but was underwhelmed. Hardly any flavor and the cooking method was interesting but I did feel I overlooked them.  Kinda wondering what all the fuss is about…

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: I marinated for several hours but the chicken was dry and not tender at all. What did I do wrong?

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: Delicious, a big hit. Served it with a simple yellow rice (basmati with turmeric). Will def make again.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: 5-star marinade – thank you Priya and, especially, aunt Sonia! It's so good, that after flipping the chicken and browning the second side a bit in Step 3, I added the remainder of the marinade over the top for the final cooking stage (which is rather what the photo accompanying the recipe looks like). Will try barbecuing next time, and also try it with fish. Find amchur (dry mango powder): you'll love it. (And a glass bowl works as well as a plastic bag, but who needs to be lectured on that?)

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: I prefer thighs to breasts.  Thoughts re: cooking times if I substitute them?

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: Would this recipe work for grilling?  Sounds delicious but I really, really love the taste of charcoal grilled meat.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: Could you use boneless chicken thighs, instead of breasts?

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody:
How many tablespoons of ginger paste and garlic paste in stead of cloves of garlic?
Would  tikka Masala hurt or help it

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: What do you think about grilling the chicken breasts rather than cooking them on the stovetop?

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: I would grill this before pan steaming it.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: Can you give me  instructions for baking (on the bone) instead of cooking on the stove?

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: I guess you could put the chicken in the 350F oven for about 10 mins (total guess on time). Cover the pan with foil or a lid to keep the chicken from drying out, maybe.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: Wish we had marinated overnight as suggested, but time only allowed for two hours. Flavor were good but not as strong as wanted. I think next time I will sous vide the chicken in the marinade and finish on the stove. But can see having this again, especially in summer as I think it would be delicious cold.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: Looks promising! I like spicy recipes like this, but my wife gets migraine triggers from fermented ingredients (vinegar, soy sauce, etc.) and also specifically from turmeric.  Any thoughts on - how essential is the turmeric for the success of this marinade? And suggestions for substitutions?

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: I sometimes use paprika as a turmeric substitute.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: Meh. Good but not great. Over cooked a bit bit followed recipe exactly.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: I actaully dry fried the coriander, turmeric and chille a bit. mango was not part of the mix; went light on the garlic and used tamarind instead of lemon. Great recipe that allows different mixes

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: Very tasty. Used bone in drumsticks and thighs, and roasted in the oven at 425 for 35 minutes.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: I made this last week for a dinner party. Everyone loved it. Used cooking method as described. Chicken was moist and flavorful but did not look like picture. Served breast whole not sliced.  Also prepared NYT Tamarind mango chutney. Perfect pairing. I did not use all the chicken and it stayed in marinade for another 48 hours and these pieces cooked up just fine. Also served Merchant Cauliflower couscous and sautéed rapini. Repeating menu for another dinner party this week.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: I was so pleased with this. The boneless skinless breasts were moist and flavorful I didn’t follow cooking instructions though. Roasted them in oven on sheet pan. 450 till done

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: Made this last night and it was excellent!  I marinated it overnight in a Pyrex dish because I didn’t have any ziplock bags and it worked fine.  I added a little yogurt at the end to make a pan sauce and it passed the picky 12 year old boy test.  Served with rice and roasted broccoli. Great recipe, thanks!  This is a keeper.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: Quite good, but turned out a little overcooked using the timing indicated. I would make it again but maybe check the temperature for done-ness sooner.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: Big hit with a larch crowd. Definitely process the garlic, ginger, mint and cilantro. I would reduce the ginger and cayenne, lots of burn if not paired with a yogurt sauce (like tzatziki). Garnished with preserved lemons and sun dried tomato.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: Made this for BF who declared it to be his favorite, even though I didn't pay attention to the recipe and left the burner on medium after I put the lid on and *really* overcooked the chicken.  Excellent recommendation from previous commenters to serve with Bobby Flay's Turmeric & Ginger Cauliflower.  I also made some saffron rice (Jamie Oliver's recipe) and the yogurt sauce.  BF ate it all mixed up, and I made a wrap out of mine.  Whole house smells like garlic still but worth it!

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: Nice recipe. I used only 2 garlic cloves, skipped the amchur. It was still quite flavorful and worked well with John's raita ((below) to which I added the juice of 1 lemon for some zip) and Gordon Ramsay's remarkably simple delicious broccoli soup (Youtube).  I pounded the chicken breasts to 1/2" , marinated as directed and cooked it on the grill. Next time I will deeply score the edges of the chicken to create more "crunchy bits".

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: So many good things about this recipe. Marinade is a flavor blast. I use a Corning Ware dish with lid to do the job. I love the cooking method. It requires a pan with a tight fitting lid. It works like a charm. I’ve had success with chicken breast halves and boneless chicken thighs. Follow the method, and you will have moist and highly flavored chicken.Only conundrum is what to do with all the leftover marinade....so much flavor!

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: The flavor tasted “dumbed down” for an American palate. It also took much longer to cook.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: Great flavors! Baked it at 350 for 25 minutes and the chicken tenders were perfect. Next time I’ll probably halve the garlic to make there ginger shine more, as it can overpower it.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: Best marinade! Cooked this for the first time in a shallow dutch-oven, using two large chicken breasts for 2 people. Cooking method worked perfectly on an electric stove, 12 minutes as the final heat-off resting time. I spooned the remaining marinade over the browned chicken breasts before putting the lid on. Served simply with steamed pumpkin and broccoli and garlic bread. Even had leftovers for the tastiest sandwiches the next day. Definitely making this again!

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: I’ve made this as written many times. Last night I used this marinade on boneless skinless chicken thighs and cooked them in the oven at 425 for about 40 minutes. Delicious.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: A nice herbaceous marinade that you can adjust to suit. I make a array of similar marinades utilizing herbs, citrus, chilies and spices to suit cuisine. I spatchcock small whole birds or bone in thighs and go indirect on a grill. I like to serve taco style w condiments or with different grain salads.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: People: wash your plastic bags, already. "Single use" is lazy.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody:
marinate in a covered bowl is fine. thighs, marinated overnight. Raita on the side:
1/2 cup plain yogurt
1/2 cup chopped seeded cucumber
2 tablespoons chopped fresh cilantro
2 teaspoons chopped green onions
1/4 teaspoon ground coriander
1/4 teaspoon ground cumin

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody:

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody:
Amchur
In fact it has qualities as lemon or lime juice and if you do not have amchur, substitute three tablespoons of lemon or lime juice for one teaspoon of amchur.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: I made this for family and everyone liked it. I marinated for about 5 hours then grilled and there was a ton of flavor. I paired it with potatoes which was ok but if I did it again I would pair it with rice or maybe a cabbage slaw.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: Delicious and different! I grilled it and then reduced the marinade and poured it over the cooked, sliced chicken breasts.. very moist and easy! served it with roasted veggies on the grill at the same time, brown rice and Samin's yogurt sauce... Can't wait to make it again. Next time maybe some greek yogurt in the marinade, and finding the amchur!

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: Grilling as kabobs turned out fantastic! The leftovers are amazing cold on a picnic! Thanks for such a stunning meal!

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: I made this last night.  What a great marinade!  I used those big fat boneless chicken breasts.  It would have been better with either small Chicken breasts or with breasts sliced as cutlets.  This method of cooking does not work with those big Chicken breasts, forcing you to put it back onto the stove and turning up the heat to get the chicken done.  But the recipe itself is great, just be forewarned not to use those big fat chicken breasts everyone sells nowadays.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: This recipe is very good served alongside basmati rice. I don't know why the author tells us to shake off the excess marinade, which is very tasty. I simply dumped the whole thing -- chicken and marinade -- into the skillet and later used the rice to absorb the extra sauce. And yes, when a recipe calls for the use of a plastic bag, I just put the ingredients into a bowl and marinade in that fashion. Use less plastic!

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: For chicken sliced thinly, do 5 minutes on low and 5 minutes off the stove.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: I included the marinade in the pan so it would cook and we’d have some delicious dipping sauce for the chicken and our side of roasted cauliflower. It’s delicious.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: SO delicious. I like to add extra garlic, ginger and cilantro. Also, if possible, marinating overnight makes it extra delicious. Such tender, delicious chicken! Thank you for this recipe!

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody:
This was fantastic. Marinated 24 hours and baked for ~25 min at 425°F. Served with cucumber-mint raita, balela chickpea salad, and tandoor bread toasted in a pan. 

Might up the ginger and garlic next time.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: This is a 5/5 - made it a few times - always great. I have BBQ’d it rather than using the oven. Comes out wonderfully.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: Can you just bake this in the oven rather than on the stovetop? If so, what would be the cook time and at what temperature?

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: I always keep a can of frozen organge and apple juice in the freezer to dip into for marinades, sauces, etc. I used a teaspoon of the frozen orange and 1/2 teaspoon of the apple in hopes that it would replicate the taste of mango. I don't know how successfully I matched the flavors, but it was yummy all the same!

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: Has anyone ever tried grilling these?

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody:
Made as written. Incredibly moist, tender meat. Served in pitas with raita. 

My Gujarati-style raita:

Plain non-fat Greek yogurt 
Seeded finely diced cucumber
Shredded carrot
Salt
Cumin and mustard seeds, popped first in hot oil then drained.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody:
Amazing dish! 
I took the advice and marinated in a glass baking dish. In order to shorten the time of marinating and cooking, we also scored the breast lightly. 

Then at the time for steaming, I put the marinade on top of the breasts.

In the end, served with plain yogurt. Absolute device!

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: A great recipe and I’m inspired to make more Indianish food. Why waste all that delicious marinade?  I dumped it into the pot of jasmine rice I was making and it was glorious. I’m old enough to remember cooking preplastic. Any covered bowl will work. I don’t believe the marinating requires vacuum packing. One thing I’d like to see in these recipes…how long will they keep?

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: Hi, can we substitute another herb for cilantro, everyone in my house thinks it tastes like soap.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: This is about a recipe.  If you don't want to use a plastic bag, no one is tying you to the track.  Meanwhile, let the rest of us enjoy the recipe.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody:
Substitute for amchur 1:1 lime or lemon juice.  In this recipe...
1 tsp.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: Absolutely awesome and is on the “repeat” list at our house. I cut the oil I half as we try to limit fats and used a non-stick skillEt. The idea to use the Basic Yogurt Sauce (no oil) was excellent so thanks for that! Accompanied with a roasted ,elder of cauliflower, red opinions, and sweet potato seasoned with a bit of Herbs de Provence.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: Too much discussion about plastic!  Just use two nested bowls with chix submerged inside.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: Delicious! Baked on a sheet pan because I didn’t have time to babysit the stove and it still came out great. I (to my dismay) did not have coriander so I substituted dashes of tarragon, basil, and cumin.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: I made this exactly per the recipe. It was terrific and the chicken was cooked perfectly. Has anyone used this method of cooking chicken breasts with other marinades?

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody:
Left off the hot spices (family doesn't like heat... Sadly.) added a few swipes of lemon zest using a microplane (surfer?) zesting gadget. Then served over rice with the juices from the cooked chicken spooned over the breasts and rice. Was a hit: and, more importantly, I loved it! 

P.S. Rinse out the plastic bag and reuse it. It's not a big deal. Really. There are far more things to concern us regarding plastic.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: This was delicious .The recipe great, the plastic bag discussion self righteous distraction. The recipe doesn't have to dictate the container you use for marinade, You can make that decision your own. Sheer silliness.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: Doubled up all the ingredients and marinated a combination of boneless chicken breasts and thighs for 6 hours and grilled over high heat and then low heat to finish. Served with raita on the side. Everyone loved the combination of the warm spiced chicken and the cool sauce. Will definitely make this a BBQ staple.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: I love using as much plastic as possible. Great recipe.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: This looks delicious, and describes a "char" on the finished chicken, but directions say to cook pieces to a light golden brown. Somehow I'm missing something that would create any sort of char on liquid-marinated chicken that is only "shaken lightly" to remove excess liquid. What am I missing?

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: Regarding the plastic bag question:  I’ve heard more and more about how micro plastics seep into our food from contact with plastic.  Maybe no one else cares (although it’s cited as a possible cause of a range of conditions).  I do wash and reuse plastic bags, but I think a recipe column may need to address this or at least keep such concerns in mind.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: This was great, giving a big shout out to Auntie Sonia.  I made a change in my cooking technique the second time around though.  I cut the chicken breasts into cubes and marinated them in a bowl with a lid in my fridge; threaded the cubes onto wooden skewers and grilled the chicken until it was cooked through.  It was delicious, and the marinade was amazing.  Speaking of marinade, I put all ingredients in my food processor and it came together in no time.  This make our summer grilling list!

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: I cooked this exactly as directed. Marinated overnight. The method was delicious but it really needs more salt. Next time!

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: Do not use a plastic bag to marinate chicken instead use a glass or ceramic bowl. Marinating the chicken and oil causes chemicals to leech from the plastic into your food.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: Was not wowed by this.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: I have made this twice, and now it is part of my summer picnic spread.  Once I cook, I serve it at room temperature.  I have made it the day before and refrigerated it, too.  Just return to room temperature.  Don't throw out the pan juice it's great to use once strained.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: Made the marinade almost as written - combined ingredients in a bowl and mixed to combine, no amchur. Chicken thighs instead of breasts. 3 hrs in the fridge, 30 minutes on the counter to bring to room temp. Grilled them 7-8 min per side. Marinade was more than adequate for 6 medium sized thighs. Outstanding!  Can't wait to make the leftovers into chicken salad or slice cold on warm Naan. Just need to find some mango chutney...

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: Meh. Would not make again.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody:
I am Indian and made the recipe with the addition of 1 cup yogurt.  I also split the marinade and used 1/2 for a block of tofu and 1/2 for the chicken.

The marinade soaked for 2 hours.  I think overnight is the way to go.

Still really tasty and well received!

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: Yum!!! Delicious and moist! Definitely will make this again (as written).

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: I made this for just two chicken breasts and did not have quite enough garlic and I used lime juice by mistake. It was fantastic and I can't wait to make as directed! So moist. Great flavor (I used called for amounts of all ingredients) and seems to be a forgiving recipe. Ace. Thanks, Priya!

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: I am sure lime juice could be substituted for dried mango (amchur} to give it the tangy taste.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: I started preparing everything and then i found out that I won’t be able to cook it today or tomorrow. Will it be okay if I freeze the marinated chicken then thaw it for the day I can cook?

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: This was fine--but I was hoping for more punch, especially with an overnight marinade. Maybe if I cooked it on a grill or the smoker?

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: In the U.S., chicken breasts are typically too dry. (Not so in India & Nepal, where I've spent 10 years, working - Global Polio Eradication Initiative (GPEI). I will try this recipe but instead use chicken thighs, adjusting cooking temperature & time accordingly.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: Those spice quantities look made for people who don't like spices. Are they for real or are they Americanized?

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: I split the breast 1/2 way to create a larger surface for the nice sauce and cook faster. Marinating in a stainless bowl covered. Will try with naan bread and the yogurt cucumber sauce tonight.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: I agree with Diane M.  Many young cooks do not have many bowls of any kind (except maybe cereal).  So it's fine to encourage using a plastic ziploc bag to marinade and thoroughly squeeze the marinade though the chicken pieces.  We are absorbing techniques as well as cooking and preparation.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: I made this dish following the recipe closely.  The chicken did not come out as flavorful as I had expected.  I think salting the chicken separately before adding to the marinade would help.  Also, marinading overnight.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: I would love to try this marinade with shrimp (not a fan of the chicken breast) but wonder whether the lemon would “cook” the shrimp and render it mushy. If shrimp isn’t a good idea, maybe I’ll try it with pork of some kind.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: Have not made this dish yet … but I wash (inside out and rinsed out) my plastic bags in a mesh large bag with a towel load in the washer.  Cold water works and I then air dry them in drier.  My sister taught this lesson to me.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: I keep wanting this to work for chicken-loving family (have cooked 3 times) as I am not fond of dry chicken, but with my available chicken breasts (1.7 lb for 2 breasts) the timing is off. Cooking in a Lodge cast iron ceramic pan. Needs far more specificity to be a good recipe.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: This was excellent. I made exactly as described. Make sure to use full cutlets, not thinned.  Technique yields juicy breasts - usually an oxymoron . Wondering if this technique could work with a different marinade eg Greek with lemon juice, olive oil, garlic, oregano?

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: Absolutely delightful!  Chicken is so moist.  Love the cooking technique.  Thanks for sharing such a family treasure!  One comment on the ziplock bag comments.  To those that don't want to use them, don't.  Stop the complaining.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: Could someone make a suggestion of the best way to complete cooking if the chicken is not up to temperature?

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: Cook it a little longer under the lid. It will get there, so be patient.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: What are your thoughts on grilling as opposed to stove top?

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: Delicious and juicy.  And what a gift to learn how to cook chicken breasts to perfection. I will definitely make this again.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody:
Loved it, but even after marinating for 6 hours, it didn’t permeate the chicken. Thoughts?
It was great with the NYT recipe for naan, Raita, the Tomato rice (meh-ish) and Sifton’s Manchurian Cauliflower

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: I may have left too much marinade on it or my chicken was previously frozen because it released lots of liquid cooking and exited a little rubbery. But the flavor was excellent (especially with a tinch of fennel after) and the timing perfect at 167 degrees for a medium but not super thick breast. I’ll make it again.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: I eagerly tried this as written. I hoped this cooking method would be a remedy to dry chicken breasts. They were very very dry even though juice accumulated in the cooking pan. Marinated over night and thought they were bland. Yogurt and cucumbers in the marinade might have saved the day. Won’t make again. Marinade sounded interesting.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: Sounds like your overcooking the chicken maybe? Honestly shocked that you found the marinade bland and the chicken was ridiculously moist. Just made it earlier tonight. Used thin cut chicken breasts but otherwise followed the recipe exactly and we were very pleased with it. I usually sous vide all my chicken these days but I found this cooking method to be on par with it as far as keeping the chicken moist.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: Are use the sous vide for the chicken. And then seared it at the end. Good flavorful and juicy doing this way boys are making the chicken dry

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: and for those of us averse to cilantro and mint????

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: I have a severe allergy to cilantro/coriander.  Does anyone know of an acceptable substitution?  I am also allergic to cumin which makes entire cuisines off limits.  I'd love a curry recipe that does not contain those ingredients.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: Just leave it out. So many other spices it won’t be missed

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: Is this best to be enjoyed with rice, bread or by itself? Thanks

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: Made this last night and ate it with frozen garlic naan from Trader Joe's but plan to make basmati rice tonight for leftover lunches. I imagine it will be delicious either way.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: It was delicious, I forgot to add the ginger, so I'm guessing is even better with it, but without it it's just so good. Totally recommend it. And the smell is amazing. The only drawback is that is a little dry, but probably just need to leave in the steam a little bit less time. or use some sauce with it, but not sure what the sauce should be.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: This was delicious. I added 10 cloves of garlic and doubled the ginger. Taking the advice of another reader, I chopped the spices and herbs in a food blender.  Everything was whisked together in a bowl,  added to a pan with  boneless chicken breasts & marinated for about 4 hours. Served with pearl couscous, it was a smash hit. Beautiful for the hot months. Don't hesitate at serving this with a big dollop of yogurt topped  with a fresh mint leaf on the side.  Thank you Ms. Krishna

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: Any have some recommended vegetables / vegetable medley that would pair nicely with this marinade?

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: Meh. This is the first NYT recipe to disappoint. Served it with raita which made the dish.  If I make it again I’ll grill the chicken.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: Exactly.  The cooking method dries out chicken breast.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: The marinade was delicious but I’ll never again make this with boneless breasts.  Very dry.  Needed the yogurt dipping sauce to moisten.  I’m sure these would be superb with boneless thighs, which is what I usually use, but I this package of breasts earmarked for chicken salad and used them for this instead.  Mistake!

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: Got most of my spices at Patel Brothers Market in Pittsburgh,PA. Markets also in other parts of the country. Great store! So much fun to shop!

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: I substituted chicken breast tenderloins and grilled them in a grill pan.  Tasty, quick and tender. An easy modification. Very good!!

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: I used 3 quite large very thick (10 oz ea) chicken breasts and the method described cooked them perfectly, not dry at  all.  For thinner breasts I would cook less.  Only sub I made was for the coriander, I didn’t have any, so I used Garam Masala instead.  I might actually keep it that way when I make it next time.  Flavor was delicious.  Spooned the liquid left at the bottom of the pan over some quinoa, delicious!

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: I almost exclusively only cook chicken thighs and am always skeptical of chicken breast recipes no matter how juicy people swear they are.  I decided to give this recipe a try having seen it demonstrated on the Today Show recently and being interested in the steaming method.  First, I followed the recipe exactly and the chicken was raw.  I had to keep cooking it after letting it sit.  The marinade is excellent but the chicken breasts are... chicken breasts.  Next time thighs.  Should have known!

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: I made this exactly by the recipe and it was phenomenal. The marinade is exceptional and you can taste all the spices. Only used three large chicken breasts that equaled about 2 pounds and it came out so lovely.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody:
Do not marinade overnight, as I did, per recipe. I was hesitant. Chicken was “mealy” having cooked some in the lemon. 
Overall, I didn’t care for the flavor, others liked it. Though strongly flavored it seemed muddy, for a lack of a better word. Obviously, I’m in the minority. Really wanted to like this.
Combined it with side of Cucumber & Cilantro Riata and Naan, which was great.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: I marinated for 2 hrs, and that seemed more than enough!

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: I made this on a whim tonight - I had all ingredients except for the mint and cilantro, so I went with it! It was delicious, and the method for cooking the chicken was perfect - I will follow it with other marinades for sure, they were tender and delicious. I didn't miss the mint and cilantro, but I'm sure it would add dimension to the flavor. Even my 9yo picky eater proclaimed it ten thumbs up - not only did it taste delicious, he said it smelled sooo good!

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: I love this as a marinade for chicken thighs, either grilled or seared in a cast iron pan and finished in a 400 degree oven.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: Would like to try amchur powder-has to be tree nut and peanut free.  Does anyone know a source?

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: Amchur is dry mango powder, made from unripe green mangoes. Its incredible. Indian stores will have it. And, so does Amazon.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: It does not say how much or what type oil to use. I used 2 tablespoons of ghee. Otherwise, I made it as written. I used a stainless steel frying pan. I was afraid it might stick, but the juice from the chicken naturally deglazes the pan as it steams.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: I made it last night, it was really delicious! Chicken thighs, served over quinoa with asparagus, baked sweet potatoes and raita. Marinated in a lidded glass container. Thank you NYTimes!

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: Please everyone, don't stress about using plastic bags for marinades;  after use just wash them inside and out with hot soapy water, thoroughly, and rinse in hot water;   dry them inside and out and hang them - open - over something, to dry, like a wooden spoon in the container beside the stove, that sort of thing.  Check that they're dry inside and out, before folding and putting away for another use.  Seems just the same as using and then washing out a glass/metal/plastic bowl to me.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: But why even use plastic bags in the first place when you can just use A BOWL?

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: Eventually, that plastic bag will have to be discarded and will be in the ground forever.  A bowl will be around for much longer (I have bowls from family that are over 100 years old !! )

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
