commentBody: one person suggested adding 1/2 c whole milk plain yogurt to marinade. Another made Raita to go along with it: 1/2 c plain whole milk yogurt, 1/2 c chopped hothouse seedless cucumber, 2T fresh cilantro, 2 t scallion, 1/4 t coriander and 1/4 t cumin

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: Heat must be high to initially brown the chicken

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: This is fantastic-next time I'm going to make it 2 days prior and grill it over coals as kababs-although it was quite nice cooked in a skillet. Would be wonderful with naan and cucumber raita-

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: Wonderful exactly as in the recipe! And yes, I used a plastic bag ;)

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: don't overcook chicken.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: I blitzed the remaining marinade and then reduced it with the pan juices for a super intense sauce. I used sumac instead of dry mango powder. I think it can use less acidity than that but it was delicious.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: Sauté the chicken in 2 tablespoons of butter, and cook for 3 minutes on each side.  Cover the pan for 8 to 10 minutes to finish cooking in their own heat.  Arrange the sliced chicken breasts on individual plates.  Add 1 teaspoon lime juice to the drippings in the pan, bring the mixture to a boil, and pour over the chicken.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: I use reusable, dishwasher safe, airtight silicone "ziplick" style bags for my marinating.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: Not bad, but could be better. Use only 1/8 teaspoon cayenne? Definitely add the salt next time. Try cooking on "lo" to make it less rubbery.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: Can this be BBQ's

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody:
Cook with Riatta sauce:
1/2 cup plain yogurt
1/2 cup chopped seeded cucumber
2 tablespoons chopped fresh cilantro
2 teaspoons chopped green onions
1/4 teaspoon ground coriander
1/4 teaspoon ground cumin

Much better if baked.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: Very yummy! A big hit with kids too! Will definitely make again! Omitted the flour because didn’t have on hand and cilantro and mint - still came out wonderful!

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: I love garlic and ginger, but the massive quantities in this marinade overpower everything else. The mint and cilantro were nowhere to be found and the fragrance of ground coriander was crushed under the onslaught. If you're going to make it, use the half the amount of ginger and a quarter the garlic.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: I’ve made this recipe three times now and my kids love it!  It’s great flavor!

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: Fabulous. Used Kashmiri chili instead of regular one and it added a welcome kick as well as a beautiful color.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: When Step 3 sayS “add It to the pan” does that mean the marinade or the chicken? I first thought it was just the chicken but then thought again, maybe add marinade and then chicken.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: Sounds like a perfect marinade to grill the chicken with. Anyone try it on the grill?

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: I thought so too, until my hubby mentioned the marinade may drip thru the grill, and not coat the chicken like the stovetop option...he doesn't even cook! But he does grill...

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: I am going to try grilling this evening.  I will report back.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: Making tonight...sounds delicious!

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: I can go you one even better. If a ziploc bag has only had bread or pasta or some other dry ingrediant, I fold it up and put it in the freezer and use it for the marinades  for which no amount of washing the bag can be used again. So Ive used it twice, i can cut it loose.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: For the readers who are griping about plastic bags, this is pretty easily solvable and I'm surprised such educated readers had not thought of solutions earlier. We use either a steel of glass bowl for marinating and if a bag is truly needed, you can use re-usable/ re-washable food-grade silicon bags. I've also used biodegradable plastic bags if the solution is only used for a short period as these bags do degrade or leak if used for longer periods.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: Added a Riata & mango chutney and this will be in heavy rotation. Grilled the chicken to get some great char.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: Fantastic!

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: Wish I had pounded the chicken breasts as they were too thick and came out a touch dry. But the marinade was amazing .

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: Delicious, and leftovers make great chicken salad sandwiches.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: I expected this to be better than it was - it definitely would have benefited from the Riata sauce that John shared in another comment. That said, the cooking method turned out beautifully juicy and perfectly done breasts. I will definitely use that again with a different marinade.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: I followed this recipe exactly, and the flavor was good but the chicken was dry.  I may swap for chicken thighs, or let the chicken sit for less time next time.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody:
Make sure that the chicken breasts are very similar in size/weight. We had one chicken breast that ended up being dryer than all the others (and, of course, I served that one to my wife who noted, "This is over-cooked." All of the others were perfect.

Left-over idea: I chopped up the remaining chicken breasts (pretty small pieces), added some shucked grilled corn, chopped celery, chopped apples, and used a mix of hummus, mayo, avocado, and cumin. Delicious!

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: I would’ve browned the chicken longer. Also I use chicken thighs and cooked for the same times in this recipe. It turned out great. I will definitely make again

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: Just okay. Served it with a green salad and warm pita bread.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: “Best chicken I have ever made” according to my husband. I was shocked my kids liked the tumeric and coriander. It is a keeper. Perfect for a picnic. Perfect for the WFH lunch leftovers. I grilled the chicken. Amazing.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: Followed cook times but ended up with very overcooked & dry chicken.  The admonition to leave the lid on in the last off-heat 10 min stage meant I didn't check the temp and I ended up at 185, far beyond ideal.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: I found the dish to be dry, wish I had "peeked" with my thermometer. Would try again but pound chicken, add yogurt to the marinade, and use my air fryer...and make a cilantro, ginger, lemon sauce.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody:
Leftovers make a fantastic next day chicken salad! 
I took two breasts (with leftover sauce) added 1/2 cup mayo, two stalks of chopped celery, a tablespoon or two of chopped cilantro and mint (also left over), salt and pepper to taste. Delicious.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: This was easy and delicious

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody:
Amchur is available on Amazon or any store that caters to South Asian cooks. 
In a pinch, puree some fresh mango and it works just fine. 
Re-use your bag (of course). 

Best tip: check Youtube for the video. It will answer all your questions. Plus it's hilarious as are all things Priya. Plus Sonia weighs in via face time.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: I made this recipe with chicken thighs a couple of weeks ago, and it was truly fabulous, especially grilled. Didn't have the exotic mango spice (hello! We're sheltering in place here), so for today's batch I'm going to add a slice of dried mango.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: This was delicious. I spooned the remaining marinade over the tops of the chicken before covering the pan with the lid. I hate to throw out all that flavor.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: So good! Followed directions but had to turn the oven back on to cook through. Will definitely make again. Thinking this might be good as an instant pot recipe.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: 2h marinade was not enough. I plan to try this recipe again with an overnight marinade. I think this has a lot of potential, and it was okay after adding salt. This was my first time using amchur so I won’t rate it until I give it another shot.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody:
I did not have powdered mango but this was a excellent recipe! 
Instant crowd pleaser and already a family favorite after one try!

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: Made as written except I used 8 boneless chicken thighs and cooked for 10 mins with a 10 min rest. Served with coconut basmati rice and broccoli. It was just OK. Nothing special. If I try it again, I will grill it to see if that ups the flavour quotient. Now what am I going to do with a full jar of amchur???

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: OK, but a lot of work effort for a so-so result. If I made this again, I would grill the chicken (I used boneless thighs) to see if that would result in more flavour than braising the meat. Just not as exciting as the article suggested.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: Made this for dinner tonight and it was amazing! Decided to throw it on the grill at the last minute and glad we did. Served it with cacik, toasted naan and roasted sweet peppers. Definitely going into the regular rotation.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: This chicken is delicious!!! I was wanting to try my hand at cooking Indian cuisine, and it was so fun to have these scents and flavors in my home!! This chicken is easy but DIVINE.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: Made this tonight. Only had 2 hours to marinate, so thought I would cook it sous vide in the mariso it would have more time with the marinade and not get over cooked. Big mistake (I should have known!). The chicken was soggy, and even trying to sauté it didn’t help. The flavor was great though (a little spicy; I used cayenne in the ratios indicated in the recipe). Next time I will marinate longer and then grill (too many comments about over cooking using recommended cooking method).

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: Could this be done on the grill?

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: Any other subs for the mango powder?  Maybe puree a handful of mango chunks?

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: No, amchur powder is an especially tart spice. You can order it online. If not, I think lime juice would be a fair substitute

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody:
I just used this to make chicken sandwiches and they were fantastic!! The garlic/ginger gives us the Asian/South Asian flavors, the turmeric gave it earthy aromas. Just grilled the breasts (halved) and put them in potato rolls with lettuce and tomato. Delicious! 

I saved this marinade recipe because I think it could work for most anything. And the spices are very healthy antioxidants!

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: This was excellent!  Followed the instructions and added a bit more time at the end as the chicken breasts were very large.  Served with roasted carrots and shallots and baked sweet potatoes.  Raita and chutney alongside with pita.  It was a beautiful night and with minimal fuss we had a lovely dinner and I was able to join my family outside while it cooked.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: Does not remotely live up to the author’s hype. It’s still dry, bland chicken breast. Garnishing with sour cream and mixed-in mint and cilantro helped.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: Would this recipe work on a grill?

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody:
The bowl you suggested (just large enough to hold the chicken+marinade should do just fine - this is what Indian cooks used in the pre-plastic era. Use your hand to massage the mixture before covering (with cling wrap or parchment) and refrigerating.
Variants of this recipe use about 1/2 cup whole-milk regular yoghurt as part of the marinade.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: Mmm...plastic bags should never be used in cooking. An environmental waste. How about revising this to do without that? Adding oil to a medium high skillet causes oil to significantly splatter plus is not so healthy. I flattened the breasts, roasted them instead, and they were crisp but not dry.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: For BBQ: 425-450, 10 mins per side. Will be a bit drier than the other method. But really great!

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: good! did not do exact marinade but method worked well-moict, flavorful

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: This was delicious, but I could have used less garlic - 8 gloves is a lot of garlic!  Next time, I'll try 2-3 cloves.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: Loved it! I got distracted early in and put the lid on too soon (not the ideal browning), then took the lid off to brown a tad, added a touch of Pinot Grigio I’d keep in the refrigerator since NYE, added a cup of chicken stock and served over rice. Delicious warm, soupy dinner in a bowl.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: This was excellent! Next time I think I’ll finish it with a squeeze of lemon juice.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: Cooked this in a Dutch oven with a splash of water and had no problems with dry chicken. Heavy lid probably helps things out. Very tasty. Served up with some mango chutney I threw together and this: https://www.bbc.co.uk/food/recipes/potato_and_pea_curry_19537

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: Followed the recipe exactly and it was delicious! Chicken moist and flavorful! It’s a keeper!

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody:
Confused by instruction "add it to the pan" - the excess marinade or the chicken. Ultimately I chose the former and found the chicken too strong (not too hot ). Probably too much garlic (though I didn't that would be possible) and ginger. For me, it needs a little sweetness buffer like raisins, pine nuts, maybe yogurt. I didn't have the mango powder, though it is said that is sour. 
Willing to try again with recipe clarification and uplifting ingredients!

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: The chicken prep here - without the garnish - is now my go-to for a very tasty chicken salad the next day. Lightly dress shredded chicken with mayo and a touch of Dijon; stir in torn parsley, diced bread & butter pickles, sliced small red grapes... it's a mashup that respects no cultural boundaries but tastes delicious.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: 5/22 Really delicious. Made as written. Served w sauteed asparagus Right Rice, raita, chutney. Second night served w Indian inspired carrots. Very easy. Marinated in the morning so had about 8 hours.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: So good! The chicken breasts I had were not huge: 8 oz.  Turned heat off as recipe directs, waited 15 minutes...chicken registered 132F (glad I checked).  Cooked chicken for another 10 minutes, covered, on low heat.  The chicken was done but not dry after the additional 10 minutes.  When I removed chicken to slice, I threw fresh spinach into pan, tossed with remnants of garlic and ginger, etc., left in pan: wilted in no time, delicious addition!

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: Just made it exactly as written except for the amchur and It is so good!  Ever had anything quite like it but wow, this is gonna be in regular rotation and going to the next get together. They’re gonna think I know what I’m doing. Thank you for this

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: This recipe is wonderful. I love the slow-cooking method for this chicken recipe. The long-marinade, slow-cooking of meat (from chicken to beef) in my Indian friends’ kitchens always creates these crazy flavorful dishes. This recipe creates that flavor (if you’re patient!!) and is not intimidating at all. Patience is key, though. The chicken may need more time to cook on low/cook in that steam under its cover, depending on what kind of stove you’re using. Marinating overnight yields best results

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: I dunno. With all the comments, I expected this recipe to knock my socks off. I followed the recipe, with the exception of the mango powder. I used dried orange peel instead. The chicken cooked (and looked) beautifully and was very moist and tender. But it needed more salt (which is strange, because I don’t add a lot of salt to my food) and something else?? I will make this again, maybe using more garlic or the Trader Joe marinate someone mentioned. Recipe has potential but I’m missing the wow.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: I made it for first time today with fresh herbs I had at hand- thai basil and mint. It was excellent. Used Dorot frozen ginger and garlic for ease of prep. Will try agin with a fluid and changing mix of fresh herbs.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: Substituted 1tsp ground sumac for the amchur and marinated in a bowl in the fridge. Cooking the chicken covered and finishing off heat is genius-- prevents dry chicken & saves energy AND cleanup. I reduced the leftover liquid to spoon over the the chicken. A winner!

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: I love the idea of the flavors in the marinade but for some reason, this one didn't seem to take very well. I would probably use a little heat to like chili or cayenne, and maybe even add some coconut milk for the last part of the simmer to give it a little more robust flavor.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: Is there a good substitute for cilantro for this recipe? We've got "that gene" and I was hoping that leaving out cilantro wouldn't change the flavor profile significantly - suggestions?

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: Ice is still covering the garden, but in another month, when the cilantro has recovered and the mint is making its first brave foray into the atmosphere, this will be on the menu!

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: To those who eschew plastic, wash it and keep using it.  No need to throw it away after one use.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: I am normally not a cilantro guy (the genetic thing... I can't stand the taste!) but everyone is raving about this recipe. I'm thinking it will be just fine in the marinade, I just won't use cilantro as a garnish. Thoughts from readers?

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: Any vegans out there tried this great looking marinade yet on baked tofu slabs?

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: AS one of those people who cannot stand cilantro, and so many of the recipes, lately, call for it, what can I substitute for it? This recipe looked so good, until I saw that ingredient.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: I would suggest parsley or perhaps basil?

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: Can you share what is in the condiment bowl? It looks like yogurt with cilantro. Would like to try that with the chicken recipe. Thank you.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: When I marinate I use my glass storage containers that have silicon and rubber lids. Best storage ever. I also have a vacuum storage container that can mimic overnight marinading in 4 hours.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: Can you use boneless skinless chicken thighs instead?

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: Love the technique but the marinade is nothing special

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: Great flavor. Used the chicken in romaine lettuce cups, topped with orange bell pepper, cilantro, mint, and a smear of Thai chili paste. Yummy!

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: This had wonderful flavor, even without mint which I couldn’t lay hands on at the time. Made marinade in the morning and cooked that evening. Cooking time was too long (gas stove, smallish chicken breasts.) I am looking forward to adding mint and serving with raita next time- definitely a keeper recipe for us!

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: This was bland -- the marinade did not reach the interior of the breasts despite marinating all night. My family was not impresed.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: This is scrumptious and so easy! I made it exactly as written and served it with a raita, garlic naan from Trader Joes, and roasted cauliflower thoroughly seasoned with cumin, coriander, and cayenne. A feast! Next time I may try to do a low-and-slow cook on the gas grill.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: I would like to try this with chicken thighs.  I had to cook the breasts a few minutes longer to get it to register at 165.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: I was disappointed that this recipe called for so much ginger and garlic, and yet the taste was overpowered by all the lemon juice. I used thighs instead of breast, and following the directions for the extra 10 mins of cooking at the end resulted in dry chicken.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody:
Amchur, or dried mango power:
You can find it for sale on the "Internet Rainforest", but most packages are more than you may use in a long, long time.  
I had a recipe that also called for Amchur, I substituted dried mango and turned it to powder in my spice grinder.  Recently, I found freeze dried mango at Trader Joes, which is a bit easier to turn to powder in a spice grinder also.
Enjoy!

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: Uh-mazing!!!

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: Very tasty. Breasts come out very juicy

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: This was quite tasty.  A tad too acidic for my taste, but overall great spice.  Agree needs more salt and I would double the mint and cilantro and ginger.  Next time will make as a curry and cook with coconut milk in bite sized pieces.    Served with roasted cauliflower and basmati rice.  I did not do the cooking method suggested as seems unreliable to get safely cooked chicken.  I used my corded instant thermometer and pulled at 165.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: Mortar and pestle to make the paste?  (Vs in a food processor  or a plastic bag to mash the ingredients …  I also try to avoid plastic bag use. ) Try the M&P to get the right consistency for the resulting marinate and then place in bowl, etc, to do it’s magic.  Works great.  And yes to double marinade as pan sauce. SO yummy!

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody:
What a delicious dish!
I could not find mangoe powder so used finely cut dried mangoe. The marinade was so tasty. I had a transparent lid on the pan and saw how the chicken cooked. It needed only a few min more cooking - large pieces. The chicken was so moist inside! 
Wonderful recipe
Will do it again!!!

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: This cooking time was waaaay off. Not sure how it worked for others. Followed the directions exactly with .5lb chicken breast and the internal temp is 100 degrees. Yikes!

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: I found that this resulted in a chewy chicken. I think the lid makes the whole thing a bit soggy.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: This was a fantastic recipe. We will redo it again!!!

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: On the marinating question: I use a porcelain bowl to hold meat and marinade (glass, plastic, or other container made of non-reactive substance) with a saucepan lid on top. If the fit of the lid is close enough, that's good enough. When finished put both in the dishwasher, or rinse and wash by hand.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: Turning down the heat to low and keeping covered was basically like poaching and it came out GREAT! Was def impressed how moist this came out. Will use this technique again for sure.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: Where to buy Amchur?  Indian grocery stores carry it; if you have one nearby, go!  If not, there is always Amazon - and maybe Penzey's in NYC, which is known for carrying spices - and does mail order.  It is dried unripe mango powder, in case you wondered.  If you need a substitute, a tsp or so of fresh lemon or lime juice should do nicely.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: Flavor on the chicken was good, but it turned out to be way overcooked. I usually rely on my thermometer so this doesn't happen, but I don't think this recipe was appealing enough that I would try to perfect the timing a second time.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: Paired with a roasted corn/avocado/tomato/cilantro/mint/lime salad and crusty garlic bread, this is a summer meal made for company. I especially like the prep-ahead, leave-to-steam aspect, because it allows me to focus on my guests and not my stove.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: Made it last night. Delicious!!!!!

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: This was great.  I’ve also done this with very firm tofu instead of chicken and it’s delicious.  If you freeze the tofu with the marinade it makes an amazing morning tofu scramble as it cooks down.  Today made this chicken and it was a great super fast workday lunch to tuck into a baguette/ciabatta with just a little mayo and mustard.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: This is a flavor bomb of deliciousness.  My whole family loved it.  I made the marinade and doubled it as per someone’s suggestion which gave it great flavor. I also used boneless skinless thighs instead of breasts and then threw it on a sheet pan with a drained can of chickpeas and cooked it at 425 for about 35-40 minutes until it looked browned and yummy. Served with rice, naan and raita. YUM!

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: Delicious! Doubled all the marinade ingredients as per another reviewer. Made a yogurt/tahini sauce with lemon juice, finely chopped mint & cilantro, salt & pepper. The amchar really makes it special.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: Hmm this didn’t work for me. Admittedly I didn’t follow the recipe. I made the marinade in a food processor (life is short) and marinated in a Pyrex baking dish, then just baked the chicken in the dish. Overcooked it, ugh, but the ginger/garlic left on the chicken didn’t give it a good taste. I should’ve added more salt in the marinade. I tried making a quick yogurt sauce to help the dryness and taste but that wasn’t enough. Bummer.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: Sumac is a good replacement for amchur. Use the same amount of either.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody:
Made as written but with some large, bone-in thighs. Definitely needed more cooking time than listed with that adjustment, so I ended up having to put medium flame under the pan after all that sauna-ing and waiting. The end result…could have been just as easily achieved in the broiler, IMO. 
The marinade was tasty but I could have doubled the spices. And I might redo with something to add some sweetness.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: I add a few tablespoons plain yogurt to the marinade mix & get the chicken pieces into the uncooked marinade immediately &  refrigerate for at least 10 hours. I’ve made using both boneless skinless breasts & thighs & grill instead of cooking on stove top or in oven. Thighs by far are the most succulent.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: Would this work well on a gas grill?

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: Liz, I can't see why this wouldn't work on a grill. The finished texture / moistness may be different from how the recipe intends it to be though.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: Would appreciate air-fryer notes with NYT recipes...  It is currently my preferred 'oven' when cooking in small quantities...   Thank you!

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: Why not purchase an air-fryer cookbook? Recipe authors provide instructions for the best cooking method and best results.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: Re: plastic bags - I plan to use my largest Stasher reusable bag for this and run it through the dishwasher.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: What do you serve it with?

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: No need to cook it to know it's worth five stars—the story is so nice!

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: Question.. do I throw out the excess marinade?  Can't wait to try this out.  Thank you

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: It should be thrown out if you have excess marinade in which the raw chicken has been sitting. If you wish to use the marinade as a basting/dipping sauce, prepare a fresh batch.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody:
Delicious, and yes, thanks, PK, for securing the recipe.   I do wish the plastic bag issue was not necessary to raise but, alas, it is.  And I appreciated the polite request made by PB.   I, too, use my old corningware cookware, add the marinade to the meat, cover, turn once or twice, and it works perfectly, and you don't need to use tons of water to clean out a plastic bag, an issue here out West.
Also appreciate finally getting a use for my mango powder.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: What type of mint do you use for this recipe? Peppermint, spearmint, lemon balm? I have all of these growing outside in my yard.  Thanks in advance for the reply.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: Amy, would think peppermint or spearmint would work for this recipe. Less so the lemon balm although if that were all you had, why not?.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: Has anyone tried this with tofu?

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: Just tried today. It was fine but not spectacular.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody:
Good Morning,

Does anyone happen to know what the white sauce on the side is?  Usually there is some link for stuff like that?

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody:
It's the raita previous reviewer provided:
1/2 cup plain yogurt 
1/2 cup chopped seeded English hothouse cucumber 
2 tablespoons chopped fresh cilantro 
2 teaspoons chopped green onions 
1/4 teaspoon ground coriander 
1/4 teaspoon ground cumin

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody:
I wash my plastic bags with soap and hot water and reuse until they no longer are viable
... have done this for years...

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: I find the plastic bag discussion a great relief. Of course we appreciate the recipe and I will try it soon and am sure it is as tasty as it sounds. Certainly we have to be hyper vigilant in our own small ways to reduce waste, stop the use of plastic whenever we can, and grab hold of our beautiful Earth and hug it with fervor by caring for it.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: Just jumping in on the plastic bag debate:  I have purchased several silicon quart and gallon zip lock bags that I use & wash & reuse over & over.  When I purchase food that comes in a plastic ziplock like rice or dried fruit, I wash & reuse those as well.  Haven't purchased a plastic bag in years.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: Same - the reusable silicone bags in different sizes are terrific

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: What is the white mixture, with what look like cilantro leaves on top, in the bowl to the side of the sliced chicken? Is there a recipe for that? It looks good. Thanks!

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: I’m willing to bet it’s either Raita or Taziki, both great sauces!

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: Would this recipe work on the grill?

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: I've grilled it before. Just the stove top method gives a perfect cook on it, but you can grill it too.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: Turn the ziplock bag inside out and throw it in the washing machine with cold water wash clothes (avoid the dryer though).

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: To add to the plastic bag conversation. I marinate in a bowl and cover with a plate or silicone lid.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: Any thoughts on how to make this in a slow cooker..??

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: I have Priya's first cookbook and this chicken recipe is in it and I've made it probably 10 times. It's delicious. There's also a recipe for a quinoa, asparagus, tomato warm salad and it pairs really well with the chicken.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: When your zip lock type bags get holes in them you can take them to an orchard and use them over fruit (immature) like apples or pears to deter insects - be sure the holes are large enough in the bottom to release any moisture that is created as the fruit matures.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: Has any done this using a sous vide and finishing in the pan?

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: Stasher makes reusable silicone bags in all different sizes. You just pop them in the dishwasher and they’re good to go again.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody:
A raita is an accompaniment to food (like a relish).
This is a marinade.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: This is a fantastic marinade! I made it exactly as written, just pound my breasts a bit so more uniform in thickness. It was so good. Next time I will double the marinade and reserve some so I can use it to drizzle on the finished dish! Thank you. The ridiculousness of the plastic bag comment was a waste of time. People feel the need to complain about anything and everything these days. How about you marinade in what ever you want. The purpose of the bag was for convenience sake!

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: Thank you, Priya, for the electric-stove instruction! Cooking with residual head is always a tricky business, so it's great to have an approximate time in Step 4. A cast iron skillet should retain enough heat to cook the breasts to the required temperature.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody:
Have been making this dish for years since I got Priya's book Indian-ish and I adore this recipe! It's worth investing in Amchur just to make this.
-I always add a little more salt, or crunchy finishing salt on the chicken at the end
-I usually cook all the leftover marinade, along with the juice that leeched out of the chicken during cooking, down into a chunky reduced sauce that I pour over the finished chicken and rice.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: I continually reuse Ziploc bags. All you have to do is put a bit of Dawn dish soap, and a spray of Clorox clean up with bleach fill with warm water shake around for a bit and rinse really well. Voila!

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: Serve with??? It would be lovely if authors would add a sentence about what veg or other items (rice? which?) to serve with.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: Just about any vegetable would do but broccoli comes to mind.  Snow peas?  Asparagus?  Brussel sprouts?

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody:
An Indian salaad and some roti would work, along with some plain yoghurt or raita. Salaad is plate of sliced red onions, tomatoes, cucumbers, chilis, carrots, and radishes, or whatever you have on hand, sprinkled with salt and pepper and accompanied with wedges of lime.
For roti, whole wheat or flour tortillas heated on a pan, or some toaster naans, lightly buttered, would go well.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody:
Would love to see fewer recommendations for using a sealable plastic bag. Maybe at least add an option of a sealable, re-usable bowl? Looking out for the environment!
Looking forward to trying this recipe.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: How generous of you to share a family secret. I know of so many favorites that have gotten lost because they didn't share.  I also understand not sharing for I have shown up at family gatherings and someone has made my dish; so, we had two of them.  I would never do that.  I have a friend who begged me for my baked glazed ham recipe with steps and I did share.  She tells her guests every meal that this is served that I shared the recipe.  Thank you. I can't wait to make this for my children.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: There are so many ‘mint’ types available. What type do you recommend?

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: Would this recipe lend itself to using a slow cooker?  I usually cook chicken dishes in my crock pot/slow cooker rather than on the stove.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: Quite interesting, fellow cooks.  I think k the important component in this recipe is the ACTUAL INGREDIENTS, and if or how theybwork together.  Focus on the marinating technique reminds me of the old PTA meetings....crusts or no crusts?  Two hour discussion.  Contents of sandwiches dispatched in 5 minutes.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: This is brilliant. Made w chicken thighs— cooked exactly according to recipe—and skipped the amchur. Served with basmati, mango chutney and the raita one commenter here was nice enough to post the recipe for. So Good.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: Curious if anyone prepared this in advance and used the sliced chicken in a salad. It's getting hot where I live and I'm thinking this would be great in a salad with mango and jicama or cucumber. Thanks!

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: What’s in the silver ramekin in the photo? It doesn’t match anything in the recipe.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: maybe tzatziki or some other yogurt based dip?

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: Made exactly according to the recipe.  Nothing special.  Will not make again.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: 165F is a recipe for seriously dry chicken breast. 150 is very safe even if you're fussy, kills what few bugs, if any, are in the bulk of the muscle,(food safe at 3 min@this temp, and getting there will kill the bugs no matter what, as they start dying rapidly at  145F) and your chicken will be much juicier and tastier.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: Great meal and easy to prepare

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: Made as written, minus the amchur, with chicken breast tenders. Seared in my staub dutch oven, then covered it for the steaming step -- amazing. With a bit of planning ahead, this is a great weeknight dinner.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: Cilantro tastes like soap to me.  Recommendations for something else? Parsley?  Or something else?

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: add extra mint

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: I have met so many cooks who've refused to divulge a recipe, getting either a blank stare and no response or, "Sorry, it's secret,"  when I asked. These were home cooks, not professionals. Could anyone tell me the psychological --- is it being vindictive? is it schadenfreude? --- reason people will not give you a recipe, literally taking it to their grave? I am always happy to share a recipe when asked. Why do so many refuse to share the joy in print, only on a plate?

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: I made this last night. And I'm making it again, tonight! So good and chicken was so tender!

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: Very tasty.  Followed the recipe and served as part of an easy mid week curry.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: Has anyone cooked this on a grill?

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: Made this last night, as written, except that I used bone-in chicken thighs since that’s what I had. It was amazing!!

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: Good recipe. Cooking times, like most NYT recipes should be cut in half. Marinade is great. Chicken was dust.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: Excellent, easy recipe. I couldn’t find amchur so added lime. I doubled the cayenne to give it a bit of heat.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: As a grower, I have over 40 cultivars of mint. I know that most don’t have this problem, but am I to always assume spearmint when a recipe just says “mint”?  To me it’s like saying “beef” in a recipe. Which cut?

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: Agreed!  STOP advocating disposable ziplock bags. That being said, most of us who are ditching the disposables, we know we can use other bowls, containers and such to do the job. I have a tupperware season serve container that all you have to do is flip it each time you reach in the fridge for something. Works great for dry rubs as well as marinades.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: I wish you would also show ingredient quantitative in grams.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: I decided to make more of a meal than just chicken. I used the foodie combination slow cooker. I use it to sauté onions, green and red peppers in the leftover marinade. Then I set it aside and cooked the chicken as directed and after 10 minutes I added the stirfry back in with cubed potatoes and made it a full meal. Delicious. Thx

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: Very good and quick!

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: This is a hilarious dish -- turned out exactly as if I had used PATAK'S curry paste coriander from the jar....No difference at all. Save yourself the time and effort of chopping and preparing all of these items and buy some Patak's marinade.. Marinate you chicken in PATAK'S for a few hours, make some raita and some rice -- and you're good to go for a weeknight meal.  Can't believe that this dish is somehow some 'discovery'....

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: due to diet issue had to change the lemon with substitute of sumac, used boneless thighs because we had them and couldn't marinade for 2 hours. enjoyed it but need to marinate more

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: Beware if you’re using standard supermarket sized chicken breasts, the cooking method was a total fail for me. Left with raw chicken at the end of the cooking time. I suspect this might work for chicken cutlets, but absolutely not for chicken breasts.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: I totally agree. Haven't made recipe yet, but chicken breasts at my local market are huge. When I poach them it takes almost 3 times longer than 18 minute recipe. Each one is enough for 3 meals (for shredding, sandwiches, etc). For this recipe, I'll use the smallest I can find or cutlets, as suggested.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: Others don't seem to have your problem. The cooking "time" is only an approximation. Step 5 tell you what temperature to cook to and "Check to make sure the breasts are cooked through". The recipe specifies chicken breasts 1/2 to 3/4 lb each. If your "standard supermarket size" piece is larger than that, slice it horizontally.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: Cooked according to the recipe and the chicken ended up with a pretty unpleasant texture. Kind of spongy, but at the same time stringy and dry. The marinade was good but I'm pretty sure this cooking method would work better with chicken thighs.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody:
Really good! Must have the riata mentioned in the reviews
1/2 cup plain yogurt 1/2 cup chopped seeded English hothouse cucumber 2 tablespoons chopped fresh cilantro 2 teaspoons chopped green onions 1/4 teaspoon ground coriander 1/4 teaspoon ground cumin

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: So this dish didn’t work for me and I followed the recipe. It might have been old tumeric that had more staining power than flavor. Or that I have no sense of smell. But then my husband does. I marinated overnight. Husband thought it was better as leftovers.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody:
Chicken breasts would be quite small, tiny even. or wouldn't you end up with raw chicken! 

What do you do with the leftover marinade?  Seems too good to discard.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody:
The recipe specifies chicken breasts 1/2 to 3/4 lb each. Step 5 has instructions regarding cooking time and final temperature.

The marinade has been sitting with raw chicken. Using it would be risky. If you are worried about waste, make only half the amount of marinade.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody:
Great recipe. I added a couple spoonfuls of yogurt and toasted the coriander seed whole, with some cumin seed then ground and added it.
Instead of complaining about plastic bags, use your common sense. Remove the Meat Diaper from the plastic tray the chicken came in. Then use the tray and the plastic wrapper, pressed to cover the marinating bird. You'd be recycling or throwing that tray away, do make use of it first.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody:
Good lord this was delicious!! And easy. I didn’t include any of the marinade in the pan, but I wonder whether doing so would have resulted in more delicious juices to pour on the chicken?

Leftovers made an excellent chicken salad with just mayo, grapes, pecans, salt, and pepper.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: I was only able to marinate for 2 hours.  It was still quite good, but would have liked a bit more of the delicious flavor, so overnight would be better.  Great with Basmati white rice and spicy Indian spinach.  Thanks too, John, for the Riata recipe.  Mmmm.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: Recipe says to massage the garlic, ginger, mint and cilantro into a paste in a plastic bag. How?  Pound it into submission which will destroy the already reviled plastic bag? I honestly do not understand this direction. I used my blender which made a beautiful paste. As per other’s recommendations, double the sauce. I used 8 bone in chicken thighs (dinner party). put some of the paste under the skin, the rest on top, grilled them on the barbecue.  Served with tahini and mango chutney.  Enjoyed.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: The ingredients to be "massaged into a paste" (garlic, ginger, mint, cilantro, lemon juice and 3 tablespoons oil) won't need to be "pounded into submission" as you wrote. Use your fingers to mix those ingredients together. Using this method means you won't have to wash your reviled blender.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: Wow, is that good!  I couldn’t find amchur, so I subbed some ground sumac. Make sure to brown the chicken well in the first cooking step. I served this with some raita, roasted cauliflower and mango chutney.  Loved it!

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: Sumac is a great substitution. I found Amchur in Patel's Indian supermarket. You can also buy it online.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: Delicious! Everyone loved the flavor

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: Used 1/2 of the cayenne. AMAZING

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody:
Very tender. Would also be good room temp or cold (over salad or with my orzo salad)
Was better a couple days later - stayed moist. 
Dave says use less garlic. 
Didn’t find amchar. Used 1/4 t. Cayenne.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody:
Great recipe.  Make life easy on yourselves. I turn my zip
locks inside out and stick them on the top shelf of the dishwasher.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: Tried it in legs and thighs on the grill. Fantastic! Next time on salmon in the air fryer. Great again!

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: I didn’t love the flavor of this dish, but I appreciate how easy it was to make.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: Very flavorful and cooked up perfectly moist following the instructions. Next time I’ll marinate it longer. (I did 3 hours.)

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: So delicious, this recipe delivered as promised! The cooking time was way off though. After the 15 minute steaming period the chicken breasts were still translucent. I tossed them into the oven (300) in the covered cast iron for another 10 minutes...and they still weren't cooked through! Worth the wait.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: This recipe looks very nice. I also avoid plastic because it seeps into the food. Any glass or ceramic bowl should be fine for marinating.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: Thanks for the recipe and the Riata. I was wondering what side dishes to go with this.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: What do you usually serve with chicken? Green salad? Cauliflower?

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: Any suggestions on how to air fry this recipe?

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: What salads and chutneys? This looks amazing and I would love to know what people are serving it with.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: A green salad with radishes and cukes (and a few roasted pine nuts?) with a simple oil and vinegar dressing,  a tzatziki dip, or a chutney with mango, scallions and diced red pepper might be good

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: This sounds great to me, with one exception:  I absolutely cannot abide turmeric.  (Sadly, this makes Indian cuisine very problematic for me.)  I am going to try this simply omitting the turmeric, but if anyone can suggest an appropriate substitution, I would appreciate it.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: Not an Indian spice but achiote produces a yellow color and mild taste. It is more of a Mexico or Central American spice though.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: I really dislike the taste of turmeric. Can it be omitted in this recipe w/out seriously affecting the balance of flavors? Is there a turmeric-free substitute?

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: My sister made this when I visited. It is incredible, and now on my list.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: Where did people find the amchur powder - or did you just leave it out. It sounds interesting.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: If you can...go to Kalustyan's (123 Lexington Avenue @ 29th Street) for any and all Indian products.  I believe you can also order their products on line.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: Sounds delicious. Unfortunately the incredibly annoying overzealous anti-plastic bag types have made me lose my appetite.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: I will try this recipe but here's my question. When you say "marinate for 1 hour or up to overnight", are we supposed to take that as start cooking it when you wake up in the morning? Or is overnight plus all day until dinner time okay? Why not just say marinate for one hour up to all day?

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: "Up to overnight" has been used for generations in recipes. It means what it says. If there's a danger of marinating for too long, a recipe will usually indicate "but not more than 12 hours." Over-marinating can change the texture of meat, so caution would be provided.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: So, so good.  I love this method of cooking chicken breasts and will adapit it for other uses.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: I would love to try this with a whole chicken...Prep/clean whole chicken, use spices under skin, pat in gently under skin, in chicken cavity...put in fridge to marinate...Bake in dutch oven with marinade

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: I’m going to make this with tofu and impress my vegetarian son. Sounds delicious.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: What type of fish would this work well with?

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: Exactly as billed - including the marinade wafting, summoning appetites in anticipation of this delightful dish. I also enjoyed trying out 15 minutes of steaming the chicken - a great technique, new to me. Thank you for sharing the recipe and the family story.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: Delicious and spicy.  Cooking time was longer than the recipe. Use Stasher bags that are easy to clean in the dishwasher and you don’t have to buy plastic.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: I followed the recipe as written, but the timing didn’t really work out. When I checked the temperature after turning off the heat, the chicken was still way under the recommended temp. The recipe was very flavorful, but it took me at least an extra 20 minutes.  I served it with the basic yogurt sauce from this site, a cilantro lime rice from this site, and roasted asparagus.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: Did anyone try this with turkey cutlets? If so, any suggestions for cooking time?

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: I didn't read the recipe carefully, and made it with bone-in chicken breasts with skin, and it was amazing! The texture of the chicken is perfect. Regarding the plastic bag debate, I bought 3 boxes of different sized plastic bags in Ikea a year ago, and have yet to throw out any of them. I wash with dish soap and water.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: So good. Made with chicken thighs

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: Made this recipe last night. It’s very garlicky but more flavorful today. Will definitely make it again.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: I'll be the naysayer.  I followed the recipe except that I incorrectly thought I had turmeric (I didn't) and I used 1/2 the suggested garlic.  I wound up with watery, overwhelmingly garlicky poached breasts.  I would like liked it better (perhaps) cooked quickly over high heat to avoid the poaching effect and to concentrate some of the other spices, which were overwhelmed by the garlic..

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: If you are two, make the whole recipe and next day make a risotto. Put pan drippings in broth for risotto. Even with reducing amount of rice we had a second meal of risotto. All delicious. Cooked risotto in oven - added peas and chicken at end.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody:
Delicious! Yummy flavor after 24 hours of marinating. I made it as suggested, and it was simple and delicious. I cooked the chicken right before my guests came and they sat covered, very politely, until it was time to plate. This recipe is probably very forgiving depending on what you have on hand.

Since the breasts are kind of plain-looking, but they looked nice sliced, I sliced them and laid them on top of a double ginger slaw I found in Food & Wine. It's a fun combo and complimentary!

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe

commentBody: Made it without amchur as one guest is allergic to mangoes.  It is overpoweringly garlicky, and i cut back on the garlic.  Good recipe for awful chicken but if you're paying $7/lb, this is a waste of good chicken.  IF i make it again, i'll use even less garlic and more of other ingredients.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: My husband  cannot have turmeric as it reacts with a medication that he takes and it makes him terribly sick.  I found that saffron is a substitute for turmeric.  I have it marinating now, and hope that it makes a difference.  I am finding turmeric in so many premade foods, and recipes.  Any other ideas for good substitutes?

commentBody: The late Narsai David, SF Bay Area chef and legend, once shared a recipe on his KCBS radio spot.  It was Assyrian barbecued chicken, a recipe handed down from his mother.  This recipe is very similar to Narsai's - I don't have my recipes with me, so I cannot compare and say what's different.  I will say, though, that it's delicious.  And the prep work is much simpler than this recipe.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: Or maybe 4 stars. So many NYT recipes are fabulous, but this one is OK. I ate it with a tzaziki/cuke salad for some moisture. I think the leftover chicken will make a really good chicken salad with mayo for sandwiches.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: I love these recipes!! However, I'm one of those folks who cannot eat cilantro (or even smell it) without ruining the rest of the meal. I really have tried to desensitize myself, to no avail.  It would be great if the NYT would provide a suitable replacement for recipes that use cilantro (I have heard of a combination of Italian parsley + spearmint). Thank you!

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: What does overnight mean? You certainly wouldn’t cook this for breakfast, I imagine. So how about up to 24 hours? Is that too long? If it’s too long, perhaps assemble it in the morning and marinate it in the fridge until you’re ready to cook it that evening. Overnight makes no sense to me, at least I don’t understand what you mean by it.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody:
Hello Mr. Morris

Chicken does not really need even 12 hours for a marinade. Two hours is more than enough. 

I would poke the pieces of chicken in many places with a point of a knife - if at all i am concerned about the marinade/spices not seeping into the flesh. 

warmly, 
-vin (Bombay, India)

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: Great marinade!

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: Cooked as written including the dry mango powder but had to use dried makeup lime leaves (8 instead of 4). I fished the leaves out after cooking because they were very tough. If I make again I will serve with rice and season the chicken breasts beforehand and marinate longer.  Usually I season meat and let sit for at least 30 minutes. I didn’t do this and the chicken breasts were larger so I think it would have helped it to be more evenly flavorful.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: Followed the directions exactly and even made a larger batch of marinade for my chicken. I had large chicken breasts which required longer cooking to reach the required temperature and not be pink— several minutes longer than the recipe provided. The marinade made the outside of the breasts tasty but the marinade did not permeate the chicken breast meat, despite me poking holes in the meat prior to marinading. I made another similar recipe from NYT with chicken thighs using my oven broiler. It was delicious while this was okay/good. I think that chicken breasts are too low in fat and dense. The denseness and lack of fat does nothing to enhance the flavor of a marinade. I will stick to the chicken thigh recipe.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: @KC I wanted to add that I did marinate the chicken breasts overnight and it still did not permeate the chicken. Maybe adding more salt and pounding the chicken beforehand would help.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: I have started cooking chicken breasts in a Sous Vide exclusively after trying out Kenji Lopez’s recipe on Serious Eats. No other cooking technique measures up. It’s like butter! Wondering if you could cook this recipe, with the marinade, in a Sous vide? (I wash and reuse the BPA-free bags.)

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: I two have stopped buying plastic zip lock bags. I save every glass jar that comes into my kitchen and find the right size needed to marinate whatever I'm cooking. You can shake the closed jar and the marinade covers the food so well and when your done you throw the jar into the dishwasher to sanitize.  I always worried about cleaning the zip lock bags enough specially with a chicken marinade

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody:
Just like all Priya’s recipes, the flavors are DELICIOUS. 

On my electric stove, chicken was not remotely cooked after 3 mins browning per side and 15 minutes with the lid on still on the burner after I turned it off. (I knew there was no chance it would cook if I removed it from the heat entirely, small chance it might if I left it on the previously hot burner.)

Next time I’ll brown it and put it straight in the 400 degree oven for 30 mins.

Nice meal. Thank you!

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody:
Outstanding! Loved this dish. although I did cut the chicken breasts into smaller pieces to get more marinade spread throughout. Anf I squeezed lime juice over the finished dish.
The bits and the liquid from the bottom of the pan… mmmmm… 
PS to the person who complained about the recommendation to use a plastic bag for marinating: I just mixed the marinade in a medium-sized bowl and was able to easily coat the chicken in there as well, and leave it in the fridge.

commentBody: Saw this on CBS Mornings & made it tonight - it is delicious! I served it with The NY Times coconut rice recipe & snow peas with sesame seeds, and my husband raved. Leftovers will be added to ramen for lunches. Thank you!

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody:
Super delicious BUT two important notes:
1. Cook time is not very well thought out in the instructions. I used regular-sized boneless breasts and it took a lot longer to reach doneness. Like, twice the time over low and twice the time steaming under the lid. I'm thinking the instructions were written with smaller breast pieces in mind. 
2. There is nothing in the instructions about spooning pan drippings over the slices when you serve it--do it! So, so good!

author: Priya Krishna

commentBody: It was good, but way, way too much trouble.  Probably won't cook it again.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: My entire family loved this at first bite a few years ago.  In winter months when I don’t want to grill outside, I use chicken tenders or boneless, skinless breasts sliced in half horizontally.  I try to marinate at least 4 hours using a little less cayenne due to family preferences.  I broil quickly indoors turning once and watching like a hawk to not overcook.  A grill pan would probably also work.  This is a quick, easy, healthy and well loved dinner.  Definitely serve with cucumber herb raita.  Leftovers?  You’re lucky—make delicious chicken sandwiches the next day.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Garlic-Ginger Chicken Breasts With Cilantro and Mint Recipe
commentBody: Made this exactly as directed, including the mango powder, which I had to get online. (After all, if it was this beloved by the family, why not try it at least once the way the chef recommends it? ) SO glad I did! Cooking method may seem complicated but good gracious - it is worth it!! I don’t think I’ve ever had such tender chicken breasts. Served with warm naan and a variety of Trader Joe’s sauces (tzatziki, plain greek  yogurt, zhoug) as well as some mango chutney on the side. Made a great alternative to the usual movie night taco bar and everyone absolutely loved it. Had the leftover chicken and naan with rice and a quick veggie tikki masala the next day, and that was super too.  The liquid that develops in the pan during the low heat cooking time is so delicious it’s almost drinkable, and definitely great liquid re-marinate any leftover chicken.  Hats off and thanks for sharing with all of us!!

commentBody: I just made this with sweet potatoes and was yum. The sweetness of the potatoes offset with the spices. I made a yoghurt sauce with roasted cumin powder, fennel powder and ginger. Drizzled that over the baked sweet potato with Serrano peppers, cilantro and onions that had been soaking in lemon juice and chaat masala.
