author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: These potatoes were the bomb! Live in a small mountain town in Colorado, so Chaat Masala wasn’t available. I looked on Wikipedia, as another reviewer suggested, and cobbled my own version together. Don’t let not having all the perfect ingredients stop you, life is too short!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
Chaat Masala Recipe:

    1 Cup coriander seeds
    1 1/4 cups cumin seeds
    1/2 cup thymol seeds / ajwain
    1/2 cup mango powder
    2 1/2 tsp garam masala
    1 1/4 cups black rock salt, powdered
    4 tsp black pepper, powdered
    2 tsp citric acid
    1/2 cup dried mint leaves, powdered

How to Make Chaat Masala

Roast the coriander, cumin and ajwain in a heavy-based pan till dark brown.  Remove from pan and cool.  Put all the ingredients in an airtight container.  Use as needed.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: My friends went crazy for these potatoes! I grated the ginger into the same bowl as the chopped serrano chile and let them marinate while the potatoes cooked, I found the spice perfectly balanced when I added the cool sour cream. If anyone reading this is in Atlanta- Dekalb Farmer's Market carries chaat masala, I got a whole tub of it for $1.50!!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I ended up boiling the potatoes for 15-20 minutes first,  then smashing them to bake or 30 minutes at 450. I also added the ginger to Greek yogurt instead of sour cream. Did a quick pickle on the red onion and pepper with sugar and lemon juice. Superb recipe. Definitely on rotation.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I did not have chaat masala and wanted protein for vegetarians. So I combined equal parts coriander, cumin, black pepper, twice as much chili powder, half as much ginger powder. Sautéed the peppers and ginger in olive oil, added the spices, then chickpeas. So the three toppings for the potatoes were: sour cream, fresh minced red onion, and the chickpea mixture.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: For a really healthy alternative I substituted Fat Free Plain Greek Yogurt, for the Sour Cream!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Made these for a group of people, and it did not go down well.  If I would make adjustments next time- use smaller potatoes, pickle the onion, add some indian chutney, and less red onion.  I would also take out the white part and mash it with butter, onion, cilantro, and spices first.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Made this for Diwali tonight. So good and easy. I did not have red potatoes so I just used Idahos and cut them in thirds after baking. I did mix the chili, ginger and red onion into the sour cream beforehand and let the flavors mingle. I used my own homemade mix of chaat masala. So many recipes out there it’s worth it to make your own. I love Raghavan Iyer’s.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Love these, but as a fellow Indian, had to add a sweet chutney (tamarind) drizzle on top. Then they’re perfect.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Yum! Make these with blue potatoes!! They're smaller, tasty, cook faster, and they're so gorgeous with the toppings! I experimented with the way the toppings were layered, but I think the recipe has it right. I soaked my onions in cold water to take away the strong bite. I ended up using a ton of onion to get enough flavor, so maybe I didn't need to soak them.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I made this for an Indian crowd, so wanted to be more on the Indian than the “ish” side of this recipe. I made smashed potatoes then added the toppings. Came out well seasoned, crispy and were a hit. I boiled the potatoes for 15 minutes, smashed them with fork, smothered them in police oil, salt, pepper, cayenne, then baked for 20 minutes, added the fresh ginger for another 5, then removed and piled on with the toppings in the recipe. Yum!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Friends, maRsala and masala are not the same thing 😬

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Burlap and Barrel for chaat masala.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Made as described, is good.  I sprinkled everything on top and didn't end up using all the toppings.  Sour cream flavor predominated.  Next time to get stronger flavor I will mix ginger and chaat masala into the sour cream and just sprinkle the onion/chile/cilantro on top.  Also will press onion/chile into sour cream to make sure more stays on.  Great recipe, got many compliments.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I bought powdered chaat masala on Amazon.  It was very inexpensive. The brand I purchased is quite salty so I use it in very small quantities.  For those of us in small towns, on-line purchasing can be the only way to find exotic ingredients.

commentBody: It makes a pretty appetizer to sprinkle all the ingredients on top of the dollop of sour cream, but like a lot of photogenic food it's messy to eat.  Just mix all the ingredients but the cilantro with the sour cream first.  It'll still be pretty but it really will be finger food.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Made these for a pot luck sunset happy hour. People raved about them. Truth be told, I couldn’t find chaat masala. So I subbed 1/2 garam masala and 1/2 madras curry. I liked the bright color of the madras curry. I also pickled the red onion with lemon juice and salt. Followed recipe other than that. Will make this again hopefully this time with chaat masala. I think this recipe has a strong foundation and can be modified according to what u have available. It is beautiful on the plate as well!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: this was an excellent side dish. very easy to prepare.  One note: gently cut a cross in the potatoes before cooking.  it makes pushing the potatoes up/out later.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Made this tonight and may never have baked potatoes any other way. Cut it down for two people but otherwise followed the recipe. Thought the ginger might be too strong but it wasn’t. Saw the picture in last Wednesday’s NYT and was happy to find the recipe on line.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: These potatoes were a delicious and bright tasting addition to Easter dinner, simple to make but a fresh, clean taste.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: We loved this recipe.  Served it with Indian Grilled Chicken, a really great go-together.  I used small red potatoes, which we had just dug up from our garden.  I misunderstood the direction "cool until they can handled."  I let them cool.  So we used the dressing as a dip.  The potatoes seemed to have steamed inside their skin and were soft and creamy.  Just delicious with the dip.  We have half the dip left over and I will use it instead of mayo with some leftover chicken.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: This is so good. Resist the temptation to throw all the spices and condiments into the sour cream and blitz it in the food processor. It’s still tasty, but the fun and flavors lie in the contrasting tastes and textures

author: Priya Krishna

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
When I found this recipe, I really, really wanted to make it, but didn’t have chaat masala. Here’s my cheat, using what I had on hand:  lemon zest, ground cumin, ground coriander, ground ginger, salt, pepper, dried shallots (you could use finely diced fresh shallots), chili powder.

It was fabulous. Don’t omit the lemon zest — that little bit of bright freshness really made the seasoning zing!

commentBody: This is fine. Added green & tamarind chutney and bhel mix with papdi. At the end I wish I had just made chaat.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Garam masala is not a good substitute for chaat masala. Try pani puri masala or mix garam masala with cumin-coriander powder if you only have those things in the pantry.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: These were good. If I make them again, I would add the toppings into the sour cream mixture, otherwise they fall off when you bit into the potatoes. I got the exact Chaat Masala as in the video. I needed to add more to really taste it, and I like it. Oh, don't get too small of potatoes or you really can't fit much on at all. Be like Goldilocks--not too big, not too small, just right.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: How do you make the chaat masala? Seems to be an important missing ingredient.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: it's a spice, you can buy it pre-blended

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: It’s a seasoning blend you can buy at the store.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: It's a spice blend. I would buy it in an Indian market.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Wonder if it would work with garam masala

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Love this!! So umami-filled.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Didn't find chaat masala at the Giant Eagle here, but most Indian stores will have it. I was heavy handed with the chillies and onions to lend a bit more crunch, and also added tofurky (vegan) sausage bits, and it was definitely a hit.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I always sous-vide salmon, or other fish fillets at 130 degree for 40 minuets.  That makes for perfect tenderness!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Made this and my husband and friends all loved it!Don’t be afraid to use sweet potatoes, it was excellent with sweet potatoes instead and I cooked them in 15 minutes in the Instant Pot which made the dish really simple. found chaat masala at my neighborhood grocery, I just went to the one with the international food aisle and it was by the dried spices in the Indian section. I had never tried it before but I love its slightly sour/spicy flavor and I can’t wait to find more uses! 5/5 stars!!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I used a whole chicken halved, and it cooked perfectly at 465 for 45 minutes. Super flavorful and the sauce on the side is great to drizzle on top!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
I used tiny baby potatoes, drizzled with oil and rolled around in a bit of kosher salt before baking, then flattened them a bit with the bottom of a mason jar just out of the oven. What a delightful little pop they make! I put them back in the oven and cut off the heat after about 5 minutes while I prepped the toppings. 
I also mixed the ginger and onion into the sour cream. Use labneh instead of sour cream if you have it. Love this!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: So delicious! Followed recipe as is. Compliments from my guests.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
I had no cilantro, but even without it, it was great, and so easy :-)
I also used greek yogurt instead of sour cream—much lower in fat.
Next time—cilantro!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
I just used curry powder rather than chaat masala and for peppers used jalapeño pepper. 


You do need to slice the ingredients very thinly, I would grate the ginger next time. 

It is difficult to get the “bloom” to look nice, so I would make this again but would not serve it to guests.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: We did not have chaat masala, so I subbed with a combination of cumin, coriander, pepper, chile powder, and garam masala on a 1-1-1-2-1 basis. Made it spicy but not flavorful. Also subbed half a seeds-removed jalapeño for the serrano. Next time I bake the red potatoes (or small russets?) another 5-10 minutes, and try combining spices with yogurt not sour cream. My wife disliked the ginger. But the red onion worked great. As the main course for meatless Monday not so good.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
My quarantine chaat masala substitute:
- equal parts ground coriander, cumin, garam masala and... wait for it... Tajin
- 2 parts sumac
- 2 parts julienned fresh mint
- finishing salt at the table

Also lightly pickled the onion in lime juice. 

Very tasty. Burnt the heck out of my fingers but worth it.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I had some thick cut bacon, diced it and rendered the fat. Removed the bacon, cooked onions, carrots etc in that. Cut up a juicy lemon and cooked it with the rest of the soup. Used a whole pound of ground chicken. Added bacon, removed lemon pieces before serving over elbow noodles. So good!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: These are excellent and easy, as you say. But please: sharpen your knife. There is nothing so dangerous as a dull knife!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Loved this! I watched her make these in the video on the app and it helped as I followed her recipe. When you cut them like she instructs, you end up with this amazing finished product - sour cream right in the middle of every bite. We made a mess eating them but it didn’t matter tonight. I will make them with tiny potatoes for guests, ensuring one-bite-size.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I agree with Rachel about hollowing out smaller potatoes and mixing with sour cream and spices and stuffing cases, although a lot more work. I got the chaat masala on Amazon.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: This was very good, great flavor and easy to make and went well with skillet chicken recipe.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Have used Greek yogurt (Fage) and have cheated with some previously made pico de gallo in place of pepper and onion — was fantastic. Sometimes par-cook potatoes in microwave before hitting the hot oven just to make it a faster snack. The spice is so good!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
better cook than me please turn this into an "indianish potato salad"
Made for a large memorial day party.  It was DELISH, but a little time consuming putting all the different toppings on 3 lbs of tiny potatoes.  Couldn't there be a sour cream dressing with all the toppings in it, and then rained with cilantro?  Can't quite figure out the amounts. help

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: The food blog Indian Ambrosia has a recipe that can be used for a potato salad: https://indianambrosia.com/aloo-chaat-recipe/ And Bon Appetit actually has a Chaat-Style Potato Salad if you search for it.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: These are so good. They are in rotation at our house. I love the seasonings, peppers, onions, and yogurt subbed for sour cream. They are fab. I had to make my own Chaat masala, which wasn’t hard—mail ordering the mango powder helped a lot.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: These potatoes are amazing! I made one slight change since I don’t eat sour cream. I made a clean dairy-free aioli, a sour cream substitute for myself but used sour cream for my guests. The chaat masala is a must. Where has this spice been hiding all my life?

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I’ve made a version of this… instead of layering try mixing all the ingredients together and then place a dollop into the open baked potato… add a pinch of the chat masala to ‘make pretty.’

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I substituted red Fresno peppers for color. Also substituted Greek plain yogurt for the sour cream. Great side dish and will now be part of our regular starch rotation.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Due to availability of ingredients, I altered the recipe a bit to great success. Smallest potatoes were 3-4 oz in size. After baking/cooling, cut each in half and laid out on platter cut side up. Sub freshly opened curry powder for chaat masala. Mixed all ingredients together, spread liberally on potatoes, sprinkled w chopped cilantro. Yummy!!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: special trip to indian store/eaterie (Vik in Berkeley, to which I bow down since forever). These potatoes were perfect. Perfectly delicious.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Big hit!  I added a tiny dollop of butter to each potato before any of the other toppings.  Three little potatoes on a plate as a side with salmon - so pretty and so delish.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: These are very appealing appetizers- like little packages guests were excited to oprrn. Easy to prepare and very tasty. I added a little finely minced chili. I can see customizing flavors to suit the night’s cuisine.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: The chaat masala I used was from Spicewalla (online and in Asheville NC).

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Can I use garam masala instead of chaat masala?

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I noticed a lot of interest in getting chaat marsala. I can easily buy it at Indian stores in Durham, NC but I noticed it is readily available at Amazon.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
Making this to transport to campus and share, I needed to streamline. Made a mixture of the onion, Chile and ginger, spices ( I had gram Marsala, ordered the chaat on Amazon) and processed/blended it with full fat sour cream. 
Drained and am saving the super flavorful lighter cream to add to a soup. Tomorrow I will heat the potatoes, let it rain cilantro and some green onion etc, then add the 
“Indian-ish” sour cream mix. Winning!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Very tasty for a super bowl appetizer. I’m thinking of taking the same method and ingredients, dicing the cooked potatoes and making an Indianish potato salad.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Not spicy enough and not enough toppings. I would double the topping for the same amount of potatoes. Also I sautéed the red onion and Serrano peppers and ginger. Definitely want to try this again —- lots of potential!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Made these, just for me, tonight. However, I didn't have red onions (used regular) didn't have chaat masala (used garam masala) didn't have tiny potatoes (used the smallest yellow potatoes I had) and it still had me dancing in the rain.  So delicious!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
Couldn’t find Chaat masala around here and had to order it.  Once it arrived made the recipe as written using a Serrano chile and Vadalia onion.  Spicy but wonderful.  Think I might cook up a big ole russet potato and dump the Indian-ish topping on it.  Made these for guests and they liked it too although the Serrano was a bit much for one. 
Give it a go!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Worked very well with cottage cheese too for low fat version.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Superb, delicious and fun.  We made these exactly as the recipe / video described. For crunch - sprinkle the brand Indian Life savory Dal Mix on top of each potato after the other toppings.  You can order it on Amazon just like the chaat masala.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: This was okay but missing something flavor wise. We paired this dish with the Indian-ish Nachos which was simply incredible

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I liked how these were made, but to go with my main dish better, I mixed some chives in with the sour cream, and then sprinkled some more on top, along with lemon zest.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I make these all the time, exactly as the recipe says and everyone loves them. You can order Chat Masala on Amazon-it’s not pricey. This is a very easy recipe.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I thought that a drizzle of olive oil would have been nice before adding all the other toppings.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: We served these as a make your own baked potato bar, using the suggested toppings and adding several others. Our guests really loked the less trsditional toppings like cilantro and marsala. It was very easy to prepare and present and with a green side salad made a hearty meal.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Subbed shallots for red onion (milder); greek yoghurt for sour cream (healthier); thai chile instead of serrano peppers (spicier and closer to what the Indian green chile is like). Mixed those and cilantro with the greek yoghurt and topped with chaat masala later — chaat is almost always added before serving, no need to marinate beforehand. I did enjoy it, but would not make again as written; it was missing something. The sweetness that most chaat dishes blend with spice, tanginess and funk?

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Potatoes for an appetizer before  a huge, starch-filled Thanksgiving dinner? Really?

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: can these potatoes be served/enjoyed at room temperature (made in advance)?

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: IFYI  used canned tomatoes successfully in this dish. I used Hunt's whole tomatoes 28oz can. Some insist on more expensive San Marzano tomatoes.  America's Test Kitchen did extensive taste testing and Hunt's was the winner a d FAR cheaper and easier to find. Also one can I bought of a different brand had only 6 tomatoes in it. Hunts had 13. Red gold also did well in the test and is even cheaper. Happy cooking!

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: did you drain the can of liquids?

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: When I last looked, there was one comment, now there are many.  I usually read many comment and appreciate the input from so many, but never make any comments myself.  I made this tonight, I had close to 4 c of cooked basmati rice, used all, a large onion, many cloves of garlic, done after the onions.  Used 1 can of TJoes diced and fire roasted organic tomatoes, and it was a winner!  I used some cheese found in the cheese drawer that melted beautifully, no name.  I had leftovers, to be frozen.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: I like your addition of garlic.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: Made a half-batch. Used canned tomatoes.  Roasted tomatoes under broiler til slightly charred.  Poured tomato juice from can and reduced after onions were sauteed (also sauteed 1 clove garlic with onion). Then added tomatoes to pan and reduced more. Added 1 minced anchovy. Rest same as recipe. Served w dollop of homemade creme fraiche.  It  is delicious! Won't change a thing next time. And there will be a next time- soon.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: Oh Joanne- anchovy, like garlic, makes EVERYTHING better. It's the umami! Anchovy totally dissolves into a sauce so it's like a flavoring. Need to do it before you salt though bc anchovies are plenty salty.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: Anybody who wants to know how good anchovies can be check out Sam Siftons recipe on this site "Soft-boiled eggs with Anchovy Toast". I was shaking the first time I tried it because it was so good.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: I needed a quick dinner tonight with what was in the house so made this with 28 oz diced tomatoes, 1/2 a seeded jalapeno and added 3 cloves of crushed garlic.  It was absolutely delicious.    Next time I'll add a can of black beans as well.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: Canned tomatoes ok?

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: I certainly would use canned, especially this time of year when fresh just aren’t as tasty.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: Yes! My friend who is an excellent chef from Bangalore made tomato rice (no cheddar) this at my home recently and she asked for a can of tomatoes because she forgot fresh ones. It worked great.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody:
IF canned tomatoes, then a 28 oz can of peeled tomatoes? 

There are times in the year when plum tomatoes are woody and tasteless, and canned is better.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: I also took on someones advise using a cast iron pan...  It was at high temp vs  cool.....  Rice on the crusty side which reminded me of my moms "Spanish Rice"...... Any time you can recreate MOM FOOD it is special!!!

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: Why preheat to 500? Why not just set the oven to broil? Is there something missing?

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: You want to heat the rice too

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: But it's only in there for 3 minutes!  Not enough time to warm or crisp anything.  Yes the broiler will work it's magic that fast  but not conductive heat

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: You preheat to 500 because some ovens, when you turn the on to broil, are still cold. They take a few minutes to warm up. especially electric or duel fuel. Having it set to 500 also will crisp the rice nicely.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: This is a crispy dream! Sautéed onions and chile. Added garlic and tomatoes and cooked on stove top stirring in a high sided cast iron pan (key) for about 30 minutes cooking down the tomato. Added the rice and about 1/3 - 1/2 the cheese and mixed well. Smoothed it out in pan.  Added the rest of the cheese on top. Lowered the heat and didn't touch it for about another 15-20 min.  It will bubble slightly but the bottom is crisping. Lit the broiler -5 min. watching- crisp top! Everyone swooned!

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody:
I would suggest using a more healthful rice; brown, or a blend of wild, sprouted red, brown and white rices, upping the fiber and protein content a wee bit. Definitely adding some garlic here.

I could eat this leftover for breakfast the morning after. It's a winner.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: ANY good dinner dish is a good leftover breakfast!  Even more reason making great dinners with extras!!!.    Much better than cereal .

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: Read several cooking notes and did the following for fabulous results. Sautéed a cup and a half of onion with one large minced garlic and a few chopped anchovies. Added two medium cans and juice of Trader Joe’s diced fire roasted tomatoes.  Added cooked basmati rice and top with a combo of cheddar and pepper jack cheese.... so good. Boil longer...watch until it gets crispy.   Going to double it and serve with grilled chicken for a party in a few weeks with a green salad...yummy!

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: What is the point of pre-heating the oven to 500F if the baking dish goes under the broiler for 3 minutes?

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: I lightly sprayed muffin tins and made 24 ‘rice cake’ appetizers with this recipe.  Went over very well.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: I made this using a 28 oz can of diced tomatoes. I also added a pound of sausage to the onions and used garlic salt instead of straight up salt. The dish came tougher super fast and was very satisfying, tasty weeknight meal.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: Whoops!  Made pretty much as written, but dumped on the floor as it came out of the oven. YIKES!  Scraped up, put back in the pan, crossed fingers, and ate. YUM.    In 14 days we shall see if this was a bad idea.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: @trace was it a bad idea?

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: Yum!  No Serrano Chile’s, so chopped up a small chipotle in Adobe.  Worked great.  Addictive!

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: I'm sorry, but this is barely a recipe and it's definitely not a meal. I thought the writer must know something I didn't and that if i followed along it would add up to more then the sum of it's parts.  Nope, it taste like rice and tomato with cheese melted on top, nothing more.... Just really didn't work for me as is, though I could see adding a whole bunch of ingredients to the base to make it flavorful and cohere into something more.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody:
Dear home cook,

Like all NYT and all recipes, it's a starting point.  Most recipes aim for the lowest common denominator - what will offend the fewest and please the most. You know your favorite tastes, your tolerance for heat, for salt, for sweetness. Improvise!!  Some people found this perfect and delicious as written. I saw it as the beginning of an awesome meal. With  much more garlic and onion in a cast iron skillet stovetop, then in the 500 oven, then the broiler, it was sublime.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: I only use Cento tomatoes, which ARE San Marzano tomatoes, but not for their place of origin. Cento tomatoes are the only tomatoes I've found which do NOT contain calcium chloride. American producers insist on adding calcium chloride to all canned tomatoes, an utterly loathsome practice. Calcium chloride is a firming agent, which prevents the tomatoes from breaking down during cooking and will utterly defeat your pasta sauce, your lasagna, your pot roast or anything else. Utterly infuriating.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: Made it this afternoon.  Took on the advice of adding more cheese into the rice/tomatoes plus including garlic , 1 TBS of anchovie paste, and a little fresh ginger. Also upped the salt to 1 1/2 teaspoon.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: Delicious comfort food.  Recipe very adaptable.  I've been making it with canned tomatoes in a dutch oven .  I don't transfer it to a baking dish but leave it in the dutch oven when I put it in the hot oven.  The cheese is a nice touch but not necessary.  Bake it a bit without the broiler so the rice gets browned on the edges and toothy.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: What is the magic of the "Wooden Spoon" for crushing tomatoes?  I use silicone spoons to protect my cast iron pans.  What am missing???

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: The magic of the wooden spoon is the texture that helps keep the tomatoes from shooting away across the kitchen . . . use whatever you have, though.  You could even crush them adead of time, I am sure.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: No magic it's just most people have a wooden spoon and you don’t want to use metal.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: Made this with leftover rice mix (had brown and wild in it because that was on hand).  It's pizza rice, used garlic and added oregano.  Only had yellow sharp cheddar, added parm.  Was great!  Forgiving and different recipe. And tasty!

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: I do this a bit backward.  I cook the onions, add tomatoes then chicken stock.  Bring to a boil and add rice.  Cook together. Then finish with cheese and broil.  I think it flavors the rice better.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: @Catherine good idea - cook it like a Mexican rice dish, and if you use an ovenproof pan you only have the one pan to wash. Otherwise one has to wash the rice pot, the sauté pan, AND the square dish.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: Great recipe! I grilled the plum tomatoes on a Weber grill after cutting them in half and seasoning with sea salt and some sugar to add some flavor to them (store-bought plum tomatoes at this time of the year  in the NE don’t have much taste). When the cheese was bubbling toward the end of the recipe I layered 4 eggs to the top and left in the oven for about another 5 minutes ( making frequent checks for doneness). Sour cream helped to temper the heat while we ate.  My wife did the clean up.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: Does it have to be a Weber grill? or would a Green Egg suffice...

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: Shakshuka!

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: Substituted: 2 small shallots for the onion; 5 chopped pepperoncini for the chilli; jar of marinara sauce for the tomatoes; extra cup of mozarella (for gooey factor) mixed in with the rice & sauce as they were combined.  Brilliant!  Pizza rice!  Has the potential to be a keeper in family recipes, because my kids loved it.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: I made this, it was so good!  And ridiculously easy too.  I added a touch of cayenne pepper to the recipe, it was a nice addition.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody:
I made it last week with lots of onion, garlic and a whole serrano chile, added seasoning salt.  It was delicious!!  My partner had 3 servings.  I heated it up in the morning and served it with fried eggs on top - in the top 20 of breakfasts ever, in any country.
I made it tonight with an anchovy (I'm tiptoeing into anchovy territory). Delicious!!!!  I used half a serrano because the heat overpowered the flavor a bit last week.  Perfect!  Lovely heat coming into the back of my mouth later.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: Why preheat the oven to 500 if the intent is to crisp the cheese with the broiler?

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: You’ll know when you taste how crispy the rice is. Residual heat does wonders for a dish like this. Don’t skip out on it!

author: Priya Krishna

commentBody: My broiler in town is separate / under -the oven. Will it still better to have the oven on - or go straight to broil?

author: Priya Krishna

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: I guess I'm one of the few that made it exactly as written, with ripe roma tomatoes.  I guess you could use canned in a pinch, but if you get a chance try it with fresh and very ripe tomatoes - it was amazing!

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: My mother made this when we were children, however she added chopped hotdogs - no kidding! Very fond memories of this.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: Leftovers?  Take a square of leftover about 3.5 by 3.5 inches and heat it in a saute pan.  Use a big spoon to make an indentation in the top, then break open and drop in a raw large egg.  Cover (preferably with a glass lid so you can keep an eye on it) and saute until the egg is cooked (i.e., poached, more or less) and serve as a breakfast dish.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: Re tomatoes: I just bought a 28-oz can each of Hunts and Mutti canned tomatoes (San Marzano style) to test for another purpose. The Hunts had a lot of juice with fewer tomatoes which for a rice dish would be okay I guess, and the flavor was meh. The Mutti brand were solid whole tomatoes, very ripe, and tasted great. For the 25-cent difference in price, the Italian tomatoes were far better.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: Wow. If there was ever a late night dish for hungry drunk people, this is it. They will die and go straight to spicy cheesy heaven. More like an Indian lasagne. My additions: using a cast iron skillet, add in a large minced clove of garlic with the onions towards the end, then add in 3/4 tsp turmeric and stir before adding tomatoes. Be very generous with the cheddar. After adding cheddar I heated the whole ensemble on the stove top to get it good and hot throughout, then finished under broiler.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: Can this be done on a stove top? I don't have an oven!

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: You could try removing the tomato rice from skillet. Wipe out pan. Sprinkle layer of cheese in pan and place tomato rice on top. Turn heat on medium low and let cheese get crispy. Then place plate over skillet and invert. Good luck!

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: Definitely reminds me of many rice dishes (arroces) in Spain where the rice is allowed to get quite crispy on the bottom. I'll try this one for sure!

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: I made a half portion as it seemed like a large dish for 2 even with leftovers.  I thought it was delicious.  I cooked it for a little longer in the oven, watching it carefully.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: This was so good! I did use a 28 oz can of tomatoes instead of fresh, and drained them before adding. I also added additional cheddar to the rice itself, and put my cast iron pan in the oven during preheating so the rice developed a nice crust.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: Didn’t have any chilis so I used an Adobo spice blend I had on hand from World Market.  Added black beans as was suggested by another user.  Used brown rice instead.  Delicious!  You have to try pretty hard to mess this dish up.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: Could one leave out the peppers?  Can't eat peppers of any kind anymore...need to find new flavors.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: Of course! Use the spicing that works for you. It looks like a very flexible recipe.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: It needs more cheese to be mixed with the rice and some in top. It also needs some herbs or anchovies to make the flavor more interesting.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: I just made this with leftover brown rice and a 28-oz can of tomatoes. I added garlic, hot pepper flakes, basil, and oregano to the sauce, plus some sliced steamed zucchini that was in the fridge and about half a can of kidney beans for protein (I'm a vegetarian). Am eating it now. It's good but not great, but I think the possibilities here are endless and it came together quickly. Will try this again.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: Has anyone tried this with brown rice? How did it go? I totally understand why basmati would be the preference here for it’s texture and being a more straightforward canvas for the other flavors, but I’m a big fan of the fiber in brown rice and I really have to pay attention to the glycemic index of my meals.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: I wondered the same thing, for the same reason.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: Laura - you ask about using brown rice instead of the basmati specified.  I don't see any reason why that couldn't be done.  Lundburg (a California grower) packages both white basmati and brown basmati.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody:
My husband and I are confined and this is the type of food that we need right now. Did have to use my imagination to add a bit of ouf to it. Of course, we had to use canned tomatoes and Tobasco sauce with half a lime and it did pick up the taste. Had left over of spinach - why not add this in. 
Leftover for tomorrow. Will add a couple of boiled eggs on the side. Why not. This is a free world. If my husband is happy, so am I.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: I made this for the first time on the evening of Election Day 2020, adding a can of white beans, drained and rinsed, to the mixture before the final broil for protein. Now I think of it as “democracy casserole”, the mother of all comfort foods.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: Don't bother dirtying a square baking dish if you're already using a Dutch oven!

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: That was my thought as I read through the recipe. Definitely going to try, but it will be one-pot. That's what dutch ovens are for.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: I made this as a side to roast chicken. It was good but not fabulous or exciting. I tossed I few black beans into the leftover rice for lunch and it was a nice lunch.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody:
This is a picky eater pleaser. Trying it with kidney beans, a bell pepper instead of chili, and some garlic. Oh yeah and Beemster cheese because its easier to get here in Europe and was on sale.
I love how you can take these basic recipes and riff off of them. My kind of cooking, and great on a dark, sleety night when we want yum but are tired from home office and online school.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: Substitute rice with shredded paneer, or tofu. Delicious. And eat with bread.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: For the substitute of tofu for rice did you still do three cups?

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: Such a an easy, quick and delicious dinner that is great for emptying out fridge/freezer. I had about an Inch left of a jar of marinara, a splash of chicken stock, 2 spicy sausages in the freezer. This meal is great as is, but is also an excellent base for whatever else you want to throw in! Easily the newest member of my “go-to” weeknight repertoire.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody:
I can’t stop eating this. I used the pepper I had on hand, a jalapeño, and canned tomatoes. Mixed some of the cheese into the rice, put the rest and more on top. 

Made it in a cast iron pan which went directly into the oven. I can’t even believe how good it is.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: My mother made a very similar dish in the 1950’s, and I loved it! Just no chile, canned tomatoes, and with the addition of probably 4 to 6 slices of diced, sautéed bacon. This was a common main dish, with salad and veggie sides. As I look back, this was certainly a satisfying, filling, and economical dish for a young family.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: We had a similar dish in our dinner rotation when I was a kid. We called it Spanish rice. It included sauteed ground beef and chopped green bell pepper. I make it once in awhile for husband and me, but spice it up with smoked paprika, cumin, and oregano.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: This is definitely Spanish Rice. My mother made a version in the 1950s as you describe. In my 1970s version I cooked the rice as part of the dish, rather than earlier, and sauteed the onion and a green pepper in the bacon fat. The crisped bacon was added back right after the cheese. It works with any rice. We tend to use long or short grain brown. Adjust liquid accordingly. In my 2021 version the bacon has disappeared again.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: Because the rice is only briefly in the oven, make sure that it will still be hot by cooking it while preparing the onions and tomatoes. Otherwise your rice-and-tomato casserole won't be hot throughout.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: Following other suggestions, I added 3 cloves of garlic, a heaping tsp of smokey paprika, and used 28 oz can of good chopped tomatoes. I also browned two chicken breasts and nestled them into the rice mixture before putting a very generous cup of sharp cheddar cheese over everything (in a cast-iron skillet). It needed closer to 15 minutes in the 500 degree oven.  I did not switch to broil, because I wanted the rice on the sides and bottom to develop a little crust. Definitely going in recipe box

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: Delicious and easy! Next time I think I'll pop some mustard and cumin seeds in the oil before adding the onion.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: Really good! Made per instructions but halved the recipe. Went under the broiler in an 8” cast iron pan, and while I halved the recipe I did use full amount of cheese to cover the expanse. Turned out great, and 3 minutes under the broiler was perfect.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: Riffed on this recipe to use odds & ends in the frig. Added chopped scallions, paprika, crushed red pepper, half orange bell pepper, mushrooms, garlic, and pickled jalapeños. Eight oz of ordinary breakfast sausage was sautéed w all the veg, then sautéed the rice. Added tomatoes and chicken broth and baked in oven. Topped at the end w gruyere and cheddar. Excellent!

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: I added a can of beans for some protein and to make this more like an entree than a side-dish.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: Add white beans. It transforms it tenfold and makes it a complete meal.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: I placed a layer of pepperoni on top right before putting in the oven. Super good. I think any favorite pizza topping would be tasty.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: I also used canned tomatoes, and the dish was still fabulous.  It was a big hit with my family.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: Finding no basmati rice in the house, I used jasmine instead.  Worked fine.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: I made this using brown rice which I’m presuming doesn’t soak up the juices as much as white rice. It was soupy-ish as I cooked it and a bit after it was broiled but after sitting for 20-30 min, it solidified and is delicious! Everyone devoured it.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody:
Very good recipe for turning leftover rice from takeout and pantry items into a satisfying dish. 

I used fire roasted diced tomatoes from a can. Red pepper flakes (did not have fresh chiles). A box of rice that came with Chinese takeout. Added a couple anchovies and garlic into the sauce. I used a cast iron and after mixing the rice into the sauce I left it on the heat for a bit to crisp the bottom before transferring under the broiler.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: added spinach

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: Really really yummy. Made as written with these changes: used brown basmatti rice and a 28 oz can of undrained San Marzano tomatoes. Used a jalapeno pepper finely diced. Added 1 rib celery amd a handful of par boiled baby carrots, both finely diced. Topped with very thin slabs of seriously sharp white cheddar. 3.5 min under broiler. I think I could eat the whole baking dish full all by myself! Big hit with the fam. Can't wait to make it again!

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: This is not about the rice, or the tomatoes. This is about the CHEESE CAP. Broil until it reaches the level of crispy chewy you like. ENJOY.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: This was quite well received in my home. To make it more of a one pot meal, we added a pound of diced ham. I kept with pork based upon many comments made about using pancetta. We also added about 1 t smoked paprika, 1/2 t each oregano and cumin. I incorporated Boursin which I had on hand into the rice and topped with almost triple the amount of sharp cheddar cheese. Added about 8 oz baby spinach.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: Really enjoyed this dish. Thought it would be a lentil with tomato soup, but it is a tomato with lentil soup. This was the first soup I made that actually tasted a like a good soup.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: Ideas: spinach, garlic

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: Such a great way to use up leftover rice. I used a cast iron skillet start-to-finish. I combined a cup of crushed tomatoes   a small can of Rotel tomatoes with green chilies for the "sauce", added a combo of cooked basmati & wild rice, and threw on whatever was left in the bottom of bags of pre-shredded cheddar & mozza.  After broiling, topped it off with freshly shredded romaine lettuce and a drizzle of ranch. It was the perfect one-bowl meal & my picky teen went for seconds!

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: Almost certain you cannot put a “regular” baking dish that is not metal under the broiler!! So perhaps that’s why it says preheat to 500 degrees and then put dish in.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: I used cast iron skillet

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: Perfectly tasty, but--to my palate--not worth making again.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: I made a half batch and followed the recipe. No garlic, no anchovies, and I think it could have used some crushed pepper and more salt. Possibly my half pepper could have been the whole one. We are heating left over for breakfast and putting an egg on top. Had I used the garlic this might have not been very appealing. Will make again!

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: Wouldn’t brown rice make this even better?

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: Want to try more chilis next time. We used canned tomatoes to save time ...

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: Made this last night but added about a tsp of Ras el Hanout and a pinch of sugar. Outstanding!

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody:
- use 28 oz canned tomatoes with only some of the sauce - maybe use fire roasted tomatoes and then don't add the serrano
- up the cheese
- use cast iron skillet
- add garlic, anchovy, sausage
- add fried egg to top
- char the tomatoes under the broiler before adding?

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: Is it OK to cook this tomato in an iron skillet?

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: Jana - I use my 12" cast iron skillet to cook tomato sauces in all the time. I try to use it for many other (much less acidic) dishes in-between times, and I always carefully reseason the pan, whispering apologies and sweet nothings to it after I abuse it with the tomatoes. I've been doing this for years, and my pan is fine.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: @Jennifer B. Absolution is one of my well seasoned iron skillet's best qualities as well.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: My mods: added a glove of minced garlic to onion, added sautéed oyster mushrooms when folding in cooked rice. Didn’t have pepper so used a pinch of red pepper flakes. I used sushi rice (what I had) and it was fine. Cooked everything in a 10-in cast iron skillet, from stovetop to oven and it worked out well, didn’t spill over. Awesome dish! Husband called it “pizza rice” and loved it. Want to experiment with other veggies and mozzarella next time.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: I made this with Arborio rice. To a can of diced tomatoes I added shallot, garlic, Serrano pepper, spinach and fresh basil. Topped with pepper jack cheese as that’s what I had. It ended up tasting very much like lasagna.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: Made this with what I had in the house– homemade marinara from the freezer, jasmine rice and feta– in a cast iron pan. Next time I'll preheat the pan with some olive oil to get a crust on the bottom and sides. The feta worked perfectly, and I drank a glass of Gavi.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: Eh, could be better. Next time I will add garlic and use canned tomatoes. The fresh were nice but didn't leave the dish with a very saucy tomatoyness.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: Wow! I followed this recipe and added chopped shiitake, egg plant, gouda and more olive oil. So simple and wow wow wow delicious. Thank you Cooking at NYTimes for the inspiration.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: Any chance you could share the Spanish-textbook recipe? Just curious. Growing up in the 1950s my mom made a super cheesy baked-rice casserole with jarred olives that she called Spanish Rice.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: This has been one of my go-to, comfort food, no-recipe recipes for years. If you, like me, love spicy food, I recommend adding a tomato paste-sized tin of harissa with the tomatoes. Adds a whole new dimension.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: This turned out to be delicious comfort food. The rice tasted buttery and delicious (used medium grain).  I added zuchinni and a clove of garlic after the onions sauted a bit.  Then I set the veggies aside and cooked the tomato (used canned whole tomatoes and squished them as the recipe said to do). I added a 1/2 teaspoon of cinnamon to the tomatoes which added a rich flavor. Next time I'll add even more vegetables: yellow pepper and celery. Very good!

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody:
Just saw the recipe and had to try with changes with what’s in the pantry.  Thinking of paella, I layered the dish, starting with pre-cooked brown
Basmati rice, which I fried on high heat without stirring for about 10 minutes to make a crunchy, light-brown bottom. Next came a layer of fried diced onions, canned diced tomatoes and canned and rinsed cannelloni beans, all heated together for 5 minutes.  I then added the grated cheese and broiled until melted; no pre-heated oven. A great find.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: Very easy and tasty. I didn’t have a hot pepper so used red pepper flakes with the onion for some heat. Added some spinach just before adding the rice. Mixed in some jarlsberg I needed to use up with the cheddar and sprinkled a layer of scallions just under the cheese. Everyone liked this, will definitely make again. And seems like an easy recipe for some variations.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: Made for lunch today and it was delicious. Based on ingredients at hand used coconut oil, half a red onion, cayenne p/o chili, regular tomatoes squished carefully and cut further with scissors & sharp yellow cheddar. Used well seasoned 12" CI pan which went beautifully under broiler.  It served the two of us! Whole thing came together in less than a 1/2 hour. Will definitely make again and again!

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody:
I loved this. I made it as a side for beans and will have the leftovers in burritos. It’s a very adaptable recipe and I enjoyed reading everyone else’s ideas. 

I kept mine pretty simple because my beans are spiced, but I used fire roasted canned tomatoes (I used the juice and let it cook down, tomato paste would be nice) and brown jasmine rice. I made it in an enameled cast iron skillet, which worked extremely well and obviated the need to dirty additional dishes (after using my rice cooker).

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: Totally get the “pizza rice” reference because the crispy cheese via the broil is so reminiscent of pizza cheese. I added green Chile sauce, garlic, a couple of anchovies and 1 TB tomato paste. I used white rice but next time will use brown rice! It soaks up more liquid and imagine the mild nutty flavor would add another dimension to this bomb dish.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: Prepared as directed in the recipe with 28 oz can of whole tomatoes. Used the microwave to cook the rice like the author's dad did in the Insta stories. Turned out perfect. This is a simple, yummy recipe. Look forward to making again.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: This is an excellent bare bones recipe. I made my own additions and substitutions to really make this taste more like pizza for my kids. They devoured it. I’m obsessed with the texture of this dish and how deep the flavors are. I recommend browning some tomato paste with the onions before you add the canned tomatoes. It added a really nice depth.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: I make this weekly for my picky husband and my not-picky toddler! I mix in a can of black beans while cooking the tomatoes and usually sub in pickled jalapeños since I usually have those on hand. I also used canned whole tomatoes and cook everything in a cast iron pan because I’m lazy! I also like to top mine with a dollop of Greek yogurt for some tang. It’s just great, easy comfort food. Definitely brings back memories of growing up in the ‘90s in California and weeknight dinners!

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody:
Had a bunch of tomatoes from my garden I needed to use up and this did the trick perfect. Didn’t have basmati rice - no problem. Didn’t have sharp cheddar either - also no problem. Regular fresh steamed white rice and Colby Jack cheese did a fine job. 
Side note: my Czech grandmother used to make almost this exact recipe but with her fresh egg noodles instead of rice. So if you’re ever feeling frisky use egg noodles instead of rice. So good.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: Delicious, one of those recipes greater than the sum of its parts!  Next time I would skip preheating the oven to 500 in August (!)  and crisp up the bottom on the stove in a cast iron pan and just run it under the broiler at the end.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: As others have said, this is a great starting point of a recipe. I’ve been using jalapeños because they’re easier to find near me, and yellow rice instead of basmati. Also added garlic to taste, about 4-5 cloves for a half batch. Don’t stick to the 3 minute broiler time. Sometimes you need to feel the broiler time in your soul. (And check after 5 minutes. There’s a difference between crispy cheddar and charred cheddar!)

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: This is what my mother called "rice guck." No recipe needed.  Throw some leftovers vegetables in.  Sprinkle some bread crumbs on top and skip the cheese.  Use some tomato paste.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: This is a deceptively simple recipe that is great as written or very receptive to additions. I’ve made it as written multiple times but have also had great luck in adding things such as chopped green chili. (Anaheim’s) cumin and garlic. The clean tomato taste of the original recipe is really hard to beat though!

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: Just made this with canned tomatoes, jasmine rice, a serrano from the garden, and added garlic (as other reviewers recommended). Definitely upped the cheese (did not measure). Very very good - the bf liked it too, and he's picky about vegetarian dinners. Will make again. Great for busy weeknights with limited cooking time and on-hand ingredients.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: Much like the others have said, this is a great starting point for a recipe. I like to add some green bell peppers and garlic in with my onions and serrano pepper, and if I don't have a spicy pepper available (read: I forgot to buy one at the store), I'll make it with yellow rice or Spanish rice instead. Pepper Jack cheese is great on this.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: Super comforting, easy as pie, perfect dinner for November 4, 2020.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: Tomato paste, smoked paprika, black beans

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: All the reviews were so positive but I was underwhelmed. Might give it another chance adding some of the extras that people suggested like cumin or beans or meat.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: We make the same dish but we add black olives and 1 diced anchovy. Oh and we use cooked orzo (pasta) instead of rice. Fantastic!

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: I tried this recipe last night and we really loved it; always looking for new veg recipes! I mostly followed the directions except for adding 2 cloves of minced garlic and my store didn't have serrano chiles so I used a jalapeno. I also added some black pepper and a bit of red pepper flakes, and used some good canned whole tomatoes. I agree with other comments that this is a recipe that you can play around with. Basmati rice seems perfect for this recipe. this is going into my regular routine.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: My rice was mushy, probably because I didn’t strain the tomatoes.  I think in the future, I’ll use the juice as part of the rice cooking liquid, or even lightly stir fry the rice to make it a bit crunchy.  The flavor was good. I added a couple of cloves of garlic, but it could have used more, plus additional pepper.  I’m a bit cautious with peppers, after ruining a few dishes with too many hatch chilies. A can of chiles or tomatoes with chilies would be an option.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: This is delicious. Added sliced mozz rounds before I baked. You can doctor this however you like.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: This was so easy and delicious. It is kind of like a cheater tomato-y risotto? I used imported canned crushed tomatoes, because that’s what I like. Did everything else exactly per the recipe. I made some slow cooker shredded pork to serve with it and the whole family raved. Will definitely be making again soon.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: This was a good easy side dish, but needs an oomph of flavor. Next time, I might add some marjoram, thyme, or sage. A little more salt would help, too.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: I increased the rice to 1.5 C (dry,rinsed jasmine), cooked that in 2.25 C chicken stock. I added several chopped poblanos to the onions and let them cook with tomatoes (I used two 14.5 oz cans chopped), with fresh bay leaf, oregano, curry, and cumin, then threw in some sautéed shrooms (leftover pizza topping) before adding the cooked rice, colby jack, chopped up baby bel cheese, and fresh mozzarella chunks. I had to add a little stock while the tomato mixture was cooking - heavenly dish!

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: This is a filling and flavorful dish with a basic technique that one needs to be reminded of, but the cumin was calling out to me so I tossed some on top of the tomatoes before the sautee.  We had it for a late lunch today with a taco smorgasborg.  Very satisfying.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: This is a wonderful side dish, but if you're like me, you're happy eating it as an entree with a salad and a glass of wine. Good comfort food.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: Instead of the chili I added several chopped chipotles in adobo with the seeds removed. This would make a great 1 dish main meal with ground meat.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: DELICIOUS! I'll be honest, I skipped out on the cheese, because I'm trying to shed some weight, and I replaced the basmati with cauliflower rice. Healthy and seriously SO SO yummy (I'm definitely trying the real deal soon!).

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: Just made this for the second time and it was so good. I added some leftover alubia blanca beans from Rancho Gordo that were waiting for their purpose in the fridge. Easy  comfort food

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: Add in some peppers and other veggies to make it more substantial, maybe some beans?

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: This is a delicious but, um, ugly dish. Looks like ground beef. I suggest making into patties in a muffin tin, using twice the cheese topping. That way it’s all crunchy edges and lots of pizza-like crust.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: Easy and full of flavor. I subbed jalapeño for serrano  chile, added roasted chicken thighs and a splash of wine before broiling with cheese. Perfect for weeknights.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: I used Hunt's whole tomatoes 28oz can. Some insist on more expensive San Marzano tomatoes. America's Test Kitchen did extensive taste testing and Hunt's was the winner a d FAR cheaper and easier to find.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: I used 4 cans of Rotel Tomatoes and Green Chilis.  Gave it a very nice spicy, but not too spicy kick.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: I think you need an even stronger, drier, sharper cheese than sharp cheddar. Following the recipe, I used sharp cheddar, and it was pretty "meh". Maybe grated parmesan/romano, or even a very aged Manchego, would work better. I also added a can of cooked, drained, black beans to put a little good protein in there.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: I liked it but next time I will double the tomato. I added Kashmir chili for heat

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: I was looking for something to do with some black beans I had, and I had put this in my recipe box awhile ago.  I made it as written, but threw in my black beans into the mix.  My kids ate it up, and it was an easy weeknight supper!

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: Does heating the oven to 500F really make much of a difference in the 3 mins it takes to broil the top of the dish? It seems a wasteful use of energy.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: I wonder if adding a teaspoon of garam masala to the tomatoes and adding a spot more cheese would make this a bit more like Indian cheese toast? Such a simple treat with a delightful flavor profile.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: I'm wondering what "Indian" dish this tries to replicate. I'm thinking of adding sambar or rasam spice powder to the this to see if it pushes the dish into more "Indian" territory and adds punchy flavor.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: With this type of dish, I try to use a stovetop to broiler dish. Less washing up, less meds in general. I too prefer garlic in this dish and always use brown rice. Brown basmati is available.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: Added 2 tsp Italian seasoning, Large Vidalia onion, three cloves garlic, 4 cups fresh tomatoes from late fall garden.  A cup of italian sausage, 1 cup of dry white wine.  cooked three cups of brown rice, kept warm.  1 tsp honey, splash of tabasco.  Cooked down to thick, chunky sauce.  Added warm rice and heated it through.  Put half in 8x8 glass pan (greased), layered some mozzarella and parm, put rest of rice mix on top, layered on parm.  Makes a lot more than four servings!

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: Instead of using the tomato recipe here, use Alison Roman’s shallot pasta tomato sauce. Before sprinkling the cheese, salt and mince garlic into a half cup of sour cream and spread it over the rice. Give a generous twists of the pepper mill on top of the cheese. Instead of broiling, bake it at 500 degrees for 10 minutes or until the cheese is dark and golden.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: I took this in a Mexican direction: I used a fresh jalapeño instead of the small chile, added garlic and cumin seeds when the onions were almost ready, then stirred in a paste consisting of 2 anchovies, 1 chipotle pepper with adobo sauce from the can, and tomato paste. Added a can of black beans and chopped cilantro when the tomatoes were cooked down. Sprinkled some more cilantro on top of the cheese when serving. Everybody loved it!

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: This was a good, quick dinner. It used up the last of our homegrown tomatoes (not bad, since Thanksgiving is in 2 days!). This could become a tradition for an easy dinner for the week when I have much cooking to do!

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody:
Excellent midweek recipe and good for breakfast the day after, too.

As many others did, I added a few anchovies to the sauce, plus a large pinch of red pepper flakes. And sprinkled a handful of chopped black olives on top, along with the cheddar cheese. 

hat makes this recipe far  above average is the selection of the best tomatoes. Following Wirecutter advice, I used Biano de Napoli organic whole peeled tomatoes. Am glad I did! They cost more, but the difference is noticeable.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: Reaction at our house was “blah”. Bland and nondescript. Made it exactly as specified, using canned tomatoes. Needs some garlic, some herbs, some acid, maybe some paprika. After thinking about it, we decided nope, just delete this one and move on.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: This is good tasting, but needs something...not sure what. 100% a side dish.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: This was delicious but I like some heat.  Next time, a liberal dash or 2 of black pepper and a dash of cayenne.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: Excellent, simple and quick chunky tomato marinara. I ditched the rice and placed the sauce over spaghetti squash. Be sure to bake the spaghetti squash al dente!

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: One of my all time favorites. Easy, adaptable to what’s in the pantry and delicious.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: First time (there will be others) 1 cooked 1/2 c. basmati, used about 3/4 c. onion, small clove garlic, half a small jalapeño, added about 1/2 tsp. each whole cumin seed and black mustard, and home-canned Romas, and mixed the cooked rice into the small cast-iron skilled that I used for the tomato sauce, plopped a fried egg on top for extra protein. When I make it again (and I will) I may try cooking it on the stovetop a bit or in the oven before adding cheese to get a little crust on the rice

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: I seem to have had a different experience than many of the commenters. Made as written. Whereas the video shows a very “wet” sauce of tomatoes, mine needed extra water to keep from drying. Also, the video showed something considerably larger than 8” x 8” dish. In my casserole of roughly that area, it was still quite a shallow filling. Finally, 1 cup of cheese didn’t seem enough. On one hand, it’s tasty. On the other, it is definitely not enough food for four people at dinner - I’d consider it a side. Am I missing something?

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: @Gregg Favalora also agree it’s not enough for four at dinner unless you added white beans or a side salad

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: Looked great but not very tasty. Would not make again.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody:
I added dried chilli flakes. 
My son usually prefers pasta but he enjoyed this dish.
I liked this recipe because it can be made with what I usually have on hand.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: Hi there, living in France. Can you suggest a substitute for sharp cheddar cheese? Not very available here. Merci!

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: Literally anything may be ordered off the internet or Amazon if you are willing to pay for it and you want to make it that badly. Or experiment with other sharp cheeses. Good luck.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: You could try Gruyere or Comte. Slightly different flavor but would still work.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: I use parmagiana or other hard salty (usually Italian) cheese to substitute for sharp cheddar

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: Made with a lb of cherry tomatoes and extra cheese. Delicious!!

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: Was it "tomatoey enough"? Basmati rice with a little bit of tomatoes sounds ho-hum.   However, extra cheese is always great!!!

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: Substitute shredded paneer [ or tofu ] for rice. See the magic ! Use bread to eat it.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody:
What is the purpose of this in Step 1: "Adjust an oven rack to the highest position ..."
I'm guessing you only need to do this if you have an electric oven?

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: Mary - you ask what is the purpose in Step 1 of adjusting an oven rack to the highest position.  That is because eventually, in Step 5, one is going to engage the broiler.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: 3 large garlic cloves, three anchovy fillets, 4 piquante jared peppers, 1/8 t cayenne powder, brown rice, extra cheese, cooked tomatoes in iron pan, mixed rice in, put it under grill. Next time will heat tomato-rice mixture in pan to crisp it before broiling.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: I followed the recipe except substituted two pounds of Roma tomatoes fresh from the garden for the plum tomatoes. Plenty moist and very tasty!

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: My hubby cannot eat hot chilies. What mild chili would be a good sub here?

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: High Desert Sharon - you ask for a mild chili recommendation to sub for the hot chili in the recipe due to your hubby's dietary needs.  You might try using an Anaheim chili.  It is several times milder, much lower on Scoville scale, than a Serrano or even a Jalapeno.  Maybe try half an Anaheim.  Or leave the chili pepper out altogether.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: @Darcy: your fusion version was good—not great—only because you strayed so far from the simplicity of the original recipe, as is the habit of many cooks in the U.S... Understanding / researching how menus are designed in the country of the recipe’s origin always helps.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: Mine is not a hint or a tip. Rather a question. What is special about a wooden spatula or wooden spoon? Other recipes also suggest using a wooden spoon. If the pan does not have a nonstick coating, why not use a stainless steel spoon or a spatula? Wood retains reside from the cooking no matter how much you clean it.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
