commentBody: Is there some reason to transfer the dish from a dutch oven to a baking dish to broil, or can cheese be added and the dutch oven placed directly into the oven?

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: Rachel - you ask why instructions are to transfer mixture to baking dish to broil rather than putting the Dutch oven directly into oven.   Good question.  Made me wonder too.  Three thoughts.  1) It may the author is thinking of serving after broiling - putting the baking dish on table, for example.  2) Or it may be that not all Dutch ovens are broiler-proof.  I use Emile Henry Dutch oven which is broil safe.  3) But also Dutch oven sides are too high for contents inside to broil I'd think.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: Added garlic, anchovies, extra cheese.  Still nothing special.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: I made this last night w leftover rice from Thai takeout and, per other reviewers suggestions, garlic, anchovy paste, and more onion.  I used a 28 oz can of SAN Marzano tomatoes w liquid plus 6 tomatoes from another can. Too much liquid even though I reduced - the rice never got crispy but really lovely flavor.  Added chicken and arugula when I served it.  Definitely a keeper and this will be where my leftover takeout rice goes.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: My mother in law made something very similar, However she added pieces of pepperoni, and used Mozzarella, or a mix of Italian cheeses. (I adjusted it by boiling the pepperoni for a short time to remove some of the fat and then skin. It gives the rice a rich, spicy flavor. No chilies.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: Not sure what I am missing because I made this as directed (though I did add garlic and used a 28 oz can of plum tomatoes), but this is one of the blandest dishes I have ever tasted. Since it is really only rice, tomatoes and cheese, I guess I shouldn’t be surprised.  Reminded me of “Spanish Rice” from high school.  Or maybe hospital food.  Not sure how I will rescue the rest of it, but there must be a way!

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody:
I added a minced garlic clove at the end of the onion/chile saute and instead of dirtying multiple items, I cooked the whole thing in a deep 12-inch oven-safe saute pan.  Also skipped preheating the oven.

Very tasty but not sure if I would use it as a main dish, even with just two people.  Would have added a can of black beans if I had one in the house.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: Made this into a one-skillet dinner by using whole wheat basmati rice, sausage, and fresh spinach. It's delicious.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: Made this last night for my skeptical family and they all liked it! Used wild rice, 28 oz can of chopped tomatoes, 2 Aidells chicken sausages, 1/2 bell pepper, package of fresh spinach, 1 garlic clove, and a bunch of mushrooms. Terrific and easy one pot meal (just put the cast iron skillet under the broiler.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: See notes about crispy

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: Using canned tomatoes.  Drain them first?

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: I didn't have a chile so used 1/4 tsp of crushed red pepper flakes. Used a 28 oz can of crushed tomatoes so didn't add salt and used leftover brown rice and mozzarella, because that was what I had on hand. Incredibly easy to make. Used one pan that went from stovetop to broiler. Cheap, simple, easy and satisfying on a cool, overcast day with rain approaching, but I don't know that I'd make it again: Cheddar definitely would have been better, but I was trying to use up some leftover mozzarella.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: Made this for  Cornoavirus Easter dinner along with a   Very improvised Asian pork shop recipe.  With limited ingredients, used chipotle peppers a long with Canned tomatoes.  Had to add Parmesan .  Still amazing.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: I know a similar recipe from Japan. Doria rice is a italian inspired Japanese modern recipe. In Japan though they hide sea food inside the rice before baking: shrimps, scallops, anything actually!

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: This is a “fancy” version of something we used to make in college.  Just a can of condensed tomato soup, rice and cheese.  Make the soup add a cup of rice and bake topped with cheese until rice is cooked.  I would stir in red pepper if I had some on hand back then. Might be helpful for those who are pantry cooking right now.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: Very similar to NYT’S Ali Slagel’s white bean and tomato bake or spicy black bean and tomato recipes. I added rice to those and added beans to this. Love the anchovy paste! Great pantry recipes that can be made as written or improvised in these perilous times.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: In lieu of chilies (was feeding to kids) I added a bunch of garlic and black beans. It was very bland. Even with my fave Green Dragon sauce served on top it was pretty meh. With a lot more cheese and more flavourful summer tomatoes it would be better but I likely won’t make again.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: Yes, I finessed this a bit and took the advice to cook in a cast iron skillet on the burner a few minutes before broiling. Used a combination of a pint of grape tomatoes roasted with garlic cloves, dried chilies & olive oil until just bursting and a bit charred with a can of drained, fire-roasted diced tomatoes. Sautéed a few shallots with the onion and stirred in a bag of baby spinach. (Love a little green).  Then mixed in some pepper jack and sharp cheddar. Fantastic.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: Great pantry staple comfort food. I used Basmati rice, made in rice cooker will prepping ingredients. I had a few Roma tomatoes so i just combined with some leftover marinara sauce. Used jalapeño, and Monterey Jack and Parmesan cheeses, as that’s what I had on hand. Followed suggestion to use cast iron skillet instead of two pans and after combining, cooked on medium low to crisp bottom as one user suggested. Topped with pepperoni and basil for pizza flair and finished under broiler.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: In the spirit of 'pizza' rice, I added 5 cloves of garlic and a big squeeze of anchovy paste after the onions and jalapeno, stirred fresh basil, oregano, and parsley into the simmering tomato sauce (2 cans diced with some juice), threw in half a can of small red beans (tired of cannellini) and topped with a packaged Italian cheese blend. Baked in my cast iron skillet at 500 for about 10-15 min. I should have reduced the tomato sauce more, but still very good and *very* filling

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: This recipe looked good but upon embarking on making it I realized it is all wrong - too many steps, too many needless pots.  There has to be a simplier way to do this.  Big thumbs down on this recipe~)

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: Gosh-this was lovely. It takes nicely to substitutions too. I used double the onion, long grain rice instead of basmati, a 28 ounce can of crushed tomatoes, increased the rice to four cups to compensate for the extra tomato, and probably doubled the cheese.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: Used good canned tomatoes and decided to go Italian with this dish. Added olive oil, pressed garlic, red pepper flakes, Italian seasoning, a little red wine - 1/2, fresh parsley and shaved parmesan. Will use short grain rice next time, but it was a good pantry dinner. Italian Chicken sausage on the side.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: Delicious. Used homecanned mixed heirloom tomatoes.about 3 cups.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: I made this dish on Easter.  It's fine but nothing to write home about.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: I kicked this up to turn it into pizza.  I pressed the fresh rice into a well-oiled hot cast iron and cooked it on the surface burner to develop a browned rice ‘crust.’ I topped the rice with a few pizza toppings: peppers, onion, sausage and mushrooms.  When the underneath crust had developed a browned glow, I then layered in top a nice tomato sauce and piled on mozzarella cheese.  The whole thing went into the oven for 20 minutes at 400 degrees and then I broiled the mozzarella golden brown.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: Nothing fancy, but quite good. I put in some minced shitake mushrooms and garlic with the onions to add a little more flavor, and used a 28 oz can of crushed tomatoes. Kids ate it up!

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: I improvised all over the place (not one of my strong suits in the kitchen), and still got thumbs up from the eaters in the house. Mozzarella instead of cheddar. Jalapenos from a jar. The tomatoes were a mix of canned and fresh. I found basil in the fridge after I'd put the cheese on top, so I kinda pushed little sprigs through the cheese. I'll be making this again.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody:
Added garlic to the onion/pepper mix and cooked up some sweet Italian sausage to mix in to the rice tomato mixture—it was delish and tasted almost like paella. Next time I think I’ll double the Serrano but otherwise wouldn’t change a thing. 

Figured out half way through that my oven doesn’t have a broil function so baked at 500 for about ten minutes instead and it worked great. 

My partner devoured this and we will definitely be making this again.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: Excellent comfort food! Made this last night. Added in a drained can of white beans to make it a little more substantial. Cannot wait to turn some of the leftovers into a frittatta for lunch!

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: Can you sub riced cauliflower for the basmati?

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: Disappointingly bland. I felt that the 8" pan to cook it in was too small, making this more like a casserole than a "pizza". It wasn't bad, but it wasn't anything exciting. Cheese didn't get crispy, just soft and bubbly, even though it was browned

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: I am convinced this recipe is missing a step, otherwise why heat the oven to 500 when you’re only using the broiler. Also, I made it as written and it was pretty soupy. Is that how it’s meant to be?

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody:
Many ovens don’t preheat when you’re using the broiler, so heating to 500 is the equivalent. It makes the rice get crispy. 

As for the soupiness, the instructions are to cook the tomato down until it’s a chunky sauce.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: Coming back to say that this dish was yummy and I’ll definitely make it again. I used fresh tomatoes and I think that’s the key to making it a flavorful dish.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: Made this tonight.  Added in garlic and red pepper flakes to the onion.  We also added in hot Italian sausage. I had 2 helpings and hubby had 3.  I never eat 2 helpings of anything.  It's so good and so comforting right now.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: Must cook with garlic, parsley (or any herbs) & pepper! Jalepenos on the side and it was great.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: Wow, better than expected! Used canned tomatoes (can't remember which brand) and it turned out great. Spice is a must, I used a serrano chile and it completed the dish. Would make this again for a group.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: Simple, delicious. For a family of 4, this was a light meal with a lemon/garlic kale salad on the side. For a more substantial meal, I would cook some sausage (veggie or meat) to go on the side.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: Made the budget/fridge clean-out version of this; leftover pasta sauce, leftover rice, onion powder, and cheddar cheese. Actually pretty good, though I look forward to trying the fresh version.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: Big hit! Even better that it was easy. Used 10” cast iron enamel skillet to keep in one pan. Good choice.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: Love this dish, have made it twice so far. But save yourself some work by making everything in a pan that can go in the oven at a high temperature -- I use a cast iron skillet and it's gorgeous served that way at the table.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody:
This is perfect anytime, however in the year of COVID 19 it’s especially perfect!  I added anchovies, garlic and tomato paste as the base for the sauce.
It was sooo good!  I suggest making extra (brown) rice so you will have leftover for the freezer.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody:
Great, easy and comforting! I couldn't decide between pickled jalapenos and chipotle in adobo as a substitute for the green chili, thanks to Nancy for tipping me to the side of the chipotle.

In terms efficiency, this can be made from start to finish in an oven/broil safe pan and there's no need to preheat the oven for a broil.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: Wow, this is simple to make and certainly delicious. Definitely for when you need comfort food and fast!

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: This was so good I almost cried with happiness . The second time I made it I added broccoli and that was great too.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: This recipe is great as is, but even better when you add some leftovers to the tomato mix - chopped up sausages, green peppers, and when I don't have chilies I use chili paste - just as good.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: This was surprisingly fantastic! I didn't have high expectations due to the simplicity of the recipe, but this was wonderful. It has a similar richness to Risotto, but is much easier to pull together. Thanks Priya!

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody:
Delicious, almost perfect as written! Only two small recommendations: 

1. Add a couple anchovy’s right before adding tomatoes, let them break down in the oil. 

2. After adding tomatoes, add a splash of white wine and let reduce. At the very least it’s worth the smell in your kitchen!

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: I cooked 1 cup of brown basmati rice and used exactly 2 lbs of tomatoes (along with the onion, oil and chile pepper), but the photo above looks more tomato-ey than mine does. However, it's easy and smells good (haven't dipped into it yet) and if it's even halfway OK, we'll probably make it again; there aren't that many tasty and simple vegetarian dishes around.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: Just made this with plum tomatoes from the farmer's market, brown basmati rice, and Hook's super sharp cheddar. It was amazing! I doubled the recipe and kept the rice and tomatoes separate so that I can eat it for multiple days in a row.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: It’s a little bland if you take the seeds out of the chili pepper. Add thyme or other herb for aroma.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: I had some mild Italian poultry sausage sitting in the freezer and added it. Glad I did: helped add texture and extra seasoning to the rice. Hot Italian sausage would have been good too: love Beyond Meat hot italian sausage. Husband loved the way the cheese topping crisped up.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: Made with 3 anchovies, white beans and canned tomatoes. Use 1/2 of a large jalapeño, but added about a teaspoon of red pepper. Was really good. Left the rice a little undone and used juice do the tomatoes. Would like it a little spicier next time but was delightfully crunchy and good.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: Great dish for leftover rice! Added 1cup extra cheese, fresh thyme, cherry tomatoes and 28oz can of Italian tomatoes, black beans and otherwise followed the recipe. Put it into the over at 500 for 20 minutes then under the broiler...delish!

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: I've made this a few times as described using canned tomatoes and it's a delicious go-to. I think it would be a bit bland without the peppers. Would be great with some bread to sop up the extra and a bright side salad.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: Add cumin seeds to the green chile and onion fry, to take the flavour up several notches, and use pepper jack cheese.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: can i leave out the chile and add garlic? any other suggestions for a substitution?no chili in the house (:

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: Bell peppers would be good - red, green, and/or yellow.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: Has anyone used brown basmati rice instead of white? Brown rice is so much  healthier and tastier.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: It's only tastier if you like it.  My family, sadly, doesn't.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: Didn't have a chile so subbed in a scant 1/2 t of Penzey's dried jalapeno and that was too much. Made for a fiery dish! Used undrained San Marzano tomatoes and let them cook down for 15 mins. and that worked well. Will make again.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: Yummy but a little underwhelming. I will make it again but I'll add some herbs - basil, oregano, maybe cumin.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: Can cauliflower rice be used instead of balsamic?

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: This dish came out a little plain. It could definitely use more than one pepper. It was a fine side but if I make it again I would the recipe as a base and add more personal touches.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: WOW! This was so good and so simple! I used 1/2 lg jalapeno diced. Everything else was as listed. Will def make again!

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody:
As mentioned-the ingredients did not magically bake into something different than the original parts.   However I have not thought to do this on my own so I gave it a try.   Kids will like it - it has cheese.   
I very simple side dish which has its merits in the crunch.  
Follow directions as they are or play with the herbs, spices and cheese flavors.   It all works.  I was thinking to add some chorizo might be nice.  Maybe next time.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: Really tasty! I made due with on the vine tomatoes because of an instacart shopping issue and it still was perfect!

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: Voted "very tasty" by the fam. Made exactly as written (with canned tomatoes) except for bumping up the rice quantity. Next time I will also add additional canned tomatoes and more cheese.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: Easy to make, and very delicious, even better with smoked paprika. FolIowing others, I added 2 cloves of garlic and few chopped anchovies. And a teaspoon sweet smoked paprika. Hot smoked paprika would work too, but with less fresh chile, or maybe seeded.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: Added some Italian jarred tuna and matchstick zucchini (salted, water squeezed out) for a one-pot meal. Perfect! Would also be good with chopped spinach (also with water squeezed out). Definitely making again!

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: I'm not sure what went wrong. Maybe it was my rice cooker. The whole dish turned out "mushy" and we kept no leftovers.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: Can I use cooked Jasmine rice?

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: Even more delicious than I had imagined. I followed the recipe pretty closely-ish and added anchovy paste per one of the other reviewers. My broiler is broken so I cooked on 400, then ate some, then put back in. The more it crisps, the better it is. This reminded me of my Italian grandmother's sauce and rice but crunchy and with a kick. This goes into my regular rotation!

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody:
Excellent the day it’s made. Not as tasty on day 2. Try reducing the recipe. 
Added garlic, anchovy, and onion.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: We eat this without the cheese on top. I have made it every week since I saw the recipe. We love it. I use my husband's canned tomatoes that he makes with tomatoes from our garden.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: I didn't have cheddar on hand, so I mixed in some pimento cheese and shredded some parmesan on top -- still delicious!

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: This is missing a vital direction. The pan needs to be broiler proof. I know, I know, read the recipe through which I did,  and of course, I should have figured it out myself, but since it did not say, broiler proof, I didn't put it in a broiler proof pan. I can't dump it all out since the cheese is on top, so it will not be crispy. Minor, but important.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: This is a keeper! I used fresh tomatoes from my CSA and it was amazing. I also substituted fresh herbs for the chili bcz my husband can't tolerate anything spicy. It worked really well.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: Quick dinner using leftover rice and canned, fire roasted tomatoes.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: Has anyone made this a day ahead and then reheated or finished in broiler the next day? I’m going back to work in person this week and am looking for something super easy to have for my family when I get home for dinner

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: This recipe is so versatile!! My go to base for throwing stuff in rice. I had fancy saffron flavored rice that elevated this to an extreme level. Only had pickled Thai chiles but that ended up also improving the taste.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: One whole onion, 3 garlic cloves , better quality rice, less tomato if using 28oz can crushed tomatoes

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: This does sound lovely but I wonder if it would be better to use a risotto type rice as the flavours are mainly Italian.  The risotto rice would absorb the tomatoes whilst remaining lush.  I always think basmati has such a distinct taste that it dominates other flavours and is best with Asian type dishes.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: Why heat the oven to 500 if you later turn on the broiler?

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: This was a tasty side dish take on what I think of as Mexican rice. I used the canned San Mariano and felt that more spice, more onion or olive oil would have taken away a one-dimensional sauce that was too acidic without a mellowing oil or spice kick.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: I’ve made this as is and it was delightful. But it turned out equally good with added garlic and sautéed mushrooms and quinoa in place of the rice. Can’t wait to make this again!

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: Really great! We added frozen broccoli and peas into the mix, which worked out great. Resist the urge to add more liquid when adding frozen vegetables -  when it looked a bit dry, I added some of the leftover strained juice from the diced canned tomatoes and ended up with some pooled liquid at the bottom (still delicious).

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: I sometimes throw in some mild Italian sausage meat or plant-based ground beef with the onions in step 2 to make it a heartier meal. Sometimes also a pinch of red pepper flakes. It's good as written too.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: This could be pizza rice or Spanish rice depending on the herbs. In any case garlic as a must!   I used some fresh tomatoes and some can tomatoes since I doubled  The recipe, 1 1/2 × rice.    Very delicious as well as a very practical sauce.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: I love this! But I can’t get the cheese to get crispy without burning. Is it b/c I need to buy a higher quality cheese???

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: Which ounces? Volume or weight?

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody:
Used fresh tomatoes, was way too soggy, even after boiling it for half an hour longer than in the recipe.

Before you waste all the good juices by "straining" the freshly cut tomatoes, use at most half the amount and add some concentrated tomato paste.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: Made with large can Italian crushed tomatoes. Wes really liked it, although he didn’t like the cheddar cheese for some reason.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: Excellent.  Made1.25cups rice, use yellow skillet.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: I thought this was fantastic!  Rather than clean two dishes, I made mine in a Cast Iron skillet and stuck it right in the oven.  I chose to top it with pepperoni and two types of cheddar, though I might try a different type of cheese next time.  Finally, I added naan which served as a great substitute pizza crust.  Fantastic, healthy, and cheap overall!

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: My second NYT recipe and first post from this amateur cook. Made as directed with the addition of the garlic and anchovies from other commenters - thank you!! I try to make as much as I can ahead of time, so I made it in the morning in an 8" square pan, refrigerated, then baked at 375⁰ for 45 minutes - browned nicely and no need to broil. Let sit for 10 minutes - perfect, rave reviews and will go into our regular rotation!

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: (K) Made this and it was fine/good, but I put in a whole, seeded jalepeno and couldn't even taste it!  Must've been a VERY mild one.   I used frozen fresh tomato chunks from last summer and it was still very moist;  the cheese browned but the rice was NOT crispy, so either need to boil it down more or maybe undercook the basmati brown rice a little. Also added some chopped fresh spinach.  Like the idea of using Rotel w/peppers and/or some kind of protein in it.  Might try again, but fairly plain

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: what. to serve with?

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: I added Spanish paprika and sliced chorizo. Delicious!

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: Half a tsp of garam masala gave it a great warm flavor and served with full-fat yogurt on top. Yum!

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: I made a half recipe and did everything in a 10" oven proof skillet so it could go straight under the broiler. Instead of heating the oven I just let the broiler heat up while the tomatoes were simmering. Garlic salt instead of plain, a mixture of leftover Basmati and Calrose rice, and red onion were a couple of little mods. Very easy and quick, a tasty lunch.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody:
I should have read the other reviews more carefully and added anchovies, garlic, and maybe more, because this was honestly a waste of summer tomatoes.  Other than the cheese topping, it's insanely bland even with a good amount of salt and seasoning as I went.  The only change I made was leaving out the chili for my toddler, and baking everything in the stainless steel skillet I sauteed it in, to save a dish.  
I'm rarely disappointed by Priya's recipes but this needs way more flavor.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: Excellent recipe and a great vehicle for using up odds and ends in the kitchen. I added a little garlic and garam masala, extra chilies, some chopped kale that needed eating, and a can of butter beans. This is a keeper!

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: Beginner question here - if I wanted to add chicken to this recipe to make it more of a meal than a side dish, what's the best way to add that?  I'm thinking of cooking it separately, cutting it up, then adding at the beginning of step 4.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: I add tomato bouillon to the rice while it’s cooking and cayenne pepper to the overall dish to add more flavor

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: Incredible. One of my new fav recipes. Definitely add red beans, they kick it up a notch. Will be making this again and again.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: Simple, comforting dish. Easy preparation. Satisfying meal with a side salad or as a side dish to meat or fish.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: Made this dish but swapped out the serrano (or other) for 1/2 c. of that TRUFF hot sauce. Outstanding.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: Used blend of cheeses (sharp white cheddar and Gruyère). Supplemented the tomatoes with some pasta sauce from the fridge and basically used this as an opportunity to clean out the veg drawer and some leftover breakfast sausage. Delicious, great base recipe with lots of opportunities to customize.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: I added fresh curry leaves to the onions when I cooked them and it added an amazing taste and scent.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: You could add pepperoni or sausage or browned hamburger or really anything you like to add to your pizza.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: Made with some homemade vegan cheese I had left over, and red pepper flakes because I didn’t have a chile - delicious and super easy!!

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: This is SOOO GOOD! I used canned sauce and skipped the 6 minute "cooking down" step with the whole tomatoes. Also added peas and boudein sausage. Absolutely amazing.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: I love this recipe - have made it multiple times. I always add a tablespoon of cumin seeds for extra flavor.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: I absolutely love this recipe, it has become a comfort dish for me. It's so easy to make and adaptable! Thank you!

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: Garlic, 1/2 onion and 1 large poblano for the sauce base.  Added the can of Marzano tomatoes, cooked it down and added 1/2 cup caramelized yellow onion (already cooked) and  ancho chile powder.  Then mixed it all, added 1/4 lb. thin-sliced crispy, roasted portabello mushrooms (already cooked) on top before adding the vegan cheddar cheese and broiling it.  It's a keeper, no other changes needed.  I love that this recipe makes four dinner servings, two for now plus leftovers for work lunches.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: I used a Serrano pepper, seeds and all. My lips are tingling, but i was happy with the amount of heat. Diced avacado on top was really nice/cool with this dish.  Will make again when i have leftover basmati (fairly often).

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: Really wonderful. I used butter instead of olive oil and cooked the rice with two tablespoons of butter. Not as healthy, but very yummy.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: I questioned why I would use the top tack, but followed directions exactly. My oven proof Pyrex 8x8 dish blew up after 3 minutes.. I have a mess in my oven. I was looking forward to this, but now with a disaster I am crushed, and I have yet to begin to clean the oven mess. Disaster of a recipe. Yes my dish had been used many times in the oven.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: Sadly, Pyrex is not for use under a broiler.  An enamel or cast iron pan would be better.  So sorry this happened to you.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: Just fine.  Similar to a Spanish rice, with cheese.  Needed extra seasoning (added parsley, crushed red pepper, cracked black pepper), but still just fine…. Definitely not bad, but not awesome either.   I’d probably just make Spanish rice next time.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: Try this in muffin tins for individual servings.  Nice for plating

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: One 2” Serrano pepper proved a little spicy for Brian. Try next time with no pepper and use regular rice.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody:
Peppers, garlic, anchovies paste
Had with swordfish at Manley’s

Sauté rice in Tbls olive oil, do rice in frying pan

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody:
Use perrtonicini , garlic, anchivey paste
Manley’s with swordfish

Sauté rice

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: This was great! I ended up substituting a bit of chili crisp for the peppers as my grocery store sold out of peppers. Used canned tomatoes. Next time I will be a bit more bold with the broiler as it wasn't very "crispy" even though the cheese seemed fairly browned and very bubbly.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: I wanted to love this but it just misses - good but not 5-star.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: I used the fresh tomatoes instead of canned and found the dish to be way too watery. It was the consistency of risotto, and cooking off the liquid was making the rice overcook. Next time I’ll do it with canned tomatoes only.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: This is a fantastic dish. The tomato sauce, done right, is very rich. The description "pizza in rice form" is apt, but the serrano pepper gives it a sharp kick.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: I made this tonight in my big 14” cast iron skillet which went from stovetop to broiler. I added 1/2lb ground beef and 3 cloves garlic and used a 28oz can of crushed tomatoes and a handful of sliced cherry tomatoes. My rice was a few portions of leftover Near East Chicken Pilaf from the freezer. It came together quickly and looked just like the picture! It was delicious.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: Can add black beans in with tomatoes and serve with green veg.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: I made this with turmeric Pearl couscous and jarred pickled hot chili peppers. Turned out!

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: Has anybody tried this with leftover regular long grain rice?

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: I made a half recipe and used crushed tomatoes and add a 1/2 c of black beans. I only had pepper Jack cheese but it worked great. Really delicious and fast. Adding to my rotation as a quick side dish although I could make it entree worthy with a fried egg on top.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: I'm going to try this next time with Trader Joe's "Unexpected Cheddar"

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: Fabulous!  Made it with added garlic and tin of diced tomatoes, toped with squares of British mature cheddar block pushed down, a sprinkle of smoked paprika and Viola!  Grilled in the heated oven for about 15 minutes in a baking tray and absolutely a must-make.    *****

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: I think some Kalamata olives and/or roasted peppers would be a good addition.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: Used cherry tomatoes and threw in a can of black beans. I wish I crisped the rice…next time! Very easy comfort dish. Yum!

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody:
It was delicious!
Did it with leftover rice.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: Nice, and very malleable. I added a pound of breakfast sausage, browned first and set aside before cooking the onions and tomato, and added lots of black pepper since I didn't have a chile pepper. I had less rice than called for so only used 13 ounces of crushed tomato, and I eyeballed just enough cheese to cover the top in a 9x12 baking dish.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: Such a great use for leftover rice from takeout.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: In the 70's, my mom used to make a dish she called "risotto".  I think it came from a health food cookbook from a local L.A. restaurant, The Aware Inn, but I'm not certain.  It was similar to this recipe but her's included chunks of ground beef, green bell pepper, onions and green olives (which I believe was a nod to a dish that fitness guru Jack LaLaine created).  Anyway, I'm excited to try this (less 70's weird) recipe!

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: delicious and nutritious with change to brown rice( that someone else recommended) and addition of pinto and garbanzos which is what I had around.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody:
Didn't have actual chilis so I used a combination of diced tomatoes with green chilis that I had in the freezer and crushed tomatoes.  To make it spicier, I added sriracha.  
Great dish

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: I didn't care for this one; it needed more spice, maybe. I felt like it was more for kids than adults.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: This is an amazing weeknight dish that comes together quickly and ranks high on the comfort food scale. Taking a cue from other other notes on this recipe, we added many cloves of garlic after the onions. We also added a ¼ cup hot sauce with the tomatoes which fired everything up. This one's a keeper!

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: Finally made this.  It was really nice.  I added some Kashmiri chili powder which worked nicely.  Served with Indian-ish grilled boneless chicken thighs and a green salad. Left it under the broiler a little longer than stated to get that crispiness.  It really did remind me of the Spanish rice my mom always made with her over-cooked (but still good) pork chops.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: I used a mix of brown rice and lentils and threw a sliced up pepperette in with the onion.  Also added some shredded cabbage to the onion to up the veggie content.  Delicious  and so easy!

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: Add garlic, oregano, black beans.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody:
I made with canned chopped tomatoes and added garlic. We had some prawns to use so I added those at the last step. 
This was lovely, with some or without the prawns!

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: Used about 1/2-1 tsp jarred Calabrian chilies in place of fresh… so good. Grown up comfort food.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: Easy and fast. Could use more seasoning, perhaps sriracha.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: Very easy and fast, as long as you have cooked rice on hand. I could use a bit more seasoning, perhaps sriracha.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: This is an excellent (and simple) recipe.  Only modification was that I used a Serrano pepper with seeds rather than the jalapeño called for in the recipe.  My reasoning: a bit more heat with the Serrano

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: Followed pretty closely but used Forbidden rice (black) because I find white  to be too insipid, and white rice with tomatoes reminds me too much of the sinfully bland midwestern favorite "Spanish Rice".  Added garlic.  Pretty solid recipe, very simple and quick.  I would likely never make this with fresh tomatoes because canned will almost always be better, plus one of the virtues of this recipe is it's essentially a pantry dish...Almost everything will already be on hand in your pantry.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: I have made this dish twice with leftover Basmati Rice, and both times we loved the effortless application with a great satisfying tum!

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody:
Han 2024
Made with fresh tomatoes, v watery and should try canned. Still yummy

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: Spread out on a larger dish and double the cheese,

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: Easy. A great way to use tomatoes from my garden.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: Easy quick dinner that I rarely need to pack up leftovers. Fresh tomatoes are great, but certainly tasty with the canned variety.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: Don’t drain the tomatoes

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: I don’t under all the wonderful comments. I found this barely edible. Reminded me of the Mexican Rice-a-Roni I ate as a child.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: Delicious! I heated the oven up to scorching 500ºF. Used one cast iron skillet to sizzle the onions and peppers. I used basmati rice, canned whole tomatoes, an 8 oz. block of extra sharp cheddar, and 1/2 block of colby/jack. Put it all under the broiler for 4 minutes total. We devoured it.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: I have my own "Spanish" rice I make with a sofrito of sweet peppers, roasted chilis, onions and garlic, and plum tomatoes, all frozen from last year 's garden.  Leftover rice is great with cheddar cheese broiled on top and makes a terrific lunch.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: Has anyone tried this but with uncooked rice to let the rice cook in with the tomato’s? If so, did you amp up the liquid? I’m wondering if I used a 28oz can of tomatoes with the liquid if that would be sufficient to cook the rice (with additional time simmering of course). I’m thinking it would amp up the taste of the rice.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: I tried this today and it was quite delicious- surprisingly so.   Easy to make and quite economical.  Recipe feeds 4-6  for around 8-10$$. I followed the recipe but can think of all sorts of additions, but truly you don't need to.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Tomato Rice With Crispy Cheddar Recipe
commentBody: Followed the recipe faithfully.  I expected it to be a heavy, sticky, clumped rice dish.  It was anything but; light, almost airy, and much more flavorful than one would think could be made out of the exceptionally limited ingredients.  I'll definitely make it again.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Can you please explain that tip about coating the pan with water first?  I thought that adding milk to water would automatically make them mix.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Somehow it re-separates after you pour the milk in. It does keep the milk from sticking to the bottom, making clean up much quicker

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: @chris woo u have to heat the water first so it boils then add the milk to the boiling water and that is where the magic happens. It really works.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Be advised that using store-bought yogurt for your starter (even organic yogurt) will produce good results for a few generations, but will often start failing after the third or fourth batch, resulting in yogurt that is still partially (or mostly) milk. If you want to make yogurt that can be used over and over again as your starter, you should use an heirloom starter. These are widely available for purchase (thank you, internet) . . . or even better, find a friend who wants to share!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Oh that's what happened to my yogurt! I thought it was the yogurt maker.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I agree with J.L.  I belong to a yogurt coop and we found that grocery store yogurt works great as a starter but does not work well after a while.  We purchase a new batch from a store once a month or so to keep the culture fresh.  Makes one wonder what the problem is with the cultures in mass produced yogurt.  Perhaps they are built to die after a while to help control contamination at the plant and maintain a consistent product.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I picked up a good tip from the book Classical Turkish Cooking by Ayla Algar. After you’ve heated up the milk, keep it heated to 190 degrees F. for around 10 minutes for a thicker result. Then let it cool.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Much simpler recipe: microwave the milk in a glass/ceramic container. Cool it until it is luke warm (you should be able dip your finger in it and keep it for 10 seconds barely). Add yoghurt culture to the container. Keep it in the oven with light on overnight. if it is not setting properly, put a dry red chilli in it -- it does the trick.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Responding to the comment about starter failure — I used to encounter starter failure after I began to make yogurt, but I found that using a store bought Icelandic skyr-type as starter avoided this problem. I prefer Siggi's due to the high content of live bacteria and protein. While this was my experience, I hope this works for anyone encountering this problem!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Does the oven need a pilot light? My oven doesn't have one. Could my yogurt rest on top the clothes drier when I'm doing laundry?

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: The oven does not need a pilot light — mine is electric. The lightbulb alone gives off enough heat.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I have no oven light or dryer. Could the oven be preheated to 200 or something and then let cool for a bit before placing in the yogurt?

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: You can use a lid and layers of blanket to cover the yogurt mixture. Blankets work as insulation and keep the mixture in right temperature. We usually keep it overnight on kitchen counter and transfer it to the fridge in the morning.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Is it 130 Farenheit or Celsius?  Getting a bit over the disregard for subscribers who live in countries outside mainland USA in your measurements NY Times Cooking!  Surely it doesn't take much to show a conversion of measurements/temperatures.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I always assume it's Farenheit with the NYT unless otherwise stated, though I operate in C for everything else.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Farenheit.  Water boils at 100° C, so it can't possibly be Celsius

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
My Greek father made yogurt every Saturday evening for the next week. His directions were nearly the same except for his tip about how to figure out when the milk has cooled to the correct level:
Hold your finger in the milk until you can just count to 8 slowly.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Made two batches. Couldn't be easier. Heated organic whole milk to about 185-190F, let it cool (about 25 minutes). Used low-fat yogurt for the culture since it was all I had. Couldn't believe that the oven light would create enough warmth for the yogurt to set but it did. Needed the oven later for bread baking, so moved everything to the laundry room and in three hours, it was set. No expensive appliance needed.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
Instant pot yogurt is so easy

A great starter is nancy’s Which has four bacteria. So does Whole Foods brand.  

I prefer organic whole milk. after heating to 190, let it cool to 105f. Then 9 hours.  PERFECTO

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I live in a tropical climate, where my yogurt practically makes itself in a covered bowl, but needs to be refrigerated soon after. Otherwise it will ferment after a few days. Because fresh milk is difficult to transport, I use UHT (boxed) milk. It works fine. I was surprised by your suggestion that the milk could be 130 degrees. My experience has been that adding culture to milk over 116 degrees will kill it.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I have been making yogurt at home for many years and the results are much better (and cheaper) than store bought yogurt.   Using a starter from a previous batch doesn't work (as noted in the other comments).  I buy a powdered starter from the New England Cheese Making Co. and use a new packet each time.   Heat to 185 F and hold at that temp for 30 minutes.  Cool to 135 - 140 and add starter.   Result is a creamy yogurt - not watery.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I’ve been using the last few spoonsful to make the next batch for years. It works fine. And cheaper than buying packets each time.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
I’ve been making homemade yogurt for nearly my entire adult life, and before that with my mother in my childhood kitchen.
Using a starter from a previous batch absolutely does work and will provide more customisable results (once one has mastered temperature and setting times)
If your prior starter isn’t working it’s an indicator that the live culture in your old starter are dying due to mishandling, or the new milk is too hot or contains preservatives, or the setting temperature is off.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I remove about a 1/4 cup of yogurt from each batch I make for the next starter and have never had an issue. That said, I would imagine that if you kept it too long the cultures would die off (most yogurts with live cultures recommend eating within 5 days of of opening), but I usually make my next batch before the first one runs out. I've also heard that the whey can be used as a starter as well but haven't tried that yet, but I do freeze it in large ice cube trays for adding it to other things.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
I lived in developing countries where fresh milk was either unavailable or unsafe, so I made my yogurt with UHT milk. It turned out fine. That said, the milk is sufficiently cool when you can hold your finger in it comfortably and count to 10. Proceed as directed.

When it gets a little too sour over time, use it for cooking where buttermilk is called for.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I've been making yogurt for 2-3 years in a Yogotherm. I used Bulgarian yogurt (maybe White Wave brand?) as my starter and it's still active; I add a little nonfat powered milk to kick up the protein-- about 1/3 cup to 1/2 gallon milk. The Fermentation God (Sandor Katz) advised a 24-hour ferment for increased sourness and it works beautifully.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Be sure to use milk that is not ultra pasteurized. The ultra pasteurizing process kills beneficial bacteria used to produce yogurt.  In many parts of the country it is impossible to get organic milk that has not been ultra pasteurized.  If you are not able to find organic milk that is ultra pasteurized use the best local milk that is pasteurized.  Yogurt can also be made with milk of lower fat content such as 2%.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I can't afford even the local milk, unfortunately. I used a moderately priced pasteurized milk from the grocery with good results.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I differ with Steve.  You can make yogurt from canned milk, powdered milk, and UHT pasteurized milk. People all over the world make yogurt, and they don't all have access to organic milk.Granted, using organic milk that has not been pasteurized with high heat is preferable, but UHT milk will make good yogurt, in fact many people skip the heating with UHT milk because that process has already changed the proteins.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: By heating the milk, you are pasteurizing it. If you use ultra pasteurized milk you can skip the heating part and go right to the fermentation process. This is commonly referred to as a "cold start"

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
This worked perfectly! I live in Northern California and used Clover organic whole milk and Brown Cow plain full fat yogurt.

I left mine in the oven for 3.5 hours and it set up nice and jiggly.  I let it sit overnight and the flavor is so light and clean and delightful.  

This recipe is a keeper!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Why preferably organic?  Science says it does not matter.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Organic does not matter. Any whole milk will do.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: This is very easy and makes plain yogurt that is not as sour as store bought.  Has a very pleasant tang.  I make mine with organic whole milk.  I put the hot mixture in a bowl covered with plastic wrap in a warm place in my kitchen (not the oven, which is often in use by husband or kids), wrapped with two kitchen towels and left it there for nine hours before putting in mason jars then in the fridge.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Making yogurt in the Instant Pot is way easier than this. Just get milk, ultrafiltered like Fairlife, and use store bought plain yogurt as the starter. Press the yogurt button and voila. 8 hrs later put it in the frig overnight.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I believe the point of this recipe is for people who *don't* have an instant pot. (Ie. you don't need special equipment).

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I’ve been making this weekly for the last few weeks. Nothing to add re:technique, but I have been so pleased to find such an easy way to remove one more source of unnecessary plastic from my life!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
You can make yogurt with any milk. Years back, in school, I made yogurt with rehydrated powdered milk, that was what I could afford. It wasn't luxurious but it worked. 

If you are scalding your milk, you are probably destroying the same bacteria and enzymes as ultrapasturizing. I  use ultrapasturized lactose free milk microwaved to 110 to 115 degrees Fahrenheit. 130 degrees would kill off my culture, which I got at health food store. If I watch the temperature, it just keeps going.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I have been doing this since January and it's still delicious! I started with a 6oz cup of Stonyfield and have kept it going ever since. Sometimes, I strain out the whey for a greek-style. I have also used it to make frozen yogurt with stunning results. Today, I happened to have in my fridge about 1/4 c. peach puree (blanched 2 whole peaches in boiling water for 15 seconds each, peeled the skins off and pureed the slices in a NutriBullet), which I whirled into about 3/4c yogurt - sublime!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Echoing the Heirloom Starter note... I started 11 years ago with a mesophilic (room temp) starter ordered online and have had the "line" going ever since with one pint Mason/Ball jars.  With a mesophilic culture, you just add two tbsps of a freshly opened jar from last week to a full jar of 2% (or other) milk, mix, set the sealed jars in a warm place (~75F) and 24 hours later you have great yogurt. With whole milk, it's rich makes it richer with half-half it with makes superb creme fraiche.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: With whole milk, it's richer and creamier.  With half-and-half, it makes superb creme fraiche. ;-)

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: No pilot light or light bulb in my oven.  I use a 6-pack sized hard-sided cooler as an incubator:  fill 2 quart jars with boiling water and place in the cooler while you heat and cool the milk.  Dump out the hot water, fill the jars with milk that has yogurt cultures added, put jars back into the (now warm) cooler, stuff any spare space with dish towels, and let sit for 8-12 hours.  Been using the same culture (originally from store bought Nancy's yogurt) for 20+ years.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
What is the purpose of the tsp of yogurt in the bottom of the container?

I find if I use more than 1 Tb yogurt in the milk, the resulting yogurt is stringy.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: My batch did not come together-it thickened slightly but not enough. I think 130 is too high a temperature for the milk before adding starter. I have made it before (using a yogurt maker for incubation) with the milk at 120 F. I may not have let it set long enough though. This is a work in progress!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Many recipes I've read call for heating milk to about 170°F, cooling it down to about 130°, & then adding starter. Upshot: there's a lot of leeway in heating up. NB: if milk's not cooled enough, then heat will kill starter.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I heat my milk to 180ish then cool to 110. I use a gallon of organic milk and whisk in about 1T culture. Cover it & pop it into my gas oven. My light doesn't work but seems warm enough with just the pilot. I try to time it so that it incubates overnight. In the morning I immediately put it in the fridge to cool. I also strain it for thicker, greek-style yogurt and the excess whey is great in mashed potatoes and other things where a buttermilk-like tang is desired.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
Don't need whole milk: 2% or skim will do.
After cooling in the refrigerator, then strain through a cheese cloth to make thick, creamy Greek style deliciousness.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I originally incubated my yogurt in a cooler filled with warm water, but then I realized that my oven has a bread proofing setting (100°). So I just pop it into the oven on the bread proofing setting and leave it overnight.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Excelente Tere!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I have a question.  Does this work with Fairlife brand lactose free milk?

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: No. Making yogurt is a fermentation process in which the bacteria eat up the lactose. When yogurt is done it becomes naturally lactose free.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Many years ago, I was still a young woman,  a Syrian lady living in Buenos Aires gave me this tip: Try the temperature of the milk with the little finger of the hand you use the least and count up to 20.( Later I understood that is the most sensitive finger). If the temperature is hot but you can stand it , it is the right temp to make the yougurt. It works perfectly! No thermometer needed.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: First-time yogurt maker here and this recipe was a great success. I discovered my candy thermometer was broken as soon as I started but did fine without it. Brought the milk to a simmer and turned it off then waited about 35 minutes until I could hold my finger in the milk w/o being burned. Left my yogurt to “culture” in my oven, checking it every hour or so. This is very simple and I’m hoping to cut down on single use plastics by doing this instead of buying endless plastic containers of yogurt

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I pour off the whey from the yogurt I make and keep it in a jar in my refrigerator. That is what I use as a starter. It works great.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: can you use a non dairy milk?

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: If you want a non-dairy milk yogurt, you may need to look up a recipe for it, as you cannot use the dairy starter used here.  Most recipes online call for emptying a container of probiotic into the cooled milk.  I have had minimal success with this method, unfortunately.  Sometimes adding a little gelatin helps, but this can make a strange texture to the  finished product.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: It worked! Very nice mild flavor. I did let the heated milk cool to 105 degrees, as a commenter suggested, and added 2 T. dry milk to the cooling mixture. I used the last quarter cup of my favorite Wallaby greek yogurt as a starter, and it seemed to do fine. I left the jar in the oven overnight. One set of plastic throwaways I can do without...

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Has anyone tried using the "Proof" setting (if you have it) on your oven? I understand that it's 110F. Too hot?

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: We use the proof setting and it works well. It takes maybe 4 hours for us.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I use a vacuum flask instead of putting the jar in the oven. It does mean decanting the yogurt rather than putting the jar straight into the fridge when it has set.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: In winter the average home temperature is 68F. Would the oven light 4W bulb behind a thick glass (or similar protective cover) will generate enough heat to aid fermentation? Has anyone checked the temperature diffrence? In my oven (Wolf) it is measly 2-3F advantage (this could be just nay imagination). Higher wattage bulb (100W?) would help but what a waste of energy.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: you can use store bought milk for your starter. I suggest making the yogurt with fresh raw milk. My family has been making yogurt every week for two years with milk from a local farm and the yogurt tastes richer, creamier, and tangier

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Just to be clear, the FDA says that raw milk can carry bacteria like salmonella, listeria, e-coli and others that cause foodborne illnesses, which are sometimes fatal. Yogurt isn't worth it. Full stop.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
A, I'm with you on the raw milk. I've been getting raw dairy for several months now, and it's great stuff.

And yes, sure, it can carry bacteria -- both bad AND good bacteria. So can lots of other foods -- eggs, hotdogs, lettuce, strawberries, you name it. Bacteria are a fact of life. Just make sure you get your milk from a clean, reputable supplier.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: the reason i'd like to make my own yogurt is to cut down on the plastic waste... and I prefer goat milk yogurt.  Does anyone have any suggestions on getting goat milk that's not already in plastic, and any special treatment for making it into yogurt?

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I have used evaporated milk and a plain yoghurt starter with live cultures for years.  The result is a beautiful, creamy, thick Greek yoghurt.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: If making one at a time is silly for a family, note that putting the jars of yogurt-to-be in a cooler filled with warm water works great.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I've been making yogurt for years, and I let it ferment for 24 hours before refrigerating it.  I do use a yogurt maker rather than tie up the oven.  The long fermentation removes almost all the lactose.  Even people who are bothered by lactose can eat it.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: When shopping for starter look for a yogurt that has only milk and cultures as the ingredients, and not for example added milk solids or gelatin. The culture strains in those simple yogurts tend to work best at home - they match up well.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Lactose Free Yogurt:  A recipe for lactose free yogurt came with my Prooofing/Slow cooker.  It says that the bacterial in the yogurt eats the lactose.  Their regular yogurt recipe has the yogurt sit at low heat for 3 hours and for lactose free yogurt to simply let the yogurt sit at low heat for 19 hours.  Yogurt to act as starter for your next batch needs to be removed after 3 hours.  I am not sure how this works if you aren't holding the yogurt at 86 degrees as the proofer does.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I will try this recipe. Back in the day (1970), living on a commune in southern Oregon with wood for heat and cooking, we used to make yogurt in 1 gallon glass jars and then they were placed in a down sleeping bag for a cozy ferment. Good memories.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Made yogurt for years using various methods. I thought >115F kills the culture. I’ve used powdered culture (most reliable) and yogurt for culture. If you’ve got one, the easiest way to get the right temp is via sous vide. Heat 1/2 gal whole milk on stove to 180F then let it rest til cools to 110F using instant read thermometer. P it 1T yogurt in each of two quart canning jars, add warm milk, cover w plastic lids and put into sous vide bath at 90F -100F til done, usually 6-8 hrs. Easy peasy!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: What is the oven temperature with a light. I do not have an oven light

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: For years I have made yogurt by freezing one quart dannon in ice cube trays and then using 2 cubes as my culture to make 4 pints yogurt. Works perfectly and I can make gallons of yogurt with fresh culture every time.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: In India, homemade yoghurt, or dahi, is standard. I want to recommend making it in traditional Indian style in an unfired clay pot - the pot absorbs some of the moisture and yields beautiful creamy dahi.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
I grew up with homemade yogurt.
1.Boil a gallon of whole milk till it starts to simmer (we ate a lot of yogurt). Let it cool according to the pinky method. I tolerate heat so I use a thermometer. It should be @ 118 degrees F. While the milk is cooling I take plain yogurt and let sit to room temp. 3. When the milk has cooled I stir in about 1/4 cup of yogurt. I stir well @2 min. then pour the mixture into a large container. Then I put the container in the microwave overnight or about 5hrs.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I have been using half of a capsule of probiotic supplements to make our yoghurt. It's easy and I thought perhaps I'd get some extra good strains along with the typical ones. The result is delicious.  If anyone has some advice, knowledge about this approach, please share!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Heat milk to 170-180 degrees, then cool to 110-115.  I let my starter come to room temperature while milk is heating.  Also, 1/4 cup of starter  is too much for just 4 cups of milk.  I use two tablespoons for a gallon of milk. Make sure your store-bought starter does not contain additives (gelatin or others) which will result in yoghurt being gritty and not smooth. I pour my yoghurt in quart jars and set jars down in an ice chest wrapped in a towel.  Usually five hours is long enough to ferment.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: The CULTURE we use is the drained whey as we strain our new yogurt to thicken it similar to 'real' Greek yogurt. Keep 'whey' in refrigerator until need new starter. No need to worry about old yogurt not dissolving properly. Whey can be used soooo many other ways. Soil additive for blueberry bushes or anything that needs acid soil. I also use the excess whey in place of any water needed for baking recipes. So get some real culture. ;)

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: As I was having issues with temperatures, it suddenly occurred to me that I needed something that would keep a constant temperature. My Instant Pot is a small one and has no yogurt setting. So, I started using my sous vide immersion cooker. Guess what? Perfect results every single time. Instructions here: https://the-good-plate.com/homemade-yogurt-with-the-sous-vide-immersion-cooker/

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
Here's an even easier recipe, with the same ingredients:
Pour the 1/4 cup of yogurt back into the container, place in fridge, and voila! Yogurt.
Now drink the milk with some cookies.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Homemade tastes so much better than store-bought, it's really worth it! And so easy! (And half the price).

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I used to put my yogurt in the oven next to the lightbulb, but I make yogurt 1-2x/week and it was annoying to replace the oven lightbulg. Now I put my two mason jars in a little travel cooler and pack the empty space around them with dishtowels and napkins - no additional energy needed and no more trips to the hardware store to buy oven bulbs.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Many years ago (decades actually) I made yogurt using a home version of microbiology lab water bath technique: electric frypan to keep the water at the desired temperature and Corning coneflower casserole to hold the milk and starter. Very easy, works great.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I started making my own yogurt when our local recycling place stopped taking plastic. All those tubs! I couldn’t stand it. Now I don’t know why anyone would buy yogurt - it is so easy to make.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I have been making yogurt for decades. I put the pot of milk in a larger one with water to make a double boiler. I bring the milk to a low simmer (about 185 degrees F), then cool the pot in a tub of cold water until it's comfortable to stick my finger into but stings a little if I move my finger around. I put my pot of yogurt (after mixing in 2 tablespoons of starter yogurt mixed with warm milk) on low on a heating pad, covered with towels, inside an insulated grocery bag.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
1/4 cup is a LOT of starter. Sure works fast because of lots of culture. However, slow is better as in many things in life.

2 tablespoons for a gallon of milk and overnight in the oven with light on works like a charm. I have been using the propagated/inherited culture for over 5 years now.

I refuse to call it the same culture as when I started. Every batch with exposure to air and environment changes. Sometimes a lot and sometimes a little. My family and I can taste those big changes.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I'm extremely skeptical of the claim that organic milk can affect textural results. Lots of industrial organic milk is ultra-pasturized (high heat for very short time) which damages much of the protein structure (you can't make solid curd cheese with ultra pasteurized milk for example). Better to use a good local milk (organic or not) which has been pasteurized at lower temp for 30 minutes. Less efficient but much better for the milk.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Absolutely. If you are lucky enough to live near a dairy farm, milk from a local farmer (often not labelled organic due to cost) is far better, for the environment, for your health, and for your tastebuds, than organic milk from industrial farms thousands of miles away.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: This is a good basic yogurt recipe. Many of the alternative recipes posted in the comments are pretty similar to this one. It's yogurt; you can improvise! If you live in D.C. as I do, and it's July, you can just put the container on your balcony for five or six hours. I wonder what would happen if I put the container in a parked car. Too hot, probably.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: If you happen to have an 'instapot' cooker. There is a yogurt button. The ingredients are the same, but the process is "push yoghurt button".

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I use skim or 2% and, as I need the calcium, I add 1/2 C powdered milk to the cold milk and mix thoroughly, then proceed.  I have used regular yogurt as my starter and change it to new every 6 months or so. Lastly, be a scientist and buy a milk thermometer!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: By using glass returnable bottles of milk (I use Strauss) and a glass jar of starter yogurt (I use Benoit) you eliminate all plastic waste, which was one reason I started making my own yogurt in the first place.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Years ago when I was visiting my brother at college his Lebanese roommate shared some yogurt with us. He made yogurt every day or two using yogurt culture that he had brought from Beirut. The culture had been passed down in his family generation after generation for the past 400 years. (It was very good yogurt!)

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: There's an even easier method that requires no special tools and no heating the milk.  I've made Caspian Sea Yogurt for twenty-two years now, over here in Japan.  I semi-sterilize pint jars half-filled in the microwave.  When they cool, I add the mother culture (saved from the previous batch), fill with milk, and leave on the counter for 10-18 hours, depending on temperature.  That's it.  Refrigerate and enjoy delicious easy to make yogurt.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I put the milk after I have boiled it in the oven to let it cool. As the milk cools  it warms the oven so I don't have to leave the oven light on. It is enough to help set the yogurt. I also wrap the container in a baby blanket to retain the heat as it sets. I have been making yogurt for about 10 years and makes me feel good about saving the plastic. I always make a big batch and share with my friends so more plastic is saved.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I add 1 tblsp of honey and scrape a vanilla bean into the milk for extra flavor. Have been using the pevious batch for my starter with no problems for years now.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Sometimes I buy ultrapasturized organic milk instead of regularly pasturized, and it makes the recipe even simpler. Omit the first heating of the milk and heat it only to 105-110. Add starter and incubate.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: one thing I picked up from other recipes- instead of incorporating the yogurt into the milk, pour some of the milk into the yogurt and stir. this gets it up to temp and makes it easier to homogenize with the milk. first time trying this recipe- my yogurt is in the oven now!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: In cooler temps, after boiling the milk to 180 degrees "cool" it down to 110 degrees F. I use a thermometer and don't worry if it is 105 or 110 degrees. I add four to five teaspoons of previously made yogurt or a packet of starter from yogourmet or the Bulgarian starter from Cultures for health or https://bacillusbulgaricus.com these are more attuned to Indian tastes...unless you have a local Indian friend who make yogurt at home (very few.) I use an Instant pot and use the Yogurt setting.

author: Priya Krishna
