commentBody: You can easily make yogurt without lighting the oven.  Prepare the milk yogurt mixture as usual and pour into good quality metal thermoses.  Cover but don't seal - with the plug and cap to keep warm.   I often put warm water in the thermos to heat it up before adding the milk mixture.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Has anyone made this with non-dairy milk?

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: 130 is too hot. 110 to 220 is ideal for adding starter.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: If you have a sous vide apparatus it is perfect for making yoghurt. Set it and forget it.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: If you like a thicker, greek-style yogurt, add milk powder when you add the starter. I add about 1/3 cup to a quart of milk. It thickens up nicely and no need to strain it later. (I also add some probiotic powder from capsules to make it a probiotic yogurt, but do this after it cools.)

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I love all of the riffs shared here on how to make yogurt. “Precious” recipes - exact temps, exact amounts, exact timing, special ingredients - do a disservice to how common yogurt has been throughout time, among many cultures (no pun intended), and across geographic regions. The greatest joy of my cooking journey has been reaching the point that every recipe is now only a suggestion.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I’ve been making yogurt for 40  years. I strain it to make it Greek like. I use a large coffee filter in the strainer and after 3-4 hours, the yogurt just peels off neat and clean. ALSO… a tip! Save the whey from the straining. It’s your new starter!! I’m surprised NYT hasn’t advised this. It’s has multiple benefits. Look it up.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I have made yogurt for some time, once the entire batch has cooled to 130 or lower I mix the yogurt starter into the big pot of soon to be yogurt and put in quart or pint canning jars. And put in my dehydrator at 115 degrees for 24 hours for regular yogurt and 48 hours for Greek yogurt

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
130 F = ca. 54.44 C
1 quart = ca. 950 ml

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I picked up a trick from a website ( wish I could credit it) to let your yogurt sit for at least two days in the fridge to strengthen the culture.  That plus the 24-hr ferment I use seems to keep my store bought culture healthy through many makings.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I do not see the "ingredient list" mentioned in the recipe. Please explain. Thank you.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I think that means you need to see "live active cultures"  on the store-bought yogurt you are adding the 1/4 cup of to the milk in the recipe.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: My batch stayed liquid, and did not set even after 6 hours. Any other tips?

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: To this quantity, try adding a couple of TBS of skim milk powder to the cooled milk.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Can skim milk be used for a non-fat version?

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I have been using organic non-fat milk for the last year, making 6 C of yogurt at a time using my starter.  I leave the jar in the oven for 11-12 hours. Lately I use an Instant Pot as my incubator. I place my jar of heated milk and yogurt starter inside the Instant Pot, cover, hit the yogurt setting and set time to 10 hours, works great! Whole milk will always taste richer but for those of us who don’t eat full fat dairy, non-fat is a viable and delicious alternative.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I just made yogurt this week from skim milk. It came out beautifully. I did strain it through a kitchen towel for a few hours to separate the whey so I could have No Fat Greek Yogurt.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Can 1% or 2% milk be used? With the same results.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: How does humidity affect the process? I live in a fairly humid climate, and have had similar yogurt recipes fail numerous times, even with careful temp checking, full fat organic milk, full fat live active yogurt. I'm wondering if it's b been the humidity all along?

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Has anyone tried non-dairy versions; almond milk? Not tried, but read it won’t work/set with commercial almond milk.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: The only non dairy milk I’ve found to work is Edensoy unsweetened. Don’t put in the starter until the milk cools to 120 degrees.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Is the finished product lactose-free?

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: The only two ingredients are literally the most iconic lactose items in any grocery store...

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: If you let it go long enough, yes. 24 hours should do it, but I find I can tolerate it well at 16 hours. 8 hours is sweeter but bothers the tummy.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: This is a legit question, so don't mind Joseph's response. Fermentation will modify the proteins in most foods. I do not know the specifics of yogurt, so i googled lactose and yogurt and found this: https://www.healthline.com/nutrition/dairy-foods-low-in-lactose#:~:text=lower%20in%20lactose.-,Summary%3A,that%20contains%20live%20bacterial%20cultures.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Can you use UP milk to make this?

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Mine came out a bit runny but wonderful flavor. I wonder if cooking the milk longer would add thickness? Only variable I think. Thoughts?

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: You should let the yogurt sit for longer in the oven. I leave it over night. If that doesn't work, add more yogurt to the warm milk.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Another question! The consistency was perfect on Day 1 and Day 2 but started to get watery after that and what was left on Day 4 and 5 was drinkable. Suggestions?

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: What is the general opinion on fermenting yogurt in instant pot?

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: It would work. I haven't tried it though. I have a Euro Cuisine yogurt maker which keeps the yogurt at the right temperature.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I  love mine. I make it directly in jars. It blurbs a bit on the boil stage but the outsides are easy to clean before I store them in the fridge.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I tried to make yogurt after a many-decades hiatus. Warmed the milk, cooled it, stirred in the Greek yogurt (all I had), and let it sit. Nothing! Thick milk is what I got. Now what should I do with a half-gallon of not-milk/not-yogurt? Suggestions eagerly sought.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: It doesn't work good with greek yoghurt; use standard plain yoghurt.  Your not-milk not yoghurt is probably bad now, but its always useful in cooking.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Get an Instant Pot with the yogurt button. Follow their directions and you'll have great yogurt. I like thick yogurt, and don't like to strain it once made. I use ultrafiltered milk by Fairlife, and about a quarter cup of store bought yogurt with ACTIVE CULTURES. Comes out great every time.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Hard to say without more details, but your "thick milk" could be used to make a pudding. Or maybe you can use it in place of heavy cream - you could try whipping it. Or simmering it with aromatics to make a rich salad dressing. I guess it's been 5 months so you probably have long since thrown away your concoction, but... for future reference!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Wonderful flavors..the rosemary oil with lemon zest added complex brightness to the subtle cauliflower .  And the crunch of the croutons...Yum!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Can I make this yogurt with Lactaid milk? Or does the process kill off the lactose?

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I have read that you can. I make mine with regular milk and let it go 16 hours. 24 hours makes it too rope-y for me and 8 hours is not enough. YMMV I use an Instant Pot so it maintains the steady temp over that length of time.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Don't bother. Yogurt is naturally lactose free. It's consumed by the bacteria in the fermentation process

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I simply heat a quantity of milk until bubbles form, wait until it feels like a baby's bathwater, and whisk into it thoroughly a bit of left-over yogurt (Fage, store-bought Lebanese) and sometimes a bit of bacillus powder I buy from Bacillus Bulgaricus (excellent source for different kinds of yogurt).  Cover and put into the oven, surrounding the pan with a Turkish towel (like a baby), and turn on the oven light.  I let it set overnight, sometimes for as long as 24 hours.  It just gets better.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: What do you mean by "leaving the oven light on"? Do you mean a pilot light?

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I use store bought yoghurt first batch, then the made yoghurt for following batches; or the whey that forms with the homemade will also work well as starter.  The whey will keep a few weeks in fridge.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: My biggest accomplishment during lock down was learning to make great yogurt in an Insta Pot! It's easy and after a few trials produces very smooth yogurt. Cooks for 8 to 9 hours start to finish (depending on how much of a tang you are looking for.) Go to bed and wake up with a pot of yogurt.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
Share
My method is even simpler. I take a quart Pyrex jug; heat just Uber a quart of whole milk till scalded, about seven minutes. The heat on half power for fifteen minutes. Cool to about 113 degrees. Whisk in about two tablespoons yoghurt from a previous batch, pour into glass one pint jars, close with a lid and leave in the oven with the light on for around six hours or overnight. Refrigerate. Lasts at least a couple of weeks, becoming more acid. Easy and a winner!!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I began making yogurt at home a couple years ago and experienced repeated failures until I found that ultra pasteurized milk doesn’t work. Trader Joe’s organic milk makes great yogurt since it is not ultra pasteurized!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I made lots of yogurt last summer, happily. Instead of the oven-light method, I plugged in a heating pad, wrapped it with a towel, and set the pot of yogurt on it overnight to 'jell.' It was fail-proof and yielded a rich, satisfying batch every time.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Recommendations for a very old-fashioned gas oven -- there's no "oven light" to turn off or on. The pilot flame is always on, and the lowest setting on my oven is 260F. Would it work to turn my oven on for little while, then turning it off and placing the yogurt jar inside the somewhat-warm oven? Or should I assume that the pilot flame will be enough?

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Enough dairy already?  Use soy milk (Trader Joe's - just soy and water - less than 2 bucks a quart).  Use a large spoonful of full fat good dairy yogurt as a starter - heat and whisk - oven over night - works well.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
It doesn't even have to be this complicated... I've been making my own yogurt for years.  My current batch is almost 2 years old, and it does not go runny at all. you are doing something wrong if it does.
These are the temps to remember - heat above 185F, cool below 115F to add starter,  Incubate between 80-100F. Especially in winter, I put a warm/hot buddy jar in the middle of 3 quart and blanket them to stay warm. It can be done at 8 hours.  I have accidentally left it 24 hours, still good.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Rather than taking up oven space, just pour the cooled milk with the start mixed in into jars with lids (canning jars are great) and put them in a cooler. Stuff any empty space in the cooler with a towel or jacket, put the lid on and let sit til yogurt has set.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I don't have an oven light, what is the alternative?

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: We started making our own yogurt following my husband's heart attack in our switch to eating as clean as possible about 4 years ago. Our process is even easier with one stovetop pan to cook, cool and culture before straining overnight in a flour towel cloth covered colander and large mixing bowl. Definitely worth doing!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I've been making yogurt for years and have this tip to save some time: to get the milk to cool faster after boiling, place the vessel with hot milk into a pot of icy water, and stir the milk once in a while until you get the temperature down. One tip I haven't followed, however, is using the unheated oven with the light on. I've been using an electric yogurt-maker--a little expensive ("Yogurtmet" brand about $40)--but I've had the same one for 15 years. But that oven trick sounds good.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I use a mix of milk and cream, that's what the husband loves. Make new from what's left of the old batch, it doesn't take much, just what's clinging to the walls of the jar. I keep it warm wrapped in a towel on a heating pad, or on top of the toaster oven set to low. Delicious.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I’ve been doing home made yoghurt now since probably May 2020, and has been my longest-running pandemic habit. To make a much thicker yogurt, I start with a half gallon (8 cups) of milk, and I typically let the culture work in the warm oven overnight. I then set up a large stainless steel bowl, a colander, and cheesecloth, and put the full set mixture into that and let it drain into a more greek-yoghurt consistency (usually between 12-24 hours).

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Too much trouble!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
I make yoghurt by the gallon. The water trick is a real winner.  I test my temperature for when to add the starter with a clean pinky, if you can leave it comfortably to a count of ten, the temperature is good to add the starter.
  I use a soft sided cooler to do my yoghurt. My big pot fits perfectly in side, then I wrap a towel around it, and fill my empty milk jug ( from the gallon of milk I just used) with hot water and place in the cooler and leave it on the counter overnight.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I haven't made yogurt in several years, but I may resume that.  I have a molded plastic warming device that holds 6 8-oz. cups.  My method was always to mix nonfat dry milk powder (like Carnation) but a little more powder than box instructs.  I used Dannon plain yogurt for a starter if I had not made a recent batch to use.  After it was well formed, I stuck it in the fridge in the containers it was made in, which had tight fitting covers.  I usually used the yogurt by mixing in granola.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: What is meant by turning on the oven light?  What temperature should the oven be set to?

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: The oven is OFF. The incandescent light in the oven usually gives off enough heat to keep the yogurt warm enough to culture. So put the yogurt in the cold oven and turn on the light inside the oven. There should be a switch somewhere on the door or top panel. If not, use another method.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Overly complicated - use a wide mouth vacuum flask to keep it warm - no need to mess around with an oven.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I have been making yogurt for several years and using the previous batch as my culture. It’s always delicious. I like thick Greek-style yogurt, so I strain out the whey for a couple hours. I start with a gallon of milk, pour into quart jars and microwave to 180°F to kill bacteria. Then cool to 110°F, add previous yogurt (~1T/quart jar), and keep in a sous vide at 110°F for 10 hours, then cool, strain, and joila!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I use an ice cube instead of water and simply run it over the the bottom of the pan. It stops scorching. I find the 5 setting on my stove works great. (Less than Medium). I use a thermometer which indicates the boiling point and cooling point. I put the lid so that I can see the thermometer which clips to the side of my pan. I also use skim milk and organic skim yogurt. My yogurt is great and everyone loves it. I also drain it to remove most of the whey and make it into Greek yogurt.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I understand the reasons for using whole milk, but for health reasons I usually eat non-fat or low-fat yogurt. Can this process be used starting with non-fat yogurt and using low-fat milk?

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: We were using so many plastic yogurt cups last spring that I started making 1-2 quarts of this about once every 1-1.5 weeks. The first couple of batches were thin. It's important stir starter in really well for consistent thickness throughout. It does thin out over the week but can be strained using a coffee filter to thicken. It's very versatile. I've eaten it not just with fruit and granola, but in pancakes, dressing, dip, and as a topper for shawarma, soups, chana masala, tacos, etc.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: We make 3 quarts of yogurt at a time. To keep the culturing jars warm, we use a deep soup pot, half filled with 115F water. Then we put the capped quart jars of cooled milk (with the yogurt culture stirred in) into the warm water bath, put lid on soup pot to retain warmth, and cover with a few kitchen towels, keeping it cozy like a tea cozy. We let the milk “culture” 6 hours or even overnight. We

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
Anybody know where I can get a glass jar in the photo ,please ? 
Best natural yoghurts in Ireland from small farms are McDonalds and Mosses.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Hi. Those are called Weck Jars. You can purchase them here: https://weckjars.com/. The one pictured is the "Mold" jar. Comes in different sizes. I just bought a bunch of them to buy bulk items at the food co-op near me. Enjoy.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Try putting milk and starter in a large thermos.  Works like a charm.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Where is the ingredient list to find live active cultures?

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: She's referring to the ingredient list on the yogurt container. There should be a statement on the container stating it contains live and active cultures in addition to the named cultures in the list.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
I have a question!
I have a Garland restaurant stove in my kitchen (don't ask cause I certainly don't use it the way it should be!).It does not have a stove light in it. Can I create one with a lightbulb on a cord? Or is there some other  method to be used!
Thank you!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Does the oven have a “proof” setting? That works well if you do. Others have mentioned that the pilot light works well enough. Good luck!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: A much easier way to make yogurt is with the cold start method.  It requires ultra-filtered milk, like Fairlife; whole or 2% works fine.  For one quart, simply add a Tbsp. of live-culture yogurt and stir.  Pour into small jars and leave uncovered or cover loosely.  If you have something like an Instant Pot, set the Yogurt to set for 7-8 hours.  If you don't have one, a 100-110°F oven will do.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I make two quarts at a time (half goat/half cow milk): 1/4c. honey, 1/3 c. yogurt (or a packet of commercial starter culture), 1/4-1/3 c. powdered milk (to give it a thicker texture like "Greek-style" yogurt). I place the jars in a pot with warm water (up to the neck of the jars) and cover the pot with a dishtowel, then wrap in a blanket & place on a shelf in our utility room (where the water heater is, so it's warm year-round) for eight hours. The longer it cures, the more bite to the yogurt.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
When I make yogurt as soon as it's set I taste it then let it sharpen its flavor at room temp until it's as sharp as I want it. Then I freeze enough to start the next batch. That way I don't have to worry about starting the next batch before the starter goes off.
Dave

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I don't have an oven light. It's a gas stove/oven. What would be an alternative? Turn on at the lowest temperature?

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Do you have a recipe using an instant pot?

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: This site has good info https://amindfullmom.com/instant-pot-yogurt/ Put milk in inner liner, hit yogurt button, (boil) when it beeps remove liner, cool milk to 115F. Stir in culture. Return liner to IP, press yogurt button, adjust time. Start w/8hrs, adjust to suit you in later batches (longer=tangier). When done, cool and transfer to jars.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I've got one of those mini-apartment gas stoves, so it doesn't have a light. It does have a warm setting though, what temperature should the oven be at while the yogurt is setting?

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Everyone in my family makes yogurt at home at least once a week (Indians eat yogurt like there's no tomorrow), and the easiest way is zapping it in the microwave and using a few scoops of the old yogurt as culture. No coating of pans, and no need to watch. Do it a couple of hours before bed and wake up to yogurt. I make mine in eight-cup pyrex measuring cups. With some rice and lemon pickle there is nothing better in the whole world.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I make yogurt in the crockpot. It takes longer, but is completely unattended. Just pour milk in, let it heat to 180, take the crock out, let it cool to 115. Add starter. Wrap it in a towel and blanket and let it sit overnight. Yogurt in the morning. Also, a nifty new toy I just bought...a yogurt tube. (https://www.etsy.com/listing/759722723/the-yogurt-tube#). It strains the yogurt to your desired consistency without cheesecloth, etc. Again, a bit more time consuming, we are all at home!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
I make yogurt by the gallon and strain it. I used the NYT larger batch recipe to get going.  
Recipes for the whey would be most appreciated. Right now I have whey cornbread, pancakes and muffins in the freezer. It makes good biscuits too.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Can I use lactaid milk?

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Don't bother. Yogurt is lactose free when it's fully fermented. The bacteria feed on the lactose in the fermentation process

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
My oven light goes on only when the oven itself is on at minimum temperature (i’ve never measured it but it’s high enough to warm plates). 
Is there a threshold temp above which this won’t work?

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: A dehydrator like the Excalibur (square box-type) works phenomenally for yogurt making.  After heating the milk and stirring in the starter, I just put the covered containers in the dehydrator, set it at 115 F, and let it go for anywhere from 6 hours to overnight.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: So, you cannot make yogurt, unless you first have some yogurt? Sorry, I am obviously a real novice here.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: There are many methods. I make it by the gallon with organic milk in a big pot. I cool it to 115 degrees and just stir in a couple of tablespoons of the old batch. Instead of the oven, I use a heating mat made for seed starting. I throw a down coat over the pot and the mat and leave it overnight. Chill for several hours.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I am surprised no one mentioned using an Instapot for yogurt. I started making it this way in May and it is quite simple. Most Instapots have a yogurt setting. It heats the milk to the right temp, then I remove the pot til it cools to 116-118, add 2-3 Tbl from the last batch mixing it with a bit of the milk so it's smooth and will easily mix into the milk. Put the pot back in and then set it for the number of hours I want it to ferment, usually 8 or 9. You are so right, homemade is SO creamy.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: A video would be perfect for this kind of recipe!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I don't remember where I got this recipe: 1/2 gallon milk (I use dairy, skim), put in crockpot and set on Low for 2 1/2 hours.  Unplug.  Remove starter yogurt (commercial at first, then homemade) from refrig, and let it and crockpot sit 3 hours.  Then stir starter into milk, and wrap crockpot in blanket.  Let sit on counter over night (I like 15 hours), then stir, and ladle into containers.  Sometimes, my yogurt carbonates; usable, but when that happens, I buy commercial yogurt to start next one

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I have found it is much quicker to bring the milk to a boil in the microwave. Also if you save the whey that leaks out after spooning out some yogurt you can us that as starter for the next batch.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: The culturing process is very forgiving. Even used a heating pad on Low buffered by a few terry towels once!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I make my own yogurt much the same way, but I use a heating pad one would use for a sore back inside an insulated shopping bag that zips closed. I put the heated milk mixture into a large glass bowl, wrap it in a towel, then let it rest however long it takes inside the insulated bag. Works like a charm. When it's ready, I drop the thickened yogurt into a cheese cloth and hang it over the sink or a bowl to let the whey drip out, occasionally squeezing, turning it into a firm, Greek-style yogurt.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Can you come up with a vegan version of yogurt?

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Can you make make lactose free milk ?

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: No need to. The fermentation process of making yogurt "eats" up all the lactose. Yogurt IS lactose free when it's done

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: What happens to the water st the bottom of the pot!??

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I’m one who has gotten hooked on homemade yogurt. My process is mostly like Melissa’s, except a) I heat the milk in a microwave; b) I use an ice bath, closely monitored, to cool the heated milk; c) I let the heated, inoculated milk ferment in a pre-warmed 2 liter thermos; and d) after 5 hours fermentation, I strain the yogurt using a #6 coffee filter set in a large drip coffee funnel to thicken, as Greek style. The whey is great for the liquid in homemade bread or as a soup base.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: There are several types of starters, the Bulgarian strain is the most used in South Indian yoghurts. IMO, Yogourmet is the closest to it. Even though recommend one packet per use, I save about four teaspoons from each batch as the starter. There are several online stores that sell starters such as: https://shop.culturesforhealth.com/collections/yogurt and: https://bacillusbulgaricus.com/product/yogurt-starter/

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: In cooler or colder climes, the oven method may or may not work. I use the Yoghurt setting in an Instant pot and use a ceramic pot for the yoghurt However the newer models goes to 8 hours while the older ones are 6 hours. At 6 hours its done. Take it out and cool it to room temp for an hour and the refrigerate the pot.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
Question for Marty or anyone:
Where can one reliably order mesophilic starter? We had a many-years-handed-down Bulgarian starter that made lovely yogurt at room temp, but left it at a friend's house when traveling and were unable to recover. Lots of Jerseys live nearby; we just need the starter.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Can I make this if my oven doesn't have a light?

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
Wrap the pot in a fluffy towel and put it in a draft free area near the fridge or something. Works for me and we set the heat  in winter at 66 degrees.
Ps 130 is a bit too warm. Thats when the bacteria starts to die. 120 is plenty. The water thing made no sense and i asked my scientist family who said it does nothing.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: This recipe should be renamed Confusing Yogurt. I can't get past Step One. How do you "coat" a pan with water? What kind of milk are we using? Does ultra pasteurized milk make this step unnecessary? In step 2, it calls for a "teaspoon of the yogurt." "The" yogurt? What yogurt? I thought we were making some. Am I supposed to have store-bought yogurt on hand here? Isn't that against the point of this recipe? I'm lost.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: There are two ingredients listed for this recipe.  One is 4 cups of milk and the other is 1/4 cup of full fat, active culture yogurt.  Think of it like a sour dough starter.  After your yogurt sets and each time you make it after, set aside 1/4 cup in the freezer for the next batch.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Can we use goat milk? (Tip: unlike store-bought goat milk which tastes awful as it's suffered shaking and jiggling during transport, a local whole milk from from pasture-fed goats produced by a trusted farmer tastes delicious, rich and creamy. Perhaps I'll just ask the farmer but if anyone's tried this I'd love to hear. Also: the fat in goat milk is suspended in far smaller globules so, I'm told, the cholesterol is not taken up in digestion.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Yogurt made from raw goat's milk is awesome! I buy a fair amt of raw goat milk from a local farmer for yogurt & cheese. My method is slightly diff: heat to 205 degrees (keep whisking to prevent boil over) then turn heat off and put pot in sink filled with cool water until temp drops to 110 F. Whisk in the 1/4 cup yogurt from last batch (or storebought with live cultures) and hold at 110 for one hour, then hold at 90-100 degrees for as long as it takes. I use a bread proofing box to hold temp.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Did not work for me but I’m going to try again with fresher Siggi yogurt.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: It's so much easier to make in a thermos bottle. Put the warm milk and yoghurt in it, lay it down on its side and let it sit out overnight, rolling the thermos a couple times. Just make sure you use yoghurt with real culture; some cheap yoghurts add thickeners to milk to fake the texture from fermentation.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I use a slightly different process than described.  First, put ice on the bottom of the heavy pan, which will stick; its release will let you know it is ready to use then discard the ice or keep for a cooling bath.  Second, I use a gallon of fat free milk, with 1 cup dried fat free milk mixed with a cup of milk, added after milk heated to 185-190, for 20-30 minutes. Third, cool mixture to 110-115, then mix in starter.  Wrap container in towels, for 2-3 hours. Then overnight in the fridge. Strain

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I made yogurt all the time when I was in Peace Corps. I didn't have an oven, so I wrapped my sleeping bag around the pot instead.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
As per a tip on the internet, I bring my cooler inside and put the filled yoghurt containers in the cooler.
I use old sterilized yoghurt quarts and lids. I leave space for a quart sized metal bowl.  Fill the metal bowl with boiling water and cover it with a lid. Shut the cooler, the hot water will keep the yoghurt warm.  After 8 hours I put the semi-set yoghurt in the fridge.  The yoghurt firms up as it cools down.
Works every time.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
If you use an ultrafiltered milk, like skim plus or Fairlife, you'll get a thicker yogurt, closer to Greek in consistency, but not as tangy, because it has more milk solids.  
I use a cheap yogurt maker, less than $30 I think, with individual jars for convenience.   And I just heat a quart of milk in the microwave for 2.5 
minutes.  Much simpler.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I use a yogurt maker, which I thought was silly because yogurt is so easy to make without one. But it's like a rice cooker, makes your life easier. I have never found rubbing with an ice cube, using water or the lid of a tin can stopped scorching. What does work is a double boiler, I found a cheap one online T-fal or JKsmart. Both have a hole in the lid so you can use a thermometer without taking the lid off.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Is it possible to use milks that have reduced cats?

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I hope not. Meow.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Have made great yogurt for the past year using the Joy of Cooking instructions. a clip-on thermometer takes out the guesswork. Heat 2 litres of whole milk to 180*. Run a couple of inches of cold water in your sink and place the hot pot with milk into it, letting it cool to 110*. I mix my organic starter into the milk with a whisk and distribute in in 5 glass jars. These go into a not too large picnic cooler into which I have placed a cup of boiling water to keep it warm. 6-8 hours later it's don

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Is it possible to make a fat free version?

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
I have followed the directions, but used almond coconut milk instead of dairy milk. 10 hrs later and it hasn’t set to a gel. Looked suitably thick after whisking; still about the same consistency. The oven is warm from the light. 
Start over?

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: My first batch took 7 hours in an oven to set (Winter in NY). The consistency is a bit looser than what I prefer, but it is silky.  I used Ronnybrook Whole Milk and Chobani raspberry yogurt with active cultures (that's all I had at home). Will try Siggi as suggested. Will also try to cook milk a little longer for thicker results. Very easy!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I waited 5 ½ hours and still not set. Hoping overnight in the fridge will do it????  I followed the direction!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: My electric oven has a proof feature. Is that warm enough since no light bulb or pilot light.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Yes! I used my proof setting on the oven for this Sunday and it worked perfectly! It set nicely and took about 3 hours.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Will the proof mode work for the yogurt?

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I have the same question about the water,  is the water diluting the milk?

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I use Gefen Unflavored Jello Powder to add to vegan soy and coconut yogurt before fermentation to get a thicker product, 1 TBS per quart.  Heat the powder to 182 F in 1/2 cup of the liquid before adding.  I think this would work to thicken milk yogurt in lieu of straining to make greek yogurt. I make the coconut yogurt with probiotic capsules rather than yogurt from a previous batch.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
1. Heating the milk to scalding/bubbling over(almost) is kyt. Can be done in microwave, trailing time based on volume of milk. Cool till luke warm(body temperature). 2. Use lyophilized yogurt starter/culture (Yogourmet brand). This culture will live forever. Store bought yogurt culture will die or become bad eventually. 
3.Use the small cooler as a warm spot. I place a bowl of 110F water bowl over or under milk. This setup gets  yogurt ready in 3.5-4hrs. Easy task to accomplish during the day.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: We started making yogurt when we bought our Miele steamer combo oven.  It has a setting on it for yogurt.  That was 4 years ago and we have been using the same starter since then.  We do use a combination of whole milk and heavy whipping cream for a rich yogurt, so I'm not sure if that makes a difference in the longevity of the starter.  But even when our oven went out for 2 months, our starter was still good and made our next batch of yogurt.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Insta-Pots have a yogurt setting, and I make it by the gallon, which is very economical.  It lasts refrigerated for 3 weeks (or longer?  ours never lasts longer).  1 gallon milk that is NOT ultra-pasturized, heated to 180 on the stove top, then cooled to 115, then add 1/4 c. yogurt starter (Fage is good), then pour into Insta-Pot and set to Yogurt.  8-9 hours later you have 4 quarts of delicious yogurt.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I’ve been making yogurt for years. (1) best milk is whole, non-homogenized, it makes all the difference, (2) Heat oven to 110 degrees and then simply shut it off before putting in the milk-yogurt moxture, this provides a wonderful incubator for the batch. Cheaper and tastier than anything you can buy—enjoy!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
How do you “evenly coat a pan with water?” This doesn’t make sense. 
Unless it’s to make a Bain Marie?

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Although we've made the yogurt successfully in our garage sale instant pot, it's not as good as the yogurt I used to get (and consume) back in the 70s in France. I was excited to see Oui "French" yogurt and wanted to use that as a starter but found that you cannot purchase plain Oui yogurt, only flavored. Does anyone know where you can get real French yogurt starter?

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: i often use flavored yogurt for the starter.  the amount is small enough that the flavor is not noticeable, particularly if the flavored yogurt has not been mixed.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: i make a pint at a time (all i need).  i scald the milk, let the temp come down to 110f, add a heaping tsp of previous yogurt and wrap the jar with a heating pad set to low.  perfectly thickened the next day.  occasionally i refresh the culture with my favorite from the store, but i am not sure this is necessary.  i did discover that there is not enough bacteria if i let my previous jar reduce to too much whey.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Has anyone use goat milk in their yogurt?

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Investing in a yogurt maker is so worth it.  Just get the milk to the right temp., stir in starter, and let it go for 8 hours or so.  I use 1% milk and add 1/4C fat free milk powder for 6 cups, and heirloom starter that only gets better.  Thick, delicious and light.  You can use FF milk, too.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
With Instant Pot, making your own yogurt is extremely easy:

1. 1/2 gal of whole milk in the IP & cycle through Yogurt button to Boil.

2. Done? Cool milk to 107 degrees F & add 5-7 oz of the best yogurt to a large bowl. I like plain whole milk organic yogurt.

3. Add a few ladles of cooled milk to the yogurt bowl and whisk at least 40 sec. Then pour into IP container and whisk another 40 sec or so.

4. Set Yogurt control to 11 hours.

5. Done? Put in container(s) and refrigerate

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Has anyone tried making yogurt using chocolate milk? Would it be the same ratio of 1/4 cup yogurt to 1 litre of chocolate milk?  Same method?

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Why smear the pan with a teaspoon of yogurt?

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
I made this yesterday, cutting the recipe in half. I love yogurt but wasn't sure I could consume it all before it got furry. 

I left it in the oven for 5 hours to thicken. After that time it was still a little too soupy for my tastes. So I lined a sieve with dampened cheesecloth and let it firm up as the whey drained. I ended up with about a cup of yogurt, which was delicious with fresh berries. I'll make it again. Next time, I'll make the full recipe. Very rich and creamy.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Can you use full-fat kefir in place of yogurt for the starter?

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I’ve been making yogurt for years once a week using a gallon of milk & 2T of last week’s yogurt. I have learned slower heating is better. I heat to 185 F, then hold it there for a few minutes, and I let it cool to 110 before I mix in the starter. When I have mixed the starter into warmer milk (even 115), I have had bad results with yogurt that does not set well. Different starters probably cause different results, but my cultures would be killed if I stirred them into a pot of 130 degree milk.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
Simple recipe:
- Amount of whole milk that we want (1 Lt., 2 Lt., etc.)
- 1 glass (125 gr) of natural yogurt
-Heat half of the milk until it starts to boil. -Mix with the other half of the milk that is at room temperature, add the yogurt ad mix. -Cover and wrap with a thick blanket. -Let it rest in a place where no one will move it for 24 hours. (You can say nice things like "Rest and become a delicious yogurt", it works!)
- - After 24 hours it will be ready to eat. Keep in the fridge.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: There are many yogurt recipes in NYT Cooking. I'm surprised to see nothing about a different milk culture call Fil Mjolk, from Sweden. It's a bit lighter and tangier, very delicious, and requires no heat. You use a bit from your last batch, add milk, and let it sit at room temperature for 10 hours or more. No fossil fuels used, no tricky temp. monitoring, no equipment needed at all. Very easy! I ordered my starter from a culture place in OR 5 years ago. It's been easy and delicious ever since!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: can the editors provide a temperature to heat the milk to in the first step? "Add the milk and heat until it just comes to a boil, watching closely: As soon as you start to see bubbles forming, take the pot off the heat." this seems to contradict itself so i'm unsure...should the milk actually boil, or should it just barely come to a simmer (i.e. when you see [tiny] bubbles forming) before you turn it off? again, a temp would alleviate the confusion here. thanks!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I make yogurt every week, a week's worth of supply. You can microwave the milk till it is almost ready to bubble over, that fully scalded. If you stick to a routine and know how much yogurt you want to make and how often, then you will know the exact time you need to microwave in your machine. Once its cooled to lukewarm or body temperature, add the culture. I recommend Yogourmet starter. This will last forever, unlike store bought yogurt. You need a warm place, be it oven, instant pot or

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Part#2 of the comment. You can use styrofoam box saved from landfill as a incubator for yogurt. I leave a bowl of warm water, on which I leave yogurt to culture. How long to wait depends on your schedule, amount of culture you add. You need a Tsp or less of culture for pint of yogurt, will take 6-8 hrs, depending on the temp, tangyness you desire. I make 1.5L of yogurt, use 1/4c of yogurt as culture and set in a styrofoam box for 3-4 hrs. this allows me to be done with it during the daytime.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
I grew up in the 50s on Mom’s Turkish-Armenian yogurt, and now I make my own using a culture from Bulgaria for about 7 years now.  

Slowly heat the milk (very low heat) to between 160-180, stir regularly, keep it there for a while to evaporate and be thicker in the end.  Let it cool to 112 or so and pour milk into container with starter, stir and put it in a cooler with water at 112 or just put towels around the jar to insulate.  Six to eight hours.  USE NON HOMOGENIZED WHOLE MILK!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Instapots are great for making yogurt.  I like nondairy, made of cashews: Boil raw cashews in water for 10 minutes or so (which kills bacteria). Let cool.  Drain cashews.  Mix with non-chlorinated water to desired texture in a high-quality blender.  Add yogurt culture or a tablespoon or so of good yogurt.  Put in stainless steel Instapot container, seal, and set to yogurt setting.  I like tangy yogurt, so I ferment for 14 or 15 hours.  Add a bit of salt to make a very good nondairy cream cheese.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Proof setting in the oven works well since I’ve made this three times in three weeks. Left it for about 8 hours, chilled it and then thickened it by draining in cheesecloth. I’ve never used that water?

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I use the whey for cooking oatmeal. I also put it on my dog's food, if I have a lot. He's crazy for it. Use it anywhere you'd use buttermilk or other tart liquid. Polenta, a pasta sauce, pancakes, etc. (Even dishes that don't start with P.)

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I use ultrapasteurized 2% organic milk and an electric yogurt maker with the little cups. I add a teaspoon of powdered milk to each cup for thickness and a probiotic tablet to each. Sets overnight. Won’t set if the probiotic isn’t good.  The second batch goes well with a tsp fresh yogurt in each cup, and so on.  Easy.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
Sorry, left out a few words word.  It should read:
 I semi-sterilize pint jars half-filled with water in the microwave, then discard the water.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I perfected my yogurt by using a single strain, bifidus infantis powder, I bought at a health food store. Single strain does not compete with all the others—it has been my same starter for over 6 years now. I don't heat the milk [I use ultra pasteurized half and half] and a TBS of leftover culture]. It goes into a yogurt maker for 11 hours and out comes a triple thick yogurt you can barely shake off a spoon! The secret is to find one strain and use ultra pasteurized milk.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Why did making yogurt stop working about a year ago?  I've made yogurt for over 50 years but when I lost my starter last summer because I had surgery, nothing I did worked anymore.  Dannon plain yogurt was my go to starter.  Ultrapasteurized organic milk was my milk of choice.  Neither worked.  I've tried other products without success.  I always use milk from antibiotic free cows.  Did emergency COVID relief allow dairies to make changes?

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
I make yogurt from soy milk made from just 2 ingredients, water and soybeans, and a vegan culture. Sometimes I add a bit of maple syrup, for the flavor.

Because the soy milk has already been cooked during it’s production, I just add the culture to the milk, stir, and incubate for about 12 hours at about 110 F. My Instant Pot has a yogurt setting which works very well.

The result is a thick creamy and tasty vegan yogurt.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I have been making my own yogurt for three years now, starting from a single cup of Ellenos yogurt, a local Greek yogurt. I have found that boiling the milk (and, as others say, any milk will do, including lactose free, but lactose free isn’t necessary if you let it grow for twelve hours… there won’t be any lactose left) until it caramelizes slightly adds a wonderful dulce de leche flavor to the yogurt. I cool to 110 F and put it in a barely warm (110F) oven for twelve to 15 hours.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Can one make keifer the same way, but using keifer as the starter, of course?

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Kefir is made with grains that regenerate.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I've made yogurt using several recipes, some rather complicated, got tired of the myriad steps.  This recipe is simple, few steps and worked great. I did watch the heating and took it off the stove immediately I saw the small bubbles.  Also used a quart jar with a lid, got less whey that way (sorry). My oven light will not stay on; used a pot filled with warm water and covered with a towel. Thanks, I'm now back to making my own!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: If you prefer a thicker Greek style yogurt, after the chilling/setting step & before stirring or whisking, line a sieve with a double layer of cheesecloth, set the sieve atop a bowl (be sure there is a few inches between the bottom of the sieve & the bottom of the bowl). Dump the set yogurt in & let the whey drain out for anywhere from 1-6 hours depending on how thick you want your yogurt. If it gets thicker than you want, just put some of the whey back in a little at a time. Whey has many uses.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: If you heat the milk on top of the stove, it can easily boil over.  Heat it in the microwave instead.  It will NEVER boil over.  Also, use a towel-lined insulated cooler bag instead of the oven.  Needs four or more hours, but saves power and leaves your oven free.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Here is my simplified version: Use 2 qts 2% milk and 8T plain yogurt (nonfat Chobani preferred). Microwave milk in 2-qt corningware casserole dish till a skin develops (~18 min). Cool to 120F (~40 min). Heat oven to 140F, turn off, and turn on oven light. Meanwhile, in a small bowl stir 6T of warmed milk into the yogurt till smooth, then stir into the rest of the milk. Cover the dish, place in oven for 8 hours. Tastes best after being refrigerated overnight. Microwaving = no burned pot!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I hate using microwave since they all give different results and usually overheat.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: The recipe isn't very clear! How do you "smear 1 tablespoon of yogurt in the bottom of a pan" before you've made the yogurt! Ridiculous

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: it says you can just leave a dollop in the middle of the pan. Not really a big deal and won't make much difference.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: That is the yogurt you're using as the starter. So take that from the 1/4 cup that you're mixing into the milk with.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I wish I had read the notes first, if I had nickel for everytime I've thought that!  After following the recipe I let it sit in the oven for 2 hrs then checked it, 45 mins more then 1 hour more. I wish I had let it sit longer. Next time I'll leave it in overnight. Thanks.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I've now been on my yogurt making journey for a few months... I started here, after watching Priya's video with Brad Leone. This is not a bad recipe, but there are two major adjustments that I have made: First, 130 degrees is OK for adding the starter culture, but is generally considered a bit hot. 110-120 is safer. Second, and MOST IMPORTANT, 1/4 cup of starter culture per quart is FAR TOO MUCH. Much better results are achieved with less - I use 1 teaspoon per quart, and it's perfect.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I bought a heirloom starter from cultures for health 15 years ago and make a new batch every week or two.  After heating and cooling milk, I mix in starter, put mixture into quart or half gallon mason jars and then put the jars in a water bath (I use an old cooler for the water bath) to keep temperature constant--I add hot water if the bath cools too much.  Works great.  Funny thing is that my yogurt seems to like one particular brand of milk and if I use a different brand it turns out runny.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Can I use raw milk?  Have a local grocer who stocks raw milk from local sources.  Would that eliminate the need to heat the milk?

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Excellent!!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: What does "oven light" mean? Is it the light to see inside? What setting of an electric oven is it?

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Thank you for your recipe, I made the yogurt before but I found out that the results depend on the milk I use. Is there a difference between ultra-pasteurized milk (tetra pack containers) and pasteurized (plastic containers). In my state of Washington raw milk is also available. Would heating it up to a boiling point make safe for consuming and tastier than pasteurized milk?

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: What if I don't have a light in my oven?

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: What if I accidentally leave the yogurt overnight in the oven? Will it spoil?

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
Thank you thank you for this recipe. I had no idea it could be so easy. Not only is it delicious and perfect in every way I am no longer throwing away all that plastic from buying yogurt.

Three tips: you don’t have to use whole milk. I use 2 percent. 

Start with siggis plain. It gives a much firmer result.

I let mine go overnight in the oven. It’s perfect to wake up to fresh yogurt in the morning. Thank you!

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Callaloo (Leafy Greens With Tomato and Onion) Recipe
commentBody:
I frequently eat and cook callalloo in Jamaica. Several things to note:

1. It almost always has stalks of fresh sage in it, a very common and cheap herb in Jamaica
2. I often find it has garlic and a little Scocth Bonnet (Jabenero) pepper cooked with it as well. Personally I rarely see it with salt Codfish
3. Technically it is the Amarynth plant. Easily grown and harvested before it goes to seed. Heirloom catalogues sell the seeds.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Callaloo (Leafy Greens With Tomato and Onion) Recipe
commentBody: Callaloo, or a variety of it, actually grows wild at least in NYS.  It covers my garden every summer and gives me lots of greens which can be frozen for winter use. This recipe is very much like what I cook. Often add bone broth for extra flavor. Don't do bacalao even tho it is available. The green is betw kale and chard in tenderness.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Callaloo (Leafy Greens With Tomato and Onion) Recipe
commentBody: Until yesterday I di not knwo that Amaranth which is a weed in my El Paso garden was Callaloo.  I am so happy to have new ways to cook this.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Callaloo (Leafy Greens With Tomato and Onion) Recipe
commentBody: Thyme is the herb to add when preparing callaloo and which is bound to bundles at the market.  Add a tomato, onion and Scotch Bonnet with salt & pepper & some oil in a dutch oven with chopped callaloo to simmer down until tender.  If you buy fresh bundles, peel the bitter outside layer off larger stalks before chopping.  Easier to buy bags of chopped callaloo at the market and your thyme separately.  Nah on boney saltfish.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Callaloo (Leafy Greens With Tomato and Onion) Recipe
commentBody:
This leafy green is grown extensively in Greece and you can find in the markets both the wild and cultivated kind .Usually it is prepared  boiled with an olive oil and lemon dressing or cooked with zucchini and tomatoes and served with crumbled feta on top.In the island of Crete is also cooked with
snails and tomatoes.Here in the state of Maryland where I live I found it 
as a weed in a commercial  farm where is used to go to pick tomatoes in
the summer.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Callaloo (Leafy Greens With Tomato and Onion) Recipe
commentBody: Callaloo I found out is Amaranth. In San Diego where i live it is naturalizing in my yard for the last 2 years. Sturdy and most productive. Thrilling to me since now I can have something that I first tasted in Jamacia over 15 years ago. Now I just need an ackee tree so I can have ackee and saltfish!

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Callaloo (Leafy Greens With Tomato and Onion) Recipe
commentBody: Bacalao was waaay too salty. Wish I’d followed directions on packet to soak overnight and change the water multiple times.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Callaloo (Leafy Greens With Tomato and Onion) Recipe
commentBody: The Kallaloo I grew up making and eating in the Virgin Islands has hocks or pig tale, salt fish, and crab, okra, onion, sweet pepper, scotch bonnet, and greens. Never tomato. And always served with a ball of fungi.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Callaloo (Leafy Greens With Tomato and Onion) Recipe
commentBody: I used rainbow chard because I was unable to find callaloo. This was so easy and delicious. Everyone at my Caribbean themed dinner loved this dish.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Goat is the most widely eaten meat in the world and is truly delicious; for this recipe I urge you to find a butcher who carries it -- halal butchers usually do. The other wonderful thing about goat is that it has fewer calories than any other meat including chicken. In addition, virtually all goats in this country are raised on small farms and thus are not subjected to the horrors of industrialized food production.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: In Fairbanks you can, from time-to-time, purchase locally raised goat at the store that is run by Mid-State Meat.  If they don't have it when you go in, they will call you when they have it.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: No, it’s not. Pork is the most widely eaten meat in the world. Goat is mainly eaten in parts of Africa, the Middle East and a few other parts of Asia.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Can this be made with chicken?  Any suggested adjustments?

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I second this as I have a partner who doesn’t eat red meat.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Don't see why not.  You may have to adjust cooking times downward, perhaps a good bit, if you use chicken pieces.  I'd probably use skinless, boneless thighs.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I make curries a lot and you can substitute with with chicken.  It does not need to cook as long though.  20 or 30 minutes should be enough., but It depends on whether you use white or dark meat.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I served this with rice and peas and steamed cabbage, and it definitely brought be back to the Caribbean. I used bone-in goat, and it wasn't quite as tender as I'd like after two hours, but the potatoes were close to disintegrating. Next time, I'll cook the goat for 2.5 hours, and add the potatoes at the one hour mark.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I put a good healthy slug of rum in it...All of my BWI family does the same.  Now, if someone had a good recipe for heavy cake which is a Cayman specialty but comes from Jamaica....they were all one country at one time.....sure would love to have that.  The recipe died with the last of the Aunties...

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Found this, which looks pretty authentic. Let us know how it compares to your Aunties! http://the-cooking-of-joy.blogspot.com/2009/02/cassava-heavy-cake.html?m=1

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Is this a candidate for an InstaPot?  Sounds like it could be...and if so, how long should it be cooked?

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I’d love to know this, too. I’ve learned, the empirical way, that not all slow-cooked meats work well in a pressure cooker! Or...if they can, I’ve not figured out how to perfectly adapt the cooking time and technique.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: This is a great recipe. I have made this several times in a countertop pressure cooker and found that pressure cooker times GENERALLY are 1/3 that of a cook top. This will obviously vary according to your cooker and the cut/type of meat. In this recipe I would suggest 40 minutes in your pressure cooker to start, release the pressure and check for tenderness. If it needs a few more minutes then I would add the potatoes so that they are not over cooked. Hope this helps. Enjoy!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: If you are looking for a substitute because goat is unavailable in your area, I suggest lamb. The taste is not the same, but much closer to goat than chicken, and better able to stand up to the spices. Cooking time is probably shorter, but not that much.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
Trinidad Homestyle Version: No onion/garlic/Lawry powder or potato!

Don’t just put the meat in a pot: chonkh or bloom the spices—mix in water to make a slurry and sauté on medium heat in 1/4 cup ghee or oil, stirring to avoid burning. Add 1/2 cup each of diced onion and fresh tomato, 1/4 cup crushed garlic and ginger, high heat stirring for 3-5 mins, then add seasoned meat, incorporating well. Add water, cover, cook on low. Finish w/cilantro or add crushed culantro at start.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: 6 hours slow cooker.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
These spices might overwhelm the chicken. I’d try boned, skinless thighs and maybe 1/2 to even 1/4 of the spices. And only cook until meat is tender.

But i’m thinking I want to try this with lamb, and with a milder pepper - maybe jalapeño.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Any protein would work with this recipe. Try goat- you won't be disappointed. It is a wonderfully lean, healthy protein that Americans should embrace.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: It's an animal. Calling it a "protein" is an attempt at whitewashing.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: @Debra M The goat meat I bought is riddled with fat, which makes it a delicious source of protein.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I think that some ingredients are suspiciously missing from this recipe.  Onions? Coconut milk? Ginger? Tomatoes? I added all these plus some carrots and plantains.  Very tasty and I'm making it again for the third time.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I have trouble imagining Jamaican curry powder without ground ginger (powder).

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Using boneless goat is a mistake. It needs to be boned pieces because much of the flavour comes from the bone stock. I've cooked this dish hundreds of times, and tried cutting corners by using boneless pieces and the difference is noticeable. You can always debone after cooking, but please, please cook this the traditional way.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
Bought my boneless goat leg at the local weekend farmers market. Starting late on Saturday afternoon we couldn't find Lawry's (sub Adobo seasoning) or fenugreek (sub mustard seeds).
It is the best curry goat we've ever had. Flavors are deep and complex without being overpowering. Removed the goat after 1 1/2 hrs, it was tender, didn't want to toughen it up. Continued cooking down the spuds for 20 mins until the sauce was perfect. Fried ripe plantains will add the needed sweetness. Thank you!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: The local markets that cater to my Hispanic and other ethnic neighbors carry Lawry’s Seasoned Salt in larger containers.  Inevitably on display near the meat/fish counters.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: was delicious. I used sambar curry mix as I had it on hand. I got the goat from a local farm and the goat was really amazingly delicious and tender.  I marinated the day before.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Would make again.  Made with 2 Lbs Australian Bone-In pre-cut 1-2” chunks. Made 3-4 servings. Personal notes: Used Jamaican Style curry powder.  Marinated 24 hours.  Seared goat before adding onion mix to pot. Added one cut potato at start after 90 min. Mashed into sauce.  Added second potato for another 30 min.  Served in bowls without rice.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Substituted homemade chicken stock and a can of coconut milk for water (3C total).  For additional acidity added 2T powdered lime, 2T vinegar, and 1T tomato paste.  Substituted jalapeño and banana pepper for scotch bonnet — plenty hot. Also added 2T minced ginger, 2C cubed pumpkin, 1 chopped carrot and 1 chopped celery.  Cooked in a well-soaked, covered clay pot in 350 oven for 90 minutes and then at 300 for 2 hours.  Wow.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Cooked in soaked clay pot 90 min at 359 and 2 hours at 300.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: This might be a silly question, but does onion and pepper get discarded prior to cooking or do they join the goat?

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
I figured it out!  They don’t.  

This is a beautiful recipe, btw.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Fabulous and a perfect amount of heat for us. Added cubed Kabocha squash with the potatoes and chopped cilantro on top. A keeper.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: This was so good. We got some goat meat from a farmer in the family and loved this. I used my pressure cooker for 30 minutes. Couldn't find fenugreek and used some cayenne instead of the scotch bonnet out of necessity - was great but I'll keep my eye out for it for next time.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I don't understand why this recipe has you make twice as much spice mixture as you need... Why not just make half the quantity? Spices lose flavor quickly after toasting and grinding. Better to use fresh. I ended up just using all of it and, ironically, I still found this to be a little anemic in the flavor department. It needed a lot of salt, some fish sauce, lime juice, and hot sauce. Not bad, but a little disappointing.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I wanted to come back and say that the flavor improves a lot by day two and three! Leftovers were delicious. Next time I will make it ahead of time. And I'll still use all the spice mixture, haha.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Yeah, i know, i know - if I didn't cook it exactly as written  . . .  Just want to share with you: worked beautifully with pork tenderloin - I was fresh out of goat!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I have made this from a similar recipe. Differences are that the other uses a spring-form pan, standard US sweet potatoes baked, and the raisins are soaked in rum and mixed into the batter, the top is sprinkled with a mix of coconut, brown sugar and cinnamon. It is a fabulous holiday desert somewhere between a cheesecake and sweet potato pie.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Can you please share this recipe? Sounds fabulous!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I found this recipe on the Epicurious website: https://www.epicurious.com/recipes/member/views/sweet-potato-pudding-cake-50107847

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Thank you to Melva L for finding that recipe. It is fantastic!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Are the yams cooked before going on the blender? It seems like they are raw in the recipe.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: This question was driving me crazy too! I looked up some other sweet potato pudding recipes, and those called for raw grated sweet potatoes, so that's what I'm going to use for this recipe instead of a food processor. My blender certainly in not powerful enough for a sweet potato. My food processor is industrial strength, put I'd rather just have to wash the grater, plus I think the grated potatoes will lend themselves to a nice texture for this dish.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Do you cook the potatoes before blending?

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Such a great and easy recipe—I cut the sugar by 100 grams and subbed a mix of monk fruit and palm sugar for about 2/3 of the sweetener. I also baked the potatoes—I did not add any water and just hand mixed. It’s nothing like the sweet potato pudding you get roadside in Jamaica but it’s delicious. Will make again!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I am Jamaican and this is a very different.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
Can this be done in a food processor instead of a blender?

Do you think dried cranberries would be good instead of raisins?

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Why not! If you can purée it, it’s the same to me. I use the blender for things that are more difficult to crush etc.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: this is a weird different recipe

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Any suggestions for a coconut milk sub? Also for the coconut topping suggestion by another commenter? One child dislikes coconut very much.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Coconut milk used this way doesn't taste much like coconut but you might substitute half and half or almond baking milk.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Should you puree the raisins or dried cranberries?

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Made this as written (with weighed ingredients) and it never solidified, despite additional cooking -- ended up gelatinous and slightly mealy due to difficulty processing raw sweet potatoes (even in a Vitamix!) It was still tasty but not what I was expecting, and guests were a bit put off by the visual. I did use exclusively white sweet potatoes, described here as "starchy", but they were not.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: same thing happened to me! Still tasted good but a let down.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Made this last night for my gluten-free friend and family. I had overlooked the fact that it had a cup of flour, so at the last minute, I added the same amount of coconut flour.  It worked great!  After reading the notes from others, I was worried about using the blender for the chunks of sweet potato, so I grated the sweet potatoes first, then used the blender.  The consistency and “setting up” was perfect.  Served it with whipped cream.  The freshly grated nutmeg was beautifully aromatic.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: This was a super sweet goopy mess. I might try it again with a little more flour and less sugar.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: We had a Caribbean-themed meal and this was the perfect dessert.  Reading the other notes, I was nervous about the texture.  I followed others' advice and I finely shredded the yams and sweet potatoes, then ran it through the food processor with the water and coconut milk.  The texture was a little gross but it baked up beautifully.  I think next time I would halve the raisins.  Otherwise, great pudding.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: sooo good

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Turkey Silog (Garlic Fried Rice With Eggs) Recipe
commentBody: I love soft-cooked eggs over rice, but I think this recipe is boring.  There is definitely not too much garlic -- maybe not enough!  And, if I make it again, I will try adding a vegetable or two -- perhaps a handful of corn or peas?  And a tiny bit of soy sauce or some mirin?  And I'll definitely fry the rice longer to get a crispy texture -- at least 4 minutes, depending on the strength of my heat source.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Turkey Silog (Garlic Fried Rice With Eggs) Recipe
commentBody: Fair enough, but if you add corn or peas I feel like you shouldn’t call it silog.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Turkey Silog (Garlic Fried Rice With Eggs) Recipe
commentBody: We really liked this except that our garlic got a bit burnt after turning up the heat and adding the rice (don't see how that's avoidable, though, with the current instructions unless you remove the smashed pieces before the rice). We added a few drizzles of soy sauce on top and some leftover crispy onions from the green bean casserole that never got used. Great use of leftovers.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Turkey Silog (Garlic Fried Rice With Eggs) Recipe
commentBody: Following Sandra and Sharon's leads: doubled garlic, removed browned garlic from the pan to avoid burning it. Instead, I minced two-thirds of it to incorporate into the crisped rice and put mirin and soy sauce on the table for those who wanted it.  I used two pans, moving the rice to a low burner. After searing meat, I placed it on a small plate atop the rice, then covered that pan loosely with foil to keep it warm. It was easier to deal with the assembly while the eggs cooked in 2nd pan.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Turkey Silog (Garlic Fried Rice With Eggs) Recipe
commentBody: Leftover turkey is best with a teaspoon of fish sauce (not soy sauce) to bring out the flavor.  It sounds weird but try it.  Fry the turkey until it gets some color.  Omit the salt.  At this point the gravy is optional.  Then proceed as directed.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Turkey Silog (Garlic Fried Rice With Eggs) Recipe
commentBody: This was really tasty :) I’ve made it three times already, playing off leftovers.  Once, combined leftover Mac and cheese with the rice on the stove.  Used both turkey and ham as the meat of choice.  Topped with chili garlic oil crunch once instead of turkey.  A lot of ways to play off the base recipe, but if you follow as written, you’ll get good results and a fun way to use up your Thanksgiving leftovers

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Turkey Silog (Garlic Fried Rice With Eggs) Recipe
commentBody: I like to add chopped bacon to my fried rice.  The way my mom would make it is to fry the rice in soy sauce and the bacon grease, add chopped green onions, mix well, then poke holes for the eggs.  We just add an egg in each hole, or I suppose you could scramble and add some of the egg in each hole.  Cover, and cook until eggs are done.  I think I will try adding the garlic cloves to my recipe, and see how it turns out.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Turkey Silog (Garlic Fried Rice With Eggs) Recipe
commentBody: Thanks for this recipe. About 25 years ago, I worked in the Philippines for a while, and looked forward to garlic rice morning, noon and night. When I asked for the recipe, tho, all I got was eye rolls. It was as if they were thinking, “do you need a recipe for a PB&J sandwich?” No one ever said that of course; too polite. I experimented when I returned home, and then abandoned the quest. This is very close. I wonder about the oil. Perhaps they used palm oil? Something else?

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Turkey Silog (Garlic Fried Rice With Eggs) Recipe
commentBody: This was very tasty. I agree it is better with day-old rice vs. fresh rice. (I tried both, haha)! I tried it with ground turkey, next time i will try bacon. Also, I was shy with the garlic, but will likely add more next time.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Turkey Silog (Garlic Fried Rice With Eggs) Recipe
commentBody: Thank you for teaching how to make fried rice real garlicky!Lol

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Turkey Silog (Garlic Fried Rice With Eggs) Recipe
commentBody:
The garlic got burnt a little - but my son discovered he likes slightly more brown garlic .In the second phase of rice, I added shallots, eggs, and soy sauce.

Overall excellent receipe.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Turkey Silog (Garlic Fried Rice With Eggs) Recipe
commentBody: Haven’t tried this yet, but will be including this after Thanksgiving. Sounds like a kind of Hawaiian loco moco!

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Turkey Silog (Garlic Fried Rice With Eggs) Recipe
commentBody: Filipino food always has acid - in the form of raw onion, pickled veg (achara) or vinegar. Try making a quick salad of tomato, onion and a bit of cane vinegar and sliced chilies to go with this. Your breath will smell but I promise it's good.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Turkey Silog (Garlic Fried Rice With Eggs) Recipe
commentBody: Adapted this for one, using three cloves of garlic (which I found got very brown very quickly, even on low heat) and leftover Indian restaurant basmati rice. Used a coconut-canola oil combo. Only needed the one phase of rice cooking. Then used two slices of deli turkey seared in the pan. Cooked the egg over-easy, seasoned with paprika, salt, pepper, and a dash of turmeric. Easy and delicious!

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Turkey Silog (Garlic Fried Rice With Eggs) Recipe
commentBody: Made this after dreading the usual Tday leftovers  for dinner. I didn’t have any gravy, but it was fine without and deliciously comforting after a long holiday weekend. My Filipino husband enjoyed it!

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: This didn't result in authentic asado offered in good restaurants back home in the Philippines. For starters, the sauce came out watery and thin. The calamansi was overpowering, so I'd reduce it to 1/2 cup or less. Quezo de bola is used in kaldereta, not asado, so omit. Frankly, I doubt if I'll do this again.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: There is no substitute for calamansi. Lemon or lime is just as good. Sometimes I use both together.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Add some potato, onions, and (for me) olives, and you have a delicious Mechado recipe too.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I cooked this last night. since the recipe says wait for 24 hours. i will wait :)

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: the Asado is to die for. I added fish sauce instead of salt at the end. This is the more traditional Filipino way and it's so good.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: This is delicious!  I made a small portion of it last week just to test it to see if I liked it.  I'm going to make a large portion tonight.  It is a great twist on what I call a pork stew.  It has such interesting flavors.  I loved it.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: This is a KEEPER.  Almost better than Bittman's Pernil.  Almost.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
I used about a tablespoon of fish sauce too.  Nice salty touch.  I used a pressure cooker for this and cut the pork in large chunks. About 25 minutes at high pressure. Meat was melting tender. Used 
Mozzarella cheese instead of Edam.  Had to stir a bit to be sure it totally melted.  Made a large amount, so I  shared with family.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: How can I duplicate the taste of calamansi? I’ve never seen them.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Lemon juice will work, or as Joel mentioned above, the puree/juice is available on Amazon.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Asian grocery stores often have calamansi juice. Often in the freezer section.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Any input on sourcing or substituting for the calamansi juice? I can find calamansi puree on amazon (I assume the sweetened drink would not suffice) or should I just use fresh-squeezed lime juice?

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Just sub in plain lemon juice. There’s also a 100% calamansi purée which would be fine.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Key limes make a decent substitute.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: Key limes seem to be the closest to kalamansi.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: The print edition (in Northern CA) did not include the Asado recipe. They must have ran out of space.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: wouldn't it be more flavorful to cook in chicken stock instead of water?  i rarely use water in recipes like this.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody:
Wait a minute...4 lbs 0f pork shoulder for 6 servings?  Even after trimming and rendering, those are huge servings.  Anyone have a thought or experience to help me scale this?
I cook for 15-25 crew on a tall ship in Bellingham WA (schoonerzodiac.com), and need to have hearty dinners in the winter, but can't abide too many leftovers or waste.
Thanks

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: What would you serve with this as a side?

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I made this last night. It was good but on the salty side. I used real Filipino ingredients that tend to have strong flavors. I would cut the soy sauce by half and the real calamansi by 1/4 of a cup.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I used real fresh calamansi juice and it turned out way to sour with the quantity indicated. I would cut this in half. Also, watch the type of soy sauce you use. Filipino soy sauce is way saltier than other brands. I had to throw away half the sauce and replace it with water. It was way too overwhelming. When I make this again, I will definitely make the adjustments as it is a very tasty dish.

author: Priya Krishna
commentBody: I've never had this before so cannot vouch for authenticity of flavor, but easy to make and tasty. Instead of kalamansi, I made w/ lemon and lime juice. Served w/ roasted japanese sweet potato and cucumber salad. Next time before serving, I'd reduce about half of the poaching liquid for a glaze. I suppose it would be a different dish, but this was good w/out the cheese and evaporated milk-- prob would skip them all together if I make again. Or I'd melt cheese on a tortilla and eat like carnitas.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Matzo Ball Soup a la Mexicana Recipe
commentBody: This is absolutely delicious. We split it up over two days, making the broth first. My husband, who is Jewish, struggled a little over the nonstandard ingredients and cooking methods (he had never cooked matzo balls in the broth, for example), but when all was said and done it was delightful. This recipe is a keeper.
