author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Matzo Ball Soup a la Mexicana Recipe
commentBody: I cannot wait to try this recipe.  The flavors are springing off the page into my imagination.  I can already smell it simmering.  Thank you NYTimes and Fany Gerson for sharing this "dynamic take on a classic" and dynamic blend of cultures.  It's all that and more.  Love it!

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Matzo Ball Soup a la Mexicana Recipe
commentBody: Made this for Rosh Hashanah to shake things up a bit. Hands down, the soup was fantastic. Deep flavorful broth, the matzoh balls may have been the best I’ve ever had! Perfect texture, loved the herbs, we all went back for more. I made two changes-kept the herbs and vegetables in the broth for the heartiness and subbed one jalapeño for the two Serrano’s (we have a million jalapeños from our garden). Served it with a home -made challah, fresh applesauce, and we were in heaven. Happy New Year!

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Matzo Ball Soup a la Mexicana Recipe
commentBody:
I've been making matzo balls for decades, but these were the best ever. 

 I kept the Mexican additions on the side and passed a bowl of New Mexican green chilies and avocado to be determined by the guest.  A huge hit.  Will use this recipe for every holiday from now on.  But don't bother putting the balls on a baking sheet before putting them in the soup or water.  There's enough to do without adding this step.  Just roll and toss (gently) into the pot.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Matzo Ball Soup a la Mexicana Recipe
commentBody: Another note: The matzah balls are the best I've ever had. Absolutely! Be careful, though, they absorb a LOT of their cooking liquid, so be sure you've got enough broth. They tasted fine when cooked in salted water, and I doubled the quantity of herbs because, why not. Rather than mix the shredded chicken into the soup pot, pour a very little but of red hot broth on the chicken and then place the chicken in the bottom of each soup bowl. A lot easier than fishing the chicken bits out of the pot.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Matzo Ball Soup a la Mexicana Recipe
commentBody: I am in recovery from making this soup. I started at 10:45 a.m., and it is now 9:25 p.m. It was an ENDLESS process. I am just glad I didn't attempt this on a weekday. The matzo balls are wonderfully light and the broth is flavorful (especially with lime squeezed in), and the whole thing is delightful to eat, but honestly exhausting to make. We will enjoy it for the next two days. We got 23 matzo balls.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Matzo Ball Soup a la Mexicana Recipe
commentBody: I often season my chicken soup with sriracha sauce and soy sauce. Then I add sliced water chestnuts and baby bok choi for crunch. My matza balls are seasoned with whatever fresh herbs I have around such as chives. I view good chicken broth as a palette for almost any taste that I care to provide.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Matzo Ball Soup a la Mexicana Recipe
commentBody: Made this soup and some of the matzo balls fell apart what did i do wrong

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Matzo Ball Soup a la Mexicana Recipe
commentBody: Oh. My. Goodness. This is one of the best soups I’ve ever had. Matzoh ball soup, by any measure, is one of my favorite things in the world, but this is simply next level. The flavors are extraordinary and the matzoh balls, cooked in the broth, are the best I’ve ever had. No, this is not traditional, but absolutely delicious in every way.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Matzo Ball Soup a la Mexicana Recipe
commentBody:
Recipe is lovely -- but not (for us) a holiday meal.  Traditionally, kreplach are made for Rosh Hashanah -- and I'd love to see her version of that.
For these matzoh balls I tried her recipe, but cooked them as is traditional in water, not the broth -- because they absorb much too much liquid as they cook.  Also, I didn't use baking powder -- as I don't like the taste it imparts (and has a tendency to turn my beautiful matzo balls too dark.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Matzo Ball Soup a la Mexicana Recipe
commentBody: Made this with veggie broth for a vegetarian Seder. I eliminated the baking powder and baking soda to keep things kosher for Passover, and still got soft fluffy matzo balls without them. Beating the egg whites probably helps. This soup was a big hit around the table - a fun new take on a classic.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Matzo Ball Soup a la Mexicana Recipe
commentBody: I made this last year at Rosh Hashana and shared it with my neighbors who loved it. The use of herbs in the matzoh balls bring a delightful fresh flavor. Instead of half baking powder/half baking soda I used a combination of commercial and baker's yeast ("levure boulanger") as this was more readily available to me in France. I made the recipe again for a Jewish friend who pronounced them the lightest she had ever eaten. And I am making them now for Rosh Hashanah for my same neighbors.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Matzo Ball Soup a la Mexicana Recipe
commentBody: I have made this recipe twice, and it was my first time making matzo balls. The matzo balls came out perfect they were light and fluffy and did not fall apart. My husband is Jewish and said this is his favorite matzo ball recipe.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Matzo Ball Soup a la Mexicana Recipe
commentBody: Without question, the best matzah ball soup I have ever had! Excellent.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Matzo Ball Soup a la Mexicana Recipe
commentBody: Prepared as instructed with one exception, extra herbs in the matzo balls (incl. cilantro) and a side of tightly rolled corn tortillas. My partner is Jewish and grew up in the San Fernando Valley, apparently a land of incomparable delis. He HATES fusion foods and LOVES Mexican foods. I served this up without telling him what I’d done. Two days after he told me he didn’t want to try this recipe, he eagerly admitted that this is the best soup he’s ever had. Don’t sleep on this.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Matzo Ball Soup a la Mexicana Recipe
commentBody: I think this is a lovely recipie.  The only complaint I have is the 2 1/2 teaspoons of salt for matzo balls.  I bought no sodium matzo meal and do believe this is too much salt!   I would reduce the salt to 1 teaspoon of kosher salt.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Matzo Ball Soup a la Mexicana Recipe
commentBody: This recipe has been a huge crowd pleaser every year I've made it.  Making the broth from scratch with the two chickens is a little decadent, but it really does pay off.  I used boxed mix for the matzo balls the second time I did this and it was still great; the key with this recipe is the stock and the colorful array of garnishes.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Matzo Ball Soup a la Mexicana Recipe
commentBody: Instead of matzo balls, make MASA balls, by subbing (organic) masa harina for the matzo meal in this, or in your favorite recipe for matzo balls. Really delicious, more Mexican tasting and gluten free.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Matzo Ball Soup a la Mexicana Recipe
commentBody: My chicken soup with matzah balls,  assorted veg., and chicken will be the main course of my rosh hashana diner. A lovely tossed salad with apples and honey/mustard dressing will round out the meal. We are eating lighter these days so brisket is a rare treat.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Matzo Ball Soup a la Mexicana Recipe
commentBody: SOunds delish. Just wondering why Matza Ball soup for Rosh ha shanna? We only eat that for Passover. Just curious. I like to record family holiday traditions. We eat Leeks, Fish, pomegranate, squash and dates. Its always interesting to see what other families cook.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Matzo Ball Soup a la Mexicana Recipe
commentBody: Respectfully, it would be really helpful if you tried the recipe and commented rather than indicating that you won't be trying it,  preferring your own recipes. This does not advance a practical understanding of THIS recipe, good or bad, as tried and cooked by readers.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Matzo Ball Soup a la Mexicana Recipe
commentBody: @Rebecca. We eat matzo ball soup year round in our house.  It’s my kids favorite soup and meal!!!!!

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Matzo Ball Soup a la Mexicana Recipe
commentBody: just made this broth using bones, carcass, necks, wings from three previously cooked chickens--not whole chickens as am using this first as "straight up" matzah ball soup without shredded chicken. INCREDIBLY DELICIOUS. A KEEPER. A fresh spicy spin on this traditional soup. LOVE THE HEAT. Full of flavor but tastes light.Will add shredded chicken from Melissa's Peruvian chicken recipe to the broth later in the week for another meal. On to the matzah balls this afternoon....

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Matzo Ball Soup a la Mexicana Recipe
commentBody: This is a flavorful soup. Did have issues with the matzo balls falling apart in the broth as well. I think next time I’ll pop them in the freezer about 10 minutes after shaping to see if they hold their shape.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Matzo Ball Soup a la Mexicana Recipe
commentBody: Delicious. I had no issue with the matzo balls maintaining their ball-ness. I think it helped to dry the onion vigorously and shape the balls gently. I ladled them into the pot with care and they were very fine indeed.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Matzo Ball Soup a la Mexicana Recipe
commentBody: Ix-nay on the baking powder in the matzah balls at Passover! The baking soda is KP for most people, but baking powder definitely not.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Matzo Ball Soup a la Mexicana Recipe
commentBody: Incredible recipe, and easy too if you split up the cooking between two days, like I did. I even made the broth 3 weeks before and froze it. I just defrosted the broth, made the matzo balls, and cooked them in the broth. So good! Everyone was impressed and asked for the recipe.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Matzo Ball Soup a la Mexicana Recipe
commentBody: The effort was great, but the outcome greater. I grew more and more featured with the seemingly unnecessary step of the recipe; the from scratch both, the whipped until peaked eggs whites "folded" into the matzo meal. But, in the end, the flavors were undeniably delicious. I will try to see if supposedly delicious flavors can be achieved with less effort (boxed both, skipping the whipped the stiff peaks egg whites), but even still, with that extra effort, I would make it again. Pray for me!

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Matzo Ball Soup a la Mexicana Recipe
commentBody: For passover 2024, made this recipe, as well as three other NYT matzo ball recipes, and this one won! It's what we'll use going forward.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Matzo Ball Soup a la Mexicana Recipe
commentBody: I added 1-2 tablespoons of chicken bouillon and a teaspoon of ground cumin; they really made a difference. So good!

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Matzo Ball Soup a la Mexicana Recipe
commentBody: My matzo balls didn't hold up while cooking in the broth. I don't know if it was the baking powder/soda or aired egg whites that made them to puff up. They broke up while cooking and it all became a mush. Maybe I didn't let them chill enough in the fridge prior to cooking them.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Matzo Ball Soup a la Mexicana Recipe
commentBody:
Can I make the matza balls the night before and refrigerate them uncooked, then boil them before serving? Makes for a much shorter day in the kitchen.

Also, Adding shredded chicken to the serving bowl complicates things. I'd definitely put the chicken shreds in the bottom of each bowl, ladle on the hot soup and then serve the hot boiled matzah balls.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Matzo Ball Soup a la Mexicana Recipe
commentBody: I do that with matzoh balls, and it works very well.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Matzo Ball Soup a la Mexicana Recipe
commentBody: I make a big pot of these amazing matzoh balls, then freeze in small bags what I don't use, pull them out every time I take chicken soup from the freezer. it is a treat all year round instead of just at Passover, though these shouldn't be used at Passover because of the leavening.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Matzo Ball Soup a la Mexicana Recipe
commentBody: really delicious and flavorful! the matzo balls were amazing

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Matzo Ball Soup a la Mexicana Recipe
commentBody: This is by far my favorite matzo ball soup recipe! Made it earlier this year with rabbit instead of chicken. The herby broth is out of this world! I've also found it does great as a vegetarian soup, with veggie stock rather than chicken and no meat. But be sure to simmer whatever broth you use with the cilantro, parsley, and serranos.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Matzo Ball Soup a la Mexicana Recipe
commentBody: You have to live in the country side,  have a huge kitchen and plenty  of time preparing such a dish. Although it sounds delicious but not for a minimalist as I am .

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Matzo Ball Soup a la Mexicana Recipe
commentBody:
Being a city person with a typical kitchen, I read through the recipe multiple times trying to understand this comment. ANY homemade "ball recipe" (whether meat balls, desserts, or matzo balls) will take time, and will require you to hand-form balls and put them on a tray! Part of the territory.

Likewise, homemade broth is going to require more time - again, part of the territory. Although I will give ya that 2 chickens plus 10 whole wings does seem excessive!

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Matzo Ball Soup a la Mexicana Recipe
commentBody: I made this in my tiny kitchen (I can open the oven door from my front door) so definitely doable: just a question of organization. Re time commitment: I find it much easier to make over two days (soup one day, matzoh balls/serve the next). It's worth it :-)

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Matzo Ball Soup a la Mexicana Recipe
commentBody:
Matzah Balls are eaten ALL year long, not just on Passover!
Why limit it to Passover and deny yourself the enjoyment of being able to eat it whenever you want. I very often add matzah balls when making pea soup. Yum...
Wonderful on any night.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Matzo Ball Soup a la Mexicana Recipe
commentBody: Wow, this is going to be my new standard recipe for chicken stock, even if we're not adding matzo balls (which were, in fact, light, fluffy and delicious). My young family was not too keen on the chili, onion, cilantro garnish, so next time I'll add some diced carrot and celery right before the matzo balls go in. The avocados were popular though, and the chili infusion in the stock gave it the perfect background of spiciness.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Matzo Ball Soup a la Mexicana Recipe
commentBody: Looking forward to trying this. But I will skin one of the chickens first and render the fat to use in the matzo balls.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Matzo Ball Soup a la Mexicana Recipe
commentBody:
I mostly followed this for the soup base(My grandmother taught me her Matzo Ball recipe) and it came out extremely good. 

I swapped in some fire roasted Hatch Chilies as that is what I had on hand, and I used only chicken breasts as I really didn't want to pick a whole bird. 

Only other thing was that I used an immersion blender on the veggies rather than straining them out. 

Real good take on the idea!

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Matzo Ball Soup a la Mexicana Recipe
commentBody: I already had a rich stock bones from roast chicken and chicken feet.Got another roast chicken. These matzo balls were fabulous. Used the chicken fat from the broth. Great garnishes. Wish I could get fresh epazote

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Matzo Ball Soup a la Mexicana Recipe
commentBody: I was a bit disappointed with this recipe. It tasted like a basic chicken soup recipe. I prefer my Yemenite soup better. The Matza balls were good after I added a bit of ground cumin and ground coriander. For the soup recipe- too much work for a mediocre result. Try Tori Avey's recipe. It is spectacular. https://toriavey.com/toris-kitchen/yemenite-soup/

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Matzo Ball Soup a la Mexicana Recipe
commentBody: I followed with a big chicken and 8 wings, 2 serrano and a jalapeno, and a tablespoon of Hatch green chile.  My family loved it.  I did just use the Yahuda Matzo Ball recipe, plus the herbs.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Matzo Ball Soup a la Mexicana Recipe
commentBody: This was a great hit at our family Hanukkah gathering. I made the broth the day before and the Matzo balls day of and it made the effort needed much more manageable.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Matzo Ball Soup a la Mexicana Recipe
commentBody: Lots of work. Big kitchen mess. Really tasty but matcha balls fell apart! Just serving like a chicken soup with rice!

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Matzo Ball Soup a la Mexicana Recipe
commentBody: So sorry to report that my almost all my matzo balls fell apart, and I'm no matzo ball novice.  Such a bummer!  Did this happen to anyone else, and how does one prevent this?  Despite that, my husband and our friend raved about the soup, particularly the broth, so I plan to try again but I'd like to prevent this from happening in the future.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Matzo Ball Soup a la Mexicana Recipe
commentBody: How many quart stockpot would be ideal for this? Thanks!

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Matzo Ball Soup a la Mexicana Recipe
commentBody: Has anyone tried a vegetarian version of this? Any suggestions? The seasonings look very good. Joan Nathan has a vegan matzo ball soup recipe in this database; if anyone has tried that work around but used these spices, any suggestions? Thanks!

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Matzo Ball Soup a la Mexicana Recipe
commentBody: Can anyone say anything about freezing portions of the soup?

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Matzo Ball Soup a la Mexicana Recipe
commentBody: My son called right before coming home from college for Thanksgiving break of his freshman year. He is sick and needs matzo ball soup to get better. He loves my broth, but my brother in law made his matzo balls with butter and he wanted matzo balls with butter. I didn’t love my brother in law’s recipe. I searched and could not find a recipe, with butter, that I liked. I made these, substituting butter for the oil, and used dill as my herb. They are phenomenal! Light, fiull of flavor, and my son said, “just what I needed!”

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Matzo Ball Soup a la Mexicana Recipe
commentBody: Can the matzoh balls be prepared (nor cooked) in advance?

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Matzo Ball Soup a la Mexicana Recipe
commentBody: My 10 year old and I made this for Passover this week. It was so good, but the matzo balls were a bit tricky to get to stick together once in the broth to cook! Packing tighter helped a bit. I made my broth/veggie base by sauteeing carrots, small chopped celery, and a chopped yellow onion, adding water w/chicken better than bouillon. I also sauteed a head of garlic minced and added to the soup base. I bundled a bunch of cilantro stems and leaves + 1/2 jalapeno into the broth for about 2 hours.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Matzo Ball Soup a la Mexicana Recipe
commentBody: Delicious. This is my first try at matzo balls soup. We celebrate Easter but I couldn’t resist I highly recommend this Jewish and Mexican fusion. It’s scrumptious.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Matzo Ball Soup a la Mexicana Recipe
commentBody: This broth changed my life. It is the most flavorful thing I have EVeR made.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Roast Chicken With Apricot Glaze Recipe
commentBody:
These look like wonderful dishes; the family story is great to read.  BUT The Times did it again!  For the umpteenth time, it publishes recipes for a Jewish holiday meal that are- blatantly not kosher.  Yes, I know.  I miniscule number of Jews keep kosher.  But it is clueless, at best, to not even note that the dishes aren't kosher.  Quintessential Jewish cooking, born of different  cultures but all Jewish, must recognize and RESPECT
the foundations of that food.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Roast Chicken With Apricot Glaze Recipe
commentBody: Haven't made this yet but I already know I will make enough glaze to  massage some under the skin before I pop the chicken in the oven.  One of my favorite methods for getting good flavor on the meat.  And with the rest of the glaze, baste as instructed.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Roast Chicken With Apricot Glaze Recipe
commentBody: butter? not if you keep kosher

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Roast Chicken With Apricot Glaze Recipe
commentBody: amazing right? I'm sure margarine or olive oil is just fine too

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Roast Chicken With Apricot Glaze Recipe
commentBody: olive oil.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Roast Chicken With Apricot Glaze Recipe
commentBody: Nowhere does it claim to be kosher. I'm sure you could replace w/ a vegetable oil of your choice and it would work just as well. I imagine avocado oil would be lovely.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Roast Chicken With Apricot Glaze Recipe
commentBody: I don't understand the pan/rack set up. How high above the liquid should the rack be? A few inches? I have a rack that is made to fit right inside the sheet pan. Should the rack be placed sideways so it rests on the sheet pan rim above the liquid? I wish NYT would provide pictures like so many other "free" cooking websites to give directions on unfamiliar set ups.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Roast Chicken With Apricot Glaze Recipe
commentBody: I haven't received such rave reviews for a chicken dish in a long time, and I cook a LOT of chicken for my family. I used bone in chicken thighs and breasts, and I followed the other note here to also rub the apricot/butter mixture under the skin before cooking. The sauce was incredible. This is a company worthy chicken dish!

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Roast Chicken With Apricot Glaze Recipe
commentBody:
This was a real 'patchke' to prepare
.  But it was the best roast chicken I ever made.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Roast Chicken With Apricot Glaze Recipe
commentBody: I am not sure what others did to get confused about this recipe and directions and why it got low ratings by some.  I thought it was fabulous. The glaze carmelizes with the garlic in pan and makes a delicious sauce and a chicken with crispy skin.  I cook lots and love it, so maybe prep for me is easy.  I used homemade broth, Maybe that made a difference.  KEEPER!

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Roast Chicken With Apricot Glaze Recipe
commentBody: For extra flavor, put some sliced onion pieces and sliced dried apricots into the cavity of the chicken when it starts to bake. Scrape these out when the chicken is done and add to the rice or whatever you're serving on the side.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Roast Chicken With Apricot Glaze Recipe
commentBody: This recipe has taught me a very important life lesson. At 74 years old, I have no business in the kitchen and deserve to be out in a fine restaurant.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Roast Chicken With Apricot Glaze Recipe
commentBody: Without the usual big family feast to prepare this year, this was the perfect dish for a small festive holiday dinner.  I was a bit worried that the cooking time was too long, but the chicken was beautifully browned, very moist and tasty.  Agree that the instructions seem more complicated than they need to be, but the sauce was delicious and the end result justified the work.  This can definitely be made kosher using certified parve unsalted margarine (e.g. Fleischmanns).

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Roast Chicken With Apricot Glaze Recipe
commentBody: Delicious and easy.  I didn't add the dried apricots or pistachios, because I didn't have them.  I also just melted the apricot butter, then poured over the chicken at the end. It was great.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Roast Chicken With Apricot Glaze Recipe
commentBody: We like crispy chicken skin.  Any suggestions on how to crisp the skin while keeping the glaze basted?  Thank you.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Roast Chicken With Apricot Glaze Recipe
commentBody: my toddlers are chicken-averse. not so with this sweet concoction--it wasn't too treacly and was very forgiving. adding to my staple of kid-friendly dishes.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Roast Chicken With Apricot Glaze Recipe
commentBody: Use smart balance or something like that.  That is what I did-no butter.  Delicious.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Roast Chicken With Apricot Glaze Recipe
commentBody: This was fantastic-- I was nervous because we were making it for the first time for some guests but it turned out amazing. Everyone was very surprised and impressed by it, and will definitely be made again in the future. The red wine vinegar wasn't crucial, I forgot to buy some. We used rice vinegar instead and it made no difference.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Roast Chicken With Apricot Glaze Recipe
commentBody: This seems like a good gluten free recipe. If I eat gluten I get a really awful skin ailment and it takes a long time to go away. I wish the Times would pay closer attention to the fact that many people can’t eat it and would address this problem and solution & clearly mark if gluten free - esp for people who have recently discovered this cure.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Roast Chicken With Apricot Glaze Recipe
commentBody: The glaze was by far and away the best part of this dish! To me its taste didn’t translate to the chicken and it was too much of a complicated, time-consuming recipe to make again. However I’d definitely make the glaze again and drizzle it on baked chicken breasts / thighs.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Roast Chicken With Apricot Glaze Recipe
commentBody: This was delicious. I ended up making a gravy, rather than an au jus and it was lovely. Followed the suggestion to toss apricots and pistachios into the cavity. Served with a pilaf(ish) of apricots, toasted pistachio and onion - yum.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Roast Chicken With Apricot Glaze Recipe
commentBody: I followed the recipe to a T with ingredients. The only thing I changed: I placed the chicken in with the broth etc on the sided pan. It was DELICIOUS. But, next time, I’m going to buy that roasting rack to rest on the sided pan. I got squeamish with putting the chicken straight onto the rack. (I read this correctly??) I need an easier clean up… but wow, that sauce!! Five stars, can’t wait to make it for the kids for a Sunday dinner.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Roast Chicken With Apricot Glaze Recipe
commentBody: "Rack set over the baking sheet" refers to a purpose-made baking rack which you could purchase from Amazon.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Roast Chicken With Apricot Glaze Recipe
commentBody: The chicken came out so moist and crisp, though it took a bit longer to cook than expected. I will say that the prep for this meal is no joke and is quite time consuming, so be prepared to get an early jump on it.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Roast Chicken With Apricot Glaze Recipe
commentBody: The best part of this is the glaze. I used home canned peach jam and dried apricots. Then, I used any leftover glaze on mashed potatoes. It's so good.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Roast Chicken With Apricot Glaze Recipe
commentBody:
Solid dish, added butter under the skin, and the dish paired well with rice to soak up that great sauce.

The second half of the recipe could more clear and should be re-written. Also, the sliced apricots and chopped nuts don’t add value; they are afterthoughts.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Roast Chicken With Apricot Glaze Recipe
commentBody: Spatchcocked the chicken, salt, peppered and squeezed lemon juice over chicken, rubbed the glaze inside the skin along with a couple of sage leaves, and basted halfway through cooking. Roasted the chicken at 450 for about 30 minutes in a preheated cast iron skillet over the (sliced) onion, (juiced) lemon, thyme and garlic cloves. After roasting discarded onion et al and made the glaze with the drippings in the pan, a bit of chicken broth and apricot butter. Delicious!

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Roast Chicken With Apricot Glaze Recipe
commentBody: Am a little confused. Recipe says to cook 1 1/4 hours then in step 5 says to baste and cook another 30 mins. And baste once more and cook 15 mins. So is the total cooking time 2 hours? Or is the basting part of the first mentioned 1 1/4 hours. Would like to know before I try this recipe. Thanks

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Roast Chicken With Apricot Glaze Recipe
commentBody: As I read it, the first step of roasting takes 1 hr 15 minutes. Then the glaze is added and the chicken is roasted another 30 minutes, to a total of 1 hour 45 min. The chicken should be basted halfway through the final 30 minutes—that’s where the “baste again after 15 minutes” direction comes in.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Roast Chicken With Apricot Glaze Recipe
commentBody: Seems like a long tome for a chicken roast. But I’ll try it anyway and pull it out after 1.5 hrs tops.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Roast Chicken With Apricot Glaze Recipe
commentBody: I made this for my wife and myself. The directions were hard to follow. I even made the pistachios and sliced apricots — that step was not worth it. In step 3 it says roast for 1 hour 15 minutes. In step 5 it says to roast for another 30 minutes. Does that mean a total of 105 minutes?

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Roast Chicken With Apricot Glaze Recipe
commentBody: my toddler are chicken-averse. not so with this sweet concoction--it wasn't too treacly and was very forgiving. adding to my staple of kid-friendly dishes.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Roast Chicken With Apricot Glaze Recipe
commentBody: For those questioning the time - the total time listed at the beginning of the recipe says “about two hours”. So yes, you add the times (same for “another hour”).

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Roast Chicken With Apricot Glaze Recipe
commentBody: This recipe yielded extremely moist chicken, which I assume was due to roasting it over broth, but it wasn't as flavorful as I'd hoped.  And there were unnecessarily finicky preparations for stuffing it and flavoring the broth and especially making the glaze, which could easily be streamlined.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Roast Chicken With Apricot Glaze Recipe
commentBody: If it were streamlined, it would likely be even less flavorful than you hoped.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Roast Chicken With Apricot Glaze Recipe
commentBody: how much liquid should the pan  dripping be?

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Roast Chicken With Apricot Glaze Recipe
commentBody: I’d like to try this with chicken pieces. Any suggestions?

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Roast Chicken With Apricot Glaze Recipe
commentBody: Start with chicken pieces.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Roast Chicken With Apricot Glaze Recipe
commentBody: The directions were inadequate, too wordy, and convoluted..     And sliced apricots as a garnish?  No, mix in the sliced apricots with the finished sauce and pour over the chicken.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Roast Chicken With Apricot Glaze Recipe
commentBody: Not kosher but used orange olive oil instead of the butter.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Roast Chicken With Apricot Glaze Recipe
commentBody:
If you can use olive oil will that help
All the Jewish cooks?

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Roast Chicken With Apricot Glaze Recipe
commentBody: Pretty much made as noted. Substituted peach balsamic vinegar for red wine vinegar and SmartBalance for butter, plus no salt. Otherwise, as noted. Tender, moist, nice sauce. Easy to do, more or less.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Roast Chicken With Apricot Glaze Recipe
commentBody: 500 degrees makes a smoky mess. Personally 425 for a bit longer is a much better option

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Roast Chicken With Apricot Glaze Recipe
commentBody: Sorry, 450 makes a smoky mess

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Roast Chicken With Apricot Glaze Recipe
commentBody: A lot of work and a little disappointed in the result. It tasted fine but for all the work involved I would have hoped for something a little more special.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Roast Chicken With Apricot Glaze Recipe
commentBody: Plenty of good vegan "butters" that can be used if you are keeping kosher

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Roast Chicken With Apricot Glaze Recipe
commentBody: I made this according to the recipe, using my own apricot preserves. The chicken was succulent and the sauce was delicious. The recipe was complicated, but well worth it.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Roast Chicken With Apricot Glaze Recipe
commentBody: I followed the recipe and served it tonight. Everyone loved it. No complaints and we look forward to delicious leftovers.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Roast Chicken With Apricot Glaze Recipe
commentBody: I used a spatchcocked chicken and it turned out beautiful and very delicious. I’ll serve it for Rosh Hashanah ladies luncheon and can’t wait to hear what they think now that I’ve given it a test run. Thanks so much- fabulous dish. I wonder if peach preserves and dried peaches would work in lieu of apricots to give it a Southern twist. Yum!

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Roast Chicken With Apricot Glaze Recipe
commentBody: This was very good: tasty sweet-and-sour sauce with an immaculately tender and glossy bird. I was a bit confused by the pan setup, but simply placed a cooling rack inside a sheet pan and that worked nicely. I wound up with a LOT of liquid, so I would reduce the stock a bit, and either way, consider mashing some flour into the final dollop of apricot-butter to make a Beurre manié and help thicken the sauce when boiling.

author: Priya Krishna
recipeTitle: Roast Chicken With Apricot Glaze Recipe
commentBody: Baking one chicken for 1 hr 45 mins seems much too long, though one comment says it's fine and the result is moist and juicy.  Hmm.  I will check with an oven thermometer to reach 165 degrees F and see how long that takes.  Loved Curtis's comment though I love cooking and being in the kitchen even though I'm almost 15 years older....
